title: Cutting down on ultra-processed foods isn’t easy. Here are eight tips that worked for my family | Well actually | The Guardian
description: Experts say reducing ultra-processed foods can be challenging in a world built around convenience, but these strategies can help make it more manageable
Author: Jen Sherman
Headline: Experts say reducing ultra-processed foods can be challenging in a world built around convenience, but these strategies can help make it more manageable
Content: Plant foods are some of the easiest, most plentiful substitutes for UPF.Well actuallyCutting down on ultra-processed foods isn’t easy. Here are eight tips that worked for my familyExperts say reducing ultra-processed foods can be challenging in a world built around convenience, but these strategies can help make it more manageableJen ShermanWed 22 Jul 2026 12.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 14.08 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleI have been trying toeat fewer ultra-processed foodswith my family for five years, and it has been challenging.Many experts agree that ultra-processed foods (UPF) pose health risks, whether because ofunhealthy ingredients, links todiseaseandenvironmental impacts, or replacing more nutritious foods. Thus, some consumers – including me – are interested in removing them from their daily diet.Getting here took time, effort and consistency, but we have had some success. Here are some strategies we used in our family.1. Eat more plantsPlant foods are some of the easiest, most plentiful substitutes for UPF. Most obvious are fruits and vegetables, but nuts, seeds, popcorn and roasted seaweed are other tasty, nutritious options.When it comes to introducing fruits and vegetables, the six to 12-month age is crucial, says Kary Rappaport, pediatric occupational therapist and the executive director ofSolid Starts, an organization that supports parents with feeding their children: “It is an exceedingly important window of time, when most babies are most developmentally open to a wide variety of foods and flavors.”Introducing new foods to older children is similar: use small tastes, different preparations and a child’s natural curiosity, she says. Focus on describing the food, rather than whether the child likes it – for example, encourage them to lick or take a bite and notice qualities like sweetness or temperature. “It’s not a value judgment on the food,” she says.I tried an anti-inflammatory diet for a week. This is what I learnedRead moreAdditionally, timing matters. “We are all more motivated to try and like things when we’re a little bit hungry,” says Rappaport. Try “putting out a tray of raw veggies with hummus while you’re cooking dinner, during that window where kids are looking to snack”, she suggests. On the other hand, don’t try to force a hangry child to try new veggies.“Even when you don’t have great options that are fully non-UPF, incorporate vegetables and fruits and raw products as much as possible,” says Julia Wolfson, an associate professor in public health at Johns Hopkins University.2. Buy the best ingredients you can affordAt our local farmers’ market, there is a stall selling fantastic apples. When in season, they are consistently crisp, sweet and apple-y. This is true for much of the food at the market.“Whatever they have in your farmers’ market is what is seasonal, and what is seasonal is going to be better,” says chef Penny Stankiewicz. “If you go to a standard American grocery store and you buy a perfectly round, somewhat red tomato, it’s going to have absolutely no flavor in January. But in August, when it’s prime tomato season, you don’t have to even do anything to that tomato to make it amazing.”Produce is typically more expensive at a farmer’s market, and not everyone has access to one. But the flavor payoff can be huge. Otherwise, try the produce at different grocery stores; additionally, frozen and canned produce is usually preserved at its peak. After you source your produce, learn the best ways to store it so it lasts as long as possible.3. Openly discuss food, its effects and its originsPart of my motivation to minimize UPF was not wanting to support powerful corporations that exploited workers, mistreated animals and harmed ecosystems. The more I learned about food systems, the less I wanted to participate in them as a consumer.Teaching children where food comes from and how to prepare it builds appreciation and understanding. Rappaport also suggests discussing how foods make their bodies feel: for instance, “if I eat too many gummy worms or I only eat a bag of Flamin’ Hot Cheetos on our car trip, I’m going to feel bad. It’s not gonna sit well in my belly.”Familiarize yourself and your family with the definition of UPF, so that you can identify one by looking at an ingredient list, says Wolfson.4. Learn to cookI already knew how to cook, but I studied the science of cooking as we embarked on this project. Now, my from-scratch meals taste better than before, sometimes more so than UPF alternatives.Often the barriers to cooking are systemic: insufficient time and energy to cook. People may also lack confidence in their kitchen skills. “We need to make it easier for people to cook healthy meals from scratch, and that means making those scratch ingredients more available and affordable, and building those skills from a young age so that people have the confidence and self-efficacy,” says Wolfson.Stankiewicz offers the following tips for newer home cooks:Learn techniques: “Once you know how to saute, it doesn’t matter what you put in the pan.”Develop knife skills: “If people want to get more comfortable in the kitchen, they should take a knife skills class. Those skills are a massive upscale for confidence in the kitchen.”Know your taste: “Know what you like, then create that, because that’s the starting place. Don’t start by making miso brownies when the core is to make a really good brownie that you like, because then you’ll do it again.”Wolfson found in her research that “when people cook more at home, they consume fewer ultra-processed foods.” She notes that even when people are cooking frequently, the majority of their calories often still come from UPF. Try not to feel defeated if you notice something similar; this speaks to the ubiquity of UPF, she says: “If you picture a typical American grocery store, almost 70% of the foods in the store are ultra processed … that’s what’s mostly available to us.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toWell ActuallyPractical advice, expert insights and answers to your questions about how to live a good lifePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion5. Learn to bakeIn terms of replacing UPF, replacing desserts and snacks was an easy sell. I wasn’t replacing cookies with carrot sticks; I was replacing them with cookies – sometimes better ones.Get a sharp knife, season properly and don’t do too much at once: how to start cookingRead moreStankiewicz says to approach baking with fearlessness. “Messing up is part of it and is really how you learn,” she says, telling me that brownies were created by accident when a baker didn’t add enough flour to the cake.The next step is to find trusted recipe sources. Start with recipes that are not overly intimidating or complicated. Read the recipe through before beginning, then gather the ingredients. Organize the ingredients (mise-en-place) – and then just do it!6. Make cooking at home easyConsuming UPF is easy. It takes very little time and mental capacity to, say, unwrap and heat something. Try to make cooking and baking at home similarly easy.First, master a few dishes. “Find a handful of recipes or techniques that are accessible in terms of ingredients, cost, number of ingredients, and how long they’re going to take to make. Then just get really good at those,” says Stankiewicz.Second, build a pantry. Keep a stock of frequently used ingredients. My husband and I maintain a shared shopping list, and if we notice something running low, we update the list.Third, make more than you need. Leftovers are great. Even better are meals that can be frozen and reheated because your past self did all the work.7. Prioritize mealtimesWe have a rule at the table: no phones or toys during mealtimes. We have conversations instead (often mundane and not terribly deep). Our meals aren’t long; they usually take 20 minutes from when we sit down to when my youngest is done.“The research is really clear that family meals, where people are eating together, have a huge positive impact on child development across many different areas in terms of school performance, mental health and social engagement,” says Rappaport.It can be difficult when schedules are varied. Rappaport has older children and strives to fit in three dinners together each week around work and extracurriculars. It’s less about “having a Norman Rockwell meal at the dinner table” than finding opportunities to engage, she says. “It can be burritos that we got for takeout while we’re all sitting at the park waiting for practice to start. I can still find those same benefits, which are that I’m asking about their day. I’m showing interest in them. We are connecting around the food.”8. Be relentlessly consistent – and don’t be too hard on yourselfWe have not completely eliminated UPF. But for the most part, I try to cook, choose whole food items and purchase better-quality ingredients. Over five years and thousands of decisions, our palates have shifted to prefer non-UPF foods. Cooking and baking have become routine. When you reach the stage where UPF is no longer desirable taste-wise, and regular cooking is as habitual as brushing your teeth, opting out of UPF becomes less daunting.Without fail, every expert I spoke with emphasized the structural factors that make it difficult for consumers to reduce UPF consumption. Their prevalence, coupled with time and cost pressures mean that UPF often makes the most sense. There will be unexpected work emergencies or illnesses that make cooking from scratch impossible. Some days are full of extracurriculars and social events, and you won’t have time to bake or cook. You may attend catered events, meetings or functions where you have no control over the food.The vast majority of us will eat at least some UPF. Your children will eat UPF – especially at birthday parties and play dates. But that’s not failure, just reality. Being realistic about your options can help you stay consistent instead of giving up entirely, and that’s ultimately what got me and my family to this point. When it comes to UPF consumption, don’t let perfect become the enemy of good.Explore more on these topicsWell actuallyFoodUltra-processed foodsHealth & wellbeingBakingDiets and dietingfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: The Treasury will check Andy Burnham’s ambitions – unless old orthodoxies are challenged | Larry Elliott | The Guardian
description: Fifty years ago, another Healey ran the Treasury. Today’s chancellor may find some things haven’t changed, says Guardian columnist Larry Elliott
Author: Larry Elliott
Headline: Fifty years ago, another Healey ran the Treasury. Today’s chancellor may find some things haven’t changed, says Guardian columnist Larry Elliott
Content: John Healey addresses staff at the Treasury, after becoming chancellor, London, 21 July 2026.Photograph: Stefan Rousseau/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenJohn Healey addresses staff at the Treasury, after becoming chancellor, London, 21 July 2026.Photograph: Stefan Rousseau/AFP/Getty ImagesOpinionJohn HealeyThe Treasury will check Andy Burnham’s ambitions – unless old orthodoxies are challengedLarry ElliottFifty years ago, another Healey ran the Treasury. Today’s chancellor may find some things haven’t changedWed 22 Jul 2026 08.57 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 12.06 EDTShareSome things never change. Fifty years ago, Britain was sweltering in the long hotsummer of 1976. The Labour government was acutely aware that the financial markets were scrutinising its every move. And a man called Healey was running the Treasury.On the surface, the challenges facing John Healey are less severe than thoseDenis Healeyhad to grapple with half a century ago. Inflation isa lot lowerand the City has reacted calmly to Andy Burnham as prime minister. And, while things could turn nasty pretty quickly, they would have to get a lot worse before Healey Mk II felt the need to ask the International Monetary Fund for anemergency loan.That’s the good news. The bad news is that today’s Healey has big problems of his own to solve, not least how to reconcile his pledge to run a tight ship with the new administration’s aims of rolling back more than 40 years of Thatcherism and reindustrialising Britain. Fine aspirations will eventually run into the quicksands of fiscal orthodoxy.But so far things have gone as well as Burnham and Healey could have hoped. Themessage of optimism– a deliberate contrast with the doom and gloom when Keir Starmer arrived in Downing Street – was the right one. The cut in VAT on electricity bills and thecap on bus fareswere largely symbolic but symbols matter. Burnham was keen to avoid a repetition of Starmer’s first big economic policy announcement: the means testing of the winter fuel payment for pensioners. That and the purging of the Starmerites from the cabinet are intended to give the impression that the real Labour government starts here.View image in fullscreenThe then chancellor Denis Healey before presenting the budget, 30 March 1976,Photograph: Peter Cade/Getty ImagesOther conclusions can be drawn. Burnham clearly sees Reform UK as his biggest threat. That’s evident not just in the makeup of the cabinet – which has a stronger north of England flavour to it – but in theemphasis on the cost of livingand rebuilding Britain’s manufacturing base. There is an attempt to reconnect the party with its working-class roots.Keeping Shabana Mahmood as the home secretary was also significant because it shows that Burnham intends to maintain a tough stance on migration. That has economic consequences since it will force businesses faced with staff shortages to hire and train UK-born employees rather than bring them in from overseas. The days of unlimited cheap labour are over. Burnham’s desire to try a different economic model seems genuine, as does his intention to be heavily involved in the big economic decisions.Prime ministers come in three types. There are those who enjoy focusing on domestic issues, there are those who get a buzz from foreign policy and there are those who want to do everything. It is not rare for a prime minister to start a term of office with a burning passion to sort out the economy only to find after a while that glad-handing on the global stage is more fun. But, for the moment, Burnham falls squarely into category one, and has plans to create an economic power base for himself at No 10 to act as a counterweight to the Treasury.That, at least, is the theory. In reality, Burnham’s first 24 hours in office showed just how much power and influence the Treasury holds. Healey said meeting theexisting fiscal ruleswas his top priority, and he now has to find the money for a fully funded defence investment plan that the Treasury denied him when he was defence secretary,precipitating his resignation.The chancellor may get lucky and be able to pay for higher spending through stronger growth, but with the strait of Hormuz still closed and oil pricescreeping up againit would be inadvisable to bank on it. That leaves borrowing more or higher taxes. A bit of extra borrowing may be available while sticking to the fiscal rules, but Burnham and Healey have big ambitions and are wary of upsetting the bond markets, which are already charging the government interest rates of 5% to service its debts. Something has to give.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAndy Burnham is playing the long game in an age of short attention spans | Rafael BehrRead moreHealey has floated the idea of war bonds to pay for higher defence spending, but that would be borrowing by a different name. Inevitably, therefore, taxes are going to rise in the autumn budget and the only real questions are: by how much, who pays them and what damage they will cause.Faced with the prospect of a sluggish economy and tax rises to come, it is a tad hubristic for the seventh occupant of Downing Street in a decade to be serving notice that he will shortly be announcing a 10-year plan for change. Burnham doesn’t have the luxury of 10 years to deliver results. A general election is due within the next three years, leaving little wiggle room if things go seriously wrong – as they did for Denis Healey.Some things have changed since the long hot summer of 1976. One of the abiding legacies of Britain’s shift to the right in the 1970s was the idea that there was no difference between the government and a household when it comes to spending, with the implication that either can go bust if it lives beyond its means. That notion lives on today, with even the most modest of ministerial spending pledges met with the challenge: how are you going to pay for it? If Burnham is serious about rolling back neoliberalism, he needs to start by challenging this entirely wrong-headed idea.Larry Elliott is a Guardian columnistExplore more on these topicsJohn HealeyOpinionEconomic policyAndy BurnhamLabourTax and spendingcommentShareReuse this content

title: Mykhailo Drapatyi: Ukrainians hope new commander will end army’s ‘Soviet culture’ | Ukraine | The Guardian
description: Drapatyi has long been a critic of the military’s command culture, but experts warn change could take time
Author: Pjotr Sauer
Headline: Drapatyi has long been a critic of the military’s command culture, but experts warn change could take time
Content: Mykhailo Drapatyi has widespread popularity in Ukraine, and is seen as a moderniser.Photograph: Oleksandr Klymenko/EPAView image in fullscreenMykhailo Drapatyi has widespread popularity in Ukraine, and is seen as a moderniser.Photograph: Oleksandr Klymenko/EPAUkraineMykhailo Drapatyi: Ukrainians hope new commander will end army’s ‘Soviet culture’Drapatyi has long been a critic of the military’s command culture, but experts warn change could take timePjotr SauerWed 22 Jul 2026 13.48 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 11.38 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThrough a haze of smoke, a lone Ukrainian armoured vehicle suddenly burst into view. The BMP-2 accelerated towards a barricade of tyres and debris, smashing through it in seconds as protesters scattered.Behind the controls, on the chaotic streets of Mariupol on 9 May 2014, was a little-known officer named Mykhailo Drapatyi. The footage became one of the defining images of Russia’s first attempt to seize easternUkraine, and made Drapatyi one of Ukraine’s military heroes.Mykhailo Drapatyi drives armoured vehicle through barricade in 2014Twelve years later, he has the top job.On Tuesday night, the president, Volodymyr Zelenskyy, named the 43-year-old major generalas the commander-in-chief of Ukraine’s armed forces, putting him at the head of the military as Kyiv tries to shift the momentum of the war.The appointment was one of the few wartime decisions in Ukraine to command near-universal support, reflecting Drapatyi’s high standing across the country’s political, military and civil society circles, and helping to steady the political mood after days of turmoil in Kyiv.My friends left Ukraine after the Russian invasion. Now I wonder if we live in the same world | Diana BergRead moreDays earlier, thousands of demonstrators had taken to the streets after thedismissal of the popular defence minister, Mykhailo Fedorov, who had clashed with the then commander-in-chief, Gen Oleksandr Syrskyi, over the conduct of the war.Drapatyi’s appointment is likely to be welcomed by many of the protesters, particularly after Drapatyi publicly backed Fedorov in his dispute with Syrskyi.“Society expected change,” said Mykola Bielieskov, a research fellow at Ukraine’s National Institute for Strategic Studies. “It’s not only about changing how the war is fought or how the battlefield is managed. We also needed to restore confidence at home after the protests and return the focus to managing the war.”9:43Zelenskyy ousts Ukraine army chief after protests - The LatestDrapatyi belongs to a younger generation of Ukrainian commanders formed by more than a decade of fighting, and built his reputation on the frontline.Supporters say he took up drone warfare early, and pushed authority down to junior officers and expected them to answer for their own decisions.Perhaps more importantly for many Ukrainians, he represents a break with the Soviet-style command culture soldiers associated with Syrskyi, whom critics accused of relying on costly frontal assaults and spending soldiers’ lives too freely.“[Drapatyi] is a critic of Soviet-style thinking and micromanagement, and he recognises the problems with lying inside the military,” said Rob Lee, a senior fellow at the Foreign Policy Research Institute.Bielieskov described Drapatyi as “humane and open to dialogue” – qualities he said were unusual among Ukraine’s senior military leadership.He said Drapatyi commanded respect among both career officers and those who joined the military after 2022, because he had risen through the ranks on merit and understood the realities of the battlefield.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThis is EuropeThe most pressing stories and debates for Europeans – from identity to economics to the environmentPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionDrapatyi’s military career began after graduating from the Kharkiv Institute of Tank Troops in 2004, when he joined the 72nd Mechanised Brigade.After Russia’s full-scale invasion, Drapatyi helped halt the Russian advance towards Kryvyi Rih and played a key role in the liberation of Kherson. As Moscow regained the initiative, he was repeatedly dispatched to Ukraine’s most difficult sectors, stabilising the front around Pokrovsk before eventually being appointed commander of the joint forces.View image in fullscreenMykhailo Drapatyi shakes hands with Volodymyr Zelenskyy during their meeting in Kyiv on Monday.Photograph: Ukrainian Presidential Press Office/APDrapatyi has also been unusually outspoken about the need to reform the army’s internal culture. In a social media post last year, he criticised what he described as “an atmosphere of fear, lack of initiative, [and] imperviousness to feedback”, arguing that such attitudes undermined the military’s effectiveness.After a Russian missile strike on a training ground killed dozens of soldiers under his command, Drapatyi publicly accepted responsibility and offered his resignation rather than shift the blame on to subordinates – a gesture that won him widespread admiration and reinforced his reputation as one of Ukraine’s most accountable commanders.“President Zelenskyy made the best possible choice,” said the Ukrainian military analyst and veteran who writes under the pseudonym Tatarigami. “It will take time for changes to take effect. But I know many soldiers have renewed hope for change, and that is very important for morale.”Analysts warn, however, that expectations may have run ahead of what the job allows.Drapatyi is expected to improve battlefield management and the army’s day-to-day functioning. But many of Ukraine’s most pressing wartime challenges lay beyond the authority of any commander-in-chief, said Bielieskov.Long-running tensions between the defence ministry and the general staff remained unresolved, while the politically fraught issue of mobilisation reform continued to weigh on the war effort, Bielieskov said. “There is a risk that in six months he will be blamed for problems he cannot fully control. Many of the biggest challenges facing Ukraine lie outside his mandate.”Explore more on these topicsUkraineEuropefeaturesShareReuse this content

title: World Cup 2030 (very early) power rankings: who will challenge Spain at top? | World Cup | The Guardian
description: The next men’s World Cup is still four years away, but we take a look at the teams with the firmest foundations who should be primed to contend
Author: Jeff Rueter
Headline: The next men’s World Cup is still four years away, but we take a look at the teams with the firmest foundations who should be primed to contend
Content: Norway’s Erling Haaland, Morocco’s Ismael Saibari and Spain’s Lamine Yamal should all play significant roles for their teams in 2030.Composite: Guardian Design; Icon Sportswire/Getty Images; Getty Images; Fifa/Getty ImageView image in fullscreenNorway’s Erling Haaland, Morocco’s Ismael Saibari and Spain’s Lamine Yamal should all play significant roles for their teams in 2030.Composite: Guardian Design; Icon Sportswire/Getty Images; Getty Images; Fifa/Getty ImageWorld CupWorld Cup 2030 (very early) power rankings: who will challenge Spain at top?The next men’s World Cup is still four years away, but we take a look at the teams with the firmest foundations who should be primed to contendJeff RueterWed 22 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 09.27 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe 2026World Cupprovided many an esteemed veteran with what could be their international swansongs, with golden greats Lionel Messi, Cristiano Ronaldo, Luka Modric, Neymar, James Rodríguez and Guillermo Ochoa all making teary exits as their tournament ended. As we turn ahead to the 2030 World Cup, any player 32 or older will be sweating their selection status and changing their daily routines to stay sharp against inevitable aging.For these power rankings, we’re overvaluing sure things. Teams with plenty of leading players comfortably in their 20s can build for this impending four-year cycle with greater confidence than a team replacing an ageless talisman. Consider these the 10 teams with the firmest foundations and viable reasons to hope to reach a quarter-final in four years’ time.Mentioned players’ ages on 1 June 2030 listed (in parentheses).The quirky side of World Cup 2026 – in picturesRead more1) SpainThe first defending World Cup champion to host the ensuing installment, Spain will enter 2030 as favorites to run it back. With Luis de la Fuente’s masterful sense of his team’s strengths and universal commitment from his players, this group could develop into a juggernaut.Unai Simón(32),Joan García(29) andDavid Raya(34) could repeat as the goalkeeping trio.Marc Pubill(26),Eric García(29),Pedro Porro(30) andPau Cubarsí(23) are a solid defensive bedrock.Dani Olmo(32),Gavi(25) andPedri(27) should return in midfield, whileRodri(33) stands a good chance to start at the midfield’s base.Nico Williams(27) andLamine Yamal(22) will hope to enjoy better fitness but demonstrated their importance in the final stages, andFerran Torres(30) may be a threat from the bench again.And, of course, there’s great talent beyond the title-winning 26.Dean Huijsen(25) andCristhian Mosquera(25) are options to replace Aymeric Laporte (36), whileFermin López(27),Pablo Barrios(26),Javier Guerra(27),Marc Bernal(23) andJesús Rodríguez(24) have featured under de la Fuente. IfSamu Aghehowa(26) orGonzalo García(26) can become a viable option at striker, Spain could wring even more goals from their possessive machine – a scary thought, indeed.2) FranceA fresh touchline voice arrives just in time, with Zinedine Zidane replacing the great Didier Deschamps after decreasing returns across his three World Cups. What lessons can Zizou glean from their stunning semi-final crumble against Spain?Mike Maignan(34) should have another cycle in his capable gloves, shielded byDayot Upamecano(31),Ibrahima Konaté(31) andWilliam Saliba(29). The midfield is in good stead withManu Koné(29) andAurélien Tchouaméni(30), as well as squad snubsEduardo Camavinga(27) andKhéphren Thuram(29).Kylian Mbappé(31) will be raring to extend his goalscoring record withMichael Olise(28) andDésiré Doué(24) in support. Talent wasn’t the issue so much as overcoming Spain’s structure, and for all his success with Real Madrid, Zidane impressed more with his squad management than his tactical chops.3) EnglandThe Three Lions showed impressive resolve, outlasting Mexico in an instant classic and coming back against Norway to return to the semi-final. Their timid failures to close out a 2-0 lead over Argentina will haunt many, but the reality is that England are poised for a very strong response at Euro 2028 and the next World Cup – with one glaring asterisk.Marc Guéhi(29) looked a leading center-back for much of the tournament, withDjed Spence(29) having finished the World Cup in top form.Declan Rice(31),Elliot Anderson(27),Jude Bellingham(26) andKobbie Mainoo(25) offer plenty of midfield combinations, andBukayo Saka(28) andAnthony Gordon(29) fared well on the wings. The biggest question is whetherHarry Kanecan be a 90-minute option at age 36, as England hasn’t yet found an obvious successor to their leading goalscorer. Goalkeeper should also be an open battle, but players who missed Thomas Tuchel’s squad such asLewis Hall(25),Cole Palmer(28),Adam Wharton(26),Alex Scott(26),Phil Foden(30),Rio Ngumoha(21) andAyden Heaven(23) reiterate the depth of the pool.View image in fullscreenDjed Spence had a good tournament for England and will be 29 at the time of the next World Cup.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty Images4) MoroccoThe groundwork is excellent for another of 2030’s primary co-hosts. Back-to-back World Cup quarter-final runs, with one berth in the semis. Legal custody of the Afcon 2025 title. A freshly installed coach who got more verve from his side without sacrificing stability. Can Morocco take things a step or two further in 2030?Achraf Hakimi(31),Chadi Riad(26),Anass Salah-Eddine(28) andNoussair Mazraoui(32) project to stick around in defense.Ayyoub Bouaddi(22) should be even better after his emergent World Cup, along with his midfield partnerAzzedine Ounahi(30).Ismael Saibari(29) was a safe bet to score before his injury, whileBilal El Khannouss(26),Gessime Yassine(24) andBrahim Díaz(30) also challenge any backline.Morocco won the most recent U-20 World Cup, led by forwardsOthmane Maamma(24) andYassir Zabiri(25) as well as goalkeeperIbrahim Gomis(25). If goalkeeping heroYassine Bounou(39) can somehow stay in his usual blistering form, they’ll be a tough draw for anybody.5) ArgentinaArgentina’s run to the final reiterated how uniquely vital Messi was to his team, both as the determinant of their attack and the heart every teammate fought hard to protect. He is, by every definition of the word, irreplaceable.Then again, Argentina were not just a one-man band.Julián Álvarez(30) has grownincreasingly comfortable as a leading man, and should again be backed up by lethalLautaro Martínez(32).Enzo Fernández(33) has a lot of apologies to give after his send-off, but should anchor midfield this cycle.Nahuel Molina(32),Cristian Romero(32) andLisandro Martínez(32) will be veteran defenders, whileEmiliano Martínez(37) should have plenty left to offer. They also were runners-up at the last U-20 World Cup, with standouts including midfielderMilton Delgado(24), left-backJulio Soler(25) and goalkeeperSantino Barbi(24).skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Twice weeklySign up toSoccer Desk: World Cup EditionJonathan Wilson and Guardian journalists bring expert analysis and reporting on the biggest stories in soccer.Preview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionNeuer to Neymar: 11 World Cup players who retired from international footballRead more6) PortugalPortugal were left to wonder what could have been with a more natural and mobile line-leader atop an otherwise terrific team. Ronaldo hasadmitted he won’t play in the 2030 tournamentat age 45, and the biggest question will become whether Roberto Martínez squandered this generation’s best chance to win a first World Cup.Diogo Costa(30) is among the world’s best goalkeepers, withGonçalo Inácio(28) andNuno Mendes(27) forming a strong defensive base.António Silva(26) will hope to get his promising career back on track, seemingly with Bournemouth, whileJoão Neves(25) andVitinha(30) would start in nearly any nation’s midfield.Gonçalo Ramos(28),João Félix(30) andFrancisco Conceição(27) return in attack, too. But with the list of aging players including the greatBruno Fernandes(35),Bernardo Silva(35) andRúben Dias(33), it’s hard not to feel the best opportunity has been missed.7) NorwayFew will underestimate Norway after their deserved run to the quarter-final, with a culture in and around the team that became the envy of the summer. Many of their men should be back in four years, including themain-est manof all.Few people seemed to have enjoyed this World Cup as much asErling Haaland(29), who should still be devastating defenses when the next one comes around.Andreas Schjelderup(26),Antonio Nusa(25) andOscar Bobb(26) are credible running mates in the final third, whileMartin Ødegaard(31) will hope to shirk his injury bugs to pull the strings once again. There will need to be ample rotation in the defensive half and in goal, but as long as Haaland is leading the line, Norway will stand a chance.View image in fullscreenThe future is bright for Amad Diallo and his Côte d’Ivoire teammates.Photograph: Mike Segar/Reuters8) Côte d’IvoireThe Elephants impressed in a tricky Group E, beating Ecuador and Curaçao before falling to Norway in the last-32. If Emerse Faé is back to lead this team, he’ll enjoy an immense level of talent to work with as (part of) the tournament returns to Africa.Yahia Fofana(29) is steady in goal, his job made easier by the collective defending ofOusmane Diomande(26),Odilon Kossounou(29) andWilfried Singo(29). Their forward options pose champagne selection issues:Amad Diallo(27),Oumar Diakité(26),Elye Wahi(27),Yan Diomandé(23),Simon Adingra(28) andAnge-Yoan Bonny(26). Midfield appears to be their only thin area especially ifFranck Kessié(33) ages out of starting status. But on paper, and on the back of a first World Cup knockout match and the Afcon 2023 title, they’re a team to watch.9) GermanyWhile the legion of club managers making their World Cup debuts in 2026 saw mixed returns, Jürgen Klopp has four years to build his ideal culture and refine a new system forDie Mannschaft. That seems a dream scenario after a trio of disappointing tournaments on the back of 2014’s triumph.The bigger question is whether Germany have the players. An injury toLennart Karl(22) on the eve of the World Cup put a damper on preparations, but the Bayern playmaker would be a welcome addition.Kai Havertz(30) should pop up with timely goals, whileFlorian Wirtz(27) andJamal Musiala(27) will hope to arrive in better form and fitness.Nathaniel Brown(26) looks to be an ideal wing-back for the next few cycles, andJoshua Kimmich(35) has already dismissed concerns that he may retire before Klopp arrives. That’s a relief given how much turnover will be necessary from this summer’s squad in the defensive half.10) United StatesThe Balogun Affair stripped the sheen off of what had been the US’s most promising men’s World Cup showing to date. Mauricio Pochettino’s status for the next cycle is uncertain. Still, the Americans’ run to the last 16 saw many players of a similar age group step up and project to be mainstays.Folarin Balogun(28) should be an even more refined forward to follow his three-goal showing this summer, whileMalik Tillman(28) offers strong dead-ball service and tidy midfield play.Chris Richards(30),Tyler Adams(31),Weston McKennie(31),Christian Pulisic(31) andAlex Freeman(25) will probably continue to be foundational pieces, whileSebastian Berhalter(29),Gio Reyna(27) andRicardo Pepi(27) should stick around after capable World Cup showings. A 12th-place finish still indicates a last-16 exit that has become the frustrating norm for the US, but perhaps the lessons of a high-pressure hosting gigwill really pay off in four years.Explore more on these topicsWorld CupWorld Cup 2026featuresShareReuse this content

title: Pass the pit diapers! Why are fans wetting themselves to stand front row at concerts? | Music | The Guardian
description: Olivia Rodrigo, Noah Kahan and Morgan Wallen have all dealt with audience effluence in recent weeks – but that’s only one part of a fearsome subculture that has built up around fandom, endurance and ‘getting barrier’
Author: Kate Solomon
Headline: Olivia Rodrigo, Noah Kahan and Morgan Wallen have all dealt with audience effluence in recent weeks – but that’s only one part of a fearsome subculture that has built up around fandom, endurance and ‘getting barrier’
Content: ‘An experience I have smelled’ … Olivia Rodrigo performing at Roskilde festival in 2025.Photograph: Colin Darbyshire/Alamy Stock Photo/Alamy Live News.View image in fullscreen‘An experience I have smelled’ … Olivia Rodrigo performing at Roskilde festival in 2025.Photograph: Colin Darbyshire/Alamy Stock Photo/Alamy Live News.MusicPass the pit diapers! Why are fans wetting themselves to stand front row at concerts?Olivia Rodrigo, Noah Kahan and Morgan Wallen have all dealt with audience effluence in recent weeks – but that’s only one part of a fearsome subculture that has built up around fandom, endurance and ‘getting barrier’Kate SolomonWed 22 Jul 2026 05.39 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 06.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe front row of a gig is a sacred space. When you love an artist, you want to be as close to them as possible, without a view obscured by someone else’s hairdo. You want the possibility that the artist will pluck you from the crowd, form a deep and enduring connection and end up marrying you. Or at least notice you.Last month,Olivia Rodrigorevealed how acutely she had noticed some of the fans lining the barriers at her shows. “People wear diapers so that they can be front row,” she said, adding, to everyone’s absolute horror: “That’s been an experience as a performer that I have smelled.”This is no aberration, and while genuine medical needs should be taken into account, situations down the front have clearly got out of control. Also last month, video of a very stoic stadium worker cleaning up human excrement at aNoah Kahangig went viral and prompted Kahan himself to post on X: “If you have to poop at a show please dear god just go to the bathroom lmao.” This week, a fan of US country star Morgan Wallen complained that he, his brother and his niece were urinated on by another concertgoer, and police are investigating. Must we be subjected to headlines such as “Someone Also Shit Their Pants at a 5 Seconds of Summer Concert”? And in 2024, someone urinated on the floor of the standing section of a Sabrina Carpenter concert, causing a run on afairly horrifying item called a “pit diaper”, whose mere existence speaks to a wider problem.View image in fullscreenMorgan Wallen on stage.Photograph: Jason Kempin/Getty Images for ABASince the pandemic, when music lovers missed out on two years of live shows and young fans were forced to wait for their first gig experiences, those hard-won spots on the front row have become more and more coveted. The cost of VIP pit tickets has rocketed, stan culture has grown ever more feral, and the social media value of scoring clear gig footage is at a premium, partly because surprise celeb cameos – as in Rosalía’s confession booth, Charli xcx’sApple danceor Sabrina Carpenter’s on-stage “arrests” – are now built into many pop shows. So as well as the adult nappies, today’s barrier culture is a world of strict fan-run queue conventions, cross-venue sprints and specialist hydration systems.In an effort to find someone who would admit to even considering wearing a nappy, I popped down to Wembley Stadium on night 11 of Harry Styles’ recent 12-nightTogether Togetherresidency. The dazzlingly sequinned girls I spoke to weathering the heatwave to queue for the final section of floor standing were mostly horrified by the question. “I think that’s a bit ridiculous,” said Eloise, 23, having once queued for 10 hours to get the barrier at Harry’s last run of Wembley shows. What will you do if you need the toilet? I asked Shannon, who was hoping to leg it to the barrier when doors opened: “Hope for the best!”View image in fullscreen‘It’s a bit ridiculous’ … Eloise.Photograph: Hannah Cauhepe/The GuardianNineteen-year-old Keeley, who started queuing at 11am, said that she, “personally”, wouldn’t wear a nappy. “That’s not for me. Especially in this heat. It’d feel really unnatural to wee just in front of everyone.”But if you’re paying hundreds of pounds for a show and are queuing for hours to be mere metres from Rodrigo or Styles, it does make a warped kind of sense that you might take the nappy route rather than risk missing a song or losing your spot at the front. On TikTok, plenty of Swifties claimed to have worn one to the Eras tour (and with shows of more than three hours, you’d assume evenTaylor Swifttook a loo break). But it’s hard to track down anyone who will admit to a Guardian journalist that they wore a pit diaper in order to keep their coveted front-row view.When Katie, a seasoned Swiftie, planned her route to the Eras tour barrier, she forwent pissing herself in a crowd. Instead, she developed a hydration strategy that kept her show-ready but dry as a bone. “I drank loads of water the day before and then as soon as I got up at 4am to queue for the barricade, I had like two pints. I kept drinking until around midday then had a hard cutoff.” Energy gels, glucose tablets and mid-show waters handed by security over the barricade kept her from passing out – a sure-fire way to lose your spot. “No point in getting barricade if you faint during the first era!” she added brightly.View image in fullscreenTaylor Swift performing in Amsterdam – hopefully not singling out a nappy-wearing fan.Photograph: Hollandse Hoogte/REX/ShutterstockThere is a long tradition of people (mostly cis men, let’s be real) pissing into pint-glasses in crowds – and in one particularly horrifying Primavera crowd, directly on to my friend’s leg. Concerts by US psychedelic rockers Phish have becomenotoriousfor fans supposedly pooping with gay abandon since aninfamous incidentin 2015. You can see how it might happen in a boozed-up festival crowd or somewhere drugs are being liberally consumed. And perhaps the Phish experience is so transcendent it elevates the listener outside the workings of their gastrointestinal tract. More desperately, fans could argue they are counterculturally rejecting toileting norms, though this behaviour is actually relatively mainstream: urination was apparently rife as far back as concerts by Frank Sinatra and the Beatles.If you are minded to just let go, the potential for it to backfire is huge. “I tried this with a sanitary towel and it didn’t work,” confesses one gig-goer, insisting on anonymity for reasons that will become obvious. Let’s call her Fiona. She and a friend had travelled to see Arctic Monkeys in Finsbury Park in 2014. Fiona was a spritely 15-year-old who had got her hands on a bottle of rosé and reached the barrier right in front of where Alex Turner and co were soon to be asking Do I Wanna Know? The answer here is an emphatic: no you don’t.“I was determined to stay there,” she says, bodily functions be damned. “I had some sanitary towels in my bag so I thought I could discreetly wee into one. But it just overfilled because, unsurprisingly, pads can’t hold a rosé-induced wee’s worth of liquid.” With huge wet patches all down her black skinny jeans, she had to shimmy her piss-soaked way out of the crowd to find a portable toilet to deal with everything. “The worst thing was, when I tried to squeeze back through to my place, no one believed that my friend was waiting for me at the barrier.” It’s hard to believe that really was the worst thing about the situation.Toilet troubles are one thing; but just “getting barrier” in the first place is a whole other matter. The Harry Styles fans queuing in the heatwave had numbers written in Sharpie on the backs of their hands as part of a fan-run system that is supposed to ensure that whoever queues the longest gets the best spots. It goes pretty far back down the queue, at least to the hundreds. The idea is that no one can push in – or at least that it’s obvious if they do – though no one was clear exactly what anyone would or could do about that.View image in fullscreenFans show their queue numbers ahead of Harry Styles’ concert.Photograph: Hannah Cauhepe/The GuardianThere are some obvious flaws in this system. I could have written a number on the back of my hand and strolled to the front, and who could deny me? It’s an honour code that some people just don’t abide by, even in England, the spiritual home of queuing. Videos of the walk into the stadiums for Styles’ shows often become popular on socials: the ones from the night I investigated had just as much panicked jostling and yelling as there would have been without the numbers.Fans of all stripes have always staked out venues to sprint in and nab a space at the front – the forthcoming documentary Metallica Saved My Life shows the every-man-for-himself combo tactic of camping out then legging it in – but the newly regimented, numbered, self-policing approach seems to have stemmed from teenage girls. Anecdotal evidence suggests these “roll calls”, as fans call them, started proliferating in the mid-2010s. “Fans policing the order in which they’re in the queue to get into the venue first has always been a thing,” says Hannah Ewens, author ofFangirls, in which she documents how the teen fangirl queue community has its own microcommunities, complete with influencers. The spirit of the community, infused with a deep love for the artist plus the need for a decent view – teenage girls tend to be among the smallest in any given gig crowd – is all part of the barrier-questing experience.View image in fullscreenStyles fans ready themselves for the dash to the front.Photograph: Hannah Cauhepe/The GuardianBut while it’s always been about getting to the front, getting a spot on the front row has also become a lot more about clout. “TikTok has made it so much more rewarding,” Ewens adds. “It’s ramped up a sense of competition.” Getting to the barrier could be the difference between your videos of the show going viral or disappearing into the digital ether, and that gives the numbering systems even more perceived weight. “Fandom is about being in a community and being part of something, but it’s also very hierarchical,” Ewens says. “Queuing scratches both those itches for fans.”Some artists have made it clear that fan-run queue systems will not be entertained. For Bring Me the Horizon’s Manchester gig earlier this month – which had no barrier at all, just free-flowing rockers right up to the stage – the FAQs included a note: “Queueing will begin from 10am on show day … Fan numbering will not be acknowledged. Order of first entry will be selected at random.” The band and promoter would not comment on the decision, and whether or not it had any effect on the toileting habits of the crowd is unclear.Can we always be this close? What the hunger for intimacy with Taylor Swift says about fandom and girlhoodRead moreFandoms are starting to push back against fan-made queuing systems, too. Search Reddit or TikTok for this subject and you will find just as many people rejecting it as participating.Louis Tomlinsonbrought in randomised wristbands to take away the incentive to queue for hours for his shows earlier this year. Whatever time fans checked in, they were assigned a number to determine the order they could enter the pit. Venues including Wembley Stadium have also banned camping, making the process fairer for people who can’t give up several days to the cause.But despite the horrors of dirty nappies and enforced fan regimes, the barrier is generally a place of bonhomie. Friendships are made, core memories created. Whenever a member of BTS chucks a worn T-shirt to a specific fan at the barrier, no one around them tries to grab it (much as you know they desperately want to). If fans need help or water, those around them will signal to security to fetch it. And some of the photos and footage coming from front-row fans at this summer’s stadium shows are of professional-level quality. Whatever they did to capture them is between them, God and the nostrils of their favourite pop star. Even Fiona has no regrets: “It was worth it,” she says, 10 years removed from the wet jeans. “And I’m glad I have the story too.”Explore more on these topicsMusicPop and rockOlivia RodrigoNoah KahanTaylor SwiftBring Me the HorizonLouis TomlinsonfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: From posh egg cups to secondhand lamps: 22 cheap ways to make your home look more expensive | Interiors | The Guardian
description: Good taste doesn’t have to cost a fortune. Whether it’s designer-looking cutlery or affordable art, these clever buys start from £2 – but punch well above their price tag
Author: Ellie Violet Bramley
Headline: Good taste doesn’t have to cost a fortune. Whether it’s designer-looking cutlery or affordable art, these clever buys start from £2 – but punch well above their price tag
Content: View image in fullscreenThe FilterInteriorsFrom posh egg cups to secondhand lamps: 22 cheap ways to make your home look more expensiveGood taste doesn’t have to cost a fortune. Whether it’s designer-looking cutlery or affordable art, these clever buys start from £2 – but punch well above their price tagDon’t get the Filter delivered to your inbox? Sign up hereEllie Violet BramleyWed 22 Jul 2026 10.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.You don’t need the budget of a boutique hotel to make your home look a bit more pulled together. It’s all down to knowing where to look, picking a few well-made objects, choosing good-quality fabrics and materials, and spotting timeless classics (or designs inspired by them) that won’t date too quickly.The Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.Here are some tricks, hidden gems and affordable-but-stylish finds, from egg cups to art, that will elevate your space – and prove good taste and quality needn’t cost the earth.How to make your home look more expensiveThe cover-upView image in fullscreenEasy chair cover£60 at BemzI recently bought an inexpensive Ikea armchair that looked a bit Identikit. So I added a made-to-order cover. This swirly pattern from Bemz spoke to me – and I am now the proud owner of an armchair that looks more Architectural Digest than aisle 78.Light workView image in fullscreenHay Common Oblong rice paper shade£34 at Holloways of Ludlow£45 at EndAkarihas been following traditional methods since the 1950s to make beautiful rice paper lamps that are rightfully a modern classic. If you can’t afford one, tasteful Danish design brand Hay has some beautiful options, adding serenity to even the most hectic home for a sixth of the price.In bloomView image in fullscreenGeraniumsFrom £14.99 at Thompson & Morgan£24.99 at B&QGeraniumsand pelargoniums exude the sort of taste vibes you’d find inCharleston House. Plus, they’re hardy, perennial, easy to grow (I managed to keep them alive and flowering for an unusually long time), and they look great in any old terracotta plant pot. Just make sure to pop them on a sunny windowsill –according to the RHSthey need “full light but shade from scorching midday sun”.Bowl-ed overView image in fullscreenBlue spot bowl£35 at Baltic TraderChoose your hero pieces wisely – by which I mean that the things that will be out on display should look nice to you. This 25cm-diameter fruit bowl, from a Polish ceramics shop in Lewes, features an elegant polka-dot design that won’t age – and there are plenty of lovely dishes and custard jugs where that came from.Picture thisView image in fullscreenSteve Camps fine art print£45 at Not Another BillIf you’re a fan of the bluey-grey maritime-themed works of Cornish painter Alfred Wallis but can’t afford their £30,000+ price tag, perhaps you could stretch to £46 + postage? Steve Camps, a retired builder from Cornwall, is an artist who sells high-quality prints of his nautical-themed art on Not Another Bill. Your home will look likeKettle’s Yardin no time.Eggs-actly rightView image in fullscreenStainless steel egg cup£2 at Nordic MuseThese stainless steel egg cups might seem like a piffling little thing, but anything that adds design wattage to a basic is a win. These wouldn’t look out of place atService Projects, the home of chic tableware and (for me) many lost hours.Frame gameView image in fullscreenMarbled paper£32.75 at Cloth HouseThere’s something unnerving about seeing the same affordable art you have on your walls in the backgrounds of numerous other people’s interiors on Instagram. This beautifully marbled handmade paper is an alternative approach – just add a frame, and you have a piece of abstract art that probably isn’t also hanging next door.Rock solidView image in fullscreenReclaimedJaisalmersandstone bowls, each£20 at English SalvageI hope it doesn’t detract from the craftsmanship of these handmade sandstone bowls to suggest they’d look lovely filled with easy peelers. No two are the same. Available via a reclamation yard based in Herefordshire, but also available online.Soft touchView image in fullscreenStripe hand towel£5.95 at John LewisThe impeccably tasteful Danish companyTeklamakes undeniably good-quality striped towels in beautiful colours. But they’re not cheap. So while you’re waiting for your Isa to mature, these designs from John Lewis, especially the dove grey stripe, should elevate your bathroom.Candle in the wind-owView image in fullscreenTwisted candles£4.68 at Søstrene GreneWax candles in sweet-shop pastels and quirky patterns have become a major (and often pricey) trend in recent years, but these more muted moss-green twirly ones from Danish chain Søstrene Grene are a more timeless way to add class to your dinner table.Wrap starView image in fullscreen£29 at Yorkshire BlanketsMade in Yorkshire and hand-cut and finished, this cosy blanket wouldn’t look out of place in even the fanciest homes of Harrogate. The fact it’s wool-blend, and not pure wool, keeps the price down, but the look of it transcends its price tag.Set pieceView image in fullscreenBlue leaf dinner set, 12 piecesskip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThe FilterGet the best shopping advice from the Filter team straight to your inbox. The Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Preview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion£45 at ArgosIf you’re after a dinner set that channels Finland’s heritage ceramics company,Arabia, then look no further. This Habitat design is straight out of Tove Jansson’s summer house, and the whole thing costs far less than a one-way ticket to Helsinki.Shelf lifeView image in fullscreenThree-tier coffee shelvesFrom £40 at EtsyI’m seeing a lot of love online for coffee stations – yet another way to glorify your morning cup of joe. But if worktop space is limited, this set of shelves is a great place to house your mugs, beans and other coffee paraphernalia. It’s made in the UK, from reclaimed wood.Table mannersView image in fullscreenStainless steel cutlery set with resin handles£17.99 at MangoMade from resin and stainless steel, this cutlery set – moreDavid Mellorthan Mango – will instantly zhoosh up your table. I love the emerald green and mustard yellow options.Bright ideaView image in fullscreenVintage alabaster lamp£26.99 at eBayIf an alabaster sculpture doesn’t fit your price tag – or the look of your one-bed – but you like the vibe of the soft, cool stone, secondhand sites offer an Uffizi’s worth of alabaster lamps. We found this one on eBay,and this one on Vinterior.Just search “mid century alabaster lamp” and take your pick.Light reliefView image in fullscreenWall-mounted candle sconce£37 at Nordic NestWall-mounting a candle sconce instantly brings calm to a space (which is a sentence I never thought I’d type). The internet seems to be awash with them right now – many in beautifully handmade ceramics, which cost hundreds of pounds. This recycled one from Ferm Living isn’t cheap for something entirely decorative, but it will lift a quiet corner of a flat.French fancyView image in fullscreenLe Provendi rotating soap holder and soap£25.50 at Le Provendi£25.50 at AmazonI love the idea of a wall-mounted soap, known in France as theporte-savon écolier. The classic design is the work of French company Laboratoire Provendi, which has been making soap since the 1950s, and it keeps soap clean, dry and always on hand. This one, the original French school soap holder, will make your house feel like it’s passed its baccalaureate.Light fantasticView image in fullscreenSilver candlestick holder£9 at IkeaThere’s something sculptural and Scandinavian about this curved candlestick holder, and not just because it’s from Ikea. Its design belies its mass-manufactured origins and with the right candle (see above) could bring a little Nordic minimalism to your home.Sew simpleView image in fullscreenStriped Irishlinen fabric for napkins£13.50 per metre at Dalston Mill FabricsDo you find yourself lusting after the kind of napkins available at fancy shops for £16, but can’t bring yourself to spend that much on something you’re just going to wipe your hands on? Consider buying linen fabric and either knocking some out yourself (this tutorial might help), or seeing if an affordable, local seamster would. It’s an easy enough job, and your dinner table and bank balance will thank you for it.Take a seatView image in fullscreenRed dining chairs, pair£165 at ArgosThese scarlet dining chairs look a little Josef Frank, but at a fraction of the price. Pair with whatever other chairs you have: there’s nothing wrong with mismatched.Shade betterView image in fullscreenHand-painted lampshadeFrom £99 at Cressida BellNearly £100 is not nothing for a lampshade, but I’ve been ogling these handmade lampshades by artist Cressida Bell ever since I first set eyes on them. This vivid green oak leaf print with gold highlights has Bloomsbury set written all over it – and there aretropical,topiary-inspiredandgeometric printsavailable, too.On the tableView image in fullscreenCheckered washed cotton tableclothFrom £29.99 at La RedouteMade from 100% cotton, this tablecloth is neutral enough to provide a versatile backdrop for brighter colours via dishes and glasses. Alone, it will also add a touch of Toast-like charm to your kitchen table.View image in fullscreenDon’t buy anything until you’ve read the Filter, the Guardian’s home for truly independent product reviews and recommendations. Whether you’re after rigorous tests or shopping inspiration, affordable gifts or advice on how to make your belongings last longer, we’re here to help you make smarter, more sustainable choices.The Filter is packed with trustworthy buying advice on everything from coffee machines to hiking boots, mascara to secateurs. So visit us today and start buying better and smarter, and wasting less.Explore the FilterExplore more on these topicsInteriorsThe FilterHomesHome improvementsMenWomenfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Burnham stirs up political row with review of release plans for violent criminals | Prisons and probation | The Guardian
description: PM says he wants to ensure ‘everything has been looked at’ amid outrage at policy from family of PC Andrew Harper
Author: Rajeev Syal
Headline: PM says he wants to ensure ‘everything has been looked at’ amid outrage at policy from family of PC Andrew Harper
Content: Andrew Harper and his wife, Lissie, who has raised concerns about the potential early release of his killers.Photograph: Courtesy of Lissie HarperView image in fullscreenAndrew Harper and his wife, Lissie, who has raised concerns about the potential early release of his killers.Photograph: Courtesy of Lissie HarperPrisons and probationBurnham stirs up political row with review of release plans for violent criminalsPM says he wants to ensure ‘everything has been looked at’ amid outrage at policy from family of PC Andrew HarperRajeev SyalandAlexandra ToppingWed 22 Jul 2026 15.02 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 07.49 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAndy Burnham is immersed in a deepening political row after ordering a review of plans for the early release of thousands of killers, rapists and sex offenders from prison in September.After the family of PC Andrew Harper expressed outrage that his killers may be eligible for release, the prime minister responded by pledging to make sure that “everything has been looked at again” before a fresh tranche of about 6,000 offenders were freed on tags.But soon after Burnham’s comments, prison reform groups, government officials and politicians warned that abandoning measures to ease jail overcrowding could lead to the criminal justice system “collapsing under its own weight”.Burnham has intervened quickly on policy issues just a few days into his premiership, including by indicating a possibleincrease in the personal allowanceon income tax, which has been frozen since 2022.Attempting to clear up confusion, he said there was no “commitment” to increase the allowance from £12,570 but the move would be considered at the budget, expected in the autumn.“I want to answer questions honestly in this job, as I said before. I think that’s all about trust in politics and doing politics differently, and I’m determined to keep working in that way,” he said.New sentencing laws were passed under Keir Starmer’s government to ease a looming prison overcrowding crisis that ministers said couldseize up the criminal justice systemin November.View image in fullscreenJessie Cole (left) and Albert Bowers were convicted of manslaughter in 2020 over the death of PC Andrew Harper.Photograph: Thames Valley Police/PAUnlike previous emergency measures under which tens of thousands of prisoners were released in the early months of Starmer’s government, the Sentencing Act includes no exemptions for prisoners convicted of serious crimes, domestic abuse or terrorism.PC Harper was 28 when he sustained fatal injuries while responding to a quad bike theft in Sulhamstead, Berkshire, in August 2019. Jessie Cole and Albert Bowers, who were passengers in the vehicle and both 17 at the time, were sentenced to 13 years in custody for manslaughter but may be eligible for early release under the new Sentencing Act.His widow, Lissie Harper, successfully campaigned for legal changes, called Harper’s Law, to bring in harsher sentences for the manslaughter of emergency workers on duty.In a statement, she said: “When I fought for Harper’s Law, I was supported not only by the Conservative government of the day but by the Labour party too. They pledged to support our cause and agreed that those who kill emergency workers should face tougher sentences because some crimes are so grave they demand meaningful punishment.“That cross-party support helped make Harper’s Law a reality. Today, it feels forgotten.”Asked to respond to the Harper family’s comments, Burnham said he would examine the plans with the justice secretary, Alex Norris. “I will review the whole of this policy. We did not invest in the prison estate when we should as a country, going back a decade or so ago, when it was clear that there was going to be pressure. That has created an unacceptable situation now.“But I want to assure myself that everything has been looked at before this policy proceeds, and I can give that assurance that every single part of this I will look into with the justice secretary. I can’t say that I can change the policy completely, but I will look at it in detail before we go further forward,” he said.Whitehall insiders have pointed out that any change in the early release scheme will contradict claims from the previous justice secretary,David Lammy, who told the Guardianthis month that without it there could be “no capacity” across prisons in England and Wales in November.One government official said: “There is very little wriggle room because it takes seven years to build a prison. Andy will need to get into the issues, but there are no easy choices because we will hit prison capacity by Christmas if he doesn’t do it.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionMahmood and Streeting accused of making ‘cheap jokes’ about prisoner early release schemeRead moreAndrea Coomber, the chief executive of the Howard League for Penal Reform, urged caution. “This is a system that is on the verge of collapsing under its own weight. When originally introducing these proposals, Shabana Mahmood described the alternative as being the ‘total breakdown of law and order’.“We need this government to finally grapple with the fact that, for 20 years, politicians have allowed sentence inflation to run rampant. With longer and longer sentences, we are sleepwalking our way into US-style mass incarceration.”Enver Solomon, the chief executive of the social justice charity Nacro, said prisons must not be allowed to fill up again. “It should not be forgotten that the reason the Labour government put in place an early release scheme was to ensure there isn’t yet another prisons capacity crisis,” he said.The former Conservative prisons minister Rory Stewart said: “Early release is not an easy choice – but it is the least bad option if we want to genuinely try to work intensively with prisoners and reduce reoffending.”Under the act, some serious offenders will be eligible for release halfway through their sentences rather than two-thirds of the way through. Those convicted of less serious crimes can be freed after serving a third of their sentence instead of 40%. Prisoners locked up for some violent or sexual offences are eligible for release halfway through their sentences rather than two-thirds.The bill was drawn up and introduced to parliament by Shabana Mahmood, the home secretary. TheConservative shadow home secretary,Chris Philp, wrote on X that the “early release scheme allows rapists and serious criminals out of prison early and it must be urgently cancelled”.He said releasing the jailed car passengers “is an insult to Andrew Harper’s memory and to police officers who put themselves at risk every day to protect us”.Burnham’s pledge to review the policy was welcomed by groups working with victims. The former safeguarding minister Jess Phillips told the Guardian it was important that “proper systems” were put in place to assess risk and involve victims. “There is a chance to radically change how victims are considered in the system and to improve victims of violence against women and girls’ end-to-end experience of the justice system,” she said.Ciara Bergman, the chief executive of Rape Crisis England and Wales, said: “Obviously there are significant challenges facing our government, including how overcrowding and under-resourcing is going to be managed, but the solutions to these challenges cannot come at survivors’ expense.”Katie Kempen, the chief executive at Victim Support, said the review was encouraging. “This review must urgently fix the fact that most victims currently have no way of knowing whether or not an offender could be released early. It is unacceptable that so many victims are being kept in the dark about this – that must change.”Explore more on these topicsPrisons and probationAndy BurnhamUK criminal justiceChris PhilpDavid LammynewsShareReuse this content

title: Lobbying giant filmed offering reporter payment for flattering client coverage in national press | Lobbying | The Guardian
description: Exclusive: Guardian investigation of CT Group uncovers practice that appears to seek hidden influence over public opinion
Author: Peter Apps
Headline: Exclusive: Guardian investigation of CT Group uncovers practice that appears to seek hidden influence over public opinion
Content: 5:46Lobbyists meet undercover journalist to commission newspaper piece – videoLobbyingLobbying giant filmed offering reporter payment for flattering client coverage in national pressExclusive: Guardian investigation of CT Group uncovers practice that appears to seek hidden influence over public opinionPeter AppsandRobert BoothWed 22 Jul 2026 11.42 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 18.52 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA political lobbying firm founded by the former electoral strategist forDavid Cameronand Boris Johnson secretly pays journalists to write pieces for British newspapers that are favourable to its clients, a Guardian investigation can reveal.In a practice that appears to create a backdoor for private companies to influence public opinion, lobbyists for CT Group – co-founded by the campaign strategistLynton Crosby– told an undercover reporter they had paid journalists to place apparently independent stories in newspapers.They offered hundreds of pounds in cash incentives to do so for a property industry client and said the deal would grant the lobbying firm increased editorial control.The Guardian began investigating the arrangements after Gavin Stollar, the head of CT’s real estate division, approached a freelance journalist and offered to pay him to pitch a piece to a British national newspaper about problems caused for the housebuilding industry by the Building Safety Regulator, set up in the wake of the Grenfell Tower fire thatkilled 72 people.Stollar hoped the journalist would write about the human consequences of the UK’s housing shortage and suggest the regulator was making the situation worse. Stollar, a Liberal Democrat activist who was awarded an OBE in 2024, said CT Group would help with research and provide human case studies of people affected by the housing shortage.View image in fullscreenBoris Johnson and Lynton Crosby in 2014.Photograph: Alan Davidson/Rex/ShutterstockStollar led the journalist to understand that while they would be paid by the newspaper for apparently independent reporting, they would also be paid by CT Group, which has property industry clients.Other CT Group staffers then proposed the freelance journalist could be paid to write stories for newspapers as part of its work for a leading property guardianship company, Live-in Guardians. One said: “You can do it either way. We don’t have to pay you, and you just go off and do how you normally do. Or, we can pay you, but we get more control over it.” They then offered him “£100 for every story that is successfully published (national or trade)”.In meetings with the reporter, the CT Group employees proposed he chair a roundtable for the company for £500 and pitch pieces to national and trade papers that would support the guardianship sector – which sometimes has a reputation for low quality housing with few protections for residents. One suggestion was to write “a call to action from the industry for [Andy] Burnham to be smart about this”.They said they did not want pieces to appear like advertorials – which are transparently signposted paid-for editorial pieces – “but clearly we’d like you to keep our client’s messaging and overall objective in mind”.View image in fullscreenA lobbyist said CT Group had worked with a journalist ‘where I paid her to do a certain piece, a certain national piece’.Photograph: The GuardianIn a statement, CT Group said the events had been “deliberately mischaracterised”. It said it did not engage the undercover journalist to do anything and did not pay him anything.CT Group is a £40m-a-year turnover lobbying company whose recent clients include Airbus Defence and Space, the tobacco company Philip Morris and the owner of the football club Manchester City. It has a global workforce of 150 staff and describes having achieved results for clients “on corporate issues, general elections, reputational transformation and legislative reform”.It is part of an industry of public affairs and political consulting firms facing growing scrutiny after Peter Mandelson’s dual role as an owner of the lobbying company Global Counsel while he served as British ambassador to Washington. Last week the Ethics and Integrity Commissiondemanded new laws to improve transparencyafter a review ordered by Keir Starmer.The investigation spanned more than four months and involved discussions in person, on video conference calls, and over email, that touched on placing stories in newspapers including the Financial Times and the Guardian.When the undercover reporter said he would feel “discomfort” at “being paid to write something with the newspaper not knowing”, one lobbyist said: “I completely understand that, but I also think if you were to tell [the editor] ‘CT has commissioned me to do this’, she would instantly bat it out of the room. She wouldn’t … I think you’d be hard pressed to find an editor who [would].”View image in fullscreenGavin Stollar, pictured in 2005.Photograph: Philippe Hays/ShutterstockDuring the same meeting the journalist noted he would ordinarily be paid by a publication, asking: “Is there a sort of financial arrangement with CT Group as well?”“Yeah,” a CT manager replied. “Historically, yeah.”In a subsequent call, another lobbyist said CT Group had since worked with a journalist “where I paid her to do a certain piece, a certain national piece”, but the journalist “never discloses to editors where she gets her sources from”. They described the financial arrangement with this unnamed journalist as “a retainer”.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionIn a statement, CT Group said: “We do not comment publicly on work we do for clients and we therefore cannot respond to the specific allegations you have made.” It said the events had been “deliberately mischaracterised” and said it did not engage the undercover journalist to do anything and did not pay him anything. It said the journalist was looking for work and “chased us relentlessly over a period of more than six months”.“We are happy to confirm, however, that we never ask journalists to behave otherwise than in accordance with their professional and ethical standards, and we trust journalists to conduct themselves by reference to those standards,” it said. “We note it is commonplace for journalists to chair or participate in roundtable and similar events, and to provide other media services to their clients, and we support the right of journalists to do so.”The lobbyists delegated to speak to the journalist did not always seem comfortable with paying to place stories. One, who had recently joined, said it seemed “a bit weird to me”, but she said if a payment was made, CT Group would need to see the story before it was submitted to a national newspaper “because you’re paying them to achieve an outcome of a piece”.Asked about the input CT Group would have over his story, another said: “The commercial relationship very much varies, there’s no sort of set formula about it. It’s not like this is how we always operate … it’s a project by project, piece by piece, sort of system.”Referring to the payment offer, he said “we can 100% take that off the table”. They said: “I can understand you being like, no, I don’t want to work in that way. That’s completely fair. And so happy for this to be a sort of much more traditional relationship.”CT Group publishes a code of conduct that states that “ethical and professional behaviour is at the heart of CT Group’s business, and those values should be followed at all times”. It says “we never do anything we cannot justify publicly”.The Guardian’scode of practicerequires journalists to tell a senior editor of “any attempts to induce favourable editorial treatment through the offer of gifts or favours”, while the FT’scode of conductstates that “no one at the FT should abuse their position for personal profit”.In a discussion about how he would pitch a piece to the FT or the Guardian, the undercover journalist asked if he would tell the newspaper he was working with CT Group or pitch it as his own story. “I think you’d pitch it in like that,” one of the CT staff members replied.“So they wouldn’t necessarily know that it kind of had a CT group angle?” the reporter asked.“No, I don’t think so,” they said. “No, I don’t think they need to. We can help you flesh out the angle and stuff to take to the editor and make sure you’ve got the case studies and stuff. But I don’t think, no, I don’t think … we kind of sit behind that.”Live-in Guardians was approached for comment.Get in touchContact us about this storyThe best public interest journalism relies on first-hand accounts from people in the know. If you have something to share on this subject, you can contact us confidentially using the following methods:Secure Messaging in the Guardian appThe Guardian app has a tool to send tips about stories. Messages are end to end encrypted and concealed within the routine activity that every Guardian mobile app performs. This prevents an observer from knowing that you are communicating with us at all, let alone what is being said.If you don’t already have the Guardian app, download it (iOS/Android) and go to the menu. Select ‘Secure Messaging’.SecureDropIf you can safely use the tor network without being observed or monitored you can send messages and documents to the Guardian via ourSecureDrop platform.Our guide attheguardian.com/tipslists several ways to contact us securely, and discusses the pros and cons of each.Show moreExplore more on these topicsLobbyingLynton CrosbyBoris JohnsonDavid CameronNewspapersNewspapers & magazinesnewsShareReuse this content

title: Woman, 70, charged with murder of British expat Karen Carter in France | UK news | The Guardian
description: Public prosecutor for the region said suspect had been initially arrested shortly after the murder on 29 April last year
Author: Jamie Grierson
Headline: Public prosecutor for the region said suspect had been initially arrested shortly after the murder on 29 April last year
Content: Karen Carter ran two gites in the village of Tremolat in south-west France.Photograph: FacebookView image in fullscreenKaren Carter ran two gites in the village of Tremolat in south-west France.Photograph: FacebookUK newsWoman, 70, charged with murder of British expat Karen Carter in FrancePublic prosecutor for the region said suspect had been initially arrested shortly after the murder on 29 April last yearJamie GriersonandKim WillsherWed 22 Jul 2026 14.28 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 14.18 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA 70-year-old woman has been charged with the murder of Karen Carter, the British expat found dead in south-westernFrancelast year.The woman was charged on 2 July with Carter’s murder in Trémolat, in the Dordogne, and is “under judicial
