supervision”.Jacques-Édouard Andrault, the public prosecutor for the region, is quoted as confirming the charge, adding the suspect had been arrested shortly after the murder on 29 April last year, but was released without charge at the time.Andrault said: “On July 2nd, the investigating judge in Périgueux formally charged a woman with murder.”He added that “this 70-year-old woman, originally from Trémolat, was taken into custody on April 30, 2025, during a preliminary investigation, before being released”.“Since then, the investigations conducted as part of the judicial inquiry opened on May 7, 2025, have led the investigating judge to indict her,” he said, adding “this woman has been placed under judicial supervision”.Carter, a 65-year-old British-South African national, ran twogites(holiday rental homes) in the village of Trémolat, a beautifully renovated 250-year-old farmhouse and neighbouring 18th-century stone barn collectively called Les Chouettes.She was said to divide her time between the Dordogne and the home she shared with her husband, Alan Carter, in KuGompo City, South Africa.An autopsy revealed that Carter died from severe blood loss after being stabbed multiple times, collapsing and dying next to her car.Eight serious wounds were noted in total, including to the “chest, abdomen and forearm”, as well as “superficial ones on the thigh, shoulder and thumbs”.Carter had been at a wine-tasting party at Cafe Village Trémolat, described on Facebook as “an association that wants to offer the people of Trémolat and the surrounding area a meeting place”, with about 15 other guests, including Eric Chassagne, the village’s mayor.A few days after the attack a then 69-year-old local woman was arrested. She was released without charge shortly after.It later emerged that Carter was discovered by a 75-year-old businessman, Jean-François Guerrier, with whom she was in a relationship. The nature of their relationship was seized upon and speculated over by tabloids.Carter’s husband, a former London Stock Exchange worker who was in South Africa at the time of the murder, expressed his shock at the revelations that his wife had “started a relationship” with another man, saying he understood Guerrier was “just a friend of hers”.Explore more on these topicsUK newsFrancenewsShareReuse this content

title: One UK mosque attacked ‘every five days’, says British Muslim Trust | UK news | The Guardian
description: More than 70 attacks have been recorded over past year, including acts of violence, property damage and vandalism
Author: Aamna Mohdin
Headline: More than 70 attacks have been recorded over past year, including acts of violence, property damage and vandalism
Content: Damage outside entrance of the mosque in Phyllis Avenue, Peacehaven, East Sussex, after an arson attack in October 2025.Photograph: Jamie Lashmar/PAView image in fullscreenDamage outside entrance of the mosque in Phyllis Avenue, Peacehaven, East Sussex, after an arson attack in October 2025.Photograph: Jamie Lashmar/PAUK newsOne UK mosque attacked ‘every five days’, says British Muslim TrustMore than 70 attacks have been recorded over past year, including acts of violence, property damage and vandalismAamna Mohdinand Jayni MakwanaWed 22 Jul 2026 19.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleOne mosque is attacked in Britain every five days, according to new data released by the government’s partner for monitoring anti-Muslim hatred.More than 70 attacks on mosques in the UK have been recorded over the past year, which include acts of intimidation, violence, property damage, harassment, anti-Muslim hate speech, online abuse and vandalism.The British Muslim Trust warned that momentum on attacks was building and called for an urgent response from the government.The top three hotspots for attacks are London, Greater Manchester and the West Midlands. There have been nine separate mosque attacks in London, and one mosque in Birmingham has suffered three attacks in the last 12 months.UK mosques advised to run lockdown drills amid fears of anti-Muslim attacksRead moreData from the Home Office shows anti-Muslim hate crime in England and Walesrose by 19%in the 12 months to March 2025.Akeela Ahmed, the chief executive of the British Muslim Trust, said: “We are seeing a steady rise in the number of attacks against mosques, and we believe the government should be doing everything it can to help mosques protect themselves.”Earlier this year, the government announced it would provide up to £40m to mosques as part of the protective security for mosques scheme. The initiative would allow mosques to apply for funding for physical security measures such as CCTV, alarms, fencing and other security personnel services.Home Office guidance says mosques should apply for “security measures” if they have experienced or feel vulnerable to hate crime, or if there has been hate crime in the area towards other places of worship or their congregants. The guidance warns applications that “do not include strong evidence or do not explain the risk clearly are unlikely to be successful”.Ahmed has previouslyurged ministers to drop these requirementsfor applying for protective security, saying it created a significant barrier to support.The British Muslim Trust said mosques applying for funding have found the scheme to be difficult to access, with some waiting up to 18 months to receive funding. The monitoring group added that not only was there a backlog, but that many were being denied funding without explanation.“A mosque is vulnerable purely because it’s a mosque. Currently, they’re waiting for months while living in fear of another attack because of the backlog. In many cases, police aren’t even responding to low-level incidents, leaving people frightened and vulnerable,” Ahmed said.“It is great that this funding and initiative exists, but we need to recognise that the momentum on attacks is building, and we need to react and prepare for this.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAhmed added: “We recommend exploring better ways to get the funding to mosques as smoothly as possible including that the fund is devolved in order to speed up its delivery, especially given recent events.”Last month, a study by the British Muslim Trust and British Future found thatone in six Britons believethe growth of the Muslim population “poses a foundational threat to UK culture”, raising alarm that hostile attitudes towards Muslims are being normalised.A Home Office spokesperson said:“Muslims in the UK face appalling levels of religious hate crime. It’s crucial that they not only are safe but feel safe.“This government has increased funding for protective security at mosques, Muslim faith schools and community centres to record levels – up to £40m in 2026-27.“We are working to process all applications as quickly as possible.”Explore more on these topicsUK newsIslamophobianewsShareReuse this content

title: Tories ask HMRC to investigate whether Nigel Farage owes tax on £5m gift | Nigel Farage | The Guardian
description: Party chair queries status of funds Reform leader received from crypto billionaire Christopher Harborne
Author: Rowena Mason
Headline: Party chair queries status of funds Reform leader received from crypto billionaire Christopher Harborne
Content: Nigel Farage said over the weekend that the £5m gift was ‘totally undeclarable in every single way’.Photograph: Joe Giddens/PAView image in fullscreenNigel Farage said over the weekend that the £5m gift was ‘totally undeclarable in every single way’.Photograph: Joe Giddens/PANigel FarageTories ask HMRC to investigate whether Nigel Farage owes tax on £5m giftParty chair queries status of funds Reform leader received from crypto billionaire Christopher HarborneRowena MasonWhitehall editorWed 22 Jul 2026 14.27 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 16.56 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Conservatives have asked HMRC to investigate whether Nigel Farage should have paid tax on the £5m gift he received from the crypto billionaireChristopher Harborne.The party chair, Kevin Hollinrake, wrote to the tax authorities questioning whether it was a payment to compensate him for running to be an MP.Farage has said the gift was purely personal and unconditional, with a document drawn up to say it was given with no expectation in return.He has also previously said the payment was to go towards his personal security costs, and a reward for campaigning for Brexit. He said it was no one’s business what he did with the money and that he could “spend it on Ferraris” if he wanted.The Guardian, however,reported this monththat three sources had said Farage informed senior Reform UK figures that he would require compensation of £1m per year to offset his loss of earnings if he were to run for office.A Reform spokesperson said at the time that the Guardian’s report was “fake and wholly incorrect”. Lawyers for Harborne referred back to his previous statements about the gift, saying he “expected nothing in return” and “did not envisage Mr Farage returning to politics”.In light of that reporting, Hollinrake has written to HMRC asking it to investigate.View image in fullscreenThe Tory party chair, Kevin Hollinrake.Photograph: Christopher Thomond/The Guardian“If those reports are accurate, they raise legitimate questions as to whether the payment was truly a personal gift, or whether it was connected to Mr Farage resuming the leadership ofReform UKand standing for election to parliament,” he wrote.“If the payment was connected to discussions about Mr Farage returning as leader of Reform UK, and standing for parliament with a view to becoming a member of parliament, there would appear to be a serious question as to whether it should properly be characterised as income rather than a gift.“I believe it is imperative that HMRC examines whether the payment falls within the ‘disguised remuneration’ regime or otherwise constitutes taxable earnings arising from an office, employment or political position.“If the payment was intended as a reward, inducement or compensation connected with Mr Farage’s roles within Reform UK, the tax treatment may be materially different from that applicable to a genuine personal gift and different to the tax status of a normal political donation (not that it was declared as such).”The tax expert Dan Neidle has already raised questions around whether the £5m should have incurred tax after the Guardian’s reporting.Reform UK has been approached for comment.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionGet in touchContact Guardian Politics about this storyThe best public interest journalism relies on first-hand accounts from people in the know. If you have something to share on this subject you can contact the Guardian's UK Politics team confidentially using the following methods:Secure Messaging in the Guardian appThe Guardian app has a tool to send tips about stories. Messages are end to end encrypted and concealed within the routine activity that every Guardian mobile app performs. This prevents an observer from knowing that you are communicating with us at all, let alone what is being said.If you don't already have the Guardian app, download it (iOS/Android) and go to the menu. Scroll down and click onSecure Messaging. When asked who you wish to contact please select thePolitics (UK)team.EmailFor encrypted email correspondence you can create a free Proton Mail account and email us at guardian.politics.desk@protonmail.com.SecureDropIf you can safely use the tor network without being observed or monitored you can send messages and documents to the Guardian via ourSecureDrop platform.Finally, our guide attheguardian.com/tipslists several ways to contact us securely, and discusses the pros and cons of each.Show moreFarage faces a standards inquiry over Harborne’s gift and other undeclared fundingfrom the fraudster George Cottrell.He told the Triggernometry podcast over the weekend that the £5m was “totally undeclarable in every single way”.He acknowledged that the parliamentary standards system could find against him, but claimed he had sought advice from a top international lawyer who told him the £5m did not need to be declared because it was unconditional and he had a document saying it had been given freely.The rules state that gifts and benefits in kind have to be declared for the 12 months before an MP enters parliament, but only if they are political.Farage is fighting a byelection against more than 30 candidates in Clacton, but none of them are from the main parties, which decided to boycott the contest.He could potentially face a second byelection if the commissioner finds against him and the standards committee recommends a substantial sanction including suspension from the Commons, triggering a possible recall petition.The major parties have said they would stand against him in a second contest were it to occur.Explore more on these topicsNigel FarageReform UKChristopher HarbornenewsShareReuse this content

title: US teen drops Meta lawsuit on social media addiction days before trial | Meta | The Guardian
description: Withdrawn case marks a victory for the social media giant after earlier landmark loss at trial over addictive claims
Author: Dara Kerr
Headline: Withdrawn case marks a victory for the social media giant after earlier landmark loss at trial over addictive claims
Content: RKC, who started using social media when he was about eight, said he became addicted to it.Photograph: Matt Cardy/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenRKC, who started using social media when he was about eight, said he became addicted to it.Photograph: Matt Cardy/Getty ImagesMetaUS teen drops Meta lawsuit on social media addiction days before trialWithdrawn case marks a victory for the social media giant after earlier landmark loss at trial over addictive claimsDara Kerrand agenciesWed 22 Jul 2026 18.33 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 17.17 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAFloridateen whose lawsuit claimedMeta’s platforms were to blame ⁠for his depression ⁠and anxiety ​dropped his case against the company just days before the trial inLos Angeleswas ⁠set to start, his attorneys said on Wednesday. It was the latest in a massive series of high-stakes lawsuits against social media companies for allegedly designing addictive products that lead to the harm of children.The lawsuit, brought by a 15-year-old boy known as ⁠RKC, originally named four defendants, Google’s YouTube, Meta’s Instagram, Snap Inc’s ​Snapchat and ByteDance’sTikTok.YouTube and TikTok ‌settled in JuneandSnap reached a tentative settlement in the case, Bloomberg ‌reportedon Monday. The terms of those settlements were confidential.Meta used AI to tag workers who took leave to be laid off, lawsuit claimsRead moreRKC, who started using social media when he was about eight, said he became addicted to it, losing sleep and suffering from depression and anxiety, according ‌to court filings.“In light of the overall successful result of the litigation and his concerns about ​enduring a grueling weeks-long trial, he has elected to withdraw his claims againstMeta,” attorneys for RKC said in a statement. “He’s ready to close this chapter and focus on his recovery ⁠and engage in therapy as he aspires to have a ​normal life.”A ​spokesperson for Meta said RKC had dropped the claims ​without receiving ‌any payment.“The claims ​never held ​up,” the company said in a statement. This outcome makes clear that we will not back away from defending ourselves against baseless lawsuits.”The dropped casecomes after Meta and YouTubesuffered a bruising lossin March in the same Los Angeles court. That trial lasted six weeks and ended with the two companies being ordered to pay a young woman $6m in damages. In the landmark decision, the juryfound Meta and YouTube liablefor deliberately designing addictive products and negligent for having failed to provide adequate warnings about the potential dangers of their platforms.In a separate lawsuit in March brought by New Mexico’s attorney general, a jury ordered Meta topay $375m in civil penaltiesover claims that it misled consumers about the safety of its platforms and enabled harm, including child sexual exploitation, against its users. The back-to-back verdicts are the first ever to find social media companies liable for how their products affect young people.These cases were the first to go to court, and are slated to be followed by more trials from two coordinated groups of more than 2,000 plaintiffs, including families, school districts and state attorneys general, who have brought lawsuits against Meta, YouTube, TikTok and Snap.The four social media companies alsosettled another major lawsuitjust before trial in May, brought by a school district in Kentucky over similar claims of addictive products leading to harm in children.This month, Meta appealed the verdict in its loss at theLos Angelescourt. It challenged the jury’s decision that found the company liable for designing addictive products to hook in young people.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toTechScapeA weekly dive in to how technology is shaping our livesPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionMeta and the other social media companies have vigorously defended their business models, saying their products aren’t addictive. In aposton Wednesday, Andy Stone, Meta’s communications director, wrote that RKC’s claims “never held up” and that he “averaged just minutes a day on Facebook and Instagram”.Lawyers for RKC said they achieved what they set out to do by bringing awareness to social media’s alleged harms.“RKC came into this process wanting to hold social media companies accountable and push for changes to protect young people like himself,” the lawyers said. “He did that.”Reuters contributed reportingExplore more on these topicsMetaLaw (US)FloridaLos AngelesCaliforniaMental healthSocial medianewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘He knows what he’s doing’: Burnham’s off-the-cuff remarks inspire headlines and headaches | Andy Burnham | The Guardian
description: New PM’s conversational tone is markedly different to his predecessor’s, but carries risk of sowing confusion
Author: Kiran Stacey
Headline: New PM’s conversational tone is markedly different to his predecessor’s, but carries risk of sowing confusion
Content: Andy Burnham is in a rare position of having become prime minister without having campaigned on any manifesto promises.Photograph: Toby Melville/PAView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham is in a rare position of having become prime minister without having campaigned on any manifesto promises.Photograph: Toby Melville/PAAndy Burnham‘He knows what he’s doing’: Burnham’s off-the-cuff remarks inspire headlines and headachesNew PM’s conversational tone is markedly different to his predecessor’s, but carries risk of sowing confusionKiran StaceyandAlexandra ToppingWed 22 Jul 2026 13.58 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 16.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleBefore he became Britain’s prime minister, Andy Burnham promised to“do politics differently”. Just three days into his time in Downing Street, that approach is already being put to the test.In the last two days, the prime minister has been accused of performing U-turns over raising the income tax threshold, raising taxes for higher earners and freezing private sector rents.The minor controversies are mostly a function of the fact Burnham has come into office with a rare combination of not having fixed his policy platform and a habit of speaking openly about his ideas before they are fully formed.Earlier this week, for example,Burnham saidthat while he was campaigning in the Makerfield byelection which secured his return to parliament, he had heard more complaints about the income tax threshold than any other issue. This prompted speculation he would raise it once he became PM.Reports on Tuesday suggested he had ruled it out, only for him to clarifyon Wednesdaythat it was still an option. As he did, Burnham expressed surprise that people had put so much stock in his original comments.“It’s funny these days you can’t answer a question honestly without then people reading everything else into it,” he told broadcasters. “I want to answer questions honestly in this job, as I said before. I think that’s all about trust in politics and doing politics differently, and I’m determined to keep working in that way.”He added: “It’s just perhaps the way politics is these days and the way people report things, but that was an honest answer.”A similar scenario played out over the issue of freezing private sector rents. Asked by reporters on Monday if such a move could be part of his package to tackle the UK’s cost of living crisis, he replied: “We’re looking at all of those things.”1:39Andy Burnham pledges to address cost of living in first speech as UK PM - videoOn Wednesday, however,it was reportedthat officials were not actively considering such a move.Burnham has alsokickstarted an unexpected debateon releasing prisoners early, saying he would review the policy, which has been one of the government’s main tools for easing overcrowding in prisons.“Andy appears to have just realised he is the prime minister,” said oneLabourofficial. “He is a talented communicator and he is right that the media spins messages and this can be a hostile environment. But it is his job to go into that with his eyes wide open.“He hasn’t fought an election or had much scrutiny, and so people are desperately trying to understand what his priorities and platforms are.”For many, Burnham’s approach is a necessary response to the buttoned-up way in which his predecessor,Keir Starmer, used to speak and to the modern media environment which demands authenticity above discipline.His open, conversational style worked well as Greater Manchester mayor, when reporters became used to his off-the-cuff comments during press conferences, and he seems determined to bring the same outlook to Downing Street.View image in fullscreenAndy Burnham is met by a large crowd of MPs after his swearing-in at the Houses of Parliament on 22 June.Photograph: Dan Kitwood/Getty Images“He’s not an idiot,” said Sean Kemp, who advised the former Liberal Democrat leader Nick Clegg and is now a partner at the political comms firm Hanbury Strategy. “He knows what he’s doing, but he doesn’t care, and he’s going to keep on saying it. And he’s done it so much now, I’m starting to think now that he’s decided this is just how he does things.”The approach is also reflected in the media he uses to communicate. He has already shown his willingness to use mischievoussocial media poststo get his message across, and took part in an online question-and-answer session on Reddit shortly after winning the Makerfield byelection.But the chances of being misunderstood were highlighted in another post-Makerfield interview, with the England footballer turned podcaster Gary Lineker. In comments that wereinterpreted by someas leaving the door open to a wealth taxand othersas ruling it out, he said: “I do believe we need a greater sense of fairness and people feeling that things are being done in the right way and a fair way.“But at the same time, you know, I don’t want to sort of be perceived as somebody who’s coming in with grudges and agendas and, you know, going to just immediately find or demonise one group or create a new way of dividing people.”Scottish Labour’s rivals decry ‘chancer’ Anas Sarwar for resigning to join Andy Burnham’s governmentRead moreBurnham’s desire to bypass traditional political journalism is not new, say political communication experts. But it does carry risks.“I think every single prime minister in the last 25 years has tried to do that,” said Kemp. “Then they realise that these are the people reporting day to day on what you are doing, and still, to a great extent, are setting the parameters of how you’re viewed.”Guto Harri, who was once the communications director for the former prime minister Boris Johnson, said: “He’s not an ordinary person any more, he is the first lord of the Treasury and the prime minister of Britain.“So if he says these things, it is reasonable for the people who hear him to think something will follow. And if something doesn’t follow, then people are likely to become cynical quite quickly.”Explore more on these topicsAndy BurnhamLabourKeir StarmerfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Trainspotting the Musical review – forgettable songs make this a bad trip  | Theatre | The Guardian
description:
Theatre Royal Haymarket, London
The chopped-up story means this ends up feeling like a weak impersonation of Danny Boyle’s classic film
Author: Arifa Akbar
Headline: Theatre Royal Haymarket, LondonThe chopped-up story means this ends up feeling like a weak impersonation of Danny Boyle’s classic film
Content: The cast during the curtain call on the opening night of Trainspotting the Musical.Photograph: Dave Benett/Alan Chapman/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThe cast during the curtain call on the opening night of Trainspotting the Musical.Photograph: Dave Benett/Alan Chapman/Getty ImagesTheatreReviewTrainspotting the Musical review – forgettable songs make this a bad tripTheatre Royal Haymarket, LondonThe chopped-up story means this ends up feeling like a weak impersonation of Danny Boyle’s classic filmArifa AkbarWed 22 Jul 2026 19.06 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 21.10 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google“Choose life …” Those iconic words from that era-defining film are said more than once in this musical take on Irvine Welsh’s novel about Renton and his renegade gang of smackheads from Leith.“Choose being in the moment,” says the disembodied voice that asks us to switch off our mobile phones before the show has begun (with, one imagines, a self-satisfied glint at the joke). “Choose life,” repeats Renton (Lewis Kidd), trying so hard, it seems, to capture the dangerous energy of that moment in the 1990s when Ewan McGregor, Jonny Lee Miller and co depicted heroin addiction as a protest against the grunting conventions of ordinary life.Sadly, the drug-addled escapades and illegal highs here look about as dangerous as a double-dose of Gaviscon.Written by Welsh and directed by Caroline Jay Ranger, the story appears chopped up so that one scene is disconnected to the next, the story bitty, the well-known scenes and lines rattled off as if from a Trainspotting-branded assembly line, albeit in a different order: first the group trip to Blackpool for a northern soul gig, then the various set-pieces, from the speed-fuelled job interview by Spud (Kieran Andrew) to the snooker-table violence from Begbie (Frankie O’Connor) and Renton’s toilet high – the latter made more vivid with the use of back screens (video design by Douglas O’Connell).There are some touching moments with the death of Alison’s (Rosie Dignan) baby and the decline of the most well-adjusted of the group, Tommy (Finlay Paul), until he discovers heroin, but these are not nearly as devastating as they should be, and are briskly enacted.None of it is half as shocking, entertaining or as poignant as the film. Nor is the imagination as sparkling when it goes its own way, such as a moment in which Spud’s mother rails against social ills (from prescription drugs to sugar and additives).The advertising declares the return of these characters, 30 years on from Danny Boyle’s film, but that’s not quite true because this train has been chugging on ever since the publication of Welsh’s novel in 1993, which has spawned a prequel, three sequels, a stage play, and a second film (T2 Trainspotting). It could be Trainspotting Inc, never ending in its mutant life forms.The leads are mutants of sorts here, visibly styled on the film actors, albeit cardboard flat: Renton with shaved head and jeans but without the dark charisma of McGregor. It is hardly Kidd’s fault – characterisation doesn’t allow nuance or individuality. Sick Boy, cocksure, with dyed blond hair; Spud a twitching dork and Begbie a pub psychopath with a moustache.In its music and songs, the cover numbers bring the few high points. There is Iggy Pop’s Lust for Life, so synonymous with the film that you can still picture McGregor’s sprint along the streets of Edinburgh, and renditions of Lou Reed’s Perfect Day. But, much like the musical itself, they do nothing more than imitate a far better original, already out there.Alongside the covers are songs written by Stephen McGuinness and Welsh which are forgettable at best, or best forgotten in the case of one with a country and western twang and another which sounds alarmingly like a riff on The Proclaimers.It’s strange to see these characters singing as they shoot up, or rage against the machine. “Would Renton really choose to return to this life,” you find yourself wondering. It all seems so tame and commercial for this bunch of anti-capitalists. A bad trip indeed.Plays until 5 September followed by UK tourExplore more on these topicsTheatreTrainspottingreviewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘The magnificent, the magical, the marvellous!’ It’s TV’s happiest endings  | Television | The Guardian
description: Gold dances, the Good Friday agreement and standup superstardom … get ready to blub because here are the most heartwarming denouements in TV history
Author: Hollie Richardson
Headline: Gold dances, the Good Friday agreement and standup superstardom … get ready to blub because here are the most heartwarming denouements in TV history
Content: Comic effect … The Marvellous Mrs Maisel.Photograph: AmazonView image in fullscreenComic effect … The Marvellous Mrs Maisel.Photograph: AmazonTelevision‘The magnificent, the magical, the marvellous!’ It’s TV’s happiest endingsGold dances, the Good Friday agreement and standup superstardom … get ready to blub because here are the most heartwarming denouements in TV historyHollie Richardson,Lucy Mangan,Phil Harrison,Kate Abbott,Hannah J Davies,Rachel Aroesti,Alexi Duggins,Dylan B Jones,Ann Lee,Jack Seale,Stuart HeritageandMichael HoganWed 22 Jul 2026 07.01 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 12.02 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWarning: this article contains spoilersThe Marvelous Mrs MaiselWhat does perfect 50s housewife Midge (Rachel Brosnahan) do after her husband leaves? She gets drunk and flashes her boobs on stage while doing an impromptu comedy set. It’s impossible not to root for her from this moment on. With the help of her unlikely manager, Susie Myers (Alex Borstein), she embarks on a standup career with the odds constantly stacked against her. Finally, Midge makes her breakthrough moment happen on a late-night show.“I want a big life,” she tells the studio audience, who have just been howling at her jokes. “They say ambition is an unattractive trait in a woman. Maybe. But you know what’s really unattractive? Waiting around for something to happen.” All of her family members – who had been so against this career – are there cheering with pride. Gordon Ford (Reid Scott) then introduces her to the world: “The magnificent, the magical, the marvellous Mrs Maisel.”But the real gut-punch moment? When Midge looks over to Susie, who has a tear in her eye and her hand on her heart. The final scene jumps to decades later, with the hugely successful pair joking over the phone as they both watch an old episode of Jeopardy.Hollie Richardson30 RockView image in fullscreenFunny business … Tina Fey as Liz Lemon and Keith Powell as Toofer in 30 Rock.Photograph: NBC/NBCU Photo Bank/Getty ImagesThe brilliance of the30 Rockfinale – aired as two episodes back-to-back in 2013 – really begins halfway through the closing series. That’s when Liz Lemon gets married and embarks, via adoption, on motherhood, both milestones at which a traditional sitcom would usually end a show centred on a female protagonist. But not for Lemon, and not for creator Tina Fey, who ends it with TGS (the show within a show) being uncancelled and brought back for one last episode – an appropriately meta end for the show itself – so that everyone else’s storylines can be tied up. Liz is shown to be back at work and realising she needs to have work and not just family in her life or she’ll live on a knife-edge of insanity as much as she did when it was just work.It gave us callbacks (even unto St Elsewhere), the full gag quotient (plus The Rural Juror song), Kenneth’s ascendance (possibly transcendence), and closure. Even Pete got a brief year of happiness before the epilogue. Above all, it gave us Jack’s lovely speech to Liz from the boat, which she eventually interrupts to tell him she platonically loves him too. Affectionate respect has never been so beautifully captured. See-through dishwasher for all!Lucy ManganDetectoristsView image in fullscreenTime for the gold dance! … Lance (Toby Jones) and Andy (Mackenzie Crook) in Detectorists.Photograph: BBCThere was rarely anything tricksy about the narrative of Detectorists – it was based round a set of simple, binary storylines concerning relationships, parenthood and mild discord over metal-detecting territory. All the same, Lance and Andy were deceptively complex characters and the show’s seemingly still emotional waters ran deep. It soon became clear that we were being offered one of the most charming and convincing depictions of friendship in television history.The show’s conclusion was gently audacious, suggesting continuity as the happiest of endings. Andy and Becky buy a delightful, dilapidated house. Toni is preparing to move in with Lance. Andy and Lance don’t actually need anything they don’t already have. Friends, lovers, a pub nearby, modest roofs over their heads, a shared passion and perhaps most importantly of all, each other. Their gold dance is, at all times, right in front of them.Phil HarrisonDerry GirlsView image in fullscreenChoose yes! … Derry Girls.Photograph: Peter Marley/PA“If YES put an X in this square.” TheDerry Girlshad to grow up sometime, and so it came to pass, after Erin and Orla made it through the wildest 18th birthday/joint first communion party ever – theme: literary monkeys – that they all got to vote on the Good Friday agreement. For a show so consistently hilarious, Lisa McGee’s cracker comedy finally turned on a sixpence into the most beautifully poignant piece of historical analysis. I was weeping with joy from the moment Granda Joe says: “What if no one else has to die? What if all this becomes a ghost story you’ll tell your weans one day. A ghost story they’ll hardly believe.” By the time all of them chose yes, chose hope, chose to end the Troubles that were all they’d ever known, I was a goner. Of course it was soundtracked to the Cranberries – the only possible choice – and of course, in the ultimate cameo, Chelsea Clinton got their letter decades late in the closing seconds! As Michelle said, being a Derry Girl is a fuckin’ state of mind, and I’ll never stop loving them. Sláinte!Kate AbbottHacksView image in fullscreenShow stopper … Deborah Vance (Jean Smart) in the Hacks finale.Photograph: SkyIf you’ve watched the finale ofHacksthis might be a hard sell. Major spoiler incoming: the final episode of this biting showbiz satire ended with our Joan Rivers-inspired antiheroine Deborah Vance (Jean Smart) revealing a terminal cancer diagnosis, and planning her death by assisted dying. Fans had already noted the morbid foreshadowing all season, but it still felt like a brutal plot twist – not only for Deborah but for Ava (Hannah Einbinder), her protege, surrogate daughter and sometime pretend girlfriend.Frankly it was all a bit triggering, not just because I lost a parent to cancer, but because Hacks is really funny and this was absolutely the unfunniest thing that could have happened. But in a way – stay with me here – Hacks did have a happy ending. At the 11th hour, Deborah changed her mind and decided to hang on a little longer and write another comedy special, delivering one of the best lines in the whole damn series: “I may not have 30 years – but I think I have another hour.” I was, like most professional journalists, watching the finale from an airport Starbucks, ugly crying. When I heard that line I got instant chills. I stopped sobbing, let out a gasp, then started crying again – but this time it was happy tears. The thought of Deborah writing one final bitchy, tell-all special really made me smile.Hannah J DaviesOnly Fools and HorsesView image in fullscreenBro selecta … Only Fools and Horses’ Sleepless in Peckham.Photograph: BBCLike one of Del Boy’s dodgy deals, Only Fools and Horses gave us two happy endings for the price of one. The first was 1996’s iconic fairytale finale: an antique watch makes Del and Rodney millionaires – cue a fever dream montage of the brothers living in the lap of luxury. Yet for the elder Trotter, the giddy euphoria was tinged with wrenching nostalgia for his old life, resulting in a profoundly bittersweet conclusion that cemented the show’s place in the British pop cultural canon. Then came the 2000s reboot: three Christmas specials, widely derided as knock-offs. Still, the eventual denouement – which involves a more modest windfall, some closure for Rodney and a long-awaited baby for him and Cassandra – is actually more uplifting and heartwarming than the original. A less artful ending, undoubtedly, but I’m still glad we said goodbye with a more relatable kind of happiness.Rachel AroestiBrooklyn Nine-NineView image in fullscreenUp lifting … the Brooklyn Nine-Nine finale.Photograph: E4How to end one of TV’s most gleefully daft comedies? With a ludicrously elaborate set-piece episode, featuring the most OTT version ever of the annual event where the characters go all out to double cross each other in a heist – naturally. This double-bill farewell was a glorious exercise in fan-pleasing that packed in a whistlestop tour of the show’s most significant moments (the warehouse where they solved their first case! The interview room behind the best ever skit – wherePeralta made suspects sing Backstreet Boys!), joy-inducing returns of fave characters and the heartfelt emotional moment that had been brewing for eight seasons – Captain Holt telling Peralta he’d be proud to have a son like him. It all ended with a quiet, beautiful moment – the lights being turned out on the precinct as they stepped into the lift together for one last time. Then they threw in a bonus scene set a year later with the crew announcing that they’d get back together every year for their annual heist. Cue a last sketch, including bad magic, hammy acting and one final rousing call of the team motto: Nine-Nine! Now that’s how you say goodbye.Alexi DugginsSex and the CityView image in fullscreenFrench connections … An American Girl in Paris (Part Deux), the Sex and the City finale.Photograph: HBOThings weren’t looking great asSex and the Citycareened and Carried its way through its too-long final season. Even Charlotte’s dog was starting to look exhausted. But writer Michael Patrick King and this beautiful cast of women really pulled it out of the Birkin for the two-parter finale in which Carrie has to choose between moving to Paris with a handsome millionaire, or staying in New York with a different handsome millionaire (look, we’ve all been there).There’s breathy old Hollywood dialogue that somehow works. There’s Sarah Jessica Parker galloping across wet cobbles to moody French hip-hop. And if you didn’t get goosebumps from Miranda gripping Mr Big’s hairy, Rolex-clad forearm and gasping “Go get our girl!” you’re as dead inside as the second movie.Dylan B JonesParks and RecreationView image in fullscreenKnope for president! … Amy Poehler as Leslie Knope in Parks and Recreation.Photograph: NBC/Channel 4Unlike most TV “happy ever afters” which can seem rushed,Parks and Recreationtreats us to a two-part finale which jumps forward in time not just once but several times, as it lovingly explores what happens next to Leslie Knope and our favourite gang of Pawnee government employees.Each character gets their storyline tied up in a way that is entirely befitting of their personality and arc – and of course, it would end with Amy Poehler’s overachieving Knope possibly becoming president of the United States. We also finally get some karmic justice for Jerry/Garry/Larry, the perennial office scapegoat, who becomes a much-beloved mayor of Pawnee and lives to the ripe old age of 100.The ending is sweet, offbeat and supremely wholesome – all the things we’ve come to expect from Parks and Rec over the years. And this cuddly sitcom couldn’t have ended in a better way – as a tribute to the power of “friends, waffles, work” (Knope’s life priorities), right to the very last credits.Ann LeeMr InbetweenView image in fullscreenMaking a killing as a cab driver … Mr Inbetween.Photograph: FXSo many classic shows have been led by a monstrous male character we secretly root for – Tony Soprano, Walter White, Doc Martin – and the protagonist in Scott Ryan’s flawless crime drama is up there with the best/worst. Ray Shoesmith has bludgeoned and gouged his way through Sydney’s criminal underworld but, in the final episode, he’s given up killing to become a rural cab driver. When two hoodlums who mean Ray harm get in the car, Ryan makes a brilliant creative decision: the last shot is Ray breaking the fourth wall, eyeballing the camera and grinning. Ray knows what we want. He’s going to horribly murder them! Hoorah!Jack SealeThe LeftoversView image in fullscreenAbsolutely perfect … Kevin and Nora in The Leftovers finale.Photograph: HBOFrom the ashes of one of the bleakest television programmes ever made (in a nutshell: 2% of the world’s population goes missing, and those left behind go mad with grief) came a beautifully hopeful ending. A long-separated couple reunite. The woman says she visited an alternative dimension where the missing 2% live happily in peace. It almost certainly isn’t the truth, but the story brought her comfort. The man says he believes her. They cry. They hold hands. They smile. For a show that never had any intention of explaining its central mystery, this ending – an emotional truth landing harder than a logical one – was absolutely perfect.Stuart HeritageGavin and StaceyView image in fullscreenWell lush … Gavin and Stacey.Photograph: BBCIt’s the drama, Mick, I just love it. There’s a reason why the Anglo-Welsh romcom’s finale became Britain’s most-watched scripted show in more than two decades. Five years after Nessa proposed to Smithy, nuptials loomed – except it was boring Sonia who the lovable lummox was marrying, not his love-hate soulmate. What was occurring? After a spine-tingling intervention at the altar, Smithy called it off and dashed to Southampton docks, where Nessa was about to set sail on a cargo ship. Now it was his turn to pop the question. The pair married in Barry while 21m viewers wept with joy into their Quality Street tins. Tidy, cracking and well lush.Michael HoganShare your happiest TV endingYou can tell us your own happiest TV ending using this form.Please share your story if you are 18 or over, anonymously if you wish. For more information please see ourterms of serviceandprivacy policy.Tell us hereYour responses, which can be anonymous, are secure as the form is encrypted and only the Guardian has access to your contributions. We will only use the data you provide us for the purpose of the feature and we will delete any personal data when we no longer require it for this purpose. For alternative ways to get in touch securely please see ourtips guide.NameWhere do you live?Tell us a bit about yourself (e.g. age, background, what you do)OptionalTell us your happiest TV show ending and whyPlease include as much detail as possible.If you are happy to, please upload a photo of yourself hereOptionalPlease note, the maximum file size is5.7 MB.Choose fileCan we publish your response?Yes, entirelyYes, but contact me firstYes, but please keep me anonymousNo, this is information onlyPhone numberOptionalYour contact details are helpful so we can contact you for more information. They will only be seen by the Guardian.Email addressYour contact details are helpful so we can contact you for more information. They will only be seen by the Guardian.You can add more information hereOptionalIf you include other people's names please ask them first.Would you be interested in speaking to our audio and/or video teams?Audio onlyVideo onlyAudio and videoNo, I'm not interestedBy submitting your response, you are agreeing to share your details with us for this feature.SubmitShow moreExplore more on these topicsTelevisionTV comedyThe Marvelous Mrs Maisel30 RockHacksSex and the CityParks and RecreationfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Jess Cartner-Morley on fashion: mermaidcore calls for shimmer and shells – just don’t go overboard | Fashion | The Guardian
description: What does a grownup mermaid look like? It starts with the silhouette …
Author: Jess Cartner-Morley
Headline: What does a grownup mermaid look like? It starts with the silhouette …
Content: Video: David Newby/The Guardian. Styling: Melanie WilkinsonJess Cartner-Morley on fashionFashionJess Cartner-Morley on fashion: mermaidcore calls for shimmer and shells – just don’t go overboardWhat does a grownup mermaid look like? It starts with the silhouette …Jess Cartner-MorleyWed 22 Jul 2026 09.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 11.05 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.Before wild swimming was even a twinkle in the eye of the culture, and before ice plunging went viral on TikTok, there were mermaids. Mermaids are the original summer It girls. Who doesn’t want hair that looks great when it’s wet and salty, shell jewellery, killer sex appeal and a life of sun, sea and winding up sailors for kicks? These chicks know how to live.The Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.Mermaidcore is this summer’s hottest style vibe. It is beach chic with a hot-sauce dash of brat energy. Every summer has its own off-duty avatar –tomato girl summer, anyone? – and 2026 is all about mermaids. For legal reasons, please note this column is not endorsing the luring of innocents to certain death. The vibe is Daryl Hannah in Splash, not selkies and sirens.In other words: you need to up your beach cover-up game. Bring on the crochet trousers. Dig out the iridescent sarong. Say yes to coral jewellery. Embrace shell-encrusted flip-flops. Mermaidcore is the romanticisation of summer, a cinematic vision of beach life with you as the main character. It is a new spin on my all-time favourite dress code, which is what Americans call “vacation formal”, and Brits call “going for dinner on holiday”.double quotation markThe trick is to borrow from the palette and texture of the ocean, not to dress as an actual residentWhat it does not mean is dressing like you are going to a kids’ pirates and mermaids party. Mermaidcore can be sophisticated. Designer Matthieu Blazy’s most recent Chanel show, held on the Atlantic shore of Biarritz, had sea anemone embroidery and seahorse jewellery, and closed with a slithering dress of seafoam sequins. Clothes for sipping rosé with a sea view, not for Ariel cosplay on a bouncy castle.So what exactly does a grownup mermaid look like? It starts with the silhouette. A certain slinkiness is in order. The switch is from an airy kaftan or maxidress to a narrower dress or long skirt. Or from sporty beach shorts to palazzo pants, snug at the hip and falling wide to the floor.View image in fullscreenPhotograph: David Newby/The GuardianLong and narrow is not the most practical silhouette, which is probably why you don’t see mermaids playing beach volleyball. This aesthetic is best suited for graceful lounging, but if you insist on exerting yourself (or have young children), fishing-net mesh or crochet work as shorter cover-ups. A shell necklace can bring much-needed glamour to a morning spent wrangling sun cream on to small bodies.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toInside SaturdayThe only way to get a look behind the scenes of the Saturday magazine. Sign up to get the inside story from our top writers as well as all the must-read articles and columns, delivered to your inbox every weekend.Preview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionJess Cartner-Morley on fashion: still wearing stripes? It’s time to join the dotsRead moreThe line between mermaidcore and fancy dress is restraint. Pile on every pearl and sparkle and you will look as if you’ve fallen into a Sea Life gift shop. The trick is to borrow from the palette and texture of the ocean, not to dress as an actual resident. The most elegant mermaid shades are those washed-out blues and green-tinted turquoises that look artfully faded by salt and sunshine – a slip dress in pale aqua, or a sheer shirt in lagoon blue or seafoam green. You want to channel the spirit of a woman who has spent the afternoon drifting through a secluded cove, not one who just escaped from a kids’ TV show.Texture matters as much as colour. Crochet, mesh, raffia and anything with a subtle shimmer all evoke the seaside without screaming it. A gauzy skirt catching the breeze has the magical quality of looking different every time the light hits it.And footwear? Once again, fashion is nudging us toward jelly shoes. These truly amphibious sandals can paddle through rock pools, survive a sea dash – and still take you to lunch. Very on-brand for anyone aspiring to spend their summer moving seamlessly between land and water. Because mermaidcore is all about embracing the dream of a carefree summer. And that’s a fantasy worth diving into.Styling: Roz Donoghue. Model: Chizoba at Milk. Styling assistant: Charlotte Gornall. Hair and makeup: Sophie Higginson using Hair by Sam McKnight and Lisa Eldridge Beauty.Swimsuit, £165, Bond-Eye.Trousers,£36, andbag, £42, both River Island.Crochet scarf, £35.99, Zara.Earrings, £320, YSSO.Necklace, £165, Rixo.Ring, £72, Ottoman Hands.Sunglasses, £180, Alemais x Le Specs.Sandals, £130, Ancient Greek SandalsExplore more on these topicsFashionJess Cartner-Morley on fashionWomenfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: The human fridge: is this really the best way to cool down in a ‘cruelly hot’ climate? | Life and style | The Guardian
description: A ‘personal cooling booth’ that promises to chill you out in 10 minutes has launched in Japan, with its makers claiming it beats air-conditioning or a fan
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: A ‘personal cooling booth’ that promises to chill you out in 10 minutes has launched in Japan, with its makers claiming it beats air-conditioning or a fan
Content: Climb in, cool down … Trusco’s Do Hiemon box.Photograph: YouTube/TruscoView image in fullscreenClimb in, cool down … Trusco’s Do Hiemon box.Photograph: YouTube/TruscoPass notesLife and styleThe human fridge: is this really the best way to cool down in a ‘cruelly hot’ climate?A ‘personal cooling booth’ that promises to chill you out in 10 minutes has launched in Japan, with its makers claiming it beats air-conditioning or a fanWed 22 Jul 2026 12.11 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 12.19 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleName:The human fridge.Age:Brand new.Appearance:A 2.1 metre-high (7ft) stainless-steel fridge for humans.Aren’t all fridges for humans?I guess that’s right, but this one is to put humans into.Oh, like a refrigerated mortuary cabinet?Yes, just like that, except you don’t have to be declared dead to use it.It still sounds grim.Don’t knock it until you have tried it. The Do Hiemon “personal cooling booth” has launched inJapanand promises to chill your body completely in just 10 minutes.How?You climb into it, it cools you down and then you leave.Why does this thing exist?Why do you think? The world is on fire. We have spent this summer being pummelled by heatwave after heatwave. Records have been broken across Europe, and this week Japan logged its first “cruelly hot” (above 40C) day of the year.What’s to be done?Well, we have two options. We can devote ourselves to achieving net zero as quickly as possible, because all the scientific evidence suggests that the longer we continue adding carbon dioxide to the atmosphere, the hotter the planet will become.And the other option?Build a big fridge and hop inside.It’s a compelling point.And a medically useful one too. The inside of the fridge is cooled to 15C, and a 5C jet of air is blown on to your head, neck, shoulders and back. The makers claim that it can ease the symptoms of heat exhaustion faster than air conditioning or a fan.It sounds expensive and wasteful though.The fridge is said to cost just 7p an hour to run, which is about three times less than a toaster. But then it does sell for about £6,900 (1.5m yen).I knew there would be a catch.But if you operate a warehouse or a factory where workers are routinely exposed to punishing heat, or you put one in a public place – like a vending machine for people who feel unwell – then it might save lives. Admit it, you want to try it.I’m still not sold. It’s such a big investment.I’lljust stick to using the budget option– hiding between the milk trolleys inthe supermarket.Your loss.Do say:“They have built a fridge for humans.”Don’t say:“Ooh, do a salad spinner for humans next.”Explore more on these topicsLife and stylePass notesJapanExtreme heatAsia PacificClimate crisisfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: The children who loved a killer | US crime | The Guardian
description: The Carr family took in Steven Judy, hoping to ‘save’ a troubled teen. They didn’t know he would become one of the most hated men in America
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The Carr family took in Steven Judy, hoping to ‘save’ a troubled teen. They didn’t know he would become one of the most hated men in America
Content: Traci Carr sits in her garage, where she keeps a box containing items left behind by her foster brother, Steven Judy, in Plainfield, Indiana. ‘People treated us like we had raised a murderer.’Photograph: Cheney OrrView image in fullscreenTraci Carr sits in her garage, where she keeps a box containing items left behind by her foster brother, Steven Judy, in Plainfield, Indiana. ‘People treated us like we had raised a murderer.’Photograph: Cheney OrrUS crimeThe children who loved a killerThe Carr family took in Steven Judy, hoping to ‘save’ a troubled teen. They didn’t know he would become one of the most hated men in AmericaMasha Hamilton with photographs by Cheney OrrWed 22 Jul 2026 04.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 16.21 EDTShareChildhood returns to Traci Carr in fragments.Night-time. She is five. She wakes to shadows on the wall, her room strange in the dark. Her footie pyjamas slip against the wood as she pads downstairs and climbs into bed next to her foster brother, Steven Judy.He scoots over, and the monsters recede. She burrows under the covers, feeling safe.Morning. Traci and Steve share Froot Loops, their favorite cereal, the milk turning pink. He teases her, tells funny stories, acts extra goofy to make her laugh.Later, they play hide and seek. He’s all theater, snapping a belt in the air as he searches for Traci and her three older siblings. They collapse in giggles when he finds them.Then one day, Steve disappears.No one explains where he went. Her parents are away for long stretches, too. When they are home, conversations drop into whispers. Doors click shut. The television is switched off when she enters the room.The year Traci turns seven, her parents take her on a car ride. At Conkle funeral home just outside Indianapolis, the room smells of carnations and furniture polish.She is led to the coffin at the end of the room. She peers over the edge.It’s Steve.double quotation markI think I knew Steve had killed somebody. But I still didn’t understand what had happened to himTraci CarrHis eyes are closed, his face composed. His hair isn’t right – a wig, she’ll learn later. But otherwise, he looks like himself.Her mother, Mary, leans close.Touch him, she urges.I don’t want to.Her mother insists.Don’t be afraid. Just touch him.Traci extends a single finger. It leaves a dent in his cold skin.Three days earlier, on 9 March 1981, the state of Indiana had executed Steven Judy. Traci would not fully grasp this for years.Decades on, she still carries the confusion of that day. It flickers across her face as we sit in her kitchen, a plastic storage bin between us filled with his belts, his wallet, his watch, letters, photographs: the small, stubborn evidence of a brother they once loved. A brother whose grave drew hate-filled handwritten notes decades after his death.For decades, Judy’s crimes have been revisited in books, documentaries and true-crime retellings. His atrocities have been replayed, his psychology dissected. But Traci and her siblings are missing from those narratives. The families of condemned people are among the least visible casualties of capital punishment, even as the death penalty is on the rise again.The Carr children loved and lost a foster brother the rest of the world called a monster. No one asked what it was like to be them. For decades, they were not ready to say.I was there the day Judy died.I was a young Associated Press journalist assigned to cover the family’s side of the story and had arrived in Michigan City on Sunday, the day before the execution.As reporters and photographers gathered outside the prison, I sat in a hotel room with the family. Over months of reporting, I had quietly slipped into their orbit. I wasn’t a friend, exactly, but I had become a witness to their unraveling at a moment when most people looked away.He hunted for his daughter’s killer for 40 years. Then he got a phone callRead moreEleven years earlier, a young man murdered my eighth-grade friend Cindy Winter in the Sonoran desert about a quarter of a mile from her home. We children had no language for this; neither did the grownups around us. After the first days of sorrow, we stopped speaking of Cindy altogether. Assigned to the Judy case, I did not yet understand that I was trying to report my way back into a story I already belonged to: violent death, bewildered survivors, and grief with nowhere to go.That day, Traci had stayed home with a babysitter. In the afternoon, I went swimming with the other Carr children. We had lunch, and then dinner, though no one ate much.Around 11pm, Judy’s foster father, Bob Carr, left for the prison. He had reluctantly agreed to Judy’s request that he witness the execution, which was scheduled shortly after midnight. By then, the kids were asleep, or appeared to be.Mary thumbed through Polaroids of Judy that she had brought with her, sometimes glancing at the clock beside the bed. She smoked the way some people pray, steadily, wordlessly, through the waiting.Shortly before 1am, Carr returned to our hotel with attorney Steve Harris. For Mary’s benefit, he described Judy’s final 71 minutes.View image in fullscreenSteve Harris, Steven Judy’s court-appointed attorney, sits in his office in Mooresville, Indiana, on 9 March 2026.A shaved head. A cigarette. A phone call to a girlfriend who had sent him a last-minute telegram – Judy joked that if she proposed marriage, he might ask for a stay. A final Polaroid taken for the Carrs, never made public until now. A Valium to calm Judy’s nerves before the end. Some tears, some jokes.At 12.04am, the guards blindfolded Judy and led him a few paces to the chair in the next room. Harris and Carr moved to the witness room.“I don’t hold no grudges. This was my doing. I’m sorry it happened,” Judy said from the chair before the switch was thrown.Mary soaked in every detail, hanging on every word, sometimes asking Harris to repeat himself. The ashtray beside her filled with cigarette stubs.Within the month, she admitted herself to a psychiatric hospital for a brief period, shattered by what had happened. I visited her there. Wearing a hospital gown, she seemed emptied of the force that had animated her for months – defending Judy, raging at the state, willing him somehow to survive. The fight had fled her body.I spent months with the Carrs, but I shed no tears for Judy. I did not mistake him for an innocent man. Still, I could not believe justice had been served by strapping him into a chair and killing him. The state had not restored his victims to life; it had only widened the circle of suffering. Our world, as I saw it, had room for outrage, punishment and fascination, but little space for certain kinds of grief.I did not yet know that this story would stay with me for the next 45 years.The facts of the case are these: on the morning of 28 April 1979, around 6.30am, Judy, 22, raped and murderedTerry Lee Chasteen, 21, along the banks of White Lick Creek outside Indianapolis.When her three children – ages two, four and five – cried, he threw them into the creek to drown.It wasn’t his first violent crime, but it was the one for which he would be executed.The four bodies were discovered later that morning by mushroom hunters. Residents called it the worst crime Morgan county had ever seen.A distinctive red and silver truck seen near the site of the killings was traced back to Bob Carr. He told police his foster son had borrowed it the night before. Judy was arrested the next day.View image in fullscreenA brick wall surrounds a tree that once marked the entrance to Central State hospital, where Steven Judy was a patient and met Tony Carr, whose family later took Judy in as a foster son, in Indianapolis, Indiana.Public outrage hardened quickly. Even before all the evidence was presented, a grassroots group called Protect the Innocent demanded the death penalty.At first, Judy said he couldn’t remember what happened. Later, he confessed. Harris, the attorney, initially pursued an insanity defense, but two court-appointed psychiatrists concluded Judy was legally sane.After the guilty verdict, Judy made his preference clear: death over life in prison. Determined to eliminate any possibility of leniency, he proposed leaping across the table to attack the prosecutor before sentencing.“You can’t do that,” Harris told him. “There are 50 officers in that courtroom.”Instead, Judy asked to address the jury. He told them he knew their names, where they lived, where they worked, and would kill anyone who failed to vote for the electric chair. Then he turned toward the bench. “That goes for you too, bud,” he said to the judge.The jury deliberated 20 minutes before recommending the death penalty.Forty-five years later, one of the people I most wanted to find was Harris. He had witnessed the crime’s aftermath and Judy’s final hours. I wondered what remained with him after all this time.Before Judy, Harris had handled three other murder cases. After the execution, he vowed never to take another death penalty case. “It was time-consuming and emotional,” he recalls now. “I just made the decision I would never do one again.” He represented only one other murder defendant, then gradually left criminal law altogether.He drove me to the bridge overlooking the stretch of White Lick Creek where the killings took place, about a mile and a half from his office. State Road 67 hums nearby now, busy and indifferent. Round the bend, the creek remains as secluded as it was then; teenagers still go there to hang out beyond the sight of their parents.A rope swing dangles beneath a railroad bridge where someone has painted: “THE DEVIL WAS HERE.” The water moves on.Steve saved Randy Carr’s life when Randy was two years old.Randy was playing in a mud puddle in the alley behind their house when a family friend known as Stinky climbed into Bob Carr’s truck and began backing it up, running over Randy’s legs. Steve saw the scene unfold and began yelling and waving his arms.View image in fullscreenRandy Carr, Steven Judy’s foster brother, stands outside his home in Indianapolis, Indiana.“He stopped him,” Randy says. “Another minute and Stinky would have run over my head.”After that, Steve became Randy’s person. “The only one who paid me any attention,” Randy recalls. Saturdays were their day. Together, they cleaned the basement, mowed the grass. When the chores were done, Steve took him for ice-cream. “Never failed,” Randy remembers.Steve entered the Carr family through a friendship with Randy’s Uncle Tony. On the day of the Chasteen murders, Randy, who was nine, and his brother Bobby, who was 11, helped Steve clean out the garage. They drove trash to the dump. They swept out their father’s pickup truck, the one Steve had been driving earlier that morning.Then they went for ice-cream.The next day, the police came. Steve was out with a friend, but they questioned the boys. They drove them back to the dump and asked where the trash had been thrown.double quotation markTo this day, I hate that [Steve] did that. Because he was everything I had in lifeRandy Carr“I wasn’t going to tell them,” Randy says. “I pointed everywhere except where we dumped.”Back at home, Randy went to the window with a pair of binoculars and watched as officers dusted the truck for fingerprints. He watched them wait for Steve to come back. “I didn’t grasp what was happening,” he says. “Dad never told us anything.”After Steve’s arrest, children at school avoided Randy. He began fighting. “I would get mad if anybody told me Steve was guilty of this. I couldn’t see it in my mind. Steve couldn’t do this.”Eventually, Steve told Randy himself.“You’ve gotta let it go,” Steve said. “I’m guilty of this. It’s something I can’t control.”He joked about the electric chair. “Man, I’m gonna ride that lightning.”Randy thought he meant an amusement park ride. “I still didn’t understand,” he says.View image in fullscreenGraffiti on a rail bridge above White Lick Creek overlooking the site where, in 1979, Steven Judy committed the crimes for which he was later executed, in Mooresville, Indiana.Years later, Randy came to believe Steve deserved to die. He says that plainly now. But then he adds, quietly, tearing up: “To this day, I hate that he did that. Because he was everything I had in life.”In the months before the execution, Randy started spiraling. He skipped school for the first time. He stole diet pills to get high. “I just felt like I needed to do something.” When his mother told Steve during a prison visit that Randy was acting out, Steve wrote Randy a seven-page letter about his own childhood, about how he had started screwing up.In a letter to Mary, Steve mentioned that he had written to Randy, and offered her some parenting advice: “Listen, Mary, I just wrote Randy that letter we talked about. It was hard, too. I don’t know if it was exactly what you wanted but I think it will help if he listens. But you have to understand if you keep telling someone not to do this or that, it sometimes pushes them to do it anyway for spite.”Randy read the letter “a thousand times” when he was a kid. He never skipped school again. “I loved Steve to death. I defended him for a long time. I broke a lot of jaws of people who didn’t really deserve it.”Randy begged Steve to appeal against the death penalty. “I cried with him for a long time. Finally Steve said: ‘Look at me. Don’t bring this up again. It’s over with. Don’t be sad, because this is what I need.’”Randy visited Steve the afternoon before his execution. “I was so upset. But Steve was laughing and carrying on like nothing was happening. It just blows my mind how he knows he’s going to his death, and he was just worried about how I was.”A few months after the funeral, Traci noticed the spine of a book jutting from a stack of papers on the kitchen countertop.Burn Judy Burn.Published between Judy’s conviction and his execution, the book was written by Bette Nunn, managing editor of the Reporter-Times in Martinsville, Indiana.Traci pulled it free and turned immediately to the photographs. One showed a small body just beneath the water’s surface. Another showed a little boy lying on a metal table, his shirt half pulled up from his torso.They were children, very small. She could tell they were close to her age.“I was starting to piece it together,” she told me. “I think I knew Steve had killed somebody. But I still didn’t understand what had happened tohim.”So she asked Randy.What happened to Steve?He told her what he could, as much as he understood.She couldn’t reconcile the brother she knew with the killer described in the book. Yet she kept returning to the photographs. “I had a morbid interest,” she says. The more she grasped, the more terrified she became. The house itself seemed altered. When she returned from school and found no one there, she wouldn’t go inside, pacing the sidewalk instead.She didn’t ask her parents any questions. She had already noticed the way her mother seemed always on the verge of tears, the way her parents argued in low, urgent voices. The whole house seemed stretched tight with things no one knew how to say aloud.My editor at the Associated Press asked me to begin reporting on the case in early March 1980, 10 months after the murders of Terry Chasteen and her three children. Judy would become the first person executed in Indiana since 1961 and among the first handful nationally after the supreme court reinstated the death penalty in 1976.I saw two possible ways into the story. I could try to reach Judy through his attorney, or I could try to understand the family waiting outside the prison walls.I called Mary Carr. She invited me over immediately.Mary worked as a drill operator at a manufacturing plant; Bob laid brick and stone for a living. Their house sat in a working-class neighborhood outside Indianapolis, small and crowded, every surface carrying its own accumulation of papers, toys, ashtrays, cups. Four children moved constantly through the rooms. Mary was not yet 31.Bob was warm but reserved. Mary directed the energy of the house. Within minutes, she had seated me in a padded brown chair and placed a photo album in my lap. She pointed to photographs of Steven Judy.“Look at him,” she said. “He’s handsome, isn’t he? He’s not cold and detached the way they say.”I felt myself recoil. I understood that I could never look at Judy the way Mary Carr did.When I was in eighth grade, my friend Cindy Winter was murdered in Tucson while riding her bicycle to a close friend’s house on a Sunday afternoon. Her body was found under a mesquite tree at the edge of a dry wash. The man who killed her was 24. He was arrested after he raped and killed another woman, leaving her two children alive beside the road.The similarities between his case and Judy’s were striking. Before Cindy’s murder, we thought the desert between houses belonged to us. Afterward, the fear never fully left.Meeting the Carrs conjured that dread back to life. After that first visit, I wrote in my journal: “Walking from my sturdy Scout to my apartment after midnight, the bushes seem to reach out as menacing men, and in the moonlight, I can see my fear.”I could never understand the killer. But I wanted to understand the family living in the aftermath of what he had done.I began to spend time at the Carrs’ kitchen table. I ate pizza with them, went swimming with the kids, phoned Mary to check in. Her warmth as a mother, I learned, was instinctive rather than structured. She wasn’t the kind of parent who tracked homework or studied report cards. What she offered instead was openness, noise, food, a place to belong. She believed that when her family took Steve in, they were finally giving him something no one else had: a loving family, a permanent place at the table.“She thought she could save him,” Traci says. And that was exactly who Mary Carr was, a woman who believed in second chances, in the redemptive power of love and a full table. She had been raised by a mother who withdrew support, but Mary had chosen a different path: she made room. She fed people. She believed damage could be repaired if you loved hard enough and long enough.This impulse would define the next eight years of her life, but Judy had arrived carrying damage the Carrs could not fully see.In court records and later interviews, Judy described his childhood as violent and unstable. Both parents drank heavily. His father beat his mother and once, in a fit of fury, butchered a pet dog, leaving pieces scattered on the porch for the children to see. His mother brought men home; sometimes the children witnessed them having sex. She occasionally summoned Judy to kiss her while she was bathing or undressing, placing him on her lap and running her fingers through his hair. Pornographic magazines, he said, were kept openly in the house.By age 10, he was pushing high school girls to the ground and molesting them. At 11, he began shoplifting; by 12, he was drinking. On 17 April 1970, at 13, he raped a 22-year-old woman and attacked her with a knife and a hatchet so severely that she required brain, abdominal and open-heart surgery to survive.double quotation markI know you can’t accept what I’m doing but I hope you don’t hate me for it eitherSteven JudyHe was detained and transferred to the juvenile section of Indiana’s Central State hospital. Opened in 1848 as the Indiana hospital for the “insane”, central state closed in 1994 amid scandals over patient treatment and funding shortfalls. The old pathology building, where autopsies were once performed in search of the physical causes of madness, now houses the Indiana Medical History Museum. Brains sit in glass jars, labeled with names, dates, symptoms.At Central State, Judy was diagnosed as a sexual psychopath. He became friends with Bob Carr’s younger brother, Tony, who also was a resident after getting in trouble at school and home. Through him, he met the rest of the family.When Judy was released at age 15, Mary and Bob agreed to take him in. As a juvenile, he needed to be released to adults; this must have felt like astonishing good fortune to him.He lived with the Carrs, ate at their table, and worked construction for Bob. But the violence continued: more assaults, more jail time, a robbery.The Carrs even paid his bond weeks before the Chasteen murders.Throughout, “Mom always loved Steve,” Traci recalls. “She never said anything bad about him, no matter what.”Prison life became a world of letters. Steve wrote and received hundreds of them.Mary kept many of them in a bedroom drawer: letters from strangers who prayed for him, or from women who had never met him but said they loved him.For Mary and Bob, he described his life in prison with flashes of humor and longing. Without his television set, he would “go bonkers”, Steve wrote. “So far I’ve earned $15,000 on the Joker’s Wild, gotten three dates on the Dating Game, and outdrawn Marshall Dillon twice.”“Tell the kids I said hi and I love them,” he wrote time and again. Sometimes he mentioned them by name: “You tell Traci I said hi and that I love her too and that I’ve got a get-rich-quick scheme for us. I’m going to write the cereal company and tell them me and her are going to quit eating cereal and put them out of business unless they pay us a million dollars (50 cents anyway, ha ha).”During prison visits, Steve offered Mary tips on staying safe and told her to pass them on to his foster sisters. Lock the car doors. Watch the blind spot. Don’t pull over if a man waves you down.After he was sentenced to death, Harris filed a routine appeal. But in October 1980, Judy told the court he did not want to pursue it, clearing the way for his execution six months later.Steve wrote to his foster parents soon after. “I know you can’t accept what I’m doing but I hope you don’t hate me for it either,” he told them. Mary had cried during a prison visit, and he had avoided telling her in front of the boys.View image in fullscreenThe last known photograph of Steven Judy, taken minutes before his execution, as he stands with his attorney, Steve Harris. It rests atop another Polaroid of Bob Carr’s mother, Rosita Mary Colvin (Judy considered her his foster grandmother), Mary Carr, Steven Judy, Bob Carr.“I’m sorry you had to find it out like you did,” he wrote. “I’m not going to get into my reasons and feelings on why I wanted it like this because I think it would be better to tell you here where we can look at each other’s faces.”Steve was determined to carry through with the death penalty, but there were moments of fear. Right after he was transferred to death row in Michigan City, he wrote: “Mary, don’t ever let anyone tell you this isn’t bad, because it is, and I’m scared to death.”Later he wrote: “I’m worried about how the kids are taking it all. There’s just so much on my mind that can’t ever be put into words.”On 5 Febuary 1981, 32 days before the execution, he apologized for not writing. “I just let myself get so far down in the dumps that it was hard to do anything.” He warned Mary that people might try to persuade her to intervene. “I know a few of them might try and get you to interfere but I hope you don’t,” he wrote. “About the only thing you could possibly do is cause a short delay and that would only hurt me.” Then, as he often did, he turned back to the children: “Tell me, how are the kids doing? I really hope they’re taking all this okay. Tell them I said hello and I send my love.”Steve considered Bob’s mother, Rosita Mary Colvin, his foster grandmother. He affectionately called her “the old lady” and asked about her in letters. She wrote him shortly before his execution: “I have loved you from the first time I saw you at Central, your blond hair flying around, you and Tony playing basketball. You was a beautiful child. If you had been my own son, I could not have loved you more. I’m so sorry, Steve. I don’t know what went wrong. I just want you to know you are loved by all of us and our lives will never be the same.”His last letter was written to his attorney. Judy told Harris that he had committed about 50 robberies and burglaries and 15 rapes. He confessed directly to one additional murder, indicated there was a second, and said he’d left women tied up so there might be others. He promised to list all his crimes in a letter to be opened after his death.double quotation markIt was kind of scary that he could be so dangerous and actually a likable person. He had a sense of humorSteve HarrisThat last day, he gave Harris a thick sealed envelope.Police knew this list of confessions was coming and they were waiting. Immediately after the execution, Harris opened the envelope.In front of him was one page of writing followed by seven blank sheets.When I visited Harris this year, he spread the letter on his desk so I could read it: “I’m sorry Steve [Harris] but I’ve decided to handle it this way because I care too much for my foster mom and family. I hope you can understand. Thank you for all you’ve done for me. Steve.”In his final photo, Judy stands next to Harris in the holding cell, minutes before the execution. His head is shaved.
