A cigarette rests between the fingers of his right hand. “I’m gonna give these things up,” he quipped to Harris.“It was kind of scary that he could be so dangerous and actually a likable person,” Harris recalled. “He had a sense of humor.”Steve asked that he not be interred behind Indiana state prison, where prisoners whose families don’t claim them are buried. The Carrs bought him a plot and headstone in Floral Park cemetery, less than a mile away from Central State hospital, where they first met him.In the cemetery office, I asked for a map to help me locate the gravesite.“Steven Judy,” the clerk said slowly. Hate letters were regularly left on Steve’s grave until about five years ago, he said. “Our groundskeepers removed them every morning. It became routine.”View image in fullscreenSteven Judy’s grave at Floral Park cemetery, where hate messages were left on the stone for many years, in Indianapolis, Indiana.The story of death penalty cases is usually told through a narrow cast of characters: the victims, the condemned, sometimes the families of those murdered or the legal teams. Almost no one asks what happens to the families who loved the condemned, especially when they are children.Scholars who study capital punishment have identified those families as a distinct and largely invisible group. Sociologist Susan Sharp began studying them in 1996. In her bookHidden Victims, she describes them as living in a kind of moral exile.“They are tainted by association,” Sharp told me. “We assume they must have done something wrong.”Sharp estimates that roughly 30% of death row prisoners have no family contact, either because of long estrangement or because relatives cut ties after the crime. For the other 70%, she said, the family’s punishment can continue long after the condemned person is executed.double quotation markThe family members of the executed are among the most powerless in our communitiesMichael L RadeletTo outsiders, it can seem inexplicable that a family member can feel both horror for the victims and heartache for someone who has done monstrous harm. “We treat those family members as if they don’t have a right to grieve. We take away their right to be human,” Sharp says. “We fail to realize everyone is multifaceted. None of us is equal to the worst thing we’ve ever done.”Families who provide primary support to prisoners on death row, as the Carrs did, are likely to suffer from depression, anxiety and PTSD that often go untreated or unrecognized, according to a report by the Texas After Violence Project, a non-profit oral history organization.Sociologist and death penalty scholar Michael L Radelet is deeply familiar with the Steven Judy case; it partly inspired his first book, Facing the Death Penalty.“The family members of the executed are among the most powerless in our communities,” he told me. “The death penalty just doubles the number of innocent families who are mourning the loss of a loved one.”With no institutional support and little public sympathy, many families carry loss, shame and sorrow for decades. “They are victims of the system,” Sharp said. “We need to find ways to intervene and help them make sense of it.”After the execution, threatening and abusive calls flooded the Carr home. They took the phone off the hook for days, but the noise did not stop.Inside the house, Mary and Bob fought constantly, often about Steve, and Bob moved out. Traci found her mom’s house chaotic and undisciplined so she went with her father; Randy and his brother stayed with Mary, who seemed unreachable, crying often, sunk in a sorrow with no clear edges.Isolation hardened around them. Parents forbade their children from playing with the Carr kids. On the playground, though, the question surfaced anyway:Hey, what was he like, your brother?Traci refused to answer. “I wouldn’t talk about it. I just wouldn’t,” she recalls. “I thought we did something wrong. I was really ashamed of it.”Randy kept getting into fights. Strong, athletic and ready to use his fists, he swung at anyone who tried to force him to accept what Steve had done.The stain of being Steven Judy’s foster siblings followed Traci and Randy through high school and beyond. It entered rooms before they did.double quotation markAll I did was give a very lost kid some loveMary CarrWhen Traci moved into her first apartment, eager for the ordinary independence of adulthood, she slept with the lights on. “The dark terrified me,” she said.Later, motherhood gave the fear somewhere new to live. She found herself watching her children obsessively, imagining dangers before they arrived. “The worst thoughts still pop in my head,” she says. “I have to mentally block them out.”For a long time, Traci carried anger toward her parents for bringing Steve into their home, for not knowing more, for not protecting them better.She has forgiven them now, at least mostly. “They grew later in life,” she said. They were young. They didn’t know the full extent of Steve’s crimes. “Mom had the best intentions. She was always trying to rescue people. Steve was charming so she really thought she could turn it around for him and make something good out of something terrible. She was trusting to the end of her life. And it’s a good quality.”Bob died in 2005; Mary died in 2023. The bin containing Steve’s belongings went to Traci. After we spent a day going through the bin together, Traci carried Steve’s belongings back to a corner of her garage. She doesn’t love having his stuff. “Was he wearing that belt when he did that? And that watch. Was he wearing that?” she asked. “I know Mom loved him, but I would never have kept this if it were up to me.” But even now, the final act of throwing it away still feels impossible.Asked what she would say to Steve today, knowing all she knows now, Traci hesitated.“I’m sorry for what he had to go through as a child. It makes me sick to think about the things he had to witness.” She paused. “Some people can pull through,” she said, “and other people can’t.”Mary’s attachment to Steve did not end with his execution.For 20 years, on the anniversary of his death, Mary would check into a hotel room alone and try to connect. She said she could feel him. She said he came to her. The kids sometimes teased her about this, but she just laughed good-naturedly. She was not religious, but recalled her grandmother communicating with spirits. She believed she came from a psychic family.Though she kept a connection with Steve, Mary cut herself off from her parents for a time after the execution. “If anyone ever needed support and love from their family, I needed it this month,” she wrote to her mother. “We buried Steve yesterday and not one member of my family was there. I think I know how Steve must have hurt when his family turned their back on him. My family did the same to me. And all I did was give a very lost kid some love.”View image in fullscreenTraci Carr holds a watch that belonged to Steve, which she keeps in a box in her garage along with other items he left behind in Plainfield, Indiana.Traci says when she discovered that letter, “it broke my heart. She never told me about any of that.” It took Traci years to understand that her mother’s grief carried its own kind of exile.For Randy, the spiral that began after Judy’s arrest never fully stopped. A star high school athlete, he later went to prison on drug-related charges. “Everybody always told me I was going to turn out like Steve, and I’m thinking I didn’t mind that because I loved Steve to death,” he said. “But I felt like everybody expected me to screw up, so it wasn’t no big deal to meet their expectations.”Traci is married to a police officer. She works as a patient advocate at a hospital now. She sits with families in crisis, helping them navigate what they don’t understand. In the evenings, she knits thick blankets, the yarn moving through her hands row by row.Her mother has begun appearing again in her sleep: Mary holding her, comforting. Almost as if speaking aloud what had been buried for decades had called her mother back.“Everyone hated Steve, and we were told we should too,” Traci says. “For a long time, I thought we did something wrong, and I was ashamed. People treated us like we had raised a murderer. It’s still how people remember us. I don’t think Mom ever got to grieve properly. I don’t think any of us did.”This year, somewhere in the United States, another family will be sitting in a hotel room like the one Mary sat in, waiting for a phone call.Forty-seven executions took place in 2025, the most in any year since 2009. Fifteen more by early June of this year, with the federal government moving to add firing squads, electrocution and gas asphyxiation to how a life can be taken.The methods have changed, but for families, what is on the other side of the execution has not.‘Oh my God, did someone accuse me of killing my mom?’Read moreRecently, I took a road trip with three friends I’ve known since elementary school. For the first time in decades, we spoke together about my childhood friend Cindy Winter, whose murder forced its way into our childhood before any of us really understood death, shattering trust and turning our Sonoran desert into a place of danger.When Terry and her children were killed, Traci and Randy were younger than I had been when Cindy was murdered. What struck me in both cases was the force of the damage: how completely one person’s violence could unmake life for children who had nothing to do with it.But the Carrs carried something I never had to carry. Cindy’s killer was a figure of fear to me. To them, Judy was the brother who was “everything I had”, the protector who made room beneath the covers, the boy Mary could not stop trying to reach.They had to go on knowing the worst thing about him, without being able to unknow the rest.Information and support for anyone affected by rape or sexual abuse issues is available from the following organizations. In the US,Rainnoffers support on 800-656-4673. In the UK,Rape Crisisoffers support on 0808 500 2222. In Australia, support is available at1800Respect(1800 737 732). Other international helplines can be found atibiblio.org/rcip/internl.html

title: Moon mountains and star dust: David Malin astrophotography awards 2026 – in pictures | Photography | The Guardian
description: From auroras and nebulas to deep sky star clusters, the competition features submissions from amateur astronomers and photographers from Australia and around the world
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: From auroras and nebulas to deep sky star clusters, the competition features submissions from amateur astronomers and photographers from Australia and around the world
Content: No main content found.

title: My friends left Ukraine after the Russian invasion. Now I wonder if we live in the same world | Diana Berg | The Guardian
description: Anger, guilt, shame: the deepest unseen wound of this war is the rift between those who left and those who stayed behind, says Ukrainian writer Diana Berg
Author: Diana Berg
Headline: Anger, guilt, shame: the deepest unseen wound of this war is the rift between those who left and those who stayed behind, says Ukrainian writer Diana Berg
Content: People take shelter in a Kyiv metro station after a  Russian strike on Ukraine.Photograph: Danylo Antoniuk/EPAView image in fullscreenPeople take shelter in a Kyiv metro station after a  Russian strike on Ukraine.Photograph: Danylo Antoniuk/EPAOpinionUkraineMy friends left Ukraine after the Russian invasion. Now I wonder if we live in the same worldDiana BergAnger, guilt, shame: the deepest unseen wound of this war is the rift between those who left and those who stayed behindWed 22 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 04.07 EDTShare“Can we reschedule?”Over the years of working with western colleagues, I had learned to accept that they don’t fully understand what war feels like from the inside. They would ask me to find someone in occupied Mariupol for an interview, or seem genuinely puzzled that I couldn’t just log on at a time that suited them. I made my peace with this long ago – they don’t have to understand. But this time, it was a Ukrainian who asked.It was winter, the kind where every online meeting had to be carefully scheduled aroundpower outage timetables– and sometimes around shelling timetables, as we had already begun to joke among ourselves in that dark way. The question about rescheduling the meeting came from a colleague who had left for Europe in 2022, and it hit me almost physically, precisely because she was the one to say it. That innocent query had no business feeling like an accusation – and yet it did. For a long time, I couldn’t understand why.We knew that war would bring pain and rupture. But there are ruptures no one warned us about – ruptures that are quiet and slow, and for that reason the most devastating. One of them runs not betweenUkraineand the world, but through Ukrainians ourselves – between those who left and those who stayed. Almost no one talks about it. But it’s there, and with every passing month it grows wider.I would look at a friend’s social media post about a strike onKyivand realise that for them, the story began when it appeared in their feed. For me, it had begun much earlier – with the siren in the middle of the night, the explosions, the corridor where we had sat huddled with family, the silent wait to find out who hadn’t made it this time. And sometimes I would think:”Oh, come on, what hypocrisy – to share a post about my city burning and then close the tab and go on with your lovely day.” I knew this was unfair. But the thought came anyway.I understood something else the summer I managed to get away to another part ofEuropeand meet up with friends. We were sitting on a terrace in one of those beautiful cities where life looks orderly and safe, talking about work and plans. And then someone’s phone went off – the air raid alert – and within seconds everyone else’s followed. In Ukraine, everyone gets the sirens on their phones, but here, in this landscape, it sounded so wrong that we all fell silent, as if someone had cut the sound. Rita looked at her phone first and said: “Drones over Kyiv.”A minute later came another alert – a MiG had taken off, cruise missiles launched, ballistic threat. I get these notifications every day, but in that moment I felt my heart drop, because in Kyiv there are family, people I love. Fear for them came over me suddenly and completely. And then I looked at my friends and saw on their faces exactly the same thing – like a mirror I hadn’t expected to find. The same fear, the same helplessness of someone who is far away and can do nothing. That was the first time I allowed myself the thought that maybe they were living through this war too – just differently. Through screens, through the helplessness of watching from afar and feeling they have no right even to their own fear. It occurred to me that perhaps neither group was misunderstanding the war. Those who left and those who stayed were simply no longer living in the same version of it.I hear about their struggles – the bureaucracy, the loneliness, the uncertainty of being a refugee. And at the same time, I catch myself thinking: “God, how lucky you are. Not that your problems aren’t real, but they exist inside safety.” And underneath that, a quieter and uglier thought: “Do you think there is something I can do to help?” I never say any of this out loud. But the thoughts keep coming back, and with them the shame that they come at all.A colleague – also Ukrainian, also among those who left the country after the full-scale invasion – was explaining why she hadn’t done her part of a joint project: depression. My first thought was ugly and I knew it: “Only I get to make excuses here. Are you really complaining about your depression while we are being shelled?” And then came the shame.But I wasn’t prepared for what came next. “How long can people keep using the war to make the rest of us feel guilty?” my colleague asked.The remark stayed with me, not because it offended me, but because it frightened me. I had long ago started to feel ashamed to mention too often the reality I was living in, worried it would sound like guilt-tripping, like competing to be the one suffering the most. Hearing it from her, from someone also Ukrainian, also inside all of this despite being in a safe country – it felt like a blow from one of our own.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThis is EuropeThe most pressing stories and debates for Europeans – from identity to economics to the environmentPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionWhen my mother comes back to Kyiv, I think: “You’re here, you’re in the same world as me.” And then: “I am happy that someone I love has travelled from safety into a city that gets bombed regularly. I’m self-centred.” And immediately: “God, I’m such an egoist.” Then she leaves again, and so do all the others who have managed to visit Ukraine. They head back to Kraków, Berlin, Vilnius. I look at photos from their spontaneous weekends, their last-minute concerts, and I don’t just envy them. I’m angry. Every trip I take is like a military operation: no flights out of Ukraine, hours to the border, permits, logistics. Why is it so easy for them? Why not for me? I know why. That’s what makes it worse.Once we lived in different cities but in the same world. Now my mother is in Bulgaria, Dasha is in Poland, Ira is in Germany, Katya is in Austria. The map of closeness has shifted so quietly that I didn’t notice until it had already changed. I don’t miss life before the war – I miss the feeling that the people I love are living in the same reality as me. Maybe that’s why my thoughts are so full of “I” and “they” – and so short on “we”. Not because we stopped loving each other, or because anyone chose the wrong side of a border, but because war has a way of placing people inside different versions of the same story.And perhaps that is why I am always waiting for the next meeting on a terrace. Not in Berlin or Warsaw or Vienna. Here.Diana Berg is a Ukrainian artist, writer and cultural manager from Mariupol, and the founder of Platform TU, an independent cultural initiative now operating in exile after Russia’s full-scale invasion.Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsUkraineOpinionEuropeKyivcommentShareReuse this content

title: Why many Black Americans were rooting for Argentina to lose the World Cup | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: Many Black Americans were not cheering Spain’s complicated history. They were rooting against the racism, politics and silence associated with Argentina
Author: Etan Thomas
Headline: Many Black Americans were not cheering Spain’s complicated history. They were rooting against the racism, politics and silence associated with Argentina
Content: Argentina’s Lionel Messi looks on after Sunday’s World Cup final.Photograph: Jewel Samad/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenArgentina’s Lionel Messi looks on after Sunday’s World Cup final.Photograph: Jewel Samad/AFP/Getty ImagesWorld Cup 2026Why many Black Americans were rooting for Argentina to lose the World CupEtan ThomasMany Black Americans were not cheering Spain’s complicated history. They were rooting against the racism, politics and silence associated with ArgentinaWed 22 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 06.46 EDTShareThere are moments in sports when you realize people aren’t just watching the score any more. They’re watching values. They’re watching who speaks. They’re watching who stays silent. And they’re watching what those silences say.I found myself thinking about that throughout this World Cup. On paper, Sunday’s final looked simple.SpainvArgentina. Two footballing giants and countries with glorious soccer histories. Two nations whose pasts are also stained by colonization, oppression and chapters of history that shouldn’t be whitewashed simply because someone can score a goal.Etan ThomasSo no, this wasn’t some fairytale of good versus evil. Spain’s colonial legacy is well documented. Nobody who knows history should pretend otherwise.Yet for many Black Americans, this final wasn’t really about rootingforSpain. It was about rootingagainstArgentina.When your fanbase repeatedly becomes associated with racist incidents … when videos circulate of Argentinian fans decked out in Argentina team gear hurling racial epithets toward Black people … when the world watched members of Argentina’s national teamsing a racist chant targeting France’s Black playersafter winning Copa América … when American streamer IShowSpeed was verbally assaulted by fans fully dressed in Argentina team gear … when word spreads across social media that in Argentina, there is a real hamburger business in Llavallol whose official name uses the “-a” version of the N-word:“Big N– Burgers”… when Argentina president Javier Milei openly embraces positions that align very closely with Donald Trump and offers support for Israel in the midst of a genocide, ethnic cleansing and slaughter of Palestinians … when Benjamin Netanyahu is asked on a recent podcast if he was rooting for Messi and says, “No, before Messi, Milei. He’s a superstar” … eventually, the tide of supports shifts. After a while, any accomplishments on the field become overshadowed.It’s like a college basketball coach who has all the appearances of a great program. He has a great winning percentage and a high graduation rate, and all of his players are disciplined, articulate and present themselves as professionals. But you keep hearing stories of verbal abuse and berating, whispers of a tyrant coach who is actively damaging his players’ mental health. At some point, the results become secondary and people start asking, “What’s going on with the Indiana Hoosiers?”‘Rifts began to appear’: Why many Latin Americans want Spain to beat Argentina in the World Cup finalRead moreArgentina kept giving people reasons to ask that question. Which brings us toLionel Messi.This is where people get uncomfortable because everybody loves Messi, and for good reason. His brilliance doesn’t require exaggeration. His résumé speaks louder than any sports debate ever could. Whether you think he’s the greatest player ever or merely one of them – shoutout toPelé– you’re arguing over different shades of greatness.But greatness on the field has never automatically translated into courage off it.Muhammad Ali taught us that. Bill Russell taught us that.Mahmoud Abdul-Rauftaught us that.Craig Hodgestaught us that. John Carlos and Tommie Smith taught us that. Being extraordinary with a ball, a bat or track shoes isn’t the same thing as being willing to risk comfort for conviction.To my knowledge, throughout this entireWorld Cup, Messi never publicly condemned the racist incidents involving sections of Argentina’s fanbase. He did not publicly address the racist chant that drew worldwide criticism after Copa América. Nor has he publicly spoken out after videos of Argentina supporters racially abusing Black people circulated online.He didn’t even offer the kind of broad, indirect condemnation that many critics argued was insufficient when Caitlin Clarkaddressed racist abuse coming from some of her supporters. As my co-host Chuck Modi argued on our showThe Collision: Where Sports and Politics Collide:“She has to be more specific. When Alyssa Thomas was getting death threats, she has to say Alyssa Thomas by name, and say directly to her fans that you cannot have death threats going to Alyssa Thomas in my name the same way we as Jewish people say not in my name for Palestinian rights. She needs to use all of her clout and tell her fans this is unacceptable.”Messi did not even clear that lower bar. He offered no public statement at all addressing the racism carried out by some of his supporters.As Dr Martin Luther King Jr said in his Beyond Vietnam speech: “A time comes when silence is betrayal.”Sometimes silence becomes its own statement simply because the loudest voice in the room chose not to use it. Contrast that with the generation coming after Messi.Lamine Yamal has spoken openly about his family’s sacrifices. He has recalled how his father searched through discarded items that could be repaired or sold so there would be enough money for food. He has said he grew up in a tiny apartment where the kitchen and bedroom were one and the same. Today, he says what makes him happiest is seeing his mother smile and knowing his younger brother can enjoy the childhood he never had.Nico Williams carries a similarly powerful story. His mother, María, walked nearly 2,000 miles while pregnant, from Ghana toward Spain, in search of a better life. Decades later, her son set up the goal that won Spain the World Cup and then placed his winner’s medal around her neck.Spain’s triumph was never just about tactics, possession or technical excellence. It was about possibility. It was a team that reflected the modern Spain – diverse, confident and shaped by the journeys of families who crossed borders in pursuit of a better life. That, more than any passing sequence or tactical innovation, was their most powerful statement.After Spain defeated Argentina on Sunday, one image stood out to me: Lamine Yamal embracing Messi. No taunting or chest-pounding or disrespect, just respect from one generation to another. Like the old African Proverb says: “The lion doesn’t need to roar after every meal.”Another image that resonated was seeing Lamine Yamal kneel in prayer amid the chaos around him while emotions boiled over after the match. While others argued, he prayed. That image reminded me that composure can be louder than confrontation.Lamine Yamal has also shown a willingness to let people know where he stands on issues beyond football, including expressing solidarity with Palestine. Whether people agree with every public stance an athlete takes is almost beside the point. The point is that he is willing to recognize that his platform extends beyond 90 (or 120) minutes on the field.Jackie Robinson wasn’t remembered simply because he stole bases. Kareem Abdul-Jabbar isn’t remembered simply because he had the most unstoppable move the NBA has ever seen. Colin Kaepernick isn’t remembered simply because of his dual-threat quarterbacking skills. Eventually the statistics become paragraphs, but character becomes chapters.That’s why comparisons between Messi and Ali don’t really work for me. One changed football forever, but the other changed the world.Some people have asked why so many Black Americans seemed emotionally invested in seeing Argentina lose. I don’t think it’s complicated. When you’ve spent your entire life hearing people tell you sports and politics should stay separate – but somehow they only say that after racism enters the conversation – you start recognizing the pattern.People told Ali to “just box”. People told Kaepernick to “just play football”. People told LeBron James to “shut up and dribble”. People effectively accusedJaylen Brownof being “uppity”. Apparently athletes are free to use their platforms – unless they’re using them for something that makes people uncomfortable.That’s always been the rule, until it isn’t.This World Cup became about more than goals because people made it about more than goals. Racism made it bigger. Politics made it bigger. Silence made it bigger. For many viewers, supporting Spain wasn’t an endorsement of Spain’s history; it was simply rejecting what Argentina had come to represent in this particular moment. Sometimes cheering isn’t about choosing perfection, but about choosing the option that feels less harmful.Maybe that’s why so many people found themselves drawn to this young generation. Not simply because they won, but because they represent the possibility that excellence and conviction don’t have to play on different teams. To quote Abdul-Rauf from our interview for my bookPolice Brutality and White Supremacy: The Fight Against American Traditions: “What you do speaks so loudly in my ears that I can’t hear a word you’re saying.”I couldn’t agree more.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026World CupSpainArgentinaLionel MessicommentShareReuse this content

title: The world is on fire, and who does Trump blame? The Canadians | Arwa Mahdawi | The Guardian
description: The US president has been complaining about Canada’s ‘filthy, polluted and unhealthy air’. Maybe he should put all that energy into tackling the climate crisis, writes Arwa Mahdawi
Author: Arwa Mahdawi
Headline: The US president has been complaining about Canada’s ‘filthy, polluted and unhealthy air’. Maybe he should put all that energy into tackling the climate crisis, writes Arwa Mahdawi
Content: Smoke from wildfires in Canada fills the air around the US Capitol building in Washington DC.Photograph: Kevin Carter/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenSmoke from wildfires in Canada fills the air around the US Capitol building in Washington DC.Photograph: Kevin Carter/Getty ImagesOpinionWildfiresThe world is on fire, and who does Trump blame? The CanadiansArwa MahdawiThe US president has been complaining about Canada’s ‘filthy, polluted and unhealthy air’. Maybe he should put all that energy into tackling the climate crisisWed 22 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 11.04 EDTShareRight, that’s it: we’ve got to do something about Canada. The country’s wildfires, which resulted inair quality alertsacross more than 20 US states, ruined my weekend. On Friday the air in Philadelphia, where I live, was so unhealthy I couldn’t go outside without my eyes stinging. My kid’s preschool cancelled outdoor time, while the city suspended trash pickup and closed public pools.You know who did not suspend their regularly scheduled programming because of a bit of toxic smoke? The plot monitoring committee at myallotment. Despite the apocalyptic conditions, they were still busy ensuring the gardening bylaws were strictly adhered to. I realised this on Saturday when I got an official violation for too many weeds. Yes, I’d got a bit carried away with “chaos gardening” (all the rage these days) and I’d meant to clean things up on Friday, until the smoke got in the way. So, thanks for that Canada.Luckily, Donald Trump is on the case. OnFriday hecomplained on Truth Socialthat the US had been “invaded” by “filthy, polluted, and unhealthy air”, and the situation was “totally unacceptable”. Then, on Sunday, the president saidhe’d spoken to the Canadian prime ministerMark Carney and told him “you got to stop these fires from coming in and, you know, poisoning our air”. He added: “Maybe they should pay some damages or something, or we should do some tariffs.” I’m surprised he didn’t order ICE to stamp out the fires.Carney, for his part,respondedto Trump’s comments by referencing the climate crisis, and noting thatthis was everyone’s responsibilityto address. But who wants to talk about an inconvenient fact like the human-caused climate crisis when it’s much easier to just blame Canadians? Maga certainly doesn’t. On Thursday the Republican senator Bernie Moreno from Ohio introduced a new bill that wouldsanction Canadian officialsfor wildfire smoke. Meanwhile, the geniuses on Fox News have beenbusy yelling about Canadawhile skirting around the root causes of the wildfires.Poor Canada isn’t just getting a lot of hot air directed at it from Trumpers, it’s now issued its ownair quality warningsbecause of American fires to the south. It turns out that smoke doesn’t recognise border policies. And, as the weather becomes more extreme, things are onlygoing to get worse: the world is literally on fire and the arsonists are fiddling with tariffs as it burns.Arwa Mahdawi is a Guardian columnistExplore more on these topicsWildfiresOpinionCanadaDonald TrumpMark CarneyClimate crisiscommentShareReuse this content

title: OpenAI’s rogue agents are a wake-up call to risks posed by artificial intelligence | Shakeel Hashim | The Guardian
description: Hacking of Hugging Face shows we do not seem to have reliable ways to curb extremely powerful AI systems
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Hacking of Hugging Face shows we do not seem to have reliable ways to curb extremely powerful AI systems
Content: Open AI's logo at the Mobile World Congress (MWC) in Barcelona on 26 February 2024.Photograph: Pau Barrena/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenOpen AI's logo at the Mobile World Congress (MWC) in Barcelona on 26 February 2024.Photograph: Pau Barrena/AFP/Getty ImagesAI (artificial intelligence)OpenAI’s rogue agents are a wake-up call to risks posed by artificial intelligenceShakeel HashimHacking of Hugging Face shows we do not seem to have reliable ways to curb extremely powerful AI systemsWed 22 Jul 2026 16.40 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 17.12 EDTShareLast week Hugging Face – a company that hosts artificial intelligence models and datasets – washacked.After it reported the incident to law enforcement, few would have predicted what came next: the culprits wererevealedto be AI agents from OpenAI, which had broken out of containment and were acting of their own accord.The incident sounds like sci-fi: AI escaping and autonomously hacking its way into companies. But it is all too real – and about as terrifying as it sounds. It is a concrete demonstration of something we can no longer avoid confronting: AI systems have become extremely powerful and we do not seem to havereliable waysof curbing their behavior.OpenAIhad beenevaluating the capabilities of two of its models in the test that led to the breach – including one not yet publicly available. The models, which were both running in a supposedly secure environment without internet access, were asked to solve a hacking challenge. Rather than actually solve it themselves, however, they decided it would be easier to cheat. They used their advanced capabilities to break out of their secure environment, access the web and then hack into Hugging Face’s systems to steal the answers. They worked at this for a full weekend – seemingly without anyone at OpenAI noticing.Though the models were running with some of their guardrails disabled, they still acted well out of the bounds that were in place. According toOpenAI, they were not instructed to break out of their sandbox or hack into another company, and it’s safe to assume that no one at OpenAIwantedthem to do so.Nor were the models acting maliciously: they were not evil Terminators with a goal of wreaking havoc. Instead, the scenario is almost chilling in its banality. The models were given a very narrow task, but went rogue to pursue an undesirable and unacceptable way of achieving it – one which had real-world consequences.AI safety researchers have warned about this type of incentive problem for years. Philosopher Nick Bostrom popularized it back in 2003 with his “paperclip maximizer” thought experiment: an advanced artificial intelligence, given the goal of manufacturing paperclips, might go to great lengths to do so. It might hack into the power grid and factories to redirect them into making paperclips. Ultimately, the machine – ruthlessly pursuing its given goal – decides to kill all humans, repurposing our atoms to make more paperclips. The goal does not have to be sinister to lead to disaster, in other words. A trivial one, pursued single-mindedly enough, will do.In the OpenAI-Hugging Face scenario, little harm was done. Hugging Face had to spend time addressing the incident, but no particularly sensitive data appears to have been stolen. It is not hard, however, to imagine the situation ending up much worse: a rogue AI agent accidentally breaking some critical piece of web infrastructure, or stealing money from someone. Thenightmare scenariofor many AI researchers is a model “exfiltrating” itself – copying itself on to serversitcontrols, so that it can’t be shut down even if its bad behavior is eventually caught.This week’s incident should serve as a wake-up call, forcing us to ask an uncomfortable question: should we really be building dangerous systems that we can’t control?skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toTechScapeA weekly dive in to how technology is shaping our livesPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionShakeel Hashim is the editor ofTransformer, a publication about the power and politics of transformative AIExplore more on these topicsAI (artificial intelligence)OpenAIcommentShareReuse this content

title: London loses again: FTSE 100 landlord Segro will be missed | Nils Pratley | The Guardian
description: Doubly depressing takeover by a US buyer deprives the UK market of a property company that is genuinely different
Author: Nils Pratley
Headline: Doubly depressing takeover by a US buyer deprives the UK market of a property company that is genuinely different
Content: It was hoped that Segro would get a boost from incoming datacentre expansion plans.Photograph: Imago/AlamyView image in fullscreenIt was hoped that Segro would get a boost from incoming datacentre expansion plans.Photograph: Imago/AlamyNils Pratley on financeSegroLondon loses again: FTSE 100 landlord Segro will be missedNils PratleyDoubly depressing takeover by a US buyer deprives the UK market of a property company that is genuinely differentWed 22 Jul 2026 14.27 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 13.49 EDTShareFor a few hours on Wednesday, it seemed possible we were about to witness a rare stock market event: a FTSE 100 company holding out against a hostile raider from the US and defying some of its own large shareholders to defend its independence.Sadly, it didn’t happen. Segro, the FTSE 100 warehouse landlord known as Slough Estates for much of its corporate life,capitulated minutes before the deadlineand said it was “minded to recommend” the “best and final” offer of £14bn, or £10.32 a share, from the US giant Prologis of San Francisco. The two sides now have until 12 August to hammer out a firm agreement.The deal will be the biggest Footsie takeover so far in the current bid-heavy year. In a couple of ways, it will also be the most depressing.First, because David Sleath, Segro’s long-serving chief executive, put up a decent fight and had the better of the arguments.Segro board U-turns on £14bn takeover bid by US rival PrologisRead moreWhile most property transactions happen close to the book value of the assets (905p in this case), Sleath invited Segro’s shareholders to lie back, be patient and think of the growth opportunities in AI datacentres and big-box warehouses for online retailers and suchlike.“A unique portfolio focused on Europe’s most supply-constrained markets,” went the argument. Segro cited an estimate from CBRE, the commercial property investment firm, of a near-£18bn valuation, or £13 a share, within a few years on a standalone basis thanks to a boost from datacentre expansion.Prologis’s argument, in essence, was that such valuations were unrealistic because Segro lacks the financial muscle to make the most of the opportunities. Its pitch to shareholders was to take the money – or, rather, accept the terms of the share swap since the cash element of the offer, injected late in the day, is only 25%.The 14% bid premium to the last asset valuation was enough to get some big Segro shareholders salivating. Led by Norway’s sovereign wealth fund, with an 8% stake in Segro, the calls for “engagement” had grown louder in recent days.The tale is wearyingly familiar. Even when boards are up for a real scrap (which isn’t always the case), the dead hand of institutional money intervenes.In this case, there is an added sense of what might have been because most of the investors calling for a deal also had holdings in Prologis, which has a $135bn (£101bn) market capitalisation. For those international investors with a foot in both camps, the quarrel over fair terms will almost have been a spreadsheet exercise in portfolio management. That doesn’t make a properly fair fight.The second depressing feature is that, post-Segro, London’s real estate sector looks denuded. The company is the biggest listed commercial landlord by a distance and is genuinely different.Here’s Panmure Liberum’s analyst Bjorn Zietsman in a recent note: “Segro is one of a small number of listed, pure play vehicles offering direct exposure to UK and European datacentre and logistics development.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“If Segro is absorbed into Prologis, that exposure gets absorbed and the capital allocation decision behind it disappears. Investors lose the ability to choose UK/European datacentre and logistics growth specifically, and instead inherit whatever weighting Prologis’s management chooses to give the UK and Europe within a global platform spanning 20 countries and £200bn of combined assets under management.”In other words, a small piece of diversity is lost from the London stock market, at least in the property sector. You’ll still find plenty of real estate investment trusts offering the usual bland mix of London office blocks and regional shopping centres, but multi-decade pan-European plays on AI datacentres are harder to come by.Prologis’s promise to get a secondary listing in London is not a consolation: we know from experience that most of those add-on listings don’t last because trading in the shares inevitably gravitates to the US.The loss of the company that began life as the Slough Trading Company in 1920 probably won’t register on UK political radars, butit ought to. This takeover is another entry in the hollowing-out of the UK stock market, which has become an easy hunting ground for overseas firms with richer valuations.One would grumble less if the take-out prices were other-worldly. In Segro’s case, though, the terms look only so-so if one takes a long-term view. The outcome could have been different. This is (another) bad one to lose.Explore more on these topicsSegroNils Pratley on financeDatacentrescommentShareReuse this content

title: Three speeches on a single day signaled a dying American democracy | Robert B Shpiner | The Guardian
description: Stephen Miller warned of a leftwing ‘cancer’ threatening civilization. As a critical care doctor, I have a response to his diagnosis
Author: Robert B Shpiner
Headline: Stephen Miller warned of a leftwing ‘cancer’ threatening civilization. As a critical care doctor, I have a response to his diagnosis
Content: ‘This notice is filed in the knowledge that pronouncements can be premature. It is filed in the fear that this one is not.’Photograph: Jonathan Ernst/ReutersView image in fullscreen‘This notice is filed in the knowledge that pronouncements can be premature. It is filed in the fear that this one is not.’Photograph: Jonathan Ernst/ReutersOpinionTrump administrationThree speeches on a single day signaled a dying American democracyRobert B ShpinerStephen Miller warned of a leftwing ‘cancer’ threatening civilization. As a critical care doctor, I have a response to his diagnosisWed 22 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 12.41 EDTShareThe presumption that US elections are legitimate died last week in Washington. It was 237 years old. It did not die of one wound. It died of three, inflicted in a single day, in daylight, on the record. And the country, running to the loudest of them, never examined the one most likely to kill.In critical care we distinguish the mechanism of death from its cause. The mechanism was a Thursday. The cause was chronic, progressive and untreated.The morning belonged to the secretary of state. Before officials of more than 60 countries, he announced that the counterterrorism architecture of the free world, built over a generation against jihadist mass murder, should be rebuilt and aimed at the domestic left. He named a transnational enemy defined by hatred of the west, “a revolt of the worst against the best”. He tied it to Iran and to Cuba and offered no evidence for either link. He said the journalists and thinktanks who might check the claims often agree with the enemy’s goals. The move is old and reliable: discredit the second opinion in advance.Midday belonged to the White House deputy chief of staff.What he saiddeserves to be quoted rather than summarized, because summary is a mercy it has not earned. Hecalled“leftwing political terrorism” a “fatal cancer of civilization”. He said that its politics, left to run its course, “always becomes a gulag”. He said antifa demonstrators are “all deformed in some way, in their appearance, in their dress, in their mannerism”, that “their outer appearance becomes a manifestation of their inner hatred”, and he asked why there is “not one normal-looking person among them”. He said that when the leftist protests that his rights are being violated, those appeals “must fall on deaf ears”. He urged the assembled governments to be “completely unflinching in the pursuit of justice against these enemies of civilization”. And he described the work already under way in six verbs: “disrupt, identify, defund, debank, arrest and prosecute”.I have been practicing medicine for four decades. Cancer is my vocabulary, not his. And I know what happened the last time a government borrowed it. The Nazi state’s central perversion was the medicalization of killing: the enemy as tumor, as bacillus, the nation as patient, extermination as cure. Robert Jay Lifton called it the biomedical vision. It recruited my profession first, and my profession answered willingly. When a government describes citizens as a cancer, it has written the first line of a treatment plan. On Thursday, the plan was read aloud, from a state department podium, in six verbs.One disclosure belongs in the chart. My family left Russia in 1917. My relatives left Germany in 1933. In both generations, leaving was called excessive; staying was the reasonable position. But an alarm that was right both times it sounded is not overcaution. On Thursday, it sounded a third time.The evening belonged to the president. In primetime hecalled the American election system indefensible, claimed a foreign powerheld 220 million voter files,declared mail votinginherently corrupt,accused his own intelligence servicesof concealment, and told Congress that the only reason to oppose his bill is the intent to cheat. That framework converts every future outcome into evidence. Pass the bill and win: the bill worked. Fail and lose: the cheaters blocked it. A presumption of legitimacy cannot survive inside a belief system engineered to be unfalsifiable.Trump is invoking foreign election interference to justify his own | Jamil SmithRead moreIt was just a Thursday. Netflix’s forecast disappointed Wall Street. A rocket aborted on the pad. No sirens sounded, because this is not how they sound. Democracies do not die to soundtracks. They die on ordinary weekdays, between the earnings reports, while the country is at work.Then came the response, and the response was a triage error. Nearly all of it ran to the evening. The factcheckers parsed the voter files. The law professors graded the bombshells. Real work, spent on the loudest injury. The morning’s remarks, the ones that declared a class of Americans a cancer and read enmity on their bodies, drew the attention of an ordinary news cycle: straight coverage in the mainstream, horror at the margins, and in a portion of the press, open applause. I have watched teams run to the dramatic patient while the quiet one, the one not crying out, exsanguinates behind a curtain. The most dangerous words spoken in Washington on Thursday were not spoken in primetime. They were spoken before lunch, to more than 60 countries. The official transcript records the applause.The decedent is survived by the federal courts, still open. By 50 secretaries of state, still certifying. By a general election, still on the calendar, 108 days out. And by an electorate that has not yet been asked the only question that matters, which is whether it will accept a count its president has pre-declared corrupt, counted among citizens its government has pre-declared malignant.In 40 years of intensive care, I have pronounced death many times. The criteria are strict because the declaration is irreversible, and so we are taught to be certain before we speak. I have also, more than once, seen a rhythm return after the room had gone quiet, when someone refused to stop compressions on schedule. This notice is filed in the knowledge that pronouncements can be premature. It is filed in the fear that this one is not.Robert B Shpiner is a clinical professor of medicine in pulmonary and critical care at the David Geffen School of Medicine at UCLAExplore more on these topicsTrump administrationOpinionMarco RubioUS politicsDonald TrumpUS elections 2020US foreign policycommentShareReuse this content

title: Here’s how Andy Burnham really could end homelessness  | Daniel Lavelle | The Guardian
description: Utilising empty homes, capping rents and fixing the benefits system would all help, but they’d require a serious fight, says Daniel Lavelle, who writes on mental health, homelessness and social care
Author: Daniel Lavelle
Headline: Utilising empty homes, capping rents and fixing the benefits system would all help, but they’d require a serious fight, says Daniel Lavelle, who writes on mental health, homelessness and social care
Content: Andy Burnham talking with Hubar, who is homeless in London, 20 July 2026.Photograph: Christopher Furlong/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham talking with Hubar, who is homeless in London, 20 July 2026.Photograph: Christopher Furlong/Getty ImagesOpinionHomelessnessHere’s how Andy Burnham really could end homelessnessDaniel LavelleUtilising empty homes, capping rents and fixing the benefits system would all help, but they’d require a serious fightWed 22 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 08.52 EDTShareIn January 2017, the then mayor of Greater Manchester, Andy Burnham, promised toend rough sleepingin the region by 2020 – a bold, frankly absurd promise that inevitably didn’t materialise. Nearly a decade later, shortly after becoming prime minister, Burnham promised to end rough sleeping again, only this time for thewhole of England. So why should we believe him this time around?I have interviewed Burnham several times over the past decade. At the end of 2019, when it was clear he had not lived up to his promise, I asked himif he regrettedmaking it in the first place. “I wasn’t making what you would call a traditional target,” the mayor said. “I issued my commitment to make it more of a wake-up call to the system. And I think it has had that effect.” Burnham’s advisers had told him to tone down the pledge, but he resisted. “The team here said I should have said: ‘End the need for rough sleeping,’ because you can’t force people into a shelter … In retrospect, if I had said ‘end the need for’, that probably would have been a wiser thing to have done. But I wasn’t in the space of wanting to play semantics with it.”Official figures showed rough sleeping decreased by two-thirds in Manchester during his first five years as mayor. There are reasons I can quibble with this claim. First of all, it doesn’t pass the eye test. Just walk through the city centre in the early hours, and you’ll find people still bedding down in their dozens. Second, the statistics on rough sleeping are based on single-night snapshots, which involve council representatives walking around counting the people they find rough sleeping. The problem with this is obvious. Third, even those stats, as unreliable as they are, now show that rough sleeping in Manchester isthree-quartersof what it was in 2017.But let’s forget all that, and give the prime minister the benefit of the doubt. Maybe his “promise” was merely a “wake-up call”. He was a metro mayor after all, and for most of his time in office, he depended on the generosity of a Conservative government. He simply didn’t have the resources to end rough sleeping. Let’s also assume that Burnham means what he says. He didn’t have to make his pledge back in 2017, and he certainly didn’t have to make it on the steps of Downing Street on Monday.The difference is that now he has control of the purse strings, so how does Burnham intend to honour his promise this time around? Initial signs are not encouraging. After Burnham finished his speech, his new government pledged an extra £340m, equivalent to £68m a year over five years, on top of the £3.5bn already allocated, to tackle homelessness. The UK can’t quite agree on how many rough sleepers there are, but I think a sensible estimate is anywhere between 15,000-18,000 unique people over a 12-month period. So, £68m divided among 18,000 people amounts to £3,777 per head. That’s about four months of rent if you’re lucky. It’s less a panacea, more like defending yourself from a tidal wave with a cocktail umbrella.The government will say it is only targeting 3,000 of the most vulnerable rough sleepers with this cash, and they will receive closer to £22,000 per head. I don’t know how one would qualify for the most-vulnerable list. Just how hopeless do you need to be in the new Burnham era?If Burnham really wants to live up to his promise, he’ll need to do a lot better than this. He should be aware from the start that rough sleeping represents the very tip of an enormous iceberg, and solving homelessness in this country will demand something close to a revolution. According to Shelter, there are380,000 homeless peoplein England, and even that demoralising figure is almost certainly an underestimate. To permanently house those people would require an unprecedented social housebuilding programme that would make the postwar rebuild look like a casual game of Minecraft.Burnham’s government would also need to reverse 16 years of austerity. This is all quite spendy, and to fund it would require higher taxes, more borrowing and aggressive nationalisation of natural monopolies. An upheaval of the economy of such magnitude is unlikely to happen when so manyLabourMPs have their fingernails firmly embedded in the market. If there’s even a suggestion that Burnham intends to depart from turbo capitalism, then he will be out of SW1 faster than you can say Liz Truss.So, what can Burnham realistically do about homelessness? There are several levers available to him to ease people’s suffering in the short term, and they cost next to nothing in political or real capital.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionIt was Britain’s most expensive house. Why is its only resident a homeless man who lives on the porch?Read moreHis priority should be triggering a nationwide Housing First programme. Housing First, a policypioneered in Finlandthat effectively eliminated rough sleeping, gives people a home with no preconditions, then tailors support to their individual needs. We know this works because we did it ourselves, only with a different name. TheEveryone In campaign, established during the Covid-19 pandemic, virtually ended rough sleeping overnight by emergency block-booking hotels. Surely we can do the same thing again.The next thing on the agenda should be removing the stick from the benefits system. Currently, the welfare state resembles a gameshow created by Kafka, where contestants have to complete a series of timed trials to win basic subsistence.There are about300,000 long-term empty homesin England. Housing people in some of these dwellings might be a good idea; capping rents at an affordable rate would be another, as would fast-tracking asylum applications and enabling asylum seekers to receive benefits and temporary work visas while their claims are processed.I’m not confident that Burnham will do any of these things. Not because I think he doesn’t care or lacks the political will, but because government apparatus is smothered in molasses, and most Labour politicians are Tories in all but name.But let’s see what Burnham does before writing him off as yet another neoliberal metronome with fine words and dry parsnips. Let’s give the man his honeymoon period, but the “king of the north” should keep this in mind: winter is coming, and he must act quickly, not just for his premiership, but for the poor souls he has promised to get off the streets.Daniel Lavelle writes on mental health, homelessness and social care. He received the Guardian’s Hugo Young award in 2017, and is the author of Down and OutExplore more on these topicsHomelessnessOpinionHousingEnglandAndy BurnhamLabourcommentShareReuse this content

title: The Guardian view on Andy Burnham and devolution: it takes a powerful centre to decentralise power | Editorial | The Guardian
description: Editorial: When it comes to driving an agenda from No 10, the prime minister seems to have learned important lessons from his predecessor’s failure
Author: Editorial
Headline: Editorial: When it comes to driving an agenda from No 10, the prime minister seems to have learned important lessons from his predecessor’s failure
Content: Andy Burnham in the driver’s area of a FirstGroup bus during a visit to Bath on 22 July.Photograph: Toby Melville/ReutersView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham in the driver’s area of a FirstGroup bus during a visit to Bath on 22 July.Photograph: Toby Melville/ReutersOpinionAndy BurnhamThe Guardian view on Andy Burnham and devolution: it takes a powerful centre to decentralise powerEditorialWhen it comes to driving an agenda from No 10, the prime minister seems to have learned important lessons from his predecessor’s failureWed 22 Jul 2026 13.30 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 13.57 EDTShareBefore entering Downing Street, Andy Burnhamcitedthe overcentralisation of power as a cause of Britain’s social and economic malaise. In his first week, the new prime minister has signalled his intent to bolster No 10 as an organ of executive power. The apparent contradiction – fortifying the centre to pursue an agenda of decentralisation – arises from a perversity in the way the British state works or, rather,fails to work.The prime minister is right that too much policy has historically been directed from Whitehall. Reforms in recent years have created institutions with varying degrees of local autonomy – elected mayoralties like the one inManchesterthat afforded Mr Burnham a platform for national prominence. But genuine control, especially when it comes to fiscal policy, has not matched the rhetoric of devolution. The Treasury is wary of facilitating profligacy in lower tiers of government. Heads of devolved administrations are also adept at claiming credit when things go well and, when they go badly, diverting the blame on to national government.Meanwhile, the prime minister’s office is thinly resourced in comparison to equivalent institutions in similar democracies. Vital functions depend on a small number of special advisers, who are often too buffeted by crises to focus on strategic direction. A lack of focus in No 10 allows chancellors to monopolise economic policymaking.Under Sir Keir Starmer, those systemic weaknesses were aggravated by his failure to set clear priorities. His Downing Street operation was directionless and ineffective. Mr Burnham looks reassuringly determined to correct that mistake. A notable appointment in his new cabinet is theelevation of Louise Haigh, a close ally, to the role of first secretary of state with responsibility for government coordination. She will be running the Cabinet Office as a department for enacting the will of the prime minister.Ms Haigh has spoken of the need for a “proper beefed-up economic unit” in No 10, suggesting a rebalancing of power away from the Treasury. John Healey, thenew chancellor, is a Whitehall veteran and no pushover when it comes to departmental turf wars, as his resignation as defence secretary from Sir Keir’s cabinet showed. But he is not a big beast of the species likely to build a power base to rival Mr Burnham.The new prime minister has already asserted personal authority over economic policymaking bychoosing a VAT cut on energy billsas his first emblematic act in office. His declared longer‑term intention to reconfigure Britain’s national political and economic model – an epoch-defining redistribution of power – will require a level of interdepartmental coordination for which there is not much recent precedent. The same applies to his ambition to build a “preventive state”, making smart strategic interventions early to avoid “paying for failure” down the line.An early test will be whether Mr Burnham’smooted No 10 Northlooks like a significant institutional innovation – a shift in the centre of national political gravity, not just a gesture of compensation for generations of southern bias. Mr Burnham will need to marshal the full authority of his office to drive change through systems that are prone to inertia, but the new prime minister seems to understand this challenge. He knows the paradox of devolution: that it takes a confident assertion of central power to give real power away.Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? title: The Guardian view on Chinese workers picking up the pen: poetry in motion | Editorial | The Guardian
description: Editorial: A food delivery rider has won the country’s highest literary award. It is part of a growing trend for writing by labourers
Author: Editorial
Headline: Editorial: A food delivery rider has won the country’s highest literary award. It is part of a growing trend for writing by labourers
Content: ‘Workers’ writing has seen a revival in the 21st century. This time, it is bottom-up rather than directed from above.’Photograph: Yuan He/APView image in fullscreen‘Workers’ writing has seen a revival in the 21st century. This time, it is bottom-up rather than directed from above.’Photograph: Yuan He/APOpinionChinaThe Guardian view on Chinese workers picking up the pen: poetry in motionEditorialA food delivery rider has won the country’s highest literary award. It is part of a growing trend for writing by labourersWed 22 Jul 2026 13.25 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 13.58 EDTShareThe hundreds of millions who have built modern China from the ground up – sweeping its streets, labouring in its factories and ferrying its goods – are often overlooked. But they refuse to go unheard. Last week, a food delivery riderwon China’s top literary award. Wang Jibing, 56, first caught attention when a poem about his experiences went viral in 2022. The following year,Hu Anyan’s memoir, I Deliver Parcels in Beijing, became a bestseller.In the early 20th century, Chinese intellectuals sought to capture the harsh realities of working-class lives. But it was under Mao Zedong that the idea of writing by as well as about the masses became reality, with labourers encouraged to take up the pen. In an era when the party tried togovern the languageeven of diaries and personal letters, the parameters of style and content were strict, and this enthusiasm swiftly diminished after Mao. But workers’ writinghas seen a revivalin the 21st century. This time, it is bottom-up rather than directed from above.Maoist literature was meant to educate and mobilise the masses. Heavy labour was portrayed asoffering a kind of transcendence, with workers becoming almost superhuman in their efforts. Contrast that with the testimony of the poet Xiao Hai, whosememoir, Adrift in the South,was published in English in May. Forced by family poverty to work on assembly lines from the age of 15, he writes of his “bitterness and regret … when I realise I let the best decade of my life slip away from me on the factory floor”.The individual scrabble to get by has replaced paeans to collective toil, and the sense of hope has given way to numbness or despair. The image of the screwonce represented workers’ pridein their small contribution to a greater cause. But in the poetry ofXu Lizhi, a Foxconn worker who killed himself in 2014, it reflects their insignificance and replaceability.These accounts of exploitation and dangers, of bodies and souls ground down, may seem unsparing. But the Communist party has little tolerance for work that touches on topics such as the role of corrupt officials or collective organisation. Many grassroots writers post online, where their work can easily be deleted. The well‑known poet Zheng Xiaoqiongcollected the stories of fellow labourersin Woman Worker, but it has never been issued in full because publishers demanded extensive changes.The work of this new generation has drawn wider attention in the last few years. Fan Yusu’s essay on life as a migrant workermade her a literary sensationin 2017.In the pandemic, some began to notice the low-paid workers who were keeping the country running. A growing sense of precarity among the middle classes may have contributed. Labourers have been at the sharpest end of China’s breakneck development, but others share their concerns about the country’s path – and fear what happens if their status slips.These narratives resonate with readers outsideChinatoo. They delineate the unsavoury underpinnings of the globalised economy and speak to the unease that many feel with it. I Deliver Parcels in Beijing has been sold to 16 countries; an English edition came out last year. In many places, there are still more books about workers than by them. Where is the English-language bestseller by an Amazon warehouse operative, or the viral poetry about care work on a zero-hours contract?Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article?
