title: The Falklands-Malvinas and fundamental rights | Falkland Islands | The Guardian
description: Letters: Jack Ford of the Falkland Islands legislative assembly says the Falkland Islands are British because that is the wish of the people who live there, in response to an article by Simon Jenkins. Plus letters from Marcelo Alberto Albarenque and Lucian Comoy
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Letters: Jack Ford of the Falkland Islands legislative assembly says the Falkland Islands are British because that is the wish of the people who live there, in response to an article by Simon Jenkins. Plus letters from Marcelo Alberto Albarenque and Lucian Comoy
Content: A mural in Buenos Aires depicts the soccer star Diego Maradona and veterans of the 1982 war between Argentina and Britain over the Falkland Islands.Photograph: Rodrigo Abd/APView image in fullscreenA mural in Buenos Aires depicts the soccer star Diego Maradona and veterans of the 1982 war between Argentina and Britain over the Falkland Islands.Photograph: Rodrigo Abd/APFalkland IslandsLettersThe Falklands-Malvinas and fundamental rightsJack Fordof the Falkland Islands legislative assembly says the Falkland Islands are British because that is the wish of the people who live there, in response to an article by Simon Jenkins. Plus letters fromMarcelo Alberto AlbarenqueandLucian ComoyWed 22 Jul 2026 12.51 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 13.01 EDTShareFor several years, Simon Jenkins has written articles on the Falkland Islands and his view that it is inevitable that one day the sovereignty of the islands will be transferred to Argentina. His latest article is no different (‘Las Malvinas son Argentinas’? Not quite – but the Falklands cannot remain British for ever, 16 July).Jenkins’ arguments misrepresent our modern relationship with the UK – one that is based on self-determination, which is a fundamental right enjoyed by all people and enshrined in the UN charter. This has been the position of successive UK governments, including the present one, and enjoys strong cross-party support.TheFalkland Islandsare British not because anyone in Whitehall says they are, but because that is the wish of the people who live on the islands, many of whom can trace their roots in the islands back 10 generations.The islands are British for as long as Falkland Islanders wish, and in 2013, islanders made their position very clear when 99.8%, on a turnout of 92%, voted in favour of remaining a UK overseas territory.If Jenkins were to visit the islands, he would see new houses, facilities and commercial premises being built. Critically, he would see a proud and confident people with their democratically elected government investing in the future – a growing population and a growing economy that is self-sufficient in all areas except defence, with resources being invested in protecting our unique environment.To portray our relationship with the UK as one-sided and transactional could not be further from the truth. We enjoy a mutually beneficial relationship.New economic developments in the Falklands are creating thousands of jobs and billions of pounds of investment in the UK, and the UK’s military presence in the islands is about far more than just defending the Falklands. It also provides a valuable training ground and protects wider UK interests in the region.Jack FordChair, Falkland Islands legislative assemblyAs an Argentinian reader, having just finished Simon Jenkins’ piece, I welcome confirmation of something we already knew on this side of the Atlantic: there is good sense and goodwill on both sides of the ocean.Mr Jenkins’ account of the 1970s leaseback talks is a useful reminder that real progress was made in a fruitful dialogue, before 1982 froze everything. Sadly, our own institutions allowed a dictator who had seized power by force to take a cause sacred to every Argentinian, regardless of age or politics, and sully it with a war that cost the blood of brave men from both our nations.That freeze carries a cost that the UK rarely reckons with: it stops our two countries from building on the historic ties that bind us, alongside the differences that are just as historic. Consider the benefits we could reap together in investment, tourism, cultural exchange, scientific research, and food and energy security.Argentina was, and is, home to Welsh, English, Scottish and Irish immigrant communities woven into our history. Indeed, there are more Argentinians of British descent on the mainland than there are people living in the Falklands-Malvinas. Our constitution guarantees the benefits of liberty “to all the men of the world who wish to dwell on Argentine soil”, and we are proud of that.As Mr Jenkins rightly says: “Argentina’s claims are not going away.” Never. It is in our DNA.Marcelo Alberto AlbarenqueMaría Grande, Entre Ríos, ArgentinaThank goodness for a voice of reason. Of course, now that blood has been shed, it would be political suicide for any British politician even to hint at discussing sovereignty for a number of years yet. Both sides have been at fault with the islands, unscrupulously exploiting the issue to gain political capital. Until 1982, the islands and Patagonia were closely integrated. Of course Argentina was at fault for invading, but Britain’s vow to protect the islanders cost what it may stinks of self-righteousness: after all, ithad considered ceding the islandsprior to the invasion.I have a foot in both camps – my wife is Argentinian – and I know that while the notion of Argentinian sovereignty is hammered home in the schools, the vast majority of the population couldn’t much care either way and would be happy to see this become a non-event, resolved in one way or another.In Argentina,voxpopulideclares that the islands should be Argentinian, but that it is fully understandable the islanders should not wish to be governed in any way by whatever self-serving government head happens to be living in the Casa Rosada. They also suspect that the only reason Britain is being so stubborn over these faraway, windswept rocks is for money: there is oil there is fish.Lucian ComoySgonico, Trieste, ItalyHave an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today? Pleaseemailus your letter and it will be considered for publication in ourletterssection.Explore more on these topicsFalkland IslandsArgentinaAmericaslettersShareReuse this content

title: Target ultra-processed food to reduce obesity in Britain | Obesity | The Guardian
description: Letter: James Capon responds to an editorial on the health select committee’s call for radical action to regulate food markets
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Letter: James Capon responds to an editorial on the health select committee’s call for radical action to regulate food markets
Content: ‘The low-fat, calorie-controlled orthodoxy inherited from last century’s flawed nutritional science still shapes labelling.’Photograph: ClarkandCompany/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘The low-fat, calorie-controlled orthodoxy inherited from last century’s flawed nutritional science still shapes labelling.’Photograph: ClarkandCompany/Getty ImagesObesityLettersTarget ultra-processed food to reduce obesity in BritainJames Caponresponds to an editorial on the health select committee’s call for radical action to regulate food marketsWed 22 Jul 2026 12.51 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 13.01 EDTShareThe MPs’ report mentioned in your editorial is welcome and overdue (The Guardian view on obesity: put public health before food industry pressure, 15 July). The health select committee is right to clarify that obesity is an engineered outcome, not a failure of willpower. But there’s a flaw running through decades of British food policy that risks being repeated: the “high in fat, salt or sugar” framework itself.“High in fat, salt or sugar” combines three nutrients as though they carry equivalent blame. However, the evidence on weight gain and type 2 diabetes points to sugar and refined carbohydrates as the primary drivers, not fat. Salt matters, chiefly when combined with sugar, but the body needs salt to function. Fat matters because without it many fat-soluble vitamins will just pass through our bodies. We need no sugar at all.The low-fat, calorie-controlled orthodoxy inherited from flawed nutritional science still shapes labelling, reformulation targets and public understanding, encouraging manufacturers to strip out fat and replace it with sweet-tasting substitutes, while ticking the “healthier” box.To hold supermarkets and manufacturers to account, the target must be sharpened. The culprit is the ultra-processed food model itself: products engineered for shelf life, craveability and margin, using cheap sugar and refined starch as primary ingredients. Ice-cream or potato crisps sold on rare occasions are fine, but the food system that has made them the cheapest, most heavily advertised, most convenient choice is the problem.We shouldn’t need to romanticise the Greggs sausage roll as some kind of national treasure. Consider it a treat, not a staple. That’s the shift in our relationship with ultra-processed food that this report should be pushing for – one built on carbohydrate and sugar reduction, not a blunt fat-and-salt panic that has already failed us.James CaponWaterloo, BelgiumHave an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today? Pleaseemailus your letter and it will be considered for publication in ourletterssection.Explore more on these topicsObesityFood & drink industryFoodHealth policyHealthPublic services policyFast foodlettersShareReuse this content

title: Kevin Keegan’s legacy and great humility | Soccer | The Guardian
description: Letters: Jennifer Basannavar says he’s the reason many German boys were given the name Kevin; Rev Henry Corbett fondly recalls his visit to Shewsy youth club
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Letters: Jennifer Basannavar says he’s the reason many German boys were given the name Kevin; Rev Henry Corbett fondly recalls his visit to Shewsy youth club
Content: Kevin Keegan’s permed hairstyle set a trend in football, says Jennifer Basannavar.Photograph: ITV/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenKevin Keegan’s permed hairstyle set a trend in football, says Jennifer Basannavar.Photograph: ITV/ShutterstockSoccerLettersKevin Keegan’s legacy and great humilityJennifer Basannavarsays he’s the reason many German boys were given the name Kevin;Rev Henry Corbettfondly recalls his visit toShewsy youth clubWed 22 Jul 2026 12.51 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 13.01 EDTShareYour obituary of Kevin Keegan (20 July) refers to his move to Hamburg and his newly permed hairstyle, which set a trend in football. Unusually, he resolved to use his time there to learn German to communicate better with his teammates. He was repaid many times by another trend: German boys being given the name Kevin.Our British-German school exchanges in the 1990s always had at least one boy called Kevin. They were very proud of their name, as I imagine they are today, when they read about the positive contribution made by Keegan. And, like Arthur Fery, at 5ft 8in he was of “diminutive stature” (Letters, 12 July).Jennifer BasannavarTwickenham, LondonI remember Kevin Keegan coming to the Shewsy youth club in Everton in inner-cityLiverpoolin the late 1970s. They say you know a famous person by what they are like away from the limelight. He was a player just back from winning two Ballon d’Ors at Hamburg, and he didn’t want any publicity. In fact, he insisted on no publicity.He stayed for two hours, played table tennis and football with the young people, and then sat on a chair and answered loads of questions with honesty, humour and wise advice. He just wanted to encourage and help young people in an inner-city area. Big respect.Rev Henry CorbettLiverpoolHave an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today? Pleaseemailus your letter and it will be considered for publication in ourletterssection.Explore more on these topicsFootballHamburgGermanyBallon d'OrNewcastle UnitedLiverpoollettersShareReuse this content

title: We must reject any notion of AI consciousness | AI (artificial intelligence) | The Guardian
description: Letter: Artificial intelligence systems won’t become conscious for the same reason they won’t become pregnant, says Dr John Pickering
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Letter: Artificial intelligence systems won’t become conscious for the same reason they won’t become pregnant, says Dr John Pickering
Content: ‘Used well Claude is of great benefit. But because it can simulate having experience doesn’t mean it actually has it.’Photograph: NurPhoto/ShutterstockView image in fullscreen‘Used well Claude is of great benefit. But because it can simulate having experience doesn’t mean it actually has it.’Photograph: NurPhoto/ShutterstockAI (artificial intelligence)LettersWe must reject any notion of AI consciousnessArtificial intelligence systems won’t become conscious for the same reason they won’t become pregnant, saysDr John PickeringWed 22 Jul 2026 12.50 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 13.11 EDTShareAnil Seth is right to point out that to overestimate artificial intelligence is to underestimate ourselves (Once again we are told AI may be conscious – I study consciousness, and I have my doubts, 15 July). But he is wrong only to have doubts about whether AI systems like Claude may become conscious. He should be certain, and should say so more forcefully.It’s not like anything to be Claude, just as it’s not like anything to be a washing machine. An academic education is not required to realise that, common sense will do. Artificial intelligence systems won’t become conscious for the same reason they won’t become pregnant; they’re not that sort of thing.Used well, Claude is of great benefit. But because it can simulate having experience doesn’t mean it actually has it.Like Richard Dawkins, Seth seems merely to be unsure about this. Such tepid equivocation from leading figures in the field is disappointing; only resounding rejection will do.Dr John PickeringLeamington Spa, WarwickshireHave an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today?
