title: Liverpool primed for business: Bezos, Bhatia and the next steps at Anfield | Liverpool | The Guardian
description: Fenway Sports Group looks to be locked in talks with a consortium led by the son-in-law of Lakshmi Mittal, while the Amazon founder has now been linked
Author: Andy Hunter
Headline: Fenway Sports Group looks to be locked in talks with a consortium led by the son-in-law of Lakshmi Mittal, while the Amazon founder has now been linked
Content: Changing times? Liverpool could soon have an injection of cash.Photograph: Peter Byrne/PAView image in fullscreenChanging times? Liverpool could soon have an injection of cash.Photograph: Peter Byrne/PALiverpoolExplainerLiverpool primed for business: Bezos, Bhatia and the next steps at AnfieldFenway Sports Group looks to be locked in talks with a consortium led by the son-in-law of Lakshmi Mittal, while the Amazon founder has now been linkedAndy HunterWed 22 Jul 2026 15.31 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 15.16 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWhat has happened?A consortium led by Amit Bhatia, the former co-owner of Queens Park Rangers and son-in-law of the Indian steel magnate Lakshmi Mittal, hasopened talks with Liverpool’s owner, Fenway Sports Group, over buying a significant stake in the club. Neither party has commented on the size of the stake or the investment amount being offered but it is believed to be a provisional offer of £1.35bn for about 30%. Discussions began three months ago but FSG insists the talks remain at a preliminary stage.Where does this value Liverpool?The reported offer values Liverpool at about £4.5bn. Manchester United were valued at slightly less when Sir Jim Ratcliffe bought aninitial 25% stake in February 2024, increased to 27.7%, although the latest valuation of United by Forbes was $7.2bn (£5.38bn). Forbes, again, had Real Madrid as the most valuable club in the world at $9.5bn, with Barcelona in second place at $7.5bn. Todd Boehly and Clearlake Capitalacquired Chelsea for £4.25bn in May 2022as a distressed asset sale after the Russian oligarch Roman Abramovich was sanctioned by the UK government.Why would FSG potentially want to sell a minority stake?First of all, £1.35bn for 30% of Liverpool would represent a healthy profit on a club itbought for £300m in 2010and whose value has soared since. And the principal owner, John W Henry, is a venture capitalist, after all. But it is also true that FSG remains fiercely competitive and to continue challenging among an elite that includes oil-rich states necessitates ever more investment. That is why FSG has sought, and occasionally found, new investors before. The current talks are not considered part of an exit strategy by Liverpool’s owners but a potential opportunity to attract considerable funds while remaining in overall control. The company has parked plans to buy a second club – a decision that led to Michael Edwards’s recent departure as FSG’s chief executive of football – and last summer funded the biggest transfer outlay in Liverpool’s history of almost £450m. To maintain and improve those levels, having made only a modest profit of £8m when winning the Premier League title under Arne Slot in 2024-25, requires injecting fresh capital into the club.View image in fullscreenLiverpool spent £125m on Alexander Isak on deadline day in August 2025, part of a £450m summer outlay.Photograph: Ian Hodgson/APHave we been here before?Several times. In March 2021, FSG sold 10% of the company to RedBird Capital Partners for £543m. In 2022, FSG hired the investment banks Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley to seek out potential buyers for a 10% stake in Liverpool. No deal was done and Henry confirmed the following year that FSG was always open to new investment but not looking to sell the club. In 2023, FSG sold a reported 4% stake in Liverpool to Dynasty Equity, an American sports investment company, for £164m. That money was largely used to pay off debts incurred during the pandemic.Who are the potential investors?Bhatia, who stepped down as co-owner and director of QPR on Tuesday after 18 years, is believed to have secured financial backing from Mittal. The Mittal family wealth is estimated to be £23bn. Those riches pale in comparison, however, with another potential investor – Amazon’s founder, Jeff Bezos. The fourth-richest person in the world could afford to buy Liverpool outright and still have $250bn left in his back pocket. Bezos has been approached about joining the consortium but is reportedly undecided on whether to invest in a Premier League club for the first time. Bezos is no longer chief executive of Amazon but is executive chair of a company that has expanded from online retail into entertainment and sports rights, including for Premier League games, in recent years.What would it mean for Liverpool’s finances?Even without Bezos on board, the consortium’s proposed offer would improve Liverpool’s capital considerably as the club begins a new era under Andoni Iraola. How much say and sway Bhatia and others would have over Liverpool’s football operations remains to be ironed out. With the FSG president, Mike Gordon, returning to a more prominent role at the club after Edwards’s exit, it seems unlikely that Liverpool’s owners would follow the Glazers’ example with Ratcliffe at Manchester United and grant a minority shareholder full control on the football side. Given talks are only at a preliminary stage, Liverpool’s transfer plans for this summer are not expected to be affected.What happens next?Talks continue until a deal is done or collapses. FSG’s willingness to confirm Bhatia’s bid in a statement to the Financial Times on Tuesday, and Bhatia’s departure from QPR that very same day, indicates negotiations are heading towards a successful conclusion.Get in touchContact Guardian SportThe best public interest journalism relies on first-hand accounts from people in the know. If you have something to share on this subject, you can contact us confidentially using the following methods:Secure Messaging in the Guardian appThe Guardian app has a tool to send tips about stories. Messages are end to end encrypted and concealed within the routine activity that every Guardian mobile app performs. This prevents an observer from knowing that you are communicating with us at all, let alone what is being said.If you don’t already have the Guardian app, download it (iOS/Android) and go to the menu. Select ‘Secure Messaging’. When asked to select a recipient, select the ‘Sport (UK and Global)’ team.SecureDropIf you can safely use the tor network without being observed or monitored you can send messages and documents to the Guardian via ourSecureDrop platform.Finally, our guide attheguardian.com/tipslists several ways to contact us securely, and discusses the pros and cons of each.Show moreExplore more on these topicsLiverpoolBusinessexplainersShareReuse this content

title: Anti-doping chiefs reject claims of Paul Seixas favouritism as Tour rivals cry foul | Tour de France 2026 | The Guardian
description: The home rider Paul Seixas has yet to be tested at night, unlike some of his rivals, but authorities deny that the French doping agency plays any role in who is tested
Author: Jeremy Whittle
Headline: The home rider Paul Seixas has yet to be tested at night, unlike some of his rivals, but authorities deny that the French doping agency plays any role in who is tested
Content: The French rider Paul Seixas is presently fourth in the Tour de France overall standings.Photograph: Loïc Venance/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThe French rider Paul Seixas is presently fourth in the Tour de France overall standings.Photograph: Loïc Venance/AFP/Getty ImagesTour de France 2026Anti-doping chiefs reject claims of Paul Seixas favouritism as Tour rivals cry foulHome rider Seixas yet to be tested at night, unlike some rivalsAuthorities deny French doping agency plays role in who is testedJeremy Whittlein VoironWed 22 Jul 2026 13.45 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 19.21 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAnti-doping authorities at theTour de Francehave rejected accusations of preferential treatment towards Paul Seixas, the teenage French rider and home favourite who is placed fourth overall.Seixas and his Decathlon CMA CGM team were tested, with the rest of the peloton, on Monday evening as part of the biological passport testing programme, but the 19-year-old has not yet been subjected to the nocturnal test that both the race leader Tadej Pogacar andthe now-withdrawn Jonas Vingegaardunderwent.Vingegaard’s and Pogacar’s night-time doping tests authorised by Paris judgeRead moreThe Slovenian Matej Mohoric, who rides for the Bahrain Victorious team and was among those visited at 5am, said: “It would be good to guarantee equal treatment for everyone. After all, we know that this French rider [Seixas], who is well placed in the general classification, didn’t undergo that [night-time] test.”The International Testing Agency denied suggestions of bias, saying: “The ITA is responsible for the strategy of the anti-doping programme for the Tour de France, not the AFLD [the French anti-doping agency]. We have our own sample collection personnel for the doping controls.“AFLD might occasionally support the ITA with additional sample collection personnel, if needed, for instance during the testing mission on Monday evening but the decision who is tested when remains with the ITA.”The ITA said: “Our decisions are based on facts, risk assessments, performances, scientific results and insights, intelligence and investigations, as well as data analysis. This approach enables us to act as precisely and impartially as possible, independently of any external influence, political interest or opinion.”Professional cycling’s governing body, the UCI, has now fully endorsed the ITA’s night-time testing regime, but insists: “Carrying out such controls outside normal hours remains an exceptional measure, reserved for limited and justified circumstances.”Mohoric said:“I was tested at 5am myself. That’s practically the middle of the night, given that we go to bed around midnight because the stages finish late and we have breakfast around 10am.“They have to prove they [the tests] are working and ensuring the sport remains clean and fair,” Mohoric said of the ITA. “We are paying the price for cycling’s past.”View image in fullscreenThe defending champion and race leader Tadej Pogacar is one of several riders who have had doping tests at night.Photograph: Gonzalo Fuentes/ReutersOn Wednesday afternoon, the UCI said that the ITA’s activities demonstrate that “the fight against doping is an absolute priority”, adding: “The UCI acknowledges that night-time testing can disrupt riders’ rest and recovery [but] these controls serve an overriding interest: the fight against doping.”On Wednesday a rare breakaway move succeeded as Jasper Philipsen led home a reduced main peloton in the 175-kilometre 17th stage from Chambéry to Voiron.In a fast and chaotic stage, in which multiple groups formed and reformed, the last man standing Jasper Stuyven was reeled in on the climb to La Commanderie, just 4km from the finish.That set up a sprint finish and a first win in this year’s race for Philipsen, who was thwarted earlier in the Tour by Tim Merlier, who has now abandoned the race, and the Dutch sprinter Olav Kooij, teammate to Seixas.But as Philipsen, the winner of the points classification in 2023, celebrated an 11th career Tour stage win, Adam Yates, a key member of the race leader Pogacar’s mountain support, was enduring a stomach bug at the back of the peloton.The British rider crossed the finish line third from last, looking pale and exhausted, having been sick for the past 48 hours. “He looked terrible,” Pogacar said of his teammate during the time trial on Tuesday.With three mountain-top finishes now coming, first at Orcières-Merlette and then the dreaded double stage finish at Alpe d’Huez, on Friday and Saturday, Yates would be expected to play a key role in the champion’s title defence.“Today he looked much better, and was eating good,” Pogacar said, “but when you have a bad day like yesterday, you pay a day later. I hope he didn’t go too deep into the hole and he will be better tomorrow.”Get in touchContact Guardian SportThe best public interest journalism relies on first-hand accounts from people in the know. If you have something to share on this subject, you can contact us confidentially using the following methods:Secure Messaging in the Guardian appThe Guardian app has a tool to send tips about stories. Messages are end to end encrypted and concealed within the routine activity that every Guardian mobile app performs. This prevents an observer from knowing that you are communicating with us at all, let alone what is being said.If you don’t already have the Guardian app, download it (iOS/Android) and go to the menu. Select ‘Secure Messaging’. When asked to select a recipient, select the ‘Sport (UK and Global)’ team.SecureDropIf you can safely use the tor network without being observed or monitored you can send messages and documents to the Guardian via ourSecureDrop platform.Finally, our guide attheguardian.com/tipslists several ways to contact us securely, and discusses the pros and cons of each.Show moreExplore more on these topicsTour de France 2026Tour de FranceCyclingnewsShareReuse this content

title: Arsenal reveal William Saliba out for ‘extended period’ with back injury | Arsenal | The Guardian
description: The France centre-back was forced off in the World Cup semi-final against Spain and will now sit out Arsenal’s pre-season
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The France centre-back was forced off in the World Cup semi-final against Spain and will now sit out Arsenal’s pre-season
Content: France’s William Saliba goes down with the injury against Spain in the World Cup semi-final.Photograph: Jean Catuffe/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenFrance’s William Saliba goes down with the injury against Spain in the World Cup semi-final.Photograph: Jean Catuffe/Getty ImagesArsenalArsenal reveal William Saliba out for ‘extended period’ with back injuryCentre-back forced off in World Cup semi-finalArsenal say problem does not require surgeryPA MediaWed 22 Jul 2026 17.05 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 17.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleArsenal, the Premier League champions, have been dealt a big injury blow after the centre-back William Saliba was ruled out for “an extended period” with a back injury.Saliba played with a back problem during France’s run to the World Cup semi-final and, although he will not require an operation, the 25-year-old is set for a significant spell on the sidelines. He was forced off with the injury after just 30 minutes ofFrance’s last-four defeat by Spain.“Subsequent specialist reviews on William’s return to London this week have confirmed that he has suffered a back injury which will require a period of rehabilitation,” Arsenal said in a statement. “Extensive assessments have concluded that surgery is not recommended, but William should now commence a managed recovery programme.“William’s rehabilitation will commence immediately, with continual management of his injury, and he is expected to be out of action for an extended period. Everyone is fully focused on supporting William to ensure he is back to full fitness as soon as possible.”After France’s loss to Spain, Saliba sat out thethird-place playoff against England. Earlier in the tournament, he admitted: “I’ve had some minor niggles for several months. I’ve been gritting my teeth because there was the Champions League and the Premier League. The World Cup comes round only once every four years, so you’ve got to grit your teeth.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionArsenal’s England duoBukayo SakaandDeclan Ricealso battled injury problems throughout the tournament, although both of them were fit enough to star in the third‑place decider, with Rice getting the first goal and Saka scoring a hat‑trick.Explore more on these topicsArsenalFranceWorld Cup 2026newsShareReuse this content

title: London Spirit’s new look, new squad and new ambition for the Hundred | The Hundred | The Guardian
description: London Spirit, the Hundred’s most valuable franchise, begin their season with ‘renewed energy and perhaps a bit of pizzazz’
Author: Simon Burnton
Headline: London Spirit, the Hundred’s most valuable franchise, begin their season with ‘renewed energy and perhaps a bit of pizzazz’
Content: Jamie Overton is the only survivor of the London Spirit men’s squad from last year.Photograph: Tom Dulat/ECB/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenJamie Overton is the only survivor of the London Spirit men’s squad from last year.Photograph: Tom Dulat/ECB/Getty ImagesThe HundredLondon Spirit’s new look, new squad and new ambition for the HundredMost valuable franchise in the competition begin their season with ‘renewed energy and perhaps a bit of pizzazz’Simon BurntonWed 22 Jul 2026 13.09 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 18.07 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWhen Jamie Overton reported to Lord’s last Friday for the start of the new Hundred season, he experienced something that, even in the often head-spinning world of franchise cricket, was extraordinary, potentially unique. Returning to a team he played for in 2025, he would have looked around the dressing room and seen none of his former teammates. He is the only survivor of the men’s squad from last year, part of a team that will be led by a new head coach, assisted by new coaches for batting, fielding, spin and pace bowling.“It definitely does feel like a different competition this year, because every team is effectively different,” he says. “Oval Invincibles were the best side over the last three or four years, but that’s spread out a little bit now and every side you look at them and go: ‘Oh yeah, they’ve got a good team.’The Hundred returns with the hits and hope that fans feel the love againRead more“It’s going to be a tough competition, and I’m going to be biased, but I’m backing this squad. We’ve got a lot of firepower, a lot of wicket-taking potential and we’ve got a hell of a lot of experience. We’ve just got to go out and perform well.”The Spirit women’s squad is twice as settled, in that among the playing group they have two veterans of 2025: Charlie Dean, who has taken over as permanent captain having stood in for the injured Heather Knight (now the general manager) in 2025; and Grace Harris. “It’s a little bit weird, but it’s not that bad,” Harris says.“It’s only bad if you miss friends. I really enjoyed playing with Sarah Glenn [now at Southern Brave] and with Dani Gibson [now captaining Sunrisers Leeds]. I enjoyed playing with a few of the London Spirit girls that were around last season, but it’s not like they’ve completely gone missing. They’re going to be playing for other teams so we’ll still see them around.”London Spirit are not one of the three Hundred sides who come into this season with a new name, but they do have a new logo, a new sponsor anda new kitmade by a new kit producer. They have relatively new co-owners in the Tech Titans, the consortium of entrepreneurswho paid £144m for a 49% stake, a deal announced in January 2025 and completed last July, making them the competition’s most valuable franchise.View image in fullscreenGrace Harris is one of two veterans of 2025’s women’s squad.Photograph: Jan Kruger/ECB/Getty ImagesAndy Flower, the new men’s coach, says they will bring “renewed energy and perhaps a bit of pizzazz”, and also new ambitions: the women’s team has reached (and won) one of the five previous finals, but the men have come last or second-last in four years out of five and never been in the top two.There is no reason, though, for them to be defined by results obtained by different people in a different era and Flower has won the Hundred, withTrent Rockets in 2022, as well as the past two Indian Premier League titles withRoyal Challengers Bengaluru. He summarises his role, now that the squad has been assembled, as “looking to get people feeling as confident and clear as possible … to make them feel confident and clear and free to go and make decisions for themselves”.As a young player Overton worked with Flower when he was the England Lions coach and the pair reunited more recently at Gulf Giants in the ILT20. “I’ve known him quite a long time and I know how he works,” Overton says.“He’s very different now than he was, but franchise cricket is very different to younger players trying to play for England. You’re on a journey in that phase of your life, whereas in franchise cricket you probably know where you want to be.“He’s developed, like all good people do. But he’s very direct in how he wants to play the game. He’ll do all the one percenters, find the small margins.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThe SpinSubscribe to our cricket newsletter for our writers' thoughts on the biggest stories and a review of the week’s actionPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe women’s team is also being led for the first time by a former England coach in Jon Lewis and will get their season under way against Manchester Super Giants on Thursday afternoon, with the men’s teams meeting in the evening. “Since it’s been privatised there’s a bit more of a buzz, just about what changes might happen,” Harris says. “But we can’t be any more professional than what it’s been over the past three seasons I’ve been involved.London Spirit use huge Hundred windfall to treble spending on off-field talentRead more“If there’s more money in the game maybe they pay for an ice bath now or we get better access to off-field facilities or more clothing to wear. Our team room’s been done up quite well at the hotel, so that’s good fun. But I don’t think your on-field expectations should change that much.“You can only do so much as a human being. You can try harder, but if you haven’t been trying up until that point then where have you been?”Entertainment between the games at Lord’s on Thursday will be provided by Tinie Tempah and if rap will never not feel incongruous at the home of cricket his most recent chart single, 2020’s Top Winners, sounds pretty apposite. “It’s about a winners’ mindset and mentality, about striving to achieve more and continuing to do better for yourself,” he says. But then, ambition is the easy bit. Top Winners peaked at No 64.Explore more on these topicsThe HundredCricketfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Trossard, Ramos, Dumfries: 10 transfers you may have missed during the World Cup | Transfer window | The Guardian
description: Too engrossed with the World Cup drama to keep up with goings-on in the transfer market? We’ve got you covered
Author: Will Magee
Headline: Too engrossed with the World Cup drama to keep up with goings-on in the transfer market? We’ve got you covered
Content: Denzel Dumfries, Robert Lewandowski and Leandro Trossard have all moved on to new clubs.Composite: Guardian picture desk; Fifa/Getty Images; Chicago Fire FC/MLS/Getty Images; Anadolu/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenDenzel Dumfries, Robert Lewandowski and Leandro Trossard have all moved on to new clubs.Composite: Guardian picture desk; Fifa/Getty Images; Chicago Fire FC/MLS/Getty Images; Anadolu/Getty ImagesTransfer windowTrossard, Ramos, Dumfries: 10 transfers you may have missed during the World CupToo engrossed with the World Cup drama to keep up with goings-on in the transfer market? We’ve got you coveredWill Magee,Niall McVeigh,Billy MundayandYara El-ShabouryWed 22 Jul 2026 15.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 17.59 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleLeandro Trossard to BesiktasAnnounced just beforeEngland’s soul-sucking semi-final defeat by Argentina, it would have been easy to miss Trossard’s £15.3m move to Istanbul. While, after three and a half seasons and at the age of 31, it feels like his time at Arsenal has come to a natural conclusion, he will be remembered as a player who scored in clutch moments and, ultimately,played a crucial role in the club’s first league title in 22 years. Trossard departs after 174 appearances and 36 goals, a significant portion of which came from the bench in times of desperate need. He will be missed and deserves to be revered as one of the cult heroes of the Mikel Arteta era.Will MageeNathaniel Brown to Bayern MunichFew players emerged from Germany’s World Cup campaign with any credit, but Brown’s energetic displays at left-back have earned him a move to Bayern Munich. Brown is a proud Bavarian, born in the town of Amberg and raised in Nürnberg’s youth academy. After two years atEintracht Frankfurtand eight Germany caps, Brown has taken the quick route to his nation’s dominant club and could be a very useful addition. With Raphaël Guerreiro leaving and Alphonso Davies still recovering from injury, Vincent Kompany will benefit from Brown’s abilities as a natural left-back, after using a rotating cast of players to patch up Bayern’s defence last term.Niall McVeighView image in fullscreenNathaniel Brown looking pretty pleased to have signed for Bayern Munich.Photograph: S Stingl/FC Bayern/Getty ImagesDenzel Dumfries to Real MadridThe Netherlands right-back is often a force at major tournaments. He scored twice at Euro 2020, which earned him a move from PSV to Inter, and helped the Netherlands past the USA in the last 16 of the 2022 World Cup with a goal and two assists. In North America, the 30-year-old provided two assists –both against Swedenat the group stage – as the Netherlands reached the last 32. Following their exit to Morocco, Dumfries put pen to paper on a four-year deal at Real Madrid after his release clause at Inter was triggered – he joins Bernardo Silva and Ibrahima Konaté as freebie arrivals. A Hollywood move for a player named after Denzel Washington.Billy MundayEmersonn to IpswichAnnounced just before the World Cup quarter-finals, the 22-year-old Brazilian striker​ arrived inToulouseonly last summer but did enough in a single Ligue 1 campaign to convince the newly promoted Premier League side to part with £24m. Standing 6ft 3in, Emersonn combines an old-fashioned target-man frame with the mobility to drift into wide areas​. Although his raw return of ​s​ix goals in ​2​7 league appearances suggests this is a transfer built on potential rather than proven output​, Gary O’Neil clearly sees shades of the modern Premier League No 9: aggressive, hard-running and capable of unsettling defenders even when the goals are not flowing. “He is a young striker with real ability and potential who has had plenty of interest this summer,” the manager said.Yara El-ShabouryHarry Winks to CagliariThere is a parallel universe where Harry Winks became a key cog in England’s midfield, earning many more caps than the 10 he has in reality. Mauricio Pochettino once suggested the tidy midfielder could be England’s answer to Andrés Iniesta. It has not worked out that way: injuries stalled Winks’s Spurs career and the struggles have continued at Leicester, despite a brief revival under Enzo Maresca. Now 30, Winks joins the Everton youngster Demi Akarakiri at Cagliari, whose only previous English player – forward Gerry Hitchens – left in 1969. It will not be Winks’s first spell in Serie A, having spent a year on loan at Sampdoria in 2022-23, which ended in relegation. This time, he may be seeking stability and a slower pace in Sardinia.NMcView image in fullscreenHarry Winks (right) has left Leicester City for a new Serie A adventure.Photograph: Plumb Images/Leicester City/Getty ImagesMathys Detourbet to Manchester CityWhile Detourbet’s £21.6m transfer to the Etihad Stadium may have been overshadowed bythe unveiling of Enzo Maresca two days laterandElliot Anderson’s £116m arrivalthe following week – not to mention the end of the World Cup group stages – the 19-year-old was the club’s first signing of the summer. Joining from Troyes, part of the City Football Group stable, having played for the French side since the age of eight, it was an intriguing move for a teenage winger described as “a source of great pride” by his former club. With three goals and six assists in 33 appearances in Ligue 2 last season, helping Troyes win promotion as champions, he has shown plenty of promise. Loaned out to Monaco for the coming campaign, he will be aiming to make his mark in Ligue 1 and catch Maresca’s eye in the process.WMIllan Meslier to ArsenalHe may face a steep uphill struggle to displace David Raya as No 1, but Meslier joining Arsenal on a free transfer could feasibly dislodge Kepa Arrizabalaga. With trust in Raya’s backup undermined by his lacklustre performance inthe Carabao Cup final last season, the arrival of the former Leeds goalkeeper appears to open the door for his departure. Meslier is five years younger than Arrizabalaga and has 107 Premier League appearances under his belt, but fell behind Karl Darlow and Lucas Perri in the pecking order at Elland Road last term. Often criticised for high-profile mistakes at Leeds, it is up to him to win over the sceptics and, if Arrizabalaga does leave, to make the No 2 role his own.WMView image in fullscreenIllan Meslier, who will play backup to David Raya, settles into his new surroundings at Arsenal.Photograph: Stuart MacFarlane/Arsenal FC/Getty ImagesGonçalo Ramos to MilanMilan broke their club-record transfer fee when they paid £60.2m to sign Ramos at the end of June. With 45 goals in 131 appearances forParis Saint-Germain, helping them to three league titles and two Champions League triumphs, the 25-year-old has been a useful player for Luis Enrique’s side but was never going to be the main man in an attack featuring Khvicha Kvaratskhelia, Ousmane Dembélé and Désiré Doué. It was telling that he had to make do with late substitute appearances in PSG’s two victorious Champions League finals, though he did score the opener in the shootout against Arsenal. As Ruben Amorim’s first signing at Milan, he will have top billing whereas at PSG he was destined to be a support act.WMView image in fullscreenGonçalo Ramos (centre) poses with Milan’s head coach, Ruben Amorim (right), and the owner, Gerry Cardinale, after signing with the club.Photograph: Claudio Villa/AC Milan/Getty ImagesRobert Lewandowski to Chicago FirePoland did not qualify for the World Cup but that has not stopped their greatest player from making the trip to the US this summer. Lewandowski said he could not imagine himself at a European club other thanBarcelonaafter ending a four-year spell in Catalonia that yielded three La Liga titles and 120 goals. The 37-year-old found the net 19 times at the highest level last season and is still in remarkable physical shape, so there is a chance of a Zlatan Ibrahimovic-style impact on Major League Soccer, just without the bluster. The former USA coach Gregg Berhalter is his new boss in Chicago.BMJakub Kaminski to Köln … and BenficaNot content with making one under-the-radar move, Kaminski has made two this summer. After breaking through at Lech Poznan, the Poland winger moved to Wolfsburg in 2022 but fell from favour, and spent last season on loan at Köln. He impressed for the Bundesliga returnees, playing every league game and filling a wide range of attacking roles. Köln paid €5.5m (£4.7m) to sign him permanently in May, but Marco Silva’s Benfica swooped to pay Kaminski’s €20m (£17.1m) release clause just two months later. Köln have made a healthy profit – and invested a portion of it onManchester Cityyoungster Reigan Heskey – but have seen a key summer recruit slip away.NMcExplore more on these topicsTransfer windowArsenalBesiktasInternazionaleReal MadridEintracht FrankfurtBayern MunichfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: New Rangers manager Derek McInnes gets four-match ban for Celtic penalty remarks | Scottish Premiership | The Guardian
description: Derek McInnes, the new Rangers manager, is poised to begin the season in the stands after being hit with a four-match ban for his comments on Celtic’s controversial penalty at Motherwell last season
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Derek McInnes, the new Rangers manager, is poised to begin the season in the stands after being hit with a four-match ban for his comments on Celtic’s controversial penalty at Motherwell last season
Content: The Rangers manager, Derek McInnes, talks with Lawrence Shankland and Emmanuel Fernandez during a pre-season trip to Spain.Photograph: Kirk O’Rourke/Rangers FC/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenThe Rangers manager, Derek McInnes, talks with Lawrence Shankland and Emmanuel Fernandez during a pre-season trip to Spain.Photograph: Kirk O’Rourke/Rangers FC/ShutterstockScottish PremiershipNew Rangers manager Derek McInnes gets four-match ban for Celtic penalty remarksFormer Hearts coach railed at ‘disgusting’ VAR decisionSFA punishment means ban for start of new seasonPA MediaWed 22 Jul 2026 13.01 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 15.53 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDerek McInnes, the newRangersmanager, is poised to begin the season in the stands after being handed a four-match ban for his comments on Celtic’s controversial penalty at Motherwell last season.The then-Hearts manager described the VAR decision to give Celtic a stoppage-time spot-kick – which proved crucial in theScottish Premiershiptitle race – as “disgusting”. On Wednesday the Scottish Football Association announced McInnes would receive a four-match ban, with one game suspended until the end of the season.McInnes watched Hearts beat Falkirk 3-0 in the penultimate game of the league season, which looked like they would have had to avoid a three-goal defeat at Parkhead on the final day to win the title for the first time since 1960. However, the scenario changed later that night when Celtic were awarded a penalty by the referee John Beaton –after an interjection by VAR Andrew Dallas– deep into added time at Fir Park.Kelechi Iheanacho scored to secure a 3-2 win that meant Martin O’Neill’s side needed any victory at all on the final day to keep the title in the east end of Glasgow for the fifth successive time, which the Hoops clinchedwith another late winner.Asked for his view of the penalty decision after beating Falkirk, McInnes described it as “disgusting” and he was charged with breaking rule 72 of the SFA disciplinary guidelines and issued with an official notice of complaint. A hearing at Hampden on Wednesday found the charge proven and McInnes was given a four-match suspension, with one match suspended until the end of the 2026-27 season.Rangers begin their league season with an away game against Dundee United on Friday week. The suspension would mean McInnes is banned from the touchline for that game, as well as home matches against Hibernian and St Mirren.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe then Motherwell midfielder Elliot Watt received a four-match suspension after using his X account to describe the same incident as “the worst VAR decision in history”. Watt, now at the Turkish club Samsunspor, was charged with breaching the Scottish FA’s disciplinary rule 72, which says players should not “criticise” decisions made by officials or make comments that “impinge” on the character of a referee.Explore more on these topicsScottish PremiershipRangersCelticHeartsnewsShareReuse this content

title: World Cup 2026 awards: the Guardian team give their final tournament verdicts | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: The best games, goals and personal highlights – from a magical Azteca to Erling Haaland breaking America
Author: Guardian sport
Headline: The best games, goals and personal highlights – from a magical Azteca to Erling Haaland breaking America
Content: Clockwise from top: Cape Verde’s keeper Vozinha; England and Mexico fans outside the Azteca; Spain captain Rodri with the World Cup; a Scotland fan; the dazzling Monterrey Stadium.Composite: Guardian Designl Imagn Images/Reuters; PA; Fifa/Getty Images; AMA/Getty Images; Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenClockwise from top: Cape Verde’s keeper Vozinha; England and Mexico fans outside the Azteca; Spain captain Rodri with the World Cup; a Scotland fan; the dazzling Monterrey Stadium.Composite: Guardian Designl Imagn Images/Reuters; PA; Fifa/Getty Images; AMA/Getty Images; Getty ImagesWorld Cup 2026World Cup 2026 awards: the Guardian team give their final tournament verdictsThe best games, goals and personal highlights – from a magical Azteca to Erling Haaland breaking AmericaGuardian sportWed 22 Jul 2026 05.01 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 05.13 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleMatch of the tournamentParaguay1-1 Germany.There were plenty more games with more goals but none could match the drama of the penalty shootout in this last-32 tie. Germany aren’t supposed to lose in thembut they did this timein spectacular fashion.Ed AaronsAlgeria 3-3 Austriawas a completely strange fever dream, seeming to exist on a separate plane of reality. I know that sounds grandiose but remember, going into this one the expectation was that it would be abiscotto– a meaningless, pre-arranged kickabout where a draw would see both sides through. For a while in the second half, it looked like that would happen. And finally,in stoppage time, chaos. There were certainly better matches in terms of quality, and maybe a couple that had this beat for drama, but no game was weirder.Alexander AbnosThe quirky side of World Cup 2026 – in picturesRead moreFrom the 14 I attended, it has to beMexico2-3 Englandat the awesome Azteca. The buildup seemed to start as soon as the draw was made – fortunately the stars aligned anda grippingly tense gameensued in a venue like no other. It was a high point for England that should cut through despite later disappointments, too.Nick AmesFrance 4-6 Englandwas borderline hilarious.Egypt 1-1 Iran in Seattlehad plenty of late drama, Iran’s disallowed stoppage-time winner bookended by them twice hitting the woodwork in the final minutes. The draw ultimately proved insufficient for unbeaten Iran, faced with endless hurdles, to progress.Ben FisherArgentina 3-2 Egypt.The defending champions looked down and out, thinking about their flights home, before a lateLionel Messi-inspired comebackenabled them to claw back a two-goal deficit and win this last-16 match in normal time.Barry GlendenningThe gameno one wanted to play.Sid LoweMexico 2-3 England. I have decided that all my answers should relate to things that happened while I was at the tournament and this was one. I mean, I wasn’t there ticking off the “bucket list” of a visit to the Azteca (I think I saw at least half a dozen British hacks claim that), but I did watch it on TV. Besides being like drinking cortisol neat, this was so dramatic, the images so evocative, that even if you are an ardent England fan (as I am) this was pure cinema.Paul MacInnesView image in fullscreenEngland’s players sing Wonderwall in front of their fans after their last-16 victory over Mexico.Photograph: José Méndez/EPAI am biased here in the sense I attended it butCape Verde2-2 Uruguayin Miami was superb. Cape Verde’s attitude and discipline were a joy.Such fun to watch.Ewan MurrayArgentina3-2 Egypt. Incredible drama, despite the questionablebehaviour of the referee.Osasu ObayiuwanaTough to pick from over 100 options, butMexico 2-3 Englandwith Thomas Tuchel’s men surviving the vaunted Azteca and the best first half Mexico could have mustered narrowly takes this category.Jeff RueterBias says Mexico v England, butArgentina 3-2 Cape Verdewas incredible, Sidny Lopes Cabral bending it top right.Max RushdenFor improbable wonder there was Argentina v Cape Verde, for sheer unpredictability there was Mexico v England. Often, though, one is drawn to something more predictable,more familiar:England 1-2 Argentina.Jack SnapeArgentina 3-2 Cape Verde. It’s not a debate.Jacob SteinbergArgentina 3-2 Egypt. The possibility of a shock, a great Messi-inspired turnaround, a couple of great goals, refereeing controversy … what else do you want?Jonathan WilsonView image in fullscreenLionel Messi (second left) fires a shot past Egypt’s keeper Mostafa Shobeir for Argentina’s equaliser in their 3-2 win.Photograph: Elsa/Getty ImagesPlayer of the tournamentRodriwasthe standout playerfor the best team in a tournament where the big stars came to the party but all ultimately fell short.EAKylian Mbappé. Yes, his Golden Boot was padded a bit by that insane third-place game, but the France striker was the best player on what looked to be the best team in the tournament ... at least untilthat loss to Spainin the semi-final.AAIt’s impossible to look further thanRodri, who looks back to his best and absolutely in his element as the fulcrum of this Spain setup. Otherwise it was good to see aWorld Cupwhere the stars largely shone. Jude Bellingham, Erling Haaland, Messi and Mbappé were all one more decisive knockout display away from rising above the rest.NARodri. Michael Olise was majestic, though should have scored a hat-trick against England. Bellingham was equally instrumental for Tuchel’s side – it is crazy to think there were people questioning his inclusion in the squad.BFRodri–capitán, líder, leyenda.BGLionel Messi.SLVozinha.What a man. I think it’s largely because his story is so relatable in these parts – middle-aged man finally receives the plaudits he has always deserved! – but the saves were also top drawer. Add in the pedigree of the teams he either shut out or frustrated, and this was an actual fairytale that didn’t need to be spun by any marketing department. And did you know that he’s 40?PMPau Cubarsí.EMVozinha, the Cape Verde goalkeeper. At 40, and without a club, he was brilliant for the World Cup debutants.OOWhile Rodri is an incredibly worthy Golden Ball winner and others made their marks,Erling Haalandleaves this World Cup with the greatest possible souvenirs: a credible if short Golden Boot campaign, a famous first Norwegian foray into the quarter-final stage, and perhaps the biggestboost in star appealin the lucrative North American market.JRView image in fullscreenEven the World Cup mascots stand in awe of Erling Haaland.Photograph: Maddie Meyer/Fifa/Getty ImagesHard not to sayRodrialthough I thought Dani Olmo was great too, and a shout for Pico Lopes.MRHaaland, Mbappé and Messi have lived up to expectations. But in those moments where a contest teeters – evading pressure with the ball, in transition to defence or staring down a 50-50 – no player has shown his quality more consistently thanRodri.JSnRodri. If only England had a player like the Spain captain.JStRodri. As Spain have reverted to a more possession-driven model, their metronome in midfield has been the outstanding figure, setting the tempo and providing control.JWGoal of the tournamentFor sheer drama, it has to beSidny Lopes Cabral’s brilliant equaliser for Cape Verde against Argentina. His reaction was almost as good as the goal itself, clasping his hands to his head before picking out his girlfriend in the crowd to celebrate with.EAI mean, it’sSidny Lopes Cabralv Argentina. But I feel that the goal from Uzbekistan’s Eldor Shomurodov against the Democratic Republic of the Congo deserves mention too: an instinctive, oddly satisfying lob from a tight angle. No other goal in the tournament was quite like it.AAJulián Alvarezhad a largely quiet World Cup but his vicious, swerving long-range goalin extra time against Switzerlandwas a jaw-dropping moment and lit up the cauldron-like stadium in Kansas City.NAThose seen in the flesh always seem to score more points. A buzz surrounded Bosnia and Herzegovina’sKerim Alajbegoviccoming into the tournament and the 18-year-old eliminating a clutch of defenders and blasting in, off balance, from the outside of the boxagainst Qataronly enhanced his reputation. Johan Manzambi’s run and pass in the buildup to Breel Embolo’s openeragainst Algeriawas also a joy.BFView image in fullscreenBosnia and Herzegovina’s Kerim Alajbegovic is embraced after opening the scoring against Qatar.Photograph: Carlos Barría/ReutersWhile Morocco’s superior quality would ultimately see them through their group-stagewin against Haiti,Wilson Isidor’s breathtaking long-range strike to put the Caribbean nation 2-1 up was as good a goal as any scored in the World Cup. Ever.BGSidny Lopes Cabral, of course.SLI had the privilege of reading the paper by the Seoul Women’s University on the aerodynamic effectknown as “drag crisis”which is particularly common when people smack an Adidas Trionda, so as such I thought a lot of the big hits were overrated. I do, however, exemptSidny Lopes Cabralfrom that critique. Absolute long-range worldie from the Cape Verde full-back-cum-winger. That it pegged Argentina to parity in extra time of a knockout round only made it more memorable.PMHarry Kane’s second forEngland against DRC.EMSidny Lopes Cabralof Cape Verde, against Argentina. Magical goal.OOI will never, in all my years, be able to recreate the sound that left my body asSidny Lopes Cabral’s audacious effort against Argentina completed its curl between a diving Emi Martínez and his far post. Masterful execution in an unprecedented moment. One of many proud moments for Cape Verde at this tournament.JRskip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionView image in fullscreenCape Verde’s Sidny Lopes Cabral unleashes his audacious effort …Photograph: Newscom/AlamyView image in fullscreen… which flies past Argentina’s keeper Emi Martínez to level the scores in extra time.Photograph: Paul Childs/ReutersBeyondSidny Lopes Cabral– the disallowed Uzbekistan one (was it Uzbekistan?) – someone walloped one into the top left-hand corner and it didn’t count.MRErling Haaland’s secondagainst Brazil, placed into the bottom-right corner from outside the box to seal the upset, was a microcosm of his effectiveness at the tournament: precise and powerful with a hint of whimsy.JSnSidny Lopes Cabral’s equaliser for Cape Verde against Argentina was a proper jaw-dropping moment. One of the great World Cup goals.JStWilson Isidorv Morocco. There were more consequential goals, but none struck quite as sweetly or quite as accurately. If Haiti have nothing else, they’ll always have that.JWPersonal highlightIt was a joy to watchEloy Room’s performance in goal forCuraçao against Ecuadoras he broke the World Cup record for the most saves made in a 90-minute match. While Cape Verde’s veteran Vozinha stole most of the headlines for his heroics, the 37-year-old Room clearly enjoyed his moment in the spotlight. “I think I need a statue in Curaçao now,” he joked.EAFifa and the stadium authorities typically guard the natural grass pitches heavily, and with good reason. But after the last games in each venue I went to, I loved watching those pitches get taken over bythe people whose names you’ll never knowbut who actually made this tournament run: stadium workers and low-level Fifa staffers, volunteers, security guards, longtime local club representatives and others. Some used the opportunity to have a little kickabout, take selfies, or just run around. An awesome perk for what were almost certainly very hard, stressful jobs over these last several weeks.AAMy first visit to theAztecawasMexico’s win over Ecuadorand I’ve never heard a noise like it. The place really is as magical and monumental as they say. The celebrations afterwards weren’t conducive to a good night’s sleep but were incredible in scale too. Football-wise I’ll also addMessi’s record-breaking goals against Austriain Dallas, which seem an awfully long time ago now but were such a thrill to witness. Those early Argentina games were essentially mass Messi pilgrimages. One more? The view from Monterrey Stadium. Just go, if you can.NAView image in fullscreenA view of Mexico’s last-32 game against Ecuador from high up inside the Azteca stands.Photograph: Hannah Peters/Fifa/Getty ImagesWatching Senegalese journalists pass around a pack ofmalted milksduring their anthem before theircollapse against Belgium. And playing eight-a-side with Danny Cipriani, who once spent six weeks training at Tottenham, Harry Kane among his teammates, will stick in the memory.BFWatching myIrish compatriotRoberto “Pico” Lopes excel while repeatedly putting his body on the line for Cape Verde, his father’s homeland. It would be nice to think Pico’s performances will earn him a life-changing move to a more lucrative league in the twilight of his career but his age could stand against him and he may well be content with being a Shamrock Rovers and Cape Verde legend.BGExplaining to a mariachi in agigantic sombrerohow it could possibly be that a team in third could get through the group and how it might still be Spain turning up at the Azteca. Alas, it wasn’t. Sharing a birthday with Martin Ainstein and Luis de la Fuente and my family. And watching a motorcade with the Spain bus pass from the sixth-floor window where Lee Harvey Oswald shot JFK was pretty surreal.SLI loved watching bothFrance eviscerate Swedenand the Instagram reels of Americans acting like they thought theTartan Armyhad permanently changed their lives.PMView image in fullscreenMembers of the Tartan Army have a kickabout in a Boston street.Photograph: Peter Cziborra/ReutersPlayers running out to warm-up to the tune ofScotlandthe Brave, via bagpipes, beforethe game against Haitiin Foxborough. Scotland’s 28-year World Cup wait was over. The less said about all that happened next, the better.EMSeeingVozinha’s mum,against all expectations, travelling from Cape Verde to the World Cup to see her son play.OOHaving covered the nation’s third-to-fifth most popular sport for more than a decade and lived throughICE’s heinous occupation of Minnesotathis winter, the sheer scale of interest in soccer was overwhelming and so invigorating –a welcome reprievein the isolationist second Trump era. Impossible to go through a couple of hours in a World Cup host city without some stranger striking up a conversation about the sport. Long may this remain a part of American society.JRShoutingParaguay. Number 10. Cover his mouth. Decision is RED CARD. Jonathan Wilson thinking Robbie Williams was Kevin Pietersen. Mark Langdon’s chow mein bridge.The pancakesat Mile End Deli. Doing a live show in Manhattan and legitimately getting to say “HELLO NEW YORK!”MRCoveringAustralia, the tournament petered out from an extraordinary high. Twenty-six minutes into the Socceroos’opening victory against Turkey, goalscorer Nestory Irankunda revived Tim Cahill’s goal celebration in a ceremonial handover to mark a new generation.JSnView image in fullscreenAustralia’s Nestory Irankunda throws punches while shadowboxing the corner flag, à la Tim Cahill.Photograph: Kaleb Tatum/APBeing at theAztecafor Mexico 2-3 England. It did not have the most quality but it was hard to beat as an occasion. An epic night at an iconic stadium. A thunderstorm pushing kick-off back, a brilliant display from Bellingham, everyone exhausted when it was over. Things didn’t end well for England but this was easily their greatest away win.JStStanding knee-deep in raging floods in Monterrey waiting for a bus having only gone to the stadium to collect a mislaid iPad that I then lost in Houston a week later anyway. That or theweirdly beautiful flyoversoutside Dallas.JWWhat did you make of the hosts?The level of interest from the American public surprised me and it was fascinating to meet so many who were interested in what was happening in the tournament. Many people in the US definitely caught World Cup fever. Logistically it was a real test of endurance with so much travelling required between venues and that wasn’t helped by so many being miles from the city centre. And it would have been much better to have played the final in Mexico City or Atlanta’s impressive downtown stadium.EAI felt unfortunate not to make it to any games in Mexico and only one in Canada. But overall I’m happy that the everyday people who populate the US seemed to show visitors their good side throughout the month. This country has plenty of infrastructure issues I’d like to have seen fixed, but the personal impact is arguably more important, and it seems like the US mostly got that right.AAIf first impressions are everything then I refer you to the often troublingweirdness of the Texas Republican party conventionin Houston. But more seriously I was very happy, in the US, to find the hoped-for warmth and openness pretty much everywhere – and in bundles. In their different ways, people really did engage with the World Cup. Less welcome was the US administration’s attempt, duly pandered to by Fifa, to bend the course of the tournament withFolarin Balogun’s reprieve. In Mexico I found a fervent, febrile, fascinating football culture that deserved not to be dwarfed by other co-hosts.NAAmericans love a big-ticket event but it was clear they were hooked by Mauricio Pochettino’s side as they progressed. It was a privilege to witnessCanada’s first World Cup gameon home soil in Toronto, where there was a match-day buzz and an afterglow despite being held to a draw by Bosnia and Herzegovina.BFView image in fullscreenCanada fans react to Cyle Larin’s goal during their opening group game against Bosnia and Herzegovina.Photograph: Joosep Martinson/Fifa/Getty ImagesWhile I didn’t meet Donald Trump, the handful of Americans whose acquaintance I did make – hotel staff, a few bartenders and one LA-based reality TV show star – could not have been friendlier.BGSo many people came up to us to say they really hoped we were enjoying this and that we understood thatthatwasn’t them. We did and they were so nice.SLI spent two days in Toronto, around Canada Day, and I think they were both welcoming and very keen to hear that they qualified as an actual football nation. People in the US were pleasant, although for travelling fans most experiences will have been complicated by the ubiquitous request for a tip. I did think, though, that sometimes there was an expectation that we should all be delighted to be there. Which we were, but perhaps not for the reasons some wanted.PMCanada and Mexico? Excellent. US stadiums were first class, as was the extent to which the nation embraced the World Cup. It is just impossible to ignore the stench of the Balogun situation.EMOn an on-field football level? Far from spectacular. The US needs a new player-development architecture to ensure there is a pipeline of high-quality players. They also need a new head coach, with the right vision for the future. Tournament organisation? The fact that Fifa took direct control of it should be a cautionary tale. A local organising committee is always needed.OOView image in fullscreenMauricio Pochettino speaks to his USA players after their defeat by Belgium which eliminated the co-hosts.Photograph: Agustín Marcarian/ReutersAll three co-hosts fared about as well as I had projected, but each may have exceeded my expectations in terms of how they went about it. Canada were thrilling at times – I covered their6-0 win over Qatarat BC Place – and had plenty of pluck with Jesse Marsch as their coach-cum-hype man. The USA played some of the prettiest soccer ever seen by a US national team of any gender, and will for ever rue a missed opportunity to do something greateragainst Belgium. Mexico were one of the biggest bandwagon teams as they barnstormed Group A, then outclassed an Ecuador team on the rise and pushed England to the brink until they ran out of ideas in the final half-hour. Jobs well done across the board.JRI had six weeks in America. All the Americans were lovely.MRThis tournament put the whirled in the World Cup, its scale ensuring it evaded generalisation. Cities such as Vancouver and Seattle – with downtown stadiums and soccer traditions – were fitting hosts, while metropolises such as Dallas and San Francisco accommodated the month-long novelty without embracing it.JSnThe people in Mexico could not have been more welcoming, even when England beat their side. It was the same in Kansas City, England’s base and where I spent most of my time. On the other hand it would have been better if getting around some US cities was easier. Public transport infrastructure isn’t really a thing in some places. Florida especially. The Boston traffic was ridiculous, too.JStA wide range depending on venue but broadly friendly, welcoming and enthusiastic. Would benefit from improved public transport and generally lower volume. Wifi hopeless, coffee weak.JWExplore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026World CupUS sportsAustralia sportfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: One billion plastic pellets spilled into River Tyne after two ships collide | Pollution | The Guardian
description: Up to 25 tonnes of nurdles, the raw material for plastic manufacturing, have spread further than initially feared
Author: Pippa Neill
Headline: Up to 25 tonnes of nurdles, the raw material for plastic manufacturing, have spread further than initially feared
Content: People who live in the surrounding area said nurdles covered the whole stretch of South Shields beach.Photograph: FidraView image in fullscreenPeople who live in the surrounding area said nurdles covered the whole stretch of South Shields beach.Photograph: FidraPollutionOne billion plastic pellets spilled into River Tyne after two ships collideUp to 25 tonnes of nurdles, the raw material for plastic manufacturing, have spread further than initially fearedPippa NeillEnvironment reporterWed 22 Jul 2026 12.14 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 11.34 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleApproximately a billion tiny plastic nurdles have entered the River Tyne after a “devastating spill”, with the pellets thought to have spread much further than first thought.Nurdles, which are usually 1mm-5mm in size, act as the raw material to manufacture most plastic products.On Sunday, the Wind Orca ship collided with the berthed container ship BG Orange at the Port of Tyne, causing damage to a dockside crane and injuring one person.The collision caused up to 25 tonnes of nurdles – about a billion in total, according to environmental charityFidra– to enter the water, said South Tyneside council.After nurdles were found washed up on South Shields beach, the council warned residents to avoid handling the pellets and to keep dogs on leads. The pellets themselves are not toxic. But once in the environment, they are almost impossible to remove and can absorb toxins present in the water.Animals can easily mistake the nurdles for food. They have been found in the stomachs and digestive tracts of a variety of species, from seabirds in the North Sea and Australia to green turtles and fish off the coasts of Brazil.The spill is less than 3 miles (4.8km) away from Marsden Bay, which is home to one of England’s most important seabird colonies.View image in fullscreenThe tiny plastic pellets act as the raw material in the manufacture of most plastic products.Photograph: Alistair LeadbetterOne person who lives locally, Alistair Leadbetter, told the Guardian he was “really shocked” at how many nurdles he saw and said they covered the whole stretch of the South Shields beach. He said he saw children playing on the beach among the pellets.Once nurdles are lost to the environment they can easily be washed, swept or blown vast distances and can accumulate on beaches worldwide. Last year, Fidrafound plastic nurdles at 84% of UK sites of special scientific interest.Fidra’snurdle map, which tracks global nurdle pollution, indicates the pellets may have already spread much further than first thought, with sightings reported as far south as Sunderland, about 7 miles from South Shields.HM Coastguard said it is supporting local authorities in the initial response to the pollution along the North Tyneside coastline, including at Tynemouth Longsands, Cullercoats and King Edward’s Bay.Heather McFarlane, senior project manager at Fidra, said it is “devastating to see another plastic pellet spill”. She said most pellet pollution can be prevented with simple measures such as better packaging and safer stowage.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toDown to EarthThe planet's most important stories. Get all the week's environment news - the good, the bad and the essentialPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionView image in fullscreenEnvironmental campaigners have called for stronger regulation on pollution after the incident in South Shields.Photograph: Alistair Leadbetter“We urgently need stronger UK regulation to prevent pollution on land and mandatory measures at sea to stop spills like this in the future,” McFarlane said.A spokesperson for South Tyneside council said it is now assessing the extent of the contamination and supporting efforts to remove pellets from affected areas where appropriate. “The council will look to recover the costs of the clean-up from the insurers of those responsible,” they said.An Environment Agency spokesperson said its officers are “working with local authorities and other partners, providing expert advice to support in the early stages of the clean-up operation”.The Guardian has contacted the Port of Tyne and the owners of the Wind Orca for comment.Get in touchContact us about this storyThe best public interest journalism relies on first-hand accounts from people in the know. If you have something to share on this subject, you can contact us confidentially using the following methods:Secure Messaging in the Guardian appThe Guardian app has a tool to send tips about stories. Messages are end to end encrypted and concealed within the routine activity that every Guardian mobile app performs. This prevents an observer from knowing that you are communicating with us at all, let alone what is being said.If you don’t already have the Guardian app, download it (iOS/Android) and go to the menu. Select ‘Secure Messaging’.SecureDropIf you can safely use the tor network without being observed or monitored you can send messages and documents to the Guardian via ourSecureDrop platform.Our guide attheguardian.com/tipslists several ways to contact us securely, and discusses the pros and cons of each.Show moreExplore more on these topicsPollutionPlasticsTyne and WearnewsShareReuse this content

title: Ex-Southern Water boss to face trial over allegedly manipulating sewage testing | UK news | The Guardian
description: Former CEO Matthew Wright and three other ex-employees named in criminal proceedings by Environment Agency
Author: Sandra Laville
Headline: Former CEO Matthew Wright and three other ex-employees named in criminal proceedings by Environment Agency
Content: Swalecliffe wastewater treatment works, operated by Southern Water, in Whitstable, Kent.Photograph: Chris J Ratcliffe/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenSwalecliffe wastewater treatment works, operated by Southern Water, in Whitstable, Kent.Photograph: Chris J Ratcliffe/Getty ImagesUK newsEx-Southern Water boss to face trial over allegedly manipulating sewage testingFormer CEO Matthew Wright and three other ex-employees named in criminal proceedings by Environment AgencySandra LavilleWed 22 Jul 2026 12.54 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 08.58 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe former chief executive of Southern Water is being prosecuted for conspiracy to defraud along with three other former employees, over allegations they manipulated sewage wastewater testing results to avoid tens of millions of pounds in fines.Matthew Wright is the first water industry boss to face a criminal charge over sewage discharges. He and three others are charged with conspiracy to defraud the Environment Agency and ​the water regulator, Ofwat, between 2012 and 2017 by ⁠creating artificial “no-flow” ​events ​at wastewater treatment ​works to ​influence ‌compliance checks.The case focuses on operator self-monitoring, which was introduced to cut regulatory costs in 2009. The other former employees are Philip Barker, Clive Massey and Mark Gregory.Operator self-monitoring allows water companies to monitor and test discharges from their wastewater treatment works, rather than the testing being carried out by an independent regulator.Strict rules govern how samples must be taken to ensure their independence and accuracy. It is an offence if there is evidence that flows or data have been deliberately manipulated to avoid compliance checks.Southern Water, which has more than 4 million customers, is also being charged with breaching environmental permits linked to the same conduct. Three further individuals are also being charged linked to failure to comply with environmental permits.The charges come after a long investigation by the agency. An attempt by the defendants to remain anonymous was overruled by the high court.An Environment Agency spokesperson said: “We can confirm that we are taking criminal proceedings against Southern Water Services Ltd and a number of former employees.“We take our responsibility to protect the environment very seriously and will always pursue and prosecute those alleged to have committed serious offending against the environment.”In a judgment published on Wednesday, the ​high court rejected Wright’s argument that the EA lacked the power to prosecute him for conspiracy to defraud.The ruling clears the way for the Environment Agency’s prosecution to proceed ahead of a criminal trial due to begin in September.According to the judgment, the EA alleges that by the company’s own assessment, the scheme allowed it to avoid penalties of about £45m, but the regulator’s assessment put the figure higher.Conspiracy to defraud carries a maximum sentence of 10 years’ imprisonment. The case will now proceed at Medway magistrates court.The environment secretary, Angela Eagle, said: “The public are rightly sick of environmental pollution and attempting to cover it up is outrageous. Anyone found guilty of this crime will face the full force of the law.“This government will always back tough enforcement action to protect our rivers, lakes and seas. We have already committed to ending the practice of operator self-monitoring to ensure this cannot happen again in the future.”Get in touchContact us about this storyThe best public interest journalism relies on first-hand accounts from people in the know. If you have something to share on this subject, you can contact us confidentially using the following methods:Secure Messaging in the Guardian appThe Guardian app has a tool to send tips about stories. Messages are end to end encrypted and concealed within the routine activity that every Guardian mobile app performs. This prevents an observer from knowing that you are communicating with us at all, let alone what is being said.If you don’t already have the Guardian app, download it (iOS/Android) and go to the menu. title: House passes $1.15tn defense bill despite standoff over Israel and Trump’s voter agenda | House of Representatives | The Guardian
description: Passage of annual bill complicated by Trump’s decision to join Israel in attacking Iran without congressional approval
Author: Chris Stein
Headline: Passage of annual bill complicated by Trump’s decision to join Israel in attacking Iran without congressional approval
Content: Tehran, Iran, after US-Israeli attacks, on 4 March 2026.Photograph: NurPhoto/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenTehran, Iran, after US-Israeli attacks, on 4 March 2026.Photograph: NurPhoto/Getty ImagesHouse of RepresentativesHouse passes $1.15tn defense bill despite standoff over Israel and Trump’s voter agendaPassage of annual bill complicated by Trump’s decision to join Israel in attacking Iran without congressional approvalChris SteinWed 22 Jul 2026 17.00 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 16.44 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe US House of Representatives passed a $1.15tn defense policy bill in a near-party-line vote on Wednesday, after Democrats refused to back the measure amid the war with Iran andDonald Trump’s push to impose new restrictions on voting nationwide.A key plank ofthe president’s planto spend $1.5tn on the military in the 2027 fiscal year, the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA), passed the Republican-controlled chamber by a 216-212 vote, with all but six Democrats opposing its passage. Seven House Republicans broke ranks to opposethe measure, which now goes to the Senate.The NDAA is a mammoth bill with effects across the armed services, and Congress passes it each year with bipartisan margins. This year’s version includes pay raises of 5-7% for the military, as well as the codification of the name “Department of War” for the Pentagon. The bill’s large overall cost is itself a key feature, which top HouseRepublicanssay is necessary to make generational investments in the armed forces.“For the first time in 40 years, we’ve been presented a budget that accounts for the true cost of American deterrence. This topline begins to reverse the damage caused by decades of underinvestment in theUS military,” Mike Rogers, the House armed services committee chair, said before the vote.The NDAA’s path to enactment has been complicated by the president’s decision to join Israel in attacking Iran without first seeking authorization from Congress. Last week, Senate Democratsblockedthe advancement of that chamber’s version of the NDAA in protest of the war, which has flared anew after a memorandum of understanding meant to spur peace talks between the US and Irancollapsed.Democrats also soured on the bill after Mike Johnson, the Republican House speaker, acceded to the demands of rightwing lawmakers and inserted into the legislation the Save America Act, a Trump-backed proposal to ban mail-in ballots and impose new identification restrictions on voters at the polls and when they register.The party also objected to the overall prospect of allocating $1.15tn to the military while slashing funding elsewhere in the federal government, just a year after Congress’s Republican majorities approvedextending an arrayof lower tax rates indefinitely.“The annual NDAA used to be a consistently bipartisan, substantive piece of legislation,” said Rosa DeLauro, the top Democratic appropriator. “I am disappointed that under Republican leadership, it has become just another partisan bill that is written to satisfy the most extreme Republican voices rather than a serious, nonpolitical framework for our national defense.”Adam Smith, the Democratic ranking member on the House armed services committee, had voted to advance the measure out of panel, but opposed its passage on the House floor, saying it had been “loaded with toxic, partisan amendments that continue the rightwing culture war assault on the LGBTQ+ community and preempt state and local laws under the guise of military readiness”.“Our country faces threats and DoD has modernization needs, but I will not support making massive cuts to critically important domestic programs and slashing taxes to meet those needs,” Smith said.Amid a wider backlash within the party over US support for Israel, some Democrats said they opposed the NDAA because of its inclusion of provisions that would deepen cooperation between the two countries’ defense industries.“This amendment is an existential threat to American sovereignty and democracy,” said the progressive congresswoman Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, who was among the no votes.The House’s passage of the bill came after the chamber considered dozens of amendments. While many were agreed to unanimously, others offered barometers for how far the Republican-controlled chamber was willing to go on the most divisive issues before the parties.A proposal by the Republican Eli Crane to halt all military aid to Ukraine was resoundingly defeated by a 76-350 vote, while an amendment from Republican Lauren Boebert to codify Trump’s executive order banning transgender service members failed by a 212-217 tally.However, the House agreed to amendments from Nancy Mace barring the military health insurer from offering some gender-related care, and banning transgender athletes from girls’ sports at defense department-run schools.Beyond the NDAA, House Republicans on Wednesday approved a budget plan thatwill authorize$73bn for the war with Iran while circumventing the Democratic filibuster in the Senate. But thatblueprint falls far short of the $350bn the Pentagon originally proposed to bring overall defense spending to $1.5tn for the upcoming fiscal year.Explore more on these topicsHouse of RepresentativesUS militaryRepublicansDemocratsUS CongressUS-Israel war on IranUS politicsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Austria opens police station at Hitler birthplace to deter neo-Nazi tourists | Adolf Hitler | The Guardian
description: It is hoped opening of unit tackling narcotics trafficking will deter people from visiting it as pilgrimage site
Author: Deborah Cole
Headline: It is hoped opening of unit tackling narcotics trafficking will deter people from visiting it as pilgrimage site
Content: Police officers, journalists and guests attend an inauguration ceremony for a new police unit at the birthplace of Adolf Hitler.Photograph: Manfred Fesl/APA/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenPolice officers, journalists and guests attend an inauguration ceremony for a new police unit at the birthplace of Adolf Hitler.Photograph: Manfred Fesl/APA/AFP/Getty ImagesAdolf HitlerAustria opens police station at Hitler birthplace to deter neo-Nazi touristsIt is hoped opening of unit tackling narcotics trafficking will deter people from visiting it as pilgrimage siteDeborah Colein BerlinWed 22 Jul 2026 11.05 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 14.42 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAustria has opened a police station in the birthplace ofAdolf Hitlerin an attempt to “neutralise” its toxic legacy, after years of debate over how to stop neo-Nazis visiting it as a pilgrimage site.Hitler was born on 20 April 1889 in a rented flat in the centre of Braunau am Inn and spent the first weeks of his life there.Now the building will be the workplace of a police unit specialised in tackling narcotics trafficking, with the new occupants meant to put off Nazi displays, which are illegal under Austrian law.Given the community’s proximity to the German border, it is on a key drugs running route, according to district police commander Stefan Haslberger.The conversion, whose estimated cost reached €20m, followed an intense public discussion about how to deter today’s fascists from using the site as a shrine.View image in fullscreenA memorial stone against war and fascism stands in front of the newly opened police station.Photograph: Martin Divíšek/EPANews agency APA reported that even construction rubble from the remodelling had beendisposed of in a secret locationto avoid it becoming a gathering place for Hitler devotees.At the opening ceremony, the Austrian interior minister, Gerhard Karner, called it a “new chapter” in which the building would be a “place of democracy, the rule of law, security”. At the same time, he said, “memory will not disappear”.In recent years skinheads laidroses and wreaths for the Nazi leader’s birthdaybefore the elegant building’s ochre facade, while extremists and drunken revellers posed for pictures doing the straight-armed Hitler salute.Local people, fed up with such stunts, often call the former residence the“house of evil”.An expert commission tasked with determining a future for the building had recommended a “use for social or charitable, or for official or administrative purposes”.It said a museum on the site could continue to draw rightwing extremists, especially those whose “blood and soil” ideology particularly reveres birthplaces and gravesites. Demolition, on the other hand, would obfuscate an important chapter of local history.View image in fullscreenAustrian police officers walk in front of the newly opened police station.Photograph: Martin Divíšek/EPAPolls showed a majority of Austrians preferred it to becomea centre for promoting democracy.The building, parts of which stem from the 1600s, hosted an inn and brewery with flats on the upper floors, one of which was briefly occupied by the Hitler family.A decade ago, the Austrian government agreed to take over ownership from a private proprietor, who had previously rented it out to the interior ministry, which used it as a care home for people with disabilities.It stood empty from 2011, with a small memorial stone outside taken from the former Mauthausen concentration camp whose inscription only hinted at its problematic past: “For peace, freedom and democracy. Never again fascism. The millions who died serve as a warning.”German news magazine Der Spiegel reported recently that hackers had replaced the building’s proper address on Google Maps with thestreet number 88 – neo-Nazis code for Heil Hitlerbased on the eighth letter of the alphabet.The government said it hoped the opening of the police station would foster the “neutralisation of the whole place” and repel Hitler admirers.Hitler’s private apartment as Nazi leader on Munich’s Prinzregentenplatz has served as a police station since the late 1940s while his official residence in Berlin’s Reichskanzlei was destroyed by bombing during the second world war.Germany under Hitler annexed Austria in 1938, a fact that historians say led many in the country to long dismiss its shared responsibility for the Holocaust and other Nazi crimes.The far-right Freedom party (FPÖ), which was founded by former Nazis,won the most votes in an Austrian general electionfor the first time since the war in September 2024 but was unable to take power because mainstream parties refused to form a coalition with it.Since then, support for the FPÖ had continued to rise.Explore more on these topicsAdolf HitlerAustriaThe far rightnewsShareReuse this content

title: Man killed in ICE ‘encounter’ was on vacation in US with tourist visa – reports | Florida | The Guardian
description: Juan Jairo Coronilla Durán ran into traffic and killed by semi after federal officials approached him at Florida gas station
Author: José Olivares
Headline: Juan Jairo Coronilla Durán ran into traffic and killed by semi after federal officials approached him at Florida gas station
