Content: People attend a vigil for Juan Jairo Coronilla Durán in St Augustine, Florida, on 15 July.Photograph: Kelly Jordan/APView image in fullscreenPeople attend a vigil for Juan Jairo Coronilla Durán in St Augustine, Florida, on 15 July.Photograph: Kelly Jordan/APFloridaMan killed in ICE ‘encounter’ was on vacation in US with tourist visa – reportsJuan Jairo Coronilla Durán ran into traffic and killed by semi after federal officials approached him at Florida gas stationJosé OlivaresWed 22 Jul 2026 13.40 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 17.02 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Mexican man who washit and killedby a semi-truck inFloridawhen running away from federal immigration officials was vacationing in the US with a tourist visa, according to news reports this week.The 28-year-old, Juan Jairo Coronilla Durán, was killed on 14 July after an “encounter” with federal officials, including those with Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), a state highway patrol spokesperson said last week.After federal officials approached him and a group of four men at a gas station that Tuesday morning, they all took off running. TheFloridahighway patrol spokesperson said Coronilla ran into traffic and was hit by a semi-truck.US immigration agents arrested a record number of people in June, data showsRead moreCoronilla’s death is one of the four ICE-related deaths that took place in less than one week. Two people, one in Texas and one in Maine,were shot and killedby ICE officers. And a fourth person died in ICE custody, while being transferred to an immigration detention center in Georgia. The four deaths have led to renewed calls by the public and lawmakers to rein in ICE and its parent agency, the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), as theycarry outthe Trump administration’s aggressive “mass deportation” campaign promise.According to the Miami Herald, Coronilla, from the Mexican state of Guanajuato, hadonly been in Florida for two dayswhen he died, having arrived in the US only three weeks prior. Coronilla had traveled to Atlanta at first to see family and friends and went to Florida to enjoy the beaches.Coronilla’s wife, who flew to the US last week to make arrangements, had not heard from her husband and found out about his death while watching the news, a local immigrant rights group told the Miami Herald. She then contacted the group, Colectivo Arbol, toask for help. The group said Coronilla left behind a seven-year-old son and seven-month-old daughter.DHS has not publicly identified Coronilla, but the Associated Press confirmed his identity with a Mexican government official.Although the Florida highway patrol’s statement said it was an ICE operation, a homeland security source familiar with the case told the Guardian last week that border patrol agents were involved in the incident.Get in touchContact us about this storyThe best public interest journalism relies on first-hand accounts from people in the know. If you have something to share on this subject, you can contact us confidentially using the following methods:Secure Messaging in the Guardian appThe Guardian app has a tool to send tips about stories. Messages are end to end encrypted and concealed within the routine activity that every Guardian mobile app performs. This prevents an observer from knowing that you are communicating with us at all, let alone what is being said.If you don’t already have the Guardian app, download it (iOS/Android) and go to the menu. Select ‘Secure Messaging’.SecureDropIf you can safely use the tor network without being observed or monitored you can send messages and documents to the Guardian via ourSecureDrop platform.Our guide attheguardian.com/tipslists several ways to contact us securely, and discusses the pros and cons of each.Show moreExplore more on these topicsFloridaICE (US Immigration and Customs Enforcement)US immigrationTrump administrationUS politicsnewsShareReuse this content

title: It cost £2.1bn to divert a river in Brazil’s outback. So where has all the water gone? | Global development | The Guardian
description: Despite official claims that the São Francisco River project supplies 12m people, many of those who had to make way for its construction in 2007 still have no water at all
Author: Constance Malleret
Headline: Despite official claims that the São Francisco River project supplies 12m people, many of those who had to make way for its construction in 2007 still have no water at all
Content: The canal taking water from the São Francisco River to Monteiro, north-east BrazilDespite official claims that the São Francisco River project supplies 12m people, many of those who had to make way for its construction in 2007 still have no water at allByConstance Malleretin Sertânia and Monteiro, Brazil. Photographs by Leonardo Carrato for the GuardianSupported byAbout this contentWed 22 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 17.46 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAconcrete canal cutting through the Caatinga scrubland feeds a surface reservoir two and a half miles from Maria de Fátima Ferreira da Silva’s home in ruralBrazil. The canal passes Sertânia and continues until it reaches Monteiro, the next town along, where it spills its precious cargo into the bed of the Paraíba River – a human-directed inflow into the seasonally dry waterway.Such proximity to a reliable water source would usually be considered a blessing in thesertão, a vast outback in Brazil’s north-east known for its punishing droughts. But not for Ferreira, who was displaced nearly 20 years ago by the construction of the canal and now lives in a house without any water supply, despite the nearby reservoir.“There’s no water; what are we supposed to do without water?” says the 54-year-old, who depends on what she can grow on her smallholding.View image in fullscreenView image in fullscreenView image in fullscreenMaria de Fátima Ferreira da Silva in her maize crop at Salão productive rural village. She bathes at the reservoir and collects water there for her smallholding and donkey, and pays to fill her barrel with drinking water each monthThe canal and reservoir are part of a big hydrological supply system billed as the solution to thesertão’s water scarcity: the diversion of the 1,700-mile-long São Francisco River, the longest river running entirely on Brazilian territory and the source of 70% of the north-east region’swater supply.The ever-present threat of drought in thesertãois only likely to increase with the climate crisis, which accelerates desertification and is already drivinga shift to a hotter, drier climatein this region. While programmes such asWater for AllandOne Million Cisternshave improved water access across the north-east, the São Francisco diversion does so at scale, providing water security to 12 million people, the government says.Butnine years after the canalfirst brought water to Monteiro, some continue to question the scheme’s success – particularly for the communities that were the most affected by the infrastructure project but feel its benefits the least.“In my view, the diversion has not been beneficial. It caused immense damage to the donor basin while yielding little effectiveness for thesertão,” says Janaína Andrade, a regional prosecutor for citizen rights, who acts like an ombudsman in Paraíba state, where Monteiro is located.View image in fullscreenA pumping station and electrical substation in Sertânia, 17 miles from MonteiroView image in fullscreenA cow’s carcass surrounded by black vultures in Paraíba state. Livestock deaths were historically linked to drought but animal diseases and predators now account for many cattle lossesdouble quotation markSome people don’t remember what it was like in the past: we were vulnerable, we were thirsty, it was difficultAgnaldo Freitas da Silva, Vila Lafayette residents’ associationPresident Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva has a personal interest in easing the water insecurity in thesertão, having migrated from there as a child.Ana Paula Pires Koga, a geography professor at the Federal University of Catalão, who has studied the project, says: “In his first term, in 2006, Lula made the diversion part of thegrowth acceleration [infrastructure] programme.” The president talked about “quenching the thirst of thesertanejos”, she adds.Construction of the £2.1bn ($2.8bn) river diversion scheme, which began in 2007, required theexpropriation of about 2,000 propertiesto make way for the infrastructure required to move enough water to feed rivers and reservoirs across four states: two main canals, each more than 130 miles long, hundreds of miles of secondary canals, as well as tunnels, aqueducts, pumping stations and electricity substations.View image in fullscreenA protest against the São Francisco River project at the presidential palace in Brasília in 2007. The banner reads: ‘We already proved that the semi-arid area has a solution without diversion.’ Photograph: Evaristo Sa/AFP/GettyThe 848 most vulnerable families were relocated to 18 planned neighbourhoods known as “productive rural villages” (VPRs), such as Vila Salão, where Ferreira lives. Each family was given a three-bedroom house and a few hectares of land as part of a compensation package that also includes financial support.Yet Vila Salão is a far cry from the government’svision of VPRsas communities connected to running water and sewage services, equipped with a health centre and a school, and receiving enough water to irrigate one hectare (2.5 acres) for each family. The houses in Vila Salão are not connected to the water mains, and the communal buildings stand abandoned in the centre of the grid-shaped neighbourhood.View image in fullscreenMaria de Lourdes Souza da Silva at home in Lafayette village in Monteiro, where the arrival of water allowed her to grow a range of fruit, vegetables and other cropsAndrade says: “People were relocated to the productive village – but without water. They had no water for human consumption, for their livestock or for agricultural production.“All these people who were relocated had artesian wells and were self-sufficient. They were taken to a territory without means of production and became dependent on benefits,” she says.That has been Ferreira’s experience. Some of her neighbours in Vila Salão have dug a well, but she cannot afford to do so. So she goes to the reservoir to bathe and collect water for her modest crops and donkey, and pays to have a 1,000-litre barrel filled with drinking water every month. “Things were better before,” she says.Even in Vila Lafayette, a VPR up the road in Monteiro with better living conditions, there is a persistent sense of loss among the uprooted people.“I can’t complain,” says Maria de Lourdes Souza da Silva, 64. But she remembers how, 17 years earlier, her mother was taken ill after being told she had two weeks to leave the home she had spent a lifetime building. Her mother died shortly afterwards.‘I fear people will go to war over water’: as wells run dry, farmers struggle to survive in BangladeshRead more“I just wasn’t that happy [about moving] because I lost my mother and the place where we lived in the process,” Souza says. “That was where we were raised, where our roots were.”“We’re trying to put down new roots,” she adds, sitting among pots of desert roses on her porch.With the diversion works concluded – albeit after long delays amidabandoned projects and cancelled contracts– the federal governmentconsiders it has done its part, although Lula continues to inaugurate various additional sections of the project since returning to power in 2023.“Some cities are feeling tangible benefits, but the big question is about water reaching the populations scattered across the semi-arid region,” says Pires Koga.The ministry of development and regional integration refused an interview and did not directly answer questions. It simply said that its responsibility was limited to transporting the water and that distribution to citizens and community relocation were “the exclusive responsibility of operators and state and municipal administrations”.In Vila Lafayette, the persistence of the residents’ association, with support from Andrade’s office, made it the first VPR to be connected to mains water and, last year, to receive the promised water for irrigation.View image in fullscreenIrrigated fields, most planted with maize, at Lafayette village. The crops are fed with water from the São Francisco RiverView image in fullscreenAgnaldo Freitas da Silva drinks coconut water from his own land in Monteiro. As vice-president of the Lafayette residents’ association, he campaigned for water accessAgnaldo Freitas da Silva, who is vice-president of the residents’ association, says: “I went knocking on the door of the state assembly, and I took part in their public hearings. I said: ‘I’m not going to let it go; the state government has to give us water. We can’t stay in this situation.’”Freitas, 52, hopes to install solar panels to reduce the community’s energy costs for pumping the irrigation water from the canal. The school, which was never staffed, has been converted into a cold-storage facility where dairy farmers can keep their milk before it is sold.It has not been easy, Freitas says, but he is satisfied with the life he has made for himself. He keeps chickens and has fish tanks, as well as growing fruit and vegetables that he sells througha government programmeto support family farming.“Some people don’t remember what it was like in the past: we were vulnerable, we were thirsty; it was difficult. Today we’re all rich; we have what we need,” he says.View image in fullscreenEdileuza dos Santos Leite and her husband, José Ricardo Leite, outside their home in Salão, Sertânia. The village has yet to receive any water from the São Francisco River project, which Edileuza blames on a lack of local unityThe residents of the shabbier Vila Salão, 17 miles away, have not been so lucky, because they have not managed to pull together, says Edileuza dos Santos Leite, 55. “People aren’t united here,” she says.She hopes the state governor, who is running for re-election this year, will fix things. “God willing, we’ll have water soon,” she says.Explore more on these topicsSouthern frontlines: Latin America and the CaribbeanAccess to waterWaterDroughtBrazilAmericasfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Sperm whales change ‘vowel’ sounds when boats are nearby, scientists discover | Cetaceans | The Guardian
description: Researchers listening off the Caribbean island of Dominica found variations in the mammals’ audible clicks when marine traffic was close
Author: Emma Bryce
Headline: Researchers listening off the Caribbean island of Dominica found variations in the mammals’ audible clicks when marine traffic was close
Content: Three sperm whales. In the latest research, in addition to the changing tempo of codas, the team identified differences in the prevalence of two vowel-like sounds in noisy conditions.Photograph: Project CetiView image in fullscreenThree sperm whales. In the latest research, in addition to the changing tempo of codas, the team identified differences in the prevalence of two vowel-like sounds in noisy conditions.Photograph: Project CetiSeascape: the state of our oceansCetaceansSperm whales change ‘vowel’ sounds when boats are nearby, scientists discoverResearchers listening off the Caribbean island of Dominica found variations in the mammals’ audible clicks when marine traffic was closeSeascape: the state of our oceans is supported byAbout this contentEmma BryceWed 22 Jul 2026 09.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 09.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleSperm whales produce a specific pattern of clicking noises when they hear the noise of boats, researchers have found. The pattern is so distinct that the scientists who identified it said they can now use it to predict when ships are nearby.Researchers fromProject Ceti(the Cetacean Translation Initiative), a non-profit research organisation working to decode sperm whale communication, spent four years tuning in on the calls of 15 sperm whales off the coast of Dominica.They discovered specific differences in sperm whale “codas”, the discrete and distinctive patterns of clicks that this species utters to communicate when those whales encountered shipping. Individual click sequences became shorter, and one type became more prominent than another. Their findings arepublishedin the journal Ecological Informatics.“This research begs the question: are whales talking about the ships, or is it an impact on their voices because the ships are around?,” said David Gruber, the founder and chief executive of Project Ceti. “And then if we’re able to discern what they’re saying about the ships, how might that impact policy and laws?”Under normal conditions, recordings from one of the whales, named “Atwood”, picked up one type of coda comprising five clicks in a 1-1-3 pattern (two clicks preceding three quicker ones), which, Gruber explained, is unique to this specific clan of sperm whales off theCaribbeancoast of Dominica. With the background hum of a ship, Atwood issued the same 1-1-3 click rhythm but noticeably faster, with the whale reducing the intervals between each click.Audio recordings of sperm whales communicatingThe association was so strong that “in general, from their vocalisations alone, we can predict whether ships are around,” said Gašper Beguš, linguistics lead at Project Ceti.For the study, Beguš and colleagues recorded more than 1,000 codas from the 15-strong sperm whale pod, using audio tags attached to whales by drones to be minimally invasive. Where background shipping noise was detected, it was verified using digital geolocation data to confirm that vessels were in the whale’s vicinity when the recording was made.Several studieshave shown that marine animalsincrease the volumeof their vocalisations when there is noise pollution, but the researchers were surprised not to see this trend in the sperm whales, finding that the creatures instead seemed to prioritise altering not just the speed but the types of codas they produced.This related to previous studies where Project Ceti researchers described the“use of vowels”in sperm whales. Communication among sperm whales has long been viewed as a series of morse code-like clicks, but in that work the team found that if the gaps between individual clicks in a coda sequence were condensed to speed up the recording, they formed a continuous unit of sound that resembled two human vowels – the sounds “aaaa” and “iiii”.View image in fullscreenResearchers from Project Ceti diving with a whale recording unit.Photograph: Project CetiIn the latest research, in addition to the changing coda tempo, the team identified differences in the prevalence of these two vowel-like sounds in noisy conditions, with sperm whales using the “aaaa” coda more frequently.“The study is actually one of the first glimpses that the vowels that we discovered might actually carry meaningful information. Because in the presence of shipping noise, they change the way they use those vowels,” Beguš said.Someresearchers have criticisedCeti’s previous studies,finding faultwith its methods and saying that describing whale communication using terms such as “vowels” and “language” stretches the associations between whale and human communication too far. Gruber says they try to address this concern by using terms such as “vowel-like” or “language-like”.‘Aural tattoos’: sperm whales use sounds to signal social identity, say scientistsRead moreNathan Merchant, an underwater noise specialist at the UK governmentmarine research agency Cefas, who was not involved in the research, said there could be other unknown factors driving the change in whale vocalisations aside from shipping noise. But he said his “hunch” was that the relationship was causal.A large body of work now showed changes in behaviour and call volume when marine animals encountered shipping noise, Merchant noted. “Something that marks this study out is that it shows changes to the fine-scale content of those calls,” he said. “It really adds another dimension of nuance to that story about how underwater noise can affect cetaceans.”He added that it would take more research to determine what these audible changes meant.“Are they [the whales] just struggling to say the same thing, or are they saying something different in the presence of this noise?” he said. “It’s a really intriguing question.”Explore more on these topicsCetaceansSeascape: the state of our oceansWhalesDominicaZoologyBiologyAmericasCaribbeannewsShareReuse this content

title: ​World’s largest meat company faces legal challenge over green credentials of $6bn global expansion  | Meat industry | The Guardian
description: Greenpeace petitions Dutch courts to order JBS to disclose how its aims are compatible with responsibilities to climate and human rights
Author: Jonathan Watts
Headline: Greenpeace petitions Dutch courts to order JBS to disclose how its aims are compatible with responsibilities to climate and human rights
Content: Cattle at a JBS facility in Tucumã, Pará, Brazil. JBS plans to invest $2.5bn in six meat-processing plants in Nigeria, which civil society groups say would threaten smallholder farmers, domestic food security and land-use priorities.Photograph: Bloomberg/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenCattle at a JBS facility in Tucumã, Pará, Brazil. JBS plans to invest $2.5bn in six meat-processing plants in Nigeria, which civil society groups say would threaten smallholder farmers, domestic food security and land-use priorities.Photograph: Bloomberg/Getty ImagesMeat industry​World’s largest meat company faces legal challenge over green credentials of $6bn global expansionGreenpeace petitions Dutch courts to order JBS to disclose how its aims are compatible with responsibilities to climate and human rightsJonathan WattsWed 22 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 04.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe world’s biggest meat company, JBS, faces a landmark legal challenge to disclose how its $6bn (£4.5bn) global expansion plan is compatible with responsibilities to the climate, nature and human rights.Courts in theNetherlands, where JBS is headquartered, will assess the petition filed by Greenpeace for information on whether the company is compliant with Dutch laws on “duty of care” as well as its own publicly stated commitments.Lawyers said the case opened a new frontier in the fight against climate change, potentially creating a precedent for legal challenges against the agriculture sector, which is the biggest cause of deforestation and a major source of methane emissions.“The primary objective is to challenge JBS’s business model and expansion model,” said Richard Brown, legal counsel at Greenpeace International. “We have a suspicion that they’re flying blind and not pulling together the information they need to understand the impacts.”He said the request for information is a preliminary stage towards filing formal litigation against the multinational meat company.Until now similar lawsuits have only been filed against fossil fuel companies. Last month, a Paris court ​ruled that the French oil company TotalEnergies must disclose the climate risks linked to emissions from its oil and gas products, and set out plans to ⁠address them in acase brought by NGOs and the city of Paris, but did not order specific measures such as limiting overseas exploration and production.In the Netherlands, Shell is beingchallenged in The Hagueby environmental groups Milieudefensie (Friends of the Earth Netherlands) and Friends of the Earth to reduce the company’s carbon dioxide output by 45% by 2030 in line with the Paris climate agreement. A final ruling is expected in early 2027.At the centre of the petition against JBS is a perceived lack of transparency over its massive expansion plans in Nigeria. In the coming years, the meat company plans toinvest $2.5bnin six giant meat-processing plants that are seen as a bridgehead to the fast-growing African market.Civil society groups say there is scant information about whether JBS has assessed the social and environmental consequences of this plan, which would threaten smallholder farmers, domestic food security, land-use priorities, as well as locking in huge quantities of methane emissions. Methane is one of the most climate-destabilising greenhouse gases.“This is the biggest agriculture investment that I have heard of in Nigeria’s history but there have been few details and no consultation for civil society,” said Elujulo Opeyemi, the spokesperson for Youth in Agroecology and Restoration Network.“The information is not there. We want the Nigerian government to take a pause until we can make a concrete cost-benefit analysis. This investment will only be worthwhile if it is good for the people and planet. Otherwise it will just create more problems.”To illustrate the risks, he said the Niger state government has already promised 1.2m hectares to JBS.JBS, which was founded in Brazil in 1953, moved its headquarters to the Netherlandslast yearas part of its strategy to list on the New York Stock Exchange. The shift allowed the company to tap international financial markets for expansion funds, and to enable the founding family’s Batista brothers – Joesley and Wesley Batista –to significantly raisetheir ownership stake.But the move toEuropealso exposed the company to more rigorous transparency rules. “We say that if you come to the Netherlands, you have to play by Dutch rules and be held to account to Dutch standards,” said Brown. “Ultimately this is about a global conglomerate and how its activities should be limited within safe planetary boundaries.”Behind the lawsuit is dismay at JBS’s environmental track record, particularly in its home country, Brazil, where it has repeatedly made sustainability commitments that it later abandoned or postponed.Beef production is the primary driver of deforestation, as trees are cleared to raise cattle, and scientists warn this has pushed the Amazon close to a tipping point that would accelerate its shift from a carbon sink into a carbon emitter. JBS, which dominates the Brazilian cattle market, promised to address this with a commitment to clean up its beef supply chain in the region by the end of 2025. But the Guardian and its investigative partnersrevealed last yearthat JBS would break its promise. JBS then failed to meet its year-end deadline and has sincescrapped its “net zero by 2040” pledge. Its latest sustainability report also excluded the calculations of indirect emissions, primarily methane, from cows – which are a primary component of JBS’s climate impact.Marieke Vellekoop, the executive director at Greenpeace Netherlands, said that the NGO had been left with “no choice but to seek our right to information through the Dutch courts.”Opeyemi said Nigeria needed transparency from JBS to ensure the full consequences of its planned investment can be calculated for the people, the environment and the wider region. “Once JBS has a foothold in Nigeria, they can encroach across Africa. That’s why the Nigerian government has to get this right,” he said. “This court case is helpful so we can get clarity.”In an email to the Guardian, JBS said it “does not comment on potential legal proceedings of which it has not been notified”.Explore more on these topicsMeat industryFoodNetherlandsEuropeFarmingnewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘A great thrill’: baby puffin is first one seen on Dorset coast in three decades | Birds | The Guardian
description: Puffling excites volunteers trying to save the last known regular nesting site on mainland of southern England
Author: Steven Morris
Headline: Puffling excites volunteers trying to save the last known regular nesting site on mainland of southern England
Content: 0:31First ‘puffling’ sighting in three decades give hope for Dorset's struggling puffin colony – videoBirds‘A great thrill’: baby puffin is first one seen on Dorset coast in three decadesPuffling excites volunteers trying to save the last known regular nesting site on mainland of southern EnglandSteven MorrisWed 22 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFor years, volunteers have craned their necks and tested their patience to peer into the crevices of the cliffs hoping for a glimpse of fluffy joy.Finally it all paid off as a puffling – baby puffin – poked its way into the sunshine, becoming the first to be spotted on the coast of Dorset in southernEnglandfor three decades.“Seeing the puffling was a great thrill,” said the marine ornithologist Richard Caldow. “Every chick matters. One puffling alone won’t save this colony but one is a whole lot better news than none.”This stretch of coast – the Purbeck coast near Swanage –is the last known regular nesting site for puffins on the mainland of southern England.In the 1950s, there were about 80 birds nesting there but the number of pairs dropped to three and there had been no definitive sightings of fledglings for 30 years. The fear was that the colony was heading to extinction.View image in fullscreenA puffin in the sea off the Purbeck coast.Photograph: Picasa/Pete ChristieOver the summer, 13 volunteers have been monitoring the ledges for 15 weeks, no easy task as the vantage point is hard to access and precarious.In April, six adult puffins were recorded at the colony. Within a few weeks, volunteers were observing puffins carrying fish into at least two separate nesting locations, indicating that more than one pair may have been attempting to raise chicks.Most of the deliveries ended after 12 June, suggesting that not all the chicks had made it but the fish drops continued to one spot and eventually the puffling was spotted and photographed.Caldrow, who is supported by volunteers from the Purbeck Natural History Forum, said they timed the taking of the photo perfectly. “We knew its parents had been feeding it since about 29 May until 5 July so by then it was 38 days old.“By then it was likely to come into the open occasionally, usually if a parent was there too. That was most likely in evening so we chartered a boat on 7 July to anchor in the right spot from 4 till 8pm. It took three hours but just after 7pm it popped out behind its parent. Great to see.”View image in fullscreenA camera being installed on the cliff.Photograph: Pete ChristieHe believes the puffling may have left that night. “Where it is now we have no idea. It will be well out at sea somewhere on its own. The parents don’t look after it once it fledges. It won’t come back for at least two years and won’t breed till 2030 at the soonest. It may come back here but it could go somewhere different.”Caldow, who has nearly 40 years’ experience researching and conserving coastal bird populations, said he was delighted at the sight of the puffling. “It tells us that Purbeck puffins can still lay fertile eggs, hatch them and, at least in some years, find enough food to rear a chick and escape the attention of predators.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toDown to EarthThe planet's most important stories. Get all the week's environment news - the good, the bad and the essentialPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionOver the last few years,the National Trusthas been working to understand why the puffin colony has been failing to produce chicks. Theories have ranged from rats to peregrine falcons to the climate emergency.View image in fullscreenDancing Ledge, which contains the fissure where the puffins nest.Photograph: Will WilkinsonMotion-sensor cameras, powered by solar panels, have been installed by high-rope experts, positioned to take images of movement within the deep fissure – the puffins nest about 2 metres back from the entrance.The cameras will be recovered after the seabird breeding season, and volunteers will then review the thousands of images taken.Ben Cooke, the rivers and coast project officer for the National Trust in Purbeck, said: “The images should hopefully reveal more of the nesting, feeding and fledging activity of the puffins. Understanding what is happening inside the nesting crevice is the missing piece of the puzzle.“If we can identify the reasons why chicks and eggs are being lost, we can begin to explore what conservation measures might help. Puffins are such an iconic bird, we want to do everything in our power to help them survive in Purbeck.”The National Trust is offeringlive puffin and clifftop webcamson the Farne Islands in north-east England. Streaming daily from dawn until dusk between May and the end of July, the cameras form part of the trust’sWindow to the Wild project,giving viewers an up-close view of the seabirds that breed on the islands.Explore more on these topicsBirdsDorsetWildlifeMarine lifeEnglandAnimalsConservationnewsShareReuse this content

title: Scottish Labour’s rivals decry ‘chancer’ Anas Sarwar for resigning to join Andy Burnham’s government | Anas Sarwar | The Guardian
description: New prime minister has made Sarwar – the first senior Labour figure to call for Keir Starmer’s resignation – a peer
Author: Robyn Vinter
Headline: New prime minister has made Sarwar – the first senior Labour figure to call for Keir Starmer’s resignation – a peer
Content: Anas Sarwar has been accused by the SNP of lying to the people of Scotland.Photograph: Andrew Milligan/PAView image in fullscreenAnas Sarwar has been accused by the SNP of lying to the people of Scotland.Photograph: Andrew Milligan/PAAnas SarwarScottish Labour’s rivals decry ‘chancer’ Anas Sarwar for resigning to join Andy Burnham’s governmentNew prime minister has made Sarwar – the first senior Labour figure to call for Keir Starmer’s resignation – a peerRobyn VinterWed 22 Jul 2026 18.17 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 12.18 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleScottish Labour’s rivals in Holyrood have slammed Anas Sarwar’s shock decision to resign as leader to sit in Andy Burnham’s new government, calling him a “chancer” and accusing him of lying to voters.The prime minister has made Sarwar – who was the first seniorLabourfigure to call for Keir Starmer to resign – a peer and given him a ministerial post at the Department for Business, Innovation, Science and Trade, No 10 announced on Wednesday.The Scottish Green party co-leader Gillian Mackay said: “Anas Sarwar is a chancer. He has gone from promising to scrap theHouse of Lordsto taking a seat in it. He has spent years attacking the very institution he is now preparing to join. The hypocrisy is breathtaking.”View image in fullscreen‘Anas Sarwar is a chancer,’ said Gillian Mackay, a Scottish Green party co-leader. ‘The hypocrisy is breathtaking.’Photograph: Murdo MacLeod/The GuardianThe SNP called for Sarwar to donate his new “bumper salary” to charity, accusing him of lying to the electorate over his intentions after previously saying he would never enter the Lords, calling it “unacceptable” in 2022.The SNP MSP George Adam said: “The Labour party andAnas Sarwarrepeatedly denied and lied to the people of Scotland about Anas Sarwar’s intentions – the fact he will now be prancing about in ermine robes won’t be forgotten.”However, despite the move happening less than three months since his election campaign – shortly before Scottish Labour suffered itsworst results in Holyrood history– those who know Sarwar do not think it was planned.Figures from within his own party were left reeling, with “surprised” being the word on the lips of a number of Scottish Labour politicians and staff on Wednesday.One Scottish Labour worker said: “If he was going to resign, it would have been clearer to have stated that in May and given everyone the chance to have a proper assessment and restructure things however needed. Doing it this way means everyone is thrown into the lurch.”As is the formality in some constituencies, Sarwar’s seat as a member of the Scottish parliament will be relinquished and handed on to the next on the list, Monica Lennon. Lennon is also seen as a credible contender for the party leadership, having been a popular MSP in the past.Michael Marra, the MSP for North EastScotland, is a name also being put forward as a potential leadership candidate from outside the power centres of Edinburgh or Glasgow.It will not be a wide field, as there are only 17 Labour MSPs to choose from. It is possible for an MP or even a Lord to take on the Scottish Labour leadership – meaning, in fact, Sarwar does not have to technically resign as leader – but it is most common for this role to be held by an MSP because of duties in the Scottish parliament.View image in fullscreenMonica Lennon, a former MSP who did not win a seat in the May Scottish parliament elections, is expected to take over Sarwar’s Glasgow seat. She is also seen as a contender for the Scottish Labour leadership.Photograph: Murdo MacLeod/The GuardianA source close to Sarwar said: “The next [Scottish] Labour leader will need to [provide] balance between the offer to Scotland that is a uniquely Scottish offer, while being part of a UK-wide party. That has been a constant conflict, a constant battle.”Another said the next leader would “have to do some educating of Andy Burnham … He’s the king of the north but he is prime minister of the United Kingdom.”Burnham is heading to Scotland for his first meeting with the leaders of the devolved nations on Thursday. He will meet the first minister of Scotland, John Swinney from the SNP, and the first minister of Wales, Rhun ap Iorwerth of Plaid Cymru, before watching the opening ceremony of the 2026 Commonwealth Games.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every daySign up toHeadlines UKGet the day’s headlines and highlights emailed direct to you every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionIn Wales, I’ve seen what happens when devolution isn’t done right. Here’s what Andy Burnham must know | Will HaywardRead moreCriticism has been made from Scotland thatBurnham’s devolution offerto the north of England and the wider country outside London and the south-east falls short when it comes to the devolved nations.Scottish politicians from across the spectrum have complained that Burnham lacks knowledge of, or interest in, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland. Sarwar’s appointment could help bridge that gap, a source said.While many people were shocked by Sarwar’s perceived willingness to leave Scotland behind, given the choice between being in power in Westminster and in opposition in Holyrood, it would be common sense for Sarwar to choose Westminster, a source close to Sarwar in the Labour party said.“I’m maybe less surprised than people out in the wider Labour world, because he’s someone who’s very ambitious. Politically, he’s someone who wants to effect change.“He’s obviously not in power, he faced the prospect of hanging around for another five years until the next Holyrood election. I just think we would have been bored had he stayed on, and he’s not someone that would like to sit around and be bored.”Sarwar said: “I am delighted and honoured to have been asked by the prime minister to serve every part of the United Kingdom as the minister of state for trade.“Throughout my life, I have only ever been motivated by public service. The opportunity to serve our country is all I have ever asked. That’s why this is an opportunity I humbly accept.“Looking back over the last five years, I am hugely proud of the work we have done since I became Scottish Labour leader.”The acting leader of the Scottish Labour party, Jackie Baillie, said: “I want to thank Anas Sarwar for his leadership and friendship. He led the Scottish Labour party with integrity and a vision to build a fairer and more equal Scotland.”Explore more on these topicsAnas SarwarScotlandLabourAndy BurnhamHouse of LordsScottish politicsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Glasgow Commonwealth Games organisers hope for late surge in ticket sales | Commonwealth Games 2026 | The Guardian
description: The Commonwealth Games opening ceremony is yet to sell out, as are several events on a much-reduced programme from the last time the city hosted in 2014
Author: Yara El-Shaboury
Headline: The Commonwealth Games opening ceremony is yet to sell out, as are several events on a much-reduced programme from the last time the city hosted in 2014
Content: The England athletes Gabrielle Idle-Beavers (left) and Evie Lambert in Glasgow on the eve of the Commonwealth Games.Photograph: Jeremy Sutton-Hibbert/The GuardianView image in fullscreenThe England athletes Gabrielle Idle-Beavers (left) and Evie Lambert in Glasgow on the eve of the Commonwealth Games.Photograph: Jeremy Sutton-Hibbert/The GuardianCommonwealth Games 2026Glasgow Commonwealth Games organisers hope for late surge in ticket salesOpening ceremony and several events yet to sell outProgramme reduced from last time city hosted in 2014Yara El-Shabouryin GlasgowWed 22 Jul 2026 12.10 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 16.22 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleCommonwealth Games organisers will hope for a late surge in ticket sales as Glasgow prepares to stage the opening ceremony on Thursday night, with seats still available across a number of events at the four competition venues despite thesignificantly reduced scale.The opening ceremony at the OVO Hydro still had adult tickets available on Wednesday, with prices ranging from £45 to £95. The 13,000-capacity arena will host live music, the parade of nations and the arrival of all 74 Commonwealth batons. The six-time Olympic cycling champion Sir Chris Hoy, who revealed in 2024that he has stage-four cancer, will also take part.Madeleine McCann’s younger brother Sean to swim for Scotland at Glasgow 2026Read moreGlasgow 2026 isa very different propositionfrom the city’s widely praised 2014 Games. The opening ceremony 12 years ago was at Celtic Park, where more than 40,000 people watched Queen Elizabeth II officially declare the Games open. This year’s event has been scaled back after the Australian state of Victoriawithdrew as hosts over rising costs.Only 500,000 tickets have been made available for the Games, less than half the 1.2 million for Glasgow 2014. Organisers have faced questions over demand, with reports suggesting ticket sales have been slower than expected and public interest more subdued than during the previous edition held in the city. Prices vary considerably across the programme: adult tickets for bowls and para bowls start from £10, while Friday’s swimming session featuring the three-time Olympic gold medallist Adam Peaty carries a starting price of £90.View image in fullscreenClyde (centre), an anthropomorphic thistle who was the mascot of the Glasgow 2014 games, is acting in that capacity for Team Scotland this year.Photograph: Jeremy Sutton-Hibbert/The GuardianThis month, members of Scotland’s Tartan Army supporters’ club were offered free tickets for selected sessions, including athletics, para powerlifting, netball and 3x3 basketball. Organisers said the promotion was a gesture of thanks to fans who supportedScotlandat the football World Cup.Scotland will be led into the opening ceremony by the veteran lawn bowler Alex Marshall and the double Commonwealth judo champion Sarah Adlington. Marshall, 59, is Scotland’s most decorated Commonwealth athlete with five gold medals, plus a silver, while Adlington won gold on home soil in 2014 and at Birmingham 2022.England’s flag bearers will be the lawn bowler Sian Honnor and the swimmer Luke Greenbank. The latter won relay gold in 2022, while the former claimed Commonwealth titles in Glasgow and Birmingham.Explore more on these topicsCommonwealth Games 2026Commonwealth GamesScotlandnewsShareReuse this content

title: Jessica Morgan named new Tate director – with ‘big pay cut’ to take job | Art | The Guardian
description: Clear frontrunner leaves lucrative role at New York’s Dia Art Foundation to join UK arts organisation at critical juncture
Author: Lanre Bakare
Headline: Clear frontrunner leaves lucrative role at New York’s Dia Art Foundation to join UK arts organisation at critical juncture
Content: Jessica Morgan, described as ‘an agent of change’ and ‘fizzing with so many ideas’.Photograph: Yvonne Tnt/BFA.com/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenJessica Morgan, described as ‘an agent of change’ and ‘fizzing with so many ideas’.Photograph: Yvonne Tnt/BFA.com/ShutterstockArtJessica Morgan named new Tate director – with ‘big pay cut’ to take jobClear frontrunner leaves lucrative role at New York’s Dia Art Foundation to join  UK arts organisation at critical junctureLanre BakareArts and culture correspondentWed 22 Jul 2026 08.11 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 15.13 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleJessica Morgan has been confirmed as the new director of Tate, marking her return to the arts organisation where she worked as a curator for more than a decade, at a critical juncture in its history.Morgan, the clear frontrunner in the race to replace Maria Balshaw, who left this spring afternine years in charge, is currently based in New York, where she has led the Dia Art Foundation since 2015.According to Dia’stax filings, Morgan was paid $814,000 in 2025 (more than £600,000), substantially more than the £220,000-£225,000 Balshaw earned at Tate.Tate’s chair, Roland Rudd, said he had paid Morgan the most he could “under the constrictions he faced”, confirming she was accepting a “very big pay cut” to take up the role.Described as “an agent of change” by Tate, Morgan will need to guide the organisation through a difficult financial landscape, raise poor staff morale and grapple with fluctuating visitor numbers.Tate at a turning point: new director must confront unwieldy ‘beast’ of an art institutionRead moreRudd told the Guardian that Morgan was going to “question a lot of things” at the organisation. “She’s an agent of change, but she takes people with her,” he said. “At Dia she transformed that museum, but she’s done it in a way where she’s carried people with her and I really like that.”Morgan said it was an immense privilege to take over at Tate, which she called the “world’s foremost institution for international modern and contemporary art and for British art”.She said her time as curator at Tate “deeply informed” her work at Dia, where she placed “artists and our public at the centre of what we do”.Morgan added: “I look forward to this pivotal next stage at Tate and, most importantly, continuing and advancing the incredible work of Tate’s committed and talented team in the years to come.”She takes over an organisation that is bruised after a difficult few years. Finances were hit hard during the pandemic, leading to rounds of redundancies, restructuring and “culture war” battles that, according to one senior staffer, left staff morale “on the floor”.View image in fullscreenTate staff strike outside Tate Britain in London in November 2025.Photograph: Alicia Canter/The GuardianLately, there has been a more positive story, with the success of Balshaw’s Tracey Emin retrospective, the record-breaking ticket sales that accompanied theFrida Kahloexhibition, and critical admiration for the Ana Mendieta show at Tate Modern.But Tate is unlike other British arts institutions, with four sites spread around the country that can make it financially exposed, and the feeling in some quarters that it has not found a new direction since the departure of Nicholas Serota, who created Tate Modern and wasin post for 28 years.Many will welcome Morgan’s appointment. As the search for a successor to Balshaw began, her name was on most people’s lips, partly because of her connections to Tate, but also because of her reputation as a talented curator and tireless fundraiser.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toArt WeeklyYour weekly art world round-up, sketching out all the biggest stories, scandals and exhibitionsPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionFrances Morris, the formerTate Moderndirector, described the 57-year-old as a “hugely hardworking individual with a very strong sense of what’s right”.Morgan studied art history at Cambridge and the Courtauld Institute ofArt, before establishing a reputation for developing talent from the Middle East, south Asia, South Korea and Latin America.“What drives me on is finding the artists we’re not talking or writing about, that we’re not showing,” she once said.View image in fullscreenTate Modern’s Frida Kahlo exhibition, overseen by its departing director Maria Balshaw, had record-breaking ticket sales.Photograph: Guy Bell/Shutterstock“She’s fizzing with so many ideas,” Rudd added. “I think within the committee, there were a few people who said she’s been incredibly hyped, is she really this good? And the answer is, yes, she really is.”She is also arguably the coolest director Tate has ever had, dressing in designers such as Rabih Kayrouz, and being profiled inNew York MagazineandThe Gentlewoman.Morgan’s appointment was one of the last tasks Keir Starmer completed as prime minister. She will start in January, with the interim director and former boss of Tate Modern, Karin Hindsbo, taking charge until then.Explore more on these topicsArtTate ModernTate LiverpoolTate St IvesTate BritainFrida KahlonewsShareReuse this content

title: NHS trust investigated for corporate manslaughter after Nottingham attacks | NHS | The Guardian
description: Trust that treated Valdo Calocane, who killed three people in 2023, sectioned him four times between 2020 and 2022
Author: Neha Gohil
Headline: Trust that treated Valdo Calocane, who killed three people in 2023, sectioned him four times between 2020 and 2022
Content: Ian Coates, Barnaby Webber and Grace O'Malley-Kumar were killed on 13 June 2023 by Valdo Calocane.Composite: Nottinghamshire PoliceView image in fullscreenIan Coates, Barnaby Webber and Grace O'Malley-Kumar were killed on 13 June 2023 by Valdo Calocane.Composite: Nottinghamshire PoliceNHSNHS trust investigated for corporate manslaughter after Nottingham attacksTrust that treated Valdo Calocane, who killed three people in 2023, sectioned him four times between 2020 and 2022Neha GohilMidlands correspondentWed 22 Jul 2026 08.32 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 08.04 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe NHS trust that treated the triple killer Valdo Calocane in the years before theNottinghamattacks is being investigated by police for corporate manslaughter.Calocane, who has paranoid schizophrenia, was sentenced to an indefinite hospital order after he fatally stabbed the 19-year-old students Barnaby Webber and Grace O’Malley Kumar, and the 65-year-old caretaker Ian Coates, on 13 June 2023.A public inquiryinto the events leading up to the 2023 attacks heard how theNottinghamshireHealthcare NHS Foundation trust had sectioned Calocane four times between 2020 and 2022.Evidence given to the inquiry detailed a string of violent incidents that Calocane had committed in the years before the Nottingham attacks and his repeated refusal to take medication. During his second hospital admission, the inquiry heard how a doctor had warned that Calocane could “end up killing someone”.Despite this, Calocane was discharged from mental health services in September 2022 because he had failed to attend his appointments and healthcare workers “couldn’t find him”.View image in fullscreenEmma Webber, the mother of Barnaby Webber, and Dr Sanjoy Kumar, the father of Grace O’Malley Kumar, at a memorial walk to lay flowers in Nottingham last year.Photograph: Danny Lawson/PANorthumbria police confirmed they were carrying out the criminal inquiry on behalf of the Nottinghamshire force. TheNHStrust said it was cooperating with the investigation, which could result in substantial fines and changes to its leadership.Charges of corporate manslaughter, a serious criminal offence, can only apply to certain organisations, including public bodies such as local authorities and NHS trusts, to ensure they can “be held properly accountable for very serious failings resulting in death”, according to the Crown Prosecution Service.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionMental health system is broken, says mother of Nottingham triple killerRead moreThe public inquiry into the attacks, which is expected to publish its findings next year, heard that there had been a warrant out for Calocane’s arrest at the time of the Nottingham attacks after he failed to appear in court for the alleged assault of a police officer.Nottinghamshire and Leicestershire police forces apologised during the inquiryfor failing to act on the warrant, which according to the Times is why the Northumbria force will carry out the investigation into the NHS trust.The Nottingham trust has been under close scrutiny since the 2023 attacks. A 2026 report by the Care Quality Commission said it needed to make significant improvements.A trust spokesperson said: “Northumbria police, acting on behalf of Nottinghamshire police, have begun an investigation into matters relating to the care and treatment of Valdo Calocane.“We know how important the independent inquiry and police investigation are for the affected families, the survivors and our local communities. We will, of course, be cooperating fully with Northumbria police, as we have with the statutory inquiry.”A spokesperson for the force said: “Our thoughts are very much with the victims, their families and all those impacted by the tragic events in Nottingham in June 2023.“We are in contact with the victims’ families and survivors and are committed to supporting them throughout this process.”Explore more on these topicsNHSNottinghamNottinghamshireHealthEnglandCare Quality Commission (CQC)newsShareReuse this content

title: Model scout for Jeffrey Epstein found dead at his home near Paris | Jeffrey Epstein | The Guardian
description: Daniel Siad faced allegations of rape and trafficking in France and appeared in more than 1,000 documents in Epstein files
Author: Amelia Gentleman
Headline: Daniel Siad faced allegations of rape and trafficking in France and appeared in more than 1,000 documents in Epstein files
Content: Daniel Siad had not yet been questioned by investigators, but had denied the allegations, saying he wanted to give his version of events.Photograph: US Department of JusticeView image in fullscreenDaniel Siad had not yet been questioned by investigators, but had denied the allegations, saying he wanted to give his version of events.Photograph: US Department of JusticeJeffrey EpsteinModel scout for Jeffrey Epstein found dead at his home near ParisDaniel Siad faced allegations of rape and trafficking in France and appeared in more than 1,000 documents in Epstein filesAmelia GentlemanWed 22 Jul 2026 09.38 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 09.37 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA modelling scout who recruited women forJeffrey Epsteinhas been found dead at his home near Paris, French officials have said.Daniel Siad, 69, was under investigation by the prosecutors specialising in human trafficking inFrance, accused of rape and other charges. His name appeared in more than 1,000 documents in the US Department of Justice Epstein files that were declassified earlier this year.Siad was one of several French men accused of aiding Epstein to traffic and abuse women. He had not yet been questioned by investigators, but had denied the allegations, saying he wanted to give his version of events.He was found dead in his home on Monday evening. An investigation to determine the cause of death has been opened and an autopsy is to be carried out.Bill Gates foundation met with Jeffrey Epstein about 30 times, external review findsRead moreSiad traveled around the world, visiting villages in easternEuropeor flying to Cuba or Sweden, trying to source women to work in the modelling industry. Alongside searching for potential models, he looked for women whom he hoped Epstein might want to meet. He compared his work to fishing, explaining to Epstein in an email in 2014 that patience was required: “In This busyness I feel like fisherman some time I cache (sic) quick, some time no fish.”The files revealedSiadto be one of Epstein’s most loyal correspondents. He remained in frequent contact with him until months before the financier’s death, receiving wired payments apparently for expenses incurred during travel to find women.Siad was well connected in the modelling world and also worked as a scout on commission for Jean-Luc Brunel, who was the leading agent in Paris, New York and Miami for more than four decades. Brunel killed himself in prison in February 2022, after spending 14 months in custody awaiting trial on charges of rape of minors and sexual harassment, which he denied along with any participation in Epstein’s sex trafficking. Epstein died in prison in 2019 while awaiting trial for sex trafficking.In an emailed statement, Siad told the Guardian in February that he believed he had been providing women to Epstein for legitimate model casting sessions in Paris. “He had a big apartment where he used to organise all his meetings, including casting for models I recruited, for Victoria’s Secret and MC2,” he wrote in an emailed statement. “The casting time would not last more than 10 minutes. I always left the place with the models immediately.”Earlier this year, the former Swedish model Ebba Karlsson accused Siad of rape. She said he had scouted her in her home town of Stockholm, Sweden, in 1990, when she was a 20-year-old model, and later raped her in France. Siad then went by a different name, she said, and she was only able to identify him after seeing images of him in reporting of Epstein files. Karlsson, who did not have any interactions with Epstein, filed criminal allegations of rape and human trafficking against Siad in Paris earlier this year. Siad’s lawyer in Paris, Ménya Arab-Tigrine, told the Guardian earlier this year that he denied the allegation.Responding to the news of Siad’s death, Karlsson told AFP it was “very frustrating”. “He was very close to be arrested. We worked so hard for this, trying to have justice.”Siad’s regular emails supplied Epstein with details about the young women he was recruiting. “Slovakia is the place to be,” Siad wrote to Epstein in 2009, noting that he had 45 women to see there. He told Epstein that he planned to spend the summer “scouting small villages” in Slovakia, the Czech Republic, Poland and Hungary. “I will make you a great surprise when you come to Paris,” he promised. He was still working for Epstein in 2016, writing: “Call me if you have time I am in Barcelona. With amazing Beauty.”Epstein’s emails to Siad were very succinct, asking: “wht about new girls?”, “Any news”, “anything worth coming to paris for?” or “Any interesting women?” The women were often referred to by nationality rather than their names. “I am in ny, is the swedish girl here?” Epstein asked Said in June 2014. Sometimes, Epstein replied to Siad’s suggested candidates with just a one-word answer: “age?”. Siad liked to emphasise that the women he had found for Epstein appeared youthful, writing on one occasion: “26 but she look 18”.Siad, who described himself as French-born, of Algerian origin with Swedish citizenship, told France TV earlier this year that Epstein had abused his trust and he had not been “in a position to know that this man was dangerous”. He maintained that his dealings with the models he scouted were purely professional. “With time, one discovers that this individual committed atrocities; fortunately, I never introduced him to any minor or non-minor who was abused. I have nothing to reproach myself for,” he said in comments broadcast on French television.The DoJ files suggest the FBI was tipped off about Siad’s work for Epstein in 2016. In February, French prosecutors announced they were setting up a team of magistrates to analyse evidence from the files, which mention a number of French nationals, including Brunel.Information and support for anyone affected by rape or sexual abuse issues is available from the following organisations. In the UK,Rape Crisisoffers support on 0808 500 2222 in England and Wales, 0808 801 0302 inScotland, or 0800 0246 991 inNorthern Ireland. In the US,Rainnoffers support on 800-656-4673. In Australia, support is available at1800Respect(1800 737 732). Other international helplines can be found atibiblio.org/rcip/internl.htmlExplore more on these topicsJeffrey EpsteinFranceEuropenewsShareReuse this content

title: Harry Potter publisher to receive millions in Anthropic copyright settlement | AI (artificial intelligence) | The Guardian
description: Bloomsbury has 14,087 titles listed in agreement between AI startup and authors over use of their work
Author: Dan Milmo
Headline: Bloomsbury has 14,087 titles listed in agreement between AI startup and authors over use of their work
Content: JK Rowling’s Harry Potter books, published by Bloomsbury, are included in the settlement.Photograph: Murdo Macleod/The GuardianView image in fullscreenJK Rowling’s Harry Potter books, published by Bloomsbury, are included in the settlement.Photograph: Murdo Macleod/The GuardianAI (artificial intelligence)Harry Potter publisher to receive millions in Anthropic copyright settlementBloomsbury has 14,087 titles listed in agreement between AI startup and authors over use of their workDan MilmoandMark SweneyWed 22 Jul 2026 09.28 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 14.33 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe publisher of Harry Potter has received a multimillion-pound payout as a beneficiary of a $1.5bn (£1.12bn) copyright settlement between the AI startupAnthropicand thousands of authors over the use of their protected work to power chatbots.Bloomsbury, which is home to the bestselling novelists Sarah J Maas and Susanna Clarke as well as JK Rowling, said it had 14,087 titles listed within the settlement, with a proposed compensation of about $3,000 a title.The London-based company expects to receive the cash from the settlement in instalments, potentially starting in the second half of this fiscal year, with the proceeds to be split with authors.After a deduction of about 10% for attorney fees and other expenses, Bloomsbury and the group of affected authors can expect to receive about $19m (£14m).Copyright has become a major battleground in the AI boom because tools such as Anthropic’s suite of Claude chatbots are trained on vast amounts of data taken from the open web, including copyright-protected work such as novels and news articles.US-based AI companies have argued that using such work is allowed under the legal doctrine of fair use, which allows use of copyrighted work without the owner’s permission in certain circumstances. However, creative professionals and artists around the worldhave been demandingthat AI companies seek permission first – or at least pay them for using their work.View image in fullscreenThe lawsuit against Anthropic was filed by the novelist Andrea Bartz and two other authors in 2024.Photograph: Richard Drew/APThe US district judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín said on Monday that the settlement provided “meaningful relief” to affected authors and publishers. The case began when the novelist Andrea Bartz and two other authorsfiled the lawsuit in 2024. About 91% of the 482,000 works covered in the suit have been claimed.The authors’ lead lawyer, Justin Nelson, described it as “the largest known copyright recovery in history”.It is the first major settlement out of dozens of AI copyright lawsuits being filed in the US on behalf of authors and news outlets, which are still going through the courts.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAnthropic’s deputy general counsel, Aparna Sridhar, said in a written statement after the ruling: “We are pleased that more than 91% of authors and publishers covered by the settlement have claimed their share of the payment, and we’re looking forward to bringing this matter to a close.”Bloomsbury announced an AI licensing deal last year which allows it to sell academic works to train up generative AI programmes.The firm said recently that more subject areas were now being considered for AI training. Authors have been given the opportunity to “opt in” to the scheme and will be paid royalties if they decide to let their work be used.Explore more on these topicsAI (artificial intelligence)AnthropicPublishingIntellectual propertyLondonChatbotsnewsShareReuse this content

title: France records more than 5,700 excess deaths during June heatwave | France | The Guardian
description: Figures come as large parts of southern Europe endure another record-breaking period of extreme temperatures
Author: Jon Henley
Headline: Figures come as large parts of southern Europe endure another record-breaking period of extreme temperatures
Content: A French firefighter responds to a flare-up to prevent the fire from reigniting in the Fontainebleau Forest, in Noisy-sur-Ecole, France.Photograph: Simon Wohlfahrt/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenA French firefighter responds to a flare-up to prevent the fire from reigniting in the Fontainebleau Forest, in Noisy-sur-Ecole, France.Photograph: Simon Wohlfahrt/AFP/Getty ImagesFranceFrance records more than 5,700 excess deaths during June heatwaveFigures come as large parts of southern Europe endure another record-breaking period of extreme temperaturesJon Henley, Europe correpondentWed 22 Jul 2026 11.34 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 18.52 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFrance recorded more than 5,700 excess deaths during its June heatwave, authorities have said, as swathes of southernEuropecontinue to endure another record-breaking period of extreme temperatures.The French national health agency said on Wednesday that it had recorded 5,764 excess deaths ⁠between ​17 June and 2 July, an excess mortality rate of 36%.More than half of the “unprecedented” total were recorded between 25 and 27 June, with deaths of people aged ⁠75 and older accounting for about two-thirds ​of ​the excess mortality, the agency said.View image in fullscreenVisitors use umbrellas to shield themselves from the sun during a heatwave as the Arc de Triomphe in Paris at the height of the heatwave.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesThose who lost their lives between 17 June and 2 July died of “all causes”, none yet clearly linked to the heat, it said.Francereported slightly fewer excess deaths – 5,722 – over the whole summer last year.The announcement came as firefighters inSpainbattled one of the country’s largest ever wildfires, which has burned about 32,000 hectares (79,000 acres) in the Guadalajara region north of Madrid, forcing the evacuation of 1,200 people from 34 villages in the sparsely populated area and confinement orders in 14 others.Authorities said on Wednesday that the fire was beginning to stabilise, and residents of five villages were allowed to return home. The prime minister, Pedro Sánchez, said on a visit to the area that Spain had already suffered 22 major wildfires so far this year, four times more than in the same period last year.View image in fullscreenAdult emergency services at the Paris-Saclay Hospital during the heatwave in Paris.Photograph: Romuald Meigneux/SIPA/ShutterstockMore than 100,000 hectares had burned, the same as the whole-year average over the past decade, he said, adding that more must be done to prevent fires. “We must raise public awareness and not trivialise the climate emergency,” he said.Spain’s national weather agency, Aemet, said the danger of wildfires remained “very high or extreme” and was expected to increase further in the coming days. It urged people to exercise extreme caution amid highs of 42-44C and a dry westerly wind.View image in fullscreenA firefighter works to contain part of the Guadalajara wildfire.Photograph: UME/AFP/Getty ImagesEurope is the world’s fastest-warming continent and scientists say human-driven climate change is increasing the frequency and intensity of heatwaves, droughts and wildfires, while also creating conditions for sudden, destructive storms.The Guadalajara fire comes less than two weeks aftera deadly blazedevastated a small expatriate community in southern Spain, killing 13 peopleincluding several foreignersin one of the country’s deadliest wildfires on record.Spain’s ecological transition minister, Sara Aagesen, described the Guadalajara blaze as “one of the largest in the recent history of our country”, with data showing it was exceeded only by the 37,000 hectares burned by a fire in north-west Spain last year.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThis is EuropeThe most pressing stories and debates for Europeans – from identity to economics to the environmentPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion‘It’s only going to get worse’: wildfires forcing firefighters to make impossible choicesRead moreIn France, where ​two firefighters died in their vehicle in the south-western region of Gironde on Tuesday, authorities urged people to avoid using barbecues in areas experiencing heatwaves, saying nine out of 10 fires started as a result of human activity.A wildfire has burned through 2,500 hectares in the south-eastern Var department, forcing 400 people to flee. “The situation has been very difficult, 1,100 firefighters fought all night,” William Vogl, a spokesperson for the Var firefighters, said.View image in fullscreenA house is engufled in flames in Cotignac, southeastern France.Photograph: Thibaud Moritz/AFP/Getty ImagesIn Greece, ​where temperatures were forecast to top 41C, authorities ​banned outdoor work at peak midday sun. In Skopje, the capital ofNorth Macedonia, 11 people were admitted ‌to hospital, three with serious injuries, after a severe storm on Tuesday.InItaly, several fires burned across Sicily on Wednesday, prompting scattered evacuations. The country’s civil protection agency has declared all of the island at the highest risk for wildfires, with high winds and 40C-plus temperatures fuelling several blazes.Reuters and Associated Press contributed to this reportExplore more on these topicsFranceSpainExtreme heatExtreme weatherWildfiresClimate crisisEuropenewsShareReuse this content

title: Charles Gaines, whose book Pumping Iron helped to popularize bodybuilding, dies at 84 | Books | The Guardian
description: 1974 book led to making of seminal documentary and introduced Arnold Schwarzenegger to the world
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: 1974 book led to making of seminal documentary and introduced Arnold Schwarzenegger to the world
Content: Charles Gaines and actor Ethan Hawke support the call for a moratorium on oil and gas exploration in the Gulf of St Lawrence in October 2015.Photograph: Andrew Vaughan/The Canadian Press/APView image in fullscreenCharles Gaines and actor Ethan Hawke support the call for a moratorium on oil and gas exploration in the Gulf of St Lawrence in October 2015.Photograph: Andrew Vaughan/The Canadian Press/APBooksCharles Gaines, whose book Pumping Iron helped to popularize bodybuilding, dies at 841974 book led to making of seminal documentary and introduced Arnold Schwarzenegger to the worldAssociated PressWed 22 Jul 2026 13.58 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 14.13 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleCharles Gaines, the author whose book and documentary Pumping Iron helped bring professional bodybuilding into the mainstream and introduceArnold Schwarzeneggerto the world, has died. He was 84.Gaines died on Tuesday, according to Greta Gaines, the author’s daughter. She did not reveal a cause.“You always marched to your own drummer and inspired everyone around you to do the same,” she wrote in a tribute to her father. “Follow that bliss, catch that fish and kiss those lips while you can because my father was the live life to the fullest kinda guy.”Gaines collaborated with George Butler to write Pumping Iron: The Art and Sport of Bodybuilding, which led to the making of the documentary Pumping Iron, for which Butler and Gaines wrote the script.In the introduction to their 1974 book, Gaines wrote that he and Butler considered it “a sort of respectful report from the interior of an interesting, colorful and heretofore ignored region of American life”.In a 2022 edition, he admitted that neither author thought “many people outside of the bodybuilding world would be interested in” the book. Gaines wrote that Schwarzenegger almost single-handedly “brought bodybuilding out of the shadows it had languished in since its earliest days in America and into the limelight”.Schwarzenegger, who won seven Mr Olympia and five Mr Universe titles and who was pictured flexing on the cover of Pumping Iron, harnessed the attention into a career, first in films – including The Terminator, True Lies, Twins and Predator – and then politics, becoming governor of California from 2003 to 2011.Gaines wrote that he had been involved in bodybuilding in one way or another since he was 16, when he began lifting weights. Pumping Iron began as an article for Sports Illustrated in 1972 on a bodybuilding contest for Mr East Coast.“If we felt at times a little like 19th century explorers, it was because we found bodybuilding to be as primeval and unmapped as parts of Labrador,” he wrote. “Nobody, we discovered, had been back into it to send out a report on what it was like.”Gaines and Butler used the book as the basis for their 1977 documentary Pumping Iron, which centers on the rivalry between Schwarzenegger and fellow bodybuilder Lou Ferrigno in the competition for a Mr Olympia title. Ferrigno later starred on TV in The Incredible Hulk.Gaines was raised in Alabama and earned a bachelor’s degree from Birmingham-Southern College and a master of fine arts in writing from the University of Iowa.Gaines published numerous books throughout his career, including Waters Far and Near: Tales of Angling Adventure and Misadventure Around the World and the novels Stay Hungry, Dangler and Survival Games. Stay Hungry was adapted into a movie starring Jeff Bridges, giving Schwarzenegger his first movie role.“He climbed mountains, trekked across ice, fished, and hunted on almost every continent, writing about every adventure in a way that captured people’s hearts and minds. He was interested in everything – and his interest was contagious,” Schwarzenegger wrote on Instagram.“I’m lucky that he was always looking for new frontiers, because that’s how he found me and our niche sport of bodybuilding.”In addition to his daughter Greta, Gaines is survived by his wife, the artist Patricia Ellisor Gaines, and two other children, Latham and Shelby Gaines.Explore more on these topicsBooksArnold SchwarzeneggernewsShareReuse this content

title: How do Lime’s ebikes make money? Share market debut sheds light on finances  | Business | The Guardian
description: The firm’s public listing in June gives an insight into a question analysts have been wondering for years: how does this business stack up?
Author: Luca Ittimani
Headline: The firm’s public listing in June gives an insight into a question analysts have been wondering for years: how does this business stack up?
Content: In 2025, a total of 19 million customers globally used Lime across the 230 cities the company operates in, with each vehicle bringing in US$7.47 a day.Photograph: Cristian M Balate/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenIn 2025, a total of 19 million customers globally used Lime across the 230 cities the company operates in, with each vehicle bringing in US$7.47 a day.Photograph: Cristian M Balate/ShutterstockBusinessHow do Lime’s ebikes make money? Share market debut sheds light on financesThe firm’s public listing in June gives an insight into a question analysts have been wondering for years: how does this business stack up?Luca IttimaniWed 22 Jul 2026 19.49 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn April,Lime brought its green ebikes and e-scootersto Canberra. Before it had even arrived, the US micro-mobility firm had invested nearly US$2m.Lime’s experience in Australia’s sprawling capital is emblematic of a broader problem at the core of a business heavy on capital outlay.The share bike operator brought in 1,500 new vehicles, which each cost US$1,300 on average. Lime also pays for staff and warehouses to store and distribute vehicles around cities. It pays to run the app that lets users hop on board. It pays to fix the vehicles as riders wear them down, vandalise or crash them. It pays permit fees to local governments.But in return, its users are charged relatively little. Each vehicle only made the company US$7.47 a day in 2025 on average across the 230 cities Lime operates in.For years, analysts have been wondering: how does this business stack up?Lime’s first public financial documents shed some light.Share market filings show the company in June owed nearly US$850m in debt repayments within the next year and had to warn investors it may never turn a profit.It gained a financial lifeline fromUberand other investors when its parent company, Neutron Holdings, debuted on the Nasdaq on 1 July. Listing on sharemarkets cleared most of its debt and allowed it to repay the rest.A spokesperson for Lime said: “Lime has been free cash flow positive for two consecutive years, while growing topline revenue. With a significantly reduced debt burden coming out of our listing, our business is healthy and we’re excited about this next chapter as we work to offer a convenient, reliable, emissions-free way to travel.”View image in fullscreenLime now operates in 29 countries, including many in Europe.Photograph: Chesnot/Getty ImagesLime was founded in 2017 in San Francisco, California, and offered share bikes and scooters in more than 100 cities by 2020. It now operates in about 230 cities across 29 countries, mostly in Europe.Sydney is awash with shared ebikes. Is Australia finally falling in love with Lime?Read moreIts top-earning country, the US, brought in 32% of revenue in 2025, according to its share market prospectus. The UK segment is growing fast, contributing 22%, while France contributed 10%. Separate local filings show Australia contributed 3% in 2024.A total of 19 million riders used Lime in 2025. Most paid via the standard pay-per-minute model, unlocking the device and letting the meter run but 28% of Lime’s earnings now come from bundle and subscription models.Lime reported its field operations run at a profit, with the workers who maintain and move its ebikes and e-scooters its biggest expense.Lime keeps 39% of the revenue from its field operationsThe filings revealed growing usage has been enabled by rapid expansion of the fleet, from an average 229,000 in 2023 to 325,000 in 2025, with more e-scooters in the field than ebikes. Revenue has accordingly surged 30% each year since 2023, making nearly $887m in 2025.But the costs of expansion far outrun its revenue. Lime spent US$98m on new vehicles in 2025. It estimated every bike and scooter to cost about US$1,300 each and took a year to pay themselves off.Each device loses value over its estimated five-year lifespan, with some bikes gone sooner as they’re trashed by vandals.
