The company also paid US$271m for its back-end operations, from advertising to customer helplines. It separately reported US$57m in reserve to cover estimated costs of numerouspersonal injury claims, which the filing said it was defending “vigorously”.How Lime burnt nearly $1bn in a yearLime totalled US$946m in operating expenses in 2025 exceeding revenue by US$59m.To cover its losses, it had run up enormous debt. It reported an impending US$846m debt bill could have forced it to shut down if it hadn’t found extra money.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |When neededSign up toBreaking News AustraliaGet the most important news as it breaksPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionLime says its share market debut has solved its debt problem.. It has no plans to pay any dividends to shareholders in the foreseeable future.While the company runs at a net loss, its free cash flow is improving. The alternative measure, which accounts for investment in new vehicles while excluding some items, has raced up from just $1m in 2023 to $103m in 2025.More than low-hanging fruitThe market debut has underlined two of Lime’s unique advantages over its competitors in the shared bike and scooter industry.Uber, Lime’s biggest shareholder, bought in further at the market listing and now owns more than 23% of a business which competes for its customers. Lime’s filing claimed shared bikes and scooters are cheaper than ride-share fares.Uber has funded and underwritten much of the debt that has fuelled Lime’s expansion. Since 2018 Uber has also enabled customers to book Lime scooters and bikes through its app, generating $126m, or over 14%, of Lime’s 2025 revenue.Gad Allon, a professor at the University of Pennsylvania’s Wharton School, said Lime’s second key advantage is its dominance in its key cities.“Lime holds … local monopolies that a rival can’t simply buy its way into,” he said.Most of the company’s revenue growth in 2025 came not from entering new cities but from expanding within existing cities, as it scaled up at lower costs, according to its chief executive, Wayne Ting.“When we deploy ​more vehicles into a single city, we improve our density, and when we improve our density, it becomes a more reliable product, and that’s ​when people adopt and engage,” Ting told Reuters.It grew fleets in existing cities by 16% in 2025 and plans to experiment with tandem riding, longer-distance travel and new pricing plans to grow its market share, according to its filings.Allon said Lime’s two advantages were also unique points of weakness, both outside its control.“[It] is betting on regulators staying friendly and Uber staying aligned, and neither is in management’s hands,” he said.This article was amended on 23 July 2026. A previous version incorrectly stated Lime was facing US$850m in debt repayments. Lime cleared its debt bill subsequent to its sharemarket listing. The headline was also amended to reflect the changes to the article.Explore more on these topicsBusinessEbikesUberfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: AI agent went rogue and hacked startup by itself, OpenAI reveals | OpenAI | The Guardian
description: Company behind ChatGPT says agent ‘cheated’ an evaluation by attacking a Hugging Face database
Author: Dan Milmo
Headline: Company behind ChatGPT says agent ‘cheated’ an evaluation by attacking a Hugging Face database
Content: Hugging Face’s chief executive said the attack was ‘mind-blowing’ but that he believed there was ‘no malicious intent’ from OpenAI.Photograph: Andre M Chang/Zuma Press Wire/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenHugging Face’s chief executive said the attack was ‘mind-blowing’ but that he believed there was ‘no malicious intent’ from OpenAI.Photograph: Andre M Chang/Zuma Press Wire/ShutterstockOpenAIAI agent went rogue and hacked startup by itself, OpenAI revealsCompany behind ChatGPT says agent ‘cheated’ an evaluation by attacking a Hugging Face databaseDan MilmoGlobal technology editorWed 22 Jul 2026 12.34 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 04.37 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleOpenAI has revealed that an autonomous AI agent powered by its technology went rogue during a test, accessed the open web and hacked a prominent startup by itself in an “unprecedented incident”.The company behind ChatGPT said the startup Hugging Face had detected and contained the agent – an AI tool designed to carry out tasks without human assistance – which had entered its systems.“We consider this incident to be an unprecedented cyber-incident, involving state-of-the-art cyber capabilities,”OpenAI said.The company said it expected this type of incident to become more commonplace as models – the technology that underpins AI tools such as chatbots and agents – become more capable. OpenAI said the hack occurred via an agent powered by a combination of its latest publicly available model, calledGPT-5.6 Sol, and an even more capable model that was yet to be released.While being tested internally on their hacking capabilities in an enclosed digital laboratory known as a sandbox, the models gained open internet access – effectively an escape route – by locating a vulnerability that had not been discovered before.The agent then hacked Hugging Face, which is a database of AI models, to locate technology that would help it pass the hacking evaluation, having “inferred” that Hugging Face might have the models, datasets and solutions for passing the test. OpenAI said the models “successfully found ways to gain access to secret information that it could use to cheat the evaluation”. The attack ended when Hugging Face’s security team and its own AI agents spotted and stopped the rogue activity.Hugging Face’s chief executive, Clément Delangue, said the attack was “mind-blowing” but believed there was “no malicious intent” from OpenAI.“We suspected last week’s cyber-attack might have come from a frontier lab, given the sophistication of the agent,” he wrote on X.When Hugging Face disclosed the hack last week it did not know OpenAI’s role in the incident, but said at the time that it had turned to a freely available Chinese AI model to analyse what had happened because the safety guardrails on commercial high-end models would not allow it to do so.The term for an unknown IT flaw is a zero-day vulnerability because developers have zero minutes to fix the problem. In April OpenAI’s close rival Anthropic said its Mythos modelhad found thousands of these flaws.The revelation of Mythos’s ability to locate and exploit zero-days led to the US government restricting exports of Mythos and its sister model, Fable 5, although it has since lifted the ban. GPT-5.6 Sol had similar restrictions but has since been rolled out worldwide.METR, a non-profit organisation that measures AI performance, said last month that Sol’s cheating rate was higher than any public model it had evaluated before. It has also recorded44 incidentsin which AI agents “deliberately acted against their users’ intentions”.The UK’s AI Security Institute (AISA)revealed in a blog post this weekthat one AI model it was evaluating, developed by an undisclosed tech firm, also went rogue and attempted to hack its testing systems. AISI said no damage had been done and it has since taken steps to make its systems more secure. It said models developed by OpenAI and Anthropic had all attempted to “cheat” during tests.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAISI warned that even more capable models may find cheating methods that are harder to detect and more damaging if they succeed, particularly in areas such as cybersecurity.One cybersecurity expert said the Hugging Face incident showed the OpenAI agent had acted “like an actual real hacker” by, for instance, seeking out zero-day vulnerabilities and using stolen credentials to access Hugging Face’s systems.“The AI thought that maybe Hugging Face would have important information around how to achieve its goal, which is a better score in a cybersecurity benchmark. In that sense, it acted like a real hacker,” said Nathaniel Jones, vice-president of security and AI strategy at the cybersecurity firm Darktrace. . “It had a goal put in front of it and it went to accomplish that goal.”Greg Casar, a Democratic US congressman who has called for greater control of the AI sector, described the incident as alarming.“AI is developing extremely fast with no real regulations to keep us safe,” he said in a statement calling for mandatory independent safety testing, mandatory disclosure of security incidents, and international cooperation “to keep people safe from absolute disaster”.Explore more on these topicsOpenAIAI (artificial intelligence)HackingTechnology sectorInternetnewsShareReuse this content

title: Norway’s national oil company’s profits double to $11.5bn amid war on Iran | Oil | The Guardian
description: Equinor benefits from decision to increase oil and gas production during strait of Hormuz blockades
Author: Kalyeena Makortoff
Headline: Equinor benefits from decision to increase oil and gas production during strait of Hormuz blockades
Content: Equinor's LNG facility outside Hammerfest, Norway.Photograph: Ole Berg-Rusten/NTB/AFP/GettyView image in fullscreenEquinor's LNG facility outside Hammerfest, Norway.Photograph: Ole Berg-Rusten/NTB/AFP/GettyOilNorway’s national oil company’s profits double to $11.5bn amid war on IranEquinor benefits from decision to increase oil and gas production during strait of Hormuz blockadesBusiness live – latest updatesKalyeena MakortoffWed 22 Jul 2026 08.45 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 04.37 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleProfits at Norway’s state oil company nearly doubled to $11.5bn (£8.6bn) in the three months to the end of June as the jump in oil and gas prices caused by the war against Iran boosted earnings.Equinor– which is the UK’s biggest gas supplier – benefited from a decision to increase oil and gas production at the start of the conflict, filling a gap in the market after a near-halt to shipping through the strait of Hormuz caused Gulf oil flows to slump.Equinor alsoprofited from the jump in oil prices. Fears over a drop in global supplies left Brent crude prices swinging between $75 and more than $100 a barrel between April and June this year. That compares with roughly $60 to $70 during the same period last year.While oil prices fell after the US and Iran signed a memorandum of understanding last month, they have begun rising again amid the resumption of hostilities. Brent crude prices rose to as high as $95 a barrel on Wednesday, before easing to $94, up 3% on the day.Equinor’s president and chief executive, Anders Opedal, said: “Strong production in the second quarter enabled us to capture value from higher prices, contributing to strong cashflow and financial results.“Reliable energy is important in a volatile world marked by heightened geopolitical tension. Our role is to deliver energy safely and efficiently every day,” he added.Wednesday briefing: How Trump’s Iran gamble has produced a crisis reshaping the global orderRead moreThe increased production and higher energy prices almost doubled Equinor’s adjusted profits of $6.5bn it made in the April to June period last year. The company also passed analysts’ predicted profits of $11.37bn.Meanwhile, the climate campaign group Uplift hit out at Equinor for “raking in billions in profits while millions of people across the UK are struggling with unaffordable energy bills”.Its executive director, Tessa Khan, said the Norwegian state oil company was also “pushing” the UK government to approve production at theRosebankoilfield, off the coast of the Shetland Islands, “to keep those profits flowing”.“Rosebank won’t cut our bills – it’s overwhelmingly oil for export – but it will make Equinor and its part-owner, the Norwegian government, even richer.” Khan urged the new UK prime minister, Andy Burnham, to step up renewable energy production and “put the public’s need for affordable energy and a safe climate ahead of this Norwegian oil giant’s profits, and reject Rosebank”.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe news comes amid a further rise in oil prices on Wednesday after the US military launched its 11th night of strikes on Iran,including onaircraft hangars and drone storage sites. The attacks have undermined hopes that diplomatic efforts cansalvage the interim ceasefire.Yemen’s Iran-aligned Houthis, who control the coast at the mouth of the Red Sea, announceda naval blockade on Saudi Arabia, which has been relying on a pipeline to the Red Sea to get millions of barrels of oil out to market, given the Hormuz route remains restricted.The news led to further rises in energy prices. “Brent crude has raced upwards again to trade around $93 a barrel, the highest level in six weeks,” said Susannah Streeter, the chief investment strategist at the investment platform Wealth Club.“Risks to supplies are mounting again, with the effective blockage of the strait of Hormuz remaining a chokehold as tankers are stranded in and around the waterway, while risks to other crude routes are also intensifying.”Explore more on these topicsOilNorwayGasCommoditiesEuropeUS-Israel war on IranStrait of HormuznewsShareReuse this content

title: Lloyd’s of London says ex-boss’s ‘close relationship’ breached compliance rules  | Lloyd's | The Guardian
description: John Neal failed to disclose ties with colleague that created a potential conflict of interest, investigation finds
Author: Kalyeena Makortoff
Headline: John Neal failed to disclose ties with colleague that created a potential conflict of interest, investigation finds
Content: John Neal undertook to modify his conduct but there was ‘no evidence of material change’, investigators said.Photograph: Chip Somodevilla/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenJohn Neal undertook to modify his conduct but there was ‘no evidence of material change’, investigators said.Photograph: Chip Somodevilla/Getty ImagesLloyd'sLloyd’s of London says ex-boss’s ‘close relationship’ breached compliance rulesJohn Neal failed to disclose ties with colleague that created a potential conflict of interest, investigation findsKalyeena MakortoffBanking correspondentWed 22 Jul 2026 06.32 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 08.58 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleLloyd’s of London has ruled that its former boss John Neal breached compliance rules by failing to disclose a “close relationship” with a female colleague that risked triggering conflict of interest concerns at the insurance market.An investigation by the Council of Lloyd’s, which is responsible for the market’s management and supervision, said it could not find any “conclusive evidence” Neal was romantically involved with the company’s then corporate affairs director, nor that there were any process failures relating to her promotion during Neal’s tenure.However, it said the pair were close enough to cause senior managers to raise questions directly with Neal.“Based on the findings of the investigation, the Council of Lloyd’s found that the relationship between Mr Neal and … Rebekah Clement was sufficiently close during their employment at Lloyd’s that it could be viewed as creating a perceived conflict of interest. This was not disclosed by either Mr Neal or Ms Clement,” the council said.Neal served as chief executive of Lloyd’s of London from 2018 to 2025. Lloyd’s launched the investigation into the relationship and disclosures in November 2025, after its chair, Sir Charles Roxburgh, became aware of fresh information about the relationship.The council said Roxburgh had also become aware of a series of whistleblower reports dating back to 2023, which had not been escalated, in a breach of the market’s own governance rules that was immediately reported to the Financial Conduct Authority.While mention of the whistleblower reports emerged in the same release as its findings against Neal, Lloyd’s said that it could not share the nature of the allegations or the identities of who the allegations related to, in order “to protect the identity of the whistleblowers”.Investigators said senior managers at Lloyd’s raised their concerns about the relationship directly with Neal “on more than one occasion”. And while the former chief executive acknowledged their concerns and his responsibilities, and “undertook to modify his conduct”, there was “no evidence of material change in Mr Neal’s conduct thereafter”.Lloyd’s of London rebuked for cutting off whistleblowing hotlineRead moreThe council said it struggled to gain relevant information at the start of the investigation, given that Neal and Clement had both left the company and “declined to answer questions relating to the nature of their relationship”. It added that Neal also turned down a request to provide access to his mobile phone. Investigators ended up interviewing 40 witnesses, many of whom, it said, came forward “late in the process”.The secrecy surrounding their relationship constituted a breach of the company’s global compliance policy, which requires any real or potential conflict of interest to be disclosed, the investigation concluded.Failure to divulge the information also “fell significantly below the standards expected of Lloyd’s senior leaders and was detrimental to the interests of the corporation and the Lloyd’s market”, it said.Lloyd’s said it had written to Neal to explain that it would have cancelled any pending payouts as a penalty. However, Neal had already forfeited his unvested pay when he resigned from Lloyd’s last year.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionRoxburgh said Lloyd’s had since strengthened its internal processes. “These were serious failures that should never have been allowed to happen.“These findings underline the importance of robust governance structures and processes. Where standards were not best in class, we have put that right. However, governance can only ever be part of the answer. Culture and personal accountability also play a vital role. That is why the Council of Lloyd’s is unequivocal about the behaviour we expect from everyone, at every level, at the Corporation of Lloyd’s.”Clement is considering legal action against Lloyd’s. Her lawyer said in a statement that she was “not surprised that Lloyd’s found no evidence of an inappropriate relationship with John Neal, nor any evidence of any failings in her promotion … Yet, Lloyd’s has still chosen to find against Rebekah, on the pretext of ‘perception’, the source of which was rumour, gossip and innuendo.”The lawyer said Clement had “cooperated with the investigation throughout” but that “for over a year there have been repeated leaks and press briefings about Rebekah, without warning or regard for the damage done to her career, reputation and health”.“While Rebekah is pleased that this protracted investigation has come to a close, she has put Lloyd’s and its senior officers on notice that she is considering legal proceedings.”Explore more on these topicsLloyd'sInsurance industryFinancial sectornewsShareReuse this content

title: Tate desperately needs a radical overhaul – but will its new director really be an agent of change? | Art and design | The Guardian
description: From over-hyped shows to the woeful Turner prize, this huge institution has been beset by problems – yet new boss Jessica Morgan seems too much like a safe pair of hands to shake things up
Author: Jonathan Jones
Headline: From over-hyped shows to the woeful Turner prize, this huge institution has been beset by problems – yet new boss Jessica Morgan seems too much like a safe pair of hands to shake things up
Content: Capacious crisis … Tate Modern.Photograph: Alex Segre/AlamyView image in fullscreenCapacious crisis … Tate Modern.Photograph: Alex Segre/AlamyArt and designTate desperately needs a radical overhaul – but will its new director really be an agent of change?Jonathan JonesFrom over-hyped shows to the woeful Turner prize, this huge institution has been beset by problems – yet new boss Jessica Morgan seems too much like a safe pair of hands to shake things upJessica Morgan named new Tate directorWed 22 Jul 2026 09.32 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 10.11 EDTShareEven Tate knows it needs to change. This top-heavy montage of four museums really needs to scrutinise itself and ask hard questions about its aims and how it works – including why it often doesn’t. Apparently aware of the crisis, it hails its new director Jessica Morgan as “an agent of change”. Well that’s a relief. But is she really?Morgan is a highly rated curator of contemporary art, recently leading the Dia Art Foundation in the US to great acclaim and with an earlier track record atTate Modernitself. Yet these very strengths suggest she will stay within a groove that Tate is all too comfortable with. It’s unlikely she’s going to rush to put on a show about art and the Reformation, for instance.View image in fullscreenHighly rated … Jessica Morgan.Photograph: Gabriella Herman/Tate/PAThis capacious institution loves to identify itself with new art but owns a lot of the old kind too. The Tate collection includes British art since 1500, butTate Britainhas become chronologically parched in its exhibitions, rarely going back into the Tudor or Stuart ages and mostly focusing on art since the Victorians. At least Gainsborough gets a show next year, but seriously, Tate needs a proper plan for its older British art. I hope Morgan will give this thought despite her background.Ana Mendieta review – were she still alive she’d be at the forefront of art in this centuryRead moreNot that Tate Modern is perfectly fine and dandy. When things go right they can be magnificent, like the current exhibition of Ana Mendieta’s shamanistic, myth-making visions. Yet alongside it is the museum’sdisappointing, over-hyped Frida Kahlo showthat was obviously problematic from an early stage. The question here is why no one raised the alarm: why there was no pre-mortem before the critics pronounced their postmortems? The same goes forlast year’s dire Turbine Hall installationby Máret Ánne Sara. The faults of this installation must have been obvious but, somehow, nobody in Tate Modern seems to have said so.View image in fullscreen‘A rather cosy sideline’ … Tate St Ives.Photograph: Matt Cardy/Getty ImagesThese unforced errors, which have also happened at Tate Britain with itsludicrously over-attitudinising collection hang, suggest a real problem with self-questioning. Tate seems to have a culture of rigid self-belief and sees all criticism as enmity. This internal complacency or lack of democracy needs fixing fast. And the Tate brand itself needs work. “Brand” – there, I’ve said it. An art gallery should not be a brand. The transformation of Tate into its current form happened at the end of the 1990s in an era of hyper-marketing and PR-massaged cool. Today it’s almost as if Tate’s PR machine and branding determine its values: even the announcement of Morgan’s appointment comes with a long list of endorsements by artists – a totally unnecessary piece of pre-judging.Morgan has to totally rethink Tate (maybe even allow it to be called “the” Tate). The outlets need serious attention. I likeTate St Ivesbut it’s arguably a rather cosy sideline. And of course, there’s the annual agony – the moribund Turner prize. Tate is too set in its ways, too dogmatic about championing “groundbreaking” art – and its curators are very confident they know what that looks like. But the idea that a museum should take sides and strike attitudes is wrong.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toArt WeeklyYour weekly art world round-up, sketching out all the biggest stories, scandals and exhibitionsPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionTate needs to surprise us. Morgan will bring that fresh eye we are promised, even if she looks like a safe pair of hands who might bring more of the same. I hope she proves to be a revolutionary Trojan horse.Explore more on these topicsArt and designTate BritainArtTate LiverpoolTate ModernTate St IvesLondoncommentShareReuse this content

title: How Pokémon Go encouraged me to find wonder in the world | Games | The Guardian
description: These days, I ‘collect’ birdsong and wild flowers, but it all started when my sons and I first set out to find Pikachu and pals• Don’t get Pushing Buttons delivered to your inbox? Sign up here
Author: Keith Stuart
Headline: These days, I ‘collect’ birdsong and wild flowers, but it all started when my sons and I first set out to find Pikachu and pals• Don’t get Pushing Buttons delivered to your inbox? Sign up here
Content: Surely we’ve caught them all? … Pokémon Go celebrates its tenth birthday.Photograph: Eduardo Muñoz/ReutersView image in fullscreenSurely we’ve caught them all? … Pokémon Go celebrates its tenth birthday.Photograph: Eduardo Muñoz/ReutersPushing Buttons newsletterGamesHow Pokémon Go encouraged me to find wonder in the worldThese days, I ‘collect’ birdsong and wild flowers, but it all started when my sons and I first set out to find Pikachu and palsDon’t get Pushing Buttons delivered to your inbox? Sign up hereKeith StuartWed 22 Jul 2026 10.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 10.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleBelieve it or not, Pokémon Go is10 years old this month. The wildly successful smartphone game, which allows you to track down lovable creatures in the real world using GPS and augmented-reality technologies, has reportedly been downloaded 800m times across 150 countries and regions. A trillion Pokémon have been caught so far – and I have been responsible for a modest percentage of that figure.When the game was first released, my sons and I spent many happy hours wandering the streets and parks of Frome searching for Jigglypuffs and Charizards, and the game’s social element, which lets you take on gym battles with other players in your vicinity, offered my autistic son a route to communicate with other children in a way he’d never been able to before.My boys have grown up now, but I enjoy the fact that the game is still seducing new players into searching their local shopping centre for Machops. There’s a part of me that never really let go of this compulsion, although it’s now evolved. I have a folder of apps on my phone that I have ready whenever I go out for a walk, or visit an unfamiliar town. Seek isa free appthat uses image recognition to help you identify plants, trees and animals, and it lets you collect species badges to celebrate new discoveries. The accuracy can be a little variable, but it has given me a new interest in wild flowers and trees, and, being a nerd, I like to imagine which particular species would win in a Pokémon-style battle. How would red valerian fare against field bindweed? What would be the special attack of the common dog violet?Another favourite is theMerlin Bird IDapp, developed by the Cornell Lab of Ornithology. It lets you identify birdsong wherever you are in the world, and provides a “bird of the day” to look out for. My wife and I use it competitively, sending each other screenshots of unusual birds we’ve heard on our travels. I can now fairly confidently identify at least 10 bird songs, and I have to admit, I do associate certain birds with specific Pokémon. Pikachu? The yellow hammer, of course. Corviknight is the raven. Pidgeots are self-explanatory.View image in fullscreenThe new Pikachu … the Merlin Bird ID app lets you identify birdsong, like that of the yellow hammer.Photograph: Ernie Janes/AlamyAlongside these, I also loveFlightradar24andMarineTraffic, which track airplane and ship movement respectively. I like to know where things are going and why, and to play Top Trumps with the vehicle data, pitting nearby cargo ships against each other in imagined naval battles based on their size and carrying capacity.I’m not sure I’d be using any of these apps if its wasn’t forPokémon Go. It opened up in me an understanding of the natural world as a vast playable space, filled with beautiful little signs and systems. When you explore a new game world, everything you encounter is potentially interesting. You have no filter of expectation – in terms of executive function, it’s like being a toddler again. As you get older, you lose that fascination with the minutiae of the world around you – you go for dramatic vistas, rather than the patches of weeds along roadsides; the dawn chorus over the solitary songbird in your garden. Sometimes you need a little prod to bring that back.During the summer of 2016, and for a few seasons after, every walk we took became an adventure. Mundane places we knew well suddenly bristled with possibilities, boring landmarks became battlegrounds, strangers became allies. I just took all that and applied it to the real things around us – the birds in the woods near where we live, the weeds in our overgrown garden, the planes overhead. I’m not alone in this. Inspired by Pokémon Go, Brisbane student Kris Norris has created a social discovery game based onleaving messages on payphonesaround the city. Hydrologists in Colorado used a Pokémon Go-style app todiscover and track small streams.I’ve also discovered other location-based experiences along the way, that I might not have thought about otherwise. The fitness gameZombies, Run!was better for me as a running tool than Couch to 5k;Pikmin Bloomis a delightful floral walking game, andGeocaching, where you track down real-life caches hidden by other players, has tricked me into a few long hikes.What I have learned, or at least had confirmed, since playing Pokémon Go is that all video games have the potential to open new intellectual doorways – new ways to envisage the world and how it works. No time spent in play is wasted. Wonder is a transferable skill.What to playView image in fullscreenPerfect for a cosy evening … The Mermaid Mask game.Photograph: SFB GamesMystery games are having a real moment, but brothers Tom and Adam Vian have been quietly perfecting their Detective Grimoire series for more than a decade. I’ve eagerly awaitedThe Mermaid Mask, in which the titular detective and his smarter sarcastic sidekick Sally try to solve a locked-room murder aboard a submarine, and it does not disappoint.I always recommend playing these games with a loved one – not because they’re difficult (the puzzles are about as taxing as you’d want for a cosy evening) but because the fully voice-acted, laugh-out-loud script makes them just as fun to watch (and backseat drive) as to actually play.Jordan Erica WebberAvailable on:PC, Switch/Switch 2, PS5Estimated playtime:10 hours, best enjoyed over a few eveningsskip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toPushing ButtonsKeza MacDonald's weekly look at the world of gamingPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionWhat to readView image in fullscreenAll the gear … Modern Warfare is the universe of choice for Paramount’s new Call of Duty film.Photograph: Activision BlizzardLast year, Paramount signed up to make aCall of Dutymovie. Now,the studio has confirmedthat it will take place in the Modern Warfare universe – which means contemporary weapons, hi-tech espionage and geopolitical slaughter. But who will play moustached SAS hero Captain Price? There are rumours Pedro Pascal is up for the role, but surely it’s got to be Henry Cavill?!So now it’s all over,it turns outEA Sports FC correctly predicted theWorld Cupwinner for the fifth year in a row. The development team ran a simulation of every group and knock-out match in the competition, where Spain emerged as the victor. I wonder if it also predicted thatEngland v Francewould be the most video game-like match of the tournament?On Saturday, GamesRadarran a feature interviewwith senior figures at Xbox, who confirmed the importance ofexclusive gamesto the console. “There has to be a reason for people to buy an Xbox,” said chief content officer Matt Booty, accurately. Gears of War: E-Day and Clockwork Revolution will be the first titles to be affected by the withdrawal from the multiplatform approach that saw titles such as Forza Horizon 6 and Sea of Thieves head to PS5.What to clickBub – living an artist’s dreams and dreads in New YorkApple Crumble: a tasty Knives Out-inspired mystery that lets you snoop on your own familyCastlevania: Belmont’s Curse – Konami classic rises again from Paris sewers and Joan of Arc is a bossD&D players raise millions in real-life campaign against ‘corporate elite’Denshattack! – time to get on board with kickflipping trains | ★★★★☆Splatoon Raiders – Nintendo’s inky shooter fades to grey | ★★☆☆☆Question BlockView image in fullscreenOne of life’s great teachers … There’s much to be learned from games like Super Mario Odyssey.Photograph: NintendoThis week, a philosophical question fromRichard:“What genuine life lessons have you learned from games? I often think of Verso’s advice to Lune inClair Obscur: Expedition 33, thatthe only voice that should be in your head is your own. Bonus hard mode: not just life lessons from dialogue or other ‘narrative’ parts of a game, but from thegamingaction itself. The hours I spent trying to beat the last level ofSuper Mario Odysseytaught me a lot about my flaws: panicking in the face of new situations, and stumbling over bits I had done umpteen times beforebecause I contemptuously thought I’d mastered them.”One lesson I took from platform games such as Impossible Mission and Jet Set Willy and later from stealth games, was to stop and observe any complex situation before leaping in. That has saved me a lot of trouble. I’ve also picked up little snippets of practical information that I’ll never get to use in real-life – I discovered the Scandinavian flick playing World Rally Championship (it’s a driving technique, not a haircut) and the Coriolis effect while playing the famed assassination mission inCall of Duty 4: Modern Warfare. Finally, as a novelist, I learned plenty of things about comedy writing fromThe Secret of Monkey Island– specifically the importance of understatement and how to set up a gag and then keep it running. The line “That’s the second-biggest monkey head I’ve ever seen” will always stick in my head in relation to both of those skills. I should have it crocheted and framed on my wall.If you have a question for Question Block – or anything else to say about the newsletter –email us atpushingbuttons@theguardian.com.Explore more on these topicsGamesPushing Buttons newsletterPokémon GofeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Why The Polygamist is so much more than trash TV and a guilty pleasure streaming hit | Television | The Guardian
description: Deemed Africa’s first Netflix success, the show’s appeal is rooted in a universal story: what people tolerate for love and money
Author: Nesrine Malik
Headline: Deemed Africa’s first Netflix success, the show’s appeal is rooted in a universal story: what people tolerate for love and money
Content: A catfight … Jonasi creates bespoke worlds of attention for his women.Photograph: NetflixView image in fullscreenA catfight … Jonasi creates bespoke worlds of attention for his women.Photograph: NetflixThe Long WaveTelevisionWhy The Polygamist is so much more than trash TV and a guilty pleasure streaming hitDeemed Africa’s first Netflix success, the show’s appeal is rooted in a universal story: what people tolerate for love and moneyDon’t already get The Long Wave in your inbox? Sign up hereNesrine MalikWed 22 Jul 2026 08.56 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 09.41 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleOver the past few weeks, I have been gripped by The Polygamist, a new, record-breaking South African Netflix drama, which racked up more than 23 million views in its first month. I’ve been zealously talking about how special it is to anyone who will listen. And now it’s your turn, dear reader.View image in fullscreenTradition v modernity … an interesting thought experiment illustrating the African middle class’s trajectory.Photograph: NetflixThe Polygamist is by all accounts the first African smash hit of the streaming age. Released in mid June, the show has topped streaming charts in Africa, and broken into the global top five non-English series globally. It has also broken through all sorts of cultural and language barriers. African and western starshave ravedabout it. Its protagonists havebecome overnight celebrities. And its twists, turns and surrounding discourse so saturate social media that I have had to forgo Instagram and TikTok to avoid spoilers.On first encounter, The Polygamist looks like little more than a guilty pleasure, a high-gloss telenovela about infidelity. When I first pressed play on it, I had absolutely no intention of getting through its staggering 22 episodes. I just wanted to satiate my curiosity about the hype. Reader, I was wrong. So wrong. And now I’m cursed with the zeal of the converted. What we have here is not trash TV, but a profound rumination on tradition v modernity, and the broader universal trajectory of an African middle class.‘This isn’t polygamy, this is dirt’View image in fullscreenCynically weaponised … the relationship dynamics betray their so-called cultural underpinnings.Photograph: NetflixThe concept is simple. The titular polygamist, Jonasi Gomora played by S’dumo Mtshali, is a wealthy business tycoon with a big appetite for good food, expensive whisky, and beautiful women. He is married (of course) to glamorous social media influencer Joyce, portrayed by Gugu Gumede, and has three children. He claims and commands a number of other women in his orbit – none of whom dislodge his wife or main family unit. But tension starts to build when it becomes clear that Jonasi does not only want mistresses, spliced alongside his family life. He wants it all. Several homes, several wives, several sets of children.The predictable cat fighting over Jonasi is juicy and unhinged. Hands are thrown, wigs are snatched and things may or may not be set on fire. But this quickly unfurls into a number of far more sophisticated and unexpected storylines. One surrounds Jonasi’s powerful characterisation. In addition to his wealth, he is charming, playful, forceful, and persuasive. We marvel as he is able to inspire devotion no matter how badly he behaves. His character transcends that of the unfaithful scoundrel into that of the alpha who can create bespoke worlds of attention for each of his women. And so they yield again and again, ultimately (and reluctantly) accepting polygamy as the best arrangement. It is better to be one of Jonasi’s women – and to retain access to this intimate and enchanting world – than to be thrust out on their own.When Jonasi takes a second wife, the discourse machine gets going. His marriage quickly becomes a matter of public debate, as he and Joyce are public figures. She cleverly decides to own the second marriage, and gives candid interviews about it, promoting the act not as a humiliation for her, but as an expression of the capaciousness and generosity of polygamy. The way that polygamy is portrayed is skilfully subtle, teasing out all the complexities of these arrangements in a postcolonial context. On the one hand, Jonasi asserts his right to polygamy as cultural and traditional authenticity, something repressed by colonialism and the secularisation of African society. But Jonasi is also clearly a man who will say and do anything to get his way. And so, like the characters, I found myself torn between the legitimacy of African marriage rites and rituals(polygamy is legal in South Africa and several other African countries), and the sense that these evocative traditions can be cynically weaponised to excuse disloyalty and misogyny. As the actor Mtshalisaid in an interview for CNN: “This isn’t polygamy, this is dirt.”FetishisingbourgeoisvaluesView image in fullscreenHolding on to the rainmaker … Jonasi’s social mobility makes staying with him an attractive proposition.Photograph: NetflixThe tensions between tradition and modernity are also sharpened by the ravages of social mobility. The show transforms from soap into serious dramatic art when it flashes back to Jonasi’s childhood in Soweto, a township with high levels of poverty and crime. Young Jonasi is smart, with ambitions to leave, and uses his charm to persuade the women around him to support his escape from poverty. His backstory feels like a metaphor for all the ravages that rapid social mobility has wreaked on a generation of post-apartheid South Africans. But it’s also relevant to all societies in which profoundly unequal economies have driven people towards wealth acquisition as their only saviour. Despite his flaws, Jonasi continues to enthral those around him, because his journey of social mobility means that he is their saviour too. Values, principles, good character, are all jettisoned in the flight from economic hardship.Relatedly, the series lays bare the toxicity of fetishising bourgeois values: characters squabble over the latest iPhone, covet expensive alcohol and, in one instance, salivate over Wagyu beef. But more unexpectedly, it also leans heavily into the unglamorous side of the characters’ lives. There are sexually transmitted diseases, lost and emotionally devastated children, and instances of physical abuse. The Polygamist does not romanticise or idealise middle- and upper-class African society. Everyone in the show seems trapped in Jonasi’s orbit, suffering higher and higher tithes in the hope of holding on to the rainmaker.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThe Long WaveNesrine Malik, Micha Frazer-Carroll and the team guide you through the biggest Black stories from around the worldPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionBut there is love too. Amid the squalor and devastation, something real somehow always survives – the love that Jonasi’s wives and families still retain for him, and the kindness and dedication among them that still endures. This is the result of some blockbuster acting. Noluthando Shabalala in particular, who plays Jonasi’s daughter, Mpume, delivers a tear-jerking portrayal of a young adulthood mangled in the spokes of Jonasi’s cruelty. Beneath the wild outfits, technicolour makeup and terrible wigs, the actors are putting in a thespian shift for the ages.A quietly radical rollercoasterView image in fullscreenBlack and African universe … the directing team represent other parts of the continent, as well as South Africa.Photograph: NetflixThe Polygamist is a pan-African affair. It is adapted from a novel by Sue Nyathi, a Zimbabwean writer, and is directed by Akin Omotoso, a Nigerian, and Nthabi Tau and Rolie Nikiwe, who are both South African. It is striking how there are no white characters, no interest in even name-checking what calamity everyone is crawling out from underneath, and why the townships existed in the first place. This is an entirely Black and African universe, not concerned with translating itself to a foreign audience. And, paradoxically, that is the reason it has cut through the way it has. Through a particular lens, it tells a universal story, of how much human beings are willing to tolerate for love, and for money.Tap inHave you seen The Polygamist? Share your reaction to the show by replying to this, or emailing us atthelongwave@theguardian.comand we may include your response in our next issue.To receive the complete version of The Long Wave in your inbox every Wednesday,please subscribe here.Explore more on these topicsTelevisionThe Long WaveDramaSouth AfricaNetflixAfricanewslettersShareReuse this content

title: Nostalgia review – Tarkovsky’s yearning cine-poem is deeply mysterious and profound | Movies | The Guardian
description: In the Russian director’s Italy-set 1983 great, a scholar has an existential crisis while researching the life of a composer
Author: Peter Bradshaw
Headline: In the Russian director’s Italy-set 1983 great, a scholar has an existential crisis while researching the life of a composer
Content: Alienation and belonging … Andrei Tarkovsky’s Nostalgia.Photograph: courtesy of Curzon FilmView image in fullscreenAlienation and belonging … Andrei Tarkovsky’s Nostalgia.Photograph: courtesy of Curzon FilmMoviesReviewNostalgia review – Tarkovsky’s yearning cine-poem is deeply mysterious and profoundA scholar has an existential crisis while researching the life of a composer in the Russian director’s Italy-set 1983 masterworkPeter BradshawWed 22 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 08.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAndrei Tarkovsky’s 1983 film is a mysterious, imagist cine-poem about alienation and belonging, whose title means a yearning not simply for a lost time or lost place but for something, anything, else – with a profound anguished sense that the here-and-now of existence is unsatisfactory.The movie takes something from the poetry of the director’s father Arseny Tarkovsky, which is quoted in the film. A distinguished Russian scholar is in Italy to research the life of the (fictional) Russian composer Pavel Sosnovsky who is supposed to have travelled there before being overwhelmed by homesick longing and returning to the motherland. His beautiful, flirtatious interpreter is reading Tarkovsky’s poetry in an Italian translation, and the Russian coldly tells her to throw the book away: “Poetry is untranslatable, like the whole of art.” Does he really mean that? This movie is itself, in a way, translated; it is set in Italy and co-written by Tarkovsky with the veteran Italian screenwriter Tonino Guerra, who worked with Fellini and Antonioni. (Like Jean-Claude Carrière, Guerra, who died in 2012, was a prolific collaborator in European cinema.)Oleg Yankovskiy plays Andrei, the Russian who is in Italy to immerse himself spiritually in the places where Sosnovsky went. On arriving in the Tuscan spa town of Bagno Vignoni, frequented by Saint Catherine herself, he appears to be overcome by a kind of breakdown or emotional paralysis, unable to respond to his surroundings and engaged in a kind of silent inner dialogue with himself, plagued with epiphanic, monochrome dreams or memories of his family at home inRussia. His gloomy, grumpy reticence exasperates his interpreter Eugenia (Domiziana Giordano), who is attracted to him and clearly wondering if something might happen. She is compelled to visit a famous church and shrine without him and witnesses birds flying out of the breast of a statue of the Blessed Virgin.Both Andrei and Eugenie are perplexed by the behaviour of a local homeless man and reputed “lunatic”; this is Domenico, played with monkish severity and calm by Erland Josephson. Domenico is notorious locally for having imprisoned his wife and children in his house to protect them from what he was convinced was the imminent end of the world. Now he believes that if he can carry a lit candle across the town’s baths without it going out, humanity and the world can be saved.Andrei is fascinated by Domenico and conflates the man’s terrified family with his own in his mind. Domenico’s existence is explained by Eugenie as a result ofItalyclosing its asylums, but he is surely an intensely Russian figure: the holy fool, the wanderer, the poor man who is also Christ, who must always be given alms if he appears at your door. Eugenie, by contrast, is a more obviously western European figure: a passionate and sexually adventurous woman who is furious at Andrei’s unavailability, and who has a criminal boyfriend in Rome.View image in fullscreenIs this the end of the world? … Nostalgia.Photograph: courtesy of Curzon FilmIn its third act, Tarkovsky’s movie removes from the bucolic peace of the countryside to the crowded and clamorous capital of Rome, a late-breaking coup which could be compared to the similar shift at the end ofIvan’s Childhood. But eventually Andrei returns to Bagno Vignoni, by which time the famous baths have dried out – is this the end of the world? – and begins the long, lugubrious process of trying to walk across them with a lit candle.Like Tarkovsky’s subsequent film, The Sacrifice, this is a movie about the absence of God. Domenico is supposed to have revealed himself as irredeemably mad with the grotesque and cruel imprisonment of his family on the grounds that the end of the world was nigh. Yet perhaps he does not regard himself as in any way rebuked by how things turned out – perhaps his family staggered out of his house into a world which had indeed ended; a world in which God was no longer believed in. Like the waters of the baths, God has evaporated, drained away, leaving only the bulbous, dirty and ugly shapes of the walls and floor of the baths and the rickety steps. It is an unimprovably stark, sad film.Nostalgia is in UK cinemas from 24 July.Explore more on these topicsFilmDrama filmsAndrei TarkovskyItalyRussiaEuropereviewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘I come from a murderer’: the woman who found out Charles Manson was her grandfather | Documentary films | The Guardian
description: Difficult new Hulu documentary follows a 26-year-old woman’s startling discovery that she’s related to the notorious, murderous cult leader
Author: Vivian Ho
Headline: Difficult new Hulu documentary follows a 26-year-old woman’s startling discovery that she’s related to the notorious, murderous cult leader
Content: Charles Manson in 1981.Photograph: APView image in fullscreenCharles Manson in 1981.Photograph: APDocumentary films‘I come from a murderer’: the woman who found out Charles Manson was her grandfatherDifficult new Hulu documentary follows a 26-year-old woman’s startling discovery that she’s related to the notorious, murderous cult leaderVivian HoWed 22 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 05.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleSophia Maddox spent her life hearing from her father about how he had never met his father. He would tuck her into bed as a child, she said, and tell her about how much he wanted to know him.Four years ago, following a match on a genealogy website, they finally learned the shocking truth: her father’s father – her grandfather – wasCharles Manson, the notorious cult leader and criminal.“When my dad found out, he couldn’t bring himself to tell me for two months because it was so dark,” Maddox told the Guardian.‘A certain kind of chaos’: Errol Morris unpacks Charles Manson theoriesRead moreThis discovery is the subject of Maddox’sdocumentary, My Grandfather Charles Manson, which premieres onHulu. Throughout the documentary, Maddox works through complex feelings around being related to one of the most infamous criminals in history. “I come from a murderer,” she says in the first few seconds of the film. “Who the fuck would accept me?”But what begins as almost a philosophical exercise on the origins of evil, with Maddox seeking to understand her grandfather through interviews with members of the Manson Family cult, evolves quickly into a practice of unpacking generational trauma and making peace with a legacy inherited from flawed figures.It’s a highly personal process that feels akin to sitting in on a therapy session, which Maddox is filmed doing several times throughout the film. But the decision to document this for an audience came almost instinctually, Maddox said.“I grew up kind of lonely and when you grow up pretty isolated and lonely, you want to be heard and seen. I think it nurtures you to want to tell people and shout from the rooftops who you are and what your story is,” Maddox said in an interview. “That’s just how I was built and raised, to be a storyteller, and maybe hopefully this allows someone else to feel a little less lonely.”Maddox, 26, is young enough that she was able to grow up away from the shadow that Manson – and the bizarre trial of the Manson Family members – has cast over the collective psyche of American culture. She only learned of his crimes when she was a teenager and Googled “Helter Skelter” by chance after listening to the Beatles’ White Album, but she didn’t truly comprehend the impact of his crimes until a few years later, when she watched Quentin Tarantino’s bloody blockbuster,Once Upon a Time … in Hollywood.As the svengali-like leader of the Manson Family cult, in August 1969 Manson had instructed his followers of mostly disaffected young women to enter the home that the actor Sharon Tate, who at the time was eight and a half months pregnant, shared with her husband, Roman Polanski (Polanski had been shooting a movie in Europe). There, they brutally stabbed and killed Tate, alongside the Hollywood hairdresser Jay Sebring, heiress Abigail Folger and family friend Wojciech Frykowski. The followers also shot and killed a fifth person, houseguest Steven Parent. The next night, the Manson Family broke into the home of the supermarket executive Leno LaBianca and his wife, Rosemary, and stabbed them to death as well.Manson and three female followers, Susan Atkins, Patricia Krenwinkel and Leslie Van Houten, were convicted of murder and conspiracy to murder, with another follower, Charles “Tex” Watson, convicted later. Mansondied in prisonin 2017 at the age of 83.Throughout the documentary, Maddox and her co-director, Alexandra Orton, juxtapose happy home videos of her father, Daniel Arguelles, laughing and doting on her as a chubby-cheeked baby, with clips of Manson, raving and wild-eyed, a Nazi swastika carved into his forehead. The physical similarities between Manson and Arguelles are striking – they share the same sharp features and penetrating brown eyes – but throughout the film, Maddox grappled with other traits Manson shared with her father: childhood trauma, mental health issues, a certain unpredictability that could manifest in volatile ways.Arguelles, who Maddox described as a great dad, had been conceived during a one- or two-night stand and was raised by his mother and stepfather, who sent him to military reform school when he was young. “My grandma, God bless her, rest in peace – I knew her as a kid, and she kept the secret to her grave,” Maddox said. “But when she was a mother to my father, she didn’t treat him the way he deserved to be and I think it was because of who his dad was … the biggest trauma that I could think of with my dad was how no one validated his experiences in childhood, and so that’s why he felt the need to tell me about them when I was a kid.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThe GuideGet our weekly pop culture email, free in your inbox every FridayPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionView image in fullscreenSophia Maddox and Daniel Arguelles.Photograph: DisneyThe deeply empathetic Maddox also clearly struggled with doing right by her grandfather’s many victims while also humanizing someone who has been depicted for decades as a symbol of pure evil. She had to suss through a lot of sensationalism surrounding her grandfather to really get to the root of what she was searching for. “I’ve made a lot of true-crime documentaries and I think society has this fascination with making monsters that are superhuman that could be in a superhero movie as the villain,” Orton said. “But if you want to understand how to stop bad things from happening, you have to understand that every monstrous thing is done by a human being.‘We’re humanizing Manson’: docuseries adds depth to a notorious criminalRead more“My therapist has been great for me and in the movie, she says that inheriting sensational wounds is not how you break generational traumas,” Maddox added. “I just had to find out what I had to break.”The ultimate lesson Maddox learns on screen is that while evil cannot be passed on generation to generation, trauma can, and that was what her father passed on to her. Arguelles also had a very difficult time with the discovery that Manson was his father – as well as facing the similarities that they shared. “I never knew him, but I know him. I am him, but I’m not him,” he said in the documentary. A film-maker and creative himself, he supported his daughter’s quest to explore their background through film, but worried about how healthy it was for her to do so. Tensions came to a head in a hard-to-watch confrontation Maddox has with her father that she captured on film, and she hasn’t spoken to him since.“You know, for all the vulnerability and openness that I have, I really learned it from my dad because he was quite lonely too … I learned how to be vulnerable and open because my dad was to me growing up,” Maddox said.Maddox said she hoped to reconcile with her father one day and that he himself will take the time to watch the documentary – to understand that with the generational trauma, he also passed on goodness to her.“I hope he feels seen,” she said. “I hope he feels seen because he lived a lot of his life with people turning a blind eye. I also hope he’s proud of me, that I’ve dug deep into our generational trauma, our mental health generational trauma as well, and I hope he can see that if I am able to get past it, he can too.”My GrandfatherCharles Mansonis available now on Hulu in the US and Disney+ elsewhereExplore more on these topicsDocumentary filmsCharles MansonFactual TVDocumentaryHuluTelevisionUS crimefeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Go woke, get rich: why rightwing hate campaigns don’t affect the box office | The Odyssey | The Guardian
description: After a bigoted online backlash, led by Elon Musk, The Odyssey achieved a monster weekend – the latest example of a targeted takedown that didn’t work
Author: Jesse Hassenger
Headline: After a bigoted online backlash, led by Elon Musk, The Odyssey achieved a monster weekend – the latest example of a targeted takedown that didn’t work
Content: Charlize Theron, Zendaya, Anne Hathaway, Lupita Nyong'o, Samantha Morton and Mia Goth at the world premiere of The Odyssey.Photograph: Gareth Cattermole/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenCharlize Theron, Zendaya, Anne Hathaway, Lupita Nyong'o, Samantha Morton and Mia Goth at the world premiere of The Odyssey.Photograph: Gareth Cattermole/Getty ImagesThe OdysseyGo woke, get rich: why rightwing hate campaigns don’t affect the box officeAfter a bigoted online backlash, led by Elon Musk, The Odyssey achieved a monster weekend – the latest example of a targeted takedown that didn’t workThe Odyssey: jaw-dropping action, dark magic and dolphin cameos – discuss with spoilersJesse HassengerWed 22 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 06.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleElon Musk was a man on a mission. Yes, he’s supposedly intent on colonizing Mars, or the moon, or maybe, if you give him some more time to move the goalposts, just a really, really tall mountain, but this spring, a more pressing task presented itself, a sequel to his early-2026mission to post white supremacist rhetoric: take down Christopher Nolan’sThe Odyssey, which he decided was the latest of “woke Hollywood’s” attempts to spread racial animus (towards white people, of course) and win Oscars while doing so. Nolan, Musk explained to the trade publication Variety, was “pissing on Homer’s grave”, referring to the author of the film’s epic-poem source material.The Odyssey: jaw-dropping action, dark magic and dolphin cameos – discuss with spoilersRead moreAs the final phases of the movie’s promotional campaign ramped up, Musk posted dozens of times on his self-owned social media platform with grievances related to the casting of Lupita Nyong’o (a Black woman) and Elliot Page (a trans man) in the movie, often spreading false statements about this casting existing to satisfy new Oscar requirements. (As detailedhere, those new Oscar diversity requirements are extremely flexible, and often satisfied by studio-wide programs that have nothing to do with casting. The Odyssey did not need to hire Oscar winner Nyong’o to be eligible for awards.) Musk may be a divisive-at-best figure who large swaths of the population do not take seriously, but he commands enough attention and adulation from a vocal minority that plenty of online accounts were soon parroting his willful misunderstanding of Hollywood and The Odyssey. Many of them abruptly and hilariously zeroed in on the 2004 movie Troy – which the right flank of social media suddenly decided was a beloved masterpiece, rather than its own, far greater distortion of Homer’s epic poem The Iliad.This story has an ending even more familiar than a Musk-branded rocket crash: after weeks to months of rightwing outrage about the failure to select an exclusively Greek cast for a movie that also features a cyclops, sea monsters and a witch who turns men into pigs, The Odyssey has becomeone of the biggest hits of the summer, with over $260m in the bank already, well on its way to outgrossing the inflation-adjusted totals for Troy. (In raw dollars, it’s already made more in the US.) The rallying cries of “go woke, go broke” have been drowned out by demand to see Nolan’s fusion of old-fashioned Hollywood spectacle with less traditional forms of filming, editing and, yes, casting.It’s easy enough to point out that any doomsaying about the prospects ofThe Odysseywas stubbornly ignoring a litany of factors, including but not limited to arguably the most popular movie director currently working; a cast packed with big names across generational and racial lines; the fact that casting some non-white actors doesn’t exactly scream “rigidly enforced diversity” when three of the most prominent stars in the film are Matt Damon, Anne Hathaway and Tom Holland; and, in the week leading up to release, terrific reviews.It would also be simple, if self-aggrandizing, to point out that for general audiences, supposedly “woke” film critics appear to serve as a powerful backstop against anti-woke clicktivism. Hence The Odyssey (95% positive reviews on Rotten Tomatoes) being inured against controversy, whether real or imagined, while other rightwing enemies like Supergirl (53% positive), targeted for its star vaguely acknowledging the difficulties of being a woman, or Snow White (39% positive), targeted for its star being mixed-race and insufficiently deferential to the source material, having their defenses substantially weakened.There’s probably some truth to that, though it may be less to do with critics than an increasing preference for picking winners. The online buzz that seems to affect movies more than ever isn’t so much about wokeness as a movie’s perceived status. A movie like The Odyssey or Obsession overperforms, creating more excitement about its success, causing more overperformance; a movie like Supergirl can get tagged as a loser from when the earliest box office numbers come in (so, essentially, about 12 hours after release).Still, culture warriors love to claim victory wherever they can, so they will point to movies like Supergirl or Snow White as proof that audiences don’t want women to be superheroes, princesses to be anything other than white, or whatever other generalization they can come up with. That gloating doesn’t always tell the full story: after Snow White’s opening weekend, for example, the Hollywood trade publication Deadline – not exactly a leftwing outpost – dived deeper into the movie’s box office.Turns out, compared with the average 63-37 breakdown between “blue”-voting counties and “red”-voting counties for family films’ ticket sales, Snow White, at a 60-40 split, actually saw slightly above-average performance in red areas. Its mediocre numbers didn’t appear particularly political. “There was no red theater blockade due to any perceived controversies with Snow White,” analyst Steve Buck told Deadline at the time.View image in fullscreenMargot Robbie in Barbie.Photograph: Courtesy Warner Bros. Pictures/APEven some of this group’s highest-profile attempts at toldja-so don’t really compute in the long run. Ten years ago, the middling numbers for an all-female reboot of Ghostbusters led to no sequels, rightwing culture warriors declaring victory, and a return to the drawing board for a more reverent legacy sequel, 2021’s Ghostbusters: Afterlife. The result? A princely addition of $1.1m at the domestic box office (so essentially, flat attendance; possibly a little less, given five years’ worth of inflation) and a slight downturn internationally, for a small decrease. A 2024 follow-up performed near identically. Maybe those near-uniform numbers are just the baseline levels of interest in a new Ghostbusters movie, rather than a referendum on women in ghostbusting? After all, movies fromBarbietoSupermantoWickedtoSinners, all subject of various forms of rightwing grumbling about various forms of wokeness, have been big hits in recent years. This isn’t applied exclusively to rightwing-coded protests, either: Scream 7 drew angry online chatter from the left about a boycott because Melissa Barrera, star of the previous two films, was fired for speaking out about the suffering of Palestine at the hands of Israel. This did not appear to move the real-world needle, as the movie quickly became the biggest-grossing Scream yet (and without the benefit of good reviews to bolster it further).Sex, beds and grisly executions: what Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey changed, invented – and left outRead moreOf course, these cases are all still just individual movies. But broader studies also suggest that audiences aren’t especially put off by diverse casts – and may, in fact, be drawn in by them. Earlier this year, UCLA’s annualHollywood diversity reportfound that movies with casts between 40 and 50% Black, Indigenous and people of color (the right wing’s dreaded Bipoc) had the highest median domestic and international box office, echoing the fact that in the United States, about 45% of the population is Bipoc. This held true for theprevious year’s report, too. Basically, it’s hard to find much positive evidence of woke-specific avoidance of any movie. It just isn’t something that most normal people seem to think much about.None of this will prevent the next batch of half-manufactured, half-deluded rightwing outrage. We should be better at ignoring it by now, but given its marginal origins, people like Musk are well-positioned to amplify it into our feeds no matter how few people it represents. Meanwhile, self-styled tell-it-like-it-is doofuses like YouTuber the Critical Drinker will go back to pretending they just review movies and analyze culture, rather than trying to froth up outrage over an Oscar-winning Black actor playing a character who is beautiful. The scene will look more like Circe (Samantha Morton) massaging Odysseus’s men back into humans after transforming them into snorting hogs: “Back to your disguises,” she says drily of their human form. But maybe after the blockbuster success of this “woke” version of The Odyssey, those disguises will be a little harder to maintain.Explore more on these topicsThe OdysseyfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Do you suffer from organ failure? Here’s why you should give this mightiest of instruments a hearing  | Classical music | The Guardian
description: A Sunday morning Prom showcases the magnificent RAH instrument, but the organ’s not just for Bach – check out pioneering musicians from Jonny Greenwood to Kali Malone. Plus, Igor Levit is cancelled – and at the top of the German charts
Author: Tom Service
Headline: A Sunday morning Prom showcases the magnificent RAH instrument, but the organ’s not just for Bach – check out pioneering musicians from Jonny Greenwood to Kali Malone. Plus, Igor Levit is cancelled – and at the top of the German charts
Content: Anna Lapwood at the organ for her Royal Albert Hall debut in 2025.Photograph: Andy ParadiseView image in fullscreenAnna Lapwood at the organ for her Royal Albert Hall debut in 2025.Photograph: Andy ParadiseTom Service on musicClassical musicDo you suffer from organ failure? Here’s why you should give this mightiest of instruments a hearingTom ServiceA Sunday morning Prom showcases the magnificent RAH instrument, but the organ’s not just for Bach – check out pioneering musicians from Jonny Greenwood to Kali Malone. Plus, Igor Levit is cancelled – and at the top of the German chartsWed 22 Jul 2026 07.58 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 08.01 EDTShareIt has9,999 pipes, it’s 155 years old and 21m high. You can see it, but not always hear it, at every single London prom. I’m talking of course about theWillis organ in the Royal Albert Hall, which takes centre stage this weekend onSunday morning’s recital by French organist Olivier Latry.With his mostly Bach programme, Latry’s recital represents the core of the organ’s repertoire, music that has been heard in cathedrals and on concert hall organs for centuries. It will be magnificent in the building-shaking flesh on Sunday morning, but my assurances might not be enough to convince non-organ fans that they should forgo a lazy Sunday morning and head to South Kensington.There are many organ-sceptics among us – and there’s no judgment here! The world of organists can feel like a cloistered domain of inaccessible lofts, mysterious machinery and liturgical codification that resists innovation and audience connection. That’s changing for this generation, thanks to such players as Latry and to the globe-trotting advocacy ofAnna Lapwood, and a generation of experimental musicians (of which more later) who are using the pipe organ as their medium of discovery and wonder.View image in fullscreenOlivier Latry at the Great Organ of Notre-Dame Cathedral in Paris, photographed before the fire and restoration.Photograph: Luc Castel/Getty ImagesThe organ is a miraculous instrument with a millennia-long history – there werewater-organs in ancient Greece, an ancestor of such behemoths as the seven-storey high and 28,762 pipes of the instrument in Wanamaker’s department store in Philadelphia –the world’s largest working organ.Pipe organs can be ear-shatteringly loud but they are also capable of the greatest sonic variety. Buildings like theRoyal Albert Hallwere designed around their organs: the architecture was built to be their resonating chambers. Organs can sustain pitches far beyond the limits of human breaths or bowing arms; they can mimic and synthesise the sounds of the musical natural world, from abyssal rumbles that feel like glaciers calving, producing frequencies that are so low they are felt as vibration rather than heard, to the heights of birdsong and glittering sonic filigree.double quotation markBuildings like the Royal Albert Hall were designed around their organs: the architecture was built to be their resonating chambersIn Halberstadt in Germany,John Cage’s Organ²/ASLSP(As Slow As Possible) is still near the beginning of a performance that’s planned to last 639 years: it began in 2001 and is scheduled to finish in 2640,with another note change coming upon 5 August this year.Jonny Greenwood’s X Years of Reverb (where X = the number of years that an organ or performance space has been extant) is a mere eight hours, made for two organists at a single instrument, a piece written, he says, “to agitate and reanimate all the music, voices and sounds that have ever filled the air/soaked into the walls of the space where the music is played. To coax them out of the fabric of the building, so they can be distilled and concentrated into just eight hours”. James McVinnie and Eliza McCarthy next play Greenwood’s piece at Manchester Cathedral in January, celebrating that instrument’s 605 years of reverb.And there are many composer-organists today who are pushing the experimental bounds of the instrument.Kali Malone’s obsession with it began when she was a pipe-organ tuner. The American musician has made albums that reclaim older tunings and still more ancient conceptions of cycles of musical time and turn them into breathtaking immersions of musical being.Kali has been inspired byEllen Arkbro, another Sweden-based pioneer who uses the organ as a portal to explore arcane and elemental musical regions; whileAnna von Hausswolffhas made the organ the foundation of a musical language that stretches from avant-pop to experimentalism.One more example of today’s pipe-organ pioneers for you: Claire M Singer, whose albums and compositions, which are often titled after Scottish landscapes, places, and histories, areorgan-scapes of slow drones and radiant horizons. Singer makes musical time move as an ecstatic, infinite moment, releasing “all the music and sounds that have ever filled the air”, as Greenwood puts it – but not only, you feel, in the buildings that accommodate these instruments, but across entire mountain ranges and cosmoses.The organ can take you to wondrous musical places. Give it a try: start with Latry as your gateway drug. He’ll be delighted.In Germany, the pianist Igor Levit has teamed up with rapper Danger Dan on anti-fascist singleKeine Angst (“No Fear”). Their scheduled performance onZDF’s satirical news TV show Die Anstalt was cancelled at the last minute earlier this week. The broadcast instead featured aminute-long shot of an empty piano and mic stand[from 47 minutes in] at the point where the song should have been performed.The hosts of Die Anstalt were just as sidelined by the decision as Danger Dan and Igor Levit themselves, andthey appear to explainhow the broadcast silence wasn’t a “technical problem, but a political one”. The German public broadcaster says that Keine Angst’s lyrics went against programme guidelines; and the hosts duly explain the arguments, but “we are in the ZDF, but against the ZDF”, they say; they tried to have Levit and Danger Dan reinstated, but lost; and they go on to discuss – and to read out – the controversial lyrics, putting them in the context of other protest songs that are not censored, and explaining that the plan had been to present the performance and then discuss its urgent provocations with Levit and Danger Dan afterwards.The words ofKeine Angst(approximateEnglish translation) are, in part, a practical list of instructions about how to organise anti-fascist protests and – most controversially for the broadcaster – the song ends by expressing sympathy for left-wing activists convicted for violent actions against the far right. Levit and Danger Dan organised their own concert and livestream last night,Die Keine Angstalt, and the single has racked up a million views and counting on YouTube and Spotify.Levit’s collaboration with Danger Dan is just the latest example of his activism and political and musical thinking, in which he refuses to separate music – from Beethoven, Shostakovich and Frederic Rzewski (his variations on the Chilean protest song,The People United Will Never Be Defeated) from its political contexts, and the role it has always played in movements for social change. InGermany, the debate about censorship, and about how culturally and politically to combat the rise of the right-wing, goes on.This week Tom has been listening to: Nicholas Collon’s debut album withthe Finnish Radio Symphony Orchestraas their chief conductor. It came out in 2022, and it starts with Sibelius’s seventh symphony. That’s as bold a statement as you could make, offering a new interpretation of one of the Finn’s finest pieces. Collon doesn’t do it by deliberate iconoclasm: he finds his own depths in the soundworld: the string polyphony near the start of the symphony, like Sibelius rewriting Palestrina, has a quality of time-suspending strangeness that’s breathtaking.Explore more on these topicsClassical musicTom Service on musicProms 2026Royal Albert HallJonny GreenwoodExperimental musiccommentShareReuse this content

title: The rise of the ‘hot literati’: how reading became sexy | Books | The Guardian
description: Celebrities are hosting literary clubs and designers are launching products emblazoned with the classics – but does reading’s trendy turn have a darker side?
Author: Michaela Makusha
Headline: Celebrities are hosting literary clubs and designers are launching products emblazoned with the classics – but does reading’s trendy turn have a darker side?
Content: Model Kaia Gerber, who runs the Library Science book club.Photograph: Edward Berthelot/GC ImagesView image in fullscreenModel Kaia Gerber, who runs the Library Science book club.Photograph: Edward Berthelot/GC ImagesBooksThe rise of the ‘hot literati’: how reading became sexyCelebrities are hosting literary clubs and designers are launching products emblazoned with the classics – but does reading’s trendy turn have a darker side?Michaela MakushaWed 22 Jul 2026 07.02 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 08.32 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFromcelebrity book clubsto the revival of theedgy literary party, being a book lover is in. Dior has launched new versions of their Book Tote emblazoned with the covers of classics such asUlyssesandDracula. Earlier this year, Coachcollaboratedwith Penguin to produce miniature literary bag charms – fully readable micro-versions of books, from I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings to Sense and Sensibility. Miu Miuhosts a Literary Club; Chanel itsRendez-vous littéraire.View image in fullscreenBeing bookish is now sexy, we’re told.i-D magazinerecently ran a piece on the Whitney Review – a literary journal based in New York – proclaiming its team part of the “hot literati reshaping criticism”. The founder describes the Review as “sexy, surprising, stubborn”, and its contributors “curious and beautiful” (corroborated by an accompanying shoot in which the Whitney team look more like a bunch of models than anything else). But the piece raised eyebrows in the comments section of i-D’sInstagram page: “the definition of vacuous and vapid”, writes one user; “No room for ugly literati, only HOT”, says another. Should we be sceptical about reading’s voguish turn?Writer and content creator Sihaam Naik believes reading has become “hot” because of the economic pressures faced by young people today: “Gen Z is a generation that will probably never be able to own houses, and so we’re more aligned with investing in taste. So you buy books, art books and niche ephemera to set yourself apart from peers or signal your taste.”Celebrity book clubsmay also be driving the trend, says Naik: the likes of Dua Lipa, Kaia Gerber and Reese Witherspoon share books by “writers who need their platform … It works so well, but it’s just unfortunate as a sign of the times”, reinforcing the idea that “something is only worth sharing online if you are conventionally attractive.” Focusing on how someone looks when reading as opposed to how they engage critically with the text inadvertently creates a new, exclusionary barrier around literary culture – you must be hot to take part.The trend no doubt has its origins in BookTok and Bookstagram, on which creators share aesthetically pleasing reading spaces, shelves full of beautiful books and special editions.
