Book creator Sol Noya (@booksconleche on Instagram) thinks that the trend is a good thing, “a welcome change from past stereotypes of readers being dull or odd, particularly given that there are still very significant percentages of the population that barely read for leisure at all.” According toYouGov pollingconducted last January, 40% of Britons had not read or listened to a book in the prior year.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toBookmarksDiscover new books and learn more about your favourite authors with our expert reviews, interviews and news stories. Literary delights delivered direct to youPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionView image in fullscreenDua Lipa speaks onstage for a book club event at The Chelsea Hotel, New York City, in December.Photograph: Arturo Holmes/Getty Images“Reading has a positive impact on critical thinking and empathy, which are two traits society needs desperately. So in that sense, reading being ‘in’ is something I’m in favour of,” she continues. However, she says the aestheticisation of reading focuses far too much on consumption; reading as a new form of “subtle wealth signalling”. “Having a huge book collection implies having the cash to spend on buying the books and space to store them.”Yet, in an age in which younger people are trying to find ways to disengage from the internet andgo analogue, there is a positive in having role models who encourage buying and reading books. Bookstagrammer Diana Clough (@literarydiana) tells the Guardian: “opting for analogue media over our phones is something we should wear with pride and feel sexy about – however genuine that might be.”“Visibly engaging with the written word stakes our claim in the joy of reading, reclaiming the narrative of it being a reclusive act. And it can very much be hot, as the popularity of romantasy attests to. If reading is trendy, then it will only encourage more readers, which is never a bad thing.”Naik, however, is a little more cynical: “I’m glad to see people treat bookworms and reading as worthy of being cool again, but is it a fleeting trend, and are we going to dunk on it next year? Who knows!”Explore more on these topicsBooksDua LipaDiorMiu MiuChanelReese WitherspoonfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: On Thin Ice by Charlie Walker review – a Siberian adventure goes off the rails  | Travel writing | The Guardian
description: Moments of beauty and horror punctuate this account of an ill-timed sub-zero camping trip
Author: Charlie Gilmour
Headline: Moments of beauty and horror punctuate this account of an ill-timed sub-zero camping trip
Content: Charlie Walker in Siberia.Photograph: Charlie WalkerView image in fullscreenCharlie Walker in Siberia.Photograph: Charlie WalkerBook of the dayTravel writingReviewOn Thin Ice by Charlie Walker review – a Siberian adventure goes off the railsMoments of beauty and horror punctuate this account of an ill-timed sub-zero camping tripCharlie GilmourWed 22 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn late February 2022, Charlie Walker set off on a hiking holiday in northern Siberia. In temperatures as low as -50C, he intended to trek a thousand miles north along the frozen surface of the river Lena, then out across the solid Laptev Sea to the port town of Tiksi, before finally flying home to London. “The plan,” he writes, “was simple in concept.” A sub-zero camping trip through a region populated by wolves and bears and scarred by centuries of historical trauma, in a country that was about to invade one of its neighbours: what could possibly go wrong?On Thin Ice is Walker’s third travelogue. Previous excursions have included a 43,000-mile bicycle ride across three continents, a 1,000-mile solo walk through the Gobi desert, and a river trip down the Congo in a leaky canoe. When not performing incredible feats of forward motion, according to his website, he gives talks to business leaders on subjects such as “risk taking” and “the line between courage and recklessness”. Reading his latest, it’s clear he’s familiar with the terrain on both sides of that line.Landing in the Siberian city of Yakutsk with his skis, sled, tent and a “comically huge sleeping bag” Walker is busy provisioning when the full-scale invasion of Ukraine begins. With the Foreign Office advising against travel toRussia, he briefly considers abandoning his plans. But, reasoning that “Yakutsk is comfortably closer to the Alaskan city of Anchorage than it is to Kyiv”, he presses on.“The journey would be different now,” he writes. “As one of the last Western tourists in Russia, I was presented with a unique opportunity … to record the thoughts and feelings of ordinary Russian people, neither soldiers nor politicians. Living far from the front lines, their various opinions would provide insight into the silent majority of 144 million citizens. This seemed worth the risk of remaining inside a police state where already-limited freedoms of expression could evaporate overnight.”Armed with a flare gun, a can of pepper spray and a dagger for protection from the wolves and bears, Walker spends weeks trudging along ice roads –zimniki– by day, shivering in his tent at night, and occasionally experiencing the extremes of Siberian hospitality. The lorry drivers he meets on thezimnikistop for photos, sharing coffee and conversation. One scolds him for pitching his tent on the road: many truckers fuel themselves with vodka, while some watch television as they drive. He’s treated to local delicacies: grilled horse ribs, reindeer shin pie, a sausage made from aged horse blood (“the puck of boiled blood escaped its horse-gut housing and smeared across the roof of my mouth, setting fast like some ungodly grouting”). Indigenous Sakha women place a wolf’s penis amulet around his neck and offer pancakes to Bayani, “spirit lord of the taiga”, to safeguard his passage.Many of the people he encounters are more than willing to talk about Ukraine. “Ukraine’s a corrupt country run by Nazis,” declares one young Sakha man. “Ukrainebelongsto Russia,” says another. Even in areas so barren trees struggle to grow, Kremlin propaganda is deeply rooted. “I heard that President Putin is saving the Ukrainian people and I’m happy to know they’re being helped,” a lone Eveni herder tells Walker over a meal of roasted reindeer spine. Almost everyone seems to believe some version of the same story: that Putin saved Russia from the chaos of the 90s, and whatever he does is for the best. One wonders –a million Russian casualties later– how that story has weathered.More than votive pancakes, more than daggers and flares, it soon becomes apparent that the survival tool Walker really should have packed was the business card of a competent local lawyer. It’s not bears who stalk him across the ice, but the FSB, who take an increasingly hostile interest in this inexplicable Englishman, especially as his intended destination, Tiksi, turns out to be a military base. He is accused of “committing journalism”; of being a spy; people he encounters are harassed, others rush to inform on him. He takes insane risks, recording his own “anti-Putin rants” on a Dictaphone – when anti-war journalists face 15-year sentences – and signing a confession without having it translated first. He ends up in a detention centre, where he spends an agonising month awaiting deportation. Reflecting on his choices, he remembers an old Russian saying: “You saw what you were buying, so eat up, even if you choke on it.”At the start of his journey, Walker had hoped he might see a rosier side of Siberia. There are moments of beauty – the aurora borealis, rare bursts of warmth from the locals – but almost everywhere else is horror and corruption. Growing sinkholes, decaying towns, roads packed with the skeletons of the prisonerswho died building them, a “blackened wasteland” the size of Belgium left by wildfires. Walker evokes it all with a masterly touch. Nobody, after reading this book, will be in a rush to book tickets to Siberia, least of all its author, who is now banned from the country.On Thin Ice: An Explorer’s Memoir of Siberia, Surveillance, and Survival by Charlie Walker is published by Duckworth (£16.99). To support the Guardian order your copy atguardianbookshop.com.Delivery charges may apply.Explore more on these topicsTravel writingBook of the dayRussiareviewsShareReuse this content

title: Venus, Vanishing by Rebecca Birrell review – art, desire and the rise of the Third Reich | Fiction | The Guardian
description: This portrait of a Jewish painter in bohemian Berlin is filled with insightful observations
Author: Gabrielle Schwarz
Headline: This portrait of a Jewish painter in bohemian Berlin is filled with insightful observations
Content: Rebecca Birrell is an art historian by training.Photograph: Sophie DavidsonView image in fullscreenRebecca Birrell is an art historian by training.Photograph: Sophie DavidsonFictionReviewVenus, Vanishing by Rebecca Birrell review – art, desire and the rise of the Third ReichThis portrait of a Jewish painter in bohemian Berlin is filled with insightful observationsGabrielle SchwarzWed 22 Jul 2026 04.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn the summer of 1937, two exhibitions sponsored by the Nazis opened in Munich. For the Degenerate Art exhibition, hundreds of confiscated works by banned modern artists – including Paul Klee, Wassily Kandinsky, Pablo Picasso – were gathered: a derisive demonstration of the supposedly anti-German spirit of contemporary culture. The show was also meant as a counterpoint to the firstGreat German Art Exhibition, essentially an art fair for state-sanctioned work, which ran annually until 1944. The official style of Aryan art was traditionalist neoclassicism; popular subjects included idealised female nudes, pastoral landscapes and heroic battle scenes. Hitler was among the main buyers. You probably haven’t heard of any of those artists.What if one of them were secretly a Jew, working under a pseudonym? Rebecca Birrell’s debut novel imagines this scenario, telling the story of a fictional Jewish artist who comes of age in Berlin in the last days of the Weimar republic. Hannah Sherman is working as a seamstress for the family business when she learns that she is about to be married off. She runs away, finding lodgings using the “desirable Christian” name of Schmidt, and begins to realise her artistic ambitions. A free-thinking New Woman, she also takes two lovers: Saul, a museum curator, and Charlotte, a dancer at a lesbian bar where Hannah likes to sketch.We all know where history is going. The worsening economic and political situation – which Hannah, our narrator, prefers to allude to only obliquely – engulfs what might otherwise have been a straightforward story of an artist’s progress. As the Great Depression sets in, Hannah turns to a former client for mending work. Elke Grese, a glamorous older woman, sees Hannah’s art and makes an offer of patronage so generous it would be mad to refuse. But as Hannah soon learns, there are strings attached. How far will she go to survive?double quotation markHannah is an arresting narrator when it comes to visual description – by contrast, her emotional landscape is not as vividly renderedBirrell is an art historian by training; in 2021 she publishedThis Dark Country, an idiosyncratic blend of group biography and cultural criticism about female artists working with still life in early 20th-century England. Her deep understanding of artists and their work is evident here. Both Hannah’s inner thoughts and her conversations with others are filled with imaginative and insightful observations, including but not limited to the old master paintings in Saul’s workplace (the Kaiser Friedrich, a real museum whose holdings were destroyed in a fire in 1945) and the postcard reproductions of famous works that she trades back and forth with family, friends and lovers.While painting her mother, for instance, Hannah is prompted to reflect on Rembrandt’s series of portraits of an elderly woman believed to be his own mother: “In my favourite, his mother’s ageing skin resembles wax, the wrinkles etched into her flesh echoed in the detail of her embroidered cloak, life weighing so heavily upon her she can hardly keep her eyes open.”Hannah sees the rest of the world through the eyes of an artist, too. On a car journey: “From the windows of the taxi, the clouds were zinc white, paint fresh from its tube.” This makes her an arresting narrator when it comes to visual description – although perhaps a touch too ready with similes and metaphors: are leaves really “like small hands in the breeze”?By contrast, her emotional landscape is not as vividly rendered. Without giving away too much of the plot – there are some good twisty turns – it often isn’t clear why Hannah gets involved with certain other characters, who seem more like sketches than people. When talking about feelings, she tends to resort to abstract nouns: “fear stirred in my chest”, “shock gave way to shame” and so on. You get the sense that she doesn’t understand her own motivations. “I didn’t interrogate why I wanted a role,” she admits at one point, when describing her choice to insert herself as a background figure in a painting of someone else. This is not unrealistic: she is, after all, a painter, not a novelist. But it can make for frustrating reading.Towards the end of the book, Birrell offers a glimpse of her own narratorial range with an abrupt shift in perspective, and for the first time enters the mind of another character. Elke – until now a rather distant and mysterious figure – is sitting in a car on a street in Munich, about to pay a visit to the Great German Art Exhibition of 1942. It’s a welcome moment of strangeness closer to the quirky style of Birrell’s nonfiction: a reminder that literature, like art, is at its best when embracing its fullest capacity for invention.Venus, Vanishing by Rebecca Birrell is published by Picador (£16.99). To support the Guardian, order your copy atguardianbookshop.comExplore more on these topicsFictionreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Red Kiss – guiding vampires through a blood-soaked cold war Berlin | Games | The Guardian
description: Inspired by interactive theatre and fuelled by the motion capture tech used by VTubers, the vampire spies in Red Kiss have personality in spades
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Inspired by interactive theatre and fuelled by the motion capture tech used by VTubers, the vampire spies in Red Kiss have personality in spades
Content: The beating (undead) heart… Red Kiss.Photograph: Fellow TravellerView image in fullscreenThe beating (undead) heart… Red Kiss.Photograph: Fellow TravellerSummer games previewsGamesRed Kiss – guiding vampires through a blood-soaked cold war BerlinInspired by interactive theatre and fuelled by the motion capture tech used by VTubers, the vampire spies in Red Kiss have personality in spadesDiego ArgüelloWed 22 Jul 2026 09.20 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google‘You look at the news and you’re like, ‘Another pointless war, another pointless bloodshed, another pointless genocide.’ And that can make you a bit hopeless.”Roy van der Schilden, narrative designer and business director at Wispfire, speaks of a very human feeling. But the protagonist of its upcoming game Red Kiss can relate – despite being a vampire.You play as an (initially human) inhabitant of cold war Berlin who has tried to no avail to endure the conflict outside the window: through partying, journalling, activism … Still, you feel empty. When offered a new life of power and purpose, you don’t need much convincing to offer up your exposed neck.“I think a lot of people who create vampire games feel the anguish and the melancholy of the genre,” says Van der Schilden. “Like, how to deal with your feeling of justice in an unjust world.”View image in fullscreenThe best fangs for the job … Red Kiss.Photograph: Fellow TravellerIn this tactical narrative RPG, you plunge into a fictional 80s Berlin by dispatching vampire agents to its fractured streets. They’re your eyes and ears, looking for clues, building reputation with city factions, and of course quenching their bloodthirst. As in last year’s superhero caper Dispatch (from AdHoc Studio), you’ll come across opportunities around the map, each with its own requirements, making use of a skillcheck system tied to six character skills. It’s up to you to pick the best fangs for the job, all while maintaining their energy, money and blood supply.The vampires under your command are the beating (undead) heart of the experience. You progressively unveil their backstories by undertaking personal missions, during which you learn about their individual pasts and struggles. It’s in these moments that you begin to enact change and share some of your newfound purpose with others as guidance, deciding how their stories are ultimately resolved.Wispfire emphasises the importance of the cast by infusing hand-drawn portraits with voice acting and motion-capture (mocap) animation. Notably, for the latter the team is using Live2D, a relatively inexpensive software tool commonly used by VTubers to animate their virtual avatars during a livestream.A good iPhone was enough to start experimenting, but the team had to develop tools to adapt the software to suit their needs, and the tech isn’t without limitations. “With a mocap character for 3D, you usually can move your entire head,” says art director and animator Aïda de Ridder. “There’s something stuck to your face and it moves with you, right? We have a static phone, so we have to make their performance more centred. There is a limit to the range of movements.”In order to make models feel “animated”, the team found that a performer needs to exaggerate their facial features to convey the desired result. But Wispfire is well acquainted with this, thanks to a shared background in interactive theatre where, as Van der Schilden says, “It’s harder to see for people in the back if you do a muted performance.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toPushing ButtonsKeza MacDonald's weekly look at the world of gamingPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionVampires shine the most when they have strong personalities and distinct looks. In Red Kiss, many elements intersect with one another to give characters power and purpose. And this is all being funnelled through a queer and dark lens – like the best vampire stories are.“We like queer media, “says Van der Schilden. “But we, as a team, always felt that a lot of the media was very pink rainbows, happy-go-lucky. We wanted to make a really dark, queer, diverse game. And I think Red Kiss is that.”Red Kiss is currently in development forPC, with a release date to be announced.Explore more on these topicsGamesSummer games previewsPCfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: BBCPhil/Storgårds / Park & Jupiter Ensemble reviews – stylish Strauss and lyrical Sibelius … and a lutenist’s singalong | Classical music | The Guardian
description: Sueye Park’s poetic, enthralling Proms debut in Sibelius’s violin concerto was followed by an effervescent late-night concert from Thomas Dunford’s Jupiter Ensemble with Laurence Kilsby
Author: Clive Paget
Headline: Sueye Park’s poetic, enthralling Proms debut in Sibelius’s violin concerto was followed by an effervescent late-night concert from Thomas Dunford’s Jupiter Ensemble with Laurence Kilsby
Content: Attention-grabbing … soloist Sueye Park at the BBC Proms.Photograph: BBC/Chris ChristodoulouView image in fullscreenAttention-grabbing … soloist Sueye Park at the BBC Proms.Photograph: BBC/Chris ChristodoulouClassical musicReviewBBCPhil/Storgårds / Park & Jupiter Ensemble reviews – stylish Strauss and lyrical Sibelius … and a lutenist’s singalongRoyal Albert Hall, LondonSueye Park’s poetic, enthralling Proms debut in Sibelius’s violin concerto was followed by an effervescent late-night concert from Thomas Dunford’s Jupiter Ensemble with Laurence KilsbyClive PagetWed 22 Jul 2026 07.01 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 08.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAlate-night Prom can sometimes serve as a musical Rennie, a soothing digestif after 90 minutes of heavyweight orchestral fare. Fortunately, none was needed here. In the early evening programme John Storgårds kept the BBC Philharmonic sufficiently light on its feet, while Sueye Park made the most poetic of Proms debuts in Sibelius’s violin concerto, a cornerstone work she owned from start to finish.If the opening wash of strings was barely audible over the whirr of theRoyal Albert Hallair-con, it wasn’t long before the South Korean violinist grabbed our attention, her lean, silvery tone penetrating and intensely lyrical. In fact, Storgårds seemed to worry unnecessarily, keeping the orchestral volume on a low simmer for much of the work’s span. Park, an enthralling musical raconteur, imbued the first movement with a forthright romantic sweep before conjuring the kind of laser-focused, ethereal fireworks that set the finale dancing.View image in fullscreenDramatically assured: John Storgårds conducts the BBC Philharmonic OrchestraPhotograph: BBC/ Chris ChristodoulouBetsy Jolas, who turns 100 in two weeks’ time, composed Tales of a Summer Sea in 1977 to overcome a childhood fear of the ocean. Louring gong and timpani accordingly threatened a storm as woozy strings and woodwind roiled and toiled, their textures flecked with tom-toms, vibraphone and celesta. At 15 minutes, it rather took its time, but with seven busy percussionists it was rarely short of incident.Richard Strauss’s Also Sprach Zarathustra fits the Albert Hall perfectly and Storgårds clearly knew it. The mighty rumble of subterranean organ turbocharged those over-familiar opening fanfares; burnished strings tugged at the heart in Of the Great Longing. Placing the bell way up in the gallery was a neat touch to ring in Nietzsche’s deep midnight. If The Dance Song lacked the last degree of Viennese schmaltz, it failed to diminish what was a stylish and dramatically assured performance.View image in fullscreenUndeniably infectious: the Jupiter Ensemble with Thomas Dunford (second from right) and tenor Laurence Kilsby.Photograph: Chris ChristodoulouSo, what about that after-hours palate cleanser? Founded by lutenist Thomas Dunford,Jupiter Ensemblemade a singularly idiosyncratic Proms debut alongside British tenorLaurence Kilsby. The programme comprised Dowland to mark 400 years since his death, and Purcell, a composer who is more than welcome anytime.Kilsby’s debonair vocals cut a dash in a slightly breathless Come Again, Sweet Love and were at their finest paired with the ever-sympathetic Dunford in moving accounts of Dowland’s Flow, My Tears and Sorrow, Stay. Jupiter, meanwhile, kicked up their heels in The King of Denmark’s Galliard, while Can She Excuse My Wrongs was deftly juxtaposed with its original for lute, The Earl of Essex Galliard. The effervescent performances may have strayed from early music orthodoxy, but the spirit was undeniably infectious. You’ll have tolisten to the broadcastif you want to know how Dunford cajoled us all into singing Hey Jude.Available on BBC Soundsuntil 12 October. The Promscontinue until 12 September.Explore more on these topicsClassical musicProms 2026BBC Philharmonic OrchestraRoyal Albert HallreviewsShareReuse this content

title: The Cherry Orchard review – RSC’s starry cast brings disappointing lack of sparkle | Royal Shakespeare Company | The Guardian
description:
Swan Theatre, Stratford-Upon-Avon
Tamara Harvey’s production of Chekhov’s final play can seem woolly in meaning and soft-edged tone mutes its force
Author: Arifa Akbar
Headline: Swan Theatre, Stratford-Upon-AvonTamara Harvey’s production of Chekhov’s final play can seem woolly in meaning and soft-edged tone mutes its force
Content: Helen Hunt plays Madame Ranyevskaya and Kenneth Branagh Lopakhin in The Cherry Orchard.Photograph: Johan PerssonView image in fullscreenHelen Hunt plays Madame Ranyevskaya and Kenneth Branagh Lopakhin in The Cherry Orchard.Photograph: Johan PerssonRoyal Shakespeare CompanyReviewThe Cherry Orchard review – RSC’s starry cast brings disappointing lack of sparkleSwan Theatre, Stratford-Upon-AvonTamara Harvey’s production of Chekhov’s final play can seem woolly in meaning and soft-edged tone mutes its forceArifa AkbarWed 22 Jul 2026 02.30 EDTFirst published on Tue 21 Jul 2026 19.19 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleLaura Wade’s reworking of Anton Chekhov’s final, plaintive play brings a dazzle of big names. Helen Hunt is the aristocratic matriarch Madame Ranyevskaya, Bill Pullman her brother, andKenneth Branagh(so soon after his lead role in the Royal Shakespeare’s production of The Tempest) their nemesis, Lopakhin, a shopkeeper’s son here who takes over the reins of the family estate and represents the rising forces against Russia’s old order of ossified elites.But for such a starry cast Tamara Harvey’s production has a disappointing lack of sparkle. You do not feel the story’s charge, its complications around love and its stinging losses. The tone is too soft-edged, almost endearing, and the cast such an ensemble that there is no magnetic centre to hold you.Wade’s new version, based on a translation by Helen Rappaport, seems less like a revisionist reimagining than an updating of language, comically benign and stranded in a pre-revolutionary Russia in which the two aristocratic siblings, Ranyevskaya and Gaev, speak in American accents, while Lopakhin and servant Yasha (Julian Moore-Cook) are Northern Irish. The point about the accents is unclear, and the production as a whole seems woolly in meaning.View image in fullscreenBill Pullman as Gaev.Photograph: Johan PerssonView image in fullscreenHelen Hunt, Guy Henry, Kenneth Branagh and Michael Elwyn.Photograph: Johan PerssonView image in fullscreenEsther Smith and Amber Gadd.Photograph: Johan PerssonThe work was regarded by Chekhov as a comedy with farcical elements and Harvey brings out the farce in places, while ancient manservant Firs (Michael Elwyn) is a highlight with his doddering comedy. This does not make up for the lack of emotional and dramatic impact, although the storyline around Ranyevskaya’s adopted daughter, Varya (Esther Smith), and her yearning for a marriage proposal from Lopakhin does develop some emotional dimensions.Like Branagh’s good-natured, sometimes joshing Prospero, his Lopakhin is amenable, lacking the sharp-pronged anger of Adeel Akhtar’s inBenedict Andrews’ astonishing version of the play at the Donmar Warehouse two years ago. When his class rage comes, after the auctioning of the estate, when he reveals that he is its owner, it is the most arresting moment of the night. But the drunken outburst melts away as abruptly after this scene, back to benign neutrality.‘Bored? You’re never good enough to get bored!’ Oscar-winner Helen Hunt on great roles, unruly audiences and her RSC debutRead moreHunt seems featureless and uncomplex as the entitled, passionate and grieving Ranyevskaya. She lacks personality in the early scenes and emanates generic concern in the later ones, when she feels the estate slipping away. Pullman, at least, brings charm and comic charisma, and you wish his role were not quite so minor.Anna Fleischle’s set design is full of faded grandeur, with chandelier and lace coverings across the furniture. There is a dash of expressionism in the bare branches twined around the room, which gesture at the cherry orchard beyond it. The sound design is subtle and effective, with birdsong and the grinding sound of trees being felled.Written in 1903, when the Russian aristocracy was on the cusp of crumbling, points about social discontent and a rising peasant class are made, often through the play’s eternal student and socialist, Trofimov (Alfred Enoch plays him as a likable prig rather than a straightforward firebrand). But they do not resonate in any significant way, either politically or dramatically.There is so much bustling on stage too and not enough languor. No one seems to feel any Chekhovian longueurs or ennui – not on that side of the stage anyway.Plays until 29 AugustExplore more on these topicsRoyal Shakespeare CompanyKenneth BranaghHelen HuntAnton ChekhovTheatreLaura WadereviewsShareReuse this content

title: My advice to Claudia Winkleman: it’s not you, it’s the giant sofa – try a different kind of chatshow | Fiona Sturges | The Guardian
description: The popular presenter is by no means the first to have come a cropper hosting a talkshow. But new formats provide new opportunities, says arts journalist Fiona Sturges
Author: Fiona Sturges
Headline: The popular presenter is by no means the first to have come a cropper hosting a talkshow. But new formats provide new opportunities, says arts journalist Fiona Sturges
Content: ‘Claudia Winkleman revealed that she will not be returning for a second season.’Photograph: BBC/So Television/PA MediaView image in fullscreen‘Claudia Winkleman revealed that she will not be returning for a second season.’Photograph: BBC/So Television/PA MediaOpinionClaudia WinklemanMy advice to Claudia Winkleman: it’s not you, it’s the giant sofa – try a different kind of chatshowFiona SturgesThe popular presenter is by no means the first to have come a cropper hosting a talkshow. But new formats provide new opportunitiesWed 22 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 04.05 EDTShareYou might think the TV chatshow is an easy format to get right. Hire a well-liked host with comic chops and some successful shows under their belt. Build a cheerful set with a big sofa in the middle. Book some A-list celebs plus the obligatory comedian, invite a live audience et voilà! A prime-time hit is surely born.Except that time and time again, it has proved not to be that simple. Just ask Claudia Winkleman,who revealed last weekthat she won’t be returning to do a second season of The Claudia Winkleman Show on the BBC. The series, which launched in March, filling the gap while The Graham Norton Show was on hiatus,was received with lukewarm reviewsand so-so ratings. The decision to call time on it is reported to be Winkleman’s – “I was just too nervous to enjoy it,” she admitted – though, given the cuts rippling through the corporation, she may have opted to jump before being pushed.Part of the problem is that Winkleman was never going to matchNorton’s impeccable hosting style, honed over nearly two decades. As well as approaching his A-list guests with warmth and cheeky irreverence, Norton is also a sharp conversational steward, adept at keeping everyone engaged and, thanks to a well-stocked bar, gently lubricated. It isn’t as easy as it looks.By contrast, stuck on a gloomy set and with the audience positioned weirdly close to the talent, Winkleman allowed conversations to drift and indulged in silly hyperbole – “That’s the best thing anybody’s ever said,”she yelped at Tom Allenafter a mildly funny gag about his fellow guests’ blathering. It may be that, following rigorous analysis of the first series, Winkleman would have found her rhythm in a second. But for a presenter stillriding high on the successof The Traitors, and who has shown herself to be sparky, spontaneous and funny fronting other shows, it wasn’t worth risking another critical drubbing.View image in fullscreenClaudia Winkleman hosts the first episode of her chatshow.Photograph: Matt Crossick/PAWinkleman is by no means the first presenter to come a cropper hosting a chatshow. In 2006, Davina McCall, then the boisterous host of Big Brother,was roundly pannedfor her self-titled chatshow which was dumped after one series. The same decade also brought the short-livedLily Allen and Friendsand Channel 4’s sub-parThe Charlotte Church Show, where both hosts’ lack of experience left them looking uncomfortable and out of their depth.The depressing common denominator in these cancelled shows is female hosts. Allen and Church were badly miscast but, like Winkleman, McCall seemed an ideal candidate. Yet, for reasons that are hard to fathom, the late-night chatshow has always been a male stronghold. Johnny Carson, Larry King, David Letterman, Jay Leno, Conan O’Brien, Jon Stewart and the Jimmys Fallon and Kimmel all ruled the chatshow roost in the US, while Michael Parkinson, Russell Harty, Terry Wogan, Jonathan Ross, Alan Carr and Graham Norton have done the same here. Comedians, it would appear, seem to land best with audiences. The only woman to have flourished in the format in the UK was under cover of a comic character: Mrs Merton, the creation of the late Caroline Aherne,brilliantly roasted her guestsin faux-naif style, asking Bernard Manning: “Who do you vote for now that Hitler’s dead?”View image in fullscreenCaroline Aherne with guest Patrick Duffy on The Mrs Merton Show.Photograph: ITV/REX/ShutterstockBut there is another more existential threat currently facing the chatshow that goes beyond a resistance to female hosts. Until recently, an A-lister with a new film/TV show/album to promote in the UK would make a beeline for either The Graham Norton Show or its ITV equivalent, The Jonathan Ross Show. But now there are scores of podcasts, most of them operating in video as well as audio, that offer not only easy exposure and a chance to plug their latest masterwork but longer and more in-depth conversations.Not all these podcasts are made equal – many are hosted by celebrities patently ill-suited to the job of listening and asking probing questions – but the public appetite for them remains undimmed. Once again, male hosts dominate, among them Joe Rogan, Steven Bartlett, Louis Theroux and Romesh Ranganathan, though women are getting more of a look-in. TakeBegin Again, the hit interview series about midlife reinvention launched in 2024 that has featured Fatboy Slim, Gisèle Pelicot, Richard E Grant and Gillian Anderson as guests. The host? That would be former chatshow kryptonite Davina McCall.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAll of which would suggest a Winkleman-fronted interview series is by no means dead in the water. Should she wish to have another crack at it, her best bet would be to dispense with the live audience and the giant sofa, and bypass traditional telly altogether.Fiona Sturges is an arts writer for the Guardian and other newspapersDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsClaudia WinklemanOpinioncommentShareReuse this content

title: A 12-year-old casualty of war recovers in hospital: Alessio Mamo’s best photograph | Art and design | The Guardian
description: ‘Manal suffered severe burns to her arms and face after a missile explosion in Iraq. Yet in many ways she seemed just like a regular young girl’
Author: Charlotte Jansen
Headline: ‘Manal suffered severe burns to her arms and face after a missile explosion in Iraq. Yet in many ways she seemed just like a regular young girl’
Content: ‘I feel a huge amount of anger seeing children suffer’ … Alessio Mamo’s Manal, War Portraits.Photograph: Alessio Mamo/ReduxView image in fullscreen‘I feel a huge amount of anger seeing children suffer’ … Alessio Mamo’s Manal, War Portraits.Photograph: Alessio Mamo/ReduxMy best shotArt and designA 12-year-old casualty of war recovers in hospital: Alessio Mamo’s best photograph‘Manal suffered severe burns to her arms and face after a missile explosion in Iraq. Yet in many ways she seemed just like a regular young girl’Interview byCharlotte JansenWed 22 Jul 2026 10.48 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWhen I started to take photographs about 18 years ago, I was more interested in the aesthetics. Then came a moment when I realised the power of the camera as a tool. On 3 October 2013 there was a huge shipwreck off the coast of Lampedusa. More than 250 migrants from Eritrea lost their lives. I was sent to cover the event, and it was during that assignment that I understood I was interested in telling stories about social injustice, about people’s lives post-conflict.At first I worked mostly on stories about refugees in Sicily, where I am from, but a few years later I had the opportunity to travel to the Middle East with a friend, the journalistMarta Bellingreri, who is an expert on the region. She was living in Jordan and I joined her there for six months . That is how our project,The Hospital of All the Wars, about the patients at the Médecins Sans Frontières Al-Mowasah hospital in Amman, began.At Al-Mowasah hospital they treat patients from across the region:Iraq, Gaza, Yemen, Syria, Lebanon. They agreed to let us spend several months there; we would visit three times a week. We knew we needed time to tell the stories of these people, to spend time with the patients, their families, the doctors and nurses. We got to know Manal - she and her mother already knew about our project and we decided to ask if she was happy for us to photograph her.double quotation markManal had many friends at the hospital, and loved telling storiesShe is a beautiful, amazing girl. She was 12 at the time. She has suffered severe burns to her arms and face after a missile explosion in Kirkuk in Iraq. She had extensive surgery and needs to wear the mask you see in this picture for around five hours a day, to protect her face from light. You can also see in the room around her the drawings she made, her balloon under her arm. Manal had many friends at the hospital, and loved telling stories, as well as taking part in the many organised activities for children there.Like many of the patients, she was undergoing long-term treatment and living in the hospital for a number of months. But in many ways she seemed to me like a regular young girl. We had a great connection with her and her mother, and the shoot was like a game with Manal. Her mother offered us Iraqi sweets and biscuits. The portrait was made quite spontaneously. It won an award at theWorld Press photography prize in 2018.I am very grateful to Manal and I am still in touch with her and her family – last year I went to visit her in Kirkuk, where she is living with her uncle. She is 19 now.This portrait affects me particularly powerfully as even today, several years after the work I did in the MSF hospital in Amman, children remain among the primary victims of conflicts.Today more than ever, having recently become a father, I feel this aspect of children’s suffering in conflict zones is increasingly urgent. This is why I will soon be travelling to Beirut with the Guardian to work on a project about children harmed by the ongoing war. A huge number have been wounded in recent months in Israeli attacks.Of course, this is a subject that is really difficult to photograph. I feel a huge amount of anger seeing children suffer. I try to channel what I see, and the anger I feel, into my work, while always trying to do my best in the hope that these stories can raise awareness among as many readers as possible.It’s important to keep telling these stories because the moment we stop, and allow them to go unheard, we will live in a world where nobody cares about the suffering of the less fortunate. And that is not a world I want my daughter to grow up in. My hope is that my work, along with that of many photojournalists covering similar issues, can plant small seeds and open small windows of awareness – seeds that might one day take root and build a critical mass.View image in fullscreenPhotograph: Oleg SolokhaAlessio Mamo’s CVBorn:Catania, Sicily, 1976High point:Winning two World Press Photo awards. The first in 2018 with this picture of little Manal.Top tip:Give yourself five years before giving up the job!See more at Alessio Mamo’swebsiteor onInstagramand forthe GuardianExplore more on these topicsArt and designMy best shotPhotographyRefugeesIraqfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: At greyhound life drawing, artists try to tackle complicated shapes | Drawing | The Guardian
description: With soulful eyes and ‘reverse chicken legs’, greyhounds are challenging to sketch. At a fundraising event in Melbourne, Josh Nicholas tries his hand
Author: Josh Nicholas
Headline: With soulful eyes and ‘reverse chicken legs’, greyhounds are challenging to sketch. At a fundraising event in Melbourne, Josh Nicholas tries his hand
Content: Fettuccine the whippet models at a greyhound life drawing class held by Greyt Greys and hosted by TKO Tattoos in Eltham, Victoria.Photograph: Charlie Kinross/The GuardianView image in fullscreenFettuccine the whippet models at a greyhound life drawing class held by Greyt Greys and hosted by TKO Tattoos in Eltham, Victoria.Photograph: Charlie Kinross/The GuardianDrawingAt greyhound life drawing, artists try to tackle complicated shapesWith soulful eyes and ‘reverse chicken legs’, greyhounds are challenging to sketch. At a fundraising event in Melbourne,Josh Nicholastries his handJosh NicholasWed 22 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 19.57 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleI’ve done life drawing before, but this time I am a bit worried. The woman across the room from me has done life drawing too, “but not with dogs”.The problem with drawing people from life is they tend to move. The same is true of greyhounds, but they also don’t understand when you ask them to hold a pose.As I enter TKO Tattoos, where greyhound life drawing classes are held once a month or so, I’m greeted by a white whippet wearing a fetching yellow, blue and black striped jumper. “That’s Fettuccine,” says TK, the parlour’s owner.We’re in Eltham, about 20km north of Melbourne’s centre, in a small industrial building set back from the road. The walls are lined with tattoo designs and other art, many of them stylised greyhounds. “I’ve always wanted my own little art studio,” TK says.View image in fullscreenHost TK with their whippet Fettuccine.Photograph: Charlie Kinross/The GuardianFinding I have no treats, Fettuccine loses interest and wanders towards the back of the studio to join the other star of the show. Nina, a smallish black greyhound, is lounging on a plush red cushion. She’s wearing a floral yellowcollar and a jumper that matches Fettuccine’s – even inside, the weather is brisk.Whippets and greyhounds look similar, with long, narrow heads and thin, muscular bodies. Whippets tend to be smaller than greyhounds. And, unlike greyhounds, they aren’t raced commercially in Australia, only by hobbyists.View image in fullscreenFettuccine the whippet strikes a pose.Photograph: Charlie Kinross/The GuardianTK’s mum rescued greyhounds, so they’ve lived with these dogs since childhood. Now they host classesto raise money forGreyt Greys, the rescue service through which they adopted Nina.Victoria has one of the largest greyhound racing industries in the world, with thousands of dogs needing rehabilitation and good homes each year. This life drawing fundraiser sold out in about two days.I take one of the seats oriented around Nina’s cushion as about 20 other artists file in – Fettuccine greeting every single person at the door.View image in fullscreenOne of Josh Nicholas’s sketches.Illustration: Josh Nicholas/The GuardianThere’s a wide mix, including some who have never taken a life drawing class. But there’s plenty of greyhound lovers.“There are six greyhound ‘faces’,” says Charlotte, sitting next to me. She has owned four greyhounds over the years; her current two dogs “have the same face”.It’s my first of many lessons about greyhounds over the next two hours – such as did you know some greyhounds have translucent legs? It’s a skin flap on the back, really. I try hard but can’t see it. Maybe it’s just the way Nina is sitting.View image in fullscreenGreyhound Nina sprawls on a plush red cushion.Photograph: Charlie Kinross/The GuardianI do some warm-up sketches of Fettuccine as he does the rounds, diligently investigating our bags for any undeclared treats. I keep drawing his face too narrow and mucking up the angle and curve of his hind legs.Someone hisses: “They’re just goofy-shaped animals!” That’s it! Have you ever noticed how weirdly shaped whippets are? Someone else mentions a “reverse chicken leg”. This may not be correct anatomically, but just about nails the problem.View image in fullscreenFettuccine does the rounds during the class, investigating participants’ bags for treats.Photograph: Charlie Kinross/The GuardianI switch over to Nina, who is facing me with her front paws off the end of the cushion. She has gorgeous, deep-brown eyes that are hard not to stare into. She’s five years old, says TK’s partner Leah, who has Nina’s leash clipped on to her belt. Nina “was intended to race”, but they don’t know whether she made it on to a track, she says.By my second attempt, Nina has shifted to face away from me. I draw the long sweep of her belly, all the way around her leg. Mostly from memory, as she keeps moving. Do greyhounds’ knees always point away from them when they lay down? Are those even knees? This leads to a heated group discussion.View image in fullscreen‘I draw the long sweep of her belly, all the way around her leg.’Photograph: Charlie Kinross/The Guardian“I keep drawing and thinking: this can’t be right! But no, that’s how she’s actually sitting,” says Emma, Charlotte’s partner. “I’ve just been ignoring the paws.”I used to think art required natural talent. Then I taught myself to draw | Josh NicholasRead moreSo have I – they’re a very complicated shape with lots of joints. TK saves us with cards they’ve drawn of greyhound body parts, showing how to use circles and other shapes to construct the joints. It definitely helps.If you’ve ever learned to draw a human head via theLoomis method, or break down the human body into a series ofstacked boxes, then you’ve seen this approach. The Loomis method starts a drawing of a head with a circle and uses that to work out the facial features. Similarly, you can think of a greyhound’s head as a circle with a cone attached. The joints in their lower legs and paws are a series of circles you slowly flesh out into a cylinder.View image in fullscreenNicholas’s subject is prone to moving.Illustration: Josh Nicholas/The GuardianBy my sixth sketch we’re winding down. We leave our drawings on our chairs and take a tour round the room. There’s a huge variety of sketches, including some incredible, quick renderings in pencil and charcoal.In the end, I’m not overjoyed with any of my drawings. But it’s not because of Nina and Fettuccine’s reverse chicken legs, or that they wouldn’t sit still. I just need practice. Perhaps a lot of it. And I’ll remember to bring treats for Fettuccine next time.View image in fullscreenJosh Nicholas, centre back, and the other life drawing participants show off their sketches.Photograph: Charlie Kinross/The GuardianTKO Tattoos’ nextgreyhound life drawing fundraiseris on 8 August at 10.30am at the Round Shader Club in Eltham, MelbourneExplore more on these topicsDrawingDogsPetsHobbiesAnimalsArtAustralian lifestylefeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Who is Jimothy? Seven things you need to know about the internet’s favourite raccoon | Life and style | The Guardian
description: From neighbourhood scavenger to baseball mascot and Daily Show guest, the Seattle animal has become social media’s newest cult hero
Author: Elle Hunt
Headline: From neighbourhood scavenger to baseball mascot and Daily Show guest, the Seattle animal has become social media’s newest cult hero
Content: ‘Jimothy runs this city’ … a Seattle raccoon goes viral.Composite: Guardian Design; Instagram/c1vrgr1View image in fullscreen‘Jimothy runs this city’ … a Seattle raccoon goes viral.Composite: Guardian Design; Instagram/c1vrgr1Celebrity crib sheetLife and styleWho is Jimothy? Seven things you need to know about the internet’s favourite raccoonFrom neighbourhood scavenger to baseball mascot and Daily Show guest, the Seattle animal has become social media’s newest cult heroElle HuntWed 22 Jul 2026 08.55 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 14.56 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GooglePygmyhippoMoo Dengis washed up and humiliated,having flunkedall three of herfootball World Cup predictions.Punch theJapanese macaqueorphaned in IkeaissoFebruary. And if you can even rememberGrumpy Cat, may sherest in peace.As of today the internet belongs to Jimothy: a raccoon with a striking silhouette who has gone from a minor celebrity inSeattleto social media main character overnight. Jimothy is suddenly all over Instagram, and if you hover over the critter for so much as a nanosecond, he will be all you will ever see on your feed.What is the deal with Jimothy, what’s the reason for his unique appearance – and what’s behind his rapid rise from contested local pest to celebrated internet pet?View image in fullscreenDoin’ my best … a Jimothy sticker.Photograph: Etsy/ ForceofNaturePressHe is a Seattle localJimothy is said to be a familiar sight in the Ballard neighbourhood of Seattle, instantly recognisable by his unusual short, round shape. Last week, Seattle resident Kiana Hallsaw the raccoonwhile on an evening walk with her husband. She posted a video of him scampering across her path on Instagram with her exclaiming in the voiceover: “What am I looking at?” Hall gave the creature a name, declaring that she and her husband had been “graced with the presence of Jimothy” for their 13th anniversary. “He just looked like a ‘Jimothy’ to me,” Halllater told Today. “I don’t have any other explanation.” The raccoon’s unique shape generated instant intrigue and conversation, leading Instagram to push the video and send it viral. According toKnow Your Meme, Hall’s video gained nearly 400,000 likes in six days. At the time of writing it has been viewed nearly 8m times.His short, round shape sets him apartJimothy is assumed to have a rare genetic condition called short spine syndrome, resulting from compressed vertebrae. This is not necessarily painful but can cause issues with mobility (though Jimothy at least appears very agile) and leads to a distinctive body shape. Of course, this is key to the instant online enthusiasm for Jimothy, who follows in a long line of animals to be made internet famous, somewhat uneasily, for their differences. The original petfluencer Grumpy Cat had feline dwarfism;Tuna the chihuahua mixan extreme overbite. Pet dogs with short spine syndrome have gone viralin the pasttoo.View image in fullscreen‘Jimothy has come to us at a time where everyone needs something to rally around’ … Ballard Avenue, Seattle.Photograph: David A Barnes/AlamyHe is a home-town hero …What may set Jimothy apart in the internet’s archive of furry favourites is the speed and scale of his ascent. Two weeks ago, he didn’t have a name, nor did he have a reputation beyond the bins of an area of Seattle. Now his viral fame has made him a home-town hero, with the Mariners last week encouraging fans in the stadium to “make noise for Jimothy”. A mural of the raccoon has already been painted in his neighbourhood depicting him hiding behind a bin at a pottery studio, while a painting of Jimothy raised $6,500 (£4,900) for a local food bank at auction. On Sunday, city council memberAlexis Mercedes Rinckwill issue an“official city proclamation” celebrating the “majestic” raccoon and declaring summer 2026 “Jimothy Summer”.… and also increasingly, absurdly, disproportionately famousOnline, Jimothy has a dedicatedfandom on Reddit, drawing tens of thousands of weekly visitors. He has been commemorated infanart, endlessmemes,gaming tributes(including his own8-bit game), acustom Lego buildand – unbelievably –at leasttwotattoos. On Monday, Jimothy was featured onThe Daily Show. The same day he was invoked from space, withNasa posting on Instagrama picture of Seattle taken from the International Space Station that highlighted the home of “the iconic raccoon”: “Jimothy runs this city.”There is merch!Naturally, the merchandising machine has leapt to cash in on Jimothy’s viral fame. The online marketplace Etsy already has more than 1,000 listings related to the raccoon, including stickers, clothing and tote bags. Many items champion Jimothy as a struggling iconoclast, with faux-inspirational mottoes such as “Stay weird”, “Doin’ my best” and “Protect at all cost”. “Hot Jimothy summer” is also catching on.View image in fullscreen‘Sudden global celebrity’ … Jimothy merch abounds.Photograph: Etsy/TheFaithfulFamiliarHe (the raccoon) apparently had a loving family lifeLittle is known about Jimothy’s life pre-fame, but last week another Seattle residentidentified asBen Trammellposted a video of whatappears to be baby Jimothy with his family. Trammell claimed Jimothy was born in a group of cedar trees in a neighbour’s back yard and he saw him “almost daily for months”. “He was so small and clumsy but his mother and siblings were always so patient and caring, keeping him from falling and sometimes just carrying him.”Do not approach himJimothy’s reported stable early life will no doubt be helping him to navigate his sudden global celebrity – as well as the crushing silence when the internet moves on. “Hot Jimothy summer” will inevitably be remembered for about as long as the “hot rat summer” last year – also, interestingly, born out of Seattle. (That same year, the city also tried to make “Humpy the salmon” happen.)Jimothy might be the city’s latest, biggest export, but his breakneck rise and commodification speaks loudest about the online economies of attention and audiences’ desperate desire for anything even the slightest bit positive to brighten their timelines. “Jimothy has come to us at a time where everyone needs something to rally around,”said the ownerof the Ballard pottery studio that had the mural painted.The official advice on Jimothy from the Washington Department of Fish and Wildlife, meanwhile,has been to“leave him alone”.Explore more on these topicsLife and styleCelebrity crib sheetAnimalsSeattlefeaturesShareReuse this content

title: The best camping mattresses and sleeping mats in the UK for every type of adventure, tested | Travel | The Guardian
description: Camping season has arrived, but which mattress is worth packing? Our expert rigorously tested 28 to find the best for durability and comfort
Author: Linda Geddes
Headline: Camping season has arrived, but which mattress is worth packing? Our expert rigorously tested 28 to find the best for durability and comfort
Content: View image in fullscreenThe FilterTravelReviewThe best camping mattresses and sleeping mats in the UK for every type of adventure, testedCamping season has arrived, but which mattress is worth packing? Our expert rigorously tested 28 to find the best for durability and comfortThe best camping chairsLinda GeddesWed 22 Jul 2026 07.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.Whether you love sleeping under the stars or only do so under duress, a decent camping mattress will at least help ensure you are well-rested and better equipped to handle the wider thrills and spills of outdoor living.The Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.The days when your only choice was between a foam roll mat or a leaky airbed and foot pump are long gone. From self-inflating mattresses to lightweight sleeping pads and insulated airbeds, the comfort and portability of sleeping mats have dramatically improved. But with so many types of camping mattresses available, costing from tens to hundreds of pounds, it can be difficult to know where to start.To save you the sleepless night of an ill-informed choice, I tested 28 and shortlisted the best camping mattresses for various scenarios.At a glanceBest camping mattress overall:Therm-a-Rest NeoLoft£212.49 at AmazonBest budget camping mattress:Decathlon Simond MT500 XL inflatable trekking mattress£49.99 at DecathlonBest double mattress:Big Agnes Rapide SL insulated tent floor pad£239.95 at WildboundsBest self-inflating mattress for luxury camping:Vango Shangri-La Lux 16 Grande£199 at AmazonBest sleeping mat for expeditions:Alpkit Radiant insulated inflatable camping mat£98.99 at AlpkitWhy you should trust meAs a keen camper and festival goer, not to mention the parent of two adventurous teens, I’ve done my fair share of backpacking and car-based camping trips, and had the bruised hips and backache to prove it. Particularly memorable was the night my self-inflating mat punctured while wild camping on Mont Lozère in the south of France, as well as the time we ignored the yellow weather warning at Port Eliot festival in Cornwall, and spent the night reinforcing our collapsing tent as the sleeping area gradually filled with water.I am also a science journalist and the author of abook on circadian rhythms and sleep, so I recognise the importance of a good night’s sleep for physical recovery, brain function and wellbeing.How I testedView image in fullscreenComfort was tested by placing several sharp stones underneath each mattress.Photograph: Linda Geddes/The GuardianI assessed each mattress on comfort, durability, packability, and how easy they were to inflate and deflate. Comfort was assessed by placing each mattress on a hard wooden floor on top of several sharp stones, lying on it inside a sleeping bag on my back and side, and rolling between positions for 20 minutes. As a wriggly side sleeper, I was particularly vigilant for any pressure points around my hips or shoulders, or mattresses that were overly narrow or slippery. Points were deducted if I could feel the stones, couldn’t comfortably stretch out, or for shiny surfaces that caused me to slither off the mattress.I assessed durability by lightly jumping barefoot on each mattress five times (against manufacturers’ instructions), and falling on to it from standing – as might happen if you were to stumble into yourtentin the middle of the night without a torch. I also gently stabbed and scratched at the surface of each mattress with a pointy stone, and sprayed the underside with water to see how easily it soaked through. Mattresses were left overnight with two 20kg boxes on them to check for any signs of deflation. Further points were deducted for mattresses that were excessively difficult to pack away after use or needed a large storage space.View image in fullscreenCould the mattresses stay inflated under 40kg of weight overnight?Photograph: Linda Geddes/The GuardianI’ve also included the R value, or thermal resistance, of each mattress. This measures how well it resists heat transfer from your body to the cold ground. A lower R value means less insulation, which is better in warmer weather, but less good for winter camping (see more below, in the What you need to know section).I tested 28 mattresses in total, and rated them for different camping scenarios, including family camping holidays, summer or winter backpacking expeditions, glamping, festivals and weekends away for two. I also enlisted my back-sleeping husband – a keen winter mountaineer – and comfort-seeking kids, aged 12 and 14, for their perspectives. Below are the 10 best camping mattresses from my experiments.Mattresses were either returned to the manufacturer or donated toKitsquad, a scheme that helps low-income people get out into the great outdoors, or thePeople’s Republic of Stokes Croft, which supports people sleeping rough in Bristol.The best camping mattresses and sleeping mats in 2026View image in fullscreen‘Should dramatically boost your chances of a good night’s sleep’: the Therm-a-Rest NeoLoft.Photograph: Linda Geddes/The GuardianBest camping mattress overall:Therm-a-Rest NeoLoftTherm-a-RestNeoLoftfrom£212.49£224.95 at Snow+Rock£212.49 at AmazonWhat we loveHits the sweet spot between comfort and easy packabilityWhat we don’t loveThe price and occasional crackle of static electricityView image in fullscreen£224.95 at Snow+Rock£212.49 at AmazonIf you crave the comfort of an airbed but need something compact and lightweight enough to carry in your backpack, Therm-a-Rest’s NeoLoft regular wide camping mat could be the answer. With a packed size not much larger than a 1-litre water bottle, chambers that rise to form subtle side rails when the pad is inflated, and internal insulation, this pad isn’t cheap, but it should dramatically boost your chances of a good night’s sleep.Why we love itThis mattress merges comfort with packability, making it a practical option for family camping holidays and backpacking alike. It feels soft and warm to lie on; it’s wide enough for back or side sleepers; and the side rails gently cradle you while you sleep.We tested the regular wide single mat, which was fine for sleepers up to 5ft 10in, but taller people may prefer thelarge version, which is 198cm long (6ft 5in). We’ve taken this mattress on several camping trips, and it was so comfortable that my children fought over who got to sleep on it. Lumpy ground is imperceptible through its 11.7cm loft, even when sleeping on your side, and while softly springy, this mattress thankfully lacks the trampoline-like bounce of a conventional airbed.It’s a shame that …while the surface of the mattress is super-soft, it does generate some static if you roll around too much.Weight:850gDimensions (packed):29 x 15cmDimensions (inflated):185 x 66 x 12cm (LWH)Rvalue:4.7Self-inflating:noPump included:yes, pump sack (a bag that fills with air and connects to the mattress valve to inflate it)Therm-a-RestNeoLoftfrom£212.49£224.95 at Snow+Rock£212.49 at AmazonWhat we loveHits the sweet spot between comfort and easy packabilityWhat we don’t loveThe price and occasional crackle of static electricityBest budget camping mattress:Decathlon Simond MT500 XL inflatable trekking mattressDecathlonSimond MT500 XL inflatable trekking mattressfrom£49.99£49.99 at Decathlon£50 at ArgosWhat we loveComfortable and versatile mattress for summer campingWhat we don’t loveYou’ll get greater comfort if you spend a bit moreView image in fullscreenPhotograph: Linda Geddes/The Guardian£49.99 at Decathlon£50 at ArgosDecathlon is a reliable go-to for affordable outdoor gear, and its own-brand Simond sleeping pad lives up to expectations. Its vertical air-filled chambers absorbed uneven ground and even larger stones with ease, to create a comfortable sleeping surface for a budget price, and its compact pack size makes it a practical choice for campers without a car. It also comes in a slightly smallerL version(180 x 52cm) costing £44.99, andan insulated three-season versioncosting £69.99.Why we love itThis is a comfortable and versatile sleeping pad suitable for most summer camping scenarios. As a side sleeper, prone to waking up and rolling over multiple times each night, I appreciated the generous width of the XL pad, as well as the silicone patches on the mattress’s surface, which helped prevent my sleeping bag from sliding off. Though considerably thinner than the Therm-a-Rest, this mattress absorbed lumps and bumps well, and I was unable to feel stones when lying on my side – even when placed directly underneath my hips.View image in fullscreenIt packs down to a rucksack-friendly size.Photograph: Linda Geddes/The GuardianThis is a lightweight and easily packable mat, which could fit inside a rucksack during expeditions.It’s a shame that …my younger family members disliked being able to feel the vertical grooves when lying on the mattress without a sleeping bag – although these were barely perceptible once wrapped inside one.Weight:600gDimensions (packed):28 x 10.5cmDimensions (inflated):195 x 60 x 5cm (LWH)R value:1.5Self-inflating:noPump included:no, inflate by mouthRead our fullDecathlon Simond MT500 camping mattress reviewDecathlonSimond MT500 XL inflatable trekking mattressfrom£49.99£49.99 at Decathlon£50 at ArgosWhat we loveComfortable and versatile mattress for summer campingWhat we don’t loveYou’ll get greater comfort if you spend a bit moreBest double mattress:Big Agnes Rapide SL insulated tent floor padBig AgnesBig Agnes Rapide SL insulated tent floor pad£239.95£239.95 at WildboundsWhat we loveRomance and comfort on camping expeditionsWhat we don’t loveRelatively expensive, and small for a double mattressView image in fullscreen£239.95 at WildboundsA night under canvas with your partner might sound romantic, but sharing the wrong mattress can make for an uncomfortable experience, with one partner being bounced awake when the other turns over, or slumped on to the hard rocky floor when their partner goes out to pee (yes, I speak from experience). This inflatable sleeping pad is extremely comfortable, tapered to fit in most two-man tents, insulated enough to see you through the colder months, and packing down small enough to fit into a backpack.Why we love itI’ve spent years grumbling about sharing a two-man tent with my husband, because we couldn’t find two compatible, comfortable mattresses that would fit. This is the solution. It fitted into the two-man expedition tent that my husband is illogically attached to, despite my grumbles about the lack of storage space, but it also worked well in our family tent, where recently I enjoyed one of my best camping sleeps ever, with no hip or backache the following morning.Like our other best mattress picks, stones and rough ground were imperceptible through its surface, and I wasn’t bounced awake at any point, even when my husband crawled out to make breakfast, while I dozed on.It’s a shame that …it’s relatively narrow for a double mattress. This is deliberate, as it’s designed to fit inside the average two-person tent, and my husband and I are both relatively slim, so it wasn’t a problem for us. However, larger couples, or those looking for more space, may find it a bit cramped. It was also relatively difficult to get the deflated mattress back inside its stuff sack.Weight:1.22kgDimensions (packed):29 x 14cmDimensions (inflated):198 x 127 x 9cm (LWH); 102cm wide at tapered endRvalue:4.8Self-inflating:noPump included:yes, pump sackBig AgnesBig Agnes Rapide SL insulated tent floor pad£239.95£239.95 at WildboundsWhat we loveRomance and comfort on camping expeditionsWhat we don’t loveRelatively expensive, and small for a double mattressBest self-inflating mattress for luxury camping:Vango Shangri-La Lux 16 GrandeVangoShangri-La Lux 16 Grandefrom£199£199.99 at Decathlon£199 at AmazonWhat we loveWide, comfortable, and super-easy to inflate and deflateWhat we don’t loveRisk of overinflation if you don’t watch outView image in fullscreenPhotograph: Linda Geddes/The Guardian£199.99 at Decathlon£199 at AmazonMost self-inflating mattresses require a wait before they fully inflate, and the thicker the mattress, the longer this takes. I’m sure I’m not the only person to get impatient and resort to puffing an extra bit of air in by mouth. Deflating them is another ordeal, and the thickest, most luxurious mattresses tend to be bulky, limiting them to car-based camping trips – and then only if you have a large car. The compensation is a close-to-bed-level of comfort, and excellent insulation from cold ground. The Vango Shangri-La Lux 16 Grande solves many of these issues by incorporating a built-in rechargeable pump, meaning it can be inflated, deflated and neatly packed away within minutes.Why we love itFor a single mattress, it is wide and comfortable, and the built-in pump means you can easily adjust the firmness to your taste. It took under a minute to fully inflate, approximately the same amount of time to deflate, and the pump was able to repeat this process five times before the battery needed recharging (which was simple to do). At 16cm thick and filled with a combination of air and foam, this mattress easily absorbs lumps, bumps and sharp stones; I also tried sleeping on this on my living room floor during the June heatwave and, though a little sweaty, it provided bed-level comfort on a hard floor. Weighing 4.4kg, with a packed size of 82 x 22cm, this mattress isn’t one you’d want to carry far from your car – but it is at least small enough to fit in most cars. A double version is also available.It’s a shame that …the built-in pump doesn’t automatically cut out once the mattress is fully inflated, so you need to monitor the process to avoid overinflating.Weight:4.4kgDimensions (packed):82 x 22cmDimensions (inflated):200 x 76 x 16cm (LWH)R value:13Self-inflating:yesPump included:built-in rechargeable electric pumpVangoShangri-La Lux 16 Grandefrom£199£199.99 at Decathlon£199 at AmazonWhat we loveWide, comfortable, and super-easy to inflate and deflateWhat we don’t loveRisk of overinflation if you don’t watch outBest sleeping mat for expeditions:Alpkit Radiant insulated inflatable camping matAlpkitRadiant insulated inflatable camping mat£98.99£98.99 at AlpkitWhat we loveDependable all-season sleeping pad that’s impressively comfyWhat we don’t loveIts shiny surface may cause sleeping bags to slither offView image in fullscreenPhotograph: Linda Geddes/The Guardian£98.99 at AlpkitThis is a lightweight and compact sleeping pad with excellent insulation, making it suitable for all sorts of outdoor adventures – including winter expeditions.Why we love itWhile there are wider and marginally more comfortable lightweight mats, Alpkit’s Radiant provides superb cushioning on rocky ground, and wins extra points for its relatively low price, high thermal resistance, 100% recycled outer fabric and water-resistant surface, which I tested by spraying it with a water bottle and leaving it for 30 minutes to soak in (it didn’t).Its high R value means it should keep you warm in all weather conditions. Weighing only 600g – less than two tins of baked beans – it will easily fit inside a rucksack, making it suitable for summer and winter expeditions alike.It’s a shame that …the surface of the mat is a little shiny and slippery.Weight:600gDimensions (packed):25 x 11cmDimensions (inflated):194 x 62 x 9cm (LWH)Rvalue:7.2Self-inflating:noPump included:yes, pump sackAlpkitRadiant insulated inflatable camping mat£98.99£98.99 at AlpkitWhat we loveDependable all-season sleeping pad that’s impressively comfyWhat we don’t loveIts shiny surface may cause sleeping bags to slither offThe best of the restView image in fullscreenPhotograph: Linda Geddes/The GuardianColeman Supercomfort self-inflating sleeping matColemanSupercomfort self-inflating double sleeping mat£195£195 at A&E LeisureWhat we loveWide and comfortable with minimal motion transferWhat we don’t loveBulky, and difficult to deflate and packView image in fullscreen£195 at A&E LeisureBest for:couples wanting a good sleepThis 12cm-thick self-inflating mattress gradually fills with air when the valves are twisted open and comes in single and double sizes. The double is wide and comfortable, and there’s minimal motion transfer when two sleepers lie side by side.In 2025, I rated this the best mattress for luxury camping, and it is still a worthy contender, but the Vango’s built-in pump gives it the edge.It didn’t make the final cut because …it’s relatively hard work to deflate, requiring a good deal of rolling around to squeeze the air out of it.Weight:6kg;dimensions (packed):65 x 32cm;dimensions (inflated):200 x 128 x 12cm (LWH);Rvalue:12.9;self-inflating:yes;pump included:yes, pump sackColemanSupercomfort self-inflating double sleeping mat£195£195 at A&E LeisureWhat we loveWide and comfortable with minimal motion transferWhat we don’t loveBulky, and difficult to deflate and packSea To Summit Ether Light XRSea To SummitEther Light XR (wide)from£187£187 at Nevisport£220 at Base Camp FoodWhat we loveLightweight and comfortable with a clever pillow systemWhat we don’t loveRelatively expensiveView image in fullscreen£187 at Nevisport£220 at Base Camp FoodBest for:expeditionsThis is an excellent lightweight inflatable sleeping mat, particularly when used in combination withSea to Summit’s Aeros pillow(see below), which attaches to its surface using adhesive hook and loop patches. Measuring 64cm across and 10cm thick, the wide version of the Ether Light XR I tested is slightly more comfortable than thesmaller sizeavailable (larger sizes are also available). My husband also had no grumbles about warmth when he used it to sleep in a Scottish bothy in March.It didn’t make the final cut because …although this was a worthy contender for best expedition mattress, it was narrowly pipped to the post by Alpkit’s Radiant on the grounds of price, eco-credentials and insulation (though it still has a four-season rating).Weight:665g;dimensions (packed):24 x 12cm;dimensions (inflated):183 x 64 x 10cm (LWH);Rvalue:4.1;self-inflating:no;pump included:yes, pump sackSea To SummitEther Light XR (wide)from£187£187 at Nevisport£220 at Base Camp FoodWhat we loveLightweight and comfortable with a clever pillow systemWhat we don’t loveRelatively expensiveBig Agnes Campmeister Deluxe insulatedBig AgnesCampmeister Deluxe insulatedfrom£187.95£187.95 at Wildbounds£200 at LD Mountain CentreWhat we loveWinter-ready comfort with a tough, stable designWhat we don’t loveRelatively expensive and heavy for its classView image in fullscreen£187.95 at Wildbounds£200 at LD Mountain CentreBest for:winter campingAnother great option for winter camping – especially if comfort is a priority. This inflatable sleeping mat is 11cm thick and 63.5cm wide, with slightly raised edges to prevent you rolling off in the night. With tough 70-denier ripstop fabric on the underside, this feels like a robust mat that’s built to last.It didn’t make the final cut because …insulation is similar to Alpkit’s Radiant, but weighing 907g, it’s more than 300g heavier. Although this is unlikely to bother the average camper, serious mountaineers may prefer a lighter, smaller mat.Weight:907g;dimensions (packed):20 x 14cm;dimensions (inflated):183 x 64 x 11cm (LWH);Rvalue:7;self-inflating:no;pump included:yes, pump sackBig AgnesCampmeister Deluxe insulatedfrom£187.95£187.95 at Wildbounds£200 at LD Mountain CentreWhat we loveWinter-ready comfort with a tough, stable designWhat we don’t loveRelatively expensive and heavy for its classEasy Camp KestrelEasy CampKestrel singlefrom£32.95£32.95 at Ligo£38.99 at AmazonWhat we loveA budget-friendly mat for smaller sleepersWhat we don’t loveNot the most comfortable; narrowView image in fullscreen£32.95 at Ligo£38.99 at AmazonBest for:family camping on a budget and festivalsThis 3.8cm self-inflating matisn’t the most comfortable we tested and, measuring 51cm across in a single size, it’s quite narrow. Even so, rocks were barely perceptible through its surface, and my back-sleeping husband reported feeling very comfortable on it. A5cm-thick versionis also available, which should provide side sleepers with a little more cushioning. This mat is also considerably cheaper than many of our other top picks, making it a great budget option, particularly for festivals and for kids or smaller adults.It didn’t make the final cut because …adults may find it too narrow for comfort, and side sleepers a little too thin.Weight:945g;dimensions (packed):28 x 16cm;dimensions (inflated):182 x 51 x 4cm (LWH);Rvalue:3;self-inflating:yes;pump included: no, but this is unnecessaryEasy CampKestrel singlefrom£32.95£32.95 at Ligo£38.99 at AmazonWhat we loveA budget-friendly mat for smaller sleepersWhat we don’t loveNot the most comfortable; narrowOutwell Dreamscape insulatedOutwellDreamscape insulated£93.12£93.12 at DeporvillageWhat we loveLofty comfort without clamminessWhat we don’t loveRequires foot pump to inflateView image in fullscreen£93.12 at DeporvillageBest for:family campingIf you like the bouncy loft of an airbed but find them unpleasantly cold and clammy to sleep on, this insulated version is a great alternative. Although the Therm-a-Rest NeoLoft is lighter and considerably more portable, the Dreamscape is cheaper and would be fine for car-based camping trips from spring to autumn. It does require a foot pump or a large pair of lungs to inflate it, though.It didn’t make the final cut because …it’s not as portable as some of our top picks.Weight:1.7kg;dimensions (packed):39 x 17cm;dimensions (inflated):205 x 77 x 12cm (LWH);R value:3.8;self-inflating:no;pump included:noOutwellDreamscape insulated£93.12£93.12 at DeporvillageWhat we loveLofty comfort without clamminessWhat we don’t loveRequires foot pump to inflateAlso considerCamping pillowWhile many people sling their regular pillow into the car as a token luxury on camping trips, this doesn’t work if you’re backpacking, when a lumpy pile of clothes is the make-do option. The necessity of packing pillows is also a frequent flashpoint for arguments when packing for family camping trips (believe me, I know).Thankfully, there are now inflatable pillows that are small enough to fit in your pocket, and compressible foam or microfibre pillows that take up far less space than a regular pillow. I tested eight camping pillows and would highly recommend Sea to Summit’s Aeros Ultralight pillow (£29.75 at Cotswold Outdoor) for a super-packable inflatable pillow or Vango’s Deep Sleep Memory pillow (£33.95 at Amazon) for a supremely comfortable alternative, provided you have more space.What you need to knowView image in fullscreenPhotograph: Linda Geddes/The GuardianWhat’s the difference between an airbed, sleeping pad, foam roll mat and self-inflating mattress?From glamping to Alpine mountaineering, there are different types of camping trip, and different mattresses will suit some better than others.Airbedsare large, inflatable mattresses, often high and bouncy, inflated with a foot- or electric-powered pump (some even have one built in). Although they’re very comfy, they’re poorly insulated, can feel cold and clammy to lie on, and tend to be susceptible to punctures. They’re also relatively heavy and bulky, making them unsuitable for backpacking, while those sharing a double airbed may be bounced awake by restless partners.Sleeping padsare ultralight inflatable mattresses designed for backpacking and mountaineering. Although they’re thinner and less bouncy than airbeds, they’re extremely comfortable and often incorporate insulating materials, making them suitable for camping in colder conditions, including winter when it comes to high-spec versions. Most also come with air sacks to aid inflation (see below) and puncture repair kits.Closed-cell foam roll matsaren’t particularly comfortable, but virtually indestructible. They’re designed to providea basic layer of padding, waterproofing and insulation between you and the ground. Being made from foam, they also tend to retain body heat well. Because of these properties, some backpackers choose to combine them with a basic air pad. They’re usually secured to the outside of a backpack with elasticated straps.Self-inflating mattressesconsist of an open-cell foam core inside an airtight fabric shell, with a valve that allows in air to inflate the mattress when it’s opened – although they often require a top-up of air from your lungs to make them firmer. Comfort ranges from moderate to high, depending on the thickness of the mattress, and the foam core tends to provide good insulation. However, they’re heavier and bulkier than air pads, and still susceptible to punctures.
