They’re deflated by opening back up the valve, and squeezing out the air, rolling as you go, and then closing the valve.The best camping stoves for cooking like a pro in the wild, testedRead moreHow long do camping mattresses last, and are they repairable?The lifespan of a camping mattress depends on its type, quality, and how well it’s cared for, but closed-cell foam mattresses tend to be the most durable, and airbeds the least so. Both self-inflating mats and air pads can also suffer from punctures and valve or seam failures, but self-inflating mattresses are often constructed from thicker fabrics and are less likely to fully deflate if punctured.To extend the life of your camping mattress, you could consider using a groundsheet to reduce the risk of punctures from sharp rocks, sticks and rough terrain, and avoid leaving inflatable mattresses in the sun. Care should also be taken not to overinflate air mattresses, while foam or self-inflating mats should ideally be stored unrolled, with inflation valves left open to maintain the loft and integrity of the foam.How do I inflate my camping mattress?It depends on the mattress. Airbeds tend to require a foot- or electric pump, while air pads are inflated by mouth or with a “pump sack” – a lightweight, air-filled bag that helps to keep moisture out of the pad (also important for longevity) and is more efficient than inflating by mouth.What’s an R value, and why is it important?The R value of a camping mattress measures how well it resists heat transfer from your body to the cold ground. A lower R value means less insulation, which is better in warmer weather, but less good for winter camping. Foam-based mattresses tend to have the highest R values, but some air pads incorporate other insulating materials, making them suitable for winter camping expeditions. As a general rule of thumb, R values of 1 to 2 tend to be sufficient for summer camping; R values of 2 to 4.5 are suitable for spring through to autumn, while for winter camping or other cold climates, you’ll need an R value of 4 to 5 or higher.How to dispose of old camping mattressesCamping mattresses are often made from a mix of materials – such as foam, coated fabric, plastic valves and adhesives – which can make them difficult to recycle. Because of this, the best option is usually to repair, reuse or donate them.If the mattress is still usable, consider selling it secondhand or giving it away locally. Some homeless charities may accept lightweight camping mats for use by people who are sleeping rough, while scout groups, cadet units and schools may be able to use them for expeditions such as the Duke of Edinburgh’s award. In the UK,Kitsquad– a charity that supports people with low incomes to get outdoors – accepts donated gear that still has life in it – including sleeping mats.Minor damage can often be repaired. Small punctures can often be glued or patched, using patch kits supplied with the mattress or bought separately (such as this£5.99 one from Decathlon), so it’s worth checking the manufacturer’s website before throwing away a deflating mattress. Some brands also offer warranties against defects in materials or workmanship and may repair other damage for a fee.If the mattress is beyond repair or reuse, check with your local household waste recycling centre to see whether they accept camping mats or bulky mixed-material items.Don’t put camping mattresses in your household recycling bin, as most cannot be processed through standard kerbside recycling, and avoid burning them, as this creates pollution.For more camping advice from the Filter:The best genuinely comfortable camping chairsThe best tents, testedThe best cool boxes and bags for camping, picnics and festivalsLinda Geddes is a former Guardian science correspondent,known for inserting herself into her own mini-science experiments (like the time she spenta week with a clown, a comic and some toddlers). Thisextends to product testing, where she delights in devising rigorous ways to distinguish high-performance stuff from marketing puff. Outside work, she can frequently be found swimming in rivers, dangling from cliffsand trudging the dawn walk to the campsite PortalooThis article was originally published on 23 May 2025. Reviews published in the Filter may be periodically updated to reflect new products and at the editor’s discretion. The date of an article’s most recent update can be found in the timestamp at the top of the page. This article was amended on 22 July 2026; the Vango Shangri-La Lux 16 Grandewas added after testing, and prices were updated throughout.Explore more on these topicsTravelThe FilterCamping holidaysSleepfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘I fell in love with the art of coffee-making’: the female baristas shaking up Somalia’s booming cafe scene | Global development | The Guardian
description: As fear and fighting ease in Mogadishu, coffee shops have become catalysts for change, bringing jobs for women and creating much-needed social spaces
Author: Moulid Hujale
Headline: As fear and fighting ease in Mogadishu, coffee shops have become catalysts for change, bringing jobs for women and creating much-needed social spaces
Content: Saabiriin Hassan behind the counter at CPlus Cafe.Photograph: Mohamud Utaama/The GuardianView image in fullscreenSaabiriin Hassan behind the counter at CPlus Cafe.Photograph: Mohamud Utaama/The GuardianGlobal development‘I fell in love with the art of coffee-making’: the female baristas shaking up Somalia’s booming cafe sceneAs fear and fighting ease in Mogadishu, coffee shops have become catalysts for change, bringing jobs for women and creating much-needed social spacesSupported byAbout this contentMoulid Hujalein MogadishuWed 22 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 06.09 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleEvery morning at six, Saabiriin Hassan slips on her apron as she takes her place behind the counter at Cplus Cafe in the heart of Mogadishu. The aroma of freshly brewed coffee fills the air, but it is Hassan’s ever-present smile that draws the attention first.It stays with her every move – from grinding beans and steaming milk to chatting with customers. Hassan is among the first female baristas inSomalia, serving coffee and helping a new cafe culture take root in a capital city once only known for conflict.“It started out of curiosity in 2020,” says the 23-year-old. “I realised there were no female baristas in the city. I decided to learn the craft and took a three-month course. Since then, I fell in love with the art of coffee-making.”Somalis have long been known for their love of camel-milk tea, but as modern coffee shops spring up across Mogadishu, social habits are being reshaped and new spaces are created for young people.At the heart of this transformation are young female baristas such as Hassan, challenging gender norms by entering a traditionally male-dominated industry.View image in fullscreenSaabiriin Hassan outside Cplus Cafe in MogadishuPhotograph: Mohamud Utaama/The Guardian“At the beginning, people criticised me for doing this job,” she says. “I was told this is a man’s job. Some even said nobody would marry a woman who serves men in public. But I did not listen to them.“I know my values and what I want in life, so I only focus on my goals,” Hassan says.Six years into her career, her persistence has paid off. The attitudes that once discouraged her are shifting and more young women are now working as baristas. As security has improved in Mogadishu, members of the Somali diaspora have returned, while fear and fighting have given way to the construction of new shopping malls, commercial centres and cafes.This cafe boom offers more than coffee, however. It brings with it job opportunities for young people – more than 70% ofSomalia’s population is under 35– in a country facing many challenges, not least high unemployment. The cafes also create spaces where artists, designers and entrepreneurs can meet and exchange ideas.Coffee may have been discovered in this part of eastAfrica– the wild beans were first found in neighbouring Ethiopia – but amid the proliferation of coffee shops in Mogadishu, many Somalis remain loyal to their sweet, spicy and milky tea. Somali tea is traditionally served piping hot, and those trying coffee for the first time request their cappuccino extra hot.Sometimes they like to mix it with camel-milk tea to make their own version of coffee. The trend of mixing is so common that there is even a word for it, “soo dakhar”, meaning to add a shot of espresso.View image in fullscreenYusra Abdifatahi making coffee at Beydan cafe in MogadishuPhotograph: Mohamud Utaama/The GuardianYusra Abdifatahi, 21, has been working as a barista at Beydan since 2023, one of the pioneering coffee shops in the capital. She was recently promoted to branch manager. “I now have more responsibility, looking after daily operations, quality control and customer satisfaction,” she says. “I also make coffee to cover the main barista.”Abdifatahi is a part-time student and says the job pays her university fees and helps her support her family. She adds that as a result of the work, her social skills and confidence have improved, too. “I now have clarity of what I want to do in life, and I believe I am on the right track to achieve my dream of having my own business,” she says.Beydan, which describes itself as a “modern pan-African coffee company”, began as a home bakery in 2018 in Mogadishu. Today, the company has more than 10 branches across Somalia and employs 136 people, with women making up more than 40% of its workforce.“When we opened our store in the Taleh neighbourhood in 2019, there was no other shop in the area,” says Najiib Abdullahi, founder of Beydan. “It was an empty street.”Other businesses, including restaurants and designer boutiques, were soon drawn to this once-quiet stretch and it has now become one of Mogadishu’s busiest streets, known locally to many asWadada Beydan, or Beydan Road.“We are proud to pave the way for others to return and invest in our country,” says Abdullahi. “Our vision is to expand across Africa, and soon we hope to open new branches in Nairobi and beyond.”View image in fullscreenHassan serving a customer at Cplus Cafe.Photograph: Mohamud Utaama/The GuardianAs demand has increased, colleges and training centres in Mogadishu are now offering barista training courses. When not behind the counter at Cplus Cafe, Saabiriin Hassan works as a trainer in one of these centres.“Most of the students are women,” she says. “And that motivates me to continue doing this job and make a difference in the lives of Somali youth. We have come a long way, and I am glad I am playing a small part in this change.”With more than70,000 followers on TikTok, Hassan shares her daily routine and tips on the art of coffee-making. Unlike other vocational trades such as plumbing, she says working as a barista carries a certain cachet among young Somalis.“Apparently,” she says, “it is not like one of those odd jobs young people despise. A lot of people ask me how they can join and where they can train.“I see a bright future in Somalia’s hospitality sector, and I hope to open my own cafe sometime,Insha’Allah[God willing].“I would encourage young women to acquire as many skills as possible. And never listen to those who say you can’t do it.”Explore more on these topicsGlobal developmentSomaliaCoffeeCities and developmentAfricaFoodMiddle East and north AfricafeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Why hot weather and chocolate can go together | Food | The Guardian
description: Fridge-friendly nougat, malty ice-cream and a hybrid brownie-chocolate bar – your summer bites are sorted
Author: Annalisa Barbieri
Headline: Fridge-friendly nougat, malty ice-cream and a hybrid brownie-chocolate bar – your summer bites are sorted
Content: ‘Wow’: Heist’s milk honey nougat chocolatePhotograph: Heist ChocolateView image in fullscreen‘Wow’: Heist’s milk honey nougat chocolatePhotograph: Heist ChocolateThe chocolate diariesFoodWhy hot weather and chocolate can go togetherFridge-friendly nougat, malty ice-cream and a hybrid brownie-chocolate bar – your summer bites are sortedAnnalisa BarbieriWed 22 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 08.06 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe summer heat requires special treatment of chocolate. Few experts would agree withkeeping it in the fridge, but sometimes you have to gently shh them and do what feels right.Heist’s Milk and Honey Nougatis like a choc-coveredtorronemorbido: a big slab of milk chocolate covering honey-laced pistachio and almond nougat. It’s a disaster in hot weather. But, kept in the fridge … wow. The pre-marked pieces are so big and generous you can’t disobey them, slice your knife into it and then eat the piece within, say, three minutes before the whole lot turns molten in your fingers.View image in fullscreenPump Street has just launched aWalnut Brownie 60% Dark Milk Barwith Haitian beans. It features brownie crumb with English Sharpham Park caramelised, hand-chopped walnuts. It’s rich and deep in flavour.My father used to have an ice-cream shop, and he made the best chocolate ice-cream (which I think is difficult to get right) because he’d mix the chocolate with chestnuts to give the gelato a wonderful mouthfeel. I keep meaning to try to emulate it. Not the same but a very worthy chocolate ice-cream, since it’s the weather for it, is Ottolenghi’sSalted Malted Chocolate ice-creammade with 73% cocoa Ecuadorian chocolate.Soon I’ll be writing about making your own chocolate treats, but I am in love withDominique Ludwig’s salted caramel bars, which feature an oat and almond base, nut butter and dates whizzed up into a dreamy caramel and drizzled with (for me, very high cocoa content) chocolate. I customise her recipe with more nut butter than she suggests and sprinkle a few roasted peanuts into the caramel innards to make a bastard child of a Twix and a Snickers. Shop-bought can go hang.Explore more on these topicsFoodThe chocolate diariesChocolateIce-cream and sorbetrecipesShareReuse this content

title: How to turn beetroot leaves into a classic south Indian vegetable dish – recipe | Food | The Guardian
description: Simple spices, fresh coconut and a handful of lentils or nuts is enough to transform leaves normally destined for the compost
Author: Tom Hunt
Headline: Simple spices, fresh coconut and a handful of lentils or nuts is enough to transform leaves normally destined for the compost
Content: Tom Hunt’s beetroot leaf poriyal.Photograph: Tom Hunt/The GuardianView image in fullscreenTom Hunt’s beetroot leaf poriyal.Photograph: Tom Hunt/The GuardianWaste notFoodHow to turn beetroot leaves into a classic south Indian vegetable dish – recipeSimple spices, fresh coconut and a handful of lentils or nuts is enough to transform leaves normally destined for the compostTom HuntWed 22 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GooglePoriyalis south India’s version of sauteed spiced vegetables, and it can be made with just about any vegetable, though green beans or a leafy green such as spinach, moringa or amaranth are most commonly used. The vegetables are fried with onions and spices, a spoon of urad dal is added for texture (if you don’t have urad dal, a handful of cashews makes a delicious alternative), and it’s finished with a grating of fresh coconut. This fragrant, spicy and flavoursome side dish also works brilliantly with the earthy flavour of beetroot leaves, and I like to serve it with boiled beetroot, yoghurt, chapatis or/and rice.Beetroot leaf poriyal, or south Indian-style sauteed greensI’ve adapted this recipe from the incredibleMaunika Gowardhan, author ofCurry, one of the New York Times best cookbooks of 2025. She writes: “The key to cooking spinach poriyal is to let the greens cook without any water. As they wilt and release their moisture, they begin to take on the flavour of the spices.” Beetroot leaves, which feature in a lot of Indian recipes, cook just like spinach leaves, and I like to use the stalks, too, not least because they impart a lovely red colour to the dish. It’s best to add them with the onions to make sure they cook through. I love the addition of freshly grated coconut at the end, and bought one especially; it is optional, however, and can be replaced with rehydrated desiccated coconut.Serves2-4as a side dish1 bunch beetroot leaves(roughly 160g), or another leafy green (spinach, kale, cabbage)1½ tbspneutral oil, or ghee1 tsp black (or white/yellow) mustard seeds1 dried red chilli(optional)6-10 fresh curry leaves, or to taste2 tsp urid dal, or 1 small handful crushed cashews1 small onion, peeled and roughly chopped¼-½ tsp chilli powder35g freshly grated coconut, or 10g desiccated coconut, pre-soaked in a little warm water for 10 minutes1 handful fresh coriander, chopped from stem to leafTo serve (all optional)Boiled beetroot, cut into wedgesYoghurtChapatis or/and riceWash the bunch of beetroot leaves and strip the leaves off the stalks. Shred the leaves and cut the stalks into 1cm lengths.Put a wok or frying pan on a medium heat, add the oil or ghee and, once it’s hot, stir in the mustard seeds and optional dried chilli. When the seeds start to pop, add a few curry leaves and the urad dal or a handful of crushed cashews, then add the chopped onion, beetroot stems and chilli powder to taste. Saute, stirring, for five minutes, or until translucent, then add the leaves and saute until wilted. Season to taste, then stir in the freshly grated coconut with a few more curry leaves and a handful of chopped coriander, and serve.Explore more on these topicsFoodWaste notFood wasteVegetablesIndian food and drinkVegetarian food and drinkBeans, pulses and legumesHerbs and spicesfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Tropical, Mexican-style and Middle Eastern: Ravinder Bhogal’s fruit salad recipes | Food | The Guardian
description: Forget the underpowered, ready-made offerings of food chains: these vibrant mixes are bursting with fruit and notes of florals, citrus and spice
Author: Ravinder Bhogal
Headline: Forget the underpowered, ready-made offerings of food chains: these vibrant mixes are bursting with fruit and notes of florals, citrus and spice
Content: Ravinder Bhogal's shaved mango and cucumber salad with tajin and tequila dressing.Photograph: Phoebe Pearson/The Guardian. Food styling: Hanna Miller. Prop styling: Lucy Turnbull. Food styling assistant: Thea Hudson.View image in fullscreenRavinder Bhogal's shaved mango and cucumber salad with tajin and tequila dressing.Photograph: Phoebe Pearson/The Guardian. Food styling: Hanna Miller. Prop styling: Lucy Turnbull. Food styling assistant: Thea Hudson.FoodTropical, Mexican-style and Middle Eastern: Ravinder Bhogal’s fruit salad recipesForget the underpowered, ready-made offerings of food chains: these vibrant mixes are bursting with fruit and notes of florals, citrus and spiceRavinder BhogalWed 22 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleMost of our experiences with fruit salad are dull: straight out of a can in saccharine syrup or unripe, slippery, unidentifiable chunks hastily bought from a sandwich chain when you’re trying to be healthy. The best ones, however, are life-enhancing and made fresh at home. And, with summer offering up a bounty of ripeness, now is the best time to make them. Eat them sweet or savoury, heighten their flavour with floral waters, spices, aromats, herbs or citrus, and enjoy for breakfast, lunch or as a refreshing after-dinner treat.Shaved mango and cucumber salad with Tajín and tequila dressing (pictured top)Tajínis a mouth-puckering and addictive spice mix that’s often sprinkled over fruit; it makes an excellent rim for margaritas, too. If you can’t find it, mix a tablespoon of mild chilli powder, half a teaspoon of ground sea salt, a quarter-teaspoon of citric acid and the zest of one lime.Prep10 minCook10 minServes43 ripe mangoes,peeled1 large cucumber1-2tbsp Tajín, or to tasteFor the dressingJuice of2 limes2tbsp tequila1tbsp agave syrup30gfresh coriander, roughly chopped60ml avocado oilShave the mangoes and cucumber using a vegetable peeler, discarding the stones and seeds as you go, then arrange on a platter.To make the dressing, blitz the lime juice, tequila, agave syrup and coriander in a blender, then slowly drizzle in the oil until emulsified. Drizzle the dressing over the shaved mango and cucumber, and toss to coat. Scatter with the Tajín and serve at once.Tropical fruit salad with lemongrass, vanilla and ginger syrupView image in fullscreenRavinder Bhogal’s tropical fruit salad with lemongrass vanilla and ginger syrup.Make this the night before, to give the fruit time to soak up the flavours of the syrup. This is the type of fruit salad you can put into a large jar and take to a barbecue or picnic.Prep20 minCook15 minChill2 hr+Serves61 small pineapple, peeled, cored and cut into bite-sized chunks2 mangoes, peeled, pitted and cut into bite-sized pieces½ honeydew or cantaloupe melon, peeled, deseeded and cut into bite-sized chunks2 guavas,peeled and cut into bite-sized pieces20 lychees, stoned1 small papaya, cut into bite-sized piecesFor the syrup4tbsp golden caster sugar8 makrut lime leaves2lemongrass stalks, bashed with something heavy2cm pieceginger, peeled and sliced1 vanilla pod2 star anise500ml waterPut all the cut fruit in a large bowl. To make the syrup, put all the ingredients in a saucepan, bring to a boil, then simmer for 10 minutes. Take off the hob, leave to cool, then strain it over the fruit. Pop the bowl in the fridge for at least two hours and ideally overnight. This is wonderful served with ice-cream.Labneh with watermelon, berries, rose and pistachio dukkahView image in fullscreenRavinder Bhogal’s labneh with watermelon, berries, rose and pistachio dukkahAdd rosewater with caution and to taste, because some brands are stronger than others – you’re looking for lightly perfumed rather than granny’s bath water here!Prep15 minDrain24 hrInfuse30 minCook20 minServes6900g greek yoghurt1 big pinchflaky sea salt800g watermelon, peeled and cut into wedges, triangles or bite-sized pieces125g strawberries, hulled and halved125g raspberries2tsprosewaterFor the sweet dukkah80g pistachios40g pine nuts1tbsp poppy seeds2tbsp sesame seedsZest of1 large orange1 heapedtsp ground cinnamon¼tsp ground cardamom2tbsp runny honey1 fat pinchsea saltLine a deep bowl with muslin. Mix the yoghurt and salt, then scoop into the muslin, bring the edges of the cloth together to form a tight bundle and tie securely with string. Hang the bundle over the bowl and leave for 24 hours in the fridge. Much of the liquid will drain out and the remaining yoghurt will turn thick, dense and fudgy.The next day, remove the labneh from its cloth packaging and use the back of a spoon to spread it over a platter. In a bowl, sprinkle the watermelon and berries with rosewater, then leave at room temperature to infuse for 30 minutes.Meanwhile, make the dukkah. Heat the oven to 180C (160C fan)/350F/gas 4. In a bowl, mix all the dukkah ingredients, then spread over a lined baking sheet and roast for eight to 10 minutes, until nutty and toasty. Leave to cool a little, then bash roughly in a mortar.To serve, top the labneh with the watermelon and berries, drizzle over any juices from their bowl and sprinkle on the dukkah.Explore more on these topicsFoodFruitDessertSaladMexican food and drinkMiddle Eastern food and drinkHerbs and spicesrecipesShareReuse this content

title: A moment that changed me: I painted a man’s portrait – and he hated it | Painting | The Guardian
description: I thought this commission would be straightforward. But my sitter’s response made me question my own attitude to my looks and body image
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: I thought this commission would be straightforward. But my sitter’s response made me question my own attitude to my looks and body image
Content: In the frame … Tom Neenan.Photograph: Matt StrongeView image in fullscreenIn the frame … Tom Neenan.Photograph: Matt StrongeA moment that changed mePaintingA moment that changed me: I painted a man’s portrait – and he hated itI thought this commission would be straightforward. But my sitter’s response made me question my own attitude to my looks and body imageTom NeenanWed 22 Jul 2026 01.45 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn September 2024, I was asked to paint the portrait of an 86-year-old man. He had apparently always wanted a formal portrait of himself, and a binge watch of Portrait Artist of the Year had finally spurred him to action. I had been receiving painting commissions for about three years, so I knew the drill: take receipt of a photo, render a painting that wouldn’t look out of place on a county cricket clubhouse wall, and pocket my semi-hard-earned money.double quotation markHe had a sense of who he was – and it didn’t have anything to do with his current appearanceOn this occasion, there were two immediate sticking points: first, the sitter wanted me to paint him in person, which would involve commuting to Reading from London and making small talk – to my mind the very worst of obligations. And second, to quote his WhatsApp message, he wanted me to “capture him”. How hard could that be, I thought?What I didn’t realise was that my sitter and I had something in common: we both had trouble identifying what we look like. For portrait painters, looking into the mirror is an essential part of the job. Every dauber worth their salt has spent hours, days, months gazing at their own reflection and recreating what they see on stretched fabric; it’s as much a narcissistic pursuit as an academic one. But I don’t enjoy it. My eyes gravitate to flaws and imperfections. I torture myself wishing I were slimmer, more sculpted, had a stronger jaw or smaller ears. In a world of looksmaxxers, I feel extremely looksminned.View image in fullscreenAn early version of the ‘Lionel’ portrait when it was a work in progress.Photograph: Courtesy of Tom NeenanSo when I came to paint this gentleman, I put together a basic composition – I sat him down in front of a nondescript wall and did a head-to-elbow painting – to create a likeness I thought he’d be happy with. It took about a week, and by the weekend I had sent him a photo of the 80% finished work.He did not hold back with his criticism. He said he didn’t recognise the man in the portrait – that I hadn’t, in fact, “captured him” at all. I had failed.He also sent me a raft of notes: his eyes weren’t as I’d shown them, they were kinder and wiser; his hair was fuller, his smile brighter. So I tried again. I flattered him so much that by the time I had finished the second painting, it was like Paul Newman and Brad Pitt had a child who had fallen out of the handsome tree and caressed every leaf on the way down. But this attempt was also met with indifference. He sensed he had been “yassified” and he wasn’t happy about it.I felt bewildered and frustrated. I could see that, like me, he had an unshakeable idea of who he was – and it didn’t necessarily have anything to do with his current appearance. He wanted me to conjure up the image of himself – inside and out – that he held as a fixed idea in his mind.I knew I needed to take a different approach. Instead of simply attempting to recreate features on a canvas, I needed to go deeper. I began chatting with him, teasing out details of his life and learning more about who he felt he was. I found he had a romantic side: he was a dreamer and a yarn spinner, a voracious reader of detective and historical novels. He loved fiction – to the extent that he was prone to embellishing or exaggerating his own story at times. What I painted became a collaboration between what he saw and what I saw. Art is a dialogue, and the eventual portrait went beyond acrylics to become a product of our conversations. Comparing the two, I can see that his smile became a fraction more impish than in my original rendition, the eyes possessed more of a glint.View image in fullscreenNeenan in his studio.Photograph: Courtesy of Tom NeenanSadly, the man died before he could see the finished painting. But I take solace in the fact that what he truly wanted was to be seen, maybe for the last time. I had shown him a work in progress, at which he had grinned broadly, and can only hope he would have approved of the finished piece.During the process, I also learned more about the disconnect – some may even call it a dysmorphia – that I often experience when it comes to self-perception. I always find plenty of room for improvement: weight to be lost, blemishes to be removed, crooked teeth to be straightened. But when I get despondent about my appearance, I also remind myself that my negative self-talk is just one side of the conversation – it’s not necessarily how someone else would paint me. Although I may gain weight and lose my hair, and although I’ll definitely age, that doesn’t make me any less “me”. Despite the desire to control how we’re seen by others, we’re all a work in progress, right until the end.Tom Neenan: Portrait of a Tom as a Young Neenanis at Underbelly, George Square, Edinburgh from 5 to 30 August at 15:15pm.Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick hereExplore more on these topicsPaintingA moment that changed meArtfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘The moles of Poblado Uno’: villagers’ mission to recover body of Venezuela quake victim | Venezuela | The Guardian
description: The Guedez García family spent 18 days searching for their missing daughter in La Guaira, Venezuela, after devastating earthquakes last month. When her body was finally found, the family and volunteers from their village of Poblado Uno travelled back home to lay her to rest
Author: Tom Phillips
Headline: The Guedez García family spent 18 days searching for their missing daughter in La Guaira, Venezuela, after devastating earthquakes last month. When her body was finally found, the family and volunteers from their village of Poblado Uno travelled back home to lay her to rest
Content: Neighbours form a caravan carrying white balloons behind the truck carrying the Guedez García family as it arrives in Poblado UnoThe Guedez García family spent 18 days searching for their missing daughter in La Guaira, Venezuela, after devastating earthquakes last month. When her body was finally found, the family and volunteers from their village of Poblado Uno travelled back home to lay her to restByTom Phillipsin El Junquito. Photographs by Manu QuinteroWed 22 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 14.15 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn a mountainside cemetery high above Caracas, far from the chaos of Venezuela’s earthquake-lashed coast, a family of three huddles in the back of a pickup truck coming to terms with their loss.“At first, it will be difficult,” says Adolfo Guedez, tears rolling down his cheeks as his eldest daughter, Carmen, hugs a wooden cremation box containing the ashes of her sister, Alexandra.View image in fullscreenYaritza García and her daughter, Carmen, hug after receiving the box containing the remains of Alexandra at El Junquito Cemetery on the outskirts of Caracas“Butshewill help us get through this,” Guedez comforts his wife and daughter, motioning to the urn holding their second child’s remains. “Shewill help us.”Alexandra Yarismar Guedez García, a 23-year-old physiotherapist, is one of more than 5,000 Venezuelans who were killed when the country’s north coast was devastated by two earthquakes on 24 June 2026.Last Sunday, after 18 days searching for their missing daughter in the wreckage of her collapsed apartment building, the Guedez García family finally found Alexandra’s body, buried in the sitting room, and started the journey back home to Poblado Uno (Village Number One), the small farming community where she was born and raised.“It’s a nightmare – it never went through my mind that I would have to live through a nightmare like this,” the victim’s despondent father said as his family prepared to set off from the temporary shack where, for nearly three weeks, they had squatted by the ruins of Alexandra’s home in Caraballeda, a beach town that was one of the worst-hit areas.View image in fullscreenYaritza García holds a photo of her late daughter, Alexandra, whose ashes lie in a wooden box behind her at her home in Poblado UnoFrom Caraballeda, the Guedez García family drove south to a picturesque mountaintop settlement called El Junquito, where they collected her remains in a graveyard with stunning views over Venezuela’s capital. For a while they sat there, absorbing the reality of Alexandra’s departure to the sound of sparrow song.As night approached, they left the cemetery and sped southwest towards their rural home in Portuguesa state, Venezuela’s agricultural heartlands, passing police checkpoints emblazoned with propaganda exalting Hugo Chávez’s “Bolivarian” revolution and the slogan “Doubting is treason”, and billboards demanding the return of Chávez’s successor, Nicolás Maduro, who was abducted by the US in January.View image in fullscreenNeighbours stand in the rain in front of the the Guedez García family home after their return to Poblado UnoView image in fullscreenWhen the convoy reached Poblado Uno, a hamlet of seven dirt streets and three avenues, it was just after 1.30am. Torrential rain hammered down on the agricultural community. But residents had turned out in force to pay their respects to Alexandra – and to salute the local farmers who, despite having no experience in rescue work, travelled to Caraballeda and spent a fortnightburrowing into the ruins of her fallen 14-storey towerin an attempt to find her.View image in fullscreenCarmen, 23, Alexandra’s sister, enters her family’s house carrying her sister’s ashes“Welcome back! Thanks to your efforts and bravery, you managed to bring Alexandra home, allowing us to say a final farewell,” read one banner village children had produced to celebrate the tireless volunteers they nicknamed “the moles of Poblado Uno”. “Your support in the midst of so much pain was an act of kindness we will never forget.”Many mourners carried white balloons to symbolise their grief. Motorbike riders honked their horns.View image in fullscreenVolunteers of Poblado Uno pose for a photograph after returning from the search for Alexandra Guedez GarcíaView image in fullscreenAdolfo Guedez talks with relatives who have travelled from a different village to pay their respects during Alexandra’s funeral in Poblado UnoView image in fullscreenCarmen Guedez at her home after their return from La GuairaView image in fullscreenView image in fullscreenYaritza García’s colleagues comfort her after a prayer during Alexandra’s funeral. Yaritza García is comforted by her mother, Carmen Sivira, in front of the altar for her daughter’s funeral in their homeGuedez, 56, voiced anger at what he called the government’s sluggish and incompetent response to the disaster that took his daughter’s life. In the crucial hours and days after the earthquakes razed scores of buildings along the coast around Alexandra’s tower, survivors claimed government rescuers were nowhere to be seen. Troops stood by with rifles in an apparent attempt to prevent the unrest the country’s authoritarian rulers seemed afraid the quakes might generate. “I don’t blame the government for this disaster … I blame it for its inefficiency,” Guedez complained.‘I can only describe it as a war zone’: the rescuers navigating Venezuela’s post-quake hellscapeRead moreView image in fullscreenVolunteers from Poblado Uno before their first day in the search for Alexandra Guedez García in La GuairaView image in fullscreenVolunteers, relatives of Guedez García’s family who travelled 700km to help, discuss the search operationView image in fullscreenSilvio Sivira, Eduardo Perozo and Edwin Rodriguez, volunteers from Poblado Uno, walk over the rubble of the Costa Brava building on 3 July 2026View image in fullscreenView image in fullscreenCarmen Guedez comforts her mother, Yaritza García, at their makeshift shelter on the ninth day after the double earthquake on 24 June 2026. Carmen and Adolfo Guedez after finding Alexandra’s body In La Guaira on 12 JulyView image in fullscreenAdolfo Guedez takes a last look at the collapsed building where his daughter spent 18 days trapped after the double earthquake in La GuairaBut he and his family were also filled with gratitude for their village’s 18 “moles” – 16 men and two women who fought day and night to recover his daughter – and to the other volunteers who fed them as they tried to find Alexandra. “Not everyone was bad,” said Alexandra’s mother, Yaritza García, 51. “We have met wonderful people too … I’m thankful to my God because he did not fail us,” she added, although she was adamant the government had.View image in fullscreenNeighbours gather outside the Guedez García family home during the arrival of the 18 volunteersView image in fullscreenView image in fullscreenEdismar García, 39, aunt of Alexandra, and neighbours await the volunteers for a surprise welcome ceremony at the entrance of Poblado Uno. A woman hugs Silvio Sivira during the arrival of the 18 volunteersView image in fullscreenView image in fullscreenA banner at the welcome ceremony reads: “Moles of Poblado Uno, God bless you”. Volunteer Eduardo Perozo jumps from the back of a truck at the Guedez García family homeAt Alexandra’s wake, there were hints of theprofound political discontent magnified by the natural disaster as dozens of locals packed the front room of the Guedez García family’s simple bungalow to visit a small altar decorated with photographs, religious images and candles. When the local mayor – a member of the ruling socialist party – showed up, many mourners walked outside, a discreet but revealing protest against an unpopular political movement, which many Venezuelans blame for their country’s economic and social collapse since Chávez won power more than a quarter of a century ago.View image in fullscreenPeople attending a night prayer at Alexandra’s funeralAlexandra, part of a generation who knew nothing but Chavista rule, had left Poblado Uno late last year after obtaining a degree in physiotherapy. Her parents recalled scrimping and saving – and sometimes going without food – in order to pay for her studies despite the pauperisation of their once affluent oil-rich country. “And we would do it all again,” said her mother, who is a teacher.Adolfo Guedez, who makes a living harvesting maize from the rusty soils of Venezuela’s plains, said he had only ever been to the university of life. “But he’s a decent man – a good father,” added his wife.View image in fullscreenEdismar García explains the plan to improve the altar for Alexandra’s funeralRelatives said Alexandra had dreamed of setting up her own physiotherapy practice in Venezuela’s capital. Her older sister, Carmen, had trained as a surgeon. Alexandra’s dreams were snuffed out by June’s earthquakes, which turned the apartment block where she lived for less than a week into a mass of debris.View image in fullscreenThe names Yale (for Alexandra), Yary (for Carmen Yarismar), Adolfo and Yaritza are handwritten on a wall inside the Guedez García family homeGuedez felt unsure about the future after his youngest child’s death. “Half of my heart has died. I have the other half left, which is my other daughter … And I will fight hard for that little girl I still have: to love her, to cherish her, and to find a way not to give up,” he vowed.As for Venezuela, Guedez felt confused. He recalled his happiness after hearing of Maduro’s capture by US special forces on 3 January this year, hoping that would finally bring political change to a nation crippled by years of misrule, economic crisis, authoritarianism and foreign meddling. But that joy has now evaporated as regime opponents adjust to the unexpected decision by the US to back Maduro’s allies under the command of his former vice president, Delcy Rodríguez.View image in fullscreenAdolfo Guedez during a conversation at his mother-in-law’s house in Poblado Uno“At least now we can speak our minds and they don’t come after us or arrest us,” Guedez said of thefledgling post-Maduro political thaw, which has seen hundreds of political prisoners freed from jail and foreign journalists allowed back into the country for the first time sinceMaduro stole the 2024 election. “But this isn’t everything. We want a free Venezuela, as it was before.”View image in fullscreenAdolfo Guedez hugs a niece in the backyard of his houseGuedez was determined not to abandon his country, as nearly 8 million Venezuelan migrants have done in recent years to escape economic strife and dictatorship. “My country is a paradise. We have it all here … oil, diamonds, iron, aluminium … and such humane people … It’s just such a shame that we have such an awful, second-rate government,” he said.In spite of everything, Guedez still felt hope – a sentiment reinforced by the backbreaking travails of those who rescued his daughter’s body from her collapsed building and by the affectionate, if devastating, homecoming afforded to Alexandra by the citizens of Village Number One.View image in fullscreenCarmen Guedez has her hair done by her aunt, Edismar García, while her grandmother, Carmen Sivira, watches from the front door of her house in Poblado Uno“These people have given me one surprise after another,” Guedez reflected as he sat in the sunshine near his home smoking a cigarette, one day after returning home with his dead child. “I didn’t expect it. I never expected it at all.”Explore more on these topicsThe Guardian picture essayVenezuelaAmericasEarthquakesfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘The bed you thought was safe, isn’t safe’: how Zoe Watts survived drug-facilitated rape – and fought back | Rape and sexual assault | The Guardian
description: For years, her husband was adding sedatives to her evening cup of tea. Now, with fellow survivor Amanda Stanhope, she is lifting the lid on a hidden and diabolical crime
Author: Anna Moore
Headline: For years, her husband was adding sedatives to her evening cup of tea. Now, with fellow survivor Amanda Stanhope, she is lifting the lid on a hidden and diabolical crime
Content: ‘There were people in town taking his side’ ... Zoe Watts.Photograph: Karen Robinson/The GuardianView image in fullscreen‘There were people in town taking his side’ ... Zoe Watts.Photograph: Karen Robinson/The GuardianRape and sexual assault‘The bed you thought was safe, isn’t safe’: how Zoe Watts survived drug-facilitated rape – and fought backFor years, her husband was adding sedatives to her evening cup of tea. Now, with fellow survivor Amanda Stanhope, she is lifting the lid on a hidden and diabolical crimeAnna MooreWed 22 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn September 2024, Amanda Stanhope watched, transfixed, from her home in Manchester, as theGisèle Pelicotcase made headlines around the world.“Gisèle’s case was the first time I knew of somebody else who had been raped while they were drugged,” says Stanhope. “I remember feeling: ‘Thank God I’m not alone. There’s one other person in the world that this has happened to.’” Stanhope’s former partner, David Rogerson,was under investigation at the time for raping her while she slept, and filming the abuse. Pelicot’s husband, Dominique, would end up being convicted for drug-facilitated rape and sexual assault (DFSA), along with 50 strangers he had recruited online to abuse his wife.In Exmouth, Devon, Zoe Watts was also following the Pelicot case. Her ex-husband, the father of her four children, was already serving an 11-year sentence for drugging and raping Watts over a number of years. “I’d never heard of it before and I absolutely did feel as if it was only me,” she says. “Gisèle was like fresh air. You know that feeling when you come into a house and open up all the doors and windows and let the air and the light come through? Well, drug-facilitated rape is one of those doors she opened.”The shock and horror that surrounded the Pelicot case was shot through with the unspoken sense that this was a truly abhorrent one-off, a monstrous anomaly. Stanhope and Watts knew then that it wasn’t – and now we all do. Just last week, in abrief hearingat Northampton crown court, a sobbing man in his 40s pleaded guilty to filming himself and another “person unknown” raping and sexually assaulting his partner as she was allegedly drugged or asleep. These attacks, which took place over an 11-year period, included tying her arms and legs, writing on her body and penetrating her with objects.Earlier this year, in a separate case, the formerSwindon councillor Philip Youngpleaded guilty to a string of sexual offences, including multiple counts of rape against his former wife, Joanne Young, over a 13-year period, administering a substance with intent to stupefy and voyeurism. In September,another case will reach court, this one involving 14 men who stand accused of the drugging and raping of one of their wives. The husband is a Stockport man in his 60s. The others, aged from 28 to 73, include a taxi driver, recruitment consultant, paramedic, university technician and delivery driver.It’s not just in the UK, of course.A case in Germanyrecently resulted in the conviction of four men on charges relating to the drugging and raping of women. They belonged to a Telegram group called “German driving school for experts”, where members shared footage of their abuse and exchanged tips on how to carry it out using coded words (women were “cars”, sedatives were “fuel” and rape was “driving”).In the summer of 2023, a pair of German investigative journalists, Isabell Beer and Isabel Ströh, working for Norddeutscher Rundfunk (NDR) and Funk, uncovered another network of men, who were using the porn site Motherless to connect, exchange advice, perform and share videos and photos of DFSA. One had been uploading footage of his sedated wife being raped, abused and sexually assaulted over a period of almost two decades, with no consequences. (“I’m just about to slip the bitch something, lets see what goes down tonight,” he bragged on one thread. “I gave her a few hard slaps in the face and she didn’t even notice,” he said in another.)View image in fullscreen‘She was like fresh air’ ... Gisèle Pelicot.Photograph: Yoan Valat/EPAIt took repeated pressure from Beer and Ströh before German police took action and intervened. The wife was in shock – she initially thought the police must have come to the wrong address. She’d previously consulted doctors about her long, deep sleeps, but certainly never suspected this. Later she said she “loved and trusted” her husband and that they had been through “thick and thin”. (He died before the case reached trial.)Further investigationinto Motherless and its associated DFSA networks by CNN led to the arrest of a man in Poland. In April this year,Project Medusawas launched – a collaborative effort to dismantle DFSA networks, led by Germany and the UK, with participation from Brazil, Canada, France, Hungary, the Netherlands, Spain, the US and Europol. To date, 156 perpetrators and victims have been identified.Watts, 43, and Stanhope, 53, were brought together by their involvement in the CNN report. It was this that inspired Watts to found asurvivor-led campaign group,the first of its kind. Stanhope has joined as an adviser. Its name, #EndEyeCheck, refers to the practice by perpetrators of pulling open the eyelid of an unconscious victim to ensure they are sedated (something that is frequently filmed and shared online). Watts has set out multiple objectives. These include education on DFSA in schools; an inquiry into its policing and prosecution; greater awareness among medical professionals, in order to spot the signs victims commonly present with; and the banning of “sleep content” from porn sites.“The first step, though, has to be gathering information,” says Watts. “Until we understand the scale and magnitude, no one is going to act.” There is little reliable data on DFSA – partly because so few cases are detected or prosecuted – but the online survey on the #EndEyeCheck site has now been completed by more than 400 people. Survivors who have contacted the group include women in their early 20s through to the oldest, who is 70, and still recovering from something that happened to her three decades ago.For these women, finding each other, sharing experiences, picking out patterns and parallels, has been life-changing after so much isolation. “This crime really dissociates you from understanding it, from making sense of it; there are so many gaps,” says Watts. “You can’t sit in a group with other women who have post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), because you have no memories. There are no dedicated facilities or support services. It’s a silent crime, just like domestic violence was 20 years ago. Now, with finding other survivors, putting the puzzle together, every day’s a school day.”Watts met her husband when she was 17 and he was 28. “He was churchgoing and everyone’s friend. He seemed to have integrity and morals,” she says. They went on to marry, had four children and worked together, running a printing business.Watts only learned he had been adding sedatives to her evening cup of tea, raping her and filming the abuse, because he told her in February 2018. By then, they had been together for 18 years and married for 14. It was a dramatic “confession” after a St Valentine’s Day church service about love, and his delivery downplayed it all. He said he had been putting medication in her tea and “having sex” with her afterwards, and that he’d taken photos because he thought she’d rather he look at her images than other women on porn sites. At the same time, he told her he’d had an affair with her friend when Watts had been pregnant with their second child, years before. The affair was something Watts “understood” immediately, and at first she focused on that. It was six months before she contacted the police to report him for drugging and raping her.“You don’t understand what you’re hearing, because, until then, I didn’t even know something like this was a possibility,” says Watts. “I’d been telling my daughters how to get home safely at night. I’d not been telling them that the person they’ve already met and fallen in love with might drug and rape them.View image in fullscreen‘You live in confusion for years because nothing makes sense’ ... Amanda Stanhope.Photograph: Florence Law/The Guardian“The experience of DFSA is different to an alleyway rape,” she continues. “Neither is ‘worse’, it’s not a competition, but this type of crime alters your whole reality. It’s not as if I’d experienced an attack, then come back to the safety of my home and comfort of my husband. It’s a nuclear bomb within the family unit. You question every conversation, every interaction. The person you thought you married, that you’d planned to spend the next 60 years with, is not that person any more. The bed you thought was safe, isn’t – it hasn’t ever been.”For Watts, raising awareness and recognising the signs is vital. “There were definitely signs, but I didn’t understand them,” she says. “One was recurrent urinary infections. Thrush. Tiredness. Nausea. Confusion. Migraines. At one point I thought I had a brain tumour. My body just wasn’t well.” All the survivors they’ve heard from had seen doctors at some point, for issues including memory loss, anxiety, confusion, altered sleeping habits – always with their partner sitting right beside them. In Watts’s case, though, in the years before her husband’s “confession”, various medical professionals had been told directly what was happening. She’d once woken to find her husband “having sex” with her, and he had acted as if he was as shocked and devastated as she was. “He said he didn’t know what he’d done, he wasn’t aware he’d been doing it and he was going to see a doctor,” says Watts. He did see doctors, more than once – Watts went with him to one appointment. He explained that he was having sex with Watts at night, when she was asleep, and he didn’t know why. (He didn’t mention that he was also sedating her.) On one occasion, he was prescribed antidepressants. On another, he was advised to go to bed wearing trousers and a belt.All this, says Watts, is stark proof of our failure to understand the crime. “He had told people, including non-medical professionals, about this thing he’d been doing and how terrible it was, and how bad he felt,” she says. “He framed it in such a way that none of them deemed it a crime. There is definitely a belief that if you were asleep, if you didn’t say no, or fight back, it doesn’t really matter, it wasn’t rape and where’s the trauma? You’re married to him, sleeping beside him, you had sex with him anyway so it doesn’t count. Pelicot’s case was horrifying because it didn’t ‘just’ involve her husband – there were 50 other men. I honestly believe that if it had ‘just’ been her husband doing this, the world wouldn’t have been following it. No one would have given a shit.”The police took Watts seriously from the beginning. “In my case, they were brilliant,” she says. “I still wasn’t sure if it was rape. They said: ‘Zoe, you can’t consent if you’re sedated.’” Still, the CPS initially ruled against charging him, despite the evidence in his medical records. Watts challenged the decision. “The CPS were getting 10 emails from me a day,” she says. Even when he was charged, her husband was not deemed a risk to women, and spent four years before the trial living close by. “He was dating, and even got a job in a recovery centre,” she says. “So he was working with vulnerable women and drugs.” In 2022, he was found guilty of rape, assault by penetration and administering a substance to overpower Watts. “Even then, there were people in town taking his side,” she says. “They said: ‘You must really hate him to have done that.’”View image in fullscreenA court artist sketch of Philip Young, who pleaded guilty to multiple counts of rape against his former wife.Photograph: Elizabeth Cook/PAStanhope, a hypnotherapist, was also failed by many of those around her. She met Rogerson in 2019 at a dance event and was swept off her feet. The relationship moved fast. But soon, his jealousy, his rages – always followed by his desperate pleas for forgiveness – were taking a toll. Stanhope was prescribed antidepressants, beta blockers and sleeping pills.She doesn’t know when Rogerson began raping her while she slept. Looking back, she can see the signs. “I’d wake up and there would be a towel underneath me,” she says. “I thought I’d gone to bed wearing pyjamas, but I wouldn’t be wearing them in the morning.” When she questioned this, Rogerson was always ready with an answer. “It was gaslighting,” she says. “He’d say different things. If I asked why a towel was there, he might say, ‘You put that there, don’t you remember?’ or he’d laugh and say: ‘You’re on so much medication, you’re losing your mind.’ Other times, he’d say: ‘Don’t you remember? You had a great time last night!’ I’d rack my brains to understand it. You live in confusion for years because nothing makes sense.”Few would leap straight to a conclusion of DFSA. But perhaps more of us should. Instead, Stanhope went back to the doctor. “For two years, I was certain I had Alzheimer’s disease,” she says, “because of the confusion, the memory loss.” Stanhope was referred to a psychiatrist and a neurologist. “It gets worse,” she says. “I was diagnosed with pseudodementia. Basically, it meant that I had all the symptoms of dementia, but it wasn’t real. They suggested it was ‘mental health’.” The “solution” was more medication. “They added antipsychotics, more sedatives, and later, lithium,” she says. “If they’d only asked the right questions: ‘How is your relationship? What are things like at home?’ Maybe it could have been spotted. Instead, I was completely drugged up. It turns out that when the relationship was over, when the abuse stopped, I didn’t need any of the medication. I don’t take anything now.“So many people have said to me, ‘How could you not know?’” she continues. “It used to make me so angry. Now, I think unless you’ve been in that situation, unless you’re there, taking these medications that are keeping you sedated, and living with a pathological liar, you can’t begin to understand.”There is something else that kept Stanhope unaware – and she only grasped this recently after hearing it from so many other survivors. “I’ve been asked in the past, ‘Don’t you physically know that you’ve had sex when you wake up?’” she says. “But so many of us have experienced the same thing. Our partners usually made sure they had sex with us earlier in the evening – so when you wake up, knowing you’ve had sex, you think it was that.”View image in fullscreenStanhope and Watts take their #EndEyeCheck campaign to Westminster.Photograph: Courtesy of Amanda StanhopeRogerson’s crimes came to light only because Stanhope sunk so low she wanted to die. She took an overdose and, though Rogerson dialled 999, he raped her unconscious body while waiting for the ambulance. As he was raping her, she came to. Afterwards, Stanhope did manage to end the relationship, but when he threatened to kill himself, she was persuaded to let him sleep on the sofa while he got his life together. Again, she woke to find him raping her. It was then that she went to the police.Their subsequent investigation found that Rogerson had filmed one assault and posted photos of Stanhope online. In April last year, he was charged with five counts of rape between 2021 and 2023, although none were related to DFSA, as Stanhope had knowingly taken the medications herself. Six weeks later, Rogerson took his own life.She is still struggling to understand all this. She believes pornography played a large part in what happened. “He was very porn oriented,” she says. “He used to spend endless time with his phone in the bathroom. It’s interesting, because there are things that I’ve been too embarrassed to say until recently. It’s only since connecting with other survivors this year, that we’re realising it was the same for all of us. It’s really strange, but lots of these men, including him, had an attraction toswinging websitesand had asked us to try it. I’d said no. The police investigation showed that he’d uploaded my photos to the swinging sites without my knowledge.”DFSA wasn’t created by pornography. Gwen Seabourne, a professor who specialises in medieval legal history, has highlighted a case from 1292 where a doctor in south-west England drugged and raped a patient. In Victorian Britain, DFSA was the subject of a social outcry, revolving around alcohol, chloroform, and “knockout drops” (a sedative called chloral hydrate), all unregulated and easily available. “There are always going to be men who rape and control and have that entitlement,” says Stanhope. “The oldest survivor to have contacted us experienced it decades ago – but I believe that online porn has accelerated it massively.”“Sleep content” isn’t hidden in dark corners. In the German investigative report on Motherless, search terms such as “passed out”, “drugged” and “raped” delivered tens of thousands of videos, images and tutorials. Motherless advertises itself as a “moral-free host” – but this content can be found on multiple sites. Previous research on Pornhub and other adult sites has found thatone in eight videoson its landing page depicts something illegal.Is there a kind of emboldening effect at work? What once might have been a fleeting thought, a fantasy, can now, with little effort, become a focus, then a fixation, then, finally, a shared pursuit. “It’s been normalised, it’s almost acceptable,” says Stanhope. “I’ve seen the messages these men share, congratulating and praising each other and giving each other advice. That’s all part of it. It’s not exactly a friendship, but it’s a shared space, a common interest, built around something so appalling. It’s diabolical.”View image in fullscreenFinding one another has helped Stanhope, left, and Watts to see the crime clearly.Photograph: Family handoutThe Norwegian psychologist Svein Overland has pointed to the similarities between DFSA and the consumption of pornography itself. In some ways, DFSA is pornography’s logical extension. Pornography is “one-way sex”, with no obligation or expectation to please, perform or interact with a partner. DFSA also eliminates a partner by rendering them absent, a passive object, a receptacle. The philosopher Kate Manne, the author of Entitled: How Male Privilege Hurts Women, believes DFSA is more than this. It’s a means to evade shame, a literal “closing her eyes to his disgrace”. Would the sobbing man tried in Northampton crown court last week have written on his wife’s body and tied her up if she could have seen him? DFSA is the “stealing of her consciousness, her vigilance, her wakefulness”,writes Manne. “Drug her; shut her up; and not only silence her but evade the ignominy of her eyes on you.”Prosecutions are rare and difficult and reliable statistics don’t yet exist. In thecrime surveyfor England and Wales ending March 2025, almost a quarter of victims were either unconscious or asleep during the most recent incident of sexual assault by rape or penetration. “It’s a silent crime that can go undetected for years,” says Watts. “Unless you stumble across the video evidence, what do you have to prove it happened? And even when you do have images, you might be told there’s no criminal case. How can you prove you didn’t consent to them?” For Watts, the answer starts with raising awareness and vigilance – shedding light on the crime and its common patterns.When it comes to DFSA’s online “communities”, we already have the tools to dismantle them, says Clare McGlynn, a professor of law at Durham University and the author of Exposed: The Rise of Extreme Porn and How We Fight Back. “These acts are clearly criminal offences,” she says. “It’s unlawful to drug and rape someone. It’s also a criminal offence to record and film nonconsensual sexual activity and share it without consent. And it’s also an offence to possess what’s termed ‘extreme pornography’, and that includes videos of rape, whether acted or real.”Again, the challenge lies in enforcement. “First, the police need to take reports seriously and even when they do investigate, users are so often anonymous, and in different jurisdictions – but those challenges aren’t insurmountable.”Regulating the platforms that host this material is crucial – and the Online Safety Act (OSA) applies to any platforms with links in the UK, regardless of where they are based. Under the OSA, they are obliged to prevent people from encountering “priority criminal offences” – such as rape and DFSA – and remove it when notified. “We need a regulator that’s swift and proactive,” says McGlynn. “Maybe there should be a precautionary approach that allows a regulator to take something down and then investigate.”When it comes to pornography platforms, Ofcom has not yet taken any action in relation to content, only age verification. An Ofcom spokesperson responded to questions around regulation of sleep content with the following statement: “The Online Safety Act imposes strict duties on firms to take proactive steps to assess and mitigate the risk of their services being used to commit or facilitate criminal offences. We are actively assessing a number of platforms’ compliance with these duties and have been working closely with law enforcement agencies to receive intelligence and support our evidence gathering. We are determined to act in cases where failings are identified, while also being mindful of the need to protect the integrity of ongoing criminal investigations.”double quotation markSleep doesn’t exist for me any more and what do I do? Take sleeping pills?Watts has chosen not to focus on the fact that images of her being raped by her husband while sedated are probably available online. “It’s a control thing,” she says. “I don’t like the thought of him being in prison and still controlling my feelings, my emotions, and my body. I have to choose where I invest my energy. I’ve already had years of feeling so disabled by what this man has done to me.”For Watts and Stanhope, recovery is lifelong. “Sleep doesn’t exist for me any more and what do I do? Take sleeping pills?” asks Watts. “I’m what you call a ‘night owl’ and I don’t think I’ll ever get peace with that. There are certain things I don’t feel safe with – even being in the car, I lock myself in. I don’t trust myself in making decisions any more, because I’ve got them so wrong in the past.”“Nobody realises the impact,” says Stanhope. “It took me a year to even be able to say the word ‘rape’. I’ve been reliving it with PTSD, I’ve had nightmares, flashbacks to those moments I woke up while it was happening. It hits you in waves. You think you’re over something and then you remember something else. I’ve had so much therapy, it’s untrue. I’ve had weekly therapy, trauma EMDR therapy, I’ve had CBT and group therapy. For two years, therapy was my life.”What has helped her the most has been #EndEyeCheck. “This has been so validating,” she says. “It’s exhausting, but I think it has healed me. I can’t dwell on my own pain when I’m focused on the bigger challenges.”Finding one another has helped them to see the crime clearly. “For so long, no friend, no doctor, no one I’d spoken to seemed to think I was being raped,” says Watts. “I’d been given the message that he was my husband, I couldn’t remember it, so there was no trauma. A lot of the victims feel like a bit of a fraud.” Meeting Stanhope changed all that. Earlier this year, the two of them attended a lobbying event in parliament. They spent the night before in a shared hotel room.“I saw Amanda getting ready for bed, and as usual, I couldn’t sleep,” says Watts. “In the middle of the night, I Iooked at her asleep and, bless her, I just thought, ‘How the hell?’ I started crying, because I suddenly saw how vulnerable we are when we are sleeping at night – and how abused we had been. And that must have been me. It hit me on a different level.”Eventually, Watts got to sleep, and the following day was full-on. She didn’t mention what had happened until the very end. “We were going to get some food and I said: ‘Amanda, I need to tell you that I was crying in the night, because I saw you asleep and I broke.’ She turned to me, and said: ‘It happened to me, too. I thought the same thing.’”Information and support for anyone affected by rape or sexual abuse issues is available from the following organisations. In the UK,Rape Crisisoffers support on 0808 500 2222 in England and Wales, 0808 801 0302 inScotland, or 0800 0246 991 inNorthern Ireland. In the US,Rainnoffers support on 800-656-4673. In Australia, support is available at1800Respect(1800 737 732). Other international helplines can be found atibiblio.org/rcip/internl.html.In the UK and Ireland,Samaritanscan be contacted on freephone 116 123. In the US, you can call or text the988 Suicide & Crisis Lifelineat 988 or chat at988lifeline.org. In Australia, the crisis support serviceLifelineis 13 11 14. Other international helplines can be found atbefrienders.orgExplore more on these topicsRape and sexual assaultWomenGisèle PelicotGisèle Pelicot rape trialCrimePornographyfeaturesShareReuse this content
