title: Canada cancels joint bridge-opening celebration with US amid trade tensions | US news | The Guardian
description: Trump’s 50% tariff threat means there will be no ribbon-cutting celebration of Gordie Howe International Bridge
Author: Olivia Bowden
Headline: Trump’s 50% tariff threat means there will be no ribbon-cutting celebration of Gordie Howe International Bridge
Content: With the tariff announcement, Donald Trump has escalated tensions with one of the country’s closest allies.Photograph: Rebecca Cook/ReutersView image in fullscreenWith the tariff announcement, Donald Trump has escalated tensions with one of the country’s closest allies.Photograph: Rebecca Cook/ReutersUS newsCanada cancels joint bridge-opening celebration with US amid trade tensionsTrump’s 50% tariff threat means there will be no ribbon-cutting celebration of Gordie Howe International BridgeOlivia Bowdenand agenciesWed 22 Jul 2026 12.35 EDTFirst published on Tue 21 Jul 2026 20.58 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA planned joint US-Canada ceremony on Friday celebrating the opening of the Gordie Howe International Bridge connecting Detroit and Windsor, Ontario, is no longer expected to take place afterDonald Trumpannounced a 50% tariff on most Canadian goods.In announcing the tariffs, the president accusedCanadaof unfairly discriminating against US automobiles, alcohol and dairy products, escalating tensions with one of the United States’ closest allies.“In light of trade action threatened by the United States earlier this week, it would be inappropriate to proceed with a celebratory event between the two countries,” said Jenna Ghassabeh, a spokesperson for Canada’s infrastructure minister, Gregor Robertson, in an emailed statement.The move came after US and Canadian officials reached an agreement earlier this month to open the Gordie Howe International Bridge after delays tied to disagreements between the two countries.Canada now plans to hold its own ceremony on Friday. It is now unclear whether the United States will hold a separate ceremony.Two US officials who spoke on the condition of anonymity to discuss internal deliberations said they still expected the bridge to open to traffic on 27 July but were less certain than they were before.“We remain committed to opening the bridge on July 27th, and to celebrating this milestone among Canadians on July 24th,” said Ghassabeh.The 1.5-mile-long (2.4km-long) bridge spans theDetroitRiver and connects Detroit with Windsor. A ribbon-cutting ceremony had initially been scheduled for 12 June but was abruptly postponed after officials said the United States and Canada were still working to resolve “outstanding issues”.Canada financed construction of the bridge under an agreement allowing it to recover the cost through toll revenue. The Trump administration had sought changes to that arrangement.The Mark Carney government had been tight-lipped on the details of the agreement. But late on Tuesday,it shared the terms of the dealafter criticism was levelled by members of parliament from opposition parties, who accused the government of being obfuscatory.The agreement states that Canada will share 50% of net bridge and crossing-related revenues with an economic development fund that is controlled by the US government.Those terms seem to contradict statements the prime minister had previously made about the deal. On 16 July, the prime minister said at an unrelated press conference that toll revenues would not be split until Canada was repaid.The project, negotiated under former Michigan governor Rick Snyder, has been under construction since 2018 and cost nearly $4.4bn.The bridge is named after the Canadian hockey great Gordie Howe, who spent 25 seasons with the Detroit Red Wings. It is expected to become another major economic artery between Canada and the United States.In February, Trump demanded in a social media post that Canada give the US government at least half ownership of the bridge and agree to other unspecified demands, part of his broader clashes with Canada over trade.Those tensions resurfaced this week. The White House said the tariffs would take effect in 30 days, leaving time for negotiations. Trump has previously delayed or backed away from some threatened import taxes.Carney said in a statement about the tariffs that his government believes in the “benefits of free and fair trade” and remains prepared to negotiate with the Trump administration.The tariffs could escalate into a broader trade war as Canada weighs how to respond.“If these tariffs proceed, Canada should respond tariff for tariff, dollar for dollar,” Ontario’s premier, Doug Ford, posted on social media.Explore more on these topicsUS newsCanadaDetroitAmericasDonald TrumpTariffsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Nicaragua’s congress announces ‘work plan’ to suspend elections | Nicaragua | The Guardian
description: US secretary of state says international community should ‘join forces’ against authoritarian Ortega regime
Author: Tiago Rogero
Headline: US secretary of state says international community should ‘join forces’ against authoritarian Ortega regime
Content: Nicaragua’s president, Daniel Ortega, and his ‘co-president’ wife, Rosario Murillo, have been accused of violating international human rights laws.Photograph: Cesar Perez/El 19 DIGITAL/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenNicaragua’s president, Daniel Ortega, and his ‘co-president’ wife, Rosario Murillo, have been accused of violating international human rights laws.Photograph: Cesar Perez/El 19 DIGITAL/AFP/Getty ImagesNicaraguaNicaragua’s congress announces ‘work plan’ to suspend electionsUS secretary of state says international community should ‘join forces’ against authoritarian Ortega regimeTiago RogeroSouth America correspondentWed 22 Jul 2026 09.33 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 10.51 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleNicaragua’s congress, controlled by the country’s long-serving president,Daniel Ortega, has announced a “work plan” to implement the authoritarian leader’s order that the country should no longer hold elections.Nicaragua’s president says country will not hold any more electionsRead moreThe UN and the US strongly condemnedSunday’s announcementby Ortega, who has been in power since 2007 and, after a controversial constitutional change last year, nowgoverns alongside his wife, Rosario Murillo, as “co-president”.During the ceremony marking the 47th anniversary of the 1979 Sandinista revolution, in which he helped overthrow the brutal dictatorship of Anastasio Somoza García, Ortega said he would work with congress to pass new laws that would “build a wall” against the opposition.On Tuesday, the Ortega-controlled National Assembly issued astatementsaying that, under the pretext of “ensuring peace, security, stability and the continuity of achievements in the fight against poverty”, it was “beginning the necessary analysis to draw up a work plan to implement the president’s directives”.View image in fullscreenCrowds gather in Managua, Nicaragua on Sunday to commemorate the 47th anniversary of the Sandinista revolution.Photograph: Cesar Perez/El 19 DIGITAL/AFP/Getty ImagesAlthough the Ortega-Murillo regime has already overseena profound erosion of democracy– marked by the violent suppression of protests, the imprisonment of thousands of people and the forced exile of nearly 1 million Nicaraguans – Sunday’s announcement marked a significant rhetorical escalation that is now being put into practice by congress.On Wednesday, the UN high commissioner for human rights, Volker Türk,called onthe Nicaraguan authorities to “reopen civic space, and restore the rule of law. People of all political viewpoints must be allowed to vote and run for office, in line with the state’s international human rights obligations.”It cost £2.1bn to divert a river in Brazil’s outback. So where has all the water gone?Read moreThe US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, who refers to the country’s government as the “Murillo-Ortega dictatorship”, said in astatementon Tuesday that “the Nicaraguan people have the right to choose their own leaders through democratic elections” and the US “calls on the international community to join forces” against the regime.Ortega’s uninterrupted period of almost 20 years in office is not his first spell in power. He led the junta that took office after the Sandinista revolution and, in 1984, was elected president, remaining in office until 1990.His current term, to which he was elected after he outlawed opposition parties and jailed all opposition presidential hopefuls before the 2021 election, had been due to end next November. However, last year’s constitutional changes extended the presidential term from five to six years.Despite the National Assembly’s announcement, it remains unclear whether the 2027 elections will be scrapped altogether or whether Ortega and Murillo will run unopposed.Explore more on these topicsNicaraguaDaniel OrtegaHuman rightsAmericasnewsShareReuse this content

title: Sperm whales change ‘vowel’ sounds when boats are nearby, scientists discover | Cetaceans | The Guardian
description: Researchers listening off the Caribbean island of Dominica found variations in the mammals’ audible clicks when marine traffic was close
Author: Emma Bryce
Headline: Researchers listening off the Caribbean island of Dominica found variations in the mammals’ audible clicks when marine traffic was close
Content: Three sperm whales. In the latest research, in addition to the changing tempo of codas, the team identified differences in the prevalence of two vowel-like sounds in noisy conditions.Photograph: Project CetiView image in fullscreenThree sperm whales. In the latest research, in addition to the changing tempo of codas, the team identified differences in the prevalence of two vowel-like sounds in noisy conditions.Photograph: Project CetiSeascape: the state of our oceansCetaceansSperm whales change ‘vowel’ sounds when boats are nearby, scientists discoverResearchers listening off the Caribbean island of Dominica found variations in the mammals’ audible clicks when marine traffic was closeSeascape: the state of our oceans is supported byAbout this contentEmma BryceWed 22 Jul 2026 09.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 09.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleSperm whales produce a specific pattern of clicking noises when they hear the noise of boats, researchers have found. The pattern is so distinct that the scientists who identified it said they can now use it to predict when ships are nearby.Researchers fromProject Ceti(the Cetacean Translation Initiative), a non-profit research organisation working to decode sperm whale communication, spent four years tuning in on the calls of 15 sperm whales off the coast of Dominica.They discovered specific differences in sperm whale “codas”, the discrete and distinctive patterns of clicks that this species utters to communicate when those whales encountered shipping. Individual click sequences became shorter, and one type became more prominent than another. Their findings arepublishedin the journal Ecological Informatics.“This research begs the question: are whales talking about the ships, or is it an impact on their voices because the ships are around?,” said David Gruber, the founder and chief executive of Project Ceti. “And then if we’re able to discern what they’re saying about the ships, how might that impact policy and laws?”Under normal conditions, recordings from one of the whales, named “Atwood”, picked up one type of coda comprising five clicks in a 1-1-3 pattern (two clicks preceding three quicker ones), which, Gruber explained, is unique to this specific clan of sperm whales off theCaribbeancoast of Dominica. With the background hum of a ship, Atwood issued the same 1-1-3 click rhythm but noticeably faster, with the whale reducing the intervals between each click.Audio recordings of sperm whales communicatingThe association was so strong that “in general, from their vocalisations alone, we can predict whether ships are around,” said Gašper Beguš, linguistics lead at Project Ceti.For the study, Beguš and colleagues recorded more than 1,000 codas from the 15-strong sperm whale pod, using audio tags attached to whales by drones to be minimally invasive. Where background shipping noise was detected, it was verified using digital geolocation data to confirm that vessels were in the whale’s vicinity when the recording was made.Several studieshave shown that marine animalsincrease the volumeof their vocalisations when there is noise pollution, but the researchers were surprised not to see this trend in the sperm whales, finding that the creatures instead seemed to prioritise altering not just the speed but the types of codas they produced.This related to previous studies where Project Ceti researchers described the“use of vowels”in sperm whales. Communication among sperm whales has long been viewed as a series of morse code-like clicks, but in that work the team found that if the gaps between individual clicks in a coda sequence were condensed to speed up the recording, they formed a continuous unit of sound that resembled two human vowels – the sounds “aaaa” and “iiii”.View image in fullscreenResearchers from Project Ceti diving with a whale recording unit.Photograph: Project CetiIn the latest research, in addition to the changing coda tempo, the team identified differences in the prevalence of these two vowel-like sounds in noisy conditions, with sperm whales using the “aaaa” coda more frequently.“The study is actually one of the first glimpses that the vowels that we discovered might actually carry meaningful information. Because in the presence of shipping noise, they change the way they use those vowels,” Beguš said.Someresearchers have criticisedCeti’s previous studies,finding faultwith its methods and saying that describing whale communication using terms such as “vowels” and “language” stretches the associations between whale and human communication too far. Gruber says they try to address this concern by using terms such as “vowel-like” or “language-like”.‘Aural tattoos’: sperm whales use sounds to signal social identity, say scientistsRead moreNathan Merchant, an underwater noise specialist at the UK governmentmarine research agency Cefas, who was not involved in the research, said there could be other unknown factors driving the change in whale vocalisations aside from shipping noise. But he said his “hunch” was that the relationship was causal.A large body of work now showed changes in behaviour and call volume when marine animals encountered shipping noise, Merchant noted. “Something that marks this study out is that it shows changes to the fine-scale content of those calls,” he said. “It really adds another dimension of nuance to that story about how underwater noise can affect cetaceans.”He added that it would take more research to determine what these audible changes meant.“Are they [the whales] just struggling to say the same thing, or are they saying something different in the presence of this noise?” he said. “It’s a really intriguing question.”Explore more on these topicsCetaceansSeascape: the state of our oceansWhalesDominicaZoologyBiologyAmericasCaribbeannewsShareReuse this content

title: Trump announces new tariffs on generic drugs to take effect in 2028 – as it happened | US news | The Guardian
description: This live blog is now closed.
Author: Shrai Popat
Headline: This live blog is now closed.
Content: 19.14 EDTTrump announces still more tariffs, this time on generic drugsDonald Trump announced a new package of tariffs on Tuesday, this time on generic drugs made overseas, to begin in 2028.The announcement, ina social media post, came just three months after the president issueda proclamationsaying that generic pharmaceuticals “are not subject to tariffs at this time” but that decision “will be reassessed in one year.”Trump’s latest head-spinning reversal on tariff policy means, he said, that generic drugs will continue to be imported tariff-free until 1 August 2028, when a 100% tariff would be imposed “for a one year period of time, and 200% thereafter.” The goal of the policy, Trump said, was to coerce drug makers into building plants in the United States.Trump did not state what legal authority he was claiming to impose future tariffs on generic drugs, particularly the projected 200% tariff which would come into effect in August 2029, six months after the end of his final term.Share

title: British woman jailed in Hong Kong for blackmail after accusing banker of rape | Hong Kong | The Guardian
description: Isabel Rose, 26, sentenced to six years for blackmail and perverting course of public justice over her allegations against British man
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Isabel Rose, 26, sentenced to six years for blackmail and perverting course of public justice over her allegations against British man
Content: Isabel Rose, 26, travelled to Hong Kong after meeting the man on holiday in Thailand.Photograph: suppliedView image in fullscreenIsabel Rose, 26, travelled to Hong Kong after meeting the man on holiday in Thailand.Photograph: suppliedHong KongBritish woman jailed in Hong Kong for blackmail after accusing banker of rapeIsabel Rose, 26, sentenced to six years for blackmail and perverting course of public justice over her allegations against British manDanny VincentWed 22 Jul 2026 08.28 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA British woman who reported being raped to police inHong Konghas been sentenced to six years in prison for blackmail and perverting the course of public justice.Isabel Rose, 26, from south-eastLondon, travelled to Hong Kong in early 2024 to visit a British banker she had met while travelling in south-east Asia.“I came here with four days of clothes, not realising I would be here for two years,” Rose said while out on bail. “Somewhere along the line, quite early on, something went wrong.”The Hong Kong district judge Adriana Noelle Tse Ching said Rose had “not shown an iota of remorse” before handing down the sentence, and called the allegation “completely fabricated”.The events manager, who was first arrested in 2024, maintained throughout the proceedings that she was raped.Radd Seiger, the spokesperson for Rose, said: “Today is obviously a difficult day for Isabel and her mother, Aysha. We will now take advice from the legal team and consider all available options.”In court, Rose, dressed in a black jacket over a white top, looked across at supporters as the judge handed down the sentence. She appeared solemn and gestured to the public gallery as she was escorted out of the dock.The case centred on allegations that Rose demanded money from the British banker, who can not be named for legal reasons, after she accused him of raping her.Rose travelled to Hong Kong in January 2024 after meeting the banker on holiday in Thailand. They stayed in touch and he later invited her to Hong Kong and offered to pay for her flights.Rose said she was raped in his apartment about 12 hours after landing in Hong Kong and reported the allegation to police.Police initially arrested the man but later released him and arrested Rose. She was subsequently charged with blackmail and perverting the course of public justice.Forensic testing to establish the rape was inconclusive. In a 300-page judgment, the judge said she accepted the man’s account and rejected Rose’s evidence.The court heard that Rose had asked for £5,000 after the alleged sexual assault and later demanded £100,000 while threatening to report him to the police, claims Rose denied.Rose said the money was compensation that the man offered to cover flights, accommodation and medical costs. The judge said the escalating demands amounted to blackmail.Rose was released on bail after the arrest but was unable to leave Hong Kong for almost two years while awaiting trial. The family said they have spent tens of thousands of pounds trying to fight the case. Rose has maintained her innocence.View image in fullscreenAysha Bell, Isabel Rose’s mother, reported the alleged rape to the Metropolitan police in London. The Met said it was ‘unable to pursue relevant lines of enquiry’.Photograph: Fung Hoi Kin/The GuardianThe case also focused on WhatsApp messages exchanged between Rose and the man after the alleged assault.In one exchange, Rose wrote: “You violated me last night, I didn’t wanna have sex.”He responded: “I am deeply sorry. I was slightly drunk and I misread your signals, it’s no excuse and I know it doesn’t make it any better.”The man told the court he misunderstood Rose’s use of the word “violated” to refer to an emotional violation rather than an allegation of rape. He said he read the message in two parts.The judge said Rose had deleted some texts and used disappearing messages. She said the banker had been “nothing but kind to the defendant” and Rose had “taken advantage of his kindness, naivety, friendship and romantic interest”.Rejecting calls from Rose’s legal team for a lighter sentence, the judge described her conduct as “wicked” and “evil”.Campaigners said the case raised important questions about how post-sexual assault behaviour was assessed in court. They said unpredictable behaviour was evidence of trauma, not fabrication.Ngozi Fulani, the founder of the London charity Sistah Space, which supports women of African and Caribbean heritage affected by domestic violence and sexual abuse, said: “We live in a world where people think there is a structured way to respond to rape and sexual assault. That is not the case. What has happened here is that a victim has been blamed for being a victim.”Supporters cried as the judge handed down the sentence. Rose’s mother, Aysha Bell, who has spent much of the past two years in Hong Kong supporting her daughter, appeared teary-eyed in court. In 2024, Bell reported the alleged rape to theMetropolitan policein London. UK police can legally investigate sexual assault cases involving British nationals abroad.The Met opened a file, assigned a detective to the case and according to correspondence seen by the Guardian, named the banker as a suspect.A Met spokesperson said: “We take reports of this nature extremely seriously and remain committed to investigating all allegations thoroughly. However, we have been unable to pursue relevant lines of enquiry in relation to this particular allegation due to a lack of jurisdiction in the relevant country. Should circumstances change, we will pursue all relevant enquiries.”The Foreign Office said it was supporting a British woman in Hong Kong and was in contact with her family and the local authorities.Explore more on these topicsHong KongRape and sexual assaultMetropolitan policeLondonAsia PacificnewsShareReuse this content

title: Australia to warn China it won’t be bullied by ‘provocative‘ actions as it builds military and nuclear arsenal  | Australian politics | The Guardian
description: Foreign minister Penny Wong to deliver speech criticising China’s recent launch of a nuclear-capable submarine-launched missile
Author: Sarah Basford Canales
Headline: Foreign minister Penny Wong to deliver speech criticising China’s recent launch of a nuclear-capable submarine-launched missile
Content: Australian foreign minister Penny Wong met with her Chinese counterpart on Tuesday night on the sidelines of the Asean summit.Photograph: Lukas Coch/AAPView image in fullscreenAustralian foreign minister Penny Wong met with her Chinese counterpart on Tuesday night on the sidelines of the Asean summit.Photograph: Lukas Coch/AAPAustralian politicsAustralia to warn China it won’t be bullied by ‘provocative‘ actions as it builds military and nuclear arsenalForeign minister Penny Wong will deliver speech criticising China’s recent launch of a nuclear-capable submarine-launched missileGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastSarah Basford CanalesandLuca IttimaniWed 22 Jul 2026 07.40 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GooglePenny Wong will warn China that Australia and other neighbouring nations will not be bullied by its “provocative” actions, after its involvement in another “destabilising and dangerous” incident this week.The foreign affairs minister – who will deliver the address on Thursday in the Philippines, where the 59th Asean foreign minister’s meeting is being held – will also caution world leaders againstplacing a toll on the strait of Hormuzafter both Iran and the US threatened to do amid the protracted conflict.The speech comes as the crisis in the Middle East escalates once again, driving up fuel prices by 40 cents in three weeks amid dwindling global oil reserves.Off the back of China’s launch of anuclear-capable submarine-launched missile in the South China Seaearlier in July, Wong will say the test “came with too little information and notification”.“It comes in the context of China’s rapid military buildup, including of its nuclear arsenal, without the transparency or reassurance the region expects,” the foreign minister will say.“Which is why Australia has described the test as provocative and destabilising.”Wong met with China’s foreign minister, Wang Yi, on the sidelines of Asean on Tuesday night AEST in their 12th meeting.Wong had earlier criticised China’s conduct as “destabilising and dangerous” after the Philippine military accused the China Coast Guard of clubbing a Filipino sailor with a baton on Monday on a disputed shoal in the South China Sea.RBA now twice as likely to hike interest rates as US-Iran war drives fuel prices higherRead more“We will continue to register our concerns about China’s vessels engaging in destabilising and dangerous conduct in the South China Sea, including towards Philippine, Vietnamese, Malaysian, and Indonesian vessels,” Wong will say on Thursday.China’s long-range missile test in the South Pacific happened just hours after Australia signed a defence agreement with Fiji, incitingwidespread condemnation. The prime minister, Anthony Albanese,expressed concern at the missile’s long-range capabilitiesand the possibility to cause “considerable, considerable damage were it to be weaponised”.In Manila, Wong will also warn the region will suffer if the strait of Hormuz is “weaponised” and the US and Iran fail to maintain a ceasefire.Heavy strikes haveresumed between the warring nationswith the US launching consecutive rounds on Iran in an attempt to force the strait’s reopening.Iran’s president on Tuesday said the country is in a “full-scale war” after the US expanded its aerial campaign and Tehran struck Washington’s Gulf allies. The Iran-allied Houthis in Yemen have also announced a blockade of Saudi ports.The renewed outbreak of fighting has sent the international Brent crude benchmark surging by 23% over the past two weeks and back within reach of $US90 a barrel.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |When neededSign up toBreaking News AustraliaGet the most important news as it breaksPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe surge in cost has pushed diesel above 220 cents per litre and petrol above 187 cents in some Australian cities. Luke Yeaman, the Commonwealth Bank chief economist, has predicted the federal government may have to restore the fuel excise discount back to 32 cents.Wong will tell the audience much of the region was fuel- and energy-dependent on the strait of Hormuz, presenting a “grave challenge”.“Across south-east Asia, energy prices have spiked, costs for families and businesses increased, and we continue to see ongoing pressure on food production,” Wong will say.“The strait of Hormuz shows what we in this region have always understood. That people suffer when a strait is weaponised. And we all know that the costs of tolls on any chokepoint would not be solely economic.“Our people depend on straits and sea lanes remaining open.”Wong will tell Asean nations their assistance in plugging Australia’s supply and trade gaps during the conflict is for the region’s benefit.“Because what goes to Australia as fuel and fertiliser comes back to the region as food,” she will say.With additional reporting by Patrick ComminsExplore more on these topicsAustralian politicsChinaAsia PacificPenny WongAustralian foreign policynewsShareReuse this content

title: Michelin-starred restaurant owner in South Korea faces jail over ant dessert | South Korea | The Guardian
description: Prosecutors seek a one-year sentence and heavy fine after chef garnished dish with insects not approved for consumption
Author: Raphael Rashid
Headline: Prosecutors seek a one-year sentence and heavy fine after chef garnished dish with insects not approved for consumption
Content: The dried ants were reportedly used to add acidity to dishes.Photograph: Ministry of Food and Drug Safety KoreaView image in fullscreenThe dried ants were reportedly used to add acidity to dishes.Photograph: Ministry of Food and Drug Safety KoreaSouth KoreaMichelin-starred restaurant owner in South Korea faces jail over ant dessertProsecutors seek a one-year sentence and heavy fine after chef garnished dish with insects not approved for consumptionRaphael RashidWed 22 Jul 2026 05.26 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 15.03 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleProsecutors inSouth Koreahave asked a court to sentence the owner of a Michelin-starred restaurant to one year in prison for serving a dessert topped with ants, an insect not approved for consumption under local food safety law.The request, made on Monday, also sought a 20m won (£10,108) fine for the company that runs the restaurant, Yonhap news agencyreported.The restaurant’s owner and the company were indicted on charges of violating the country’s food sanitation act. The ministry of food and drug safety launched its investigation after spotting mentions of the ant dishes in blog posts and social media, rather than through a routine inspection.The restaurant, which holds two Michelin stars and is located in Seoul’s upmarket district of Gangnam, is reported to have served a sorbet dessert sprinkled with ants for customers who wanted the flavour.Under South Korean law, only 10 insect species, including grasshoppers, mealworms and silkworm pupae, are approved for use as food ingredients. Ants are not among them.Businesses wishing to use an unapproved insect must first obtain temporary approval, a process the restaurant did not follow, theministry said. Violating the law carries a maximum penalty of five years in prison or a 50m won fine.Prosecutors allege the restaurant imported dried ants from the US and Thailand from 2021 and served them on dishes for around four years, selling the ant-topped dessert roughly 12,200 times for total revenue of about 120m won (£60,647). Prosecutors estimated about 49,000 ants were used in total.The restaurant disputed that figure in court, arguing that only about 60% of customers accepted the ant topping when offered, meaning the prosecution’s estimate, based on all customers, was too high.They also noted that only a small number of dishes on their 15-course menu included ants, and that restaurants in countries including Denmark, the UK and Australia use ants as an ingredient.It wasreportedly explainedto prosecutors that the restaurant’s chef had used ants to add acidity to dishes while working in the US and Europe, and had not realised doing so was illegal in South Korea.Sentencing is scheduled for 2 September.Explore more on these topicsSouth KoreaAsia PacificRestaurantsFoodnewsShareReuse this content

title: Japan records its first ‘cruelly hot day’ as cities swelter in 40-degree heat | Japan | The Guardian
description: In April Japan coined term kokushobi for days above 40C as country advises workers to wear shorts to the office to cope with stifling heat
Author: Gavin Blair
Headline: In April Japan coined term kokushobi for days above 40C as country advises workers to wear shorts to the office to cope with stifling heat
Content: Japan’s meteorological agency coined the termkokushobi– or “cruelly hot day” to warn the public of dangers tied to extreme heat.Photograph: Nicolas Datiche/SIPA/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenJapan’s meteorological agency coined the termkokushobi– or “cruelly hot day” to warn the public of dangers tied to extreme heat.Photograph: Nicolas Datiche/SIPA/ShutterstockJapanJapan records its first ‘cruelly hot day’ as cities swelter in 40-degree heatIn April Japan coined new termkokushobifor days above 40C as country advises workers to wear shorts to the office to cope with stifling heatGavin Blairin TokyoTue 21 Jul 2026 23.48 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 23.49 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleJapan logged its first officialkokushobior “cruelly hot day” on Tuesday since the new term for when temperatures top 40 degrees Celsius was introduced in April. TheJapanMeteorological Agency coined the new designation to warn the public of the heat dangers of days that were once freak weather events but have now become more common.The central Japan cities of Gujo and Tajimi in Gifu prefecture, along with Toyota in Aichi prefecture, all hit 40C. Gujo’s 40C and Toyota’s 40.1C were all-time highs recorded at their respective weather stations.Alleged ice-cream cartel in Japan investigated as sweltering summer loomsRead moreAcross 914 weather stations nationwide, 278 registered temperatures exceeding 35C ormoshobi– “extremely hot days,” the highest level of warning until the recent introduction of the new terminology. Days over 25C are callednatsubior “summer days”, while those exceeding 30C are known asmanatsubi– “true summer days.”On Monday, a man in his 40s was found dead on a street in Sanjo, Niigata prefecture, while another in his 70s died in Kanagawa, southwest of Tokyo. Both are believed to have succumbed to the heat.Heatstroke deaths topped 2,000 for the first time in 2024, while more than 100,000 people were taken to hospital last year between the beginning of May and late September, according to the fire and disaster management agency.The rising heat is affecting many aspects of Japanese life – including yields and the quality of rice, a staple. In response, farmers are planting more heat-tolerant varieties, while some are switching to crops such as avocado that have not traditionally been grown at scale in Japan.Impacts are also being seen in sport, with the most high-profile being the restructuring of the hugely popular koshien national high school baseball tournament. Played in the sweltering heat of August, the games have been split into morning and evening sessions since 2024 to reduce the chances of heatstroke for both players and spectators.‘When I get home, my whole body aches’: extreme heat saps health and wages of India’s poorest workersRead moreMeanwhile, office workers are being encouraged to wear shorts on hot summer days, the latest relaxing of Japan’s buttoned-down corporate culture as part of “Cool Biz” measures to reduce electricity consumption through lower use of airconditioning. Previous initiatives have encouraged “salarymen” to shed jackets and ties, and wear short-sleeved shirts but this year’s expected scorching summer has prompted calls for another level of casualness.Tokyo governor Yuriko Koike announced the new clothing recommendations for office wear which also include polo shirts, T-shirts and trainers where appropriate, against the backdrop of expected high temperatures and a “challenging outlook for electricity supply”.A study by the National Institute for Environmental Studies (NIES) and Tokyo’s Waseda University in 2025 found that without drastic cuts to greenhouse gas emissions, school sports will become unplayable outdoors by the 2060s.Average summer temperatures in Japan have risen about 1.3C over the past 100 years, according to the Japan Meteorological Agency (JMA). Both 2023 and 2024 were the hottest years since records began in 1898, but last year saw those levels surpassed again. The summer of 2025 was 2.4C above the average of the previous three decades, with 132 weather stations logging their highest ever average heat and a new all-time Japanese high of 41.8C at Isesaki, Gunma, north of Tokyo, on 5 August.Explore more on these topicsJapanAsia PacificExtreme heatnewsShareReuse this content

title: Why are OpenAI and Anthropic cheering on regulation in Australia? The answer has global reach | AI (artificial intelligence) | The Guardian
description: The companies hope to follow in the footsteps of SpaceX, which raised $86bn and soared to a $2.1tn valuation after it listed on public markets in June
Author: Luca Ittimani
Headline: The companies hope to follow in the footsteps of SpaceX, which raised $86bn and soared to a $2.1tn valuation after it listed on public markets in June
Content: OpenAI and Anthropic lost billions in predicted value after being shown up by a Chinese startup.Photograph: Dado Ruvić/ReutersView image in fullscreenOpenAI and Anthropic lost billions in predicted value after being shown up by a Chinese startup.Photograph: Dado Ruvić/ReutersAI (artificial intelligence)AnalysisWhy are OpenAI and Anthropic cheering on regulation in Australia? The answer has global reachLuca IttimaniThe companies hope to follow in the footsteps of SpaceX, which raised $86bn and soared to a $2.1tn valuation after it listed on public markets in JuneGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastWed 22 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 20.23 EDTShareTop US AI developersAnthropicand OpenAI cheered when Australia announced it would set new AI rules.Big tech celebrating limits on their Silicon Valley VC-funded free-for-all might seem counterintuitive but there’s a much broader play than just what happens in one relatively small market.Anthropic andOpenAIaren’t even on sharemarkets yet but this week lost billions in predicted value after being shown up by a Chinese startup.Moonshot AI on Friday launched a new model, Kimi K3, advertising it as the first open-source model of its size, allowing more user modification than Anthropic’s Claude and OpenAI’s ChatGPT.Headaches for Silicon Valley as China chips away at the US’s lead in the AI raceRead moreAnalysts are judging K3 is competitive with both the leading US models, which are also less freely customisable. Moonshot had to temporarily pause new subscriptions after demand for the new model strained capacity.Implied values for Anthropic’s sharemarket debut have slumped US$232bn to $1.56tn from Friday to Tuesday on IG’s trading platform. OpenAI dropped US$160bn to $1.16tn.The two companies are still privately owned and valued at less than US$1tn each, so IG’s numbers are market bets, not true values.But they hope to follow in the footsteps of SpaceX, which raised $86bn and soared to a $2.1tn valuation after it listed on public markets in June.Anthropic has already begun registering to list on US sharemarkets, which would allow it to raise billions from new investors. The better the story it can tell, the more money it can raise.Chinese competition does not make for a good story. Nor do surprise lawsuits from unhappy creatives, such as the one Anthropic had to settle for US$1.5bn on Monday (Tuesday AEST).The company will pay authors about US$3,000 for each of an estimated 500,000 books covered by the settlement.One of the authors whose work Anthropic scraped was Andrew Charlton, Australia’s assistant technology minister. Charlton says the new regulation will mean Australian creatives can choose whether AI models are trained on their work and big tech companies will have to pay them.Yet those new rules could be a boon to big tech, Charlton says.“If we are upfront with what we expect in this Australian standard, we can give clarity to investors that in some ways enhances the attractiveness of Australia,” he told Sky News on Monday.Malik Ahmed Khan, an equity analyst at Morningstar Equity, says technology companies would find it much easier to invest in Australia as they learn where regulation is heading.View image in fullscreenAndrew Charlton says new Australian regulations will give clarity to investors.Photograph: Mick Tsikas/AAPThe companies will also have an easier job convincing investors to buy in at high prices if they can show they’re focusing on building AI and managing the risk of running afoul of governments or local laws, Khan says.While Australia is one of few countries to now be regulating AI directly, with little direct impact on companies based overseas, Anthony Albanese hopes to set the global tone. In his speech announcing the regulations, he cited Australia’s leadership on social media bans for under-16s as proof.Khan says more countries following Australia’s lead on AI could boost companies’ prospects and market values.“A positive deal in Australia could enable them to pursue similar deals across the globe,” he said.Avoiding a tech wreckRegulation would also give companies the prospect of favourable terms.Albanese signalled he would consult “closely” with industry and other countries to legislate regulations that would aim to enable AI’s arrival, not restrict it. OpenAI and Anthropic were quick to position themselves as partners ready to help shape the new approach.Dario Amodei, Anthropic’s chief executive and a former OpenAI vice-president, has already given an indication of what big tech thinks it should look like.In a June essay, he called for a coalition of democracies to dominate in AI and lock out China.While there are real national security concerns in this area, there’s also a healthy dose of self-interest.With the launch of Kimi’s K3, analysts believe it is increasingly likely that the US will block businesses from exclusively using Chinese-based models over national security fears.Man of his word: Pope Leo speeches declared human-authored by Australian AI detection toolRead moreAmodei also called for domestic regulation, asking governments to test, evaluate and even block AI models if they presented “unacceptable risks” – but with protections against local “political favoritism or arbitrary decisions”.Anthropic’s feud with the Trump administration has left it on a US supply chain blacklist. Days after Amodei published his essay, the US governmentforced Anthropic to suspend accessto its Mythos 5 models for foreign nationals.Anthropic’s critics have accused it of self-interest as it nears its initial public offering (IPO) on sharemarkets. The Pentagon’s Kirsten Davies said in June: “Some things are simply more important than revenue cycles, clickbait and pre-IPO valuation.”Yet Amodei’s warnings about security concerns have been echoed by Albanese and byFive Eyes intelligence agencies.Aruna Sathanapally, the CEO of Australia’s Grattan Institute, met the Anthropic chief when he visited Canberra in April. She said Amodei’s concerns seemed sincere.“He was seeing things within his own company that were giving him great concern about what Anthropic, as well as their competitors, may be capable of developing,” Sathanapally said.“Just because they have vested interests in the particular shape of regulation, it doesn’t mean that the calls for regulation themselves are not well founded. It just means … you need to keep alive to competitive neutrality in how you design it.”Explore more on these topicsAI (artificial intelligence)Australian politicsOpenAIAnthropicanalysisShareReuse this content

title: How will Australia’s emergency services cope if satellites go down? With a sextant and a street directory  | Australia news | The Guardian
description: Military and SES personnel being taught analog communication and navigation techniques as experts warn solar flare could knock out ‘critical space infrastructure’
Author: Tory Shepherd
Headline: Military and SES personnel being taught analog communication and navigation techniques as experts warn solar flare could knock out ‘critical space infrastructure’
Content: The defence force is already offering sextant training, so members can navigate by the stars, along with terrestrial navigation training.Photograph: Dino Fracchia/AlamyView image in fullscreenThe defence force is already offering sextant training, so members can navigate by the stars, along with terrestrial navigation training.Photograph: Dino Fracchia/AlamyAustralia newsHow will Australia’s emergency services cope if satellites go down? With a sextant and a street directoryMilitary and SES personnel being taught analog communication and navigation techniques as experts warn solar flare could knock out ‘critical space infrastructure’Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastTory ShepherdWed 22 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 21.52 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAustralia’s emergency services and military personnel are being trained to read paper maps, navigate without GPS support and sail by the stars in case something knocks out the nation’s space capabilities.A panel at the 19th AustralianSpaceForum in Adelaide on Wednesday was asked to consider what would happen “if we suddenly lost access to critical space infrastructure – no satellites, no GPS, no remote sensing, no secure communications?”Sign up for the Breaking News Australia emailThe experts warned that Australians are unaware just how dependent they are on space, and how vulnerable that connection is to enemy attacks and solar flares.It’s “not science fiction”, the Optus satellite and space systems head, Nick Leake, said.He described ATM networks going down, emergency services losing their situational awareness, aircraft navigation degrading, Defence losing secure communications and remote communities being entirely cut off.Aviation, freight and energy grid communications all rely on satellites and financial systems could face “immediate cascading failure”, he said. People may only realise their service is being delivered by a satellite when they lose it.“Most Australians have no idea that space is part of their everyday life,” Leake said.‘This feels fragile’: how a satellite-smashing chain reaction could spiral out of controlRead more“This isn’t science fiction. This is how critical satellite infrastructure is. Australia has one of the highest per capita dependencies on satellite infrastructure in the world.“Right now, it’s largely unguarded.”Flinders University adjunct professor Jackie Craig AM is an expert in cyber and virtual warfare and volunteers in an incident management operations support with the South Australian state emergency service.She said emergency services are “increasingly dependent on increasingly vulnerable systems”.View image in fullscreenAustralians are not aware of how dependent they are on space and how vulnerable that connection is to solar flares, experts say.Photograph: Solar Dynamics Observatory/APShe said they now teach members to navigate with street directories and maps instead of phones – a once ubiquitous skill that has become lost in the age of Google Maps.“What we discovered is the young people of the digital revolution can’t actually use a UBD [street directory]. They’re also very poor at reading and using maps,” she said.“A lot of us can’t navigate.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |When neededSign up toBreaking News AustraliaGet the most important news as it breaksPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionShe said if the satellite systems went down emergency services would be “back to pencil and paper”, radios and hard copies of procedure manuals.Michael Murphy, the deputy chief executive of the Royal Flying Doctor Service SA/NT, said if satellite systems went dark tomorrow they would continue as normal – for about 24 hours, thanks to inbuilt contingencies and redundancies. Then, they would prioritise life-saving missions, relying on manual procedures and alternative communication methods.He said they could go “old school” with high frequency radio and use the bush telegraph to share information in an analogue way.“[And] we’ve got heaps of really good landmarks, the river systems, deserts, ranges, borders, water. If you’re familiar with the land, you’ll get around pretty well,” he said.The Australian Defence Force’s air commodore, Chris Robson, said planning for a loss of satellite support wasn’t “doomsday prepping” or a “scare tactic” but “something we need to be honest about”.“I don’t think the general public understands,” the space forces group and joint forces space component commander said.The defence force is alreadyoffering sextant training, so members can navigate by the stars, along with terrestrial navigation training. But Robson did not think it would be frequently used.“I don’t think that you will see too many people sitting on the bridge looking at a sextant,” he said.The biggest risk was not an enemy attack, he said, but “that big orange glowing thing that on any single day can have a hiccup and take out a significant portion of our space capabilities”.The panel discussed ways to make the network more resilient, including having satellite redundancy in multiple orbits, more Earth stations, and sovereign satellite capabilities, instead of relying on US companies Amazon and Starlink.Explore more on these topicsAustralia newsSpaceEmergency servicesSouth AustraliaAustralian militarynewsShareReuse this content

title: Great Barrier Reef microbiome mapped as researchers discover 500 bacterial species previously unknown to science | Great Barrier Reef | The Guardian
description: Research identifies 808,585 viral genomes belonging to what scientists estimate to be 362,802 different viruses
Author: Donna Lu
Headline: Research identifies 808,585 viral genomes belonging to what scientists estimate to be 362,802 different viruses
Content: Scientists have identified the vast communities of microbes that make up the Great Barrier Reef.Photograph: Jürgen Freund/WWF/PAView image in fullscreenScientists have identified the vast communities of microbes that make up the Great Barrier Reef.Photograph: Jürgen Freund/WWF/PAGreat Barrier ReefGreat Barrier Reef microbiome mapped as researchers discover 500 bacterial species previously unknown to scienceResearch identifies 808,585 viral genomes belonging to what scientists estimate to be 362,802 different virusesFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastDonna LuWed 22 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 16.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleMore than a decade after thehuman microbiomewas comprehensively mapped, scientists have now identified the vast communities of microbes that make up the Great Barrier Reef.Analysing DNA from seawater samples of 48 reefs, researchers have mapped the microbiome of theGreat Barrier Reef, documenting more than 360,000 distinct viruses as well as more than 500 new bacterial species not previously known to science.The research, published in the journalNature, was led by scientists at the University of Queensland and the Australian Institute of Marine Science (AIMS).Dr Yun Kit Yeoh, a senior research scientist at AIMS and a senior author of the paper, described microorganisms as a “very important part of the reef ecosystem” that underpinned marine food chains.Sign up for the Breaking News Australia email“Many of the microbes on the reef can photosynthesise … like plants, they can take light energy [and] can convert carbon dioxide into oxygen.“They are eaten by krill and other zooplankton which are then consumed by other animals.“We haven’t been able to really study a lot of them except for a select few that we can grow properly in the lab on petri dishes.”But recent technological advances have allowed scientists to sequence DNA taken from the environment, giving a fuller picture of these microscopic communities.The field – known as metagenomics – is akin to solving thousands of jigsaw puzzles simultaneously; a single drop of seawater can contain the DNA of thousands of different microbes.“We wouldn’t have been able to do this, say, 10 years ago, but fortunately computing power keeps increasing at an exponential rate,” said Prof Philip Hugenholtz, a microbiologist at the University ofQueenslandand a senior author on the paper.The research, which first began more than five years ago, identified 808,585 viral genomes belonging to what the researchers estimate to be 362,802 different viruses.The analysis also identified 5,283 bacterial and archaeal genomes representing 876 distinct species – 584 of which have never been documented before.Hugenholtz said those large figures were expected. “This is just how huge the microbial world is,” he said. “We know that everywhere we sequence … there’s always new viruses turning up.“I was a little bit surprised that we found over 500 new bacterial species.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |When neededSign up toBreaking News AustraliaGet the most important news as it breaksPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“The human gut and the marine ecosystem I would say are the two most sequenced ecosystems on the planet.”The discovery raises the question as to whether the species were unique to the Great Barrier Reef, or were yet to be discovered in other reef ecosystems, Hugenholtz added.The researchers believe the mapped microbiome could be used as a reef monitoring tool. “We can now use it to start exploring what makes a healthy reef system,” Yeoh said. “Then we can examine how these microbes respond to, say, changes … associated with climate change, with heat stress, bleaching, human activity, or other environmental perturbations.“Microbial communities can tell you an awful lot. Often you’ll see changes in the microbial populations before you see more visible changes [on the reef].”The microbes in a particular marine area can indicate whether fishing has occurred in a no-take zone, for example. They can also indicate whether heavy metal contamination has occurred.“It’s quite expensive to get a readout of heavy metals – it’s cheaper to get a readout of the changes that they have on microbial communities,” Hugenholtz said.The researchers used long-read sequencing technology, which enables large strands of DNA to be read at one time, without the need to break them into smaller fragments.Hugenholtz likens it to doing jigsaw puzzles with bigger pieces, “which means you’ll be able to put those jigsaw puzzles back together more easily”.Explore more on these topicsGreat Barrier ReefMarine lifeQueenslandnewsShareReuse this content

title: Zelenskyy under growing pressure to reinstate sacked defence minister  | Ukraine | The Guardian
description: Campaigners in Ukraine pledge to continue nationwide protests until Mykhailo Fedorov gets his job back
Author: Luke Harding
Headline: Campaigners in Ukraine pledge to continue nationwide protests until Mykhailo Fedorov gets his job back
Content: Volodymyr Zelenskyy with (from left) acting defence minister Yevhenii Khmara, Oleksandr Syrskyi and newly appointed commander-in-chief Mykhailo Drapatyi.Photograph: Ukrainian Presidential Press Service/ReutersView image in fullscreenVolodymyr Zelenskyy with (from left) acting defence minister Yevhenii Khmara, Oleksandr Syrskyi and newly appointed commander-in-chief Mykhailo Drapatyi.Photograph: Ukrainian Presidential Press Service/ReutersUkraineZelenskyy under growing pressure to reinstate sacked defence ministerCampaigners in Ukraine pledge to continue nationwide protests until Mykhailo Fedorov gets his job backLuke Hardingin DniproWed 22 Jul 2026 14.31 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 19.32 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleVolodymyr Zelenskyy was under growing pressure to reinstate his sacked defence minister on Wednesday, after campaigners said they would continue indefinite nationwide protests until Mykhailo Fedorov got his job back.On Tuesday, Zelenskyy bowed to public demands byfiring Ukraine’s commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi. He appointedMykhailo Drapatyi as the new head of the country’s armed forces, as part of a radical reshuffle of the top military command.But Zelenskyy’s attempts to defuse popular anger by offering Fedorov a new government role so far appear to have failed. Fedorov has reportedly turned down the post of deputy prime minister for technological development.Instead, he wants to be reinstated as defence minister, arguing that it is the only way of implementing his ambitious plan for modernising and reforming Ukraine’s army.The post is no longer vacant after Zelenskyy appointed a special forces commander, Yevhenii Khmara, as acting minister.View image in fullscreenMykhailo Fedorov was ousted as defence minister last week after just six months in the role.Photograph: Global Images Ukraine/Getty ImagesProtesters have called for a rally to be held on Friday evening outside Zelenskyy’s presidential office in Ivana Franka Square,Kyiv. The announcement – made by prominent veteran Dmytro Koziatynsky – suggests that Ukraine’s political crisis is likely to roll into a second week.“We should not forget about the second demand of the protest – to return Mykhailo Fedorov to the position of minister of defence,” Koziatynsky wrote on social media. He added: “We must do everything possible to ensure that the government hears and satisfies all the demands of society.”One senior army source told the Guardian that Zelenskyy faced a major dilemma. “If he reverses his decision, this would be a big victory for Fedorov. It would be humiliating for the president and reveal him to be weak. But if he doesn’t, the protests will keep going,” they said.In recent months Ukraine’s unmanned systems forces have carried out a series of long and mid-rangestrikes on Russian territory, as well as on occupied southern Ukraine and Crimea. Targets have included oil refineries and military depots, as well as ships and oil tankers in the Sea of Azov and Black Sea.View image in fullscreenProtesters in Kyiv had called for the dismissal of Oleksandr Syrskyi.Photograph: Alessio Mamo/The GuardianOvernight, Ukrainian drones hit warehouses belonging to theRussian online retailer Wildberries, sparking large fires in Krasnodar and Nevinnomyssk. The attacks were part of an ongoing campaign to cripple Russia’s economy, so that it can no longer fund its war.Zelenskyy held a meeting on Wednesday with his top generals. Afterwards, Drapatyi wrote on Facebook that he had been given instructions to “intensify offensive operations in all domains”.He said other tasks included planning operations behind enemy lines, developing new technologies, and building up the military’s capabilities.Drapatyi’s appointment has been met with widespread approval. Ukraine’s former president Petro Poroshenko said the army trusted him. “I know him as a strategically minded, brave, and decisive man. He cherishes people. The Ukrainian people place great hopes in him,” Poroshenko said.Mykhailo Drapatyi: Ukrainians hope new commander will end army’s ‘Soviet culture’Read moreOne high-ranking military figure also welcomed Drapatyi’s appointment but said he faced significant challenges. “It won’t be easy,” they predicted. One was the likelihood that Vladimir Putin will bring in some form of mobilisation after elections in September, flooding the battlefield with new Russian recruits.The crisis in Ukraine’s military leadership was triggered by a generational conflict between Fedorov, a 35-year-old tech-savvy moderniser, and Syrskyi, a 60-year-old Soviet-trained traditionalist. Last week Zelenskyy said he had tried to mediate between the two sides, before firing Fedorov as a last resort.In a valedictory post, Syrskyi defended his record, which includes defending Kyiv and Kharkiv in the early weeks of Russia’s 2022 invasion. He said Ukraine had retaken more than 700 sq km (270 sq miles) ​of Ukrainian territory this year and said that he had created a separate drone branch of the armed forces – the first of its kind in the world.“I am handing over to my successor an army that is not only holding the defence but is also on the offensive – with initiative, with structure, with people who know how to beat the enemy,” he said. He added that he sincerely hoped the offensive would continue, adding: “Everything is in place for this.”9:43Zelenskyy ousts Ukraine army chief after protests – The LatestAs part of his shake-up, Zelenskyy said ⁠major general ⁠Ihor ​Skybiuk would replace ⁠Andrii Hnatov as the head of ⁠the general ​staff. ‌The president described Skybiuk as a “highly experienced officer who defended Ukraine shoulder ‌to shoulder with the ​new commander-in-chief.”Russian bloggers have acknowledged that Drapatyi is a formidable opponent. “He’s smart, strong, and hates Russia,” one remarked.They also noted that he had risen from battalion commander to head of the armed forces – a career which is practically impossible in Russia, where promotions are decided on the basis of connections and “intrigue”.The Kremlin said on Wednesday that Drapatyi’s appointment would not alter the situation on the frontline.“I don’t think this will lead to any changes,” Putin’s spokesperson Dmitry Peskov said, adding that Moscow has no intention of stopping its full-scale offensive, which has lasted for nearly four and a half years.Explore more on these topicsUkraineVolodymyr ZelenskyyRussiaKyivEuropenewsShareReuse this content

title: France records more than 5,700 excess deaths during June heatwave | France | The Guardian
description: Figures come as large parts of southern Europe endure another record-breaking period of extreme temperatures
Author: Jon Henley
Headline: Figures come as large parts of southern Europe endure another record-breaking period of extreme temperatures
Content: A French firefighter responds to a flare-up to prevent the fire from reigniting in the Fontainebleau Forest, in Noisy-sur-Ecole, France.Photograph: Simon Wohlfahrt/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenA French firefighter responds to a flare-up to prevent the fire from reigniting in the Fontainebleau Forest, in Noisy-sur-Ecole, France.Photograph: Simon Wohlfahrt/AFP/Getty ImagesFranceFrance records more than 5,700 excess deaths during June heatwaveFigures come as large parts of southern Europe endure another record-breaking period of extreme temperaturesJon Henley, Europe correpondentWed 22 Jul 2026 11.34 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 18.52 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFrance recorded more than 5,700 excess deaths during its June heatwave, authorities have said, as swathes of southernEuropecontinue to endure another record-breaking period of extreme temperatures.The French national health agency said on Wednesday that it had recorded 5,764 excess deaths ⁠between ​17 June and 2 July, an excess mortality rate of 36%.More than half of the “unprecedented” total were recorded between 25 and 27 June, with deaths of people aged ⁠75 and older accounting for about two-thirds ​of ​the excess mortality, the agency said.View image in fullscreenVisitors use umbrellas to shield themselves from the sun during a heatwave as the Arc de Triomphe in Paris at the height of the heatwave.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesThose who lost their lives between 17 June and 2 July died of “all causes”, none yet clearly linked to the heat, it said.Francereported slightly fewer excess deaths – 5,722 – over the whole summer last year.The announcement came as firefighters inSpainbattled one of the country’s largest ever wildfires, which has burned about 32,000 hectares (79,000 acres) in the Guadalajara region north of Madrid, forcing the evacuation of 1,200 people from 34 villages in the sparsely populated area and confinement orders in 14 others.Authorities said on Wednesday that the fire was beginning to stabilise, and residents of five villages were allowed to return home. The prime minister, Pedro Sánchez, said on a visit to the area that Spain had already suffered 22 major wildfires so far this year, four times more than in the same period last year.View image in fullscreenAdult emergency services at the Paris-Saclay Hospital during the heatwave in Paris.Photograph: Romuald Meigneux/SIPA/ShutterstockMore than 100,000 hectares had burned, the same as the whole-year average over the past decade, he said, adding that more must be done to prevent fires. “We must raise public awareness and not trivialise the climate emergency,” he said.Spain’s national weather agency, Aemet, said the danger of wildfires remained “very high or extreme” and was expected to increase further in the coming days. It urged people to exercise extreme caution amid highs of 42-44C and a dry westerly wind.View image in fullscreenA firefighter works to contain part of the Guadalajara wildfire.Photograph: UME/AFP/Getty ImagesEurope is the world’s fastest-warming continent and scientists say human-driven climate change is increasing the frequency and intensity of heatwaves, droughts and wildfires, while also creating conditions for sudden, destructive storms.The Guadalajara fire comes less than two weeks aftera deadly blazedevastated a small expatriate community in southern Spain, killing 13 peopleincluding several foreignersin one of the country’s deadliest wildfires on record.Spain’s ecological transition minister, Sara Aagesen, described the Guadalajara blaze as “one of the largest in the recent history of our country”, with data showing it was exceeded only by the 37,000 hectares burned by a fire in north-west Spain last year.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThis is EuropeThe most pressing stories and debates for Europeans – from identity to economics to the environmentPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion‘It’s only going to get worse’: wildfires forcing firefighters to make impossible choicesRead moreIn France, where ​two firefighters died in their vehicle in the south-western region of Gironde on Tuesday, authorities urged people to avoid using barbecues in areas experiencing heatwaves, saying nine out of 10 fires started as a result of human activity.A wildfire has burned through 2,500 hectares in the south-eastern Var department, forcing 400 people to flee. “The situation has been very difficult, 1,100 firefighters fought all night,” William Vogl, a spokesperson for the Var firefighters, said.View image in fullscreenA house is engufled in flames in Cotignac, southeastern France.Photograph: Thibaud Moritz/AFP/Getty ImagesIn Greece, ​where temperatures were forecast to top 41C, authorities ​banned outdoor work at peak midday sun. In Skopje, the capital ofNorth Macedonia, 11 people were admitted ‌to hospital, three with serious injuries, after a severe storm on Tuesday.InItaly, several fires burned across Sicily on Wednesday, prompting scattered evacuations. The country’s civil protection agency has declared all of the island at the highest risk for wildfires, with high winds and 40C-plus temperatures fuelling several blazes.Reuters and Associated Press contributed to this reportExplore more on these topicsFranceSpainExtreme heatExtreme weatherWildfiresClimate crisisEuropenewsShareReuse this content

title: ​Andy Burnham’s ​domestic ​revolution ​could ​unobtrusively pull Britain ​closer to Europe | World news | The Guardian
description: The new PM talks less about the continent than his predecessor, but his agenda is more closely aligned with the EU’s
Author: Jon Henley
Headline: The new PM talks less about the continent than his predecessor, but his agenda is more closely aligned with the EU’s
Content: Andy Burnham appears more sympathetic towards the EU than Keir Starmer.Photograph: WPA/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham appears more sympathetic towards the EU than Keir Starmer.Photograph: WPA/Getty ImagesThis is EuropeWorld news​Andy Burnham’s ​domestic ​revolution ​could ​unobtrusively pull Britain ​closer to EuropeThe new PM talks less about the continent than his predecessor, but his agenda is more closely aligned with the EU’sDon’t get This Is Europe delivered to your inbox? Sign up hereJon HenleyWed 22 Jul 2026 10.40 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 11.15 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleEurope’s leaders queued up this week towelcome Andy Burnhamas Britain’s seventh prime minister since the Brexit vote – all expressing the hope that his tenure would continue the thaw in EU-UK relations initiated by his predecessor, Keir Starmer.Ursula von der Leyen, the European Commission chief, said she was looking forward to strengthening cross-Channel ties, adding that a “very good” first discussion had confirmed a common commitment to “bringing the EU and UK closer and closer”. France’s Emmanuel Macron said he hoped for a deeper relationship that gave “new momentum to the partnership” between the EU and the UK. Germany’s Friedrich Merz said he, too, was looking forward to forging an even closer bond.But what, realistically and concretely, can Europe expect from the Liverpudlian dubbed the “King of the North”? How might his approach to the bloc differ from Starmer’s much-trumpeted (and as often maligned) “reset” in post-Brexit relations?We know practically nothing about what Burnham’s foreign policy might look like: his priorities, asPaul Taylor noted, are famously domestic. We do know his baseline instincts are pro-European: he argued against the “isolation” of Brexit in 2016.Last year, he said he hoped Britain mightrejoin the EU during his lifetime. This year, campaigning for the byelection he had to fight and win in order to become PM, in a constituency estimated to have voted 65% leave, he carefullyclarified his position. Brexit “has been damaging”, he said, but, he added, “I also believe the last thing we should do right now is rerun those arguments.” His aim was to “unify the country”, not divide it further, so he was “not proposing that the UK considers rejoining the EU”.But even if his headline focus is on the UK, Burnham knows Britain’s “economic stagnation, declining relative living standards and latent social discontent” are at least partly down to the huge Brexit hit to UK trade and investment, Taylor wrote. “Even without the compelling security and geopolitical reasons for rapprochement with the EU,” he argued, “closer engagement with Britain’s main export market would be a no-brainer to deliver healthier growth.”But how exactly, given the immovable red lines laid down in the Labour party’slast election manifesto– no single market, no customs union, no freedom of movement – might Burnham go about it? Starmer’s longed-for “reset” was all about security cooperation and easing Brexit trade barriers. Ironically, Burnham’s focus on industrialisation, regionalism and economic sovereignty could ultimately bring the UK closer to the EU.Ina detailed analysisfor the UK in a Changing Europe thinktank, Joël Reland argued that Burnham’s growth agenda, centred on domestic devolution rather than diplomacy, meant negotiations with the EU may drop down the list of UK priorities. But while the new prime minister may speak a lot less about trade access, his vision for building the UK’s “sovereign capabilities” – in fields such as AI, energy, digital infrastructure – closely mirrors the EU’s own “strategic autonomy” drive.View image in fullscreenThe EU is pushing back against the prevalence of US big tech.Photograph: Thierry Monasse/Getty ImagesBurnham’s desire to reduce UK reliance on foreign-owned datacentres and US-centric tech policy aligns well with Brussels’ tech sovereignty package, perhaps leading to pragmatic UK-EU cooperation in areas such as critical minerals and cloud storage.In short, argued Reland, Burnham maysoundmarkedly less pro-European than his predecessor in 10 Downing St, “but he is formulating an economic and security agenda which could bring London into closer line with Brussels”. If this happens, UK-EU relations could see occasional leaders’ summits replaced, for example, with project-based re-engagement via platforms such as the EU’s committee of the Regions, and stronger support for the UK-EU joint parliamentary assembly.At theEuropean Council on Foreign Relations(ECFR) thinktank, senior policy fellow Anthony Dworkin likewise observed that Burnham was likely to ground foreign policy very much in local economic realities, in a way Starmer never did. In the short term, Dworkin argued, the new government’s focus will certainly be on completing the three EU-UK agreements that were due to signed at a UK-EU summit meeting on 22 July, but will now be held sometime in the autumn.AsJennifer Rankin explained, the two sides are due to sign deals on reducing paperwork and certification for fresh food; linking emissions trading systems; and an exchange scheme allowing 18- to 30-year-olds to study and work in the EU and UK.Longer term, the ECFR suggested, Burnham’s drive for regional regeneration – such as green manufacturing in the north – could connect directly to EU supply chain partnerships, structurally repositioning the UK alongside the EU in key sectors.Jill Rutter at theInstitute for Governmentobserved that Starmer had spent substantial political capital on warm rhetoric toward the EU, but his intended reset had delivered only modest economic returns due to Labour’s structural red lines. Burnham would face an early choice, Rutter said, over “how much personal effort he puts into pulling this reset over the line – and what price he is prepared to pay for that – as well as how much emphasis he puts on further rounds”.In short, Andy Burnham’s pragmatic domestic industrial goals naturally overlap with EU policy. Without shouting about it, by aligning the UK with the EU’s own priorities, he may just achieve a quieter, but far more effective UK-EU partnership.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThis is EuropeThe most pressing stories and debates for Europeans – from identity to economics to the environmentPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionWe’ll see.Call the DoctorTurns out if you spend nearly four decades writing European allsorts from Athens to Aarhus and Prague to Poitiers, eventually someone gives you a strange-looking hat.Last week, I was awarded an honorary doctorate by the University of London for my reporting on Europe. Personally, to be honoured for doing a job you love (well, most of the time) was, of course, doubly gratifying.But 10 years after the UK voted to leave the EU, it was also, I like to think, a nod to history’s biggest ever experiment in cooperation over conflict.Europe is, most certainly, not perfect. But the fact so many of you subscribe to this newsletter, and read the rest of the Guardian’s expanded European coverage, shows how much you think it matters – and never ceases to fascinate, surprise and frustrate.Stick with it.To receive the complete version of This Is Europe in your inbox every Wednesday,please subscribe here.Explore more on these topicsWorld newsThis is EuropeEuropeAndy BurnhamEuropean UnionnewslettersShareReuse this content

title: Food price fears after Europe’s heatwave destroys estimated €2bn of crops | Farming | The Guardian
description: Record June temperatures expected to cut grain harvest by 9m tonnes to its lowest level since 2018
Author: Joanna Partridge
Headline: Record June temperatures expected to cut grain harvest by 9m tonnes to its lowest level since 2018
Content: British farmers are reporting dry conditions, with East Anglia and parts of south-east England being hit the worst.Photograph: Matthew Ashmore/AlamyView image in fullscreenBritish farmers are reporting dry conditions, with East Anglia and parts of south-east England being hit the worst.Photograph: Matthew Ashmore/AlamyFarmingFood price fears after Europe’s heatwave destroys estimated €2bn of cropsRecord June temperatures expected to cut grain harvest by 9m tonnes to its lowest level since 2018Joanna PartridgeWed 22 Jul 2026 10.12 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe heatwave thatswept across Europe in Juneis estimated to have cost the continent’s grain farmers €2bn (£1.7bn) in lost revenue and destroyed 9m tonnes of crops, sparking concern about the impact on the price of food.Coceral, the European cereals and oilseeds trade association, made the estimate for the harvest of crops such as wheat, barley, maize and oats following the heatwave.The reduction of 9m tonnes would mean the 27 EU member states and the UK will record their smallest harvest since 2018, and represents lost crop production larger than the combined total forecast grain production of Austria, Ireland and the Netherlands.Coceral has linked the crop losses to thehottest June on record in western Europe. Maize was affected during the crucial pollination period, while wheat was affected during the time known as “grain fill”, when cereals need moisture to fill out, a key stage of development for crop yield.Based on current prices, the value of lost grain production is estimated by the Energy and Climate Intelligence Unit (ECIU) thinktank to be about €2bn, although this figure does not include wider costs of the heatwave, such as lost vegetable crops and livestock.Extreme heatwaves may cause global decline in dairy production, scientists warnRead moreTom Lancaster, the ECIU’s land, food and farming analyst, said: “This will hit farmers in the pockets, reducing their income and undermining European food security at the same time. In the UK we have already seen three of the five worst harvests on record this decade, and recent heatwaves here will inevitably have cost British farmers again.”France is expected to suffer reductions in grain production, where an estimated 3.4m tonnes of maize has been lost, with the cost of lost grains expected to reach €891m. The next biggest grain losses are expected in Hungary (2.4m tonnes) and Spain (1.4m tonnes).Benoît Merlo, a farmer in the Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes region of France, said a decade of adapting his farm to hotter and drier conditions had not helped this year: “With temperatures exceeding 38C for weeks on end, everything is breaking down.
