I’m expecting yield losses of at least 50%, or even 70%, for certain crops, such as soya.”British farmers are reporting dry conditions across much of the UK following the June heatwave and a dry spring, although this has been felt most acutely in East Anglia and parts of south-east England, with regional variations expected.Helen Plant, the lead analyst for cereals and oilseeds, at theAgricultureand Horticulture Development Board (AHDB), said: “The dry conditions through April and much of May have clearly taken a toll on crop development, particularly for spring crops and winter cereals with limited moisture reserves.”Theresult of the UK harvest is uncertain, the AHDB said, while many farmers have had to bring forward harvesting as a result of the hot, dry weather.Paul Tompkins, deputy president of the National Farmers’ Union, said: “Farmers and growers across the country are working around the clock to harvest crops which have come early, keep fruit and vegetables watered, and care for their livestock.“For many arable farmers, this could be another difficult and expensive harvest after extreme weather impacts in both 2024 and 2025 which saw both drought and flood.”The lost crops are expected to make Europe more dependent on grain imports, pushing global commodity prices higher, although this will also be affected by the southern hemisphere harvest in late 2026 and early 2027.The shortfall in European grain production comes as global crop supplies are also tightening, as southern and western parts of the US aresuffering from severe drought, affecting grain and meat production.Explore more on these topicsFarmingEuropeEuropean UnionAgricultureEurope weatherFood (Environment)Cereals and grainsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Iran warns of ‘eye for an eye’ response if US follows through on Trump’s threats to destroy infrastructure – as it happened | US-Israel war on Iran | The Guardian
description: US president vows to destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant for each strait of Hormuz attack; Iran’s military says it will stop Gulf oil flow and target regional infrastructure if US acts on threats, state tv reports
Author: Mark Saunokonoko
Headline: US president vows to destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant for each strait of Hormuz attack; Iran’s military says it will stop Gulf oil flow and target regional infrastructure if US acts on threats, state tv reports
Content: 15.08 EDTIran warns of 'eye for an eye' response if infrastructure hitFurther to that,Iran’s foreign ministerAbbas Araghchisaid Wednesday that Tehran would respond in kind to any attack on its infrastructure, afterDonald Trump’sthreatto bomb a bridge or power plant for every ship targeted in thestrait of Hormuz.“Our defence doctrine is clear: eye for an eye. Any aggression againstIran, including our infrastructure, will compel a powerful and decisive response,” Araghchi said on X.Per my lastpost, Iran’s state broadcaster quoted the military as saying that US threats “will have no result other than the expansion of the war”.Share

title: Mamdani says he can’t arrest Netanyahu as mayor and urges US to enforce ICC warrant | Zohran Mamdani | The Guardian
description: Mamdani admits he lacks power to arrest Israeli prime minister after saying he’s ‘not welcome’ in New York City
Author: Alice Speri
Headline: Mamdani admits he lacks power to arrest Israeli prime minister after saying he’s ‘not welcome’ in New York City
Content: Zohran Mamdani speaks during a press conference at city hall on Wednesday.Photograph: Erik Pendzich/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenZohran Mamdani speaks during a press conference at city hall on Wednesday.Photograph: Erik Pendzich/ShutterstockZohran MamdaniMamdani says he can’t arrest Netanyahu as mayor and urges US to enforce ICC warrantMamdani admits he lacks power to arrest Israeli prime minister after saying he’s ‘not welcome’ in New York CityAlice SperiWed 22 Jul 2026 13.07 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 11.47 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleZohran Mamdani, theNew York Citymayor, doubled down on his support for the international criminal court (ICC) on Wednesday andcalled onfederal authorities to execute the court’s warrant forBenjamin Netanyahu’s arrest should he visit the city in the fall.“I call on the federal government to join the international criminal court and to execute both this warrant as well as any warrant the international criminal court holds,” Mamdani told reporters at a press conference on Wednesday.Those remarks came less than 24 hours after Mamdani called the Israeli prime minister a “war criminal” who is “not welcome in New York City” in a videostatementposted on social media. In the statement, the mayor acknowledged he had no legal authority to direct New York police to arrest Netanyahu should he attend the United Nations general assembly in September.Mamdani had previously said he was “in conversation” with city lawyers to determine whether his administration could arrest Netanyahu, as he hadpledgedto do during his mayoral campaign last year.On Wednesday, Mamdani rejected arguments from critics that Israel’swarcrimes in Gaza have nothing to do with New York City. Israel’s actions in Gaza, he said, had “offended the conscience, the morals, the mere humanity of so many New Yorkers”.Mamdani’s statements about Netanyahu were remarkable for a US municipal leader and a signal of how support for Palestinian rights remains a signature policy for him.His insistence that the US government arrest Netanyahu has been met with condemnation and ridicule from pro-Israel officials. Pro-Palestinian advocates’ reactions were more mixed – some praised the mayor’s Tuesday statement for its “moral clarity” while others expressed disappointment that he walked back on his promise to seek the Israeli leader’s arrest.Mamdani urges US government to arrest Netanyahu if Israeli PM visits USRead more“I think the mayor should stay in his lane,” Jonathan Harounoff, Israel’s former international spokesperson at the United Nations,told NY1, accusing him of “political theater”.John Fetterman of Pennsylvania, one of the most staunchlypro-IsraelDemocrats in the Senate, called Mamdani a “clown” who should “sit down”.“America’s not even part of that corrupt court,” Fettermansaidin an interview with Fox News over the weekend. “That’s really not your purview.”Simcha Eichenstein, a pro-Israel New York state assemblymember, had previouslycalledon the state attorney general to review whether Mamdani’s comments about Netanyahu violated state law.The ICC issued arrest warrants against Netanyahu and the former Israeli defense minister Yoav Gallant in 2024, accusing them of war crimes and crimes against humanity, including “starvation as a method of warfare, and … murder, persecution, and other inhumane acts”.“I believe Prime Minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” Mamdanitoldthe New York Times last week, referring to the city in the Netherlands that is home to the court.Neither the US nor Israel are members of the ICC, which the US government has long opposed and which Marco Rubio, the secretary of state, this month threatened to “dismantle” by pressuring other countries to abandon it.Mamdani said he concluded his administration had no authority to execute the ICC arrest warrant, but shifted the burden on theTrump administrationto do so.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Around 2-3 times a daySign up toBreaking News USGet the most important news as it breaksPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant,” he said in Tuesday’s statement. “The federal government, however, does – and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant.”Donald Trump hasalready saidhe will do no such thing.Netanyahu “will not be arrested” on US soil, the presidentsaidin a social media post earlier this week, adding that the Israeli prime minister was “fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran” and “the only ones that should be arrested are the people that led Iran into this unprecedented spiral of death and destruction”.Trump says Benjamin Netanyahu ‘will not be arrested’ if he visits the USRead moreNetanyahu had said in the past he was “not concerned” with Mamdani’s threat, and his office said in astatementlast week that the mayor “appears interested in diverting public attention from his follies and attacking the leader of the Jewish state and the only democracy in the Middle East”. It called the ICC a “kangaroo court”.Supporters of the court have also praised Mamdani’s efforts to stand up for the tribunal’s mission.“My mayor is doing what any NYC mayor would do if Vladimir Putin or any other indicted war criminal planned to visit NYC,”saidAdil Haque, a legal scholar. “He is exploring every legal avenue and exhausting all legal options. If his hands are tied, he will use his voice.”Explore more on these topicsZohran MamdaniBenjamin NetanyahuNew YorkIsraelMiddle East and north AfricaUS politicsDonald TrumpnewsShareReuse this content

title: Mamdani urges US government to arrest Netanyahu if Israeli PM visits US | Zohran Mamdani | The Guardian
description: New York mayor admits he does not have authority to arrest Netanyahu, adding Israeli PM is ‘architect of a horrific genocide’
Author: Robert Mackey
Headline: New York mayor admits he does not have authority to arrest Netanyahu, adding Israeli PM is ‘architect of a horrific genocide’
Content: 2:13Mamdani urges US government to arrest Netanyahu if he visits US – videoZohran MamdaniMamdani urges US government to arrest Netanyahu if Israeli PM visits USNew York mayor admits he does not have authority to arrest Netanyahu, adding Israeli PM is ‘architect of a horrific genocide’Robert MackeyWed 22 Jul 2026 02.41 EDTFirst published on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.08 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleZohran Mamdani, theNew York Citymayor, acknowledged on Tuesday he does not have the authority to arrestBenjamin Netanyahu, but urged US federal authorities to do so if the Israeli prime minister visits the US later this year.“Benjamin Netanyahu is a war criminal, the architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people,” Mamdani said in avideo posted to social media.Mamdani said he believed the international criminal court warrant for Netanyahu’s arrest over alleged war crimes was justified, but admitted he lacked the legal authority to enforce it.‘He’s a war criminal’: Mamdani exploring whether he can arrest Netanyahu if he visits New YorkRead more“My administration has reviewed every avenue available under applicable law to determine whether New York City could execute the international criminal court’s arrest warrant ifBenjamin Netanyahucame here,” Mamdani said. “It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant.“The federal government, however, does – and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant,” the mayor added. “And I want to be equally clear: Benjamin Netanyahu is not welcome inNew YorkCity, nor is any other war criminal at large.”Netanyahu is expected to travel to New York in September for the UN general assembly.Mamdani saidlast weekhe was reviewing whether he would be able to arrest Netanyahu during that visit, afterpledgingto do so during his mayoral campaign last year.Trump says Benjamin Netanyahu ‘will not be arrested’ if he visits the USRead more“I believe that Prime Minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” Mamdani said in reference to the Dutch city where the international criminal court, which issued anarrest warrantfor Netanyahu in 2024, is located.The US does not recognize the ICC’s authority and is not a party to it.His remarks drew rebuke from Donald Trump, whoinsistedNetanyahu “will not be arrested” on US soil.Trump said in a social media post Netanyahu is “fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran” and “the only ones that should be arrested are the people that led Iran into this unprecedented spiral of death and destruction”.Netanyahu, meanwhile, said he was “not concerned” about Mamdani’s comments.Explore more on these topicsZohran MamdaniBenjamin NetanyahuNew YorkIsraelGazaUnited NationsPalestinenewsShareReuse this content

title: Wednesday briefing: How Trump’s Iran gamble has produced a crisis reshaping the global order | Donald Trump | The Guardian
description: In today’s newsletter: ​A White House operating without expertise, strategy or restraint ​is stuck in a conflict with no easy way out, which is sending economic shockwaves far beyond its own borders
Author: Hettie O'Brien
Headline: In today’s newsletter: ​A White House operating without expertise, strategy or restraint ​is stuck in a conflict with no easy way out, which is sending economic shockwaves far beyond its own borders
Content: Donald Trump misjudged how quickly he could win this war.Photograph: Abedin Taherkenareh/EPAView image in fullscreenDonald Trump misjudged how quickly he could win this war.Photograph: Abedin Taherkenareh/EPAFirst Edition newsletterDonald TrumpWednesday briefing: How Trump’s Iran gamble has produced a crisis reshaping the global orderIn today’s newsletter: ​A White House operating without expertise, strategy or restraint ​is stuck in a conflict with no easy way out, which is sending economic shockwaves far beyond its own bordersHettie O'BrienWed 22 Jul 2026 01.45 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 01.47 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleGood morning. Within two hours of entering No 10 on Monday afternoon, Andy Burnham had his first call withDonald Trump. After chit-chatting about the football (both agreed England had an “excellent tournament”) and Manchester (our new prime minister invited the president to visit the city Trump once described as a “town”), they got on to far trickier territory: the strait of Hormuz.The US has attackedIranfor the eleventh consecutive night, hitting aircraft hangars and drone storage sites across the country. It came after Iran launched retaliatory strikes on US air bases and neighbouring Gulf states, which have accused it of deliberately targeting desalination plants that are essential to life in the region. Each side now claims it is preventing the other from gaining control over the strait of Hormuz, through which 20% of the world’s oil was shipped before the war began. Vessels carrying crucial shipments of oil, gas and fertiliser remain at a standstill.“Operation Epic Fury” started with the aim ofregime change. Since then, its objective has morphed intoreopening the straitand undoing the damage caused by the US and Israel’s decision to start the war. The president drastically underestimated Iran’s capacity for enduring pain, and is now trying to bomb his way out of a conflict that he created. For today’s First Edition, I spoke toVali Nasr, professor of International Affairs and Middle East Studies and author of Iran’s Grand Strategy, about the war over the world’s most critical bottleneck. That’s after the headlines.Five big storiesUK politics| Andy Burnham isunder mounting pressureto explain how he will pay for his policy pledges as he implored his new cabinet to examine “all possible ways” to tackle the cost of living crisis.UK news| A manhas appeared in courtcharged with the murder of the former MP and Reform UK spokesperson Ann Widdecombe. Joshua Kerry, 28, is accused of killing the former Conservative minister at her home in Haytor, Devon, by hitting her on the head with a hammer 21 times.Middle East| The US defense secretary, Pete Hegseth, told the Senate the US-Israel war on Iranhad cost $37.5bn so far, as he faced lawmakers furious over the conflict’s soaring price tag and the deaths last week of three American service members.Climate crisis| About 23 million people will be banned from using hosepipes by this weekend, as England and Walesmoves closer to drought status.Ukraine| Volodymyr Zelenskyyhas fired Ukraine’s commander-in-chiefOleksandr Syrskyi and offered a “prominent position” to the defence minister he sacked last week in a move that sparked nationwide protests.In depth: ‘In his mind, this was a legacy-building transformation’View image in fullscreenDonald Trump and Benjamin Netanyahu seemed to have not thought through the full consequences of attacking Iran.Photograph: Joe Raedle/Getty Images“I’m not going to start wars,” Donald Trump told voters on election night in 2024. His decision to bomb Iran on 28 February in partnership with Benjamin Netanyahuwas astonishing bothin its aggression and its audaciousbreak with this promise. Trump did nothing to prepare the US for war, gave seemingly no thought to the fact that Iran would inevitably hold the strait hostage if it came under attack, and resorted to contradictory explanations for his decision. In the war’s early days, he described “what we did in Venezuela” as a model for what he hoped to achieve. “He thought he would go down in history as the American president who had resolved the Iran problem,” Nasr told me. “In his mind, this was a legacy-building transformation that would be quick and cost-free.”That has proven a fatal miscalculation. Pete Hegseth, the US self-styled “secretary of war”, said the early US strikes that killed Iran’s Supreme Leader, Ali Khamenei, had “cut off the head of the snake.” But regime change is now a distant prospect. Trump underestimated the resilience of the Iranian regime and overestimated the power of protests against it. After just five months, his misadventure is as unpopular with American voters as the Vietnam war was six years in. It could yet escalate into a wider regional conflict, with the Houthis threatening a maritime embargo on Saudi Arabia. Even Trump – whose private firm, The Trump Organization, is at work on a skyscraper in Dubai – won’t be insulated from the consequences of this violence.What happened to the ceasefire?The memorandum of understanding, a vague, short document that the US and Iran signed on 17 June, gave a brief respite from the strikes and (supposedly) initiated a 60-day negotiation period in which time both sides could reach permanent agreement on – among other things – the future of the strait. But it broke down when Iran fired onthree commercial tankersthat had used a shipping lane close to the shores of Oman, rather than a route off the Iranian coast approved by Tehran. “When ceasefires are violated, you have two options; either react to preserve the ceasefire, or use the violation as an excuse to end it,” Nasr says.Trump went with the latter option, calling Iran’s leaders“scum”and declaring the ceasefire “over” earlier this month. This week, the US energy secretary Chris Wright said the US mission had shifted towards ensuring oil shipments could move through the strait. It’s worth remembering that the strait had never been closed for an extended period. Before 28 February, ships had been sailing through it without obstruction. The US is now framing the potential of a return to this old status quo as a victory.Iran and the US are still negotiating, and Tehran has signalled its desire to permanently control the strait, including by charging tolls on passing vessels. “The US is now in a situation where it either has a long, protracted war, which would keep the strait closed, or keeps escalating to the next level with the hope of knocking Iran out,” Nasr says.A serious misjudgmentThere are signs that Trump and Netanyahu seriously misjudged the likelihood that Iran would seize control of the strait. In an interview earlier this year, Netanyahu admitted “the problem of the Hormuz strait became understood as the war went on.” As our diplomatic editor Patrick Wintourwroteat the time, in a piece suggesting the consequences of Trump’s excursion could prove greater than the Vietnam war, this was an “astonishing admission.” The closure of the strait is the type of doomsday scenario that previous administrations, and most Iran experts, would have considered.But the US government now has very few Iran experts, because Trump has instead chosen to surround himself with a royal court of loyalists. Nate Swanson, a former state department representative on Trump’s Iran negotiating team, was forced out earlier this year after far-right activist Laura Loomer accused him of disloyalty.“Folks who’ve watched this Iranian system … would have told [Trump]: ‘They’re not businessmen from New York. They’re made of a different type of DNA’,” Alex Vatanka, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute in Washington DC,recently toldGuardian US political correspondent, Robert Tait.Consequences, consequencesInflation caused by rising oil prices is making everyday life more expensive and hiking the costs of borrowing for governments (including Burnham’s here in the UK). If there is no agreement until 2027, some are predictingglobal growthcould fall by 1.3%, if not more. Europe may be uniquely exposed: since the Russia-Ukraine war, the continent has turned towards the Middle East for its gas, particularly to Qatar, which has had to slow production during the conflict. We could be facing a winter of shortages, bringing to mind the 1970s oil crisis, when the British government enforced power cuts to conserve energy supplies.Who’s in charge of the sea?Many of Trump’s proposed solutions to the conflict seem to have been thought up on the spot. He briefly suggested that the US could charge tolls on ships entering the strait in exchange for safe passage, despite European leaders and evenMarco Rubio, his own secretary of state, having previously said these would be incompatible with international law. The fact the US and Iran have even proposed such tolls is a sign of the wilful disregard for international law that has become a defining feature of this conflict (notably, Iran, the US and Israel are among a small handful of nations that don’t ratify the UN convention on the laws of the sea). Freedom of navigation is a cornerstone of international law: without it, shipping starts to resemble a protection racket, where nations extract fees from passing vessels.Some experts now worry that other shipping routes could become more militarised, as coastal nations watching Iran use the strait as the ultimate bargaining chip try to take advantage of other important waterways. “A lot of other countries that feel they’re like Iran, and could use passageways as a deterrent” Nasr says. “If nations like the US and Israel can attack your country at any time, you’re going to resort to whatever you can in order to protect yourself.”What else we’ve been enjoyingView image in fullscreen‘Our nervous systems, built over millennia to bond with other humans, have been exponentially overtaken by Moore’s Law.’Photograph: Cavan Images/Getty Images/Cavan Images RFThisthoughtful pieceby Alice Lassman on how Gen Z has outsourced intimacy toAIreflects conversations I’ve been having with younger pals lately.LibbyEd Miliband was trying to start agreen energyrevolution in Britain. Now he’s at the Foreign Office, what happens to that agenda? I enjoyed reading this piece by Fiona Harvey, whichhas some answers.Hettie“It’s a crime that alters your whole reality.” Anna Moore on theimpact of crime, here onthe survivor-led campaignto end drug-facilitated sexual rape.LibbyIf (like me) you feel creeped out by reading aboutMeta’s new “smartglasses”, which allow wearers to film people without their consent, it’s worth reading this pieceby Gaby Hinsliff.HettieHerald writer Neil Mackay makesa powerful argumentthat the future health of theScottish independencemovement depends on Burnham’s success or failure in Westminster.Libbyskip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionSportView image in fullscreenRemco Evenepoel enjoys the view from the podium after his stage 16 Tour win.Photograph: Pete Goding/ShutterstockCycling| Remco Evenepoel tookback-to-back stage winsin the Tour de France, and eroded some of Tadej Pogacar’s overall lead, with victory in the stage 16 time trial from Evian to Thonon-les-Bains.Tennis| The French Open has become the first grand slam tournament to offer playersa share of the event’s revenuein a significant step forward in the row over prize money.Football| La Liga’s president, Javier Tebas, has accused Fifa of“destroying the football industry”and called for its president, Gianni Infantino, to quit.The front pagesView image in fullscreenPhotograph: The Guardian“Burnham implores cabinet to find ways to tackle cost of living crisis”, is theGuardian’s front page today, theTimeswrites “Burnham signals cuts to pay for spending pledges”, and thei Paper’s take is “Cost of living, cost of living, cost of living”. TheMailasks “Who’s going to pay the bill for Andy’s largesse?” and theFThas “Burnham backs away from rise in tax allowance ‘to show fiscal discipline’”.Elsewhere, theTelegraphleads on “Pc Harper’s killers set for early release”. TheMirror,Sun, MetroandExpressall splash on details from court with variations of “Ann ‘hit 21 times with hammer’”.The LatestPhotograph: The GuardianUS soldiers killed: has Trump lost control of Iran war?The US has carried out a fresh round of strikes against Iran, after three US soldiers were killed in action. The Pentagon has also admitted that nearly 100 US service members have been injured over the last two weeks, as Tehran launched a wave of reprisals to the tenth consecutive nights of attacks, even as peace talks mediated by Pakistan continue. Lucy Houghspeaks to senior international correspondent, Julian Borger.Cartoon of the day | Ben JenningsView image in fullscreenIllustration: Ben Jennings/The GuardianThe UpsideA bit of good news to remind you that the world’s not all badView image in fullscreen‘We’re only at the beginning of this trajectory. And around the country, a lot of people don’t feel this way at all.’Photograph: Justin Lane/EPAYotam Maron, a key organiser in theOccupy Wall Street movementwhich spawned a global network in 2011, has worked on multiple social movements since then. He identifies a “politics of powerlessness” which too often hobbles them, mistaking “a healthy distrust of institutional power for a failure to build a strong, coherent strategy for political change”. Since then, he founded the Wildfire Project, a training organisation for grassroots groups to counter that misplaced internal logic and argues now, even with Democrat socialists in the ascendancy across some parts of the US, that people power must continue to be built through movements not elected politicians.Sign up herefor a weekly roundup of The Upside, sent to you every SundayBored at work?And finally, the Guardian’s puzzles are here to keep you entertained throughout the day. Until tomorrow.Quick crosswordCryptic crosswordWordiplyExplore more on these topicsDonald TrumpFirst Edition newsletterBenjamin NetanyahuUS-Israel war on IranStrait of HormuzUS politicsIranMiddle East and north AfricanewslettersShareReuse this content

title: Opposition parties shut down Indian parliament in solidarity with ‘cockroach’ protests | India | The Guardian
description: Proceedings suspended as politicians call for Modi government to address police violence and education scandals
Author: Hannah Ellis-Petersen
Headline: Proceedings suspended as politicians call for Modi government to address police violence and education scandals
Content: A Cockroach Janta party supporter holding a placard in New Delhi during protests demanding the resignation of the education minister.Photograph: Sajjad Hussain/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenA Cockroach Janta party supporter holding a placard in New Delhi during protests demanding the resignation of the education minister.Photograph: Sajjad Hussain/AFP/Getty ImagesIndiaOpposition parties shut down Indian parliament in solidarity with ‘cockroach’ protestsProceedings suspended as politicians call for Modi government to address police violence and education scandalsHannah Ellis-Petersenin DelhiWed 22 Jul 2026 06.04 EDTFirst published on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.06 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIndia’s opposition parties have shut down parliament in a protest calling for the resignation of the education minister, in solidarity with the newyouth-led “cockroach” movement.Opposition politicians, led by the Congress party leaderRahul Gandhi, gathered outside the parliament building in Delhi on Wednesday, many dressed in black and waving placards calling on Dharmendra Pradhan to quit.Who are India’s Cockroach Janta party and why are they staging protests?Read moreInside the parliament chamber, proceedings were suspended after opposition figures demanded that Narendra Modi’s ruling government address the police violence against young people at a protest on Monday, and the scandals that have engulfed crucial exams.The previous day, Gandhi had been briefly detained by police after staging a sit-in outside Modi’s residence, accusing the prime minister of “destroying the future of India’s youth”.0:38Rahul Gandhi detained and pulled on to bus after calling for PM Modi to resign – videoGandhi’s sister, Priyanka, a senior Congress party strategist, was also briefly detained on Tuesday. Police confirmed they were later released.The opposition protests at parliament were in support of a new mass youth movement, known as the Cockroach Janata party (CJP), that began as a satirical online joke but has grown into a significant political challenge to the Modi government.On Monday, the CJP held one of the biggest anti-government protests in years. Dozens of people were injured after police used teargas and baton charges against the demonstrators.View image in fullscreenRahul Gandhi being carried away by police from a sit-in outside Narendra Modi’s residence in Delhi.Photograph: Adnan Abidi/ReutersMore than 1,000 people remained camped out at the CJP’s protest site in centralDelhion Wednesday despite the gruelling heat and humidity. Many of the protesters had travelled for miles to be there and vowed they would not move until the education minister had resigned and the Modi government listened to their demands.Food deliveries sent by supporters – some ordered from abroad – have continued to arrive, keeping the protesters well fed on biryani, pizza, rice and dal. Morale remained high as slogans and songs rang out across the site.“In the country where people had forgotten how to ask questions, in the country where criticising the government was considered treason – we have now awakened that country,” said CJP spokesman Ashutosh Ranka, to loud cheers from the crowd.View image in fullscreenPolice detaining Priyanka Gandhi outside the prime minister’s residence in Delhi.Photograph: Ritesh Shukla/Getty ImagesThe CJP’s demands for changes to the education system and the resignation of Pradhan arose after repeated scandals over testing, but it has grown into a larger movement giving voice to the frustration’s of India’s youth.It was estimated that more than 50,000 people had protested in Delhi on Monday, even though the police denied them permission to march on parliament. It began as a peaceful demonstration, but by late afternoon police began to clash with the protesters.India’s Cockroach movement protesters show no sign of backing downRead morePolice said 60 civilians and 118 police personnel had been injured, and they accused protesters of instigating the clashes.But organisers condemned the “high-handedness” of the authorities and said hundreds of peaceful protesters had been injured by the police, including at least one woman now on a ventilator in intensive care from her injuries.Amnesty International have accused Delhi police of using police excessive force. India’s Supreme Court on Wednesday refused to hear a petition alleging police brutality against the protesters.View image in fullscreenA Cockroach Janta party supporter holding a placard at a protest in Delhi on Wednesday.Photograph: Sajjad Hussain/AFP/GettyCongress accused the Modi government of refusing to address the issues of the students and the alleged “brutality unleashed on students” in parliament.“The government doesn’t want to take any accountability, nor does it want to have a debate on it in parliament,” said Gandhi.Protests supporting the CJP movement were also held in Mumbai and Bengaluru, drawing hundreds of people.View image in fullscreenPolice stand guard at a sit-in protest calling for the resignation of Narendra Modi outside his residence in Delhi.Photograph: Arun Sankar/AFP/GettyModi has not commented directly on the protests, but the education minister, who had dismissed the CJP protesters as a “B-team of terrorists”, took a more conciliatory tone in his first comments since Monday’s protests.In a social media post, Pradhan said: “We owe them [students] answers, reforms and accountability. That is exactly what our government remains committed to delivering.He added: “The students of India deserve far better than being treated as props in a political campaign,” he said, referring to Gandhi’s protest.Allow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueSeveral key opposition parties have come out in support of the students’ movement and condemned the police violence against Monday’s protest.Sandeep Dikshit, a senior leader of the Congress party, said: “What happened yesterday is our constitution got trampled upon. After that, no self-respecting politician or citizen can stand aside.”Explore more on these topicsIndiaRahul GandhiSouth and central AsiaProtestNarendra ModiDelhiActivismnewsShareReuse this content

title: One UK mosque attacked ‘every five days’, says British Muslim Trust | UK news | The Guardian
description: More than 70 attacks have been recorded over past year, including acts of violence, property damage and vandalism
Author: Aamna Mohdin
Headline: More than 70 attacks have been recorded over past year, including acts of violence, property damage and vandalism
Content: Damage outside entrance of the mosque in Phyllis Avenue, Peacehaven, East Sussex, after an arson attack in October 2025.Photograph: Jamie Lashmar/PAView image in fullscreenDamage outside entrance of the mosque in Phyllis Avenue, Peacehaven, East Sussex, after an arson attack in October 2025.Photograph: Jamie Lashmar/PAUK newsOne UK mosque attacked ‘every five days’, says British Muslim TrustMore than 70 attacks have been recorded over past year, including acts of violence, property damage and vandalismAamna Mohdinand Jayni MakwanaWed 22 Jul 2026 19.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleOne mosque is attacked in Britain every five days, according to new data released by the government’s partner for monitoring anti-Muslim hatred.More than 70 attacks on mosques in the UK have been recorded over the past year, which include acts of intimidation, violence, property damage, harassment, anti-Muslim hate speech, online abuse and vandalism.The British Muslim Trust warned that momentum on attacks was building and called for an urgent response from the government.The top three hotspots for attacks are London, Greater Manchester and the West Midlands. There have been nine separate mosque attacks in London, and one mosque in Birmingham has suffered three attacks in the last 12 months.UK mosques advised to run lockdown drills amid fears of anti-Muslim attacksRead moreData from the Home Office shows anti-Muslim hate crime in England and Walesrose by 19%in the 12 months to March 2025.Akeela Ahmed, the chief executive of the British Muslim Trust, said: “We are seeing a steady rise in the number of attacks against mosques, and we believe the government should be doing everything it can to help mosques protect themselves.”Earlier this year, the government announced it would provide up to £40m to mosques as part of the protective security for mosques scheme. The initiative would allow mosques to apply for funding for physical security measures such as CCTV, alarms, fencing and other security personnel services.Home Office guidance says mosques should apply for “security measures” if they have experienced or feel vulnerable to hate crime, or if there has been hate crime in the area towards other places of worship or their congregants. The guidance warns applications that “do not include strong evidence or do not explain the risk clearly are unlikely to be successful”.Ahmed has previouslyurged ministers to drop these requirementsfor applying for protective security, saying it created a significant barrier to support.The British Muslim Trust said mosques applying for funding have found the scheme to be difficult to access, with some waiting up to 18 months to receive funding. The monitoring group added that not only was there a backlog, but that many were being denied funding without explanation.“A mosque is vulnerable purely because it’s a mosque. Currently, they’re waiting for months while living in fear of another attack because of the backlog. In many cases, police aren’t even responding to low-level incidents, leaving people frightened and vulnerable,” Ahmed said.“It is great that this funding and initiative exists, but we need to recognise that the momentum on attacks is building, and we need to react and prepare for this.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAhmed added: “We recommend exploring better ways to get the funding to mosques as smoothly as possible including that the fund is devolved in order to speed up its delivery, especially given recent events.”Last month, a study by the British Muslim Trust and British Future found thatone in six Britons believethe growth of the Muslim population “poses a foundational threat to UK culture”, raising alarm that hostile attitudes towards Muslims are being normalised.A Home Office spokesperson said:“Muslims in the UK face appalling levels of religious hate crime. It’s crucial that they not only are safe but feel safe.“This government has increased funding for protective security at mosques, Muslim faith schools and community centres to record levels – up to £40m in 2026-27.“We are working to process all applications as quickly as possible.”Explore more on these topicsUK newsIslamophobianewsShareReuse this content

title: Scottish Labour’s rivals decry ‘chancer’ Anas Sarwar for resigning to join Andy Burnham’s government | Anas Sarwar | The Guardian
description: New prime minister has made Sarwar – the first senior Labour figure to call for Keir Starmer’s resignation – a peer
Author: Robyn Vinter
Headline: New prime minister has made Sarwar – the first senior Labour figure to call for Keir Starmer’s resignation – a peer
Content: Anas Sarwar has been accused by the SNP of lying to the people of Scotland.Photograph: Andrew Milligan/PAView image in fullscreenAnas Sarwar has been accused by the SNP of lying to the people of Scotland.Photograph: Andrew Milligan/PAAnas SarwarScottish Labour’s rivals decry ‘chancer’ Anas Sarwar for resigning to join Andy Burnham’s governmentNew prime minister has made Sarwar – the first senior Labour figure to call for Keir Starmer’s resignation – a peerRobyn VinterWed 22 Jul 2026 18.17 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 12.18 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleScottish Labour’s rivals in Holyrood have slammed Anas Sarwar’s shock decision to resign as leader to sit in Andy Burnham’s new government, calling him a “chancer” and accusing him of lying to voters.The prime minister has made Sarwar – who was the first seniorLabourfigure to call for Keir Starmer to resign – a peer and given him a ministerial post at the Department for Business, Innovation, Science and Trade, No 10 announced on Wednesday.The Scottish Green party co-leader Gillian Mackay said: “Anas Sarwar is a chancer. He has gone from promising to scrap theHouse of Lordsto taking a seat in it. He has spent years attacking the very institution he is now preparing to join. The hypocrisy is breathtaking.”View image in fullscreen‘Anas Sarwar is a chancer,’ said Gillian Mackay, a Scottish Green party co-leader. ‘The hypocrisy is breathtaking.’Photograph: Murdo MacLeod/The GuardianThe SNP called for Sarwar to donate his new “bumper salary” to charity, accusing him of lying to the electorate over his intentions after previously saying he would never enter the Lords, calling it “unacceptable” in 2022.The SNP MSP George Adam said: “The Labour party andAnas Sarwarrepeatedly denied and lied to the people of Scotland about Anas Sarwar’s intentions – the fact he will now be prancing about in ermine robes won’t be forgotten.”However, despite the move happening less than three months since his election campaign – shortly before Scottish Labour suffered itsworst results in Holyrood history– those who know Sarwar do not think it was planned.Figures from within his own party were left reeling, with “surprised” being the word on the lips of a number of Scottish Labour politicians and staff on Wednesday.One Scottish Labour worker said: “If he was going to resign, it would have been clearer to have stated that in May and given everyone the chance to have a proper assessment and restructure things however needed. Doing it this way means everyone is thrown into the lurch.”As is the formality in some constituencies, Sarwar’s seat as a member of the Scottish parliament will be relinquished and handed on to the next on the list, Monica Lennon. Lennon is also seen as a credible contender for the party leadership, having been a popular MSP in the past.Michael Marra, the MSP for North EastScotland, is a name also being put forward as a potential leadership candidate from outside the power centres of Edinburgh or Glasgow.It will not be a wide field, as there are only 17 Labour MSPs to choose from. It is possible for an MP or even a Lord to take on the Scottish Labour leadership – meaning, in fact, Sarwar does not have to technically resign as leader – but it is most common for this role to be held by an MSP because of duties in the Scottish parliament.View image in fullscreenMonica Lennon, a former MSP who did not win a seat in the May Scottish parliament elections, is expected to take over Sarwar’s Glasgow seat. She is also seen as a contender for the Scottish Labour leadership.Photograph: Murdo MacLeod/The GuardianA source close to Sarwar said: “The next [Scottish] Labour leader will need to [provide] balance between the offer to Scotland that is a uniquely Scottish offer, while being part of a UK-wide party. That has been a constant conflict, a constant battle.”Another said the next leader would “have to do some educating of Andy Burnham … He’s the king of the north but he is prime minister of the United Kingdom.”Burnham is heading to Scotland for his first meeting with the leaders of the devolved nations on Thursday. He will meet the first minister of Scotland, John Swinney from the SNP, and the first minister of Wales, Rhun ap Iorwerth of Plaid Cymru, before watching the opening ceremony of the 2026 Commonwealth Games.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every daySign up toHeadlines UKGet the day’s headlines and highlights emailed direct to you every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionIn Wales, I’ve seen what happens when devolution isn’t done right. Here’s what Andy Burnham must know | Will HaywardRead moreCriticism has been made from Scotland thatBurnham’s devolution offerto the north of England and the wider country outside London and the south-east falls short when it comes to the devolved nations.Scottish politicians from across the spectrum have complained that Burnham lacks knowledge of, or interest in, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland. Sarwar’s appointment could help bridge that gap, a source said.While many people were shocked by Sarwar’s perceived willingness to leave Scotland behind, given the choice between being in power in Westminster and in opposition in Holyrood, it would be common sense for Sarwar to choose Westminster, a source close to Sarwar in the Labour party said.“I’m maybe less surprised than people out in the wider Labour world, because he’s someone who’s very ambitious. Politically, he’s someone who wants to effect change.“He’s obviously not in power, he faced the prospect of hanging around for another five years until the next Holyrood election. I just think we would have been bored had he stayed on, and he’s not someone that would like to sit around and be bored.”Sarwar said: “I am delighted and honoured to have been asked by the prime minister to serve every part of the United Kingdom as the minister of state for trade.“Throughout my life, I have only ever been motivated by public service. The opportunity to serve our country is all I have ever asked. That’s why this is an opportunity I humbly accept.“Looking back over the last five years, I am hugely proud of the work we have done since I became Scottish Labour leader.”The acting leader of the Scottish Labour party, Jackie Baillie, said: “I want to thank Anas Sarwar for his leadership and friendship. He led the Scottish Labour party with integrity and a vision to build a fairer and more equal Scotland.”Explore more on these topicsAnas SarwarScotlandLabourAndy BurnhamHouse of LordsScottish politicsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Paramount’s £80bn takeover of Warner Brothers a step closer after EU approval | Media | The Guardian
description: European Commission says Paramount Skydance’s decision to end Universal venture addresses merger concerns
Author: Hayden Vernon
Headline: European Commission says Paramount Skydance’s decision to end Universal venture addresses merger concerns
Content: Paramount signed the takeover deal in February after beating Netflix in a bidding war.Photograph: Justin Sullivan/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenParamount signed the takeover deal in February after beating Netflix in a bidding war.Photograph: Justin Sullivan/Getty ImagesMediaParamount’s £80bn takeover of Warner Brothers a step closer after EU approvalEuropean Commission says Paramount Skydance’s decision to end Universal venture addresses merger concernsHayden VernonWed 22 Jul 2026 15.09 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 15.10 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleParamount Skydance’s £80bn takeover of Warner Brothers took a step closer to being realised after it won conditional approval from the European Union.The European Commission said Paramount’s offer to ⁠end a film ⁠distribution ​joint venture with Universal Pictures addressed its concerns over the potential merger. As part of its commitments in the EU, Paramount has 13 months from the date of closing the deal to pull out from the venture.“These commitments fully address the competition concerns identified by the commission by ensuring that the films of the merged entity will not be distributed jointly with those of Universal or Disney,” EU competition regulators said in a statement.They also said Paramount should not directly or indirectly “enter into any agreement or understanding with Universal to jointly co-distribute films in the EEA” for a period of 10 years.Paramount signed the deal in February after beating Netflix in a bidding war.If the deal goes ahead, the combined company would control a vast array of media assets including CNN, Warner Brothers Pictures, TNT Sports and the HBO Max streaming service.View image in fullscreenParamount Skydance’s takeover of Warner Brothers would give the combined company control of a vast array of media assets.Photograph: Kevin Dietsch/Getty ImagesDespite getting the green light in the EU, the transaction faces tougher challenges in the US. Last week Paramount Skydance was ordered ‌by a US court to pause the deal, which has been cleared by the US Department of Justice, after a ​California-led coalition of 12 states argued the merger would irreparably harm competition.US president Donald Trump has made it clear he is interested in what the merger will mean for CNN, a broadcaster he is often critical of. Paramount CEO David Ellison is the son of Oracle co-founder Larry Ellison, a close ally of Trump.There will be a US hearing on 3 August regarding a preliminary injunction, which would prevent the transaction being closed before a final decision.A prolonged interruption will cost the company financially, with Paramount liable to pay Warner Brothers shareholders about £5m for each day the merger is ​delayed past 30 September.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe deal is also ​the target of a lawsuit by the Writers Guild ​of America, which ‌said it would ​jeopardise writers’ ​livelihoods and threaten the health of US entertainment.The deal faces another potential hurdle in the UK, which last month said it may intervene because of the potential impact on news, children’s television and streaming services.“Following engagement with the parties and independent research, my department has today written to the current and proposed owners ofWarner BrosDiscovery on my behalf to inform them that I am minded to intervene,”said culture secretary Lisa Nandy, in a written ministerial statement.Explore more on these topicsMediaTelevision industrynewsShareReuse this content

title: Burnham stirs up political row with review of release plans for violent criminals | Prisons and probation | The Guardian
description: PM says he wants to ensure ‘everything has been looked at’ amid outrage at policy from family of PC Andrew Harper
Author: Rajeev Syal
Headline: PM says he wants to ensure ‘everything has been looked at’ amid outrage at policy from family of PC Andrew Harper
Content: Andrew Harper and his wife, Lissie, who has raised concerns about the potential early release of his killers.Photograph: Courtesy of Lissie HarperView image in fullscreenAndrew Harper and his wife, Lissie, who has raised concerns about the potential early release of his killers.Photograph: Courtesy of Lissie HarperPrisons and probationBurnham stirs up political row with review of release plans for violent criminalsPM says he wants to ensure ‘everything has been looked at’ amid outrage at policy from family of PC Andrew HarperRajeev SyalandAlexandra ToppingWed 22 Jul 2026 15.02 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 07.49 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAndy Burnham is immersed in a deepening political row after ordering a review of plans for the early release of thousands of killers, rapists and sex offenders from prison in September.After the family of PC Andrew Harper expressed outrage that his killers may be eligible for release, the prime minister responded by pledging to make sure that “everything has been looked at again” before a fresh tranche of about 6,000 offenders were freed on tags.But soon after Burnham’s comments, prison reform groups, government officials and politicians warned that abandoning measures to ease jail overcrowding could lead to the criminal justice system “collapsing under its own weight”.Burnham has intervened quickly on policy issues just a few days into his premiership, including by indicating a possibleincrease in the personal allowanceon income tax, which has been frozen since 2022.Attempting to clear up confusion, he said there was no “commitment” to increase the allowance from £12,570 but the move would be considered at the budget, expected in the autumn.“I want to answer questions honestly in this job, as I said before. I think that’s all about trust in politics and doing politics differently, and I’m determined to keep working in that way,” he said.New sentencing laws were passed under Keir Starmer’s government to ease a looming prison overcrowding crisis that ministers said couldseize up the criminal justice systemin November.View image in fullscreenJessie Cole (left) and Albert Bowers were convicted of manslaughter in 2020 over the death of PC Andrew Harper.Photograph: Thames Valley Police/PAUnlike previous emergency measures under which tens of thousands of prisoners were released in the early months of Starmer’s government, the Sentencing Act includes no exemptions for prisoners convicted of serious crimes, domestic abuse or terrorism.PC Harper was 28 when he sustained fatal injuries while responding to a quad bike theft in Sulhamstead, Berkshire, in August 2019. Jessie Cole and Albert Bowers, who were passengers in the vehicle and both 17 at the time, were sentenced to 13 years in custody for manslaughter but may be eligible for early release under the new Sentencing Act.His widow, Lissie Harper, successfully campaigned for legal changes, called Harper’s Law, to bring in harsher sentences for the manslaughter of emergency workers on duty.In a statement, she said: “When I fought for Harper’s Law, I was supported not only by the Conservative government of the day but by the Labour party too. They pledged to support our cause and agreed that those who kill emergency workers should face tougher sentences because some crimes are so grave they demand meaningful punishment.“That cross-party support helped make Harper’s Law a reality. Today, it feels forgotten.”Asked to respond to the Harper family’s comments, Burnham said he would examine the plans with the justice secretary, Alex Norris. “I will review the whole of this policy. We did not invest in the prison estate when we should as a country, going back a decade or so ago, when it was clear that there was going to be pressure. That has created an unacceptable situation now.“But I want to assure myself that everything has been looked at before this policy proceeds, and I can give that assurance that every single part of this I will look into with the justice secretary. I can’t say that I can change the policy completely, but I will look at it in detail before we go further forward,” he said.Whitehall insiders have pointed out that any change in the early release scheme will contradict claims from the previous justice secretary,David Lammy, who told the Guardianthis month that without it there could be “no capacity” across prisons in England and Wales in November.One government official said: “There is very little wriggle room because it takes seven years to build a prison. Andy will need to get into the issues, but there are no easy choices because we will hit prison capacity by Christmas if he doesn’t do it.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionMahmood and Streeting accused of making ‘cheap jokes’ about prisoner early release schemeRead moreAndrea Coomber, the chief executive of the Howard League for Penal Reform, urged caution. “This is a system that is on the verge of collapsing under its own weight. When originally introducing these proposals, Shabana Mahmood described the alternative as being the ‘total breakdown of law and order’.“We need this government to finally grapple with the fact that, for 20 years, politicians have allowed sentence inflation to run rampant. With longer and longer sentences, we are sleepwalking our way into US-style mass incarceration.”Enver Solomon, the chief executive of the social justice charity Nacro, said prisons must not be allowed to fill up again. “It should not be forgotten that the reason the Labour government put in place an early release scheme was to ensure there isn’t yet another prisons capacity crisis,” he said.The former Conservative prisons minister Rory Stewart said: “Early release is not an easy choice – but it is the least bad option if we want to genuinely try to work intensively with prisoners and reduce reoffending.”Under the act, some serious offenders will be eligible for release halfway through their sentences rather than two-thirds of the way through. Those convicted of less serious crimes can be freed after serving a third of their sentence instead of 40%. Prisoners locked up for some violent or sexual offences are eligible for release halfway through their sentences rather than two-thirds.The bill was drawn up and introduced to parliament by Shabana Mahmood, the home secretary. TheConservative shadow home secretary,Chris Philp, wrote on X that the “early release scheme allows rapists and serious criminals out of prison early and it must be urgently cancelled”.He said releasing the jailed car passengers “is an insult to Andrew Harper’s memory and to police officers who put themselves at risk every day to protect us”.Burnham’s pledge to review the policy was welcomed by groups working with victims. The former safeguarding minister Jess Phillips told the Guardian it was important that “proper systems” were put in place to assess risk and involve victims. “There is a chance to radically change how victims are considered in the system and to improve victims of violence against women and girls’ end-to-end experience of the justice system,” she said.Ciara Bergman, the chief executive of Rape Crisis England and Wales, said: “Obviously there are significant challenges facing our government, including how overcrowding and under-resourcing is going to be managed, but the solutions to these challenges cannot come at survivors’ expense.”Katie Kempen, the chief executive at Victim Support, said the review was encouraging. “This review must urgently fix the fact that most victims currently have no way of knowing whether or not an offender could be released early. It is unacceptable that so many victims are being kept in the dark about this – that must change.”Explore more on these topicsPrisons and probationAndy BurnhamUK criminal justiceChris PhilpDavid LammynewsShareReuse this content

title: Woman, 70, charged with murder of British expat Karen Carter in France | UK news | The Guardian
description: Public prosecutor for the region said suspect had been initially arrested shortly after the murder on 29 April last year
Author: Jamie Grierson
Headline: Public prosecutor for the region said suspect had been initially arrested shortly after the murder on 29 April last year
Content: Karen Carter ran two gites in the village of Tremolat in south-west France.Photograph: FacebookView image in fullscreenKaren Carter ran two gites in the village of Tremolat in south-west France.Photograph: FacebookUK newsWoman, 70, charged with murder of British expat Karen Carter in FrancePublic prosecutor for the region said suspect had been initially arrested shortly after the murder on 29 April last yearJamie GriersonandKim WillsherWed 22 Jul 2026 14.28 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 14.18 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA 70-year-old woman has been charged with the murder of Karen Carter, the British expat found dead in south-westernFrancelast year.The woman was charged on 2 July with Carter’s murder in Trémolat, in the Dordogne, and is “under judicial supervision”.Jacques-Édouard Andrault, the public prosecutor for the region, is quoted as confirming the charge, adding the suspect had been arrested shortly after the murder on 29 April last year, but was released without charge at the time.Andrault said: “On July 2nd, the investigating judge in Périgueux formally charged a woman with murder.”He added that “this 70-year-old woman, originally from Trémolat, was taken into custody on April 30, 2025, during a preliminary investigation, before being released”.“Since then, the investigations conducted as part of the judicial inquiry opened on May 7, 2025, have led the investigating judge to indict her,” he said, adding “this woman has been placed under judicial supervision”.Carter, a 65-year-old British-South African national, ran twogites(holiday rental homes) in the village of Trémolat, a beautifully renovated 250-year-old farmhouse and neighbouring 18th-century stone barn collectively called Les Chouettes.She was said to divide her time between the Dordogne and the home she shared with her husband, Alan Carter, in KuGompo City, South Africa.An autopsy revealed that Carter died from severe blood loss after being stabbed multiple times, collapsing and dying next to her car.Eight serious wounds were noted in total, including to the “chest, abdomen and forearm”, as well as “superficial ones on the thigh, shoulder and thumbs”.Carter had been at a wine-tasting party at Cafe Village Trémolat, described on Facebook as “an association that wants to offer the people of Trémolat and the surrounding area a meeting place”, with about 15 other guests, including Eric Chassagne, the village’s mayor.A few days after the attack a then 69-year-old local woman was arrested. She was released without charge shortly after.It later emerged that Carter was discovered by a 75-year-old businessman, Jean-François Guerrier, with whom she was in a relationship. The nature of their relationship was seized upon and speculated over by tabloids.Carter’s husband, a former London Stock Exchange worker who was in South Africa at the time of the murder, expressed his shock at the revelations that his wife had “started a relationship” with another man, saying he understood Guerrier was “just a friend of hers”.Explore more on these topicsUK newsFrancenewsShareReuse this content

title: Possible bloodstains were detected at D4vd’s home, law enforcement testify | Los Angeles | The Guardian
description: Gory details about tarp, mats and inflatable pool come to light during hearing over death of Celeste Rivas Hernandez
Author: Uwa Ede-Osifo
Headline: Gory details about tarp, mats and inflatable pool come to light during hearing over death of Celeste Rivas Hernandez
Content: D4vd in court on Tuesday in Los Angeles.Photograph: Bill Robles via APView image in fullscreenD4vd in court on Tuesday in Los Angeles.Photograph: Bill Robles via APLos AngelesPossible bloodstains were detected at D4vd’s home, law enforcement testifyGory details about tarp, mats and inflatable pool come to light during hearing over death of Celeste Rivas HernandezUwa Ede-Osifoin Los AngelesWed 22 Jul 2026 19.40 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 14.09 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAt singer D4vd’s former Hollywood Hills home, law enforcement detected possible bloodstains – invisible to the naked eye – on items including a green tarp, black rubber mats and a crumpled inflatable pool that had the odor of fresh laundry.The gory details came to light on Wednesday, the second day of a hearing to determine whether there was probable cause for the musician, whose full name is David Anthony Burke, to stand trial for the murder and sexual abuse of Celeste Rivas Hernandez.Rivas Hernandez’s body was found last year in gruesome conditions in a car registered to the musician.Prosecutors accuse Burke of stabbing Rivas Hernandez, 14, to death at hisLos Angelesresidence to silence her from divulging the sexual nature of their relationship and jeopardizing his skyrocketing career.LA prosecutors present gruesome evidence in murder case against singer D4vdRead moreBurke has pleadednot guiltyto charges that include first-degree murder, “lewd and lascivious acts” with a child under the age of 14, and the mutilation of human remains. His defense team has said their client “was not the cause” of Rivas Hernandez’s death.Last September, Lauren Wallace, a criminalist with the Los Angeles police department, found two chainsaws in the garage of a Hollywood Hills home where the Houston-raised musician had been living. The chainsaws tested negative for possible blood.Rubber mats covering a garage floor, however, likely had blood stains, Wallace testified. Linear cuts, consistent with that of a sharp object, were found on the mats.Wallace also observed damage to the pool. “It appeared that it had been cut,” she said.Chelsea Murillo, another criminalist in LAPD’s forensic science division, examined the 2023 black Tesla Model X that contained Rivas Hernandez’s remains.A layer of dust coated the vehicle’s exterior, Murillo said on Wednesday, which suggested it had been outside for a prolonged period. In the front trunk, she observed maggots and flies. A strong odor that resembled a decomposing body came from the car, she said, even amid multiple air fresheners. Possible traces of blood were found in the Tesla’s third row.Defense attorney Blair Berk cast skepticism over the findings of the criminalists’ forensic testing.It’s “only presumptive. It doesn’t mean you found blood,” Berk asked during her cross-examination of Murillo.“Yes,” Murillo replied.The scope of the singer’s earnings also came into clear view on Wednesday, as prosecutors sought to establish the musician had financial motivation to kill Rivas Hernandez. Burke had a multimillion-dollar recording deal, an executive at his former label, Interscope, testified.The label terminated his contract in December 2025.From 2022 to 2025, Interscope paid Burke and affiliated entities more than $7.2m, a figure that encompassed legal fees and a trust mandated byCalifornialaw for underage entertainers, according to a summary of payments exhibited to the courtroom.Details of Burke’s compensation followed a testy exchange in the morning, as prosecutors countered the defense’s suggestion that their client was not privy to his finances.Attorneys for Burke repeatedly objected to questions from deputy district attorney Beth Silverman about whether the singer’s former financial manager, Benjamin Gregor, knew of Burke arranging tickets for Rivas Hernandez to travel to London and his home in Texas.Rivas Hernandez’s parents, Jesus Rivas and Mercedes Martinez, returned to court on Wednesday. Dressed in all black, the two sat in the first row, accompanied by their lawyer.Martinez dropped her head and wiped her eyes in the early afternoon, when prosecutors presented a photograph of her daughter’s passport, which a Caltrans employee discovered in January 2026 in a dirt embankment off State Route 154 in Santa Barbara.“Once he found the card, he Googled the name to try and see if it was someone that lived locally in the area. He began seeing articles and news stories and when he realized what he had found, he called our dispatch,” Sean Buenrostro, a California highway patrol officer, said on the stand.At the hearing’s start, prosecutors showed graphic photographs of Rivas Hernandez’s dismembered body and alleged the singer had severely mutilated her remains. They also presented evidence they say shows the singer used his credit cards to purchase chainsaws, a body bag and a shovel.The preliminary hearing is expected to last from three to five days.Burke, now 21, wasarrested in Apriland has been held in custody since then without bail. He has appeared in court in an orange jumpsuit and thick black glasses.If convicted of murder, he could face the death penalty, or life in prison without the possibility of parole.Prosecutors have not yet indicated whether they will pursue capital punishment.After court concluded on Wednesday, Patrick Steinfeld, an attorney for the Rivas Hernandez family, spoke to reporters outside.“It’s a hard thing for the family. They are very private individuals and they’re doing the best they can,” he said in response to a question about how Rivas Hernandez’s parents reacted to the evidence presented about the Tesla.“They have a hole in their heart that will never be replaced,” he said.The Associated Press contributed to this reportExplore more on these topicsLos AngelesCaliforniaWest CoastnewsShareReuse this content

title: Trump scoffs at ‘affordability’ as he touts economic policies at Georgia rally | Donald Trump | The Guardian
description: President visits swing state to promote ‘Trump accounts’ and says high prices lingering from Joe Biden’s presidency
Author: George Chidi
Headline: President visits swing state to promote ‘Trump accounts’ and says high prices lingering from Joe Biden’s presidency
Content: Trump speaks at Wheeler high school in Marietta, Georgia, on Wednesday.Photograph: Evelyn Hockstein/ReutersView image in fullscreenTrump speaks at Wheeler high school in Marietta, Georgia, on Wednesday.Photograph: Evelyn Hockstein/ReutersDonald TrumpTrump scoffs at ‘affordability’ as he touts economic policies at Georgia rallyPresident visits swing state to promote ‘Trump accounts’ and says high prices lingering from Joe Biden’s presidencyGeorge Chidiin Marietta, GeorgiaWed 22 Jul 2026 19.31 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 19.32 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDonald Trump scoffed at the word “affordability” as a political message, describing it at a campaign rally on Wednesday as a term concocted by Democratic political consultants to criticize his presidency. Yet even as the president dismissed the notion of affordability, he devoted much of his speech at Wheeler high school to touting the bread-and-butter benefits of his economic policies.US trade chief defends tariffs as Democrats say Trump ‘in denial’ over price hikesRead more“So I just want you to remember that if you’re paying a little bit more – a lot of it’s come down now, we got it coming down very rapidly actually,” Trump said in Marietta, an Atlanta suburb, casting higher prices today as a lingering effect of the Biden administration.Americans are broadly disappointed with Trump’s handling of the economy, with just 33% of voters approving in a recentWashington Post-Ipsos poll, an assessment that could hurt his party’s hopes of bucking historical precedent and retaining control of Congress in November’s midterm elections.The president was inGeorgia, a politically divided state where high-profile contests for governor and Senate are playing out, to promote his newly funded “Trump accounts”, which establish tax-free investment funds for every child born through 2028, seeded with $1,000 from the federal government.Speaking before the president, treasury secretary Scott Bessent described the accounts as the “triumph of capitalism over socialism”, taking a swipe at New York mayor Zohran Mamdani.“Look around our campuses and classrooms today – apart from those here at Wheeler, of course – you will find that our students are being sold the seductive lie that government can solve every problem, redistribute every dollar and supplant its judgment for our own,” Bessent said.“In New York, the current mayor rose to power by repackaging failed ideals of the past as a moral cause of the future. But in Georgia, you know that beneath that youthful veneer lies the oldest impulse in politics which is to concentrate power and then to call it compassion.”Several Republican candidates joined Trump in Marietta, where the president likened US senator Jon Ossoff, a Democrat seeking re-election in one of the most closely watched races of the cycle, to the character Pee-wee Herman, portrayed by the late actor Paul Reubens. Polling shows Ossoff with a lead over the Republican nominee in the Senate race, US congressman Mike Collins, who was in Washington where his vote was needed to helppass a budget billto fund the Iran war and other White House priorities.Rick Jackson, Georgia’s Republican candidate for governor, pledged to ask the state legislature to match the $1,000 federal contribution to childhood Trump investment accounts if elected. If they failed to do so, Jackson said he would match the figure himself.Jackson is the founder of Jackson Healthcare, a medical staffing company. His personal net worth has been estimated to be more than $3bn.In 2024, the state of Georgia recorded more than 126,000 live births, which equates to $126m in annual contributions for Trump accounts. Georgia began the 2026 fiscal year with an unprecedented budget cushion of about $15bn in the bank, with $9bn in unrestricted funds. This surplus resulted from conservative budgeting that began in 2021 as a reaction to pandemic pressures.That money has become an intense political question, withRepublicanslooking for ways to provide property tax relief while Democrats arguing for a broader investment in health care and child care support.Parents, friends and employers will be able to deposit as much as $5,000 a year into the Trump accounts, which will be controlled by parents and guardians until their children turn 18, after which the account holder gains control and can use saved-up funds to pay for college, buy a home or start a business. The $1,000 contributions will cost the government $17bn through 2028, according to the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget.“For decades, Washington politicians gave our children absolutely nothing but debt,” Trump said. “But the Trump accounts, we’re finally seeing and ensuring that every young American gets a great start in life and a really fair shot at the American dream, which is what we all want. They all aspire to that.”Trump increased thenational debtby about $8tn in his first term, more than any other president on an inflation-adjusted basis and nearly as much as the government accrued during Barack Obama’s two terms in office. Spending during Trump’s second term is on pace with his first-term mark, with an additional $3bn accrued so far.Inflation currently stands at about 3%, with a decrease of 0.4% last month, driven primarily by a nearly 10% drop in gasoline and oil prices following the short-lived truce in the Iran war. Core inflation was flat for the first time in five years.But gasoline prices have again risen above $4 a gallon in the US, according to AAAtracking, a direct result of oil prices climbing after reawakened hostilities with Iran and the contested strait of Hormuz, a critical waterway for global oil and gas shipments.Trump said he had just come from Dover air force base in Delaware to attend the dignified transfer ceremony for US service members killed in the conflict with Iran. The family of 1st Lt Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii accompanied the president on Air Force One to Georgia and attended the event in Marietta, Trump said.Trump continued to claim that his policies have drawn pledges of $19.2tn into the US economy, a figure thateconomistsfrom the libertarian Cato Institute and elsewhere consider inflated by illusory investment promises.Explore more on these topicsDonald TrumpGeorgiaUS politicsTrump administrationRepublicansnewsShareReuse this content

title: Anti-doping chiefs reject claims of Paul Seixas favouritism as Tour rivals cry foul | Tour de France 2026 | The Guardian
description: The home rider Paul Seixas has yet to be tested at night, unlike some of his rivals, but authorities deny that the French doping agency plays any role in who is tested
Author: Jeremy Whittle
Headline: The home rider Paul Seixas has yet to be tested at night, unlike some of his rivals, but authorities deny that the French doping agency plays any role in who is tested
Content: The French rider Paul Seixas is presently fourth in the Tour de France overall standings.Photograph: Loïc Venance/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThe French rider Paul Seixas is presently fourth in the Tour de France overall standings.Photograph: Loïc Venance/AFP/Getty ImagesTour de France 2026Anti-doping chiefs reject claims of Paul Seixas favouritism as Tour rivals cry foulHome rider Seixas yet to be tested at night, unlike some rivalsAuthorities deny French doping agency plays role in who is testedJeremy Whittlein VoironWed 22 Jul 2026 13.45 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 19.21 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAnti-doping authorities at theTour de Francehave rejected accusations of preferential treatment towards Paul Seixas, the teenage French rider and home favourite who is placed fourth overall.Seixas and his Decathlon CMA CGM team were tested, with the rest of the peloton, on Monday evening as part of the biological passport testing programme, but the 19-year-old has not yet been subjected to the nocturnal test that both the race leader Tadej Pogacar andthe now-withdrawn Jonas Vingegaardunderwent.Vingegaard’s and Pogacar’s night-time doping tests authorised by Paris judgeRead moreThe Slovenian Matej Mohoric, who rides for the Bahrain Victorious team and was among those visited at 5am, said: “It would be good to guarantee equal treatment for everyone. After all, we know that this French rider [Seixas], who is well placed in the general classification, didn’t undergo that [night-time] test.”The International Testing Agency denied suggestions of bias, saying: “The ITA is responsible for the strategy of the anti-doping programme for the Tour de France, not the AFLD [the French anti-doping agency]. We have our own sample collection personnel for the doping controls.“AFLD might occasionally support the ITA with additional sample collection personnel, if needed, for instance during the testing mission on Monday evening but the decision who is tested when remains with the ITA.”The ITA said: “Our decisions are based on facts, risk assessments, performances, scientific results and insights, intelligence and investigations, as well as data analysis. This approach enables us to act as precisely and impartially as possible, independently of any external influence, political interest or opinion.”Professional cycling’s governing body, the UCI, has now fully endorsed the ITA’s night-time testing regime, but insists: “Carrying out such controls outside normal hours remains an exceptional measure, reserved for limited and justified circumstances.”Mohoric said:“I was tested at 5am myself. That’s practically the middle of the night, given that we go to bed around midnight because the stages finish late and we have breakfast around 10am.“They have to prove they [the tests] are working and ensuring the sport remains clean and fair,” Mohoric said of the ITA. “We are paying the price for cycling’s past.”View image in fullscreenThe defending champion and race leader Tadej Pogacar is one of several riders who have had doping tests at night.Photograph: Gonzalo Fuentes/ReutersOn Wednesday afternoon, the UCI said that the ITA’s activities demonstrate that “the fight against doping is an absolute priority”, adding: “The UCI acknowledges that night-time testing can disrupt riders’ rest and recovery [but] these controls serve an overriding interest: the fight against doping.”On Wednesday a rare breakaway move succeeded as Jasper Philipsen led home a reduced main peloton in the 175-kilometre 17th stage from Chambéry to Voiron.In a fast and chaotic stage, in which multiple groups formed and reformed, the last man standing Jasper Stuyven was reeled in on the climb to La Commanderie, just 4km from the finish.That set up a sprint finish and a first win in this year’s race for Philipsen, who was thwarted earlier in the Tour by Tim Merlier, who has now abandoned the race, and the Dutch sprinter Olav Kooij, teammate to Seixas.But as Philipsen, the winner of the points classification in 2023, celebrated an 11th career Tour stage win, Adam Yates, a key member of the race leader Pogacar’s mountain support, was enduring a stomach bug at the back of the peloton.The British rider crossed the finish line third from last, looking pale and exhausted, having been sick for the past 48 hours. “He looked terrible,” Pogacar said of his teammate during the time trial on
