Tuesday.With three mountain-top finishes now coming, first at Orcières-Merlette and then the dreaded double stage finish at Alpe d’Huez, on Friday and Saturday, Yates would be expected to play a key role in the champion’s title defence.“Today he looked much better, and was eating good,” Pogacar said, “but when you have a bad day like yesterday, you pay a day later. I hope he didn’t go too deep into the hole and he will be better tomorrow.”Get in touchContact Guardian SportThe best public interest journalism relies on first-hand accounts from people in the know. If you have something to share on this subject, you can contact us confidentially using the following methods:Secure Messaging in the Guardian appThe Guardian app has a tool to send tips about stories. Messages are end to end encrypted and concealed within the routine activity that every Guardian mobile app performs. This prevents an observer from knowing that you are communicating with us at all, let alone what is being said.If you don’t already have the Guardian app, download it (iOS/Android) and go to the menu. Select ‘Secure Messaging’. When asked to select a recipient, select the ‘Sport (UK and Global)’ team.SecureDropIf you can safely use the tor network without being observed or monitored you can send messages and documents to the Guardian via ourSecureDrop platform.Finally, our guide attheguardian.com/tipslists several ways to contact us securely, and discusses the pros and cons of each.Show moreExplore more on these topicsTour de France 2026Tour de FranceCyclingnewsShareReuse this content

title: World Cup 2030 (very early) power rankings: who will challenge Spain at top? | World Cup | The Guardian
description: The next men’s World Cup is still four years away, but we take a look at the teams with the firmest foundations who should be primed to contend
Author: Jeff Rueter
Headline: The next men’s World Cup is still four years away, but we take a look at the teams with the firmest foundations who should be primed to contend
Content: Norway’s Erling Haaland, Morocco’s Ismael Saibari and Spain’s Lamine Yamal should all play significant roles for their teams in 2030.Composite: Guardian Design; Icon Sportswire/Getty Images; Getty Images; Fifa/Getty ImageView image in fullscreenNorway’s Erling Haaland, Morocco’s Ismael Saibari and Spain’s Lamine Yamal should all play significant roles for their teams in 2030.Composite: Guardian Design; Icon Sportswire/Getty Images; Getty Images; Fifa/Getty ImageWorld CupWorld Cup 2030 (very early) power rankings: who will challenge Spain at top?The next men’s World Cup is still four years away, but we take a look at the teams with the firmest foundations who should be primed to contendJeff RueterWed 22 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 09.27 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe 2026World Cupprovided many an esteemed veteran with what could be their international swansongs, with golden greats Lionel Messi, Cristiano Ronaldo, Luka Modric, Neymar, James Rodríguez and Guillermo Ochoa all making teary exits as their tournament ended. As we turn ahead to the 2030 World Cup, any player 32 or older will be sweating their selection status and changing their daily routines to stay sharp against inevitable aging.For these power rankings, we’re overvaluing sure things. Teams with plenty of leading players comfortably in their 20s can build for this impending four-year cycle with greater confidence than a team replacing an ageless talisman. Consider these the 10 teams with the firmest foundations and viable reasons to hope to reach a quarter-final in four years’ time.Mentioned players’ ages on 1 June 2030 listed (in parentheses).The quirky side of World Cup 2026 – in picturesRead more1) SpainThe first defending World Cup champion to host the ensuing installment, Spain will enter 2030 as favorites to run it back. With Luis de la Fuente’s masterful sense of his team’s strengths and universal commitment from his players, this group could develop into a juggernaut.Unai Simón(32),Joan García(29) andDavid Raya(34) could repeat as the goalkeeping trio.Marc Pubill(26),Eric García(29),Pedro Porro(30) andPau Cubarsí(23) are a solid defensive bedrock.Dani Olmo(32),Gavi(25) andPedri(27) should return in midfield, whileRodri(33) stands a good chance to start at the midfield’s base.Nico Williams(27) andLamine Yamal(22) will hope to enjoy better fitness but demonstrated their importance in the final stages, andFerran Torres(30) may be a threat from the bench again.And, of course, there’s great talent beyond the title-winning 26.Dean Huijsen(25) andCristhian Mosquera(25) are options to replace Aymeric Laporte (36), whileFermin López(27),Pablo Barrios(26),Javier Guerra(27),Marc Bernal(23) andJesús Rodríguez(24) have featured under de la Fuente. IfSamu Aghehowa(26) orGonzalo García(26) can become a viable option at striker, Spain could wring even more goals from their possessive machine – a scary thought, indeed.2) FranceA fresh touchline voice arrives just in time, with Zinedine Zidane replacing the great Didier Deschamps after decreasing returns across his three World Cups. What lessons can Zizou glean from their stunning semi-final crumble against Spain?Mike Maignan(34) should have another cycle in his capable gloves, shielded byDayot Upamecano(31),Ibrahima Konaté(31) andWilliam Saliba(29). The midfield is in good stead withManu Koné(29) andAurélien Tchouaméni(30), as well as squad snubsEduardo Camavinga(27) andKhéphren Thuram(29).Kylian Mbappé(31) will be raring to extend his goalscoring record withMichael Olise(28) andDésiré Doué(24) in support. Talent wasn’t the issue so much as overcoming Spain’s structure, and for all his success with Real Madrid, Zidane impressed more with his squad management than his tactical chops.3) EnglandThe Three Lions showed impressive resolve, outlasting Mexico in an instant classic and coming back against Norway to return to the semi-final. Their timid failures to close out a 2-0 lead over Argentina will haunt many, but the reality is that England are poised for a very strong response at Euro 2028 and the next World Cup – with one glaring asterisk.Marc Guéhi(29) looked a leading center-back for much of the tournament, withDjed Spence(29) having finished the World Cup in top form.Declan Rice(31),Elliot Anderson(27),Jude Bellingham(26) andKobbie Mainoo(25) offer plenty of midfield combinations, andBukayo Saka(28) andAnthony Gordon(29) fared well on the wings. The biggest question is whetherHarry Kanecan be a 90-minute option at age 36, as England hasn’t yet found an obvious successor to their leading goalscorer. Goalkeeper should also be an open battle, but players who missed Thomas Tuchel’s squad such asLewis Hall(25),Cole Palmer(28),Adam Wharton(26),Alex Scott(26),Phil Foden(30),Rio Ngumoha(21) andAyden Heaven(23) reiterate the depth of the pool.View image in fullscreenDjed Spence had a good tournament for England and will be 29 at the time of the next World Cup.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty Images4) MoroccoThe groundwork is excellent for another of 2030’s primary co-hosts. Back-to-back World Cup quarter-final runs, with one berth in the semis. Legal custody of the Afcon 2025 title. A freshly installed coach who got more verve from his side without sacrificing stability. Can Morocco take things a step or two further in 2030?Achraf Hakimi(31),Chadi Riad(26),Anass Salah-Eddine(28) andNoussair Mazraoui(32) project to stick around in defense.Ayyoub Bouaddi(22) should be even better after his emergent World Cup, along with his midfield partnerAzzedine Ounahi(30).Ismael Saibari(29) was a safe bet to score before his injury, whileBilal El Khannouss(26),Gessime Yassine(24) andBrahim Díaz(30) also challenge any backline.Morocco won the most recent U-20 World Cup, led by forwardsOthmane Maamma(24) andYassir Zabiri(25) as well as goalkeeperIbrahim Gomis(25). If goalkeeping heroYassine Bounou(39) can somehow stay in his usual blistering form, they’ll be a tough draw for anybody.5) ArgentinaArgentina’s run to the final reiterated how uniquely vital Messi was to his team, both as the determinant of their attack and the heart every teammate fought hard to protect. He is, by every definition of the word, irreplaceable.Then again, Argentina were not just a one-man band.Julián Álvarez(30) has grownincreasingly comfortable as a leading man, and should again be backed up by lethalLautaro Martínez(32).Enzo Fernández(33) has a lot of apologies to give after his send-off, but should anchor midfield this cycle.Nahuel Molina(32),Cristian Romero(32) andLisandro Martínez(32) will be veteran defenders, whileEmiliano Martínez(37) should have plenty left to offer. They also were runners-up at the last U-20 World Cup, with standouts including midfielderMilton Delgado(24), left-backJulio Soler(25) and goalkeeperSantino Barbi(24).skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Twice weeklySign up toSoccer Desk: World Cup EditionJonathan Wilson and Guardian journalists bring expert analysis and reporting on the biggest stories in soccer.Preview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionNeuer to Neymar: 11 World Cup players who retired from international footballRead more6) PortugalPortugal were left to wonder what could have been with a more natural and mobile line-leader atop an otherwise terrific team. Ronaldo hasadmitted he won’t play in the 2030 tournamentat age 45, and the biggest question will become whether Roberto Martínez squandered this generation’s best chance to win a first World Cup.Diogo Costa(30) is among the world’s best goalkeepers, withGonçalo Inácio(28) andNuno Mendes(27) forming a strong defensive base.António Silva(26) will hope to get his promising career back on track, seemingly with Bournemouth, whileJoão Neves(25) andVitinha(30) would start in nearly any nation’s midfield.Gonçalo Ramos(28),João Félix(30) andFrancisco Conceição(27) return in attack, too. But with the list of aging players including the greatBruno Fernandes(35),Bernardo Silva(35) andRúben Dias(33), it’s hard not to feel the best opportunity has been missed.7) NorwayFew will underestimate Norway after their deserved run to the quarter-final, with a culture in and around the team that became the envy of the summer. Many of their men should be back in four years, including themain-est manof all.Few people seemed to have enjoyed this World Cup as much asErling Haaland(29), who should still be devastating defenses when the next one comes around.Andreas Schjelderup(26),Antonio Nusa(25) andOscar Bobb(26) are credible running mates in the final third, whileMartin Ødegaard(31) will hope to shirk his injury bugs to pull the strings once again. There will need to be ample rotation in the defensive half and in goal, but as long as Haaland is leading the line, Norway will stand a chance.View image in fullscreenThe future is bright for Amad Diallo and his Côte d’Ivoire teammates.Photograph: Mike Segar/Reuters8) Côte d’IvoireThe Elephants impressed in a tricky Group E, beating Ecuador and Curaçao before falling to Norway in the last-32. If Emerse Faé is back to lead this team, he’ll enjoy an immense level of talent to work with as (part of) the tournament returns to Africa.Yahia Fofana(29) is steady in goal, his job made easier by the collective defending ofOusmane Diomande(26),Odilon Kossounou(29) andWilfried Singo(29). Their forward options pose champagne selection issues:Amad Diallo(27),Oumar Diakité(26),Elye Wahi(27),Yan Diomandé(23),Simon Adingra(28) andAnge-Yoan Bonny(26). Midfield appears to be their only thin area especially ifFranck Kessié(33) ages out of starting status. But on paper, and on the back of a first World Cup knockout match and the Afcon 2023 title, they’re a team to watch.9) GermanyWhile the legion of club managers making their World Cup debuts in 2026 saw mixed returns, Jürgen Klopp has four years to build his ideal culture and refine a new system forDie Mannschaft. That seems a dream scenario after a trio of disappointing tournaments on the back of 2014’s triumph.The bigger question is whether Germany have the players. An injury toLennart Karl(22) on the eve of the World Cup put a damper on preparations, but the Bayern playmaker would be a welcome addition.Kai Havertz(30) should pop up with timely goals, whileFlorian Wirtz(27) andJamal Musiala(27) will hope to arrive in better form and fitness.Nathaniel Brown(26) looks to be an ideal wing-back for the next few cycles, andJoshua Kimmich(35) has already dismissed concerns that he may retire before Klopp arrives. That’s a relief given how much turnover will be necessary from this summer’s squad in the defensive half.10) United StatesThe Balogun Affair stripped the sheen off of what had been the US’s most promising men’s World Cup showing to date. Mauricio Pochettino’s status for the next cycle is uncertain. Still, the Americans’ run to the last 16 saw many players of a similar age group step up and project to be mainstays.Folarin Balogun(28) should be an even more refined forward to follow his three-goal showing this summer, whileMalik Tillman(28) offers strong dead-ball service and tidy midfield play.Chris Richards(30),Tyler Adams(31),Weston McKennie(31),Christian Pulisic(31) andAlex Freeman(25) will probably continue to be foundational pieces, whileSebastian Berhalter(29),Gio Reyna(27) andRicardo Pepi(27) should stick around after capable World Cup showings. A 12th-place finish still indicates a last-16 exit that has become the frustrating norm for the US, but perhaps the lessons of a high-pressure hosting gigwill really pay off in four years.Explore more on these topicsWorld CupWorld Cup 2026featuresShareReuse this content

title: Why many Black Americans were rooting for Argentina to lose the World Cup | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: Many Black Americans were not cheering Spain’s complicated history. They were rooting against the racism, politics and silence associated with Argentina
Author: Etan Thomas
Headline: Many Black Americans were not cheering Spain’s complicated history. They were rooting against the racism, politics and silence associated with Argentina
Content: Argentina’s Lionel Messi looks on after Sunday’s World Cup final.Photograph: Jewel Samad/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenArgentina’s Lionel Messi looks on after Sunday’s World Cup final.Photograph: Jewel Samad/AFP/Getty ImagesWorld Cup 2026Why many Black Americans were rooting for Argentina to lose the World CupEtan ThomasMany Black Americans were not cheering Spain’s complicated history. They were rooting against the racism, politics and silence associated with ArgentinaWed 22 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 06.46 EDTShareThere are moments in sports when you realize people aren’t just watching the score any more. They’re watching values. They’re watching who speaks. They’re watching who stays silent. And they’re watching what those silences say.I found myself thinking about that throughout this World Cup. On paper, Sunday’s final looked simple.SpainvArgentina. Two footballing giants and countries with glorious soccer histories. Two nations whose pasts are also stained by colonization, oppression and chapters of history that shouldn’t be whitewashed simply because someone can score a goal.Etan ThomasSo no, this wasn’t some fairytale of good versus evil. Spain’s colonial legacy is well documented. Nobody who knows history should pretend otherwise.Yet for many Black Americans, this final wasn’t really about rootingforSpain. It was about rootingagainstArgentina.When your fanbase repeatedly becomes associated with racist incidents … when videos circulate of Argentinian fans decked out in Argentina team gear hurling racial epithets toward Black people … when the world watched members of Argentina’s national teamsing a racist chant targeting France’s Black playersafter winning Copa América … when American streamer IShowSpeed was verbally assaulted by fans fully dressed in Argentina team gear … when word spreads across social media that in Argentina, there is a real hamburger business in Llavallol whose official name uses the “-a” version of the N-word:“Big N– Burgers”… when Argentina president Javier Milei openly embraces positions that align very closely with Donald Trump and offers support for Israel in the midst of a genocide, ethnic cleansing and slaughter of Palestinians … when Benjamin Netanyahu is asked on a recent podcast if he was rooting for Messi and says, “No, before Messi, Milei. He’s a superstar” … eventually, the tide of supports shifts. After a while, any accomplishments on the field become overshadowed.It’s like a college basketball coach who has all the appearances of a great program. He has a great winning percentage and a high graduation rate, and all of his players are disciplined, articulate and present themselves as professionals. But you keep hearing stories of verbal abuse and berating, whispers of a tyrant coach who is actively damaging his players’ mental health. At some point, the results become secondary and people start asking, “What’s going on with the Indiana Hoosiers?”‘Rifts began to appear’: Why many Latin Americans want Spain to beat Argentina in the World Cup finalRead moreArgentina kept giving people reasons to ask that question. Which brings us toLionel Messi.This is where people get uncomfortable because everybody loves Messi, and for good reason. His brilliance doesn’t require exaggeration. His résumé speaks louder than any sports debate ever could. Whether you think he’s the greatest player ever or merely one of them – shoutout toPelé– you’re arguing over different shades of greatness.But greatness on the field has never automatically translated into courage off it.Muhammad Ali taught us that. Bill Russell taught us that.Mahmoud Abdul-Rauftaught us that.Craig Hodgestaught us that. John Carlos and Tommie Smith taught us that. Being extraordinary with a ball, a bat or track shoes isn’t the same thing as being willing to risk comfort for conviction.To my knowledge, throughout this entireWorld Cup, Messi never publicly condemned the racist incidents involving sections of Argentina’s fanbase. He did not publicly address the racist chant that drew worldwide criticism after Copa América. Nor has he publicly spoken out after videos of Argentina supporters racially abusing Black people circulated online.He didn’t even offer the kind of broad, indirect condemnation that many critics argued was insufficient when Caitlin Clarkaddressed racist abuse coming from some of her supporters. As my co-host Chuck Modi argued on our showThe Collision: Where Sports and Politics Collide:“She has to be more specific. When Alyssa Thomas was getting death threats, she has to say Alyssa Thomas by name, and say directly to her fans that you cannot have death threats going to Alyssa Thomas in my name the same way we as Jewish people say not in my name for Palestinian rights. She needs to use all of her clout and tell her fans this is unacceptable.”Messi did not even clear that lower bar. He offered no public statement at all addressing the racism carried out by some of his supporters.As Dr Martin Luther King Jr said in his Beyond Vietnam speech: “A time comes when silence is betrayal.”Sometimes silence becomes its own statement simply because the loudest voice in the room chose not to use it. Contrast that with the generation coming after Messi.Lamine Yamal has spoken openly about his family’s sacrifices. He has recalled how his father searched through discarded items that could be repaired or sold so there would be enough money for food. He has said he grew up in a tiny apartment where the kitchen and bedroom were one and the same. Today, he says what makes him happiest is seeing his mother smile and knowing his younger brother can enjoy the childhood he never had.Nico Williams carries a similarly powerful story. His mother, María, walked nearly 2,000 miles while pregnant, from Ghana toward Spain, in search of a better life. Decades later, her son set up the goal that won Spain the World Cup and then placed his winner’s medal around her neck.Spain’s triumph was never just about tactics, possession or technical excellence. It was about possibility. It was a team that reflected the modern Spain – diverse, confident and shaped by the journeys of families who crossed borders in pursuit of a better life. That, more than any passing sequence or tactical innovation, was their most powerful statement.After Spain defeated Argentina on Sunday, one image stood out to me: Lamine Yamal embracing Messi. No taunting or chest-pounding or disrespect, just respect from one generation to another. Like the old African Proverb says: “The lion doesn’t need to roar after every meal.”Another image that resonated was seeing Lamine Yamal kneel in prayer amid the chaos around him while emotions boiled over after the match. While others argued, he prayed. That image reminded me that composure can be louder than confrontation.Lamine Yamal has also shown a willingness to let people know where he stands on issues beyond football, including expressing solidarity with Palestine. Whether people agree with every public stance an athlete takes is almost beside the point. The point is that he is willing to recognize that his platform extends beyond 90 (or 120) minutes on the field.Jackie Robinson wasn’t remembered simply because he stole bases. Kareem Abdul-Jabbar isn’t remembered simply because he had the most unstoppable move the NBA has ever seen. Colin Kaepernick isn’t remembered simply because of his dual-threat quarterbacking skills. Eventually the statistics become paragraphs, but character becomes chapters.That’s why comparisons between Messi and Ali don’t really work for me. One changed football forever, but the other changed the world.Some people have asked why so many Black Americans seemed emotionally invested in seeing Argentina lose. I don’t think it’s complicated. When you’ve spent your entire life hearing people tell you sports and politics should stay separate – but somehow they only say that after racism enters the conversation – you start recognizing the pattern.People told Ali to “just box”. People told Kaepernick to “just play football”. People told LeBron James to “shut up and dribble”. People effectively accusedJaylen Brownof being “uppity”. Apparently athletes are free to use their platforms – unless they’re using them for something that makes people uncomfortable.That’s always been the rule, until it isn’t.This World Cup became about more than goals because people made it about more than goals. Racism made it bigger. Politics made it bigger. Silence made it bigger. For many viewers, supporting Spain wasn’t an endorsement of Spain’s history; it was simply rejecting what Argentina had come to represent in this particular moment. Sometimes cheering isn’t about choosing perfection, but about choosing the option that feels less harmful.Maybe that’s why so many people found themselves drawn to this young generation. Not simply because they won, but because they represent the possibility that excellence and conviction don’t have to play on different teams. To quote Abdul-Rauf from our interview for my bookPolice Brutality and White Supremacy: The Fight Against American Traditions: “What you do speaks so loudly in my ears that I can’t hear a word you’re saying.”I couldn’t agree more.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026World CupSpainArgentinaLionel MessicommentShareReuse this content

title: Inter Miami sign Casemiro as MLS announces investigation into tampering | MLS | The Guardian
description: The midfielder has signed a short deal with an extension option, while a league investigation is centered on the mechanics of the transfer
Author: Alexander Abnos
Headline: The midfielder has signed a short deal with an extension option, while a league investigation is centered on the mechanics of the transfer
Content: Casemiro has 91 caps with Brazil.Photograph: Martin Rickett/PAView image in fullscreenCasemiro has 91 caps with Brazil.Photograph: Martin Rickett/PAMLSInter Miami sign Casemiro as MLS announces investigation into tamperingMidfielder signs a one-year deal with extension optionLeague investigation centers on the transferAlexander AbnosWed 22 Jul 2026 13.42 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 13.46 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleInter Miami have signed Casemiro as a free agent, but Major League Soccer is investigating the South Florida side for potential tampering in the process of acquiring the Brazilian midfielder.Casemiro has signed what amounts to a one-year contract with Miami, beginning now and ending at the conclusion of the 2027 “sprint season” that precedes the league’s switch to a fall-to-spring calendar in 2027-28. There is an option for an extension that would see the Brazilian stay in Miami through the 2028-29MLSseason.Both the club and league announcement said that Inter Miami had acquired the discovery rights to Casemiro from the Los Angeles Galaxy. The league did not specify how or under what terms Miami acquired Casemiro’s discovery priority.MLS return storylines: Berhalter’s future, Son’s goal drought and what’s next for Messi?Read more“Major League Soccer is reviewing a tampering allegation against Inter Miami CF,” the MLS statement said. “The league is gathering all relevant information and will refrain from further comment until the review is complete. While Inter Miami CF and the LA Galaxy have reached a settlement for the Discovery Priority to sign Casemiro, the terms will be released upon conclusion of the tampering investigation.”Casemiro, a five-time Champions League winner with Real Madrid, recently completed a four-season stint at Manchester United. He played with Brazil at the 2026 World Cup as theSeleçaowere eliminated by Norway in the last 16. A strong defensive midfielder, Casemiro joins an Inter Miami side already boasting Lionel Messi, Rodrigo De Paul and Luis Suárez as the stars of a team defending their 2025 MLS Cup title win.In MLS, most major player acquisitions from outside the league are handled via the discovery process. In the discovery process, each team maintains a list of up to five players for whom they have priority to sign should they decide to come to the league. However, that does not mean that only that club can sign that player – MLS clubs can work out deals with each other to transfer the discovery rights from one club to the other, enabling the other to enter into negotiations with the player. Marco Reus, for example, was initially on Charlotte FC’s discovery list when the LA Galaxy sought to sign him in 2024, resulting in the Galaxy paying Charlotte $400,000 for Reus’ discovery rights.MLS’s announcement of the investigation into Inter Miami did not specify whether the tampering charge concerned any negotiations Inter Miami may have had with Casemiro directly before acquiring his discovery rights, or whether it was focused more on the acquisition of the discovery rights.The 34-year-old is considered one of the best midfielders of his generation. The São Paulo native moved to Real Madrid in 2013 and went on to win those five Champions League titles in addition to three La Liga crowns and a Copa del Rey. His biggest success with Manchester United came in winning the 2023-24 FA Cup.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Twice weeklySign up toSoccer Desk: World Cup EditionJonathan Wilson and Guardian journalists bring expert analysis and reporting on the biggest stories in soccer.Preview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“Casemiro embodies everything this club stands for: leadership, a winning mentality, and an extraordinary track record at the highest level of the game,” Inter Miami managing owner Jorge Mas said in the club’s announcement.Inter Miami co-owner David Beckham added: “He is a person and a player that I have admired for a long time. He is a winner who has achieved so much in the game and after such an incredible career with Real Madrid and Manchester United I’m delighted that he’s decided to make Miami his next home.”Explore more on these topicsMLSUS sportsBrazilnewsShareReuse this content

title: As Dodgers head to White House, loyal fans are conflicted: ‘Trump is tearing this city apart’ | Trump administration | The Guardian
description: After ICE raids and protest crackdown, the LA team’s supporters are ambivalent about the World Series winners’ visit
Author: Andrew Gumbel
Headline: After ICE raids and protest crackdown, the LA team’s supporters are ambivalent about the World Series winners’ visit
Content: ‘At the end of the day, it’s jarring to see players smiling with a person who harbors hatred toward our community.’Photograph: Kevin Dietsch/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘At the end of the day, it’s jarring to see players smiling with a person who harbors hatred toward our community.’Photograph: Kevin Dietsch/Getty ImagesTrump administrationAs Dodgers head to White House, loyal fans are conflicted: ‘Trump is tearing this city apart’After ICE raids and protest crackdown, the LA team’s supporters are ambivalent about the World Series winners’ visitAndrew Gumbelin Los AngelesWed 22 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 08.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleLike many children of Latino immigrants inLos Angeles, Juan grew up a Dodgers fan. He watched their baseball games from home when he was little – even though his parents, who are Guatemalan, were more interested in soccer and basketball – and has been to Dodger Stadium many times as an adult. He sports a Dodgers logo, among other symbols, on an elaborate tattoo on his left forearm.These days, though, he’s not sure about his allegiance. Juan – whom the Guardian is only identifying by his first name amid the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown – saw his family ripped apart when his father, who had been in the US for close to 30 years, was plucked off the street last year and deported. From that moment on, a team that many immigrants in LA see as a symbol of integration and belonging became, for him, an organization unwilling to stand up for its large immigrant fanbase.In the preceding months, as the Trump administration conducted heavy-handed raids inLos Angelesand sent national guard troops and marines to respond to the ensuing protests, two of the city’s soccer teams had put out statements of solidarity with immigrant families – but not the Dodgers.Juan also knew that Guggenheim Partners, the multibillion-dollar ownership group behind the franchise, had a stake in the Geo Group, a major private operator of immigration detention centers that works under contract withUS immigrationauthorities.Juan went to one game late last season, at the invitation of a church organization supporting his family, but he couldn’t stop thinking about Geo Group and the hundreds of dollars his family had been forced to pay for items with shockinglyhigh mark-upswhile his father was in detention: $50 to open a phone account, $80 for a rudimentary long-sleeve shirt to protect him against frigid temperatures (roughly what the Dodgers charge for amuch thicker, high end team hoodie), and more for basic necessities from the commissary.View image in fullscreenDozens of people protest outside Dodger Stadium, criticizing the Los Angeles Dodgers for their lack of support for immigrants and their cooperation with ICE on 21 June.Photograph: Katie McTiernan/Anadolu via Getty ImagesThe idea that the Dodgers were part of a corporate structure benefiting from these charges was more than he could stomach. “I don’t think I’ve watched a full Dodger game since,” he said, “and I definitely have not been back. For anyone who has been truly affected [by the administration’s policies], it’s kind of hard. I don’t want to give my money to an ownership that was profiting off me during a terrible time.”Those complicated feelings have been compounded by the fact that the Dodgers are visiting the White House on Thursday. It’s a rite of passage for any team that wins the World Series, as the Dodgers have in each of the last two years, but it is also shaded by Donald Trump’s depiction of LA as atrash heapcontrolled by “illegal aliens” and criminals and by aggressive policies that have spread fear and heartbreak in a city that is close to 50% Latino.It’s not just immigrants questioning the team’s judgment. “The Dodgers do know they play in Los Angeles, right?” the LA Times sports columnist Bill Plaschkewrote. “Then why in the hell do they insist on embracing the person trying to tear this city apart?”Juan, like many Latino fans, has more mixed feelings, wanting to cheer on the success that the White House invitation betokens while also shuddering at what that entails with this particular president. “It was never controversial to go to the White House until Trump came into office,” he said. “I’m very conflicted.”“We want our sports to be non-political, but we don’t apply that standard equally,” said Natalia Molina, a professor of American studies and ethnicity at the University of SouthernCalifornia. Molina sees the team, with its lineup of Japanese superstars, Latinos and Americans of different ethnicities, as a vehicle for getting Americans to think more positively about other peoples and other cultures – something she has also seen happening during the World Cup.But she can’t imagine how the team would put on a show of unity and hope with Trump in the same room. “At the end of the day, it’s jarring to see players smiling with a person who harbors hatred toward our community,” she said. “How do you reconcile that?”Asked a similar question, Dave Roberts, the Dodgers manager,told reportershe was a coach, not a politician, and it was up to individual players to decide whether they wanted to participate.View image in fullscreenDave Roberts of the Los Angeles Dodgers looks on before the game against the New York Yankees at Yankee Stadium on 17 July.Photograph: Caleb Bowlin/Getty ImagesBut, he added, going to the White House was something teams have done for decades, and he expected a strong turnout. “My personal line, my company line, is I hope we get this invitation every year because that’s the goal, to win a championship,” he said.Still, at least two prominent players will be staying away. Kiké Hernández, a Puerto Rican fan favorite whosaid last summerhe could not stand “to see our community being violated, profiled, abused and ripped apart”, was quick to announce he was not going. Since he is rehabbing from an injury, he hasn’t been traveling with the team. But hetoldthe Los Angeles Times: “If I was active, I probably wouldn’t have gone anyways.”Mookie Betts, another fan favorite who ducked out of a Trump White House visit in 2019 when he played for the previous year’s World Series champions, the Boston Red Sox, said he would use the baseball-free day on the road to spend time with his wife and baby. “I’m not trying to make this a whole big deal,” hetoldthe California Post. “You don’t get many days off. They’re coming, and [I] just want to hang out with the fam.”Hernández and Betts attended last year’s White House ceremony – andendured criticismfor it along with the rest of the team. That, though, was before the surge in immigration raids and an attempt by ICE to use an area outside Dodger Stadium as a staging ground.Under significant public pressure, the team told ICE to stay away and pledged $1.1m to community groups supporting families like Juan’s who were grieving the arrest and deportation of a loved one. According to Securities and Exchange Commission filings, they also sold their $12m stake in the Geo Group.Religious, labor and community groups in the city believe the team could be doing a lot more, however. They point to a teamvideoput out in support of Black Lives Matter following the murder of George Floyd in Minneapolis in 2020 – including the Dodgers’ then-star pitcher, Clayton Kershaw, saying “silence is no longer an option” – and wonder why the players can’t do the same to support immigrant families now.Father Brendan Busse, pastor at the Dolores Mission in downtown Los Angeles, said the owners of the Dodgers face a fundamental choice between serving the city and serving themselves. “Are we here to make money off one another, or as a loving community?” he asked. “Are LA and its people just slaves of the Roman empire thrown into the Coliseum, or can the Dodgers treat people with dignity and respect?”It’s a question that has kicked around in some form from the day the Dodgers first arrived in Los Angeles. The team has always enjoyed a loyal Latino following and taken great care to emphasize the importance of family and community. But it also built its stadium on land that saw abrutal city-led campaignto evict Latino families, and it has been regarded with some ambivalence ever since.The modern-day Dodgers declined to comment.View image in fullscreenA protest outside Dodger Stadium, criticizing the Los Angeles Dodgers for their lack of support for immigrants and their cooperation with ICE on 21 June.Photograph: Katie McTiernan/Anadolu via Getty ImagesJoseph Tomás McKellar, who runs Pico California, a faith-based community organizing network, said he hoped the team could respond positively to what he termed “loving pressure” and galvanize other sports teams “in ways that express solidarity and transform human suffering”.Neither he nor Busse thought the Dodgers should boycott the White House. Rather, they saw the visit as an opportunity to send a message – whether by making a statement about the team’s values, or wearing clothes celebrating Latino cultures (as pitcher Joe Kelly did in 2021 when he wore a Mexicanmariachi jacket), or some other respectful but firm gesture.“Bringing Dodger blue into the White House is better than all the gold trinkets that have been brought in recently,” Busse said. “Showing up, as difficult as that can be, can be a way of holding to the truth that it’s not [Trump’s] house, it’s not a castle for a king.”Molina, by contrast, worried that even a symbolic statement would be taken as a poke in Trump’s eye and questioned whether anyone would feel comfortable making such a stand. “They could all wear Jackie Robinson’s number,” she said, referring to the Black Dodgers legend who first broke the sport’s color barrier in the 1940s and wasbriefly woke-purgedfrom a Pentagon website last year, “but in the context of this administration even that would seem a radical act.“There’s a question, even, whether the Spanish-language players can speak Spanish in the halls of the White House,” Molina added. “Can you bring your whole self, or do you need to police even that? Once you are in the zone of self-policing, I don’t know how much the visit is about celebration.”Explore more on these topicsTrump administrationLos AngelesBaseballCaliforniaUS immigrationDonald TrumpWest CoastfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: A host of issues: World Cup focus turns to Morocco with costs and legacy topping agenda | World Cup | The Guardian
description: With just over 1,400 days to go until 2030 kicks off, the co-hosts are facing a series of challenges familiar to their South African predecessors
Author: Osasu Obayiuwana
Headline: With just over 1,400 days to go until 2030 kicks off, the co-hosts are facing a series of challenges familiar to their South African predecessors
Content: Boards surround the site of the new stadium development near Casablanca, which is set to have a capacity of 115,000.Photograph: Paul Ellis/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenBoards surround the site of the new stadium development near Casablanca, which is set to have a capacity of 115,000.Photograph: Paul Ellis/AFP/Getty ImagesWorld CupA host of issues: World Cup focus turns to Morocco with costs and legacy topping agendaWith just over 1,400 days to go until 2030 kicks off, the co-hosts are facing a series of challenges familiar to their South African predecessorsOsasu Obayiuwanain Los AngelesWed 22 Jul 2026 14.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 18.15 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleMorocco’s World Cup adventure came to a halt withtheir quarter-final defeat by France. But taking a break, even for a brief post-season summer, is no option for officials of the FRMF, the country’s football federation.The Women’s Africa Cup of Nations (Wafcon), in its newly expanded 16-team format, begins in Rabat on Sunday, a week after theWorld Cupfinal in New Jersey, and Morocco then hosts the Under-17 Women’s World Cup in the autumn.The main focus, however, with just over 1,400 days before the start of the centenary 2030 World Cup finals, whichMoroccowill co-host, mainly with Spain and Portugal, is to ensure that preparations are professionally managed for the second World Cup to take place on African soil.With 50-60 billion Moroccan dirhams (£4bn-£4.8bn) budgeted for direct football investments, and further expenditure for infrastructure upgrades totalling a reported £17bn, the task is herculean. An International Monetary Fund assessment found that accelerated expenditure between 2024 and 2030 would amount to the equivalent of 11.9% of the country’s 2024 GDP.View image in fullscreenProtesters march against overspending on World Cup 2030 infrastructure, and call for healthcare and education reforms, in Casablanca last October.Photograph: Mosa’ab Elshamy/APThe Morocco 2030 Foundation – the country’s tournament preparation organisation, which started work in 2024 – has spent considerable time in the United States to learn from its organisation of the World Cup.Meetings with Spain and Portugal happen monthly “to align cooperation” and Fifa is expected to designate the host cities at the end of this year and decide whether it will revert to the local organising committee (LOC) model ditched for this year’s tournament because world football’s governing body managed preparations from its Miami office.View image in fullscreenThe World Cup stands in front of the flags of Morocco, Portugal and Spain.Photograph: Harold Cunningham/Fifa/Getty Images“The World Cup, for Morocco, is beyond football; it is about developing infrastructure that would be beneficial for the country going forward,” said a leading official who declined to be named. “Our stadiums in Rabat and Tangier are 100% ready.”King Hassan II, who reigned for 38 years and died in July 1999, began the country’s quest to host the World Cup with a failed bid to stage the 1994 tournament. Three subsequent attempts – 2006, 2010 and 2026 – also failed, before Morocco got the 2030 nod, under its current monarch, King Mohammed VI.View image in fullscreenConstruction work continues on Morocco’s Grand Hassan II Stadium.Photograph: Jalal Morchidi/EPA“Being in my 40s, I bear testimony to all the unsuccessful bids to host the World Cup,” says the journalist Amine El Amri. “Hosting a World Cup is a project that engages every Moroccan in a common dream, knowing that a World Cup comes with a cost, but also with a huge boost to the infrastructure of a very young and very demanding society.”Abderrahim Bourkia, a sports sociologist at the Institute of Sports Science in Settat, does not dismiss the view of those who question the World Cup’s importance to the country. “Others with political positions wonder whether the huge investments should be directed toward education, health, or employment,” he says.“Social status and educational background can shape perceptions too. Public opinion, therefore, remains somewhat ambivalent, reflecting differences in social background, political views, and economic expectations.“Morocco is investing heavily in transport infrastructure, airports, railways, stadiums, urban renewal and digital services. Beyond physical infrastructure, the country is strengthening its institutional capacity to organise mega-events, coordinate policies and improve governance.”Morocco would be well advised to draw on South Africa’s 2010 hosting knowledge. “Our experience shows that ‘success’ lies in planning for legacy, promoting national unity and ensuring infrastructure does not become a burden over the long term,” says Dennis Mumble, a former CEO of the South African Football Association, who was a member of the 2010 World Cup’s LOC.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“Some stadiums can become a burden on the fiscus, if not utilised optimally. I don’t believe we built white elephants, but we may not have learned how to position these venues as multi-purpose venues, for concerts of global performers, multi-sports use, church and massive political events.”View image in fullscreenThe president of Fifa, Gianni Infantino, cuts the ribbon during the inauguration ceremony of the Fifa offices in Rabat.Photograph: Abdel Majid Bziouat/AFP/Getty ImagesWith many Moroccans apprehensive about the cost of hosting, Mumble tells a cautionary tale about South Africa’s experience. “There were significant cost overruns on the stadium development because the contractors knew the government approved the funds quite late. The result was predatory pricing imposed by contractors who knew we had no choice but to pay whatever they charged.“They were later [criminally] charged and convicted, paying relatively steep fines for this unlawful conduct. Planning to the most minute detail became everything for us.”Morocco’s average annual salary is $7,400 – some fans are paying double that for World Cup ticketsRead moreMorocco is framing its co-hosting of the World Cup as “one with African colours”, but many within the continent’s football community, particularly its sub-Saharan members, are wondering whether, beyond the PR slogans, there will be genuine legacy benefits.Over the past decade, the FRMF has entered into bilateral cooperation agreements with the majority of football federations on the continent. With nearly half of African countries lacking first-rate pitches to stage international matches, forcing them to play official games outside their countries, Moroccan officials say a partnership with the Confederation of African Football to solve this is a part of its World Cup legacy programme.Mumble counsels, based on his experience in the four-year buildup to the 2010 event, that global reputation management will be as big a challenge for Morocco as the nuts-and-bolts preparations, with its political, economic and social issues put under closer media scrutiny.That is something Qatar, the 2022 host, knows a lot about, having had to deal with deeply negative criticism overthe deaths of migrant workersandhuman rights abuses.Explore more on these topicsWorld CupMoroccoAfricafeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Financial ‘exceptions’ made for Pep Guardiola to become next Italy head coach | Italy | The Guardian
description: Financial ‘exceptions’ could be made in order for Pep Guardiola to become Italy’s next head coach, the president of the Italian Football Federation has said
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Financial ‘exceptions’ could be made in order for Pep Guardiola to become Italy’s next head coach, the president of the Italian Football Federation has said
Content: Pep Guardiola has never managed at international level and is free to do so following his departure from Manchester City at the end of last season.Photograph: Adam Davy/PAView image in fullscreenPep Guardiola has never managed at international level and is free to do so following his departure from Manchester City at the end of last season.Photograph: Adam Davy/PAItalyFinancial ‘exceptions’ made for Pep Guardiola to become next Italy head coachItalian football federation confirm interest in GuardiolaMancini and Pirlo also linked with national team vacancyPA MediaWed 22 Jul 2026 05.21 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 18.14 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFinancial “exceptions” are in place to allowPep Guardiolato become Italy’s next head coach, the president of the Italian Football Federation (FIGC), Giovanni Malagò, has said.Guardiola left Manchester City at the end of last season after winning six Premier League titles, three FA Cups, five League Cups and the Champions League during a hugely successful 10-year reign.Southampton head coach Tonda Eckert charged with misconduct by FA over spygate scandalRead moreThe Spaniard is free to take up another job, having also coached Barcelona and Bayern Munich, and has been linked with theItalyvacancy for some time, with it reported earlier this week that he has held talks with the FIGC.Gennaro Gattuso resigned from the post in April after the four-time World Cup champions failed to qualify for a third tournament in a row.“[Financial] exceptions have been made, exceptions that for example may concern the name that is so overbearing in these hours: Pep Guardiola,” Malagò is quoted as saying by Gazzetta dello Sport. “Exceptions for obvious reasons that I am not here to explain, but it is not certain that this thing will go through.”Roberto Mancini, who led Italy to Euro 2020 glory, and the formerAzzurrimidfielder Andrea Pirlo are other reported candidates for the vacancy.Asked if Guardiola, Mancini and Pirlo were the only names on the shortlist, Malagò added: “Absolutely not. We think about a certain profile and certainly these names fall into that category.”Italian football ​has hit its ​lowest point in nearly 40 ​years, following an early wholesale exit from European club competitions last season ​and the ‌failure to reach ​the World Cup.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe ​appointment of a new national coach is viewed as the first step in overhauling a youth and talent infrastructure that has largely collapsed. Italy’s next fixture is the Nations League Group A match against Belgium on 25 September.Explore more on these topicsItalyPep GuardiolanewsShareReuse this content

title: Liverpool primed for business: Bezos, Bhatia and the next steps at Anfield | Liverpool | The Guardian
description: Fenway Sports Group looks to be locked in talks with a consortium led by the son-in-law of Lakshmi Mittal, while the Amazon founder has now been linked
Author: Andy Hunter
Headline: Fenway Sports Group looks to be locked in talks with a consortium led by the son-in-law of Lakshmi Mittal, while the Amazon founder has now been linked
Content: Changing times? Liverpool could soon have an injection of cash.Photograph: Peter Byrne/PAView image in fullscreenChanging times? Liverpool could soon have an injection of cash.Photograph: Peter Byrne/PALiverpoolExplainerLiverpool primed for business: Bezos, Bhatia and the next steps at AnfieldFenway Sports Group looks to be locked in talks with a consortium led by the son-in-law of Lakshmi Mittal, while the Amazon founder has now been linkedAndy HunterWed 22 Jul 2026 15.31 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 15.16 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWhat has happened?A consortium led by Amit Bhatia, the former co-owner of Queens Park Rangers and son-in-law of the Indian steel magnate Lakshmi Mittal, hasopened talks with Liverpool’s owner, Fenway Sports Group, over buying a significant stake in the club. Neither party has commented on the size of the stake or the investment amount being offered but it is believed to be a provisional offer of £1.35bn for about 30%. Discussions began three months ago but FSG insists the talks remain at a preliminary stage.Where does this value Liverpool?The reported offer values Liverpool at about £4.5bn. Manchester United were valued at slightly less when Sir Jim Ratcliffe bought aninitial 25% stake in February 2024, increased to 27.7%, although the latest valuation of United by Forbes was $7.2bn (£5.38bn). Forbes, again, had Real Madrid as the most valuable club in the world at $9.5bn, with Barcelona in second place at $7.5bn. Todd Boehly and Clearlake Capitalacquired Chelsea for £4.25bn in May 2022as a distressed asset sale after the Russian oligarch Roman Abramovich was sanctioned by the UK government.Why would FSG potentially want to sell a minority stake?First of all, £1.35bn for 30% of Liverpool would represent a healthy profit on a club itbought for £300m in 2010and whose value has soared since. And the principal owner, John W Henry, is a venture capitalist, after all. But it is also true that FSG remains fiercely competitive and to continue challenging among an elite that includes oil-rich states necessitates ever more investment. That is why FSG has sought, and occasionally found, new investors before. The current talks are not considered part of an exit strategy by Liverpool’s owners but a potential opportunity to attract considerable funds while remaining in overall control. The company has parked plans to buy a second club – a decision that led to Michael Edwards’s recent departure as FSG’s chief executive of football – and last summer funded the biggest transfer outlay in Liverpool’s history of almost £450m. To maintain and improve those levels, having made only a modest profit of £8m when winning the Premier League title under Arne Slot in 2024-25, requires injecting fresh capital into the club.View image in fullscreenLiverpool spent £125m on Alexander Isak on deadline day in August 2025, part of a £450m summer outlay.Photograph: Ian Hodgson/APHave we been here before?Several times. In March 2021, FSG sold 10% of the company to RedBird Capital Partners for £543m. In 2022, FSG hired the investment banks Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley to seek out potential buyers for a 10% stake in Liverpool. No deal was done and Henry confirmed the following year that FSG was always open to new investment but not looking to sell the club. In 2023, FSG sold a reported 4% stake in Liverpool to Dynasty Equity, an American sports investment company, for £164m. That money was largely used to pay off debts incurred during the pandemic.Who are the potential investors?Bhatia, who stepped down as co-owner and director of QPR on Tuesday after 18 years, is believed to have secured financial backing from Mittal. The Mittal family wealth is estimated to be £23bn. Those riches pale in comparison, however, with another potential investor – Amazon’s founder, Jeff Bezos. The fourth-richest person in the world could afford to buy Liverpool outright and still have $250bn left in his back pocket. Bezos has been approached about joining the consortium but is reportedly undecided on whether to invest in a Premier League club for the first time. Bezos is no longer chief executive of Amazon but is executive chair of a company that has expanded from online retail into entertainment and sports rights, including for Premier League games, in recent years.What would it mean for Liverpool’s finances?Even without Bezos on board, the consortium’s proposed offer would improve Liverpool’s capital considerably as the club begins a new era under Andoni Iraola. How much say and sway Bhatia and others would have over Liverpool’s football operations remains to be ironed out. With the FSG president, Mike Gordon, returning to a more prominent role at the club after Edwards’s exit, it seems unlikely that Liverpool’s owners would follow the Glazers’ example with Ratcliffe at Manchester United and grant a minority shareholder full control on the football side. Given talks are only at a preliminary stage, Liverpool’s transfer plans for this summer are not expected to be affected.What happens next?Talks continue until a deal is done or collapses. FSG’s willingness to confirm Bhatia’s bid in a statement to the Financial Times on Tuesday, and Bhatia’s departure from QPR that very same day, indicates negotiations are heading towards a successful conclusion.Get in touchContact Guardian SportThe best public interest journalism relies on first-hand accounts from people in the know. If you have something to share on this subject, you can contact us confidentially using the following methods:Secure Messaging in the Guardian appThe Guardian app has a tool to send tips about stories. Messages are end to end encrypted and concealed within the routine activity that every Guardian mobile app performs. This prevents an observer from knowing that you are communicating with us at all, let alone what is being said.If you don’t already have the Guardian app, download it (iOS/Android) and go to the menu. Select ‘Secure Messaging’. When asked to select a recipient, select the ‘Sport (UK and Global)’ team.SecureDropIf you can safely use the tor network without being observed or monitored you can send messages and documents to the Guardian via ourSecureDrop platform.Finally, our guide attheguardian.com/tipslists several ways to contact us securely, and discusses the pros and cons of each.Show moreExplore more on these topicsLiverpoolBusinessexplainersShareReuse this content

title: Trossard, Ramos, Dumfries: 10 transfers you may have missed during the World Cup | Transfer window | The Guardian
description: Too engrossed with the World Cup drama to keep up with goings-on in the transfer market? We’ve got you covered
Author: Will Magee
Headline: Too engrossed with the World Cup drama to keep up with goings-on in the transfer market? We’ve got you covered
Content: Denzel Dumfries, Robert Lewandowski and Leandro Trossard have all moved on to new clubs.Composite: Guardian picture desk; Fifa/Getty Images; Chicago Fire FC/MLS/Getty Images; Anadolu/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenDenzel Dumfries, Robert Lewandowski and Leandro Trossard have all moved on to new clubs.Composite: Guardian picture desk; Fifa/Getty Images; Chicago Fire FC/MLS/Getty Images; Anadolu/Getty ImagesTransfer windowTrossard, Ramos, Dumfries: 10 transfers you may have missed during the World CupToo engrossed with the World Cup drama to keep up with goings-on in the transfer market? We’ve got you coveredWill Magee,Niall McVeigh,Billy MundayandYara El-ShabouryWed 22 Jul 2026 15.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 17.59 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleLeandro Trossard to BesiktasAnnounced just beforeEngland’s soul-sucking semi-final defeat by Argentina, it would have been easy to miss Trossard’s £15.3m move to Istanbul. While, after three and a half seasons and at the age of 31, it feels like his time at Arsenal has come to a natural conclusion, he will be remembered as a player who scored in clutch moments and, ultimately,played a crucial role in the club’s first league title in 22 years. Trossard departs after 174 appearances and 36 goals, a significant portion of which came from the bench in times of desperate need. He will be missed and deserves to be revered as one of the cult heroes of the Mikel Arteta era.Will MageeNathaniel Brown to Bayern MunichFew players emerged from Germany’s World Cup campaign with any credit, but Brown’s energetic displays at left-back have earned him a move to Bayern Munich. Brown is a proud Bavarian, born in the town of Amberg and raised in Nürnberg’s youth academy. After two years atEintracht Frankfurtand eight Germany caps, Brown has taken the quick route to his nation’s dominant club and could be a very useful addition. With Raphaël Guerreiro leaving and Alphonso Davies still recovering from injury, Vincent Kompany will benefit from Brown’s abilities as a natural left-back, after using a rotating cast of players to patch up Bayern’s defence last term.Niall McVeighView image in fullscreenNathaniel Brown looking pretty pleased to have signed for Bayern Munich.Photograph: S Stingl/FC Bayern/Getty ImagesDenzel Dumfries to Real MadridThe Netherlands right-back is often a force at major tournaments. He scored twice at Euro 2020, which earned him a move from PSV to Inter, and helped the Netherlands past the USA in the last 16 of the 2022 World Cup with a goal and two assists. In North America, the 30-year-old provided two assists –both against Swedenat the group stage – as the Netherlands reached the last 32. Following their exit to Morocco, Dumfries put pen to paper on a four-year deal at Real Madrid after his release clause at Inter was triggered – he joins Bernardo Silva and Ibrahima Konaté as freebie arrivals. A Hollywood move for a player named after Denzel Washington.Billy MundayEmersonn to IpswichAnnounced just before the World Cup quarter-finals, the 22-year-old Brazilian striker​ arrived inToulouseonly last summer but did enough in a single Ligue 1 campaign to convince the newly promoted Premier League side to part with £24m. Standing 6ft 3in, Emersonn combines an old-fashioned target-man frame with the mobility to drift into wide areas​. Although his raw return of ​s​ix goals in ​2​7 league appearances suggests this is a transfer built on potential rather than proven output​, Gary O’Neil clearly sees shades of the modern Premier League No 9: aggressive, hard-running and capable of unsettling defenders even when the goals are not flowing. “He is a young striker with real ability and potential who has had plenty of interest this summer,” the manager said.Yara El-ShabouryHarry Winks to CagliariThere is a parallel universe where Harry Winks became a key cog in England’s midfield, earning many more caps than the 10 he has in reality. Mauricio Pochettino once suggested the tidy midfielder could be England’s answer to Andrés Iniesta. It has not worked out that way: injuries stalled Winks’s Spurs career and the struggles have continued at Leicester, despite a brief revival under Enzo Maresca. Now 30, Winks joins the Everton youngster Demi Akarakiri at Cagliari, whose only previous English player – forward Gerry Hitchens – left in 1969. It will not be Winks’s first spell in Serie A, having spent a year on loan at Sampdoria in 2022-23, which ended in relegation. This time, he may be seeking stability and a slower pace in Sardinia.NMcView image in fullscreenHarry Winks (right) has left Leicester City for a new Serie A adventure.Photograph: Plumb Images/Leicester City/Getty ImagesMathys Detourbet to Manchester CityWhile Detourbet’s £21.6m transfer to the Etihad Stadium may have been overshadowed bythe unveiling of Enzo Maresca two days laterandElliot Anderson’s £116m arrivalthe following week – not to mention the end of the World Cup group stages – the 19-year-old was the club’s first signing of the summer. Joining from Troyes, part of the City Football Group stable, having played for the French side since the age of eight, it was an intriguing move for a teenage winger described as “a source of great pride” by his former club. With three goals and six assists in 33 appearances in Ligue 2 last season, helping Troyes win promotion as champions, he has shown plenty of promise. Loaned out to Monaco for the coming campaign, he will be aiming to make his mark in Ligue 1 and catch Maresca’s eye in the process.WMIllan Meslier to ArsenalHe may face a steep uphill struggle to displace David Raya as No 1, but Meslier joining Arsenal on a free transfer could feasibly dislodge Kepa Arrizabalaga. With trust in Raya’s backup undermined by his lacklustre performance inthe Carabao Cup final last season, the arrival of the former Leeds goalkeeper appears to open the door for his departure. Meslier is five years younger than Arrizabalaga and has 107 Premier League appearances under his belt, but fell behind Karl Darlow and Lucas Perri in the pecking order at Elland Road last term. Often criticised for high-profile mistakes at Leeds, it is up to him to win over the sceptics and, if Arrizabalaga does leave, to make the No 2 role his own.WMView image in fullscreenIllan Meslier, who will play backup to David Raya, settles into his new surroundings at Arsenal.Photograph: Stuart MacFarlane/Arsenal FC/Getty ImagesGonçalo Ramos to MilanMilan broke their club-record transfer fee when they paid £60.2m to sign Ramos at the end of June. With 45 goals in 131 appearances forParis Saint-Germain, helping them to three league titles and two Champions League triumphs, the 25-year-old has been a useful player for Luis Enrique’s side but was never going to be the main man in an attack featuring Khvicha Kvaratskhelia, Ousmane Dembélé and Désiré Doué. It was telling that he had to make do with late substitute appearances in PSG’s two victorious Champions League finals, though he did score the opener in the shootout against Arsenal. As Ruben Amorim’s first signing at Milan, he will have top billing whereas at PSG he was destined to be a support act.WMView image in fullscreenGonçalo Ramos (centre) poses with Milan’s head coach, Ruben Amorim (right), and the owner, Gerry Cardinale, after signing with the club.Photograph: Claudio Villa/AC Milan/Getty ImagesRobert Lewandowski to Chicago FirePoland did not qualify for the World Cup but that has not stopped their greatest player from making the trip to the US this summer. Lewandowski said he could not imagine himself at a European club other thanBarcelonaafter ending a four-year spell in Catalonia that yielded three La Liga titles and 120 goals. The 37-year-old found the net 19 times at the highest level last season and is still in remarkable physical shape, so there is a chance of a Zlatan Ibrahimovic-style impact on Major League Soccer, just without the bluster. The former USA coach Gregg Berhalter is his new boss in Chicago.BMJakub Kaminski to Köln … and BenficaNot content with making one under-the-radar move, Kaminski has made two this summer. After breaking through at Lech Poznan, the Poland winger moved to Wolfsburg in 2022 but fell from favour, and spent last season on loan at Köln. He impressed for the Bundesliga returnees, playing every league game and filling a wide range of attacking roles. Köln paid €5.5m (£4.7m) to sign him permanently in May, but Marco Silva’s Benfica swooped to pay Kaminski’s €20m (£17.1m) release clause just two months later. Köln have made a healthy profit – and invested a portion of it onManchester Cityyoungster Reigan Heskey – but have seen a key summer recruit slip away.NMcExplore more on these topicsTransfer windowArsenalBesiktasInternazionaleReal MadridEintracht FrankfurtBayern MunichfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Arsenal reveal William Saliba out for ‘extended period’ with back injury | Arsenal | The Guardian
description: The France centre-back was forced off in the World Cup semi-final against Spain and will now sit out Arsenal’s pre-season
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The France centre-back was forced off in the World Cup semi-final against Spain and will now sit out Arsenal’s pre-season
Content: France’s William Saliba goes down with the injury against Spain in the World Cup semi-final.Photograph: Jean Catuffe/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenFrance’s William Saliba goes down with the injury against Spain in the World Cup semi-final.Photograph: Jean Catuffe/Getty ImagesArsenalArsenal reveal William Saliba out for ‘extended period’ with back injuryCentre-back forced off in World Cup semi-finalArsenal say problem does not require surgeryPA MediaWed 22 Jul 2026 17.05 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 17.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleArsenal, the Premier League champions, have been dealt a big injury blow after the centre-back William Saliba was ruled out for “an extended period” with a back injury.Saliba played with a back problem during France’s run to the World Cup semi-final and, although he will not require an operation, the 25-year-old is set for a significant spell on the sidelines. He was forced off with the injury after just 30 minutes ofFrance’s last-four defeat by Spain.“Subsequent specialist reviews on William’s return to London this week have confirmed that he has suffered a back injury which will require a period of rehabilitation,” Arsenal said in a statement. “Extensive assessments have concluded that surgery is not recommended, but William should now commence a managed recovery programme.“William’s rehabilitation will commence immediately, with continual management of his injury, and he is expected to be out of action for an extended period. Everyone is fully focused on supporting William to ensure he is back to full fitness as soon as possible.”After France’s loss to Spain, Saliba sat out thethird-place playoff against England. Earlier in the tournament, he admitted: “I’ve had some minor niggles for several months. I’ve been gritting my teeth because there was the Champions League and the Premier League. The World Cup comes round only once every four years, so you’ve got to grit your teeth.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionArsenal’s England duoBukayo SakaandDeclan Ricealso battled injury problems throughout the tournament, although both of them were fit enough to star in the third‑place decider, with Rice getting the first goal and Saka scoring a hat‑trick.Explore more on these topicsArsenalFranceWorld Cup 2026newsShareReuse this content

title: World Cup 2026 awards: the Guardian team give their final tournament verdicts | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: The best games, goals and personal highlights – from a magical Azteca to Erling Haaland breaking America
Author: Guardian sport
Headline: The best games, goals and personal highlights – from a magical Azteca to Erling Haaland breaking America
Content: Clockwise from top: Cape Verde’s keeper Vozinha; England and Mexico fans outside the Azteca; Spain captain Rodri with the World Cup; a Scotland fan; the dazzling Monterrey Stadium.Composite: Guardian Designl Imagn Images/Reuters; PA; Fifa/Getty Images; AMA/Getty Images; Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenClockwise from top: Cape Verde’s keeper Vozinha; England and Mexico fans outside the Azteca; Spain captain Rodri with the World Cup; a Scotland fan; the dazzling Monterrey Stadium.Composite: Guardian Designl Imagn Images/Reuters; PA; Fifa/Getty Images; AMA/Getty Images; Getty ImagesWorld Cup 2026World Cup 2026 awards: the Guardian team give their final tournament verdictsThe best games, goals and personal highlights – from a magical Azteca to Erling Haaland breaking AmericaGuardian sportWed 22 Jul 2026 05.01 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 05.13 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleMatch of the tournamentParaguay1-1 Germany.There were plenty more games with more goals but none could match the drama of the penalty shootout in this last-32 tie. Germany aren’t supposed to lose in thembut they did this timein spectacular fashion.Ed AaronsAlgeria 3-3 Austriawas a completely strange fever dream, seeming to exist on a separate plane of reality. I know that sounds grandiose but remember, going into this one the expectation was that it would be abiscotto– a meaningless, pre-arranged kickabout where a draw would see both sides through. For a while in the second half, it looked like that would happen. And finally,in stoppage time, chaos. There were certainly better matches in terms of quality, and maybe a couple that had this beat for drama, but no game was weirder.Alexander AbnosThe quirky side of World Cup 2026 – in picturesRead moreFrom the 14 I attended, it has to beMexico2-3 Englandat the awesome Azteca. The buildup seemed to start as soon as the draw was made – fortunately the stars aligned anda grippingly tense gameensued in a venue like no other. It was a high point for England that should cut through despite later disappointments, too.Nick AmesFrance 4-6 Englandwas borderline hilarious.Egypt 1-1 Iran in Seattlehad plenty of late drama, Iran’s disallowed stoppage-time winner bookended by them twice hitting the woodwork in the final minutes. The draw ultimately proved insufficient for unbeaten Iran, faced with endless hurdles, to progress.Ben FisherArgentina 3-2 Egypt.The defending champions looked down and out, thinking about their flights home, before a lateLionel Messi-inspired comebackenabled them to claw back a two-goal deficit and win this last-16 match in normal time.Barry GlendenningThe gameno one wanted to play.Sid LoweMexico 2-3 England. I have decided that all my answers should relate to things that happened while I was at the tournament and this was one. I mean, I wasn’t there ticking off the “bucket list” of a visit to the Azteca (I think I saw at least half a dozen British hacks claim that), but I did watch it on TV. Besides being like drinking cortisol neat, this was so dramatic, the images so evocative, that even if you are an ardent England fan (as I am) this was pure cinema.Paul MacInnesView image in fullscreenEngland’s players sing Wonderwall in front of their fans after their last-16 victory over Mexico.Photograph: José Méndez/EPAI am biased here in the sense I attended it butCape Verde2-2 Uruguayin Miami was superb. Cape Verde’s attitude and discipline were a joy.Such fun to watch.Ewan MurrayArgentina3-2 Egypt. Incredible drama, despite the questionablebehaviour of the referee.Osasu ObayiuwanaTough to pick from over 100 options, butMexico 2-3 Englandwith Thomas Tuchel’s men surviving the vaunted Azteca and the best first half Mexico could have mustered narrowly takes this category.Jeff RueterBias says Mexico v England, butArgentina 3-2 Cape Verdewas incredible, Sidny Lopes Cabral bending it top right.Max RushdenFor improbable wonder there was Argentina v Cape Verde, for sheer unpredictability there was Mexico v England. Often, though, one is drawn to something more predictable,more familiar:England 1-2 Argentina.Jack SnapeArgentina 3-2 Cape Verde. It’s not a debate.Jacob SteinbergArgentina 3-2 Egypt. The possibility of a shock, a great Messi-inspired turnaround, a couple of great goals, refereeing controversy … what else do you want?Jonathan WilsonView image in fullscreenLionel Messi (second left) fires a shot past Egypt’s keeper Mostafa Shobeir for Argentina’s equaliser in their 3-2 win.Photograph: Elsa/Getty ImagesPlayer of the tournamentRodriwasthe standout playerfor the best team in a tournament where the big stars came to the party but all ultimately fell short.EAKylian Mbappé. Yes, his Golden Boot was padded a bit by that insane third-place game, but the France striker was the best player on what looked to be the best team in the tournament ... at least untilthat loss to Spainin the semi-final.AAIt’s impossible to look further thanRodri, who looks back to his best and absolutely in his element as the fulcrum of this Spain setup. Otherwise it was good to see aWorld Cupwhere the stars largely shone. Jude Bellingham, Erling Haaland, Messi and Mbappé were all one more decisive knockout display away from rising above the rest.NARodri. Michael Olise was majestic, though should have scored a hat-trick against England. Bellingham was equally instrumental for Tuchel’s side – it is crazy to think there were people questioning his inclusion in the squad.BFRodri–capitán, líder, leyenda.BGLionel Messi.SLVozinha.What a man. I think it’s largely because his story is so relatable in these parts – middle-aged man finally receives the plaudits he has always deserved! – but the saves were also top drawer. Add in the pedigree of the teams he either shut out or frustrated, and this was an actual fairytale that didn’t need to be spun by any marketing department. And did you know that he’s 40?PMPau Cubarsí.EMVozinha, the Cape Verde goalkeeper. At 40, and without a club, he was brilliant for the World Cup debutants.OOWhile Rodri is an incredibly worthy Golden Ball winner and others made their marks,Erling Haalandleaves this World Cup with the greatest possible souvenirs: a credible if short Golden Boot campaign, a famous first Norwegian foray into the quarter-final stage, and perhaps the biggestboost in star appealin the lucrative North American market.JRView image in fullscreenEven the World Cup mascots stand in awe of Erling Haaland.Photograph: Maddie Meyer/Fifa/Getty ImagesHard not to sayRodrialthough I thought Dani Olmo was great too, and a shout for Pico Lopes.MRHaaland, Mbappé and Messi have lived up to expectations. But in those moments where a contest teeters – evading pressure with the ball, in transition to defence or staring down a 50-50 – no player has shown his quality more consistently thanRodri.JSnRodri. If only England had a player like the Spain captain.JStRodri. As Spain have reverted to a more possession-driven model, their metronome in midfield has been the outstanding figure, setting the tempo and providing control.JWGoal of the tournamentFor sheer drama, it has to beSidny Lopes Cabral’s brilliant equaliser for Cape Verde against Argentina. His reaction was almost as good as the goal itself, clasping his hands to his head before picking out his girlfriend in the crowd to celebrate with.EAI mean, it’sSidny Lopes Cabralv Argentina. But I feel that the goal from Uzbekistan’s Eldor Shomurodov against the Democratic Republic of the Congo deserves mention too: an instinctive, oddly satisfying lob from a tight angle. No other goal in the tournament was quite like it.AAJulián Alvarezhad a largely quiet World Cup but his vicious, swerving long-range goalin extra time against Switzerlandwas a jaw-dropping moment and lit up the cauldron-like stadium in Kansas City.NAThose seen in the flesh always seem to score more points. A buzz surrounded Bosnia and Herzegovina’sKerim Alajbegoviccoming into the tournament and the 18-year-old eliminating a clutch of defenders and blasting in, off balance, from the outside of the boxagainst Qataronly enhanced his reputation. Johan Manzambi’s run and pass in the buildup to Breel Embolo’s openeragainst Algeriawas also a joy.BFView image in fullscreenBosnia and Herzegovina’s Kerim Alajbegovic is embraced after opening the scoring against Qatar.Photograph: Carlos Barría/ReutersWhile Morocco’s superior quality would ultimately see them through their group-stagewin against Haiti,Wilson Isidor’s breathtaking long-range strike to put the Caribbean nation 2-1 up was as good a goal as any scored in the World Cup. Ever.BGSidny Lopes Cabral, of course.SLI had the privilege of reading the paper by the Seoul Women’s University on the aerodynamic effectknown as “drag crisis”which is particularly common when people smack an Adidas Trionda, so as such I thought a lot of the big hits were overrated. I do, however, exemptSidny Lopes Cabralfrom that critique. Absolute long-range worldie from the Cape Verde full-back-cum-winger. That it pegged Argentina to parity in extra time of a knockout round only made it more memorable.PMHarry Kane’s second forEngland against DRC.EMSidny Lopes Cabralof Cape Verde, against Argentina. Magical goal.OOI will never, in all my years, be able to recreate the sound that left my body asSidny Lopes Cabral’s audacious effort against Argentina completed its curl between a diving Emi Martínez and his far post. Masterful execution in an unprecedented moment.
