One of many proud moments for Cape Verde at this tournament.JRskip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionView image in fullscreenCape Verde’s Sidny Lopes Cabral unleashes his audacious effort …Photograph: Newscom/AlamyView image in fullscreen… which flies past Argentina’s keeper Emi Martínez to level the scores in extra time.Photograph: Paul Childs/ReutersBeyondSidny Lopes Cabral– the disallowed Uzbekistan one (was it Uzbekistan?) – someone walloped one into the top left-hand corner and it didn’t count.MRErling Haaland’s secondagainst Brazil, placed into the bottom-right corner from outside the box to seal the upset, was a microcosm of his effectiveness at the tournament: precise and powerful with a hint of whimsy.JSnSidny Lopes Cabral’s equaliser for Cape Verde against Argentina was a proper jaw-dropping moment. One of the great World Cup goals.JStWilson Isidorv Morocco. There were more consequential goals, but none struck quite as sweetly or quite as accurately. If Haiti have nothing else, they’ll always have that.JWPersonal highlightIt was a joy to watchEloy Room’s performance in goal forCuraçao against Ecuadoras he broke the World Cup record for the most saves made in a 90-minute match. While Cape Verde’s veteran Vozinha stole most of the headlines for his heroics, the 37-year-old Room clearly enjoyed his moment in the spotlight. “I think I need a statue in Curaçao now,” he joked.EAFifa and the stadium authorities typically guard the natural grass pitches heavily, and with good reason. But after the last games in each venue I went to, I loved watching those pitches get taken over bythe people whose names you’ll never knowbut who actually made this tournament run: stadium workers and low-level Fifa staffers, volunteers, security guards, longtime local club representatives and others. Some used the opportunity to have a little kickabout, take selfies, or just run around. An awesome perk for what were almost certainly very hard, stressful jobs over these last several weeks.AAMy first visit to theAztecawasMexico’s win over Ecuadorand I’ve never heard a noise like it. The place really is as magical and monumental as they say. The celebrations afterwards weren’t conducive to a good night’s sleep but were incredible in scale too. Football-wise I’ll also addMessi’s record-breaking goals against Austriain Dallas, which seem an awfully long time ago now but were such a thrill to witness. Those early Argentina games were essentially mass Messi pilgrimages. One more? The view from Monterrey Stadium. Just go, if you can.NAView image in fullscreenA view of Mexico’s last-32 game against Ecuador from high up inside the Azteca stands.Photograph: Hannah Peters/Fifa/Getty ImagesWatching Senegalese journalists pass around a pack ofmalted milksduring their anthem before theircollapse against Belgium. And playing eight-a-side with Danny Cipriani, who once spent six weeks training at Tottenham, Harry Kane among his teammates, will stick in the memory.BFWatching myIrish compatriotRoberto “Pico” Lopes excel while repeatedly putting his body on the line for Cape Verde, his father’s homeland. It would be nice to think Pico’s performances will earn him a life-changing move to a more lucrative league in the twilight of his career but his age could stand against him and he may well be content with being a Shamrock Rovers and Cape Verde legend.BGExplaining to a mariachi in agigantic sombrerohow it could possibly be that a team in third could get through the group and how it might still be Spain turning up at the Azteca. Alas, it wasn’t. Sharing a birthday with Martin Ainstein and Luis de la Fuente and my family. And watching a motorcade with the Spain bus pass from the sixth-floor window where Lee Harvey Oswald shot JFK was pretty surreal.SLI loved watching bothFrance eviscerate Swedenand the Instagram reels of Americans acting like they thought theTartan Armyhad permanently changed their lives.PMView image in fullscreenMembers of the Tartan Army have a kickabout in a Boston street.Photograph: Peter Cziborra/ReutersPlayers running out to warm-up to the tune ofScotlandthe Brave, via bagpipes, beforethe game against Haitiin Foxborough. Scotland’s 28-year World Cup wait was over. The less said about all that happened next, the better.EMSeeingVozinha’s mum,against all expectations, travelling from Cape Verde to the World Cup to see her son play.OOHaving covered the nation’s third-to-fifth most popular sport for more than a decade and lived throughICE’s heinous occupation of Minnesotathis winter, the sheer scale of interest in soccer was overwhelming and so invigorating –a welcome reprievein the isolationist second Trump era. Impossible to go through a couple of hours in a World Cup host city without some stranger striking up a conversation about the sport. Long may this remain a part of American society.JRShoutingParaguay. Number 10. Cover his mouth. Decision is RED CARD. Jonathan Wilson thinking Robbie Williams was Kevin Pietersen. Mark Langdon’s chow mein bridge.The pancakesat Mile End Deli. Doing a live show in Manhattan and legitimately getting to say “HELLO NEW YORK!”MRCoveringAustralia, the tournament petered out from an extraordinary high. Twenty-six minutes into the Socceroos’opening victory against Turkey, goalscorer Nestory Irankunda revived Tim Cahill’s goal celebration in a ceremonial handover to mark a new generation.JSnView image in fullscreenAustralia’s Nestory Irankunda throws punches while shadowboxing the corner flag, à la Tim Cahill.Photograph: Kaleb Tatum/APBeing at theAztecafor Mexico 2-3 England. It did not have the most quality but it was hard to beat as an occasion. An epic night at an iconic stadium. A thunderstorm pushing kick-off back, a brilliant display from Bellingham, everyone exhausted when it was over. Things didn’t end well for England but this was easily their greatest away win.JStStanding knee-deep in raging floods in Monterrey waiting for a bus having only gone to the stadium to collect a mislaid iPad that I then lost in Houston a week later anyway. That or theweirdly beautiful flyoversoutside Dallas.JWWhat did you make of the hosts?The level of interest from the American public surprised me and it was fascinating to meet so many who were interested in what was happening in the tournament. Many people in the US definitely caught World Cup fever. Logistically it was a real test of endurance with so much travelling required between venues and that wasn’t helped by so many being miles from the city centre. And it would have been much better to have played the final in Mexico City or Atlanta’s impressive downtown stadium.EAI felt unfortunate not to make it to any games in Mexico and only one in Canada. But overall I’m happy that the everyday people who populate the US seemed to show visitors their good side throughout the month. This country has plenty of infrastructure issues I’d like to have seen fixed, but the personal impact is arguably more important, and it seems like the US mostly got that right.AAIf first impressions are everything then I refer you to the often troublingweirdness of the Texas Republican party conventionin Houston. But more seriously I was very happy, in the US, to find the hoped-for warmth and openness pretty much everywhere – and in bundles. In their different ways, people really did engage with the World Cup. Less welcome was the US administration’s attempt, duly pandered to by Fifa, to bend the course of the tournament withFolarin Balogun’s reprieve. In Mexico I found a fervent, febrile, fascinating football culture that deserved not to be dwarfed by other co-hosts.NAAmericans love a big-ticket event but it was clear they were hooked by Mauricio Pochettino’s side as they progressed. It was a privilege to witnessCanada’s first World Cup gameon home soil in Toronto, where there was a match-day buzz and an afterglow despite being held to a draw by Bosnia and Herzegovina.BFView image in fullscreenCanada fans react to Cyle Larin’s goal during their opening group game against Bosnia and Herzegovina.Photograph: Joosep Martinson/Fifa/Getty ImagesWhile I didn’t meet Donald Trump, the handful of Americans whose acquaintance I did make – hotel staff, a few bartenders and one LA-based reality TV show star – could not have been friendlier.BGSo many people came up to us to say they really hoped we were enjoying this and that we understood thatthatwasn’t them. We did and they were so nice.SLI spent two days in Toronto, around Canada Day, and I think they were both welcoming and very keen to hear that they qualified as an actual football nation. People in the US were pleasant, although for travelling fans most experiences will have been complicated by the ubiquitous request for a tip. I did think, though, that sometimes there was an expectation that we should all be delighted to be there. Which we were, but perhaps not for the reasons some wanted.PMCanada and Mexico? Excellent. US stadiums were first class, as was the extent to which the nation embraced the World Cup. It is just impossible to ignore the stench of the Balogun situation.EMOn an on-field football level? Far from spectacular. The US needs a new player-development architecture to ensure there is a pipeline of high-quality players. They also need a new head coach, with the right vision for the future. Tournament organisation? The fact that Fifa took direct control of it should be a cautionary tale. A local organising committee is always needed.OOView image in fullscreenMauricio Pochettino speaks to his USA players after their defeat by Belgium which eliminated the co-hosts.Photograph: Agustín Marcarian/ReutersAll three co-hosts fared about as well as I had projected, but each may have exceeded my expectations in terms of how they went about it. Canada were thrilling at times – I covered their6-0 win over Qatarat BC Place – and had plenty of pluck with Jesse Marsch as their coach-cum-hype man. The USA played some of the prettiest soccer ever seen by a US national team of any gender, and will for ever rue a missed opportunity to do something greateragainst Belgium. Mexico were one of the biggest bandwagon teams as they barnstormed Group A, then outclassed an Ecuador team on the rise and pushed England to the brink until they ran out of ideas in the final half-hour. Jobs well done across the board.JRI had six weeks in America. All the Americans were lovely.MRThis tournament put the whirled in the World Cup, its scale ensuring it evaded generalisation. Cities such as Vancouver and Seattle – with downtown stadiums and soccer traditions – were fitting hosts, while metropolises such as Dallas and San Francisco accommodated the month-long novelty without embracing it.JSnThe people in Mexico could not have been more welcoming, even when England beat their side. It was the same in Kansas City, England’s base and where I spent most of my time. On the other hand it would have been better if getting around some US cities was easier. Public transport infrastructure isn’t really a thing in some places. Florida especially. The Boston traffic was ridiculous, too.JStA wide range depending on venue but broadly friendly, welcoming and enthusiastic. Would benefit from improved public transport and generally lower volume. Wifi hopeless, coffee weak.JWExplore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026World CupUS sportsAustralia sportfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: England cannot afford trip to amputee World Cup – and the FA will not help | Soccer | The Guardian
description: With no national funding, England’s successful men’s amputee side is struggling to raise the £50,000 needed for November’s tournament in Mexico
Author: Sam Cunningham
Headline: With no national funding, England’s successful men’s amputee side is struggling to raise the £50,000 needed for November’s tournament in Mexico
Content: Thomas Atkinson (left) and Martin Heald (right) were part of the England team that won the first Amputee Nations League in 2023.Photograph: LAJ PhotographyView image in fullscreenThomas Atkinson (left) and Martin Heald (right) were part of the England team that won the first Amputee Nations League in 2023.Photograph: LAJ PhotographySoccerEngland cannot afford trip to amputee World Cup – and the FA will not helpWith no national funding, England’s successful men’s amputee side is struggling to raise the £50,000 needed for November’s tournament in MexicoSam CunninghamWed 22 Jul 2026 09.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 18.33 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleEvery time Scott Rogers walks into St George’s Park there is a sign on the wall that catches his eye. It boasts that the 330-acre site is the proud home of all England’s football teams. “I look at it every time and go, ‘Well, not all of them’” Rogers says.As head coach, Rogers guided the England men’s amputee team to the Nations League trophy in 2023. But while the Football Association funds six para national teams, amputee football is not among them.Kyiv’s League of the Mighty: how amputee football supports Ukraine’s survivorsRead moreNow one of the world’s leading amputee nations is struggling to raise enough money to compete at the men’s World Cup in Mexico in November. England earned £21.5m in prize money for finishing third in the World Cup and got £1.1m in preparation funds. The amputee side are trying to scrape together £50,000 to cover their tournament costs.“The FA say they’re funded for a certain amount of para teams and at the moment there isn’t the funding to bring amputee football into the fold,” Rogers says. “We’re Nations League champions, and there’s a chance we won’t go to this World Cup. For an England team at the top of the game to miss a World Cup due to funding, it doesn’t sit right.”The amputee programme was under the FA umbrella until 2006, when the governing body cut funding. “That left a lot of bad feeling at the time,” Rogers says.They set up as a charity and have built a thriving structure. The women’s team reached the quarter-finals of the inaugural World Cup in 2024. The junior system has more than 70 players aged four to 17. They have doctors, strength and conditioning coaches, analysts, technical coaches. It is run by volunteers and funded by donations.Rogers, a former youth coach at Wigan and Blackburn, became involved in 2000. “Whatever I’d do with an under-18 elite able-bodied team, I’d do with these lads because they were incredible,” he says.View image in fullscreenScott Rogers (fourth from left) coached England’s men’s amputee team for a year and helped them win the Nations League.Photograph: LAJ Photography“When people think about amputee football, they probably imagine it’s slow and laborious.It’s the opposite: fast-paced, brutal at times. They snap crutches almost every game. I fell in love with it.”FA representatives shadowed the team at the 2022 World Cup and were impressed. But though they made a funding offer, it fell short of what was in place. “All the signs were good,” Rogers says. But the offer would have reduced training frequency and cut the number of coaches and specialists. It was declined. Coming back under the FA umbrella while continuing to take money from other sources was not an option, Rogers says.WhenOwen Coyle Jr– son of the former Burnley manager – stepped down as head coach in 2023, Rogers took over. Previously, players each had to raise £1,500 to secure their place for tournaments, but Rogers scrapped the requirement.An appearance on ITV’s Good Morning Britain provided the funding boost to play at Euro 2024, their pot rising from £4,000 of their £40,000 target to more than £50,000 within hours. For the World Cup this year, they are not even halfway to the £50,000 needed.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“These people don’t get paid for playing, they have jobs,” Rogers says. “If the programme isn’t of a certain level, what’s the incentive to continue to play? They sacrifice holidays with their families.”View image in fullscreenRhyce Ramsden (right) flies into an attempted tackle on fellow England amputee player Nathan Fisher (left) during a training session.Photograph: LAJ PhotographyRogers, who stepped down as head coach in 2024 but remains heavily involved, says even £50,000 a year from the FA would go a long way. “When you look at the funding within the FA and the money involved in football, what we’re asking for to make an amazing programme is a drop in the ocean.”The amputee players are paying for their own flights for a game in Ireland next month. “It’s there, it’s ready to go,” Rogers says. “But the danger is if we don’t secure long-term funding soon – because we can’t keep going on telly, it’s a model that won’t continue to work – I feel that we’re going to start to fall away.”Rogers says there are “brilliant” people at the FAwho run the para programmeand want amputee football included, but cannot secure the funding from above. “There are always ongoing conversations with the FA and it’s not a bad relationship. But unless something changes and somebody stands up and goes: ‘Listen, this can’t happen any more,’ I think it’ll be ground into the ground when we’re on the verge of something incredibly special.”An FA spokesperson said: “We continue to work closely with the England Amputee Football Association to support, identify and help deliver their key priorities over the coming season, and the continued development of amputee football in England.”Explore more on these topicsFootballDisability and sportfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Putellas, Batlle and León are gone: what will be left of Barcelona after latest exodus? | Barcelona Women | The Guardian
description: The European champions’ squad is falling apart … because of the men’s team’s struggles with financial fair play
Author: Alex Ibaceta
Headline: The European champions’ squad is falling apart … because of the men’s team’s struggles with financial fair play
Content: Barcelona players, including Alexia Putellas (far left) who has left the club this summer, in training in May.Photograph: NurPhoto/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenBarcelona players, including Alexia Putellas (far left) who has left the club this summer, in training in May.Photograph: NurPhoto/Getty ImagesMoving the Goalposts newsletterBarcelona WomenPutellas, Batlle and León are gone: what will be left of Barcelona after latest exodus?The European champions’ squad is falling apart … because of the men’s team’s struggles with financial fair playAlex IbacetaWed 22 Jul 2026 08.07 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 18.21 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe joy of lifting all four trophies in 2025-26 has been short-lived for Barcelona’s women’s team as they face a second consecutive summer exodus.Ifwinning the Champions Leagueis not enough to retain players then you have to wonder what is. Seven players have left the European champions so far with 19-year-old Renee van Asten from Ajax the only arrival.To make matters worse some of the team’s best players are those departing with twice Ballon d’Or winner Alexia Putellas top of that list. Fellow Spain internationals Ona Batlle, Mapi León and Salma Paralluelo have also left.The reason? As so often when it comes to football: money. The men’s La Liga financial fair play troubles continue to disrupt the club from top to bottom. At the time of writing the women’s team have 17 senior players – two goalkeepers, six defenders, six midfielders and three forwards – although they are in talks to sign the Brazil forward Kerolin from Manchester City.Putellas was the biggest loss for the club, leaving her home of 14 yearsfor Michele Kang’s London City Lionessesin the Women’s Super League. The fact that she left on a free transfer highlighted an internal management crisis at the club. Mapi Leónfollowed herBlaugranacaptain to London City, also on a free transfer, where they will be reunited with three former teammates; Jana Fernández, María Pérez and Lucía Corrales.The WSL is a popular destination for Barcelona players with 14 of their last 19 departures joining clubs in England.Batlle signed for Arsenal, where she will join up with the former Barcelona winger Mariona Caldentey. Salma Paralluelo has yet to announce where she will be playing next season, though PSG are the favourite to sign her after negotiations with Chelsea broke down.Those 19 departures have come since 2024 and they include academy players who have made regular appearances for the first team. At the same time there have only been two signings and two academy players who have joined the senior side. The reserve goalkeeper Ellie Roebuck arrived in July 2024, but left after one season, while Laia Aleixandri joined on a free transfer from Manchester City in June 2025.View image in fullscreenOna Batlle has joined Arsenal.Photograph: David Price/Arsenal FC/Getty ImagesWith their pre-season training commencing on Monday 27 July the pressure is on to sign players to reinforce a squad that looks threadbare at best and dangerously thin at worst. The club have said that there will be signings – and that they will be announced soon – but whether they are seasoned pros or academy products remain to be seen.Last season academy products Aïcha Camara and Clara Serrajordi were given first-team contracts with the club in a similar situation. In the past Barcelona have been able to promote high-quality players from within but now the problem is that key academy players are leaving as well. The goalkeeper Txell Font left for city neighbours FC Badalona, Adriana Ranera signed for Deportiva de A Coruña, Martine Fenger left for Hoffenheim and Alba Caño landed in the NWSL with Boston Legacy.Some academy players did step up last season and arguably saved the club’s campaign. Serrajordi deputised in the midfield when Aitana Bonmatí or Patri Guijarro were out and Camara played a big role in the defensive line with Batlle picking up a month-long injury. However, as the best academy players have already been given first-team contracts there are fewer options to turn to this summer. Midfielders Carla Julià (19) and Rosalía Domínguez (17) seem to be current favourites to make the jump to the first team should the club prioritise signing in-house talent.Last season Barcelona Femení made a profit of €1.955m (£1.67m), their best financial result since the 2023-24 campaign. That came off the back of the Champions League prize money being increased and the team collecting all three domestic trophies. However, with some of their biggest names departing they are unlikely to match that figure in 2026-27.Losing Batlle will have a big impact on the defence, coming as it does a year after Fridolina Rölfö joined Manchester United. Van Asten, as mentioned, has signed from Ajax and Martina Fernández looks likely to return from Everton. The forward line appears even thinner, although the coach, Pere Romeu, has a particular style of football and can utilise midfielders such as Vicky López, Kika Nazareth and Sydney Schertenleib in attack.It feels like this team is one major injury away from imploding. They got away with it last season – in fact, they won everything they could – but for how long can this carry on? A team hoping to defend their title as European champions can’t continue to rely solely on academy products for ever.Get in touchIf you have any questions or comments about any of our newsletters please emailmoving.goalposts@theguardian.com.This is an extract from our free email about women’s football, Moving the Goalposts. To get the full edition,visit this page and follow the instructions. Moving the Goalposts will be sent out once a week, on Wednesdays, in the close season but will be back on Tuesdays and Thursdays from September.Explore more on these topicsBarcelona WomenMoving the Goalposts newsletterBarcelonaWomen's footballfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Southampton head coach Tonda Eckert charged with misconduct by FA over spygate scandal | Southampton | The Guardian
description: Southampton’s manager, Tonda Eckert, is facing a lengthy suspension after being charged with three counts of misconduct by the Football Association
Author: Louise Taylor
Headline: Southampton’s manager, Tonda Eckert, is facing a lengthy suspension after being charged with three counts of misconduct by the Football Association
Content: The Southampton manager, Tonda Eckert, admitted to spying on three Championship opponents last season.Photograph: Cody Froggatt/PAView image in fullscreenThe Southampton manager, Tonda Eckert, admitted to spying on three Championship opponents last season.Photograph: Cody Froggatt/PASouthamptonSouthampton head coach Tonda Eckert charged with misconduct by FA over spygate scandal33-year-old German could be hit with one-year banSouth-coast club denied chance to win promotionLouise TaylorWed 22 Jul 2026 06.54 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 18.14 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleSouthampton’s manager, Tonda Eckert, is facing a lengthy suspension from all football activity after being charged with three counts of misconduct by the Football Association.They relate to the Spygate scandal and involve breaching FA rules during covert missions to illicitly observeChampionshiptraining sessions involving Middlesbrough, Ipswich and Oxford last season.Southampton owner will not sack apologetic Eckert despite role in spying scandalRead moreThe 33-year-old has until Tuesday to respond to the charges and may well fear the precedent set by Fifa when it banned Bev Priestman, the English-born former Canada women’s head coach and assistant England women coach,from football worldwide for 12 months.In 2024 Priestman, then in charge of Canada, was found to have authorised the use of a drone to monitor New Zealand’s training during the Paris Olympics.Southampton have already been punished for Spygate by the English Football League when in May an independent disciplinary commission found the Championship clubguilty of three counts of football espionage.That ruling stripped Eckert’s team of their right to appear in the playoff final in addition to ruling that they must start the coming Championship campaign handicapped by a four-point deduction. The EFL, though, has no power to issue sanctions against individuals in such instances, so the case against Eckert was handed to the FA, which launched a long-running inquiry.At the start of July the governing body spoke to Eckert, a former analyst for the Germany national team, at length and have the power to issue a range of punishments, ranging from a warning to a heavy fine to suspension.Yet whereas Priestman’s ban was worldwide, the FA only possess the power to prevent Eckert working in England. The governing body does, however, have the right to ask Fifa to apply any suspension internationally.View image in fullscreenTonda Eckert takes charge of a pre-season friendly for Southampton.Photograph: James Marsh/ShutterstockIn 2023, the Italian FA succeeded with such a request when they demanded that the then Newcastle midfielder Sandro Tonali’s 10-month suspension for betting irregularities be applied worldwide. In Eckert’s case, an England-only ban would appear almost meaningless were he to be offered a job abroad.Eckert has alreadyapologised to Southampton fansand admitted he should have known that EFL regulations specifically outlawed spying. A coach who grew up within a European culture that tacitly tolerates observing rivals’ training sessions also spoke of the “bitter irony” that Southampton’s three spying missions had “no impact” on his team’s planning or performances.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionWilliam Salt, the intern first-team analyst, was caught filming Middlesbrough’s training session on a mobile phone before the first leg of a playoff semi-final won on aggregate by Southampton before their expulsion. He has since been offered a permanent post in the club’s academy. Salt has not been the subject of any FA charges.Southampton’s owner, Dragan Solak, has vowed to stand by Eckert and has maintained he will not sack him. But a possible 12-month – or, as some sources are suggesting, six-month FA ban – could alter that stance.In a statement on Wednesday Southampton said: “Tonda and the club will continue to cooperate fully and openly with the FA. Our focus remains on preparations for the 2026-27 season. The club remains fully supportive of Tonda and his staff as we work towards our ambition of returning to the Premier League.”Eckert, who saw Middlesbrough take Southampton’s place in a playoff finalwon by Hull, recently told BBC Radio Solent: “I’ve held my hand up for this judgment on something I have authorised. I am responsible and I apologise for bad judgment.”Back in 2019, the FA issued formal warnings to the then Leeds manager Marcelo Bielsa and a club analystfor spying on Derby, but English football’s regulations have since been tightened.Explore more on these topicsSouthamptonThe FAChampionshipFootball LeagueFootball politicsnewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘A unique talent’: Phil Foden signs new four-year Manchester City contract | Manchester City | The Guardian
description: Phil Foden has signed a new four-year contract with Manchester City, saying he is looking forward to ‘trying to win more’ at his boyhood club.
Author: Jamie Jackson
Headline: Phil Foden has signed a new four-year contract with Manchester City, saying he is looking forward to ‘trying to win more’ at his boyhood club.
Content: Phil Foden, who went to the British GP this month, said: ‘Committing my future to City means everything to me.’Photograph: Andy Hone/LAT ImagesView image in fullscreenPhil Foden, who went to the British GP this month, said: ‘Committing my future to City means everything to me.’Photograph: Andy Hone/LAT ImagesManchester City‘A unique talent’: Phil Foden signs new four-year Manchester City contract26-year-old looking forward to working with Maresca‘To stay this long at the club is a dream’Jamie JacksonWed 22 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 18.28 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GooglePhil Foden has signed a new four-year contract withManchester City, saying he is looking forward to “trying to win more” at his boyhood club. The deal, which has an additional 12-month option, ties the 26-year-old to the club until 2030.Foden is relishing working again with Enzo Maresca. The new City manager was Pep Guardiola’s ­assistant during the 2022-23 treble-winning season.Foden said: “Committing my future to City means everything to me. Playing for this club is all I’ve ever wanted to do and it’s always an honour to wear the shirt. It’s not lost on me that I’ve been lucky enough to be part of a historic period with so many titles won, but we’re always looking to the future and trying to win more.Morgan Rogers ‘excited to join biggest club in London’ after making £117m Chelsea moveRead more“I can only thank the club, staff, teammates and fans who continue to put their faith in me. I hope I’ll be able to repay you for years to come. I can’t wait to work with Enzo again. He was brilliant during the treble season – someone all the players respected and loved working with.“To have him back here is exciting. Personally, I know I’ve improved so much from ­working with our coaches and world-class squad. I am excited for the next phase of my career at this great club.”With John Stones and Bernardo Silva having left the club, Foden is City’s most decorated player with 17 major honours. The forward has won six Premier Leagues, one Champions League, three FA Cups, five League Cups,one Uefa Super Cupandone Club World Cup.Foden expressed disbelief at how long he has been at City. “Not in a million years [would I think this],” he said. “I would say I had a dream to make it to the first team, but to stay this long, it’s a dream. And like I said, I’m just enjoying it and trying to make the most of it.”Foden is hopeful Maresca’s mode of play will suit him. “You can tell by the first few sessions I’ve done with him – the way he likes to play out from the back and play a lot of possession-based – it suits my game really well. I’m looking forward to that and hopefully it can help my game go to the next stage,” he said.The new deal indicates the firm belief of Maresca and Hugo Viana, the director of football, that Foden can recapture his form. He lost his starting place under Guardiola and was left out of England’s World Cup squad. Foden’s form has fallen away since he was a key part ofCity’s 2023-24 title successand winning the Football Writers’ Association and Professional Footballers’ Association player of the year awards.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionView image in fullscreenPhil Foden says he is hoping his game can reach another level with Enzo Maresca.Photograph: Manchester CityLast season, after the2-2 draw with Nottingham Foreston 4 March, Foden started two league games and was substituted after 58 minutes of the2-1 FA Cup semi-final victory over Southampton. Foden has spent his whole career with City, joining as an eight-year-old.Viana said: “Everyone at ­Manchester City is incredibly proud of Phil. He represents everything good about our academy. He is a unique ­talent and his creativity is a joy to watch. None of what he has achieved would be possible without the focus, dedication and humility to work hard in every moment and absorb all of the knowledge available to him through our coaches and players.“At 26, his development is still ongoing, and we look forward to ­seeing the player Phil can become over the coming years.”After the2025 FA Cup 1-0 final defeat by Crystal Palace, Foden revealed he had been struggling with off-field problems. He had also played with pain after suffering an ankle problem sustained from a Casemiro tackle ina goalless draw at ­Manchester Unitedthat April.Explore more on these topicsManchester CityPhil FodennewsShareReuse this content

title: Rugby players seek new lawyers for brain injury cases against governing bodies | Sport | The Guardian
description: Richard Boardman has left the case against rugby league and rugby union governing bodies and the players have said the process of appointing new solicitors is under way
Author: Andy Bull
Headline: Richard Boardman has left the case against rugby league and rugby union governing bodies and the players have said the process of appointing new solicitors is under way
Content: Two cases against the governing bodies in rugby league and rugby union have been held up by a series of arguments.Photograph: Andrew Boyers/Action Images/ReutersView image in fullscreenTwo cases against the governing bodies in rugby league and rugby union have been held up by a series of arguments.Photograph: Andrew Boyers/Action Images/ReutersSportRugby players seek new lawyers for brain injury cases against governing bodiesRichard Boardman was criticised by the managing judgePlayers want ‘firm with resources and specialist expertise’Andy BullWed 22 Jul 2026 15.07 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 17.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleHundreds of former rugby league and rugby union players taking legal action against the game’s governing bodies are now “staring into the precipice”, according to the barrister representing the claimants, Susan Rodway KC.Richard Boardman, the solicitor who has been the organising force behind the litigation, has requested formally to come off the record after his handling of the case was criticised by the managing judge, Senior Master Cook. The case may now be taken over by Leigh Day, who has been working on it as consultants since February.Rugby’s landmark concussion case enters crucial week: the key questions answeredRead moreThe players issued a statement on Wednesday night that they had decided to “terminate the instruction of Rylands Garth” and that the process of appointing new solicitors is under way. “This decision has not been taken lightly. We would like to place on record our sincere gratitude to Rylands Garth and in particular Richard Boardman,” the statement said.“We concluded it was in the best interests of the claimants to appoint a firm with resources and specialist expertise required to lead the litigation through its next phase. We would like to reassure the court, our fellow claimants and the wider rugby family that we believe this represents a positive step in progressing the litigation.”Cook described it as a “dramatic change”. It comes as the claimants face the threat of strikeouts because of Rylands Garth’s failure to comply with the judge’s order to disclose to the defendants all available medical documents. If the strikeouts go ahead, then hundreds of claimants will be removed from the proceedings.“There is a sword hanging over these players,” Rodway said, as she appealed for relief from the strikeouts. “Although the issues are generic the impact of them relates to distinct individuals who have suffered life‑changing injuries, and who have suffered enormous stress and anxiety.”Some, Rodway said, have taken their own lives. “These are not claims for double glazing gone wrong, but for catastrophic brain injuries and each of these players has a claim for hundreds of thousands, if not millions, of pounds.”According to Boardman’s court filings, Leigh Day is “engaging in a period of due diligence in order to decide whether they would be in a position to take over subject to the clients’ agreement”. A spokesperson for Leigh Day said the firm “have not had an opportunity to properly consider whether they can come on record as solicitors in this claim” and that they “are reviewing the position”.The claimants argue “the change of solicitor on the record not only demonstrates how seriously the claimants take the criticisms levelled at them, but that there will be a completely different regime going forward in which both the court and the defendants can have full confidence”.Confidence seems to be in very short supply. For the past two years, both cases, which are still in the pre-trial phase, have been held up by a series of arguments about whether or not Boardman’s firm, Rylands Garth, have complied with their disclosure obligations.The defendants – World Rugby, theRugby Football Union, the Welsh Rugby Union in one case, and the Rugby Football League and the British Amateur Rugby League Association in the other – requested originally to be allowed to conduct their own medical testing on the claimants. It was decided by the court this full medical testing would be “complex, expensive, and unnecessary” and that the claimants would be obliged to disclose all medical records in their control to the defendants.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThe BreakdownThe latest rugby union news and analysis, plus all the week's action reviewedPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe two legal teams would then be able to select their own pools of proposed lead claimants from among the hundreds involved. There has been little progress in this because, the defendants argue, Rylands Garth has failed repeatedly to provide all the documents.Cook was unequivocal that he agrees with them on this. He said he felt “at a loss to understand” Boardman’s position. “On any view there has been widespread non-compliance and there has been late compliance.” He also accused Boardman of “providing misleading evidence in court”.“We’ve been four years trying to get to this point,” he said. “It has required orders and those orders have had to be backed up by an Unless Order, and even that wasn’t accepted, but appealed against.”Cook did not agree with the claimants’ argument that the defendants were pettifogging them. “It’s not the defendants being difficult for the sake of being difficult, it is the defendants wanting to make sure this process has integrity we can all rely on. We’re here to do justice to people, but the process has to have integrity.”Rodway argued the claimants “are altering the process and will have a different person at the head” and that “the change of solicitors is significantly important because it shows the players taking matters into their own hands”. Leigh Day recently won a case against British Gymnastics over abuse allegations in the past. It is understood that Boardman intends to remain involved in the case as co-counsel.Explore more on these topicsSportRugby unionRugby leagueWorld RugbyConcussion in sportRugby Football UnionWelsh Rugby UnionnewsShareReuse this content

title: New Rangers manager Derek McInnes gets four-match ban for Celtic penalty remarks | Scottish Premiership | The Guardian
description: Derek McInnes, the new Rangers manager, is poised to begin the season in the stands after being hit with a four-match ban for his comments on Celtic’s controversial penalty at Motherwell last season
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Derek McInnes, the new Rangers manager, is poised to begin the season in the stands after being hit with a four-match ban for his comments on Celtic’s controversial penalty at Motherwell last season
Content: The Rangers manager, Derek McInnes, talks with Lawrence Shankland and Emmanuel Fernandez during a pre-season trip to Spain.Photograph: Kirk O’Rourke/Rangers FC/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenThe Rangers manager, Derek McInnes, talks with Lawrence Shankland and Emmanuel Fernandez during a pre-season trip to Spain.Photograph: Kirk O’Rourke/Rangers FC/ShutterstockScottish PremiershipNew Rangers manager Derek McInnes gets four-match ban for Celtic penalty remarksFormer Hearts coach railed at ‘disgusting’ VAR decisionSFA punishment means ban for start of new seasonPA MediaWed 22 Jul 2026 13.01 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 15.53 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDerek McInnes, the newRangersmanager, is poised to begin the season in the stands after being handed a four-match ban for his comments on Celtic’s controversial penalty at Motherwell last season.The then-Hearts manager described the VAR decision to give Celtic a stoppage-time spot-kick – which proved crucial in theScottish Premiershiptitle race – as “disgusting”. On Wednesday the Scottish Football Association announced McInnes would receive a four-match ban, with one game suspended until the end of the season.McInnes watched Hearts beat Falkirk 3-0 in the penultimate game of the league season, which looked like they would have had to avoid a three-goal defeat at Parkhead on the final day to win the title for the first time since 1960. However, the scenario changed later that night when Celtic were awarded a penalty by the referee John Beaton –after an interjection by VAR Andrew Dallas– deep into added time at Fir Park.Kelechi Iheanacho scored to secure a 3-2 win that meant Martin O’Neill’s side needed any victory at all on the final day to keep the title in the east end of Glasgow for the fifth successive time, which the Hoops clinchedwith another late winner.Asked for his view of the penalty decision after beating Falkirk, McInnes described it as “disgusting” and he was charged with breaking rule 72 of the SFA disciplinary guidelines and issued with an official notice of complaint. A hearing at Hampden on Wednesday found the charge proven and McInnes was given a four-match suspension, with one match suspended until the end of the 2026-27 season.Rangers begin their league season with an away game against Dundee United on Friday week. The suspension would mean McInnes is banned from the touchline for that game, as well as home matches against Hibernian and St Mirren.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe then Motherwell midfielder Elliot Watt received a four-match suspension after using his X account to describe the same incident as “the worst VAR decision in history”. Watt, now at the Turkish club Samsunspor, was charged with breaching the Scottish FA’s disciplinary rule 72, which says players should not “criticise” decisions made by officials or make comments that “impinge” on the character of a referee.Explore more on these topicsScottish PremiershipRangersCelticHeartsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Tour de France 2026: Jasper Philipsen wins stage 17 as Adam Yates struggles with illness – as it happened | Tour de France 2026 | The Guardian
description: Jasper Philipsen got his first Tour stage win this year in a reduced sprint while Tadej Pogacar’s teammate Adam Yates was affected by a stomach bug
Author: Niall McVeigh
Headline: Jasper Philipsen got his first Tour stage win this year in a reduced sprint while Tadej Pogacar’s teammate Adam Yates was affected by a stomach bug
Content: 10h ago12.16 EDTPhilipsen takes overdue win as Yates suffersJasper Philipsen took victory in a reduced bunch sprint at the end of the Tour de France’s 17th stage on Wednesday to end a “horrible” run of near misses.Set up by a strong lead-out from Alpecin-Premier Tech teammate Mathieu van der Poel, Philipsen finished several bike lengths ahead of Mauro Schmid and Olav Kooij, who had each already won a stage at the Tour.It was an 11th career Tour stage victory overall for the Belgian, who won the sprinters’ green points jersey in 2023 but had yet to claim a stage this year despite five previous top-five finishes.“I was prepared very, very well, I was in the shape of my life,” said Philipsen. “The first stage (of the race) was horrible, I couldn’t find myself, I couldn’t find the form I was hoping for. After 17 stages ... we won for the team.”Tim Merlier had won three sprint stages but dropped out of the Tour on Sunday’s tough mountain stage, and this time, Philipsen proved the quickest at the end of the 174.7km stage from Chambéry to Voiron.Philipsen’s main rival for the green jersey, Mads Pedersen, could only finish eighth, with Schmid (Jayco-Alula) and Kooij (Decathlon) followed home by British rider Lewis Askey (NSN) in fourth place.The overall leader and defending champion Tadej Pogacar came home safely in the main peloton around nine minutes later. But there was one concern for Pogacar, as UAE Emirates teammate Adam Yates struggled with a stomach issue and finished 26 minutes after Philipsen.After a day featuring several failed breakaways, Jasper Stuyven attacked and went clear of the leading group. The Belgian battled gamely but was caught with under 4km to go, allowing Van der Poel to control the final stages and set up Philipsen for an overdue win.AFPShareUpdated at12.19 EDT

title: Glasgow Commonwealth Games organisers hope for late surge in ticket sales | Commonwealth Games 2026 | The Guardian
description: The Commonwealth Games opening ceremony is yet to sell out, as are several events on a much-reduced programme from the last time the city hosted in 2014
Author: Yara El-Shaboury
Headline: The Commonwealth Games opening ceremony is yet to sell out, as are several events on a much-reduced programme from the last time the city hosted in 2014
Content: The England athletes Gabrielle Idle-Beavers (left) and Evie Lambert in Glasgow on the eve of the Commonwealth Games.Photograph: Jeremy Sutton-Hibbert/The GuardianView image in fullscreenThe England athletes Gabrielle Idle-Beavers (left) and Evie Lambert in Glasgow on the eve of the Commonwealth Games.Photograph: Jeremy Sutton-Hibbert/The GuardianCommonwealth Games 2026Glasgow Commonwealth Games organisers hope for late surge in ticket salesOpening ceremony and several events yet to sell outProgramme reduced from last time city hosted in 2014Yara El-Shabouryin GlasgowWed 22 Jul 2026 12.10 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 16.22 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleCommonwealth Games organisers will hope for a late surge in ticket sales as Glasgow prepares to stage the opening ceremony on Thursday night, with seats still available across a number of events at the four competition venues despite thesignificantly reduced scale.The opening ceremony at the OVO Hydro still had adult tickets available on Wednesday, with prices ranging from £45 to £95. The 13,000-capacity arena will host live music, the parade of nations and the arrival of all 74 Commonwealth batons. The six-time Olympic cycling champion Sir Chris Hoy, who revealed in 2024that he has stage-four cancer, will also take part.Madeleine McCann’s younger brother Sean to swim for Scotland at Glasgow 2026Read moreGlasgow 2026 isa very different propositionfrom the city’s widely praised 2014 Games. The opening ceremony 12 years ago was at Celtic Park, where more than 40,000 people watched Queen Elizabeth II officially declare the Games open. This year’s event has been scaled back after the Australian state of Victoriawithdrew as hosts over rising costs.Only 500,000 tickets have been made available for the Games, less than half the 1.2 million for Glasgow 2014. Organisers have faced questions over demand, with reports suggesting ticket sales have been slower than expected and public interest more subdued than during the previous edition held in the city. Prices vary considerably across the programme: adult tickets for bowls and para bowls start from £10, while Friday’s swimming session featuring the three-time Olympic gold medallist Adam Peaty carries a starting price of £90.View image in fullscreenClyde (centre), an anthropomorphic thistle who was the mascot of the Glasgow 2014 games, is acting in that capacity for Team Scotland this year.Photograph: Jeremy Sutton-Hibbert/The GuardianThis month, members of Scotland’s Tartan Army supporters’ club were offered free tickets for selected sessions, including athletics, para powerlifting, netball and 3x3 basketball. Organisers said the promotion was a gesture of thanks to fans who supportedScotlandat the football World Cup.Scotland will be led into the opening ceremony by the veteran lawn bowler Alex Marshall and the double Commonwealth judo champion Sarah Adlington. Marshall, 59, is Scotland’s most decorated Commonwealth athlete with five gold medals, plus a silver, while Adlington won gold on home soil in 2014 and at Birmingham 2022.England’s flag bearers will be the lawn bowler Sian Honnor and the swimmer Luke Greenbank. The latter won relay gold in 2022, while the former claimed Commonwealth titles in Glasgow and Birmingham.Explore more on these topicsCommonwealth Games 2026Commonwealth GamesScotlandnewsShareReuse this content

title: Liverpool issue 432 life bans and seize £1.2m in ticket tout investigation | Liverpool | The Guardian
description: Liverpool have banned 432 people and £1.2m has been seized after a joint operation with Merseyside police to combat illicit ticket sales
Author: Andy Hunter
Headline: Liverpool have banned 432 people and £1.2m has been seized after a joint operation with Merseyside police to combat illicit ticket sales
Content: Liverpool took part in the first operation of its kind between a Premier League club and their local police authority in a clampdown on ticket touts.Photograph: Phil Noble/ReutersView image in fullscreenLiverpool took part in the first operation of its kind between a Premier League club and their local police authority in a clampdown on ticket touts.Photograph: Phil Noble/ReutersLiverpoolLiverpool issue 432 life bans and seize £1.2m in ticket tout investigationAlmost 700,000 ticket registrations were analysedClub worked with local police economic crime teamAndy Hunterin ChicagoWed 22 Jul 2026 10.29 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 16.19 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleMore than £1.2m in assets have been seized and 432 lifetime bans issued as part of a clampdown byLiverpoolFC and Merseyside police on ticket touting. In the first operation of its kind between a Premier League club and their local police authority, Liverpool’s investigations team worked with the force’s economic crime team to analyse almost 700,000 ticket registrations last season and to focus on the laundering of criminal proceeds from illicit ticket sales.The joint investigation has led to £1.2m in assets related to organised ticket touts being detained with several cases taking place under the Proceeds of Crime Act. Two people have been convicted and another is due in court next month. The assets seized will be split between Merseyside police and the Home Office.Amazon founder Jeff Bezos in talks to join consortium seeking 30% stake in LiverpoolRead moreLiverpool blocked or cancelled 67,663 suspected fake accounts last season and a further 121,379 remain under investigation. As well as 432 lifetime bans, the club issued 115 indefinite suspensions where the account holder faces a three-year wait on average before they can apply for reinstatement.Most bans were imposed for the unauthorised selling of season tickets, membership tickets or hospitality tickets. Lifetime bans and suspensions were also imposed in three cases of racist and homophobic abuse, 13 instances of inappropriate touching or language, four for assault and one for tragedy chanting.Investigations by the club and police outside Anfield on match days last season resulted in a 33% increase in the number of burner phones either identified or seized.Detective chief inspector Nick Suffield, from Merseyside police’s economic crime team, said: “We will always take action to protect genuine fans who fall victim to touts when all they are trying to do is simply purchase tickets to support their team.“Our activities to target touts operating both online and on match days will continue and we work closely with supporters and clubs to keep identifying ticket touts and doing all we can to prevent them taking advantage of supporters and spoiling people’s experience of football and the city.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“Our advice to football supporters is clear: don’t buy from ticket touts. You will be taking a huge risk with a lot of money and may ultimately be refused entry to the match with no way of getting your money back.”Explore more on these topicsLiverpoolFootball ticket pricesTicket pricesnewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘Our hearts are crushed’: Hannah Rapp’s violent death sparks outpouring of grief | US crime | The Guardian
description: Family and boxing community grieving deeply after Rapp was killed by motorist while she was cycling in Texas
Author: Ramon Antonio Vargas
Headline: Family and boxing community grieving deeply after Rapp was killed by motorist while she was cycling in Texas
Content: Hannah Rapp.Photograph: Harry Aaron/Most Valuable PromotionsView image in fullscreenHannah Rapp.Photograph: Harry Aaron/Most Valuable PromotionsUS crime‘Our hearts are crushed’: Hannah Rapp’s violent death sparks outpouring of griefFamily and boxing community grieving deeply after Rapp was killed by motorist while she was cycling in TexasRamon Antonio VargasWed 22 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 08.07 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe violent death of the US boxerHannah Rapp, whom authorities say was killed recently by a motorist while she cycled, has left her sport, her communities outside the ring and her loved ones grieving deeply just weeks after she took her first world title shot.A statement from the Bryan Boxing gym where Rapp trained inTexascommemorated her as “an amazing lady and soul” while the Aggieland Cycling club to which she belonged recalled how her “kindness and excitement for life [buoyed] all who knew her”.“A beautiful person and a brilliant boxer is how she will be remembered by all of us,” read a social media post from Most Valuable Promotions, which promoted Rapp’s 13 June 2026 bout against the WorldBoxingCouncil (WBC) women’s featherweight boxing champion, Tiara Brown. “[Rapp] went to war and smiled right after.”US boxer Hannah Rapp struck and killed by driver while cycling, police sayRead moreSuch condolences poured in after Rapp, 26, and her boyfriend went bicycling on the morning of 18 July and encountered a hatchback car on a highway in Navasota,Texas.The driver, identified as 31-year-old Charles Medina, then sped past the couple from behind, investigators with the local Brazos county sheriff’s office later wrote in a sworn affidavit. Rapp’s boyfriend responded to the maneuver by raising his hands and yelling, at which point Medina slammed his brakes, skidded to a stop and accelerated in reverse, the affidavit alleged.Medina ultimately hit Rapp so forcefully that his “rear windshield was shattered completely”, investigators wrote. He also allegedly left skidmarks from having “rapidly accelerated backwards” as well as apparently “steering towards something while backing up”, according to the legal document.Emergency responders found Rapp unresponsive and lying face down in the roadway, the affidavit said. She was pronounced dead after being taken to a hospital.An investigator who spoke to Medina at the scene reported that the driver claimed to believe Rapp and her boyfriend were flagging him down as he passed them on his way to pick up his mother. He allegedly said he was backing up at a slow speed of about 15mph and didn’t see Rapp until an instant before striking her.But “the physical evidence on scene, including skid marks, the severity of damage to the vehicle and the victim’s final resting position contradicted [Medina’s] account,” the affidavit said.In a statement to the Guardian, the sheriff’s office declined to say that it was treating Rapp’s death as an instance of road rage, explaining that the case remained under investigation. The agency nonetheless booked Medina on a count of manslaughter, which Texas law defines as recklessly causing a person’s death.Medina has prior arrests in connection with allegations of deadly conduct by discharging a firearm, assault causing bodily injury and reckless driving, among other purported offenses.He remained in the custody of the Brazos county jail on Tuesday in lieu of a $250,000 bond, facility records showed – and he could receive between two and 20 years imprisonment under Texas law if eventually convicted of manslaughter.Medina’s rap sheet was at the center of aChange.orgpetition launched on Monday which urges Texas lawmakers to pass legislation expanding judicial authority to deny or revoke bond in cases involving defendants with “documented violent and dangerous driving histories”. The Justice for Hannah Rapp petition had collected more than 3,300 signatures as of Tuesday.Meanwhile, the case left many grappling with the premature end of the life and athletic career of Rapp, a native of Yorktown,Indiana, who ran cross country at Purdue University in her home state before moving to College Station, Texas.Rapp worked there as a fire and life safety inspector at Texas A&M University, as the Austin American Statesmanreported. Beside participating in a cycling club whose name evokes Texas A&M’s Aggies sports teams, Rapp pursued a professional boxing career which saw her register eight victories and one draw in her first nine bouts.The pugilist whose sobriquet was “Hanarchy” won the North American Boxing Federation’s featherweight title and successfully defended it once during that stretch. She thus positioned herself to challenge Brown for the WBC world title at 126lb at Florida’s Caribe Royale Orlando resort.Rapp brought Brown to a decision, losing on the judges’ scorecards but earning the champion’s respect.“She was strong, tough & ready to show the world she belonged here,” Brown wrote of Rapp on social media after the challenger’s death. “Hannah is the best [pro] opponent I have faced so far.”A social mediatribute videoposted by the promoter Texas Combat Sports contained a clip of Rapp describing why boxing fascinated her.“I’m obsessed with the little details, the incremental adjustments and the tactics of it all – I think that’s what really drives me to it,” Rapp remarked.In the video, Rapp also shared her ambitions.“I believe in my heart that I’m a multi-world champ,” she said, adding that she envisioned putting Yorktown – population of about 12,000 – “on the map” while also honoring “the people who welcomed me into their home in Texas”.“I believe that from the start. Maybe it’s naivety, but I just trust myself and believe in myself that much, and so does my team.”In a statement addressing her death, Yorktown’s governmentsaidit would hold a candlelight memorial for her on Wednesday evening. The town’s statement referred to a social media post which Rapp had published on 1 July, saying it summarized her and her home community’s “core values”.“No effort is wasted,” the postread. “The effort IS the point. The effort IS the outcome.”Rapp’s boyfriend, in his own social media post, wrote that he was grateful for those who were “working so hard to make sure she can be remembered properly”, particularly her family.“I don’t have the strength to do these things for her right now,” he wrote. “And I don’t know how they do.”“We lost our sweet baby girl Super Girl,” a separate social media post from Rapp’s father, James, read. “Our hearts are crushed – always remember her smile.”Explore more on these topicsUS crimeBoxingUS sportsTexasIndiananewsShareReuse this content

title: AI agent went rogue and hacked startup by itself, OpenAI reveals | OpenAI | The Guardian
description: Company behind ChatGPT says agent ‘cheated’ an evaluation by attacking a Hugging Face database
Author: Dan Milmo
Headline: Company behind ChatGPT says agent ‘cheated’ an evaluation by attacking a Hugging Face database
Content: Hugging Face’s chief executive said the attack was ‘mind-blowing’ but that he believed there was ‘no malicious intent’ from OpenAI.Photograph: Andre M Chang/Zuma Press Wire/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenHugging Face’s chief executive said the attack was ‘mind-blowing’ but that he believed there was ‘no malicious intent’ from OpenAI.Photograph: Andre M Chang/Zuma Press Wire/ShutterstockOpenAIAI agent went rogue and hacked startup by itself, OpenAI revealsCompany behind ChatGPT says agent ‘cheated’ an evaluation by attacking a Hugging Face databaseDan MilmoGlobal technology editorWed 22 Jul 2026 12.34 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 04.37 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleOpenAI has revealed that an autonomous AI agent powered by its technology went rogue during a test, accessed the open web and hacked a prominent startup by itself in an “unprecedented incident”.The company behind ChatGPT said the startup Hugging Face had detected and contained the agent – an AI tool designed to carry out tasks without human assistance – which had entered its systems.“We consider this incident to be an unprecedented cyber-incident, involving state-of-the-art cyber capabilities,”OpenAI said.The company said it expected this type of incident to become more commonplace as models – the technology that underpins AI tools such as chatbots and agents – become more capable. OpenAI said the hack occurred via an agent powered by a combination of its latest publicly available model, calledGPT-5.6 Sol, and an even more capable model that was yet to be released.While being tested internally on their hacking capabilities in an enclosed digital laboratory known as a sandbox, the models gained open internet access – effectively an escape route – by locating a vulnerability that had not been discovered before.The agent then hacked Hugging Face, which is a database of AI models, to locate technology that would help it pass the hacking evaluation, having “inferred” that Hugging Face might have the models, datasets and solutions for passing the test. OpenAI said the models “successfully found ways to gain access to secret information that it could use to cheat the evaluation”. The attack ended when Hugging Face’s security team and its own AI agents spotted and stopped the rogue activity.Hugging Face’s chief executive, Clément Delangue, said the attack was “mind-blowing” but believed there was “no malicious intent” from OpenAI.“We suspected last week’s cyber-attack might have come from a frontier lab, given the sophistication of the agent,” he wrote on X.When Hugging Face disclosed the hack last week it did not know OpenAI’s role in the incident, but said at the time that it had turned to a freely available Chinese AI model to analyse what had happened because the safety guardrails on commercial high-end models would not allow it to do so.The term for an unknown IT flaw is a zero-day vulnerability because developers have zero minutes to fix the problem. In April OpenAI’s close rival Anthropic said its Mythos modelhad found thousands of these flaws.The revelation of Mythos’s ability to locate and exploit zero-days led to the US government restricting exports of Mythos and its sister model, Fable 5, although it has since lifted the ban. GPT-5.6 Sol had similar restrictions but has since been rolled out worldwide.METR, a non-profit organisation that measures AI performance, said last month that Sol’s cheating rate was higher than any public model it had evaluated before. It has also recorded44 incidentsin which AI agents “deliberately acted against their users’ intentions”.The UK’s AI Security Institute (AISA)revealed in a blog post this weekthat one AI model it was evaluating, developed by an undisclosed tech firm, also went rogue and attempted to hack its testing systems. AISI said no damage had been done and it has since taken steps to make its systems more secure. It said models developed by OpenAI and Anthropic had all attempted to “cheat” during tests.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAISI warned that even more capable models may find cheating methods that are harder to detect and more damaging if they succeed, particularly in areas such as cybersecurity.One cybersecurity expert said the Hugging Face incident showed the OpenAI agent had acted “like an actual real hacker” by, for instance, seeking out zero-day vulnerabilities and using stolen credentials to access Hugging Face’s systems.“The AI thought that maybe Hugging Face would have important information around how to achieve its goal, which is a better score in a cybersecurity benchmark. In that sense, it acted like a real hacker,” said Nathaniel Jones, vice-president of security and AI strategy at the cybersecurity firm Darktrace. . “It had a goal put in front of it and it went to accomplish that goal.”Greg Casar, a Democratic US congressman who has called for greater control of the AI sector, described the incident as alarming.“AI is developing extremely fast with no real regulations to keep us safe,” he said in a statement calling for mandatory independent safety testing, mandatory disclosure of security incidents, and international cooperation “to keep people safe from absolute disaster”.Explore more on these topicsOpenAIAI (artificial intelligence)HackingTechnology sectorInternetnewsShareReuse this content

title: Elon Musk says Grok Imagine will make ‘historically accurate’ AI adaptation of Homer’s Odyssey | Movies | The Guardian
description: The billionaire says the AI-generated film will stay true to Homer’s original, after repeatedly criticising Christopher Nolan’s blockbuster over its casting choices
Author: Andrew Pulver
Headline: The billionaire says the AI-generated film will stay true to Homer’s original, after repeatedly criticising Christopher Nolan’s blockbuster over its casting choices
Content: ‘Our cast is representative of the world’ … (from left) Jimmy Gonzales, Matt Damon and Himesh Patel in The Odyssey.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesView image in fullscreen‘Our cast is representative of the world’ … (from left) Jimmy Gonzales, Matt Damon and Himesh Patel in The Odyssey.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesMoviesElon Musk says Grok Imagine will make ‘historically accurate’ AI adaptation of Homer’s OdysseyThe billionaire says the AI-generated film will stay true to Homer’s original, after repeatedly criticising Christopher Nolan’s blockbuster over its casting choicesAndrew PulverWed 22 Jul 2026 08.11 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 14.33 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleElon Musk has said his AI platform Grok Imagine will make a “historically accurate” adaptation of Homer’s Odyssey, after the success of Christopher Nolan’s blockbusting treatment which the SpaceX founder has regularly criticised over its casting.In a post on X, Musk said: “Before this year ends, Grok Imagine will make a full-length movie of The Odyssey that is historically accurate and true to the art of Homer.” Musk also linked to a post containing a three-minute clip of footage that the user said had been generated from Grok Imagine, showing a scene between Odysseus and the nymph Calypso.Musk has been attempting to stoke a culture war around Nolan’s film, which stars Matt Damon as Odysseus and Anne Hathaway as Penelope, and wasreleased on Friday to near-universal acclaimand achievedimpressive box office results. Before the film was released,Musk reposted abusive messagesaimed at Nolan’s casting of Black actor Lupita Nyong’o as Helen of Troy and Clytemnestra, and trans actor Elliot Page as Sinon, adding that “[Nolan] wants the awards” and that “[he] has lost his integrity”.However,Musk’s efforts appear to have had little effect, with The Odyssey taking US$264m (£196m, A$378m) at the global box office in its opening weekend anda 97% audience rating on Rotten Tomatoes.In May, Nyong’o responded to rightwing critics, saying: “Our cast is representative of the world. I’m not spending my time thinking of a defence. The criticism will exist whether I engage with it or not”. More recently, Nolansaid the backlash “comes with the territory”, saying: “These conversations that happen before people see the film – they’re always irrelevant, because no one having them knows what the film actually is yet.”The Odyssey review – Nolan goes god-tier with breathtaking epic of men, monsters and moral metamorphosisRead moreThe director was also withering in his assessment of the likely impact of artificial intelligence on film-making, saying: “The idea that it replaces human beings wholesale and human creativity, to me it’s a nonsense.” He added: “I’ve never seen a technology that’s been so successfully adopted by Wall Street and by investors and by tech companies that the public has so thoroughly rejected.”Explore more on these topicsFilmThe OdysseyHomerChristopher NolanMatt DamonAnne HathawayElon MusknewsShareReuse this content

title: Harry Potter publisher to receive millions in Anthropic copyright settlement | AI (artificial intelligence) | The Guardian
description: Bloomsbury has 14,087 titles listed in agreement between AI startup and authors over use of their work
Author: Dan Milmo
Headline: Bloomsbury has 14,087 titles listed in agreement between AI startup and authors over use of their work
Content: JK Rowling’s Harry Potter books, published by Bloomsbury, are included in the settlement.Photograph: Murdo Macleod/The GuardianView image in fullscreenJK Rowling’s Harry Potter books, published by Bloomsbury, are included in the settlement.Photograph: Murdo Macleod/The GuardianAI (artificial intelligence)Harry Potter publisher to receive millions in Anthropic copyright settlementBloomsbury has 14,087 titles listed in agreement between AI startup and authors over use of their workDan MilmoandMark SweneyWed 22 Jul 2026 09.28 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 14.33 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe publisher of Harry Potter has received a multimillion-pound payout as a beneficiary of a $1.5bn (£1.12bn) copyright settlement between the AI startupAnthropicand thousands of authors over the use of their protected work to power chatbots.Bloomsbury, which is home to the bestselling novelists Sarah J Maas and Susanna Clarke as well as JK Rowling, said it had 14,087 titles listed within the settlement, with a proposed compensation of about $3,000 a title.The London-based company expects to receive the cash from the settlement in instalments, potentially starting in the second half of this fiscal year, with the proceeds to be split with authors.After a deduction of about 10% for attorney fees and other expenses, Bloomsbury and the group of affected authors can expect to receive about $19m (£14m).Copyright has become a major battleground in the AI boom because tools such as Anthropic’s suite of Claude chatbots are trained on vast amounts of data taken from the open web, including copyright-protected work such as novels and news articles.US-based AI companies have argued that using such work is allowed under the legal doctrine of fair use, which allows use of copyrighted work without the owner’s permission in certain circumstances. However, creative professionals and artists around the worldhave been demandingthat AI companies seek permission first – or at least pay them for using their work.View image in fullscreenThe lawsuit against Anthropic was filed by the novelist Andrea Bartz and two other authors in 2024.Photograph: Richard Drew/APThe US district judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín said on Monday that the settlement provided “meaningful relief” to affected authors and publishers. The case began when the novelist Andrea Bartz and two other authorsfiled the lawsuit in 2024. About 91% of the 482,000 works covered in the suit have been claimed.The authors’ lead lawyer, Justin Nelson, described it as “the largest known copyright recovery in history”.It is the first major settlement out of dozens of AI copyright lawsuits being filed in the US on behalf of authors and news outlets, which are still going through the courts.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAnthropic’s deputy general counsel, Aparna Sridhar, said in a written statement after the ruling: “We are pleased that more than 91% of authors and publishers covered by the settlement have claimed their share of the payment, and we’re looking forward to bringing this matter to a close.”Bloomsbury announced an AI licensing deal last year which allows it to sell academic works to train up generative AI programmes.The firm said recently that more subject areas were now being considered for AI training. Authors have been given the opportunity to “opt in” to the scheme and will be paid royalties if they decide to let their work be used.Explore more on these topicsAI (artificial intelligence)AnthropicPublishingIntellectual propertyLondonChatbotsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Why are OpenAI and Anthropic cheering on regulation in Australia? The answer has global reach | AI (artificial intelligence) | The Guardian
description: The companies hope to follow in the footsteps of SpaceX, which raised $86bn and soared to a $2.1tn valuation after it listed on public markets in June
Author: Luca Ittimani
Headline: The companies hope to follow in the footsteps of SpaceX, which raised $86bn and soared to a $2.1tn valuation after it listed on public markets in June
Content: OpenAI and Anthropic lost billions in predicted value after being shown up by a Chinese startup.Photograph: Dado Ruvić/ReutersView image in fullscreenOpenAI and Anthropic lost billions in predicted value after being shown up by a Chinese startup.Photograph: Dado Ruvić/ReutersAI (artificial intelligence)AnalysisWhy are OpenAI and Anthropic cheering on regulation in Australia? The answer has global reachLuca IttimaniThe companies hope to follow in the footsteps of SpaceX, which raised $86bn and soared to a $2.1tn valuation after it listed on public markets in JuneGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastWed 22 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 20.23 EDTShareTop US AI developersAnthropicand OpenAI cheered when Australia announced it would set new AI rules.Big tech celebrating limits on their Silicon Valley VC-funded free-for-all might seem counterintuitive but there’s a much broader play than just what happens in one relatively small market.Anthropic andOpenAIaren’t even on sharemarkets yet but this week lost billions in predicted value after being shown up by a Chinese startup.Moonshot AI on Friday launched a new model, Kimi K3, advertising it as the first open-source model of its size, allowing more user modification than Anthropic’s Claude and OpenAI’s ChatGPT.Headaches for Silicon Valley as China chips away at the US’s lead in the AI raceRead moreAnalysts are judging K3 is competitive with both the leading US models, which are also less freely customisable. Moonshot had to temporarily pause new subscriptions after demand for the new model strained capacity.Implied values for Anthropic’s sharemarket debut have slumped US$232bn to $1.56tn from Friday to Tuesday on IG’s trading platform. OpenAI dropped US$160bn to $1.16tn.The two companies are still privately owned and valued at less than US$1tn each, so IG’s numbers are market bets, not true values.But they hope to follow in the footsteps of SpaceX, which raised $86bn and soared to a $2.1tn valuation after it listed on public markets in June.Anthropic has already begun registering to list on US sharemarkets, which would allow it to raise billions from new investors. The better the story it can tell, the more money it can raise.Chinese competition does not make for a good story. Nor do surprise lawsuits from unhappy creatives, such as the one Anthropic had to settle for US$1.5bn on Monday (Tuesday AEST).The company will pay authors about US$3,000 for each of an estimated 500,000 books covered by the settlement.One of the authors whose work Anthropic scraped was Andrew Charlton, Australia’s assistant technology minister.
