Charlton says the new regulation will mean Australian creatives can choose whether AI models are trained on their work and big tech companies will have to pay them.Yet those new rules could be a boon to big tech, Charlton says.“If we are upfront with what we expect in this Australian standard, we can give clarity to investors that in some ways enhances the attractiveness of Australia,” he told Sky News on Monday.Malik Ahmed Khan, an equity analyst at Morningstar Equity, says technology companies would find it much easier to invest in Australia as they learn where regulation is heading.View image in fullscreenAndrew Charlton says new Australian regulations will give clarity to investors.Photograph: Mick Tsikas/AAPThe companies will also have an easier job convincing investors to buy in at high prices if they can show they’re focusing on building AI and managing the risk of running afoul of governments or local laws, Khan says.While Australia is one of few countries to now be regulating AI directly, with little direct impact on companies based overseas, Anthony Albanese hopes to set the global tone. In his speech announcing the regulations, he cited Australia’s leadership on social media bans for under-16s as proof.Khan says more countries following Australia’s lead on AI could boost companies’ prospects and market values.“A positive deal in Australia could enable them to pursue similar deals across the globe,” he said.Avoiding a tech wreckRegulation would also give companies the prospect of favourable terms.Albanese signalled he would consult “closely” with industry and other countries to legislate regulations that would aim to enable AI’s arrival, not restrict it. OpenAI and Anthropic were quick to position themselves as partners ready to help shape the new approach.Dario Amodei, Anthropic’s chief executive and a former OpenAI vice-president, has already given an indication of what big tech thinks it should look like.In a June essay, he called for a coalition of democracies to dominate in AI and lock out China.While there are real national security concerns in this area, there’s also a healthy dose of self-interest.With the launch of Kimi’s K3, analysts believe it is increasingly likely that the US will block businesses from exclusively using Chinese-based models over national security fears.Man of his word: Pope Leo speeches declared human-authored by Australian AI detection toolRead moreAmodei also called for domestic regulation, asking governments to test, evaluate and even block AI models if they presented “unacceptable risks” – but with protections against local “political favoritism or arbitrary decisions”.Anthropic’s feud with the Trump administration has left it on a US supply chain blacklist. Days after Amodei published his essay, the US governmentforced Anthropic to suspend accessto its Mythos 5 models for foreign nationals.Anthropic’s critics have accused it of self-interest as it nears its initial public offering (IPO) on sharemarkets. The Pentagon’s Kirsten Davies said in June: “Some things are simply more important than revenue cycles, clickbait and pre-IPO valuation.”Yet Amodei’s warnings about security concerns have been echoed by Albanese and byFive Eyes intelligence agencies.Aruna Sathanapally, the CEO of Australia’s Grattan Institute, met the Anthropic chief when he visited Canberra in April. She said Amodei’s concerns seemed sincere.“He was seeing things within his own company that were giving him great concern about what Anthropic, as well as their competitors, may be capable of developing,” Sathanapally said.“Just because they have vested interests in the particular shape of regulation, it doesn’t mean that the calls for regulation themselves are not well founded. It just means … you need to keep alive to competitive neutrality in how you design it.”Explore more on these topicsAI (artificial intelligence)Australian politicsOpenAIAnthropicanalysisShareReuse this content

title: Trump administration says 15 agencies will get $5bn in ‘AI for science’ effort | Trump administration | The Guardian
description: Administration will also overhaul how US government funds federal research by supporting individual scientists and AI over universities
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Administration will also overhaul how US government funds federal research by supporting individual scientists and AI over universities
Content: Michael Kratsios, chief technology adviser to Donald Trump, in Lisbon on 7 November 2019.Photograph: Patrícia de Melo Moreira/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenMichael Kratsios, chief technology adviser to Donald Trump, in Lisbon on 7 November 2019.Photograph: Patrícia de Melo Moreira/AFP/Getty ImagesTrump administrationTrump administration says 15 agencies will get $5bn in ‘AI for science’ effortAdministration will also overhaul how US government funds federal research by supporting individual scientists and AI over universitiesReutersWed 22 Jul 2026 07.51 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 13.42 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe US will spend $5bn to tackle longstanding scientific problems across multiple fields usingAI, ⁠theTrump administrationsaid in a statement on Wednesday. The agencies will use the funding to identify the root causes ⁠of chronic diseases, accelerate drug ⁠discovery and ​develop longer-lasting building materials, among other tasks, according to the statement.Scientists will have access to the Department of Energy’s supercomputers, AI and specialized ⁠datasets, along with other components needed to run experiments using algorithms, said Michael Kratsios, the chief technology adviser toDonald Trump, in an ⁠interview with Reuters.Fifteen federal agencies will be involved in the project, including the Departments ​of Health and Human Services, Energy, Transportation, ‌Defense and Interior.“The White ‌House is helping bring together all of the different agencies that work on different ‌domains to make them part of this larger effort on AI for science,” Kratsios said.OpenAI ‘in early talks to give 5% stake to US government’Read moreTheTrump administrationalso plans to overhaul how the US government funds federal research by supporting more individual scientists and the use of AI, rather than universities, according to a White House report released on Tuesday night, authored by Kratsios. This would ‌give the Trump administration more control over how the money is used.“Federal support for science must be politically accountable,” the report said. During his ​second term, Trump has tried to exert more control over how federal research dollars are allocated. Federal courts have pushed back, with an appeals panel saying in January the Trump administration cannot cut federal grant funding provided by the National Institutes of Health to ⁠universities engaged in scientific and medical research.AI models need data to identify ​patterns, generate predictions and automate ​decisions. The US government is ​home to some of the largest datasets in the world, ​including records about chemicals, ‌critical minerals and ​patient health.The $5bn ​research initiative is an opportunity to train AI models on that information and use those algorithms to answer scientific questions, Kratsios said.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toTechScapeA weekly dive in to how technology is shaping our livesPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionMicrosoftwill donate $40m in AI computing credits over three years to support the effort, according to a company statement.Explore more on these topicsTrump administrationAI (artificial intelligence)ComputingDonald TrumpMicrosoftnewsShareReuse this content

title: OpenAI’s rogue agents are a wake-up call to risks posed by artificial intelligence | Shakeel Hashim | The Guardian
description: Hacking of Hugging Face shows we do not seem to have reliable ways to curb extremely powerful AI systems
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Hacking of Hugging Face shows we do not seem to have reliable ways to curb extremely powerful AI systems
Content: Open AI's logo at the Mobile World Congress (MWC) in Barcelona on 26 February 2024.Photograph: Pau Barrena/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenOpen AI's logo at the Mobile World Congress (MWC) in Barcelona on 26 February 2024.Photograph: Pau Barrena/AFP/Getty ImagesAI (artificial intelligence)OpenAI’s rogue agents are a wake-up call to risks posed by artificial intelligenceShakeel HashimHacking of Hugging Face shows we do not seem to have reliable ways to curb extremely powerful AI systemsWed 22 Jul 2026 16.40 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 17.12 EDTShareLast week Hugging Face – a company that hosts artificial intelligence models and datasets – washacked.After it reported the incident to law enforcement, few would have predicted what came next: the culprits wererevealedto be AI agents from OpenAI, which had broken out of containment and were acting of their own accord.The incident sounds like sci-fi: AI escaping and autonomously hacking its way into companies. But it is all too real – and about as terrifying as it sounds. It is a concrete demonstration of something we can no longer avoid confronting: AI systems have become extremely powerful and we do not seem to havereliable waysof curbing their behavior.OpenAIhad beenevaluating the capabilities of two of its models in the test that led to the breach – including one not yet publicly available. The models, which were both running in a supposedly secure environment without internet access, were asked to solve a hacking challenge. Rather than actually solve it themselves, however, they decided it would be easier to cheat. They used their advanced capabilities to break out of their secure environment, access the web and then hack into Hugging Face’s systems to steal the answers. They worked at this for a full weekend – seemingly without anyone at OpenAI noticing.Though the models were running with some of their guardrails disabled, they still acted well out of the bounds that were in place. According toOpenAI, they were not instructed to break out of their sandbox or hack into another company, and it’s safe to assume that no one at OpenAIwantedthem to do so.Nor were the models acting maliciously: they were not evil Terminators with a goal of wreaking havoc. Instead, the scenario is almost chilling in its banality. The models were given a very narrow task, but went rogue to pursue an undesirable and unacceptable way of achieving it – one which had real-world consequences.AI safety researchers have warned about this type of incentive problem for years. Philosopher Nick Bostrom popularized it back in 2003 with his “paperclip maximizer” thought experiment: an advanced artificial intelligence, given the goal of manufacturing paperclips, might go to great lengths to do so. It might hack into the power grid and factories to redirect them into making paperclips. Ultimately, the machine – ruthlessly pursuing its given goal – decides to kill all humans, repurposing our atoms to make more paperclips. The goal does not have to be sinister to lead to disaster, in other words. A trivial one, pursued single-mindedly enough, will do.In the OpenAI-Hugging Face scenario, little harm was done. Hugging Face had to spend time addressing the incident, but no particularly sensitive data appears to have been stolen. It is not hard, however, to imagine the situation ending up much worse: a rogue AI agent accidentally breaking some critical piece of web infrastructure, or stealing money from someone. Thenightmare scenariofor many AI researchers is a model “exfiltrating” itself – copying itself on to serversitcontrols, so that it can’t be shut down even if its bad behavior is eventually caught.This week’s incident should serve as a wake-up call, forcing us to ask an uncomfortable question: should we really be building dangerous systems that we can’t control?skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toTechScapeA weekly dive in to how technology is shaping our livesPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionShakeel Hashim is the editor ofTransformer, a publication about the power and politics of transformative AIExplore more on these topicsAI (artificial intelligence)OpenAIcommentShareReuse this content

title: How Pokémon Go encouraged me to find wonder in the world | Games | The Guardian
description: These days, I ‘collect’ birdsong and wild flowers, but it all started when my sons and I first set out to find Pikachu and pals• Don’t get Pushing Buttons delivered to your inbox? Sign up here
Author: Keith Stuart
Headline: These days, I ‘collect’ birdsong and wild flowers, but it all started when my sons and I first set out to find Pikachu and pals• Don’t get Pushing Buttons delivered to your inbox? Sign up here
Content: Surely we’ve caught them all? … Pokémon Go celebrates its tenth birthday.Photograph: Eduardo Muñoz/ReutersView image in fullscreenSurely we’ve caught them all? … Pokémon Go celebrates its tenth birthday.Photograph: Eduardo Muñoz/ReutersPushing Buttons newsletterGamesHow Pokémon Go encouraged me to find wonder in the worldThese days, I ‘collect’ birdsong and wild flowers, but it all started when my sons and I first set out to find Pikachu and palsDon’t get Pushing Buttons delivered to your inbox? Sign up hereKeith StuartWed 22 Jul 2026 10.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 10.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleBelieve it or not, Pokémon Go is10 years old this month. The wildly successful smartphone game, which allows you to track down lovable creatures in the real world using GPS and augmented-reality technologies, has reportedly been downloaded 800m times across 150 countries and regions. A trillion Pokémon have been caught so far – and I have been responsible for a modest percentage of that figure.When the game was first released, my sons and I spent many happy hours wandering the streets and parks of Frome searching for Jigglypuffs and Charizards, and the game’s social element, which lets you take on gym battles with other players in your vicinity, offered my autistic son a route to communicate with other children in a way he’d never been able to before.My boys have grown up now, but I enjoy the fact that the game is still seducing new players into searching their local shopping centre for Machops. There’s a part of me that never really let go of this compulsion, although it’s now evolved. I have a folder of apps on my phone that I have ready whenever I go out for a walk, or visit an unfamiliar town. Seek isa free appthat uses image recognition to help you identify plants, trees and animals, and it lets you collect species badges to celebrate new discoveries. The accuracy can be a little variable, but it has given me a new interest in wild flowers and trees, and, being a nerd, I like to imagine which particular species would win in a Pokémon-style battle. How would red valerian fare against field bindweed? What would be the special attack of the common dog violet?Another favourite is theMerlin Bird IDapp, developed by the Cornell Lab of Ornithology. It lets you identify birdsong wherever you are in the world, and provides a “bird of the day” to look out for. My wife and I use it competitively, sending each other screenshots of unusual birds we’ve heard on our travels. I can now fairly confidently identify at least 10 bird songs, and I have to admit, I do associate certain birds with specific Pokémon. Pikachu? The yellow hammer, of course. Corviknight is the raven. Pidgeots are self-explanatory.View image in fullscreenThe new Pikachu … the Merlin Bird ID app lets you identify birdsong, like that of the yellow hammer.Photograph: Ernie Janes/AlamyAlongside these, I also loveFlightradar24andMarineTraffic, which track airplane and ship movement respectively. I like to know where things are going and why, and to play Top Trumps with the vehicle data, pitting nearby cargo ships against each other in imagined naval battles based on their size and carrying capacity.I’m not sure I’d be using any of these apps if its wasn’t forPokémon Go. It opened up in me an understanding of the natural world as a vast playable space, filled with beautiful little signs and systems. When you explore a new game world, everything you encounter is potentially interesting. You have no filter of expectation – in terms of executive function, it’s like being a toddler again. As you get older, you lose that fascination with the minutiae of the world around you – you go for dramatic vistas, rather than the patches of weeds along roadsides; the dawn chorus over the solitary songbird in your garden. Sometimes you need a little prod to bring that back.During the summer of 2016, and for a few seasons after, every walk we took became an adventure. Mundane places we knew well suddenly bristled with possibilities, boring landmarks became battlegrounds, strangers became allies. I just took all that and applied it to the real things around us – the birds in the woods near where we live, the weeds in our overgrown garden, the planes overhead. I’m not alone in this. Inspired by Pokémon Go, Brisbane student Kris Norris has created a social discovery game based onleaving messages on payphonesaround the city. Hydrologists in Colorado used a Pokémon Go-style app todiscover and track small streams.I’ve also discovered other location-based experiences along the way, that I might not have thought about otherwise. The fitness gameZombies, Run!was better for me as a running tool than Couch to 5k;Pikmin Bloomis a delightful floral walking game, andGeocaching, where you track down real-life caches hidden by other players, has tricked me into a few long hikes.What I have learned, or at least had confirmed, since playing Pokémon Go is that all video games have the potential to open new intellectual doorways – new ways to envisage the world and how it works. No time spent in play is wasted. Wonder is a transferable skill.What to playView image in fullscreenPerfect for a cosy evening … The Mermaid Mask game.Photograph: SFB GamesMystery games are having a real moment, but brothers Tom and Adam Vian have been quietly perfecting their Detective Grimoire series for more than a decade. I’ve eagerly awaitedThe Mermaid Mask, in which the titular detective and his smarter sarcastic sidekick Sally try to solve a locked-room murder aboard a submarine, and it does not disappoint.I always recommend playing these games with a loved one – not because they’re difficult (the puzzles are about as taxing as you’d want for a cosy evening) but because the fully voice-acted, laugh-out-loud script makes them just as fun to watch (and backseat drive) as to actually play.Jordan Erica WebberAvailable on:PC, Switch/Switch 2, PS5Estimated playtime:10 hours, best enjoyed over a few eveningsskip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toPushing ButtonsKeza MacDonald's weekly look at the world of gamingPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionWhat to readView image in fullscreenAll the gear … Modern Warfare is the universe of choice for Paramount’s new Call of Duty film.Photograph: Activision BlizzardLast year, Paramount signed up to make aCall of Dutymovie. Now,the studio has confirmedthat it will take place in the Modern Warfare universe – which means contemporary weapons, hi-tech espionage and geopolitical slaughter. But who will play moustached SAS hero Captain Price? There are rumours Pedro Pascal is up for the role, but surely it’s got to be Henry Cavill?!So now it’s all over,it turns outEA Sports FC correctly predicted theWorld Cupwinner for the fifth year in a row. The development team ran a simulation of every group and knock-out match in the competition, where Spain emerged as the victor. I wonder if it also predicted thatEngland v Francewould be the most video game-like match of the tournament?On Saturday, GamesRadarran a feature interviewwith senior figures at Xbox, who confirmed the importance ofexclusive gamesto the console. “There has to be a reason for people to buy an Xbox,” said chief content officer Matt Booty, accurately. Gears of War: E-Day and Clockwork Revolution will be the first titles to be affected by the withdrawal from the multiplatform approach that saw titles such as Forza Horizon 6 and Sea of Thieves head to PS5.What to clickBub – living an artist’s dreams and dreads in New YorkApple Crumble: a tasty Knives Out-inspired mystery that lets you snoop on your own familyCastlevania: Belmont’s Curse – Konami classic rises again from Paris sewers and Joan of Arc is a bossD&D players raise millions in real-life campaign against ‘corporate elite’Denshattack! – time to get on board with kickflipping trains | ★★★★☆Splatoon Raiders – Nintendo’s inky shooter fades to grey | ★★☆☆☆Question BlockView image in fullscreenOne of life’s great teachers … There’s much to be learned from games like Super Mario Odyssey.Photograph: NintendoThis week, a philosophical question fromRichard:“What genuine life lessons have you learned from games? I often think of Verso’s advice to Lune inClair Obscur: Expedition 33, thatthe only voice that should be in your head is your own. Bonus hard mode: not just life lessons from dialogue or other ‘narrative’ parts of a game, but from thegamingaction itself. The hours I spent trying to beat the last level ofSuper Mario Odysseytaught me a lot about my flaws: panicking in the face of new situations, and stumbling over bits I had done umpteen times beforebecause I contemptuously thought I’d mastered them.”One lesson I took from platform games such as Impossible Mission and Jet Set Willy and later from stealth games, was to stop and observe any complex situation before leaping in. That has saved me a lot of trouble. I’ve also picked up little snippets of practical information that I’ll never get to use in real-life – I discovered the Scandinavian flick playing World Rally Championship (it’s a driving technique, not a haircut) and the Coriolis effect while playing the famed assassination mission inCall of Duty 4: Modern Warfare. Finally, as a novelist, I learned plenty of things about comedy writing fromThe Secret of Monkey Island– specifically the importance of understatement and how to set up a gag and then keep it running. The line “That’s the second-biggest monkey head I’ve ever seen” will always stick in my head in relation to both of those skills. I should have it crocheted and framed on my wall.If you have a question for Question Block – or anything else to say about the newsletter –email us atpushingbuttons@theguardian.com.Explore more on these topicsGamesPushing Buttons newsletterPokémon GofeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Red Kiss – guiding vampires through a blood-soaked cold war Berlin | Games | The Guardian
description: Inspired by interactive theatre and fuelled by the motion capture tech used by VTubers, the vampire spies in Red Kiss have personality in spades
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Inspired by interactive theatre and fuelled by the motion capture tech used by VTubers, the vampire spies in Red Kiss have personality in spades
Content: The beating (undead) heart… Red Kiss.Photograph: Fellow TravellerView image in fullscreenThe beating (undead) heart… Red Kiss.Photograph: Fellow TravellerSummer games previewsGamesRed Kiss – guiding vampires through a blood-soaked cold war BerlinInspired by interactive theatre and fuelled by the motion capture tech used by VTubers, the vampire spies in Red Kiss have personality in spadesDiego ArgüelloWed 22 Jul 2026 09.20 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google‘You look at the news and you’re like, ‘Another pointless war, another pointless bloodshed, another pointless genocide.’ And that can make you a bit hopeless.”Roy van der Schilden, narrative designer and business director at Wispfire, speaks of a very human feeling. But the protagonist of its upcoming game Red Kiss can relate – despite being a vampire.You play as an (initially human) inhabitant of cold war Berlin who has tried to no avail to endure the conflict outside the window: through partying, journalling, activism … Still, you feel empty. When offered a new life of power and purpose, you don’t need much convincing to offer up your exposed neck.“I think a lot of people who create vampire games feel the anguish and the melancholy of the genre,” says Van der Schilden. “Like, how to deal with your feeling of justice in an unjust world.”View image in fullscreenThe best fangs for the job … Red Kiss.Photograph: Fellow TravellerIn this tactical narrative RPG, you plunge into a fictional 80s Berlin by dispatching vampire agents to its fractured streets. They’re your eyes and ears, looking for clues, building reputation with city factions, and of course quenching their bloodthirst. As in last year’s superhero caper Dispatch (from AdHoc Studio), you’ll come across opportunities around the map, each with its own requirements, making use of a skillcheck system tied to six character skills. It’s up to you to pick the best fangs for the job, all while maintaining their energy, money and blood supply.The vampires under your command are the beating (undead) heart of the experience. You progressively unveil their backstories by undertaking personal missions, during which you learn about their individual pasts and struggles. It’s in these moments that you begin to enact change and share some of your newfound purpose with others as guidance, deciding how their stories are ultimately resolved.Wispfire emphasises the importance of the cast by infusing hand-drawn portraits with voice acting and motion-capture (mocap) animation. Notably, for the latter the team is using Live2D, a relatively inexpensive software tool commonly used by VTubers to animate their virtual avatars during a livestream.A good iPhone was enough to start experimenting, but the team had to develop tools to adapt the software to suit their needs, and the tech isn’t without limitations. “With a mocap character for 3D, you usually can move your entire head,” says art director and animator Aïda de Ridder. “There’s something stuck to your face and it moves with you, right? We have a static phone, so we have to make their performance more centred. There is a limit to the range of movements.”In order to make models feel “animated”, the team found that a performer needs to exaggerate their facial features to convey the desired result. But Wispfire is well acquainted with this, thanks to a shared background in interactive theatre where, as Van der Schilden says, “It’s harder to see for people in the back if you do a muted performance.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toPushing ButtonsKeza MacDonald's weekly look at the world of gamingPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionVampires shine the most when they have strong personalities and distinct looks. In Red Kiss, many elements intersect with one another to give characters power and purpose. And this is all being funnelled through a queer and dark lens – like the best vampire stories are.“We like queer media, “says Van der Schilden. “But we, as a team, always felt that a lot of the media was very pink rainbows, happy-go-lucky. We wanted to make a really dark, queer, diverse game. And I think Red Kiss is that.”Red Kiss is currently in development forPC, with a release date to be announced.Explore more on these topicsGamesSummer games previewsPCfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: OpenAI’s rogue agents are a wake-up call to risks posed by artificial intelligence | Shakeel Hashim | The Guardian
description: Hacking of Hugging Face shows we do not seem to have reliable ways to curb extremely powerful AI systems
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Hacking of Hugging Face shows we do not seem to have reliable ways to curb extremely powerful AI systems
Content: Open AI's logo at the Mobile World Congress (MWC) in Barcelona on 26 February 2024.Photograph: Pau Barrena/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenOpen AI's logo at the Mobile World Congress (MWC) in Barcelona on 26 February 2024.Photograph: Pau Barrena/AFP/Getty ImagesAI (artificial intelligence)OpenAI’s rogue agents are a wake-up call to risks posed by artificial intelligenceShakeel HashimHacking of Hugging Face shows we do not seem to have reliable ways to curb extremely powerful AI systemsWed 22 Jul 2026 16.40 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 17.12 EDTShareLast week Hugging Face – a company that hosts artificial intelligence models and datasets – washacked.After it reported the incident to law enforcement, few would have predicted what came next: the culprits wererevealedto be AI agents from OpenAI, which had broken out of containment and were acting of their own accord.The incident sounds like sci-fi: AI escaping and autonomously hacking its way into companies. But it is all too real – and about as terrifying as it sounds. It is a concrete demonstration of something we can no longer avoid confronting: AI systems have become extremely powerful and we do not seem to havereliable waysof curbing their behavior.OpenAIhad beenevaluating the capabilities of two of its models in the test that led to the breach – including one not yet publicly available. The models, which were both running in a supposedly secure environment without internet access, were asked to solve a hacking challenge. Rather than actually solve it themselves, however, they decided it would be easier to cheat. They used their advanced capabilities to break out of their secure environment, access the web and then hack into Hugging Face’s systems to steal the answers. They worked at this for a full weekend – seemingly without anyone at OpenAI noticing.Though the models were running with some of their guardrails disabled, they still acted well out of the bounds that were in place. According toOpenAI, they were not instructed to break out of their sandbox or hack into another company, and it’s safe to assume that no one at OpenAIwantedthem to do so.Nor were the models acting maliciously: they were not evil Terminators with a goal of wreaking havoc. Instead, the scenario is almost chilling in its banality. The models were given a very narrow task, but went rogue to pursue an undesirable and unacceptable way of achieving it – one which had real-world consequences.AI safety researchers have warned about this type of incentive problem for years. Philosopher Nick Bostrom popularized it back in 2003 with his “paperclip maximizer” thought experiment: an advanced artificial intelligence, given the goal of manufacturing paperclips, might go to great lengths to do so. It might hack into the power grid and factories to redirect them into making paperclips. Ultimately, the machine – ruthlessly pursuing its given goal – decides to kill all humans, repurposing our atoms to make more paperclips. The goal does not have to be sinister to lead to disaster, in other words. A trivial one, pursued single-mindedly enough, will do.In the OpenAI-Hugging Face scenario, little harm was done. Hugging Face had to spend time addressing the incident, but no particularly sensitive data appears to have been stolen. It is not hard, however, to imagine the situation ending up much worse: a rogue AI agent accidentally breaking some critical piece of web infrastructure, or stealing money from someone. Thenightmare scenariofor many AI researchers is a model “exfiltrating” itself – copying itself on to serversitcontrols, so that it can’t be shut down even if its bad behavior is eventually caught.This week’s incident should serve as a wake-up call, forcing us to ask an uncomfortable question: should we really be building dangerous systems that we can’t control?skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toTechScapeA weekly dive in to how technology is shaping our livesPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionShakeel Hashim is the editor ofTransformer, a publication about the power and politics of transformative AIExplore more on these topicsAI (artificial intelligence)OpenAIcommentShareReuse this content

title: Democrats need to hammer Trump on his unprecedented corruption | Steven Greenhouse | The Guardian
description: To win in this year’s midterm elections, the party must highlight that Trump has made huge financial gains while millions struggle to make ends meet
Author: Steven Greenhouse
Headline: To win in this year’s midterm elections, the party must highlight that Trump has made huge financial gains while millions struggle to make ends meet
Content: ‘Democrats should repeatedly hammer Trump for his greed, corruption and conflicts of interest the same way that Hungary’s opposition repeatedly attacked Viktor Orbán.’Photograph: Mark Schiefelbein/APView image in fullscreen‘Democrats should repeatedly hammer Trump for his greed, corruption and conflicts of interest the same way that Hungary’s opposition repeatedly attacked Viktor Orbán.’Photograph: Mark Schiefelbein/APOpinionDonald TrumpDemocrats need to hammer Trump on his unprecedented corruptionSteven GreenhouseTo win in this year’s midterm elections, the party must highlight that Trump has made huge financial gains while millions struggle to make ends meetWed 22 Jul 2026 10.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 16.35 EDTShareIf the Democrats hope to maximize their chances of winning in this November’s elections, there’s a subject they need to scream far and wide about: Donald Trump’s immense and unprecedented corruption. The Democrats need to make clear to every American that Trump has shamelessly used his second term to increase his wealth by an astounding amount – bymore than $2bn– all while millions of Americans are struggling to make ends meet, their struggles made worse by Trump policies that havepushed up inflationandgas prices.Every day through election day, the Democrats should convey loud and clear that America’s billionaire president has focused far more on making himself richer than on improving the lives of average Americans. Trump’s corruption and conflicts of interest are so vast and outrageous that many Americans have little idea how vast and outrageous they are: ranging from Trump’s making more than$1.4bn from cryptolast year to granting pardons to criminalswhose familieshave donatedto Trump’s campaign to his sons’ investing in startup companies thatreceive generous helpfrom the Trump administration.Democrats should repeatedly hammer Trump for his greed, corruption and conflicts of interest the same way that Hungary’s opposition repeatedly attacked Viktor Orbán for his immense corruption – a strategy that caused Orbán’s popularity to plummet and caused Hungarian voters to oust their country’s authoritarian leader last April. Peter Magyar, Orbán’s challenger,defeated Orban by focusing on three issuesthe Democrats would be smart to focus on: the cost of living, the lack of economic opportunities, and the fact that their country’s leader and his family grew ever richer even as many people grew poorer.Trump’s corruption and continuous push for self-enrichment are by far the worst of any president in US history. “It is completely unprecedented,” Megan Gorman, author of All the Presidents’ Money, a book on the history of presidential wealth since 1776, told the New York Times. Gorman said Trump’s focus on enriching himself is “a betrayal of the American social contract: that those who lead the country prioritize country over self”. Norm Eisen, the White House ethics czar under Barack Obama, told the Washington Post: “It’s corruption on a scalethat, to be honest, has few rivals in world history.”Before Trump won a second term, the Teapot Dome was considered the worst presidential financial scandal in US history. Every US high school student studies that ugly scandal, which occurred under Warren Harding (president from 1921 to 1923). But Teapot Dome is peanuts compared with Trump’s cornucopia of scandals. Harding wasn’t even involved in Teapot Dome, nor did he profit from it. It instead involved his secretary of the interior, Albert B Fall, who took $400,000 in bribes and gifts to lease government-owned oil reserves. In today’s dollars, those bribes and gifts would be worth about $8m – that’s chump change compared with Trump’s corruption. By enriching himself by$2.2bn last year, according to White House financial disclosures, Trump has in effect jacked up his wealth by about $8m every two days since returning to the White House.Trump’s corruption and conflicts have taken many forms. After originallydenouncing cryptocurrencies as “a scam”, Trump, in a stunning about-face,worked with his sonsto create World Liberty Financial, a crypto company that enriched him by$799m last year. Trump and his sons enjoyed a huge windfall when a member of the United Arab Emirates’ royal familypaid $500m for a secret 49% stakein their company. Not long after, Trump agreed to do something the UAE badly wanted; helet the UAEbuy leading-edge Nvidia AI chips, a move thatsome national security officials opposedbecause they feared that China would ultimately get many of those chips.Trump cashed in big-time in another way – he made an astounding $636m last year by selling $Trump memecoins, a speculative cryptocurrency with little practical value. Trump urged his fans to buy those coins, writing: “It’s time to celebrate everything we stand for: WINNING … GET YOUR $TRUMP NOW.” Nearly a million people, many of them Maga loyalists,lost a total of $3.8bnfrom investing in $TRUMP coins, whichhave lost 97% of their valuesince their peak.At the same time Trump has won big in crypto, his administrationreduced regulations on crypto,disbanded its crypto enforcement unit, and terminated somelegal casesallegingcrypto fraud.The US constitution’s emolument clause prohibits presidents from accepting gifts from foreign states, but in a blatant violation of that clause, Trumpaccepted a retired $400m luxury Boeing 747from Qatar’s royal family. Trump is already using it, and after his second term ends, it will go to Trump’s presidential library, and presumably his personal use. Trump has called the Qatari gift “the world’s most luxurious plane”, while Senator Chris Murphy of Connecticut called the gift “the definition of corruption”.Trump has turned presidential pardons of criminals into an ethically dubious pay-to-play game. Hepardoned Changpeng Zhao, the billionaire founder of the world’s largest crypto exchange, after Zhao had quietly helped boost the value of theTrump family’s crypto company by nearly $2bn. Zhao had spent four months in prison after pleading guilty to money laundering.Trump alsopardoned Trevor Milton, founder of the Nikola electric vehicle company – he had beensentenced to four yearsin prison for defrauding investors. Before Trump granted that pardon, Milton and his wife haddonated more than $1.8m to Trump’s campaign. Similarly, Trumppardoned Paul Walczak, a nursing home executive who pleaded guilty to stealing more than $10m in employee payroll taxes. That pardon came after Walczak’s mother attended a $1m Trump fundraiser.The White House press secretary, Karoline Leavitt, has vigorously defended Trump against allegations of corruption, saying: “Neither the president nor his familyhas ever engaged, or will ever engage, in conflicts of interest.” Anna Kelly, a White House spokesperson, added that Trump “only acts in the best interests of the American public”.Trump’s repeated use of no-bid contracts also raises big ethical questions. Trump awarded asecret $500m no-bid contractto build the East Wing ballroom. His administration also awarded a$1.7m no-bid contractto a business owned by Trump’s longtime ally John Cafaro, who had given more than $300,000 to political committees tied to Trump. That no-bid contract was for installing a water-purification system for the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool, which had a huge algae bloom after the renovations Trump ordered. (In 2001, Cafaropleaded guilty to conspiracy to bribinga congressman.)Trump’s sons are also cashing in on Trump’s power and presidency. After Trump helped persuade Kazakhstan’s leader to give a little-known US company access to the world’s largest untapped reserve of tungsten, a metal crucial for making missile warheads and AI chips, Trump’s sons Don Jr and Erictook an ownership stake in the projectdays before the deal was announced. The Export-Import Bank and a second federal agency have pledged to lend $1.6bn to the little-known US company that got access to the mine, which by one estimate holds up to$80bn worth of tungsten.Not stopping there, Don Jr and Eric haveinvested in a drone manufacturerthat is seeking to sell drones to Gulf states that Iran has attacked and that rely on the Trump administration for military aid. In a move that Senator Elizabeth Warren criticized as “corruption in plain sight”, the Pentagonawarded a $24m contractto a startup company whose chief strategy officer is Eric Trump to test an android for the marines. Warren said: “Is the Pentagonjust a cash machine for Trump’s kids now?” The Washington Post found that Don Jr and Eric haveinvested in more than a dozen defense-related startupsand that those companies have generated at least $3.2bn in direct government business since Trump’s sons made those investments.Unlike previous presidents, Trump has refused to set up a blind trust for his holdings. Thathas raised serious concernsbecause Trump’s investment accounts mademore than 21,000 trades last year, with many appearing to be timed to take maximum advantage of Trump’s public statements that moved the stock markets. (The White House defends the trades and says Trump’s investment accounts are managed by professional advisers who do not regularly communicate with him.)On 23 July, the day before Trump issued a plan that largely deregulated AI, his investment accountsbought up to $5m in stock eachin numerous AI companies, including Broadcom, Meta, Amazon, Apple, Microsoft and Nvidia (he also purchased stocks unrelated to AI that day).Trump’s corruption leaves us cynical – and complacent | Judith LevineRead moreIn an unprecedented deal for a president – onewidely condemnedascorrupt self-dealing– Trump got his justice department to promise that theIRS would drop any existing auditsof Trump, his family and his business while also dropping efforts to pursue back taxes from him even though, by some estimates, Trump owesmore than $100m in back taxes. Last week, a federal judgedenounced that dealas improper self-dealing.The list goes on and on. Indeed I could detail many more scandals and conflicts, including the Trump family’s pursuit ofbranding deals in Saudi Arabiaand a South Korean aluminum companypaying $2m to Trump’s holding companyas it faced a trade investigation.If any previous president had done one-tenth of these things, the public would be up in arms. Meanwhile, Republicans in Congress have inexcusably turned a blind eye to Trump’s corruption, giving him a pass on his unprecedented self-enrichment and self-dealing. And while Trump has enriched himself handsomely, he has enthusiastically backed legislation thatcut Medicaidandfood stampsto millions of struggling Americans, while also forcing up healthcare premiums for millions by blocking an extension of Obamacare subsidies.Importantly, the US public is growing increasingly upset with Trump’s corrupt shenanigans. AnEconomist/YouGov pollthis week found that 49% of voters said they would use the word “corrupt” to describe Trump, while 19% wouldn’t describe him that way.Voters should remember all this corruption and unethical self-enrichment when they vote in November – andDemocratsneed to do their best to make sure that voters don’t forget.Steven Greenhouse is a journalist and author, focusing on labor and the workplace, as well as economic and legal issuesExplore more on these topicsDonald TrumpOpinionDemocratsUS politicsUS midterm elections 2026commentShareReuse this content

title: Three speeches on a single day signaled a dying American democracy | Robert B Shpiner | The Guardian
description: Stephen Miller warned of a leftwing ‘cancer’ threatening civilization. As a critical care doctor, I have a response to his diagnosis
Author: Robert B Shpiner
Headline: Stephen Miller warned of a leftwing ‘cancer’ threatening civilization. As a critical care doctor, I have a response to his diagnosis
Content: ‘This notice is filed in the knowledge that pronouncements can be premature. It is filed in the fear that this one is not.’Photograph: Jonathan Ernst/ReutersView image in fullscreen‘This notice is filed in the knowledge that pronouncements can be premature. It is filed in the fear that this one is not.’Photograph: Jonathan Ernst/ReutersOpinionTrump administrationThree speeches on a single day signaled a dying American democracyRobert B ShpinerStephen Miller warned of a leftwing ‘cancer’ threatening civilization. As a critical care doctor, I have a response to his diagnosisWed 22 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 12.41 EDTShareThe presumption that US elections are legitimate died last week in Washington. It was 237 years old. It did not die of one wound. It died of three, inflicted in a single day, in daylight, on the record. And the country, running to the loudest of them, never examined the one most likely to kill.In critical care we distinguish the mechanism of death from its cause. The mechanism was a Thursday. The cause was chronic, progressive and untreated.The morning belonged to the secretary of state. Before officials of more than 60 countries, he announced that the counterterrorism architecture of the free world, built over a generation against jihadist mass murder, should be rebuilt and aimed at the domestic left. He named a transnational enemy defined by hatred of the west, “a revolt of the worst against the best”. He tied it to Iran and to Cuba and offered no evidence for either link. He said the journalists and thinktanks who might check the claims often agree with the enemy’s goals. The move is old and reliable: discredit the second opinion in advance.Midday belonged to the White House deputy chief of staff.What he saiddeserves to be quoted rather than summarized, because summary is a mercy it has not earned. Hecalled“leftwing political terrorism” a “fatal cancer of civilization”. He said that its politics, left to run its course, “always becomes a gulag”. He said antifa demonstrators are “all deformed in some way, in their appearance, in their dress, in their mannerism”, that “their outer appearance becomes a manifestation of their inner hatred”, and he asked why there is “not one normal-looking person among them”. He said that when the leftist protests that his rights are being violated, those appeals “must fall on deaf ears”. He urged the assembled governments to be “completely unflinching in the pursuit of justice against these enemies of civilization”. And he described the work already under way in six verbs: “disrupt, identify, defund, debank, arrest and prosecute”.I have been practicing medicine for four decades. Cancer is my vocabulary, not his. And I know what happened the last time a government borrowed it. The Nazi state’s central perversion was the medicalization of killing: the enemy as tumor, as bacillus, the nation as patient, extermination as cure. Robert Jay Lifton called it the biomedical vision. It recruited my profession first, and my profession answered willingly. When a government describes citizens as a cancer, it has written the first line of a treatment plan. On Thursday, the plan was read aloud, from a state department podium, in six verbs.One disclosure belongs in the chart. My family left Russia in 1917. My relatives left Germany in 1933. In both generations, leaving was called excessive; staying was the reasonable position. But an alarm that was right both times it sounded is not overcaution. On Thursday, it sounded a third time.The evening belonged to the president. In primetime hecalled the American election system indefensible, claimed a foreign powerheld 220 million voter files,declared mail votinginherently corrupt,accused his own intelligence servicesof concealment, and told Congress that the only reason to oppose his bill is the intent to cheat. That framework converts every future outcome into evidence. Pass the bill and win: the bill worked. Fail and lose: the cheaters blocked it. A presumption of legitimacy cannot survive inside a belief system engineered to be unfalsifiable.Trump is invoking foreign election interference to justify his own | Jamil SmithRead moreIt was just a Thursday. Netflix’s forecast disappointed Wall Street. A rocket aborted on the pad. No sirens sounded, because this is not how they sound. Democracies do not die to soundtracks. They die on ordinary weekdays, between the earnings reports, while the country is at work.Then came the response, and the response was a triage error. Nearly all of it ran to the evening. The factcheckers parsed the voter files. The law professors graded the bombshells. Real work, spent on the loudest injury. The morning’s remarks, the ones that declared a class of Americans a cancer and read enmity on their bodies, drew the attention of an ordinary news cycle: straight coverage in the mainstream, horror at the margins, and in a portion of the press, open applause. I have watched teams run to the dramatic patient while the quiet one, the one not crying out, exsanguinates behind a curtain. The most dangerous words spoken in Washington on Thursday were not spoken in primetime. They were spoken before lunch, to more than 60 countries. The official transcript records the applause.The decedent is survived by the federal courts, still open. By 50 secretaries of state, still certifying. By a general election, still on the calendar, 108 days out. And by an electorate that has not yet been asked the only question that matters, which is whether it will accept a count its president has pre-declared corrupt, counted among citizens its government has pre-declared malignant.In 40 years of intensive care, I have pronounced death many times. The criteria are strict because the declaration is irreversible, and so we are taught to be certain before we speak. I have also, more than once, seen a rhythm return after the room had gone quiet, when someone refused to stop compressions on schedule. This notice is filed in the knowledge that pronouncements can be premature. It is filed in the fear that this one is not.Robert B Shpiner is a clinical professor of medicine in pulmonary and critical care at the David Geffen School of Medicine at UCLAExplore more on these topicsTrump administrationOpinionMarco RubioUS politicsDonald TrumpUS elections 2020US foreign policycommentShareReuse this content

title: Has Trump dragged the US into a forever war in Iran? | Mohamad Bazzi | The Guardian
description: He decried endless wars in Iraq and Afghanistan. Now he may be stuck in one of his own making
Author: Mohamad Bazzi
Headline: He decried endless wars in Iraq and Afghanistan. Now he may be stuck in one of his own making
Content: ‘Trump shows little sign of pulling back from the brink of a wider war with Iran.’Photograph: Amir Hossein Khorgooei/ISNA/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘Trump shows little sign of pulling back from the brink of a wider war with Iran.’Photograph: Amir Hossein Khorgooei/ISNA/AFP/Getty ImagesOpinionUS-Israel war on IranHas Trump dragged the US into a forever war in Iran?Mohamad BazziHe decried endless wars in Iraq and Afghanistan. Now he may be stuck in one of his own makingWed 22 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 06.10 EDTShareAs Donald Trump launched a war of aggression against Iran nearly five months ago, he insisted Washington would achieve a decisive victory within weeks. Trump and his advisers scoffed at any suggestion that they were breakingrepeated campaign promisesnot to start new conflicts, and to end America’s legacy of forever wars, especially in Afghanistan and Iraq.In early March, a week into the war,Trump declaredhe would only agree to “unconditional surrender” by Iran’s theocratic government.He vowedto destroy much of Tehran’s military, called for a popular uprising andtouted the overthrowof the regime that rose to power after Iran’s 1979 Islamic revolution. Of course, Trump’s grandiose promises turned out to be the latest chapter in a long history of American imperial hubris.Not to mention that Trump contradicted multiple pledges he had made to a weary US public that he wouldn’t start new wars during his second presidency. In November 2024, in his election night victory speech from Florida, Trump confidentlytold Americans: “I’m not going to start a war. I’m going to stop wars.”Now, the ceasefire he negotiated with Tehran has collapsed and Trump risks dragging the US into yet another forever war – inIran.In mid-June, the US and Iran reached a memorandum of understanding that included reopening the strait of Hormuz, through which a fifth of the world’s oil and liquid gas supplies transited before Iran shut it during the war. That agreement was effectively a 60-day extension of a ceasefire that was reached in early April, but did not lead to reopening the crucial waterway because Trump imposed a naval blockade against Iranian ships in the region.Trump saidthe memo “achieves everything we set out to accomplish”, even though it had deferred the most difficult questions – including limitations on Iran’s nuclear program and Tehran’s potential control over of the strait of Hormuz – to future negotiations.Trump, not Iran, is the world’s greatest danger. He’s a one-man weapon of mass destruction | Simon TisdallRead more“Ships of the World, start your engines,” Trump wrote onsocial mediawhen he announced the interim deal last month. “Let the oil flow!”But each side had divergent interpretations of the agreement, and the deal collapsed after less than a month. Over the past 10 days, tit-for-tat attacks have lurched the region back towards an all-out war. Iran fired on oil tankers that passed through the strait near the coastline of Oman in the waterway’s southern corridor, which the US military has encouraged vessels to use to avoid Iran’s control. The Pentagon has launched daily waves of airstrikes against what it described as Iranian command centers, missile and drone launch sites, air defense systems and communication networks. In recent days, the US also bombed bridges across southern Iran – and Trump hasthreatened to destroyIran’s power plants and other civilian infrastructure, which would probably constitute a war crime.Tehran has retaliated with missile and drone strikes against US military bases across the Middle East, and it threatened to renew attacks on the energy infrastructure of its Gulf neighbors. Trump reimposed a blockade against Iranian shipping, and traffic through the strait of Hormuz has slowed to a trickle. The price of oil reached its highest level in a month, passing $90 a barrel, and once again disrupting the global economy. On Friday, Iranian ballistic missiles struck an airbase in Jordan, killing two US military personnel, with a third missing in action. The Pentagon said another service member had been killed on Saturday in northern Iraq, where the US has bases. Since the war began, 17 US service membershave been killedand about 430 wounded.Trump had previously declared that the killing of US personnel would elicit large-scale American retaliation against Iran. It’s yet another way that Trump has cornered himself into an endless cycle of bombing and military escalation. He has no strategy to turn US military superiority into a lasting political agreement with Tehran, and he has wavered for months between making a grand deal and threatening to restart a full-scale war. For much of the conflict, the scope of negotiations – and overall US policy toward Iran – seemed to depend on Trump’s shifting mood.For its part, the Iranian regime survived the assassination of its top leaders and withstood weeks of intense bombing by the US and Israel, two of the world’s most powerful militaries. On 28 February, in the conflict’s early hours, joint US-Israeli airstrikes killed the supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, and more than 30 other top Iranian officials. The regime’s surviving leadership quickly installed Khamenei’s son, Mojtaba, as the new supreme leader. He is supported by hardline generals of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, the country’s most powerful military and economic force.Iran’s leaders emerged more emboldened and with greater leverage than they had before Trump’s ill-fated war. They realized Tehran could hold the world’s economy hostage by deploying a weapon that is far more effective than its missiles and drones: control over the strait of Hormuz, which allows Iran to disrupt shipments of not just oil and liquified gas, but also key agricultural products such as fertilizer.The Guardian view on Trump and Tehran: everyone loses when the US and Iran overplay their hands | EditorialRead moreAnd Iran has more economic leverage it could deploy if Trump decides to resume an all-out war. Tehran could pressure the Houthi militia, its ally which controls large parts of Yemen, to disrupt international shipping through the Red Sea, which would further destabilize global energy supplies. After Israel’s invasion of Gaza in 2023, the Houthis began firing missiles and drones at vessels sailing around the Bab el-Mandab strait, where the Red Sea comes closest to Houthi-controlled areas of Yemen. Shipping companies rerouted hundreds of vessels around South Africa, increasing insurance premiums and adding thousands of miles to a freighter’s journey between Asia and Europe.For now, the Houthis have not fully entered the conflict, but on Monday the militiaannounced a blockadeof Saudi Arabia’s ports and ships. This could put more pressure on energy prices because Saudi Arabia has been exporting millions of barrels of its oil through the Red Sea route, so that it could circumvent Iran’s shuttering of the strait of Hormuz. Tehran can disrupt energy markets in other ways: it could widen its attacks on oil and natural gas infrastructure in its Gulf neighbors, including Kuwait, Qatar, Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates.All of these scenarios would probably cause energy prices to soar, and could trigger a global recession. And yet Trump shows little sign of pulling back from the brink of a wider war with Iran, which could involve the deployment of US ground troops and spread into neighboring countries.Trump is falling into a potential forever war of his own making.Mohamad Bazzi is a Guardian US columnist. He is also director of the Center for Near Eastern Studies and a journalism professor at New York UniversityExplore more on these topicsUS-Israel war on IranOpinionTrump administrationIranUS foreign policyMiddle East and north AfricacommentShareReuse this content

title: The world is on fire, and who does Trump blame? The Canadians | Arwa Mahdawi | The Guardian
description: The US president has been complaining about Canada’s ‘filthy, polluted and unhealthy air’. Maybe he should put all that energy into tackling the climate crisis, writes Arwa Mahdawi
Author: Arwa Mahdawi
Headline: The US president has been complaining about Canada’s ‘filthy, polluted and unhealthy air’. Maybe he should put all that energy into tackling the climate crisis, writes Arwa Mahdawi
Content: Smoke from wildfires in Canada fills the air around the US Capitol building in Washington DC.Photograph: Kevin Carter/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenSmoke from wildfires in Canada fills the air around the US Capitol building in Washington DC.Photograph: Kevin Carter/Getty ImagesOpinionWildfiresThe world is on fire, and who does Trump blame? The CanadiansArwa MahdawiThe US president has been complaining about Canada’s ‘filthy, polluted and unhealthy air’. Maybe he should put all that energy into tackling the climate crisisWed 22 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 11.04 EDTShareRight, that’s it: we’ve got to do something about Canada. The country’s wildfires, which resulted inair quality alertsacross more than 20 US states, ruined my weekend. On Friday the air in Philadelphia, where I live, was so unhealthy I couldn’t go outside without my eyes stinging. My kid’s preschool cancelled outdoor time, while the city suspended trash pickup and closed public pools.You know who did not suspend their regularly scheduled programming because of a bit of toxic smoke? The plot monitoring committee at myallotment. Despite the apocalyptic conditions, they were still busy ensuring the gardening bylaws were strictly adhered to. I realised this on Saturday when I got an official violation for too many weeds. Yes, I’d got a bit carried away with “chaos gardening” (all the rage these days) and I’d meant to clean things up on Friday, until the smoke got in the way. So, thanks for that Canada.Luckily, Donald Trump is on the case. OnFriday hecomplained on Truth Socialthat the US had been “invaded” by “filthy, polluted, and unhealthy air”, and the situation was “totally unacceptable”. Then, on Sunday, the president saidhe’d spoken to the Canadian prime ministerMark Carney and told him “you got to stop these fires from coming in and, you know, poisoning our air”. He added: “Maybe they should pay some damages or something, or we should do some tariffs.” I’m surprised he didn’t order ICE to stamp out the fires.Carney, for his part,respondedto Trump’s comments by referencing the climate crisis, and noting thatthis was everyone’s responsibilityto address. But who wants to talk about an inconvenient fact like the human-caused climate crisis when it’s much easier to just blame Canadians? Maga certainly doesn’t. On Thursday the Republican senator Bernie Moreno from Ohio introduced a new bill that wouldsanction Canadian officialsfor wildfire smoke. Meanwhile, the geniuses on Fox News have beenbusy yelling about Canadawhile skirting around the root causes of the wildfires.Poor Canada isn’t just getting a lot of hot air directed at it from Trumpers, it’s now issued its ownair quality warningsbecause of American fires to the south. It turns out that smoke doesn’t recognise border policies. And, as the weather becomes more extreme, things are onlygoing to get worse: the world is literally on fire and the arsonists are fiddling with tariffs as it burns.Arwa Mahdawi is a Guardian columnistExplore more on these topicsWildfiresOpinionCanadaDonald TrumpMark CarneyClimate crisiscommentShareReuse this content

title: Why many Black Americans were rooting for Argentina to lose the World Cup | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: Many Black Americans were not cheering Spain’s complicated history. They were rooting against the racism, politics and silence associated with Argentina
Author: Etan Thomas
Headline: Many Black Americans were not cheering Spain’s complicated history. They were rooting against the racism, politics and silence associated with Argentina
Content: Argentina’s Lionel Messi looks on after Sunday’s World Cup final.Photograph: Jewel Samad/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenArgentina’s Lionel Messi looks on after Sunday’s World Cup final.Photograph: Jewel Samad/AFP/Getty ImagesWorld Cup 2026Why many Black Americans were rooting for Argentina to lose the World CupEtan ThomasMany Black Americans were not cheering Spain’s complicated history. They were rooting against the racism, politics and silence associated with ArgentinaWed 22 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 06.46 EDTShareThere are moments in sports when you realize people aren’t just watching the score any more. They’re watching values. They’re watching who speaks. They’re watching who stays silent. And they’re watching what those silences say.I found myself thinking about that throughout this World Cup. On paper, Sunday’s final looked simple.SpainvArgentina. Two footballing giants and countries with glorious soccer histories. Two nations whose pasts are also stained by colonization, oppression and chapters of history that shouldn’t be whitewashed simply because someone can score a goal.Etan ThomasSo no, this wasn’t some fairytale of good versus evil. Spain’s colonial legacy is well documented. Nobody who knows history should pretend otherwise.Yet for many Black Americans, this final wasn’t really about rootingforSpain. It was about rootingagainstArgentina.When your fanbase repeatedly becomes associated with racist incidents … when videos circulate of Argentinian fans decked out in Argentina team gear hurling racial epithets toward Black people … when the world watched members of Argentina’s national teamsing a racist chant targeting France’s Black playersafter winning Copa América … when American streamer IShowSpeed was verbally assaulted by fans fully dressed in Argentina team gear … when word spreads across social media that in Argentina, there is a real hamburger business in Llavallol whose official name uses the “-a” version of the N-word:“Big N– Burgers”… when Argentina president Javier Milei openly embraces positions that align very closely with Donald Trump and offers support for Israel in the midst of a genocide, ethnic cleansing and slaughter of Palestinians … when Benjamin Netanyahu is asked on a recent podcast if he was rooting for Messi and says, “No, before Messi, Milei. He’s a superstar” … eventually, the tide of supports shifts. After a while, any accomplishments on the field become overshadowed.It’s like a college basketball coach who has all the appearances of a great program. He has a great winning percentage and a high graduation rate, and all of his players are disciplined, articulate and present themselves as professionals. But you keep hearing stories of verbal abuse and berating, whispers of a tyrant coach who is actively damaging his players’ mental health. At some point, the results become secondary and people start asking, “What’s going on with the Indiana Hoosiers?”‘Rifts began to appear’: Why many Latin Americans want Spain to beat Argentina in the World Cup finalRead moreArgentina kept giving people reasons to ask that question. Which brings us toLionel Messi.This is where people get uncomfortable because everybody loves Messi, and for good reason. His brilliance doesn’t require exaggeration. His résumé speaks louder than any sports debate ever could. Whether you think he’s the greatest player ever or merely one of them – shoutout toPelé– you’re arguing over different shades of greatness.But greatness on the field has never automatically translated into courage off it.Muhammad Ali taught us that. Bill Russell taught us that.Mahmoud Abdul-Rauftaught us that.Craig Hodgestaught us that. John Carlos and Tommie Smith taught us that. Being extraordinary with a ball, a bat or track shoes isn’t the same thing as being willing to risk comfort for conviction.To my knowledge, throughout this entireWorld Cup, Messi never publicly condemned the racist incidents involving sections of Argentina’s fanbase. He did not publicly address the racist chant that drew worldwide criticism after Copa América. Nor has he publicly spoken out after videos of Argentina supporters racially abusing Black people circulated online.He didn’t even offer the kind of broad, indirect condemnation that many critics argued was insufficient when Caitlin Clarkaddressed racist abuse coming from some of her supporters. As my co-host Chuck Modi argued on our showThe Collision: Where Sports and Politics Collide:“She has to be more specific. When Alyssa Thomas was getting death threats, she has to say Alyssa Thomas by name, and say directly to her fans that you cannot have death threats going to Alyssa Thomas in my name the same way we as Jewish people say not in my name for Palestinian rights. She needs to use all of her clout and tell her fans this is unacceptable.”Messi did not even clear that lower bar. He offered no public statement at all addressing the racism carried out by some of his supporters.As Dr Martin Luther King Jr said in his Beyond Vietnam speech: “A time comes when silence is betrayal.”Sometimes silence becomes its own statement simply because the loudest voice in the room chose not to use it. Contrast that with the generation coming after Messi.Lamine Yamal has spoken openly about his family’s sacrifices. He has recalled how his father searched through discarded items that could be repaired or sold so there would be enough money for food. He has said he grew up in a tiny apartment where the kitchen and bedroom were one and the same. Today, he says what makes him happiest is seeing his mother smile and knowing his younger brother can enjoy the childhood he never had.Nico Williams carries a similarly powerful story. His mother, María, walked nearly 2,000 miles while pregnant, from Ghana toward Spain, in search of a better life. Decades later, her son set up the goal that won Spain the World Cup and then placed his winner’s medal around her neck.Spain’s triumph was never just about tactics, possession or technical excellence. It was about possibility. It was a team that reflected the modern Spain – diverse, confident and shaped by the journeys of families who crossed borders in pursuit of a better life. That, more than any passing sequence or tactical innovation, was their most powerful statement.After Spain defeated Argentina on Sunday, one image stood out to me: Lamine Yamal embracing Messi. No taunting or chest-pounding or disrespect, just respect from one generation to another. Like the old African Proverb says: “The lion doesn’t need to roar after every meal.”Another image that resonated was seeing Lamine Yamal kneel in prayer amid the chaos around him while emotions boiled over after the match. While others argued, he prayed. That image reminded me that composure can be louder than confrontation.Lamine Yamal has also shown a willingness to let people know where he stands on issues beyond football, including expressing solidarity with Palestine. Whether people agree with every public stance an athlete takes is almost beside the point. The point is that he is willing to recognize that his platform extends beyond 90 (or 120) minutes on the field.Jackie Robinson wasn’t remembered simply because he stole bases. Kareem Abdul-Jabbar isn’t remembered simply because he had the most unstoppable move the NBA has ever seen. Colin Kaepernick isn’t remembered simply because of his dual-threat quarterbacking skills. Eventually the statistics become paragraphs, but character becomes chapters.That’s why comparisons between Messi and Ali don’t really work for me. One changed football forever, but the other changed the world.Some people have asked why so many Black Americans seemed emotionally invested in seeing Argentina lose. I don’t think it’s complicated. When you’ve spent your entire life hearing people tell you sports and politics should stay separate – but somehow they only say that after racism enters the conversation – you start recognizing the pattern.People told Ali to “just box”. People told Kaepernick to “just play football”. People told LeBron James to “shut up and dribble”. People effectively accusedJaylen Brownof being “uppity”. Apparently athletes are free to use their platforms – unless they’re using them for something that makes people uncomfortable.That’s always been the rule, until it isn’t.This World Cup became about more than goals because people made it about more than goals. Racism made it bigger. Politics made it bigger. Silence made it bigger. For many viewers, supporting Spain wasn’t an endorsement of Spain’s history; it was simply rejecting what Argentina had come to represent in this particular moment. Sometimes cheering isn’t about choosing perfection, but about choosing the option that feels less harmful.Maybe that’s why so many people found themselves drawn to this young generation. Not simply because they won, but because they represent the possibility that excellence and conviction don’t have to play on different teams. To quote Abdul-Rauf from our interview for my bookPolice Brutality and White Supremacy: The Fight Against American Traditions: “What you do speaks so loudly in my ears that I can’t hear a word you’re saying.”I couldn’t agree more.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026World CupSpainArgentinaLionel MessicommentShareReuse this content

title: Pauline Hanson’s comments about domestic violence are more than vile. They are dangerous for women | Nicole Yade | The Guardian
description: A woman in Australia is killed by someone she trusted every 11 days and misinformation about family violence does real harm to victims
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: A woman in Australia is killed by someone she trusted every 11 days and misinformation about family violence does real harm to victims
Content: ‘Pauline Hanson’s suggestion that women should simply “not put up with it” reveals a profound misunderstanding of how abuse works,’ writes Nicole Yade.Photograph: Mick Tsikas/AAPView image in fullscreen‘Pauline Hanson’s suggestion that women should simply “not put up with it” reveals a profound misunderstanding of how abuse works,’ writes Nicole Yade.Photograph: Mick Tsikas/AAPOpinionDomestic violencePauline Hanson’s comments about domestic violence are more than vile. They are dangerous for womenNicole YadeA woman in Australia is killed by someone she trusted every 11 days and misinformation about family violence does real harm to victimsWed 22 Jul 2026 19.13 EDTShareOn Wednesday, senator Pauline Hanson claimed domestic and family violence is “a two-way street”, blamed the cost of living for perpetrators’ actions, and suggested women experiencing abuse should simply “not put up with it”.These comments are not an aberration. They sit within a broader political project that has, for decades, attempted to build support on the basis of normalising prejudice, particularly against migrants, Muslim communities and communities of colour. That same platform can also risk lending credibility to dangerous myths about domestic and family violence.Words matter. Particularly when they come from elected representatives. Misinformation about domestic and family violence does real harm because it reinforces myths that keep victim-survivors silent, isolated and unsafe.Facts matter too.Brittany Higgins accuses Pauline Hanson of victim-blaming for calling domestic violence a ‘two-way street’Read moreViolence is gendered.One in four womenhave experienced violence by an intimate partner since the age of 15, compared with one in 14 men. One instance, committed by anyone, is too many, but when a woman in Australia is killed by someone she trusted every 11 days, there can be no doubt where the true crisis lies.At the Women’s and Girls’ Emergency Centre, we don’t debate this reality. We live it. Every night, we support about 200 women and children impacted by homelessness, domestic violence and systemic disadvantage.I also know this reality personally. I am a victim-survivor of domestic and family violence, and I know first-hand that leaving is not as simple as choosing to walk away. Fear, coercion, financial insecurity, concern for children and the hope that things will change can all be powerful forces that keep people trapped.Hanson’s suggestion that women should simply “not put up with it” reveals a profound misunderstanding of how abuse works.Domestic violence experts have long identified a common pattern of behaviour used by many perpetrators: tension building, intimidation, escalation, remorse, pursuit, a honeymoon period, and repeat.After an abusive incident, displays of remorse, love bombing, gifts and promises to change often manipulate victims into believing the violence will never happen again. Then the cycle often repeats.This is one of the reasons leaving an abusive relationship can be so difficult. For many it’s not an option. Others may attempt to leave multiple times before they can do so. Others stay because they desperately want the violence to stop and believe the promises that it will.Leaving is even harder when the alternative is homelessness. Domestic and family violence remains theleading causeof homelessness for women in Australia. When the choice is between a dangerous home and a dangerous world, it is no choice at all.Hanson also sought to link her vile comments with her politically convenient raison d’etre – the cost of living. On this, once again, she is wrong. Responsibility for violence always lies with the perpetrator. There is no excuse for it, no societal or economic conditions that provide a cover of acceptability to abuse.Victim-survivors and women trapped in abusive relationships may hear Hanson’s comments and believe they are alone. That they are not seen. Perhaps even that they are at fault.Pauline Hanson shares Seven pitch for Spotlight special that says program would highlight ‘uncontrolled immigration’Read moreFor people from communities that have long been the target of Hanson’s rhetoric, these comments will not be experienced in isolation. They reinforce a pattern of public discourse that diminishes people’s experiences, legitimises prejudice and undermines safety.Let me be quite clear. Women are not at fault. They cannot always just leave. There is no excuse for the violence they experience. And there is a support network out there ready to support them and who work every day to prevent the very violence they are forced to suffer.Hanson may never apologise for her comments. But the rest of us have a responsibility to challenge misinformation whenever we hear it, to stand with victim-survivors and to ensure the national conversation about domestic and family violence is grounded in evidence, compassion and the lived reality of those experiencing it.Because the stakes are simply too high for anything less.Nicole Yade is the chief executive of the Women’s and Girls’ Emergency Centre (WAGEC)Explore more on these topicsDomestic violenceOpinionPauline HansoncommentShareReuse this content

title: Pressure is growing inside Labor to properly tax gas exports. Here’s what a 25% tax could pay for | Greg Jericho | The Guardian
description: A change in the ALP’s platform signals the fight to get a fair return for our resources is not over
Author: Greg Jericho
Headline: A change in the ALP’s platform signals the fight to get a fair return for our resources is not over
Content: ‘Anthony Albanese’s lines have wrung so hollow that ALP members are clearly unimpressed and are pushing for a commitment to properly tax gas,’ Greg Jericho writes.Photograph: Dan Himbrechts/AAPView image in fullscreen‘Anthony Albanese’s lines have wrung so hollow that ALP members are clearly unimpressed and are pushing for a commitment to properly tax gas,’ Greg Jericho writes.Photograph: Dan Himbrechts/AAPGrogonomicsTaxPressure is growing inside Labor to properly tax gas exports.
