Here’s what a 25% tax could pay forGreg JerichoA change in the ALP’s platform signals the fight to get a fair return for our resources is not overFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastWed 22 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 19.53 EDTShareOn Thursday at its national conference, the ALP will vote to change its platform to include a pledge to deliver a fair return on Australia’s natural resources.The proposal is that the platform includes the lines: “Labor will ensure that the Australian people receive a fairer return from their natural resources including through appropriate taxation arrangements, while securing Australia’s role as a reliable international energy supplier and investment partner.”Manufacturing union says Australia’s tax system is ‘rigged’ in bold manifesto ahead of Labor conferenceRead moreThe language is a bit “all things to all people”, but it clearly relates to better taxing gas – essentially to implement the ACTU’s proposal of a 25% tax on gas exports.Right now, Australia isthe second-biggest exporter of liquefied natural gas in the world, behind only the US and ahead of Qatar. Twenty-five years ago, LNG exports accounted for just 2% of all goods exported from Australia; now it’s about 12%.Such a boom should deliver a similar soaring of tax revenue via the petroleum resource rent tax (PRRT), which was set up in the 1980s to tax oil and gas profits.But no. In 2025-26, Australia exported $52.6bnmoreLNG than 25 years earlier (a lazy 1,968% increase), but the government raised $979mlessPRRT (a 41% drop):If the graph does not display click hereWe haven’t even had a decent increase from royalties in the same way we did from the iron ore boom, because 56% of LNG exports come from royalty-free gas – because it is offshore.Take the Japanese company Inpex – a company the prime minister cited as an example ofhow the PRRT is working. It has projects off the Western Australia and Northern Territory coast. It has paid no royalties and no PRRT and barely any company tax despite boasting that ithas exported $195bn worth of LNG:If the graph does not display click hereThe problem is the PRRT is complex. It taxes natural gas, not the LNG exports, and it also allows companies to offset vast costs through accounting methods that could in effect make an LNG profit never eligible to pay PRRT.Nearly a decade ago, then treasurer Scott Morrison commissioned a review into the PRRT (the Callaghan review). The review recommended a few minor changes, which Morrison didn’t bother to make.In 2023, the treasurer, Jim Chalmers, commissioned another review.It recommended three changes.The least favoured choice by Treasurywas a 90% cap on the proportion of PRRT-assessable income that can be offset. It was the most favoured by the gas industry, and that was the one that was implemented.Gas companies, you see, are very good at lobbying.The government could not admit this would raise more tax; instead, the phrase became “more tax sooner”. The changes meant gas companies would have to pay tax earlier than they otherwise could have been able to put off.And yet the estimated revenue from the PRRT keeps getting revised down.If the graph does not display click hereThis year the Greens, supported byindependent senator David Pocock, conducted another review into the tax of gas (I co-wrote a submission). The impetus came from the proposal by the ACTU (for which I assisted with research) to ditch the PRRT and instead put a 25% tax on gas exports.This was estimated to raise up to $17bn a year – rather more than the $1.3bn average that has been raised over the past decade by the PRRT.The gas industry lobby fired up.They said this would be folly, because the boom in PRRT revenue was coming. Yes, the gas industry seriously tried to sell us that keeping the PRRT was better because it would pay more tax.Anthony Albanese repeated theirspin, telling reporters that“the PRRT is designed to ramp up how much revenue it creates”.One gas industry analyst toldThe Australian in Aprilthat “around the end of this decade … we’re going to get a huge influx of PRRT”.Four weeks later, the May budget released the first estimates for PRRT revenue in 2029–30 (ie the end of the decade). It was just $1.25bn – the lowest amount in six years.As a percentage of GDP and as a share of total tax raised, it would be the lowest in 40 years:If the graph does not display click hereBy the end of the decade, PRRT will raise less revenue than excises on wine, beer, spirits, other alcoholic beverages and tobacco, the major bank levy and visa application charges:If the graph does not display click hereThe reality is the gas industry won the PRRT fight. All reforms and fixes result in the gas industry paying less.That is a pathetic state of affairs. Embarrassing for all or us, let alone the treasurer or prime minister overseeing it.A 25% tax on gas exports is clean – it does not affect the price paid by customers; it just affects the after-tax profits of the gas companies. It creates an incentive for producers to sell gas to Australia at a lower price (to avoid paying the tax).And it would raise $17bn a year.How much is that?More than enough to fully pay fordental in Medicare, or free childcare, or to double spending on public schools:If the graph does not display click hereIn 2029-30 the national disability insurance scheme is being cut by $16.4bn because the government instead chose not to upset the gas industry.The “more sooner” and Albanese “ramping up” lines have rung so hollow that ALP members are clearly unimpressed and are pushing for a commitment to properly tax gas.It would be foolish to think the platform change will automatically change government policy, but it signals that the fight to get a fair return for our resources continues. The pressure isn’t just coming from the Greens or independents, but now from inside the party.Greg Jericho is a Guardian columnist and chief economist at the Australia InstituteExplore more on these topicsTaxGrogonomicsLabor partyGasAustralian politicsAustralian economyAnthony AlbaneseCommoditiescommentShareReuse this content

title: Labor has a proud history of debate. So why won’t it interrogate the $368bn Aukus pact?  | Peter Garrett | The Guardian
description: Despite substantial reservations about the submarine deal, it’s unlikely to be seriously examined at the ALP national conference
Author: Peter Garrett
Headline: Despite substantial reservations about the submarine deal, it’s unlikely to be seriously examined at the ALP national conference
Content: ‘Reservations about the Aukus pact have not abated,’ Peter Garrett writes. Pictured: Anthony Albanese, Joe Biden and Rishi Sunak speak about the Aukus nuclear submarine deal in San Diego, California, in 2023.Photograph: Leah Millis/ReutersView image in fullscreen‘Reservations about the Aukus pact have not abated,’ Peter Garrett writes. Pictured: Anthony Albanese, Joe Biden and Rishi Sunak speak about the Aukus nuclear submarine deal in San Diego, California, in 2023.Photograph: Leah Millis/ReutersOpinionAukusLabor has a proud history of debate. So why won’t it interrogate the $368bn Aukus pact?Peter GarrettDespite substantial reservations about the submarine deal, it’s unlikely to be seriously examined at the ALP national conferenceFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastWed 22 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 19.53 EDTShareIn 2021 the leaders of Australia, the UK and the US – Scott Morrison, Boris Johnson and Joe Biden – stood together to announce a plan to co-design and build eight nuclear-powered submarines for Australia.Morrison championed the pact, having reneged on a previous agreement to purchase French-built submarines that would have cost about $90bn, (the cancellation cost alone was $5bn), and after losing office went on to work with the US defence industry.His decision, effectively made in secret, was accepted by the incoming Albanese Labor government.The independent Aukus public inquiry,led by myself, has been instigated to enable Australians to better understand the scope, implications and rationale of the largest ever single defence expenditure made in this country.There will be disagreement at next week’s Labor conference – but don’t expect the feuds of the pastRead moreIt is publicly funded, transparent and open to all views. As befitting of a healthy democracy, it also embodies the right of citizens to gather, listen, discuss, consider and publish on matters of national importance.Neither the federal parliament nor the major parties have chosen to formally inquire into or substantively debate the merits of this deal.While substantial concerns about Aukus have been expressed in motions by several Labor party branches and by senior figures including former prime ministerPaul Keatingand former foreign affairs ministersGareth EvansandBob Carr, this week’s Labor conference in Adelaide – one of the key locations for Aukus, as it happens – looks unlikely to accommodate any meaningful debate on the issue.This is a great pity. Labor would be forfeiting the opportunity to reclaim public trust at a time when support for the two major parties is fragile. Retreating from its auspicious past, where robust debates and serious consideration of foreign affairs was a hallmark, Labor would be refusing to meaningfully interrogate the most important national conversation about defence decision-making since 1945.Halfway through the public inquiry it is clear that substantial reservations about theAukuspact have not abated.double quotation markDespite an open invitation to the government to submit to and appear at the inquiry, none has been forthcomingAustralia will commit nearly $10bn to subsidise US and UK naval shipyards so that we eventually receive newly built nuclear-powered submarines.Experts have queried whether this payment, intended to speed up the US Virginia-class submarine building program, will actually deliver any submarines given current build rates.The UK, already experiencing chronic difficulties with existing naval construction, is considered highly unlikely to build from scratch a new, yet-to-be-designed Aukus SSN submarine, on time or on budget.Repeated concerns about cost blowouts have been raised. The current$368bndoes not include establishing infrastructure to safely dispose of medium- and high-levelradioactive waste, including weapons-grade uranium associated with the decommissioning of the submarines.The fundamental question of whether the submarines will be fit for purpose has also emerged as a key issue. The 1:3 ratio in submarine deployment – where for every submarine at sea, two are undergoing repairs and maintenance – means that eight submarines, in the words of Australia’s foremost naval submarine expertPeter Briggs, are “mathematically insufficient to maintain a two-ocean presence”.View image in fullscreenFormer foreign affairs minister Gareth Evans has said Aukus would see Australia’s nuclear submarines function as an extension of the American fleet.Photograph: Lukas Coch/AAPSignificant statements about Aukus have come from former Coalition defence ministers who support the deal. Linda Reynolds’ observation that under Aukus, the Stirling submarine base in Western Australia (where US nuclear-powered and armed submarines will be based on rotation from 2027) would be a nuclear target remains a startling admission.Gareth Evans, one of Australia’s most experienced foreign affairs ministers, told the inquiry Aukus would see Australia’s nuclear submarines function as anextension of the American fleet.Security and defence policy experts continue to raise crucial questions about what Aukus means for our relationships with regional neighbours, Australia’s adherence to the international nuclear non-proliferation treaty regime and whether the submarines will actually be suitable for patrolling the shallow waters of our northern coastline.From First Nations communities and those living in suburbs close to future Aukus activity we have heard deep anger at the absence of any prior notice, or meaningful consultation.Despite an open invitation to the government and relevant agencies to submit to and appear at the inquiry, none has been forthcoming.All of the questions about Aukus deserve to be fully explored and better understood at a time of rising cost-of-living pressures, global political upheaval, the spectre of increased climate risk to national security, and more. If governments won’t attend diligently to the task, then this public inquiry will.Peter Garrett is a former Labor environment minister and leads the community-based public inquiry into the Aukus defence pactExplore more on these topicsAukusOpinionLabor partyAustralian politicsAustralian militaryAustralian security and counter-terrorismAustralian foreign policyNuclear powercommentShareReuse this content

title: The Treasury will check Andy Burnham’s ambitions – unless old orthodoxies are challenged | Larry Elliott | The Guardian
description: Fifty years ago, another Healey ran the Treasury. Today’s chancellor may find some things haven’t changed, says Guardian columnist Larry Elliott
Author: Larry Elliott
Headline: Fifty years ago, another Healey ran the Treasury. Today’s chancellor may find some things haven’t changed, says Guardian columnist Larry Elliott
Content: John Healey addresses staff at the Treasury, after becoming chancellor, London, 21 July 2026.Photograph: Stefan Rousseau/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenJohn Healey addresses staff at the Treasury, after becoming chancellor, London, 21 July 2026.Photograph: Stefan Rousseau/AFP/Getty ImagesOpinionJohn HealeyThe Treasury will check Andy Burnham’s ambitions – unless old orthodoxies are challengedLarry ElliottFifty years ago, another Healey ran the Treasury. Today’s chancellor may find some things haven’t changedWed 22 Jul 2026 08.57 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 12.06 EDTShareSome things never change. Fifty years ago, Britain was sweltering in the long hotsummer of 1976. The Labour government was acutely aware that the financial markets were scrutinising its every move. And a man called Healey was running the Treasury.On the surface, the challenges facing John Healey are less severe than thoseDenis Healeyhad to grapple with half a century ago. Inflation isa lot lowerand the City has reacted calmly to Andy Burnham as prime minister. And, while things could turn nasty pretty quickly, they would have to get a lot worse before Healey Mk II felt the need to ask the International Monetary Fund for anemergency loan.That’s the good news. The bad news is that today’s Healey has big problems of his own to solve, not least how to reconcile his pledge to run a tight ship with the new administration’s aims of rolling back more than 40 years of Thatcherism and reindustrialising Britain. Fine aspirations will eventually run into the quicksands of fiscal orthodoxy.But so far things have gone as well as Burnham and Healey could have hoped. Themessage of optimism– a deliberate contrast with the doom and gloom when Keir Starmer arrived in Downing Street – was the right one. The cut in VAT on electricity bills and thecap on bus fareswere largely symbolic but symbols matter. Burnham was keen to avoid a repetition of Starmer’s first big economic policy announcement: the means testing of the winter fuel payment for pensioners. That and the purging of the Starmerites from the cabinet are intended to give the impression that the real Labour government starts here.View image in fullscreenThe then chancellor Denis Healey before presenting the budget, 30 March 1976,Photograph: Peter Cade/Getty ImagesOther conclusions can be drawn. Burnham clearly sees Reform UK as his biggest threat. That’s evident not just in the makeup of the cabinet – which has a stronger north of England flavour to it – but in theemphasis on the cost of livingand rebuilding Britain’s manufacturing base. There is an attempt to reconnect the party with its working-class roots.Keeping Shabana Mahmood as the home secretary was also significant because it shows that Burnham intends to maintain a tough stance on migration. That has economic consequences since it will force businesses faced with staff shortages to hire and train UK-born employees rather than bring them in from overseas. The days of unlimited cheap labour are over. Burnham’s desire to try a different economic model seems genuine, as does his intention to be heavily involved in the big economic decisions.Prime ministers come in three types. There are those who enjoy focusing on domestic issues, there are those who get a buzz from foreign policy and there are those who want to do everything. It is not rare for a prime minister to start a term of office with a burning passion to sort out the economy only to find after a while that glad-handing on the global stage is more fun. But, for the moment, Burnham falls squarely into category one, and has plans to create an economic power base for himself at No 10 to act as a counterweight to the Treasury.That, at least, is the theory. In reality, Burnham’s first 24 hours in office showed just how much power and influence the Treasury holds. Healey said meeting theexisting fiscal ruleswas his top priority, and he now has to find the money for a fully funded defence investment plan that the Treasury denied him when he was defence secretary,precipitating his resignation.The chancellor may get lucky and be able to pay for higher spending through stronger growth, but with the strait of Hormuz still closed and oil pricescreeping up againit would be inadvisable to bank on it. That leaves borrowing more or higher taxes. A bit of extra borrowing may be available while sticking to the fiscal rules, but Burnham and Healey have big ambitions and are wary of upsetting the bond markets, which are already charging the government interest rates of 5% to service its debts. Something has to give.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAndy Burnham is playing the long game in an age of short attention spans | Rafael BehrRead moreHealey has floated the idea of war bonds to pay for higher defence spending, but that would be borrowing by a different name. Inevitably, therefore, taxes are going to rise in the autumn budget and the only real questions are: by how much, who pays them and what damage they will cause.Faced with the prospect of a sluggish economy and tax rises to come, it is a tad hubristic for the seventh occupant of Downing Street in a decade to be serving notice that he will shortly be announcing a 10-year plan for change. Burnham doesn’t have the luxury of 10 years to deliver results. A general election is due within the next three years, leaving little wiggle room if things go seriously wrong – as they did for Denis Healey.Some things have changed since the long hot summer of 1976. One of the abiding legacies of Britain’s shift to the right in the 1970s was the idea that there was no difference between the government and a household when it comes to spending, with the implication that either can go bust if it lives beyond its means. That notion lives on today, with even the most modest of ministerial spending pledges met with the challenge: how are you going to pay for it? If Burnham is serious about rolling back neoliberalism, he needs to start by challenging this entirely wrong-headed idea.Larry Elliott is a Guardian columnistExplore more on these topicsJohn HealeyOpinionEconomic policyAndy BurnhamLabourTax and spendingcommentShareReuse this content

title: Here’s how Andy Burnham really could end homelessness  | Daniel Lavelle | The Guardian
description: Utilising empty homes, capping rents and fixing the benefits system would all help, but they’d require a serious fight, says Daniel Lavelle, who writes on mental health, homelessness and social care
Author: Daniel Lavelle
Headline: Utilising empty homes, capping rents and fixing the benefits system would all help, but they’d require a serious fight, says Daniel Lavelle, who writes on mental health, homelessness and social care
Content: Andy Burnham talking with Hubar, who is homeless in London, 20 July 2026.Photograph: Christopher Furlong/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham talking with Hubar, who is homeless in London, 20 July 2026.Photograph: Christopher Furlong/Getty ImagesOpinionHomelessnessHere’s how Andy Burnham really could end homelessnessDaniel LavelleUtilising empty homes, capping rents and fixing the benefits system would all help, but they’d require a serious fightWed 22 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 08.52 EDTShareIn January 2017, the then mayor of Greater Manchester, Andy Burnham, promised toend rough sleepingin the region by 2020 – a bold, frankly absurd promise that inevitably didn’t materialise. Nearly a decade later, shortly after becoming prime minister, Burnham promised to end rough sleeping again, only this time for thewhole of England. So why should we believe him this time around?I have interviewed Burnham several times over the past decade. At the end of 2019, when it was clear he had not lived up to his promise, I asked himif he regrettedmaking it in the first place. “I wasn’t making what you would call a traditional target,” the mayor said. “I issued my commitment to make it more of a wake-up call to the system. And I think it has had that effect.” Burnham’s advisers had told him to tone down the pledge, but he resisted. “The team here said I should have said: ‘End the need for rough sleeping,’ because you can’t force people into a shelter … In retrospect, if I had said ‘end the need for’, that probably would have been a wiser thing to have done. But I wasn’t in the space of wanting to play semantics with it.”Official figures showed rough sleeping decreased by two-thirds in Manchester during his first five years as mayor. There are reasons I can quibble with this claim. First of all, it doesn’t pass the eye test. Just walk through the city centre in the early hours, and you’ll find people still bedding down in their dozens. Second, the statistics on rough sleeping are based on single-night snapshots, which involve council representatives walking around counting the people they find rough sleeping. The problem with this is obvious. Third, even those stats, as unreliable as they are, now show that rough sleeping in Manchester isthree-quartersof what it was in 2017.But let’s forget all that, and give the prime minister the benefit of the doubt. Maybe his “promise” was merely a “wake-up call”. He was a metro mayor after all, and for most of his time in office, he depended on the generosity of a Conservative government. He simply didn’t have the resources to end rough sleeping. Let’s also assume that Burnham means what he says. He didn’t have to make his pledge back in 2017, and he certainly didn’t have to make it on the steps of Downing Street on Monday.The difference is that now he has control of the purse strings, so how does Burnham intend to honour his promise this time around? Initial signs are not encouraging. After Burnham finished his speech, his new government pledged an extra £340m, equivalent to £68m a year over five years, on top of the £3.5bn already allocated, to tackle homelessness. The UK can’t quite agree on how many rough sleepers there are, but I think a sensible estimate is anywhere between 15,000-18,000 unique people over a 12-month period. So, £68m divided among 18,000 people amounts to £3,777 per head. That’s about four months of rent if you’re lucky. It’s less a panacea, more like defending yourself from a tidal wave with a cocktail umbrella.The government will say it is only targeting 3,000 of the most vulnerable rough sleepers with this cash, and they will receive closer to £22,000 per head. I don’t know how one would qualify for the most-vulnerable list. Just how hopeless do you need to be in the new Burnham era?If Burnham really wants to live up to his promise, he’ll need to do a lot better than this. He should be aware from the start that rough sleeping represents the very tip of an enormous iceberg, and solving homelessness in this country will demand something close to a revolution. According to Shelter, there are380,000 homeless peoplein England, and even that demoralising figure is almost certainly an underestimate. To permanently house those people would require an unprecedented social housebuilding programme that would make the postwar rebuild look like a casual game of Minecraft.Burnham’s government would also need to reverse 16 years of austerity. This is all quite spendy, and to fund it would require higher taxes, more borrowing and aggressive nationalisation of natural monopolies. An upheaval of the economy of such magnitude is unlikely to happen when so manyLabourMPs have their fingernails firmly embedded in the market. If there’s even a suggestion that Burnham intends to depart from turbo capitalism, then he will be out of SW1 faster than you can say Liz Truss.So, what can Burnham realistically do about homelessness? There are several levers available to him to ease people’s suffering in the short term, and they cost next to nothing in political or real capital.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionIt was Britain’s most expensive house. Why is its only resident a homeless man who lives on the porch?Read moreHis priority should be triggering a nationwide Housing First programme. Housing First, a policypioneered in Finlandthat effectively eliminated rough sleeping, gives people a home with no preconditions, then tailors support to their individual needs. We know this works because we did it ourselves, only with a different name. TheEveryone In campaign, established during the Covid-19 pandemic, virtually ended rough sleeping overnight by emergency block-booking hotels. Surely we can do the same thing again.The next thing on the agenda should be removing the stick from the benefits system. Currently, the welfare state resembles a gameshow created by Kafka, where contestants have to complete a series of timed trials to win basic subsistence.There are about300,000 long-term empty homesin England. Housing people in some of these dwellings might be a good idea; capping rents at an affordable rate would be another, as would fast-tracking asylum applications and enabling asylum seekers to receive benefits and temporary work visas while their claims are processed.I’m not confident that Burnham will do any of these things. Not because I think he doesn’t care or lacks the political will, but because government apparatus is smothered in molasses, and most Labour politicians are Tories in all but name.But let’s see what Burnham does before writing him off as yet another neoliberal metronome with fine words and dry parsnips. Let’s give the man his honeymoon period, but the “king of the north” should keep this in mind: winter is coming, and he must act quickly, not just for his premiership, but for the poor souls he has promised to get off the streets.Daniel Lavelle writes on mental health, homelessness and social care. He received the Guardian’s Hugo Young award in 2017, and is the author of Down and OutExplore more on these topicsHomelessnessOpinionHousingEnglandAndy BurnhamLabourcommentShareReuse this content

title: My advice to Claudia Winkleman: it’s not you, it’s the giant sofa – try a different kind of chatshow | Fiona Sturges | The Guardian
description: The popular presenter is by no means the first to have come a cropper hosting a talkshow. But new formats provide new opportunities, says arts journalist Fiona Sturges
Author: Fiona Sturges
Headline: The popular presenter is by no means the first to have come a cropper hosting a talkshow. But new formats provide new opportunities, says arts journalist Fiona Sturges
Content: ‘Claudia Winkleman revealed that she will not be returning for a second season.’Photograph: BBC/So Television/PA MediaView image in fullscreen‘Claudia Winkleman revealed that she will not be returning for a second season.’Photograph: BBC/So Television/PA MediaOpinionClaudia WinklemanMy advice to Claudia Winkleman: it’s not you, it’s the giant sofa – try a different kind of chatshowFiona SturgesThe popular presenter is by no means the first to have come a cropper hosting a talkshow. But new formats provide new opportunitiesWed 22 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 04.05 EDTShareYou might think the TV chatshow is an easy format to get right. Hire a well-liked host with comic chops and some successful shows under their belt. Build a cheerful set with a big sofa in the middle. Book some A-list celebs plus the obligatory comedian, invite a live audience et voilà! A prime-time hit is surely born.Except that time and time again, it has proved not to be that simple. Just ask Claudia Winkleman,who revealed last weekthat she won’t be returning to do a second season of The Claudia Winkleman Show on the BBC. The series, which launched in March, filling the gap while The Graham Norton Show was on hiatus,was received with lukewarm reviewsand so-so ratings. The decision to call time on it is reported to be Winkleman’s – “I was just too nervous to enjoy it,” she admitted – though, given the cuts rippling through the corporation, she may have opted to jump before being pushed.Part of the problem is that Winkleman was never going to matchNorton’s impeccable hosting style, honed over nearly two decades. As well as approaching his A-list guests with warmth and cheeky irreverence, Norton is also a sharp conversational steward, adept at keeping everyone engaged and, thanks to a well-stocked bar, gently lubricated. It isn’t as easy as it looks.By contrast, stuck on a gloomy set and with the audience positioned weirdly close to the talent, Winkleman allowed conversations to drift and indulged in silly hyperbole – “That’s the best thing anybody’s ever said,”she yelped at Tom Allenafter a mildly funny gag about his fellow guests’ blathering. It may be that, following rigorous analysis of the first series, Winkleman would have found her rhythm in a second. But for a presenter stillriding high on the successof The Traitors, and who has shown herself to be sparky, spontaneous and funny fronting other shows, it wasn’t worth risking another critical drubbing.View image in fullscreenClaudia Winkleman hosts the first episode of her chatshow.Photograph: Matt Crossick/PAWinkleman is by no means the first presenter to come a cropper hosting a chatshow. In 2006, Davina McCall, then the boisterous host of Big Brother,was roundly pannedfor her self-titled chatshow which was dumped after one series. The same decade also brought the short-livedLily Allen and Friendsand Channel 4’s sub-parThe Charlotte Church Show, where both hosts’ lack of experience left them looking uncomfortable and out of their depth.The depressing common denominator in these cancelled shows is female hosts. Allen and Church were badly miscast but, like Winkleman, McCall seemed an ideal candidate. Yet, for reasons that are hard to fathom, the late-night chatshow has always been a male stronghold. Johnny Carson, Larry King, David Letterman, Jay Leno, Conan O’Brien, Jon Stewart and the Jimmys Fallon and Kimmel all ruled the chatshow roost in the US, while Michael Parkinson, Russell Harty, Terry Wogan, Jonathan Ross, Alan Carr and Graham Norton have done the same here. Comedians, it would appear, seem to land best with audiences. The only woman to have flourished in the format in the UK was under cover of a comic character: Mrs Merton, the creation of the late Caroline Aherne,brilliantly roasted her guestsin faux-naif style, asking Bernard Manning: “Who do you vote for now that Hitler’s dead?”View image in fullscreenCaroline Aherne with guest Patrick Duffy on The Mrs Merton Show.Photograph: ITV/REX/ShutterstockBut there is another more existential threat currently facing the chatshow that goes beyond a resistance to female hosts. Until recently, an A-lister with a new film/TV show/album to promote in the UK would make a beeline for either The Graham Norton Show or its ITV equivalent, The Jonathan Ross Show. But now there are scores of podcasts, most of them operating in video as well as audio, that offer not only easy exposure and a chance to plug their latest masterwork but longer and more in-depth conversations.Not all these podcasts are made equal – many are hosted by celebrities patently ill-suited to the job of listening and asking probing questions – but the public appetite for them remains undimmed. Once again, male hosts dominate, among them Joe Rogan, Steven Bartlett, Louis Theroux and Romesh Ranganathan, though women are getting more of a look-in. TakeBegin Again, the hit interview series about midlife reinvention launched in 2024 that has featured Fatboy Slim, Gisèle Pelicot, Richard E Grant and Gillian Anderson as guests. The host? That would be former chatshow kryptonite Davina McCall.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAll of which would suggest a Winkleman-fronted interview series is by no means dead in the water. Should she wish to have another crack at it, her best bet would be to dispense with the live audience and the giant sofa, and bypass traditional telly altogether.Fiona Sturges is an arts writer for the Guardian and other newspapersDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsClaudia WinklemanOpinioncommentShareReuse this content

title: Andy Burnham is playing the long game in an age of short attention spans | Rafael Behr | The Guardian
description: It is hard to make the case for a 10-year plan in our political culture of constant campaigning frenzy – but the new PM will try, says Guardian columnist Rafael Behr
Author: Rafael Behr
Headline: It is hard to make the case for a 10-year plan in our political culture of constant campaigning frenzy – but the new PM will try, says Guardian columnist Rafael Behr
Content: Andy Burnham’s first cabinet meeting in Downing Street, London, 21 July 2026.Photograph: Chris Ratcliffe/EPAView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham’s first cabinet meeting in Downing Street, London, 21 July 2026.Photograph: Chris Ratcliffe/EPAOpinionAndy BurnhamAndy Burnham is playing the long game in an age of short attention spansRafael BehrIt is hard to make the case for a 10-year plan in our political culture of constant campaigning frenzy – but the new PM will tryWed 22 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 08.41 EDTShareIt all sounded feasible but improbable. A few days after Labour’s disastrous performance in local and devolved elections in May, one of Andy Burnham’s allies confidently forecast for me the sequence of events that would convey the then mayor of Greater Manchester to No 10. A friendly MP would surrender a parliamentary seat. Keir Starmer would be persuaded to resign. Other leadership contenders would see the futility of resistance to the Burnham bandwagon. It would roll, unchallenged, up Downing Street. It was a plan with many moving parts, and a lot that could go wrong.That it all worked as predicted took more than luck. The Makerfield byelection was winnable for Labour only with theright candidateand a strong campaign. The scale of Burnham’s victory generated momentum sufficient to push other obstacles aside. The “Andy” brand was stronger than his party’s logo.Crucially forLabourMPs, Burnham was the author of his own success. Few said that of Starmer. He was the frontman for his 2024 general election triumph, not its mastermind. He didn’t like campaigning, wasn’t comfortable with it and outsourced the messy business of political organisation to others. He thought the size of his majority afforded him space to govern without having to keep explaining himself to the public or cluttering his schedule with party management. But he also seemed unsure of what he wanted to achieve with all that power.The contrast with Burnham is clear. Labour MPs find it reassuring to be led by a man who knows how to plan and who actually likes politics. The new prime minister is a vocal disparager of Westminster and its dysfunctional ways, but he isn’t a stranger to party machination. Unlike his predecessor, he doesn’t abhor discussion of ideas. Starmer, as one of his more loyal ministers once told me, “could only ever speak politics as a second language”.Burnham’s native fluency is an asset when it comes to navigating the labyrinth of Labour factions. It also makes him a better public communicator. The quality generally recognised as “authenticity” is not, as often construed, a gift that distinguishes natural leaders from “professional politicians”. It is professional performance at the highest level, so intuitive that personality fills the screen while calculation remains imperceptible.Nigel Farage is famously good at this. Burnham isn’t bad, although the comparison is unfair. The new prime minister’s style has to be more ponderous because his words are freighted with governing responsibility and liable to be mangled in transmission by a mostly hostile press. The Reform UK leader enjoys the levity of style that flows from indifference to consequence – saying anything one day, denying it the next – and from confidence that his message will be amplified by indulgent media.Or that is the service Farage has come to expect. He has hated the scrutiny of his personal and party finances in recent weeks. Questions about theprobity of his donorsand the legality of their munificence have turned his grinning bonhomie to a peevish snarl. The attempt to shut down the inquiry bycalling a byelectionin his Clacton constituency, framing it as a local plebiscite on his anti-establishment credentials, has backfired.The refusal of mainstream parties to field candidates leaves Farage shooing away pantomime challengers in an empty arena. The spectacle is demeaning for a man whose outsized influence in British politics is derived from adeptness at commanding attention, especially on the campaign trail. He deployed his favourite weapon to get out of a jam, and it fired blanks.The opportunity this presents to Burnham goes beyond the simple advantage of an opponent being distracted. The Clacton circus captures the raw cynicism of Reform UK’s political model. The party feeds on antagonism and dreads accountability.Farage reached for the byelection lever because his career is sustained by keeping politics whipped into a perpetual campaign frenzy. The battery of a populist movement needs a steady supply of public rage to stay charged. It cannot allow government any pause in political combat, when seeds of workable policy might have a chance to bear fruit. It has to deny the necessary time lag between inception of a plan and its delivery, filling every moment with the clamour for instant gratification and bitter recrimination.This is one reason why pragmatic incumbents struggle and ideological oppositions thrive in the digital age. There is no need to wait for an election to mobilise voter discontent when every day online is an angry referendum campaign with the question being whether things are better now than in an ideal world, or better today than they were in a misremembered, mythologised past. The answer is always no.Burnham seems to understand these constraints. He knows he has togive peoplesome “breathing space” on the cost of living straight away, and that this has to be seen as down payment on more profound improvements in collective wellbeing over time. He needs people to believe he is a man with a 10-year plan and that it is worth waiting to see if it can work.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThat leaves a lot of room for things to go wrong. No one is forecasting a sudden economic bonanza. Without one, Burnham’s hints of tax relief and universal social care point to a painful exercise in zero-sum budgeting. Resources will be siphoned from one Whitehall department to another. John Healey, thenew chancellor, will buy relief for voters in one area at a cost in fresh disappointment elsewhere. Pleasing one Labour caucus brings dismay to a rival faction.Labour’s radical left is always on a hair trigger, poised to denounce compromise as betrayal of socialist principle. Now there is also a small but aggrieved band of Starmer loyalists in exile poised to highlight (or relish) missteps and vulnerabilities. Darren Jones, sacked from his role at the Cabinet Office, wasted no time beforeprobing a budget holein the prime minister’s announcement of a VAT cut on energy bills, supposedly funded by scrapping thedigital ID scheme. The Burnham bandwagon will lose momentum quickly if that is a guide to the coming rate of backbench friction.The new leader needs a united party if he is going to revive wilted confidence that Labour is capable of fulfilling people’s everyday aspirations. Harder still, he has to restore flagging faith in democratic politics as the method. He needs to generate patience in an intemperate age. He has to stretch his meagre ration of goodwill, the honeymoon portion granted to a new prime minister, and make it cover a long period when things will not feel much better. In the era of politics as relentless campaign mania, when attention spans are short and incumbents are an easy target, Burnham must somehow earn voters’ trust and hold their gaze while deferring their gratification. Does he have the rare political talent to pull that off? It sounds improbable, but at least feasible.Rafael Behr is an Guardian columnistExplore more on these topicsAndy BurnhamOpinionLabourcommentShareReuse this content

title: My friends left Ukraine after the Russian invasion. Now I wonder if we live in the same world | Diana Berg | The Guardian
description: Anger, guilt, shame: the deepest unseen wound of this war is the rift between those who left and those who stayed behind, says Ukrainian writer Diana Berg
Author: Diana Berg
Headline: Anger, guilt, shame: the deepest unseen wound of this war is the rift between those who left and those who stayed behind, says Ukrainian writer Diana Berg
Content: People take shelter in a Kyiv metro station after a  Russian strike on Ukraine.Photograph: Danylo Antoniuk/EPAView image in fullscreenPeople take shelter in a Kyiv metro station after a  Russian strike on Ukraine.Photograph: Danylo Antoniuk/EPAOpinionUkraineMy friends left Ukraine after the Russian invasion. Now I wonder if we live in the same worldDiana BergAnger, guilt, shame: the deepest unseen wound of this war is the rift between those who left and those who stayed behindWed 22 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 04.07 EDTShare“Can we reschedule?”Over the years of working with western colleagues, I had learned to accept that they don’t fully understand what war feels like from the inside. They would ask me to find someone in occupied Mariupol for an interview, or seem genuinely puzzled that I couldn’t just log on at a time that suited them. I made my peace with this long ago – they don’t have to understand. But this time, it was a Ukrainian who asked.It was winter, the kind where every online meeting had to be carefully scheduled aroundpower outage timetables– and sometimes around shelling timetables, as we had already begun to joke among ourselves in that dark way. The question about rescheduling the meeting came from a colleague who had left for Europe in 2022, and it hit me almost physically, precisely because she was the one to say it. That innocent query had no business feeling like an accusation – and yet it did. For a long time, I couldn’t understand why.We knew that war would bring pain and rupture. But there are ruptures no one warned us about – ruptures that are quiet and slow, and for that reason the most devastating. One of them runs not betweenUkraineand the world, but through Ukrainians ourselves – between those who left and those who stayed. Almost no one talks about it. But it’s there, and with every passing month it grows wider.I would look at a friend’s social media post about a strike onKyivand realise that for them, the story began when it appeared in their feed. For me, it had begun much earlier – with the siren in the middle of the night, the explosions, the corridor where we had sat huddled with family, the silent wait to find out who hadn’t made it this time. And sometimes I would think:”Oh, come on, what hypocrisy – to share a post about my city burning and then close the tab and go on with your lovely day.” I knew this was unfair. But the thought came anyway.I understood something else the summer I managed to get away to another part ofEuropeand meet up with friends. We were sitting on a terrace in one of those beautiful cities where life looks orderly and safe, talking about work and plans. And then someone’s phone went off – the air raid alert – and within seconds everyone else’s followed. In Ukraine, everyone gets the sirens on their phones, but here, in this landscape, it sounded so wrong that we all fell silent, as if someone had cut the sound. Rita looked at her phone first and said: “Drones over Kyiv.”A minute later came another alert – a MiG had taken off, cruise missiles launched, ballistic threat. I get these notifications every day, but in that moment I felt my heart drop, because in Kyiv there are family, people I love. Fear for them came over me suddenly and completely. And then I looked at my friends and saw on their faces exactly the same thing – like a mirror I hadn’t expected to find. The same fear, the same helplessness of someone who is far away and can do nothing. That was the first time I allowed myself the thought that maybe they were living through this war too – just differently. Through screens, through the helplessness of watching from afar and feeling they have no right even to their own fear. It occurred to me that perhaps neither group was misunderstanding the war. Those who left and those who stayed were simply no longer living in the same version of it.I hear about their struggles – the bureaucracy, the loneliness, the uncertainty of being a refugee. And at the same time, I catch myself thinking: “God, how lucky you are. Not that your problems aren’t real, but they exist inside safety.” And underneath that, a quieter and uglier thought: “Do you think there is something I can do to help?” I never say any of this out loud. But the thoughts keep coming back, and with them the shame that they come at all.A colleague – also Ukrainian, also among those who left the country after the full-scale invasion – was explaining why she hadn’t done her part of a joint project: depression. My first thought was ugly and I knew it: “Only I get to make excuses here. Are you really complaining about your depression while we are being shelled?” And then came the shame.But I wasn’t prepared for what came next. “How long can people keep using the war to make the rest of us feel guilty?” my colleague asked.The remark stayed with me, not because it offended me, but because it frightened me. I had long ago started to feel ashamed to mention too often the reality I was living in, worried it would sound like guilt-tripping, like competing to be the one suffering the most. Hearing it from her, from someone also Ukrainian, also inside all of this despite being in a safe country – it felt like a blow from one of our own.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThis is EuropeThe most pressing stories and debates for Europeans – from identity to economics to the environmentPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionWhen my mother comes back to Kyiv, I think: “You’re here, you’re in the same world as me.” And then: “I am happy that someone I love has travelled from safety into a city that gets bombed regularly. I’m self-centred.” And immediately: “God, I’m such an egoist.” Then she leaves again, and so do all the others who have managed to visit Ukraine. They head back to Kraków, Berlin, Vilnius. I look at photos from their spontaneous weekends, their last-minute concerts, and I don’t just envy them. I’m angry. Every trip I take is like a military operation: no flights out of Ukraine, hours to the border, permits, logistics. Why is it so easy for them? Why not for me? I know why. That’s what makes it worse.Once we lived in different cities but in the same world. Now my mother is in Bulgaria, Dasha is in Poland, Ira is in Germany, Katya is in Austria. The map of closeness has shifted so quietly that I didn’t notice until it had already changed. I don’t miss life before the war – I miss the feeling that the people I love are living in the same reality as me. Maybe that’s why my thoughts are so full of “I” and “they” – and so short on “we”. Not because we stopped loving each other, or because anyone chose the wrong side of a border, but because war has a way of placing people inside different versions of the same story.And perhaps that is why I am always waiting for the next meeting on a terrace. Not in Berlin or Warsaw or Vienna. Here.Diana Berg is a Ukrainian artist, writer and cultural manager from Mariupol, and the founder of Platform TU, an independent cultural initiative now operating in exile after Russia’s full-scale invasion.Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsUkraineOpinionEuropeKyivcommentShareReuse this content

title: A 12-year-old casualty of war recovers in hospital: Alessio Mamo’s best photograph | Art and design | The Guardian
description: ‘Manal suffered severe burns to her arms and face after a missile explosion in Iraq. Yet in many ways she seemed just like a regular young girl’
Author: Charlotte Jansen
Headline: ‘Manal suffered severe burns to her arms and face after a missile explosion in Iraq. Yet in many ways she seemed just like a regular young girl’
Content: ‘I feel a huge amount of anger seeing children suffer’ … Alessio Mamo’s Manal, War Portraits.Photograph: Alessio Mamo/ReduxView image in fullscreen‘I feel a huge amount of anger seeing children suffer’ … Alessio Mamo’s Manal, War Portraits.Photograph: Alessio Mamo/ReduxMy best shotArt and designA 12-year-old casualty of war recovers in hospital: Alessio Mamo’s best photograph‘Manal suffered severe burns to her arms and face after a missile explosion in Iraq. Yet in many ways she seemed just like a regular young girl’Interview byCharlotte JansenWed 22 Jul 2026 10.48 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWhen I started to take photographs about 18 years ago, I was more interested in the aesthetics. Then came a moment when I realised the power of the camera as a tool. On 3 October 2013 there was a huge shipwreck off the coast of Lampedusa. More than 250 migrants from Eritrea lost their lives. I was sent to cover the event, and it was during that assignment that I understood I was interested in telling stories about social injustice, about people’s lives post-conflict.At first I worked mostly on stories about refugees in Sicily, where I am from, but a few years later I had the opportunity to travel to the Middle East with a friend, the journalistMarta Bellingreri, who is an expert on the region. She was living in Jordan and I joined her there for six months . That is how our project,The Hospital of All the Wars, about the patients at the Médecins Sans Frontières Al-Mowasah hospital in Amman, began.At Al-Mowasah hospital they treat patients from across the region:Iraq, Gaza, Yemen, Syria, Lebanon. They agreed to let us spend several months there; we would visit three times a week. We knew we needed time to tell the stories of these people, to spend time with the patients, their families, the doctors and nurses. We got to know Manal - she and her mother already knew about our project and we decided to ask if she was happy for us to photograph her.double quotation markManal had many friends at the hospital, and loved telling storiesShe is a beautiful, amazing girl. She was 12 at the time. She has suffered severe burns to her arms and face after a missile explosion in Kirkuk in Iraq. She had extensive surgery and needs to wear the mask you see in this picture for around five hours a day, to protect her face from light. You can also see in the room around her the drawings she made, her balloon under her arm. Manal had many friends at the hospital, and loved telling stories, as well as taking part in the many organised activities for children there.Like many of the patients, she was undergoing long-term treatment and living in the hospital for a number of months. But in many ways she seemed to me like a regular young girl. We had a great connection with her and her mother, and the shoot was like a game with Manal. Her mother offered us Iraqi sweets and biscuits. The portrait was made quite spontaneously. It won an award at theWorld Press photography prize in 2018.I am very grateful to Manal and I am still in touch with her and her family – last year I went to visit her in Kirkuk, where she is living with her uncle. She is 19 now.This portrait affects me particularly powerfully as even today, several years after the work I did in the MSF hospital in Amman, children remain among the primary victims of conflicts.Today more than ever, having recently become a father, I feel this aspect of children’s suffering in conflict zones is increasingly urgent. This is why I will soon be travelling to Beirut with the Guardian to work on a project about children harmed by the ongoing war. A huge number have been wounded in recent months in Israeli attacks.Of course, this is a subject that is really difficult to photograph. I feel a huge amount of anger seeing children suffer. I try to channel what I see, and the anger I feel, into my work, while always trying to do my best in the hope that these stories can raise awareness among as many readers as possible.It’s important to keep telling these stories because the moment we stop, and allow them to go unheard, we will live in a world where nobody cares about the suffering of the less fortunate. And that is not a world I want my daughter to grow up in. My hope is that my work, along with that of many photojournalists covering similar issues, can plant small seeds and open small windows of awareness – seeds that might one day take root and build a critical mass.View image in fullscreenPhotograph: Oleg SolokhaAlessio Mamo’s CVBorn:Catania, Sicily, 1976High point:Winning two World Press Photo awards. The first in 2018 with this picture of little Manal.Top tip:Give yourself five years before giving up the job!See more at Alessio Mamo’swebsiteor onInstagramand forthe GuardianExplore more on these topicsArt and designMy best shotPhotographyRefugeesIraqfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Pass the pit diapers! Why are fans wetting themselves to stand front row at concerts? | Music | The Guardian
description: Olivia Rodrigo, Noah Kahan and Morgan Wallen have all dealt with audience effluence in recent weeks – but that’s only one part of a fearsome subculture that has built up around fandom, endurance and ‘getting barrier’
Author: Kate Solomon
Headline: Olivia Rodrigo, Noah Kahan and Morgan Wallen have all dealt with audience effluence in recent weeks – but that’s only one part of a fearsome subculture that has built up around fandom, endurance and ‘getting barrier’
Content: ‘An experience I have smelled’ … Olivia Rodrigo performing at Roskilde festival in 2025.Photograph: Colin Darbyshire/Alamy Stock Photo/Alamy Live News.View image in fullscreen‘An experience I have smelled’ … Olivia Rodrigo performing at Roskilde festival in 2025.Photograph: Colin Darbyshire/Alamy Stock Photo/Alamy Live News.MusicPass the pit diapers! Why are fans wetting themselves to stand front row at concerts?Olivia Rodrigo, Noah Kahan and Morgan Wallen have all dealt with audience effluence in recent weeks – but that’s only one part of a fearsome subculture that has built up around fandom, endurance and ‘getting barrier’Kate SolomonWed 22 Jul 2026 05.39 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 06.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe front row of a gig is a sacred space. When you love an artist, you want to be as close to them as possible, without a view obscured by someone else’s hairdo. You want the possibility that the artist will pluck you from the crowd, form a deep and enduring connection and end up marrying you. Or at least notice you.Last month,Olivia Rodrigorevealed how acutely she had noticed some of the fans lining the barriers at her shows. “People wear diapers so that they can be front row,” she said, adding, to everyone’s absolute horror: “That’s been an experience as a performer that I have smelled.”This is no aberration, and while genuine medical needs should be taken into account, situations down the front have clearly got out of control. Also last month, video of a very stoic stadium worker cleaning up human excrement at aNoah Kahangig went viral and prompted Kahan himself to post on X: “If you have to poop at a show please dear god just go to the bathroom lmao.” This week, a fan of US country star Morgan Wallen complained that he, his brother and his niece were urinated on by another concertgoer, and police are investigating. Must we be subjected to headlines such as “Someone Also Shit Their Pants at a 5 Seconds of Summer Concert”? And in 2024, someone urinated on the floor of the standing section of a Sabrina Carpenter concert, causing a run on afairly horrifying item called a “pit diaper”, whose mere existence speaks to a wider problem.View image in fullscreenMorgan Wallen on stage.Photograph: Jason Kempin/Getty Images for ABASince the pandemic, when music lovers missed out on two years of live shows and young fans were forced to wait for their first gig experiences, those hard-won spots on the front row have become more and more coveted. The cost of VIP pit tickets has rocketed, stan culture has grown ever more feral, and the social media value of scoring clear gig footage is at a premium, partly because surprise celeb cameos – as in Rosalía’s confession booth, Charli xcx’sApple danceor Sabrina Carpenter’s on-stage “arrests” – are now built into many pop shows. So as well as the adult nappies, today’s barrier culture is a world of strict fan-run queue conventions, cross-venue sprints and specialist hydration systems.In an effort to find someone who would admit to even considering wearing a nappy, I popped down to Wembley Stadium on night 11 of Harry Styles’ recent 12-nightTogether Togetherresidency. The dazzlingly sequinned girls I spoke to weathering the heatwave to queue for the final section of floor standing were mostly horrified by the question. “I think that’s a bit ridiculous,” said Eloise, 23, having once queued for 10 hours to get the barrier at Harry’s last run of Wembley shows. What will you do if you need the toilet? I asked Shannon, who was hoping to leg it to the barrier when doors opened: “Hope for the best!”View image in fullscreen‘It’s a bit ridiculous’ … Eloise.Photograph: Hannah Cauhepe/The GuardianNineteen-year-old Keeley, who started queuing at 11am, said that she, “personally”, wouldn’t wear a nappy. “That’s not for me. Especially in this heat. It’d feel really unnatural to wee just in front of everyone.”But if you’re paying hundreds of pounds for a show and are queuing for hours to be mere metres from Rodrigo or Styles, it does make a warped kind of sense that you might take the nappy route rather than risk missing a song or losing your spot at the front. On TikTok, plenty of Swifties claimed to have worn one to the Eras tour (and with shows of more than three hours, you’d assume evenTaylor Swifttook a loo break). But it’s hard to track down anyone who will admit to a Guardian journalist that they wore a pit diaper in order to keep their coveted front-row view.When Katie, a seasoned Swiftie, planned her route to the Eras tour barrier, she forwent pissing herself in a crowd. Instead, she developed a hydration strategy that kept her show-ready but dry as a bone. “I drank loads of water the day before and then as soon as I got up at 4am to queue for the barricade, I had like two pints. I kept drinking until around midday then had a hard cutoff.” Energy gels, glucose tablets and mid-show waters handed by security over the barricade kept her from passing out – a sure-fire way to lose your spot. “No point in getting barricade if you faint during the first era!” she added brightly.View image in fullscreenTaylor Swift performing in Amsterdam – hopefully not singling out a nappy-wearing fan.Photograph: Hollandse Hoogte/REX/ShutterstockThere is a long tradition of people (mostly cis men, let’s be real) pissing into pint-glasses in crowds – and in one particularly horrifying Primavera crowd, directly on to my friend’s leg. Concerts by US psychedelic rockers Phish have becomenotoriousfor fans supposedly pooping with gay abandon since aninfamous incidentin 2015. You can see how it might happen in a boozed-up festival crowd or somewhere drugs are being liberally consumed. And perhaps the Phish experience is so transcendent it elevates the listener outside the workings of their gastrointestinal tract. More desperately, fans could argue they are counterculturally rejecting toileting norms, though this behaviour is actually relatively mainstream: urination was apparently rife as far back as concerts by Frank Sinatra and the Beatles.If you are minded to just let go, the potential for it to backfire is huge. “I tried this with a sanitary towel and it didn’t work,” confesses one gig-goer, insisting on anonymity for reasons that will become obvious. Let’s call her Fiona. She and a friend had travelled to see Arctic Monkeys in Finsbury Park in 2014. Fiona was a spritely 15-year-old who had got her hands on a bottle of rosé and reached the barrier right in front of where Alex Turner and co were soon to be asking Do I Wanna Know? The answer here is an emphatic: no you don’t.“I was determined to stay there,” she says, bodily functions be damned. “I had some sanitary towels in my bag so I thought I could discreetly wee into one. But it just overfilled because, unsurprisingly, pads can’t hold a rosé-induced wee’s worth of liquid.” With huge wet patches all down her black skinny jeans, she had to shimmy her piss-soaked way out of the crowd to find a portable toilet to deal with everything. “The worst thing was, when I tried to squeeze back through to my place, no one believed that my friend was waiting for me at the barrier.” It’s hard to believe that really was the worst thing about the situation.Toilet troubles are one thing; but just “getting barrier” in the first place is a whole other matter. The Harry Styles fans queuing in the heatwave had numbers written in Sharpie on the backs of their hands as part of a fan-run system that is supposed to ensure that whoever queues the longest gets the best spots. It goes pretty far back down the queue, at least to the hundreds. The idea is that no one can push in – or at least that it’s obvious if they do – though no one was clear exactly what anyone would or could do about that.View image in fullscreenFans show their queue numbers ahead of Harry Styles’ concert.Photograph: Hannah Cauhepe/The GuardianThere are some obvious flaws in this system. I could have written a number on the back of my hand and strolled to the front, and who could deny me? It’s an honour code that some people just don’t abide by, even in England, the spiritual home of queuing. Videos of the walk into the stadiums for Styles’ shows often become popular on socials: the ones from the night I investigated had just as much panicked jostling and yelling as there would have been without the numbers.Fans of all stripes have always staked out venues to sprint in and nab a space at the front – the forthcoming documentary Metallica Saved My Life shows the every-man-for-himself combo tactic of camping out then legging it in – but the newly regimented, numbered, self-policing approach seems to have stemmed from teenage girls. Anecdotal evidence suggests these “roll calls”, as fans call them, started proliferating in the mid-2010s. “Fans policing the order in which they’re in the queue to get into the venue first has always been a thing,” says Hannah Ewens, author ofFangirls, in which she documents how the teen fangirl queue community has its own microcommunities, complete with influencers. The spirit of the community, infused with a deep love for the artist plus the need for a decent view – teenage girls tend to be among the smallest in any given gig crowd – is all part of the barrier-questing experience.View image in fullscreenStyles fans ready themselves for the dash to the front.Photograph: Hannah Cauhepe/The GuardianBut while it’s always been about getting to the front, getting a spot on the front row has also become a lot more about clout. “TikTok has made it so much more rewarding,” Ewens adds. “It’s ramped up a sense of competition.” Getting to the barrier could be the difference between your videos of the show going viral or disappearing into the digital ether, and that gives the numbering systems even more perceived weight. “Fandom is about being in a community and being part of something, but it’s also very hierarchical,” Ewens says. “Queuing scratches both those itches for fans.”Some artists have made it clear that fan-run queue systems will not be entertained. For Bring Me the Horizon’s Manchester gig earlier this month – which had no barrier at all, just free-flowing rockers right up to the stage – the FAQs included a note: “Queueing will begin from 10am on show day … Fan numbering will not be acknowledged. Order of first entry will be selected at random.” The band and promoter would not comment on the decision, and whether or not it had any effect on the toileting habits of the crowd is unclear.Can we always be this close? What the hunger for intimacy with Taylor Swift says about fandom and girlhoodRead moreFandoms are starting to push back against fan-made queuing systems, too. Search Reddit or TikTok for this subject and you will find just as many people rejecting it as participating.Louis Tomlinsonbrought in randomised wristbands to take away the incentive to queue for hours for his shows earlier this year. Whatever time fans checked in, they were assigned a number to determine the order they could enter the pit. Venues including Wembley Stadium have also banned camping, making the process fairer for people who can’t give up several days to the cause.But despite the horrors of dirty nappies and enforced fan regimes, the barrier is generally a place of bonhomie. Friendships are made, core memories created. Whenever a member of BTS chucks a worn T-shirt to a specific fan at the barrier, no one around them tries to grab it (much as you know they desperately want to). If fans need help or water, those around them will signal to security to fetch it. And some of the photos and footage coming from front-row fans at this summer’s stadium shows are of professional-level quality. Whatever they did to capture them is between them, God and the nostrils of their favourite pop star. Even Fiona has no regrets: “It was worth it,” she says, 10 years removed from the wet jeans. “And I’m glad I have the story too.”Explore more on these topicsMusicPop and rockOlivia RodrigoNoah KahanTaylor SwiftBring Me the HorizonLouis TomlinsonfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘The door slid open and my jaw dropped’: the astonishing sculptures only ever seen by 20 people | Art and design | The Guardian
description: Ray Exworth spent his life filling Cornish sheds with breathtaking sculptures – but wouldn’t let anyone in to view them. Now, after his death, his secret body of work has been revealed
Author: Charlotte Jansen
Headline: Ray Exworth spent his life filling Cornish sheds with breathtaking sculptures – but wouldn’t let anyone in to view them. Now, after his death, his secret body of work has been revealed
Content: ‘One of the truly original sculptors of our time’ … Ray Exworth’s circus shed.Photograph: © Jem SouthamView image in fullscreen‘One of the truly original sculptors of our time’ … Ray Exworth’s circus shed.Photograph: © Jem SouthamArt and design‘The door slid open and my jaw dropped’: the astonishing sculptures only ever seen by 20 peopleRay Exworth spent his life filling Cornish sheds with breathtaking sculptures – but wouldn’t let anyone in to view them. Now, after his death, his secret body of work has been revealedCharlotte JansenWed 22 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 04.29 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleRay Exworth spent six decades creating one of Britain’s most extraordinary bodies of sculpture – and almost nobody has seen it. Hidden inside a labyrinth of sheds on a remote Cornish smallholding, his vast, obsessive constructions have remained locked away from the world, visited only by a fortunate few. Even his wife Susie, 92, was largely kept out. “I could not visit without an invitation!” she recalls.Exworth, who died in 2015 aged 84, had just one major exhibition in his lifetime, at the Whitechapel Gallery in 1975. Since then, the man who might belong among Britain’s most notable artists has disappeared almost entirely. His works – sprawling, intricate and wildly ambitious, assembled from an array of salvaged materials – have remained exactly where he left them: filling seven studio sheds and a scattering of shipping containers, unseen.A new book by photographer Jem Southam, Ray’s Sheds, provides unprecedented access to Exworth’s stunning work for the first time. Southam believes only 20 people have seen inside Ray’s sheds. His book is not only a detailed document of a exceptional art practice that may never otherwise see the light of day, but an intriguing inquiry into what it means to be an artist – and what the purpose of art is – when it is never publicly seen.View image in fullscreenWhat is the purpose of art when it is never seen … part of The Circus.Photograph: © Jem SouthamExworth was born in 1930 in Ipswich, the eldest of five sons. His father, Southam tells me, was a highly skilled but not well paid sandcaster, who would also make toys for his sons during the war. “Ray got his marvel at the skill and craft of making things from his father,” Southam says.The family’s home was hit in a bombing raid and burned down in 1941; then their next home was also bombed. These traumatic wartime experiences left a mark on Exworth – his sculptures return again and again to childhood memories and objects: clowns, dolls, and even the garden at the family home his father rebuilt from scratch once they were able to return. Exworth made an exact one-to-one replica of the garden from memory, a 60ft by 30ft floor-based sculpture complete with forks, spades, lettuces, flowering roses and bamboo beanpoles – all made of beaten lead. The work, one of three similarly massive lead pieces by Exworth, doesn’t appear in Southam’s new book – it’s so huge that it’s stored away in a container on the smallholding.Southam first met Exworth in the 1980s. One day Southam received a phone call from the artist inviting him to come and take photographs of his work. “I don’t know how he had heard about me, but I went slightly reluctantly to his tiny cottage. He took me through the tangled garden to the first shed – and as soon as the door slid open and I looked inside, like anyone else who has been there, my jaw dropped. What was inside was astonishing. I knew I was in the presence of a remarkable artist.”View image in fullscreen‘He found the act of making, far more rewarding than presenting’ … a larger-than-life clown.Photograph: © Jem SouthamThe first shed Southam encountered was known as the circus shed – a space vividly depicted in Southam’s photographs in the book, a sculptural environment (Exworth never referred to them as “installations”) stacked and piled from floor to ceiling with pieces crafted from whatever Exworth could get his hands on – plaster armatures, layered with newspaper and rags, metal, wood, textiles, glass, string and wax, all refined and finessed with ingenuity and skill. Full-size ringmasters, performers, horses, magicians – and a juggler with a frozen cascade of balls in the air are among the myriad figures in the wonderland. For the juggler’s balls, Exworth repurposed steel nails from wooden builders’ pallets, hammering them out and welding them together to create an arch, a painstaking problem-solving process that would have taken months. Exworth worked on The Circus six days a week for an entire decade.Exworth got the idea, Southam says, while working at the Meteorological Office (as part of his national service in the RAF), when he would pass through Piccadilly Circus regularly, at day and night. “Somehow that morphed into a real circus – wherever he started from, his imagination took over.”View image in fullscreenHe worked on it six days a week for an entire decade … part of The Circus.Photograph: © Jem SouthamEven Susie remembers being “awestruck by his work” the first time she entered the sheds, several years after Exworth started working in them. “I could not equate the man I knew in our daily life with the man he became when he entered the shed to work.” The photographs capture the low lighting and depth of the space, an unprecedented exploration of the endless mountains of works, some still in progress, partially covered in sheets.Across the seven sheds, each with their own atmosphere and theme, are life size, lifelike figures and animals, as well as miniatures, tableaux of found objects, ladders, old papers, boxes, buckets, complex assemblages of forms and materials, some indecipherable and unfinished. The workshop shed shows the scale and breadth of Exworth’s technical acuity and thinking – everything from drills, jigsaws, sanders and glass cutters to grinders, putty and tiny paintbrushes.double quotation markHe was a genuine artist. To him, making work was more rewarding than presenting itSoutham initially made 12 exposures on a plate camera for Exworth, for a grant application. The two became friends – but for the next 30 years, “no matter how much I tried, he would never let me in the sheds with a camera again”.It was only after Exworth died that Southam approached Susie to ask if he could make a photographic record of the work in the sheds, as a way of preserving it. The book took 10 years to make – Southam first shot everything he could on a digital camera, which allowed him to squeeze into the tight corners of the packed sheds, “but I was dissatisfied with the pictures because they didn’t really show what it felt like to be inside these spaces, surrounded by this work”. He returned with a huge, 8x10 Victorian box-like camera and tripod, “with an extremely wide, almost freakish lens” that allowed him to capture the spaces in a more encompassing way.Southam’s book includes around 60 pictures, as well as accounts of his personal experiences with Ray and in his sheds. There are many more works Southam couldn’t photograph or hasn’t included – such as 700 charcoal drawings Exworth made of the pair of blackbirds that visited the couple’s backdoor every day.David Heseltine was Exworth’s long-term friend and former colleague at Falmouth School ofArt– where Exworth was head of sculpture from 1959 to 1974. Heseltine remembers him as “well read and his art historical knowledge exhaustive. He found genius and inspiration in Henry Moore and Jacob Epstein, although his creative vision ran counter to the work of many of his contemporaries, who he thought lacked compassion.”When Exworth eventually quit teaching to devote himself entirely to his practice, he seems to have had few ambitions beyond quietly tinkering away on his own in his sheds, working on his wild visions – that perhaps transported him back to happier times.View image in fullscreen‘He had absolute certainty about his vision’ … the Night Table.Photograph: © Jem Southam“He was reclusive and, although described as irascible to the Arts Council purchasing committee, he was sensitive, compassionate and passionately interested in people and all aspects of life,” Heseltine says. “He had absolute certainty about his vision, but an amazing humility about what it achieved.”Ray’s Sheds, Heseltine adds, “is of course much more than an archival record and hugely important to Ray’s legacy. Given that the sculptures are almost immovable and that Ray was more concerned about making than promoting them, the book will be vital in introducing Ray’s work to a much wider audience, in acknowledging his importance, his contribution to 20th-century British art and establishing his rightful status as one of the truly original sculptors of our time.”The book is also about the unique, unwavering dedication of an artist solely to his craft without pretension. “For me, he was a really genuine artist – solving problems about how to make things. He was engaged in the activity of making, and found that far more rewarding than presenting it. Ray was just engrossed every day in how to move forward and make something.”As for Susie, she still goes into the sheds frequently – because, she says, “there is a strong feeling of Ray’s presence in each one”.Ray’s Sheds by Jem Southam is published by RRB PhotobooksThis article has been corrected: Exworth was born in 1930, not 1939.Explore more on these topicsArt and designSculptureArtfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: How Pokémon Go encouraged me to find wonder in the world | Games | The Guardian
description: These days, I ‘collect’ birdsong and wild flowers, but it all started when my sons and I first set out to find Pikachu and pals• Don’t get Pushing Buttons delivered to your inbox? Sign up here
Author: Keith Stuart
Headline: These days, I ‘collect’ birdsong and wild flowers, but it all started when my sons and I first set out to find Pikachu and pals• Don’t get Pushing Buttons delivered to your inbox? Sign up here
Content: Surely we’ve caught them all? … Pokémon Go celebrates its tenth birthday.Photograph: Eduardo Muñoz/ReutersView image in fullscreenSurely we’ve caught them all? … Pokémon Go celebrates its tenth birthday.Photograph: Eduardo Muñoz/ReutersPushing Buttons newsletterGamesHow Pokémon Go encouraged me to find wonder in the worldThese days, I ‘collect’ birdsong and wild flowers, but it all started when my sons and I first set out to find Pikachu and palsDon’t get Pushing Buttons delivered to your inbox?
