Sign up hereKeith StuartWed 22 Jul 2026 10.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 10.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleBelieve it or not, Pokémon Go is10 years old this month. The wildly successful smartphone game, which allows you to track down lovable creatures in the real world using GPS and augmented-reality technologies, has reportedly been downloaded 800m times across 150 countries and regions. A trillion Pokémon have been caught so far – and I have been responsible for a modest percentage of that figure.When the game was first released, my sons and I spent many happy hours wandering the streets and parks of Frome searching for Jigglypuffs and Charizards, and the game’s social element, which lets you take on gym battles with other players in your vicinity, offered my autistic son a route to communicate with other children in a way he’d never been able to before.My boys have grown up now, but I enjoy the fact that the game is still seducing new players into searching their local shopping centre for Machops. There’s a part of me that never really let go of this compulsion, although it’s now evolved. I have a folder of apps on my phone that I have ready whenever I go out for a walk, or visit an unfamiliar town. Seek isa free appthat uses image recognition to help you identify plants, trees and animals, and it lets you collect species badges to celebrate new discoveries. The accuracy can be a little variable, but it has given me a new interest in wild flowers and trees, and, being a nerd, I like to imagine which particular species would win in a Pokémon-style battle. How would red valerian fare against field bindweed? What would be the special attack of the common dog violet?Another favourite is theMerlin Bird IDapp, developed by the Cornell Lab of Ornithology. It lets you identify birdsong wherever you are in the world, and provides a “bird of the day” to look out for. My wife and I use it competitively, sending each other screenshots of unusual birds we’ve heard on our travels. I can now fairly confidently identify at least 10 bird songs, and I have to admit, I do associate certain birds with specific Pokémon. Pikachu? The yellow hammer, of course. Corviknight is the raven. Pidgeots are self-explanatory.View image in fullscreenThe new Pikachu … the Merlin Bird ID app lets you identify birdsong, like that of the yellow hammer.Photograph: Ernie Janes/AlamyAlongside these, I also loveFlightradar24andMarineTraffic, which track airplane and ship movement respectively. I like to know where things are going and why, and to play Top Trumps with the vehicle data, pitting nearby cargo ships against each other in imagined naval battles based on their size and carrying capacity.I’m not sure I’d be using any of these apps if its wasn’t forPokémon Go. It opened up in me an understanding of the natural world as a vast playable space, filled with beautiful little signs and systems. When you explore a new game world, everything you encounter is potentially interesting. You have no filter of expectation – in terms of executive function, it’s like being a toddler again. As you get older, you lose that fascination with the minutiae of the world around you – you go for dramatic vistas, rather than the patches of weeds along roadsides; the dawn chorus over the solitary songbird in your garden. Sometimes you need a little prod to bring that back.During the summer of 2016, and for a few seasons after, every walk we took became an adventure. Mundane places we knew well suddenly bristled with possibilities, boring landmarks became battlegrounds, strangers became allies. I just took all that and applied it to the real things around us – the birds in the woods near where we live, the weeds in our overgrown garden, the planes overhead. I’m not alone in this. Inspired by Pokémon Go, Brisbane student Kris Norris has created a social discovery game based onleaving messages on payphonesaround the city. Hydrologists in Colorado used a Pokémon Go-style app todiscover and track small streams.I’ve also discovered other location-based experiences along the way, that I might not have thought about otherwise. The fitness gameZombies, Run!was better for me as a running tool than Couch to 5k;Pikmin Bloomis a delightful floral walking game, andGeocaching, where you track down real-life caches hidden by other players, has tricked me into a few long hikes.What I have learned, or at least had confirmed, since playing Pokémon Go is that all video games have the potential to open new intellectual doorways – new ways to envisage the world and how it works. No time spent in play is wasted. Wonder is a transferable skill.What to playView image in fullscreenPerfect for a cosy evening … The Mermaid Mask game.Photograph: SFB GamesMystery games are having a real moment, but brothers Tom and Adam Vian have been quietly perfecting their Detective Grimoire series for more than a decade. I’ve eagerly awaitedThe Mermaid Mask, in which the titular detective and his smarter sarcastic sidekick Sally try to solve a locked-room murder aboard a submarine, and it does not disappoint.I always recommend playing these games with a loved one – not because they’re difficult (the puzzles are about as taxing as you’d want for a cosy evening) but because the fully voice-acted, laugh-out-loud script makes them just as fun to watch (and backseat drive) as to actually play.Jordan Erica WebberAvailable on:PC, Switch/Switch 2, PS5Estimated playtime:10 hours, best enjoyed over a few eveningsskip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toPushing ButtonsKeza MacDonald's weekly look at the world of gamingPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionWhat to readView image in fullscreenAll the gear … Modern Warfare is the universe of choice for Paramount’s new Call of Duty film.Photograph: Activision BlizzardLast year, Paramount signed up to make aCall of Dutymovie. Now,the studio has confirmedthat it will take place in the Modern Warfare universe – which means contemporary weapons, hi-tech espionage and geopolitical slaughter. But who will play moustached SAS hero Captain Price? There are rumours Pedro Pascal is up for the role, but surely it’s got to be Henry Cavill?!So now it’s all over,it turns outEA Sports FC correctly predicted theWorld Cupwinner for the fifth year in a row. The development team ran a simulation of every group and knock-out match in the competition, where Spain emerged as the victor. I wonder if it also predicted thatEngland v Francewould be the most video game-like match of the tournament?On Saturday, GamesRadarran a feature interviewwith senior figures at Xbox, who confirmed the importance ofexclusive gamesto the console. “There has to be a reason for people to buy an Xbox,” said chief content officer Matt Booty, accurately. Gears of War: E-Day and Clockwork Revolution will be the first titles to be affected by the withdrawal from the multiplatform approach that saw titles such as Forza Horizon 6 and Sea of Thieves head to PS5.What to clickBub – living an artist’s dreams and dreads in New YorkApple Crumble: a tasty Knives Out-inspired mystery that lets you snoop on your own familyCastlevania: Belmont’s Curse – Konami classic rises again from Paris sewers and Joan of Arc is a bossD&D players raise millions in real-life campaign against ‘corporate elite’Denshattack! – time to get on board with kickflipping trains | ★★★★☆Splatoon Raiders – Nintendo’s inky shooter fades to grey | ★★☆☆☆Question BlockView image in fullscreenOne of life’s great teachers … There’s much to be learned from games like Super Mario Odyssey.Photograph: NintendoThis week, a philosophical question fromRichard:“What genuine life lessons have you learned from games? I often think of Verso’s advice to Lune inClair Obscur: Expedition 33, thatthe only voice that should be in your head is your own. Bonus hard mode: not just life lessons from dialogue or other ‘narrative’ parts of a game, but from thegamingaction itself. The hours I spent trying to beat the last level ofSuper Mario Odysseytaught me a lot about my flaws: panicking in the face of new situations, and stumbling over bits I had done umpteen times beforebecause I contemptuously thought I’d mastered them.”One lesson I took from platform games such as Impossible Mission and Jet Set Willy and later from stealth games, was to stop and observe any complex situation before leaping in. That has saved me a lot of trouble. I’ve also picked up little snippets of practical information that I’ll never get to use in real-life – I discovered the Scandinavian flick playing World Rally Championship (it’s a driving technique, not a haircut) and the Coriolis effect while playing the famed assassination mission inCall of Duty 4: Modern Warfare. Finally, as a novelist, I learned plenty of things about comedy writing fromThe Secret of Monkey Island– specifically the importance of understatement and how to set up a gag and then keep it running. The line “That’s the second-biggest monkey head I’ve ever seen” will always stick in my head in relation to both of those skills. I should have it crocheted and framed on my wall.If you have a question for Question Block – or anything else to say about the newsletter –email us atpushingbuttons@theguardian.com.Explore more on these topicsGamesPushing Buttons newsletterPokémon GofeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Tate desperately needs a radical overhaul – but will its new director really be an agent of change? | Art and design | The Guardian
description: From over-hyped shows to the woeful Turner prize, this huge institution has been beset by problems – yet new boss Jessica Morgan seems too much like a safe pair of hands to shake things up
Author: Jonathan Jones
Headline: From over-hyped shows to the woeful Turner prize, this huge institution has been beset by problems – yet new boss Jessica Morgan seems too much like a safe pair of hands to shake things up
Content: Capacious crisis … Tate Modern.Photograph: Alex Segre/AlamyView image in fullscreenCapacious crisis … Tate Modern.Photograph: Alex Segre/AlamyArt and designTate desperately needs a radical overhaul – but will its new director really be an agent of change?Jonathan JonesFrom over-hyped shows to the woeful Turner prize, this huge institution has been beset by problems – yet new boss Jessica Morgan seems too much like a safe pair of hands to shake things upJessica Morgan named new Tate directorWed 22 Jul 2026 09.32 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 10.11 EDTShareEven Tate knows it needs to change. This top-heavy montage of four museums really needs to scrutinise itself and ask hard questions about its aims and how it works – including why it often doesn’t. Apparently aware of the crisis, it hails its new director Jessica Morgan as “an agent of change”. Well that’s a relief. But is she really?Morgan is a highly rated curator of contemporary art, recently leading the Dia Art Foundation in the US to great acclaim and with an earlier track record atTate Modernitself. Yet these very strengths suggest she will stay within a groove that Tate is all too comfortable with. It’s unlikely she’s going to rush to put on a show about art and the Reformation, for instance.View image in fullscreenHighly rated … Jessica Morgan.Photograph: Gabriella Herman/Tate/PAThis capacious institution loves to identify itself with new art but owns a lot of the old kind too. The Tate collection includes British art since 1500, butTate Britainhas become chronologically parched in its exhibitions, rarely going back into the Tudor or Stuart ages and mostly focusing on art since the Victorians. At least Gainsborough gets a show next year, but seriously, Tate needs a proper plan for its older British art. I hope Morgan will give this thought despite her background.Ana Mendieta review – were she still alive she’d be at the forefront of art in this centuryRead moreNot that Tate Modern is perfectly fine and dandy. When things go right they can be magnificent, like the current exhibition of Ana Mendieta’s shamanistic, myth-making visions. Yet alongside it is the museum’sdisappointing, over-hyped Frida Kahlo showthat was obviously problematic from an early stage. The question here is why no one raised the alarm: why there was no pre-mortem before the critics pronounced their postmortems? The same goes forlast year’s dire Turbine Hall installationby Máret Ánne Sara. The faults of this installation must have been obvious but, somehow, nobody in Tate Modern seems to have said so.View image in fullscreen‘A rather cosy sideline’ … Tate St Ives.Photograph: Matt Cardy/Getty ImagesThese unforced errors, which have also happened at Tate Britain with itsludicrously over-attitudinising collection hang, suggest a real problem with self-questioning. Tate seems to have a culture of rigid self-belief and sees all criticism as enmity. This internal complacency or lack of democracy needs fixing fast. And the Tate brand itself needs work. “Brand” – there, I’ve said it. An art gallery should not be a brand. The transformation of Tate into its current form happened at the end of the 1990s in an era of hyper-marketing and PR-massaged cool. Today it’s almost as if Tate’s PR machine and branding determine its values: even the announcement of Morgan’s appointment comes with a long list of endorsements by artists – a totally unnecessary piece of pre-judging.Morgan has to totally rethink Tate (maybe even allow it to be called “the” Tate). The outlets need serious attention. I likeTate St Ivesbut it’s arguably a rather cosy sideline. And of course, there’s the annual agony – the moribund Turner prize. Tate is too set in its ways, too dogmatic about championing “groundbreaking” art – and its curators are very confident they know what that looks like. But the idea that a museum should take sides and strike attitudes is wrong.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toArt WeeklyYour weekly art world round-up, sketching out all the biggest stories, scandals and exhibitionsPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionTate needs to surprise us. Morgan will bring that fresh eye we are promised, even if she looks like a safe pair of hands who might bring more of the same. I hope she proves to be a revolutionary Trojan horse.Explore more on these topicsArt and designTate BritainArtTate LiverpoolTate ModernTate St IvesLondoncommentShareReuse this content

title: Go woke, get rich: why rightwing hate campaigns don’t affect the box office | The Odyssey | The Guardian
description: After a bigoted online backlash, led by Elon Musk, The Odyssey achieved a monster weekend – the latest example of a targeted takedown that didn’t work
Author: Jesse Hassenger
Headline: After a bigoted online backlash, led by Elon Musk, The Odyssey achieved a monster weekend – the latest example of a targeted takedown that didn’t work
Content: Charlize Theron, Zendaya, Anne Hathaway, Lupita Nyong'o, Samantha Morton and Mia Goth at the world premiere of The Odyssey.Photograph: Gareth Cattermole/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenCharlize Theron, Zendaya, Anne Hathaway, Lupita Nyong'o, Samantha Morton and Mia Goth at the world premiere of The Odyssey.Photograph: Gareth Cattermole/Getty ImagesThe OdysseyGo woke, get rich: why rightwing hate campaigns don’t affect the box officeAfter a bigoted online backlash, led by Elon Musk, The Odyssey achieved a monster weekend – the latest example of a targeted takedown that didn’t workThe Odyssey: jaw-dropping action, dark magic and dolphin cameos – discuss with spoilersJesse HassengerWed 22 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 06.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleElon Musk was a man on a mission. Yes, he’s supposedly intent on colonizing Mars, or the moon, or maybe, if you give him some more time to move the goalposts, just a really, really tall mountain, but this spring, a more pressing task presented itself, a sequel to his early-2026mission to post white supremacist rhetoric: take down Christopher Nolan’sThe Odyssey, which he decided was the latest of “woke Hollywood’s” attempts to spread racial animus (towards white people, of course) and win Oscars while doing so. Nolan, Musk explained to the trade publication Variety, was “pissing on Homer’s grave”, referring to the author of the film’s epic-poem source material.The Odyssey: jaw-dropping action, dark magic and dolphin cameos – discuss with spoilersRead moreAs the final phases of the movie’s promotional campaign ramped up, Musk posted dozens of times on his self-owned social media platform with grievances related to the casting of Lupita Nyong’o (a Black woman) and Elliot Page (a trans man) in the movie, often spreading false statements about this casting existing to satisfy new Oscar requirements. (As detailedhere, those new Oscar diversity requirements are extremely flexible, and often satisfied by studio-wide programs that have nothing to do with casting. The Odyssey did not need to hire Oscar winner Nyong’o to be eligible for awards.) Musk may be a divisive-at-best figure who large swaths of the population do not take seriously, but he commands enough attention and adulation from a vocal minority that plenty of online accounts were soon parroting his willful misunderstanding of Hollywood and The Odyssey. Many of them abruptly and hilariously zeroed in on the 2004 movie Troy – which the right flank of social media suddenly decided was a beloved masterpiece, rather than its own, far greater distortion of Homer’s epic poem The Iliad.This story has an ending even more familiar than a Musk-branded rocket crash: after weeks to months of rightwing outrage about the failure to select an exclusively Greek cast for a movie that also features a cyclops, sea monsters and a witch who turns men into pigs, The Odyssey has becomeone of the biggest hits of the summer, with over $260m in the bank already, well on its way to outgrossing the inflation-adjusted totals for Troy. (In raw dollars, it’s already made more in the US.) The rallying cries of “go woke, go broke” have been drowned out by demand to see Nolan’s fusion of old-fashioned Hollywood spectacle with less traditional forms of filming, editing and, yes, casting.It’s easy enough to point out that any doomsaying about the prospects ofThe Odysseywas stubbornly ignoring a litany of factors, including but not limited to arguably the most popular movie director currently working; a cast packed with big names across generational and racial lines; the fact that casting some non-white actors doesn’t exactly scream “rigidly enforced diversity” when three of the most prominent stars in the film are Matt Damon, Anne Hathaway and Tom Holland; and, in the week leading up to release, terrific reviews.It would also be simple, if self-aggrandizing, to point out that for general audiences, supposedly “woke” film critics appear to serve as a powerful backstop against anti-woke clicktivism. Hence The Odyssey (95% positive reviews on Rotten Tomatoes) being inured against controversy, whether real or imagined, while other rightwing enemies like Supergirl (53% positive), targeted for its star vaguely acknowledging the difficulties of being a woman, or Snow White (39% positive), targeted for its star being mixed-race and insufficiently deferential to the source material, having their defenses substantially weakened.There’s probably some truth to that, though it may be less to do with critics than an increasing preference for picking winners. The online buzz that seems to affect movies more than ever isn’t so much about wokeness as a movie’s perceived status. A movie like The Odyssey or Obsession overperforms, creating more excitement about its success, causing more overperformance; a movie like Supergirl can get tagged as a loser from when the earliest box office numbers come in (so, essentially, about 12 hours after release).Still, culture warriors love to claim victory wherever they can, so they will point to movies like Supergirl or Snow White as proof that audiences don’t want women to be superheroes, princesses to be anything other than white, or whatever other generalization they can come up with. That gloating doesn’t always tell the full story: after Snow White’s opening weekend, for example, the Hollywood trade publication Deadline – not exactly a leftwing outpost – dived deeper into the movie’s box office.Turns out, compared with the average 63-37 breakdown between “blue”-voting counties and “red”-voting counties for family films’ ticket sales, Snow White, at a 60-40 split, actually saw slightly above-average performance in red areas. Its mediocre numbers didn’t appear particularly political. “There was no red theater blockade due to any perceived controversies with Snow White,” analyst Steve Buck told Deadline at the time.View image in fullscreenMargot Robbie in Barbie.Photograph: Courtesy Warner Bros. Pictures/APEven some of this group’s highest-profile attempts at toldja-so don’t really compute in the long run. Ten years ago, the middling numbers for an all-female reboot of Ghostbusters led to no sequels, rightwing culture warriors declaring victory, and a return to the drawing board for a more reverent legacy sequel, 2021’s Ghostbusters: Afterlife. The result? A princely addition of $1.1m at the domestic box office (so essentially, flat attendance; possibly a little less, given five years’ worth of inflation) and a slight downturn internationally, for a small decrease. A 2024 follow-up performed near identically. Maybe those near-uniform numbers are just the baseline levels of interest in a new Ghostbusters movie, rather than a referendum on women in ghostbusting? After all, movies fromBarbietoSupermantoWickedtoSinners, all subject of various forms of rightwing grumbling about various forms of wokeness, have been big hits in recent years. This isn’t applied exclusively to rightwing-coded protests, either: Scream 7 drew angry online chatter from the left about a boycott because Melissa Barrera, star of the previous two films, was fired for speaking out about the suffering of Palestine at the hands of Israel. This did not appear to move the real-world needle, as the movie quickly became the biggest-grossing Scream yet (and without the benefit of good reviews to bolster it further).Sex, beds and grisly executions: what Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey changed, invented – and left outRead moreOf course, these cases are all still just individual movies. But broader studies also suggest that audiences aren’t especially put off by diverse casts – and may, in fact, be drawn in by them. Earlier this year, UCLA’s annualHollywood diversity reportfound that movies with casts between 40 and 50% Black, Indigenous and people of color (the right wing’s dreaded Bipoc) had the highest median domestic and international box office, echoing the fact that in the United States, about 45% of the population is Bipoc. This held true for theprevious year’s report, too. Basically, it’s hard to find much positive evidence of woke-specific avoidance of any movie. It just isn’t something that most normal people seem to think much about.None of this will prevent the next batch of half-manufactured, half-deluded rightwing outrage. We should be better at ignoring it by now, but given its marginal origins, people like Musk are well-positioned to amplify it into our feeds no matter how few people it represents. Meanwhile, self-styled tell-it-like-it-is doofuses like YouTuber the Critical Drinker will go back to pretending they just review movies and analyze culture, rather than trying to froth up outrage over an Oscar-winning Black actor playing a character who is beautiful. The scene will look more like Circe (Samantha Morton) massaging Odysseus’s men back into humans after transforming them into snorting hogs: “Back to your disguises,” she says drily of their human form. But maybe after the blockbuster success of this “woke” version of The Odyssey, those disguises will be a little harder to maintain.Explore more on these topicsThe OdysseyfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: The rise of the ‘hot literati’: how reading became sexy | Books | The Guardian
description: Celebrities are hosting literary clubs and designers are launching products emblazoned with the classics – but does reading’s trendy turn have a darker side?
Author: Michaela Makusha
Headline: Celebrities are hosting literary clubs and designers are launching products emblazoned with the classics – but does reading’s trendy turn have a darker side?
Content: Model Kaia Gerber, who runs the Library Science book club.Photograph: Edward Berthelot/GC ImagesView image in fullscreenModel Kaia Gerber, who runs the Library Science book club.Photograph: Edward Berthelot/GC ImagesBooksThe rise of the ‘hot literati’: how reading became sexyCelebrities are hosting literary clubs and designers are launching products emblazoned with the classics – but does reading’s trendy turn have a darker side?Michaela MakushaWed 22 Jul 2026 07.02 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 08.32 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFromcelebrity book clubsto the revival of theedgy literary party, being a book lover is in. Dior has launched new versions of their Book Tote emblazoned with the covers of classics such asUlyssesandDracula. Earlier this year, Coachcollaboratedwith Penguin to produce miniature literary bag charms – fully readable micro-versions of books, from I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings to Sense and Sensibility. Miu Miuhosts a Literary Club; Chanel itsRendez-vous littéraire.View image in fullscreenBeing bookish is now sexy, we’re told.i-D magazinerecently ran a piece on the Whitney Review – a literary journal based in New York – proclaiming its team part of the “hot literati reshaping criticism”. The founder describes the Review as “sexy, surprising, stubborn”, and its contributors “curious and beautiful” (corroborated by an accompanying shoot in which the Whitney team look more like a bunch of models than anything else). But the piece raised eyebrows in the comments section of i-D’sInstagram page: “the definition of vacuous and vapid”, writes one user; “No room for ugly literati, only HOT”, says another. Should we be sceptical about reading’s voguish turn?Writer and content creator Sihaam Naik believes reading has become “hot” because of the economic pressures faced by young people today: “Gen Z is a generation that will probably never be able to own houses, and so we’re more aligned with investing in taste. So you buy books, art books and niche ephemera to set yourself apart from peers or signal your taste.”Celebrity book clubsmay also be driving the trend, says Naik: the likes of Dua Lipa, Kaia Gerber and Reese Witherspoon share books by “writers who need their platform … It works so well, but it’s just unfortunate as a sign of the times”, reinforcing the idea that “something is only worth sharing online if you are conventionally attractive.” Focusing on how someone looks when reading as opposed to how they engage critically with the text inadvertently creates a new, exclusionary barrier around literary culture – you must be hot to take part.The trend no doubt has its origins in BookTok and Bookstagram, on which creators share aesthetically pleasing reading spaces, shelves full of beautiful books and special editions. Book creator Sol Noya (@booksconleche on Instagram) thinks that the trend is a good thing, “a welcome change from past stereotypes of readers being dull or odd, particularly given that there are still very significant percentages of the population that barely read for leisure at all.” According toYouGov pollingconducted last January, 40% of Britons had not read or listened to a book in the prior year.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toBookmarksDiscover new books and learn more about your favourite authors with our expert reviews, interviews and news stories. Literary delights delivered direct to youPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionView image in fullscreenDua Lipa speaks onstage for a book club event at The Chelsea Hotel, New York City, in December.Photograph: Arturo Holmes/Getty Images“Reading has a positive impact on critical thinking and empathy, which are two traits society needs desperately. So in that sense, reading being ‘in’ is something I’m in favour of,” she continues. However, she says the aestheticisation of reading focuses far too much on consumption; reading as a new form of “subtle wealth signalling”. “Having a huge book collection implies having the cash to spend on buying the books and space to store them.”Yet, in an age in which younger people are trying to find ways to disengage from the internet andgo analogue, there is a positive in having role models who encourage buying and reading books. Bookstagrammer Diana Clough (@literarydiana) tells the Guardian: “opting for analogue media over our phones is something we should wear with pride and feel sexy about – however genuine that might be.”“Visibly engaging with the written word stakes our claim in the joy of reading, reclaiming the narrative of it being a reclusive act. And it can very much be hot, as the popularity of romantasy attests to. If reading is trendy, then it will only encourage more readers, which is never a bad thing.”Naik, however, is a little more cynical: “I’m glad to see people treat bookworms and reading as worthy of being cool again, but is it a fleeting trend, and are we going to dunk on it next year? Who knows!”Explore more on these topicsBooksDua LipaDiorMiu MiuChanelReese WitherspoonfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘The magnificent, the magical, the marvellous!’ It’s TV’s happiest endings  | Television | The Guardian
description: Gold dances, the Good Friday agreement and standup superstardom … get ready to blub because here are the most heartwarming denouements in TV history
Author: Hollie Richardson
Headline: Gold dances, the Good Friday agreement and standup superstardom … get ready to blub because here are the most heartwarming denouements in TV history
Content: Comic effect … The Marvellous Mrs Maisel.Photograph: AmazonView image in fullscreenComic effect … The Marvellous Mrs Maisel.Photograph: AmazonTelevision‘The magnificent, the magical, the marvellous!’ It’s TV’s happiest endingsGold dances, the Good Friday agreement and standup superstardom … get ready to blub because here are the most heartwarming denouements in TV historyHollie Richardson,Lucy Mangan,Phil Harrison,Kate Abbott,Hannah J Davies,Rachel Aroesti,Alexi Duggins,Dylan B Jones,Ann Lee,Jack Seale,Stuart HeritageandMichael HoganWed 22 Jul 2026 07.01 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 12.02 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWarning: this article contains spoilersThe Marvelous Mrs MaiselWhat does perfect 50s housewife Midge (Rachel Brosnahan) do after her husband leaves? She gets drunk and flashes her boobs on stage while doing an impromptu comedy set. It’s impossible not to root for her from this moment on. With the help of her unlikely manager, Susie Myers (Alex Borstein), she embarks on a standup career with the odds constantly stacked against her. Finally, Midge makes her breakthrough moment happen on a late-night show.“I want a big life,” she tells the studio audience, who have just been howling at her jokes. “They say ambition is an unattractive trait in a woman. Maybe. But you know what’s really unattractive? Waiting around for something to happen.” All of her family members – who had been so against this career – are there cheering with pride. Gordon Ford (Reid Scott) then introduces her to the world: “The magnificent, the magical, the marvellous Mrs Maisel.”But the real gut-punch moment? When Midge looks over to Susie, who has a tear in her eye and her hand on her heart. The final scene jumps to decades later, with the hugely successful pair joking over the phone as they both watch an old episode of Jeopardy.Hollie Richardson30 RockView image in fullscreenFunny business … Tina Fey as Liz Lemon and Keith Powell as Toofer in 30 Rock.Photograph: NBC/NBCU Photo Bank/Getty ImagesThe brilliance of the30 Rockfinale – aired as two episodes back-to-back in 2013 – really begins halfway through the closing series. That’s when Liz Lemon gets married and embarks, via adoption, on motherhood, both milestones at which a traditional sitcom would usually end a show centred on a female protagonist. But not for Lemon, and not for creator Tina Fey, who ends it with TGS (the show within a show) being uncancelled and brought back for one last episode – an appropriately meta end for the show itself – so that everyone else’s storylines can be tied up. Liz is shown to be back at work and realising she needs to have work and not just family in her life or she’ll live on a knife-edge of insanity as much as she did when it was just work.It gave us callbacks (even unto St Elsewhere), the full gag quotient (plus The Rural Juror song), Kenneth’s ascendance (possibly transcendence), and closure. Even Pete got a brief year of happiness before the epilogue. Above all, it gave us Jack’s lovely speech to Liz from the boat, which she eventually interrupts to tell him she platonically loves him too. Affectionate respect has never been so beautifully captured. See-through dishwasher for all!Lucy ManganDetectoristsView image in fullscreenTime for the gold dance! … Lance (Toby Jones) and Andy (Mackenzie Crook) in Detectorists.Photograph: BBCThere was rarely anything tricksy about the narrative of Detectorists – it was based round a set of simple, binary storylines concerning relationships, parenthood and mild discord over metal-detecting territory. All the same, Lance and Andy were deceptively complex characters and the show’s seemingly still emotional waters ran deep. It soon became clear that we were being offered one of the most charming and convincing depictions of friendship in television history.The show’s conclusion was gently audacious, suggesting continuity as the happiest of endings. Andy and Becky buy a delightful, dilapidated house. Toni is preparing to move in with Lance. Andy and Lance don’t actually need anything they don’t already have. Friends, lovers, a pub nearby, modest roofs over their heads, a shared passion and perhaps most importantly of all, each other. Their gold dance is, at all times, right in front of them.Phil HarrisonDerry GirlsView image in fullscreenChoose yes! … Derry Girls.Photograph: Peter Marley/PA“If YES put an X in this square.” TheDerry Girlshad to grow up sometime, and so it came to pass, after Erin and Orla made it through the wildest 18th birthday/joint first communion party ever – theme: literary monkeys – that they all got to vote on the Good Friday agreement. For a show so consistently hilarious, Lisa McGee’s cracker comedy finally turned on a sixpence into the most beautifully poignant piece of historical analysis. I was weeping with joy from the moment Granda Joe says: “What if no one else has to die? What if all this becomes a ghost story you’ll tell your weans one day. A ghost story they’ll hardly believe.” By the time all of them chose yes, chose hope, chose to end the Troubles that were all they’d ever known, I was a goner. Of course it was soundtracked to the Cranberries – the only possible choice – and of course, in the ultimate cameo, Chelsea Clinton got their letter decades late in the closing seconds! As Michelle said, being a Derry Girl is a fuckin’ state of mind, and I’ll never stop loving them. Sláinte!Kate AbbottHacksView image in fullscreenShow stopper … Deborah Vance (Jean Smart) in the Hacks finale.Photograph: SkyIf you’ve watched the finale ofHacksthis might be a hard sell. Major spoiler incoming: the final episode of this biting showbiz satire ended with our Joan Rivers-inspired antiheroine Deborah Vance (Jean Smart) revealing a terminal cancer diagnosis, and planning her death by assisted dying. Fans had already noted the morbid foreshadowing all season, but it still felt like a brutal plot twist – not only for Deborah but for Ava (Hannah Einbinder), her protege, surrogate daughter and sometime pretend girlfriend.Frankly it was all a bit triggering, not just because I lost a parent to cancer, but because Hacks is really funny and this was absolutely the unfunniest thing that could have happened. But in a way – stay with me here – Hacks did have a happy ending. At the 11th hour, Deborah changed her mind and decided to hang on a little longer and write another comedy special, delivering one of the best lines in the whole damn series: “I may not have 30 years – but I think I have another hour.” I was, like most professional journalists, watching the finale from an airport Starbucks, ugly crying. When I heard that line I got instant chills. I stopped sobbing, let out a gasp, then started crying again – but this time it was happy tears. The thought of Deborah writing one final bitchy, tell-all special really made me smile.Hannah J DaviesOnly Fools and HorsesView image in fullscreenBro selecta … Only Fools and Horses’ Sleepless in Peckham.Photograph: BBCLike one of Del Boy’s dodgy deals, Only Fools and Horses gave us two happy endings for the price of one. The first was 1996’s iconic fairytale finale: an antique watch makes Del and Rodney millionaires – cue a fever dream montage of the brothers living in the lap of luxury. Yet for the elder Trotter, the giddy euphoria was tinged with wrenching nostalgia for his old life, resulting in a profoundly bittersweet conclusion that cemented the show’s place in the British pop cultural canon. Then came the 2000s reboot: three Christmas specials, widely derided as knock-offs. Still, the eventual denouement – which involves a more modest windfall, some closure for Rodney and a long-awaited baby for him and Cassandra – is actually more uplifting and heartwarming than the original. A less artful ending, undoubtedly, but I’m still glad we said goodbye with a more relatable kind of happiness.Rachel AroestiBrooklyn Nine-NineView image in fullscreenUp lifting … the Brooklyn Nine-Nine finale.Photograph: E4How to end one of TV’s most gleefully daft comedies? With a ludicrously elaborate set-piece episode, featuring the most OTT version ever of the annual event where the characters go all out to double cross each other in a heist – naturally. This double-bill farewell was a glorious exercise in fan-pleasing that packed in a whistlestop tour of the show’s most significant moments (the warehouse where they solved their first case! The interview room behind the best ever skit – wherePeralta made suspects sing Backstreet Boys!), joy-inducing returns of fave characters and the heartfelt emotional moment that had been brewing for eight seasons – Captain Holt telling Peralta he’d be proud to have a son like him. It all ended with a quiet, beautiful moment – the lights being turned out on the precinct as they stepped into the lift together for one last time. Then they threw in a bonus scene set a year later with the crew announcing that they’d get back together every year for their annual heist. Cue a last sketch, including bad magic, hammy acting and one final rousing call of the team motto: Nine-Nine! Now that’s how you say goodbye.Alexi DugginsSex and the CityView image in fullscreenFrench connections … An American Girl in Paris (Part Deux), the Sex and the City finale.Photograph: HBOThings weren’t looking great asSex and the Citycareened and Carried its way through its too-long final season. Even Charlotte’s dog was starting to look exhausted. But writer Michael Patrick King and this beautiful cast of women really pulled it out of the Birkin for the two-parter finale in which Carrie has to choose between moving to Paris with a handsome millionaire, or staying in New York with a different handsome millionaire (look, we’ve all been there).There’s breathy old Hollywood dialogue that somehow works. There’s Sarah Jessica Parker galloping across wet cobbles to moody French hip-hop. And if you didn’t get goosebumps from Miranda gripping Mr Big’s hairy, Rolex-clad forearm and gasping “Go get our girl!” you’re as dead inside as the second movie.Dylan B JonesParks and RecreationView image in fullscreenKnope for president! … Amy Poehler as Leslie Knope in Parks and Recreation.Photograph: NBC/Channel 4Unlike most TV “happy ever afters” which can seem rushed,Parks and Recreationtreats us to a two-part finale which jumps forward in time not just once but several times, as it lovingly explores what happens next to Leslie Knope and our favourite gang of Pawnee government employees.Each character gets their storyline tied up in a way that is entirely befitting of their personality and arc – and of course, it would end with Amy Poehler’s overachieving Knope possibly becoming president of the United States. We also finally get some karmic justice for Jerry/Garry/Larry, the perennial office scapegoat, who becomes a much-beloved mayor of Pawnee and lives to the ripe old age of 100.The ending is sweet, offbeat and supremely wholesome – all the things we’ve come to expect from Parks and Rec over the years. And this cuddly sitcom couldn’t have ended in a better way – as a tribute to the power of “friends, waffles, work” (Knope’s life priorities), right to the very last credits.Ann LeeMr InbetweenView image in fullscreenMaking a killing as a cab driver … Mr Inbetween.Photograph: FXSo many classic shows have been led by a monstrous male character we secretly root for – Tony Soprano, Walter White, Doc Martin – and the protagonist in Scott Ryan’s flawless crime drama is up there with the best/worst. Ray Shoesmith has bludgeoned and gouged his way through Sydney’s criminal underworld but, in the final episode, he’s given up killing to become a rural cab driver. When two hoodlums who mean Ray harm get in the car, Ryan makes a brilliant creative decision: the last shot is Ray breaking the fourth wall, eyeballing the camera and grinning. Ray knows what we want. He’s going to horribly murder them! Hoorah!Jack SealeThe LeftoversView image in fullscreenAbsolutely perfect … Kevin and Nora in The Leftovers finale.Photograph: HBOFrom the ashes of one of the bleakest television programmes ever made (in a nutshell: 2% of the world’s population goes missing, and those left behind go mad with grief) came a beautifully hopeful ending. A long-separated couple reunite. The woman says she visited an alternative dimension where the missing 2% live happily in peace. It almost certainly isn’t the truth, but the story brought her comfort. The man says he believes her. They cry. They hold hands. They smile. For a show that never had any intention of explaining its central mystery, this ending – an emotional truth landing harder than a logical one – was absolutely perfect.Stuart HeritageGavin and StaceyView image in fullscreenWell lush … Gavin and Stacey.Photograph: BBCIt’s the drama, Mick, I just love it. There’s a reason why the Anglo-Welsh romcom’s finale became Britain’s most-watched scripted show in more than two decades. Five years after Nessa proposed to Smithy, nuptials loomed – except it was boring Sonia who the lovable lummox was marrying, not his love-hate soulmate. What was occurring? After a spine-tingling intervention at the altar, Smithy called it off and dashed to Southampton docks, where Nessa was about to set sail on a cargo ship. Now it was his turn to pop the question. The pair married in Barry while 21m viewers wept with joy into their Quality Street tins. Tidy, cracking and well lush.Michael HoganShare your happiest TV endingYou can tell us your own happiest TV ending using this form.Please share your story if you are 18 or over, anonymously if you wish. For more information please see ourterms of serviceandprivacy policy.Tell us hereYour responses, which can be anonymous, are secure as the form is encrypted and only the Guardian has access to your contributions. We will only use the data you provide us for the purpose of the feature and we will delete any personal data when we no longer require it for this purpose. For alternative ways to get in touch securely please see ourtips guide.NameWhere do you live?Tell us a bit about yourself (e.g. age, background, what you do)OptionalTell us your happiest TV show ending and whyPlease include as much detail as possible.If you are happy to, please upload a photo of yourself hereOptionalPlease note, the maximum file size is5.7 MB.Choose fileCan we publish your response?Yes, entirelyYes, but contact me firstYes, but please keep me anonymousNo, this is information onlyPhone numberOptionalYour contact details are helpful so we can contact you for more information. They will only be seen by the Guardian.Email addressYour contact details are helpful so we can contact you for more information. They will only be seen by the Guardian.You can add more information hereOptionalIf you include other people's names please ask them first.Would you be interested in speaking to our audio and/or video teams?Audio onlyVideo onlyAudio and videoNo, I'm not interestedBy submitting your response, you are agreeing to share your details with us for this feature.SubmitShow moreExplore more on these topicsTelevisionTV comedyThe Marvelous Mrs Maisel30 RockHacksSex and the CityParks and RecreationfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Possible bloodstains were detected at D4vd’s home, law enforcement testify | Los Angeles | The Guardian
description: Gory details about tarp, mats and inflatable pool come to light during hearing over death of Celeste Rivas Hernandez
Author: Uwa Ede-Osifo
Headline: Gory details about tarp, mats and inflatable pool come to light during hearing over death of Celeste Rivas Hernandez
Content: D4vd in court on Tuesday in Los Angeles.Photograph: Bill Robles via APView image in fullscreenD4vd in court on Tuesday in Los Angeles.Photograph: Bill Robles via APLos AngelesPossible bloodstains were detected at D4vd’s home, law enforcement testifyGory details about tarp, mats and inflatable pool come to light during hearing over death of Celeste Rivas HernandezUwa Ede-Osifoin Los AngelesWed 22 Jul 2026 19.40 EDTFirst published on Wed 22 Jul 2026 14.09 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAt singer D4vd’s former Hollywood Hills home, law enforcement detected possible bloodstains – invisible to the naked eye – on items including a green tarp, black rubber mats and a crumpled inflatable pool that had the odor of fresh laundry.The gory details came to light on Wednesday, the second day of a hearing to determine whether there was probable cause for the musician, whose full name is David Anthony Burke, to stand trial for the murder and sexual abuse of Celeste Rivas Hernandez.Rivas Hernandez’s body was found last year in gruesome conditions in a car registered to the musician.Prosecutors accuse Burke of stabbing Rivas Hernandez, 14, to death at hisLos Angelesresidence to silence her from divulging the sexual nature of their relationship and jeopardizing his skyrocketing career.LA prosecutors present gruesome evidence in murder case against singer D4vdRead moreBurke has pleadednot guiltyto charges that include first-degree murder, “lewd and lascivious acts” with a child under the age of 14, and the mutilation of human remains. His defense team has said their client “was not the cause” of Rivas Hernandez’s death.Last September, Lauren Wallace, a criminalist with the Los Angeles police department, found two chainsaws in the garage of a Hollywood Hills home where the Houston-raised musician had been living. The chainsaws tested negative for possible blood.Rubber mats covering a garage floor, however, likely had blood stains, Wallace testified. Linear cuts, consistent with that of a sharp object, were found on the mats.Wallace also observed damage to the pool. “It appeared that it had been cut,” she said.Chelsea Murillo, another criminalist in LAPD’s forensic science division, examined the 2023 black Tesla Model X that contained Rivas Hernandez’s remains.A layer of dust coated the vehicle’s exterior, Murillo said on Wednesday, which suggested it had been outside for a prolonged period. In the front trunk, she observed maggots and flies. A strong odor that resembled a decomposing body came from the car, she said, even amid multiple air fresheners. Possible traces of blood were found in the Tesla’s third row.Defense attorney Blair Berk cast skepticism over the findings of the criminalists’ forensic testing.It’s “only presumptive. It doesn’t mean you found blood,” Berk asked during her cross-examination of Murillo.“Yes,” Murillo replied.The scope of the singer’s earnings also came into clear view on Wednesday, as prosecutors sought to establish the musician had financial motivation to kill Rivas Hernandez. Burke had a multimillion-dollar recording deal, an executive at his former label, Interscope, testified.The label terminated his contract in December 2025.From 2022 to 2025, Interscope paid Burke and affiliated entities more than $7.2m, a figure that encompassed legal fees and a trust mandated byCalifornialaw for underage entertainers, according to a summary of payments exhibited to the courtroom.Details of Burke’s compensation followed a testy exchange in the morning, as prosecutors countered the defense’s suggestion that their client was not privy to his finances.Attorneys for Burke repeatedly objected to questions from deputy district attorney Beth Silverman about whether the singer’s former financial manager, Benjamin Gregor, knew of Burke arranging tickets for Rivas Hernandez to travel to London and his home in Texas.Rivas Hernandez’s parents, Jesus Rivas and Mercedes Martinez, returned to court on Wednesday. Dressed in all black, the two sat in the first row, accompanied by their lawyer.Martinez dropped her head and wiped her eyes in the early afternoon, when prosecutors presented a photograph of her daughter’s passport, which a Caltrans employee discovered in January 2026 in a dirt embankment off State Route 154 in Santa Barbara.“Once he found the card, he Googled the name to try and see if it was someone that lived locally in the area. He began seeing articles and news stories and when he realized what he had found, he called our dispatch,” Sean Buenrostro, a California highway patrol officer, said on the stand.At the hearing’s start, prosecutors showed graphic photographs of Rivas Hernandez’s dismembered body and alleged the singer had severely mutilated her remains. They also presented evidence they say shows the singer used his credit cards to purchase chainsaws, a body bag and a shovel.The preliminary hearing is expected to last from three to five days.Burke, now 21, wasarrested in Apriland has been held in custody since then without bail. He has appeared in court in an orange jumpsuit and thick black glasses.If convicted of murder, he could face the death penalty, or life in prison without the possibility of parole.Prosecutors have not yet indicated whether they will pursue capital punishment.After court concluded on Wednesday, Patrick Steinfeld, an attorney for the Rivas Hernandez family, spoke to reporters outside.“It’s a hard thing for the family. They are very private individuals and they’re doing the best they can,” he said in response to a question about how Rivas Hernandez’s parents reacted to the evidence presented about the Tesla.“They have a hole in their heart that will never be replaced,” he said.The Associated Press contributed to this reportExplore more on these topicsLos AngelesCaliforniaWest CoastnewsShareReuse this content

title: Charles Gaines, whose book Pumping Iron helped to popularize bodybuilding, dies at 84 | Books | The Guardian
description: 1974 book led to making of seminal documentary and introduced Arnold Schwarzenegger to the world
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: 1974 book led to making of seminal documentary and introduced Arnold Schwarzenegger to the world
Content: Charles Gaines and actor Ethan Hawke support the call for a moratorium on oil and gas exploration in the Gulf of St Lawrence in October 2015.Photograph: Andrew Vaughan/The Canadian Press/APView image in fullscreenCharles Gaines and actor Ethan Hawke support the call for a moratorium on oil and gas exploration in the Gulf of St Lawrence in October 2015.Photograph: Andrew Vaughan/The Canadian Press/APBooksCharles Gaines, whose book Pumping Iron helped to popularize bodybuilding, dies at 841974 book led to making of seminal documentary and introduced Arnold Schwarzenegger to the worldAssociated PressWed 22 Jul 2026 13.58 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 14.13 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleCharles Gaines, the author whose book and documentary Pumping Iron helped bring professional bodybuilding into the mainstream and introduceArnold Schwarzeneggerto the world, has died. He was 84.Gaines died on Tuesday, according to Greta Gaines, the author’s daughter. She did not reveal a cause.“You always marched to your own drummer and inspired everyone around you to do the same,” she wrote in a tribute to her father. “Follow that bliss, catch that fish and kiss those lips while you can because my father was the live life to the fullest kinda guy.”Gaines collaborated with George Butler to write Pumping Iron: The Art and Sport of Bodybuilding, which led to the making of the documentary Pumping Iron, for which Butler and Gaines wrote the script.In the introduction to their 1974 book, Gaines wrote that he and Butler considered it “a sort of respectful report from the interior of an interesting, colorful and heretofore ignored region of American life”.In a 2022 edition, he admitted that neither author thought “many people outside of the bodybuilding world would be interested in” the book. Gaines wrote that Schwarzenegger almost single-handedly “brought bodybuilding out of the shadows it had languished in since its earliest days in America and into the limelight”.Schwarzenegger, who won seven Mr Olympia and five Mr Universe titles and who was pictured flexing on the cover of Pumping Iron, harnessed the attention into a career, first in films – including The Terminator, True Lies, Twins and Predator – and then politics, becoming governor of California from 2003 to 2011.Gaines wrote that he had been involved in bodybuilding in one way or another since he was 16, when he began lifting weights. Pumping Iron began as an article for Sports Illustrated in 1972 on a bodybuilding contest for Mr East Coast.“If we felt at times a little like 19th century explorers, it was because we found bodybuilding to be as primeval and unmapped as parts of Labrador,” he wrote. “Nobody, we discovered, had been back into it to send out a report on what it was like.”Gaines and Butler used the book as the basis for their 1977 documentary Pumping Iron, which centers on the rivalry between Schwarzenegger and fellow bodybuilder Lou Ferrigno in the competition for a Mr Olympia title. Ferrigno later starred on TV in The Incredible Hulk.Gaines was raised in Alabama and earned a bachelor’s degree from Birmingham-Southern College and a master of fine arts in writing from the University of Iowa.Gaines published numerous books throughout his career, including Waters Far and Near: Tales of Angling Adventure and Misadventure Around the World and the novels Stay Hungry, Dangler and Survival Games. Stay Hungry was adapted into a movie starring Jeff Bridges, giving Schwarzenegger his first movie role.“He climbed mountains, trekked across ice, fished, and hunted on almost every continent, writing about every adventure in a way that captured people’s hearts and minds. He was interested in everything – and his interest was contagious,” Schwarzenegger wrote on Instagram.“I’m lucky that he was always looking for new frontiers, because that’s how he found me and our niche sport of bodybuilding.”In addition to his daughter Greta, Gaines is survived by his wife, the artist Patricia Ellisor Gaines, and two other children, Latham and Shelby Gaines.Explore more on these topicsBooksArnold SchwarzeneggernewsShareReuse this content

title: Elon Musk says Grok Imagine will make ‘historically accurate’ AI adaptation of Homer’s Odyssey | Movies | The Guardian
description: The billionaire says the AI-generated film will stay true to Homer’s original, after repeatedly criticising Christopher Nolan’s blockbuster over its casting choices
Author: Andrew Pulver
Headline: The billionaire says the AI-generated film will stay true to Homer’s original, after repeatedly criticising Christopher Nolan’s blockbuster over its casting choices
Content: ‘Our cast is representative of the world’ … (from left) Jimmy Gonzales, Matt Damon and Himesh Patel in The Odyssey.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesView image in fullscreen‘Our cast is representative of the world’ … (from left) Jimmy Gonzales, Matt Damon and Himesh Patel in The Odyssey.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesMoviesElon Musk says Grok Imagine will make ‘historically accurate’ AI adaptation of Homer’s OdysseyThe billionaire says the AI-generated film will stay true to Homer’s original, after repeatedly criticising Christopher Nolan’s blockbuster over its casting choicesAndrew PulverWed 22 Jul 2026 08.11 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 14.33 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleElon Musk has said his AI platform Grok Imagine will make a “historically accurate” adaptation of Homer’s Odyssey, after the success of Christopher Nolan’s blockbusting treatment which the SpaceX founder has regularly criticised over its casting.In a post on X, Musk said: “Before this year ends, Grok Imagine will make a full-length movie of The Odyssey that is historically accurate and true to the art of Homer.” Musk also linked to a post containing a three-minute clip of footage that the user said had been generated from Grok Imagine, showing a scene between Odysseus and the nymph Calypso.Musk has been attempting to stoke a culture war around Nolan’s film, which stars Matt Damon as Odysseus and Anne Hathaway as Penelope, and wasreleased on Friday to near-universal acclaimand achievedimpressive box office results. Before the film was released,Musk reposted abusive messagesaimed at Nolan’s casting of Black actor Lupita Nyong’o as Helen of Troy and Clytemnestra, and trans actor Elliot Page as Sinon, adding that “[Nolan] wants the awards” and that “[he] has lost his integrity”.However,Musk’s efforts appear to have had little effect, with The Odyssey taking US$264m (£196m, A$378m) at the global box office in its opening weekend anda 97% audience rating on Rotten Tomatoes.In May, Nyong’o responded to rightwing critics, saying: “Our cast is representative of the world. I’m not spending my time thinking of a defence. The criticism will exist whether I engage with it or not”. More recently, Nolansaid the backlash “comes with the territory”, saying: “These conversations that happen before people see the film – they’re always irrelevant, because no one having them knows what the film actually is yet.”The Odyssey review – Nolan goes god-tier with breathtaking epic of men, monsters and moral metamorphosisRead moreThe director was also withering in his assessment of the likely impact of artificial intelligence on film-making, saying: “The idea that it replaces human beings wholesale and human creativity, to me it’s a nonsense.” He added: “I’ve never seen a technology that’s been so successfully adopted by Wall Street and by investors and by tech companies that the public has so thoroughly rejected.”Explore more on these topicsFilmThe OdysseyHomerChristopher NolanMatt DamonAnne HathawayElon MusknewsShareReuse this content

title: BBCPhil/Storgårds / Park & Jupiter Ensemble reviews – stylish Strauss and lyrical Sibelius … and a lutenist’s singalong | Classical music | The Guardian
description: Sueye Park’s poetic, enthralling Proms debut in Sibelius’s violin concerto was followed by an effervescent late-night concert from Thomas Dunford’s Jupiter Ensemble with Laurence Kilsby
Author: Clive Paget
Headline: Sueye Park’s poetic, enthralling Proms debut in Sibelius’s violin concerto was followed by an effervescent late-night concert from Thomas Dunford’s Jupiter Ensemble with Laurence Kilsby
Content: Attention-grabbing … soloist Sueye Park at the BBC Proms.Photograph: BBC/Chris ChristodoulouView image in fullscreenAttention-grabbing … soloist Sueye Park at the BBC Proms.Photograph: BBC/Chris ChristodoulouClassical musicReviewBBCPhil/Storgårds / Park & Jupiter Ensemble reviews – stylish Strauss and lyrical Sibelius … and a lutenist’s singalongRoyal Albert Hall, LondonSueye Park’s poetic, enthralling Proms debut in Sibelius’s violin concerto was followed by an effervescent late-night concert from Thomas Dunford’s Jupiter Ensemble with Laurence KilsbyClive PagetWed 22 Jul 2026 07.01 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 08.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAlate-night Prom can sometimes serve as a musical Rennie, a soothing digestif after 90 minutes of heavyweight orchestral fare. Fortunately, none was needed here. In the early evening programme John Storgårds kept the BBC Philharmonic sufficiently light on its feet, while Sueye Park made the most poetic of Proms debuts in Sibelius’s violin concerto, a cornerstone work she owned from start to finish.If the opening wash of strings was barely audible over the whirr of theRoyal Albert Hallair-con, it wasn’t long before the South Korean violinist grabbed our attention, her lean, silvery tone penetrating and intensely lyrical. In fact, Storgårds seemed to worry unnecessarily, keeping the orchestral volume on a low simmer for much of the work’s span. Park, an enthralling musical raconteur, imbued the first movement with a forthright romantic sweep before conjuring the kind of laser-focused, ethereal fireworks that set the finale dancing.View image in fullscreenDramatically assured: John Storgårds conducts the BBC Philharmonic OrchestraPhotograph: BBC/ Chris ChristodoulouBetsy Jolas, who turns 100 in two weeks’ time, composed Tales of a Summer Sea in 1977 to overcome a childhood fear of the ocean. Louring gong and timpani accordingly threatened a storm as woozy strings and woodwind roiled and toiled, their textures flecked with tom-toms, vibraphone and celesta. At 15 minutes, it rather took its time, but with seven busy percussionists it was rarely short of incident.Richard Strauss’s Also Sprach Zarathustra fits the Albert Hall perfectly and Storgårds clearly knew it. The mighty rumble of subterranean organ turbocharged those over-familiar opening fanfares; burnished strings tugged at the heart in Of the Great Longing. Placing the bell way up in the gallery was a neat touch to ring in Nietzsche’s deep midnight. If The Dance Song lacked the last degree of Viennese schmaltz, it failed to diminish what was a stylish and dramatically assured performance.View image in fullscreenUndeniably infectious: the Jupiter Ensemble with Thomas Dunford (second from right) and tenor Laurence Kilsby.Photograph: Chris ChristodoulouSo, what about that after-hours palate cleanser? Founded by lutenist Thomas Dunford,Jupiter Ensemblemade a singularly idiosyncratic Proms debut alongside British tenorLaurence Kilsby. The programme comprised Dowland to mark 400 years since his death, and Purcell, a composer who is more than welcome anytime.Kilsby’s debonair vocals cut a dash in a slightly breathless Come Again, Sweet Love and were at their finest paired with the ever-sympathetic Dunford in moving accounts of Dowland’s Flow, My Tears and Sorrow, Stay. Jupiter, meanwhile, kicked up their heels in The King of Denmark’s Galliard, while Can She Excuse My Wrongs was deftly juxtaposed with its original for lute, The Earl of Essex Galliard. The effervescent performances may have strayed from early music orthodoxy, but the spirit was undeniably infectious. You’ll have tolisten to the broadcastif you want to know how Dunford cajoled us all into singing Hey Jude.Available on BBC Soundsuntil 12 October. The Promscontinue until 12 September.Explore more on these topicsClassical musicProms 2026BBC Philharmonic OrchestraRoyal Albert HallreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Do you suffer from organ failure? Here’s why you should give this mightiest of instruments a hearing  | Classical music | The Guardian
description: A Sunday morning Prom showcases the magnificent RAH instrument, but the organ’s not just for Bach – check out pioneering musicians from Jonny Greenwood to Kali Malone. Plus, Igor Levit is cancelled – and at the top of the German charts
Author: Tom Service
Headline: A Sunday morning Prom showcases the magnificent RAH instrument, but the organ’s not just for Bach – check out pioneering musicians from Jonny Greenwood to Kali Malone. Plus, Igor Levit is cancelled – and at the top of the German charts
Content: Anna Lapwood at the organ for her Royal Albert Hall debut in 2025.Photograph: Andy ParadiseView image in fullscreenAnna Lapwood at the organ for her Royal Albert Hall debut in 2025.Photograph: Andy ParadiseTom Service on musicClassical musicDo you suffer from organ failure? Here’s why you should give this mightiest of instruments a hearingTom ServiceA Sunday morning Prom showcases the magnificent RAH instrument, but the organ’s not just for Bach – check out pioneering musicians from Jonny Greenwood to Kali Malone. Plus, Igor Levit is cancelled – and at the top of the German chartsWed 22 Jul 2026 07.58 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 08.01 EDTShareIt has9,999 pipes, it’s 155 years old and 21m high. You can see it, but not always hear it, at every single London prom. I’m talking of course about theWillis organ in the Royal Albert Hall, which takes centre stage this weekend onSunday morning’s recital by French organist Olivier Latry.With his mostly Bach programme, Latry’s recital represents the core of the organ’s repertoire, music that has been heard in cathedrals and on concert hall organs for centuries. It will be magnificent in the building-shaking flesh on Sunday morning, but my assurances might not be enough to convince non-organ fans that they should forgo a lazy Sunday morning and head to South Kensington.There are many organ-sceptics among us – and there’s no judgment here! The world of organists can feel like a cloistered domain of inaccessible lofts, mysterious machinery and liturgical codification that resists innovation and audience connection. That’s changing for this generation, thanks to such players as Latry and to the globe-trotting advocacy ofAnna Lapwood, and a generation of experimental musicians (of which more later) who are using the pipe organ as their medium of discovery and wonder.View image in fullscreenOlivier Latry at the Great Organ of Notre-Dame Cathedral in Paris, photographed before the fire and restoration.Photograph: Luc Castel/Getty ImagesThe organ is a miraculous instrument with a millennia-long history – there werewater-organs in ancient Greece, an ancestor of such behemoths as the seven-storey high and 28,762 pipes of the instrument in Wanamaker’s department store in Philadelphia –the world’s largest working organ.Pipe organs can be ear-shatteringly loud but they are also capable of the greatest sonic variety. Buildings like theRoyal Albert Hallwere designed around their organs: the architecture was built to be their resonating chambers. Organs can sustain pitches far beyond the limits of human breaths or bowing arms; they can mimic and synthesise the sounds of the musical natural world, from abyssal rumbles that feel like glaciers calving, producing frequencies that are so low they are felt as vibration rather than heard, to the heights of birdsong and glittering sonic filigree.double quotation markBuildings like the Royal Albert Hall were designed around their organs: the architecture was built to be their resonating chambersIn Halberstadt in Germany,John Cage’s Organ²/ASLSP(As Slow As Possible) is still near the beginning of a performance that’s planned to last 639 years: it began in 2001 and is scheduled to finish in 2640,with another note change coming upon 5 August this year.Jonny Greenwood’s X Years of Reverb (where X = the number of years that an organ or performance space has been extant) is a mere eight hours, made for two organists at a single instrument, a piece written, he says, “to agitate and reanimate all the music, voices and sounds that have ever filled the air/soaked into the walls of the space where the music is played. To coax them out of the fabric of the building, so they can be distilled and concentrated into just eight hours”. James McVinnie and Eliza McCarthy next play Greenwood’s piece at Manchester Cathedral in January, celebrating that instrument’s 605 years of reverb.And there are many composer-organists today who are pushing the experimental bounds of the instrument.Kali Malone’s obsession with it began when she was a pipe-organ tuner. The American musician has made albums that reclaim older tunings and still more ancient conceptions of cycles of musical time and turn them into breathtaking immersions of musical being.Kali has been inspired byEllen Arkbro, another Sweden-based pioneer who uses the organ as a portal to explore arcane and elemental musical regions; whileAnna von Hausswolffhas made the organ the foundation of a musical language that stretches from avant-pop to experimentalism.One more example of today’s pipe-organ pioneers for you: Claire M Singer, whose albums and compositions, which are often titled after Scottish landscapes, places, and histories, areorgan-scapes of slow drones and radiant horizons. Singer makes musical time move as an ecstatic, infinite moment, releasing “all the music and sounds that have ever filled the air”, as Greenwood puts it – but not only, you feel, in the buildings that accommodate these instruments, but across entire mountain ranges and cosmoses.The organ can take you to wondrous musical places. Give it a try: start with Latry as your gateway drug. He’ll be delighted.In Germany, the pianist Igor Levit has teamed up with rapper Danger Dan on anti-fascist singleKeine Angst (“No Fear”). Their scheduled performance onZDF’s satirical news TV show Die Anstalt was cancelled at the last minute earlier this week. The broadcast instead featured aminute-long shot of an empty piano and mic stand[from 47 minutes in] at the point where the song should have been performed.The hosts of Die Anstalt were just as sidelined by the decision as Danger Dan and Igor Levit themselves, andthey appear to explainhow the broadcast silence wasn’t a “technical problem, but a political one”. The German public broadcaster says that Keine Angst’s lyrics went against programme guidelines; and the hosts duly explain the arguments, but “we are in the ZDF, but against the ZDF”, they say; they tried to have Levit and Danger Dan reinstated, but lost; and they go on to discuss – and to read out – the controversial lyrics, putting them in the context of other protest songs that are not censored, and explaining that the plan had been to present the performance and then discuss its urgent provocations with Levit and Danger Dan afterwards.The words ofKeine Angst(approximateEnglish translation) are, in part, a practical list of instructions about how to organise anti-fascist protests and – most controversially for the broadcaster – the song ends by expressing sympathy for left-wing activists convicted for violent actions against the far right. Levit and Danger Dan organised their own concert and livestream last night,Die Keine Angstalt, and the single has racked up a million views and counting on YouTube and Spotify.Levit’s collaboration with Danger Dan is just the latest example of his activism and political and musical thinking, in which he refuses to separate music – from Beethoven, Shostakovich and Frederic Rzewski (his variations on the Chilean protest song,The People United Will Never Be Defeated) from its political contexts, and the role it has always played in movements for social change. InGermany, the debate about censorship, and about how culturally and politically to combat the rise of the right-wing, goes on.This week Tom has been listening to: Nicholas Collon’s debut album withthe Finnish Radio Symphony Orchestraas their chief conductor. It came out in 2022, and it starts with Sibelius’s seventh symphony. That’s as bold a statement as you could make, offering a new interpretation of one of the Finn’s finest pieces. Collon doesn’t do it by deliberate iconoclasm: he finds his own depths in the soundworld: the string polyphony near the start of the symphony, like Sibelius rewriting Palestrina, has a quality of time-suspending strangeness that’s breathtaking.Explore more on these topicsClassical musicTom Service on musicProms 2026Royal Albert HallJonny GreenwoodExperimental musiccommentShareReuse this content
