title: A Toxic Love Story review – this astonishing real-life saga will leave you goggle-eyed and barely breathing | Television | The Guardian
description: In a jaw-dropping return to true-crime form, Netflix’s new documentary delves into the Anaheim Craigslist case – and it’s a horrifying monster indeed
Author: Lucy Mangan
Headline: In a jaw-dropping return to true-crime form, Netflix’s new documentary delves into the Anaheim Craigslist case – and it’s a horrifying monster indeed
Content: ‘This is a monster of a case’ … Michelle Hadley in A Toxic Love Story.Photograph: NetflixView image in fullscreen‘This is a monster of a case’ … Michelle Hadley in A Toxic Love Story.Photograph: NetflixTV reviewTelevisionReviewA Toxic Love Story review – this astonishing real-life saga will leave you goggle-eyed and barely breathingIn a jaw-dropping return to true-crime form, Netflix’s new documentary delves into the Anaheim Craigslist case – and it’s a horrifying monster indeedLucy ManganWed 22 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe sound of barrel-scraping during true-crime documentaries is becoming more and more frequent at Netflix these days (the dismalWorst Neighbor Everbeing the most recent case in point). Such are the perils of being the pioneer of a form; you run out of material first but must keep trying to recapture past glories while you search desperately for another way to seize the fickle audience’s attention once again.A Toxic Love Story is a welcome, if doubtless temporary, return to form. It is a rigorous, 88-minute recounting of an astonishing story that became notorious about 10 years ago in the US and was known as “the Anaheim Craigslist case” or “the Anaheim Craigslist rape fantasy case”, depending on just how many local Craigslist-based delinquencies were occurring at the exact time of reference. It has, after all, been the most criminally fertile of lists.This latest film harks back to the early giants of the genre. The opening third sets up the clear victims – deputy US marshal Ian Diaz and his new partner Angela Connell, who moved into his apartment after a whirlwind romance and soon fell pregnant with twins only to find herself the recipient of ever more upsettingly threatening emails. “You have stolen my life … Good luck getting back to the condo,” ran one of the first. “We will steal your child and watch as it dies,” said one of the later missives, attached to which were often pictures of decapitated bodies or aborted foetuses. The emails came from a stalker going by the name Lilithistruth. Eventually, Lilithistruth even posed as Angela to reply to rape fantasists on Craigslist saying that she would be a willing participant. One of them attacked her. When the police investigated, they found a digital trail that led from Lilithistruth’s messages to Ian’s former fiancee Michelle Hadley. She denied everything but was duly arrested and spent 88 days in custody while officers investigated. (All charges against her were later dropped.)You will have noted, being the savvy media consumers of 2026 that you are, that I said this was the subject of the first third of the film. In the second part, Angela Diaz comes into sharp focus, beginning with a 2am phone call Ian makes to his best friend, Leslie, to tell her brokenly that “the twins aren’t real”.“To put it lightly,” says one of the investigators, “this is a monster of a case.” The question of quite how many monsters it involves is one that keeps you watching, goggle-eyed and barely breathing, until the end. The final third, which opens up a different set of horrors (albeit ones a true-crime story is almost never without), continues to adhere to the previous unsensational approach. It interrogates the accounts of the victims and perpetrators who populate this extraordinary tale, in which fake sonograms and cancer claims are the very least of the betrayals and violations of trust involved, and arrives calmly at its conclusions.More importantly, A Toxic Love Story succeeds in embedding its specifics in a wider context and raising bigger questions. There is the inescapable issue of the lack of interest from police, which seems to have been instrumental in the escalation of Lilithistruth’s threats. There are the virtually uncountable matters raised by the existence of rape fantasists – one of whom appears in the film disguised for his safety but unapologetic about his proclivities – and a place for them to gather, to say nothing of the lack of protection for people who can be targeted at any point in the chain supplying the fantasy material. Which leads to the apparently insoluble problem of the terrible freedoms offered by online anonymity and how we are forced to live in a world in which technology enables the very worst of humanity to fulfil their desires on an unprecedented scale.Far from unrelated to this is Michelle’s testimony, which invites us to think about the harms done by the myths about love that a society deals in, especially to girls. How lies told about the power of staying sweet, the prizing of male attention and of marriage and the pressure put on young women to ignore their “irrational” instincts make them so mentally and physically vulnerable. A Toxic Love Story ultimately asks what it says about our culture that we continue, in effect, to do so much to smooth the paths for predators.A Toxic Love Story is on Netflix nowExplore more on these topicsTelevisionTV reviewreviewsShareReuse this content

title: TV tonight: Katie Price on her wild wedding and life with Peter Andre | Television | The Guardian
description: The model tells all in her highly entertaining, eyebrow-raising documentary. Plus, revisiting a classic Play for Today. Here’s what to watch this evening
Author: Hollie Richardson
Headline: The model tells all in her highly entertaining, eyebrow-raising documentary. Plus, revisiting a classic Play for Today. Here’s what to watch this evening
Content: Peter Andre and Katie Price at the National Television Awards in 2005.Photograph: Tim Whitby/WireImageView image in fullscreenPeter Andre and Katie Price at the National Television Awards in 2005.Photograph: Tim Whitby/WireImageTV tonightTelevisionTV tonight: Katie Price on her wild wedding and life with Peter AndreThe model tells all in her highly entertaining, eyebrow-raising documentary. Plus, revisiting a classic Play for Today.  Here’s what to watch this eveningHollie Richardson,Phil Harrison,Priya ElanandSimon WardellWed 22 Jul 2026 01.15 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 11.50 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleKatie Price: Nothing to Hide9pm, Sky DocumentariesTwenty years before Taylor and Travis’s stadium wedding bonanza, there was Katie Price and Peter Andre’s fairytale ceremony – or, as Kerry Katona puts it, “like Cinderella on coke and speed on a seven-day bender”. The third episode of Price’s documentary focuses on the marriage she says provided the best years of her life … until it didn’t. “I’d love you to interview Pete,” she says. “The chances are zero.” And she’s right.Hollie RichardsonOur Lives: Workhorse7pm, BBC OneAnother little delight in the series that tells the stories of people around the UK. This time, Kate Mobbs-Morgan in south Wales pulls at the heartstrings as she explains how she set up a workhorse business after a cancer diagnosis in her 30s and why she particularly loves her first animal, Kipp: “I couldn’t love somebody more than him.”HRDeal or No Deal: Banker’s Best Battles7.30pm, ITV1This clip selection looks back on the more memorable moments from the hardy gameshow. This time, Georgia and Matt do battle against the Banker – but will they take his offer or hold out in the hope of a bigger reward? Stephen Mulhern is at the helm.Phil HarrisonCasualty 24/7: Every Second Counts8pm, Channel 5Here, we spend 24 hours in the real life of Barnsley’s A&E department. Sister Benita is attempting to juggle rising patient numbers with a limited number of beds. And Jake, a plumber, has mysteriously injured his ankle while walking his dog and keeps finding doggy treats secreted on his person.Priya ElanMy Floating Home9pm, More4View image in fullscreenCan Peter and Tracy’s dream narrowboat plans be realised?Photograph: Windfall FilmsPeter and Tracy live on a narrowboat with a four-poster bed – but after 10 years, it’s time for something different. They take their big design dreams – including a modern kitchen – to boat builder Chris Williams, who has 30 years’ experience. But how will he fare with a £180,000 budget and a three-month schedule?HRPlay for Today: Edna, the Inebriate Woman10pm, BBC FourA selection of landmark Play for Today episodes are available on iPlayer, including this 1971 one written by Cathy Come Home’s Jeremy Sandford. It follows the travails of homeless and jobless elderly alcoholic Edna, played by Patricia Hayes, who won a Bafta for her performance.HRFilm choiceView image in fullscreenLéa Seydoux and Raphaël Quenard in The Second Act.Photograph: Chi-Fou-Mi/Arte France CinemaThe Second Act (Quentin Dupieux, 2024),12.45am, Film4This surreal provocation from Quentin Dupieux could be about the self-importance of actors or the dangers posed to human creativity by AI – but it might simply be a cheeky exercise in dramatic rug-pulling. A group of performers (including Léa Seydoux and Vincent Lindon) play scenes – for an AI director – that almost immediately fall apart as they go off-script, get into arguments, fight, even quit. A meta comedy where you’re never quite sure which film within a film you’re watching.Simon WardellThis article was amended on 22 July 2026. An earlier version misnamed Our Lives: Workhouse as “Our Lives: Heavy Horses”.Explore more on these topicsTelevisionTV tonightfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: My advice to Claudia Winkleman: it’s not you, it’s the giant sofa – try a different kind of chatshow | Fiona Sturges | The Guardian
description: The popular presenter is by no means the first to have come a cropper hosting a talkshow. But new formats provide new opportunities, says arts journalist Fiona Sturges
Author: Fiona Sturges
Headline: The popular presenter is by no means the first to have come a cropper hosting a talkshow. But new formats provide new opportunities, says arts journalist Fiona Sturges
Content: ‘Claudia Winkleman revealed that she will not be returning for a second season.’Photograph: BBC/So Television/PA MediaView image in fullscreen‘Claudia Winkleman revealed that she will not be returning for a second season.’Photograph: BBC/So Television/PA MediaOpinionClaudia WinklemanMy advice to Claudia Winkleman: it’s not you, it’s the giant sofa – try a different kind of chatshowFiona SturgesThe popular presenter is by no means the first to have come a cropper hosting a talkshow. But new formats provide new opportunitiesWed 22 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 04.05 EDTShareYou might think the TV chatshow is an easy format to get right. Hire a well-liked host with comic chops and some successful shows under their belt. Build a cheerful set with a big sofa in the middle. Book some A-list celebs plus the obligatory comedian, invite a live audience et voilà! A prime-time hit is surely born.Except that time and time again, it has proved not to be that simple. Just ask Claudia Winkleman,who revealed last weekthat she won’t be returning to do a second season of The Claudia Winkleman Show on the BBC. The series, which launched in March, filling the gap while The Graham Norton Show was on hiatus,was received with lukewarm reviewsand so-so ratings. The decision to call time on it is reported to be Winkleman’s – “I was just too nervous to enjoy it,” she admitted – though, given the cuts rippling through the corporation, she may have opted to jump before being pushed.Part of the problem is that Winkleman was never going to matchNorton’s impeccable hosting style, honed over nearly two decades. As well as approaching his A-list guests with warmth and cheeky irreverence, Norton is also a sharp conversational steward, adept at keeping everyone engaged and, thanks to a well-stocked bar, gently lubricated. It isn’t as easy as it looks.By contrast, stuck on a gloomy set and with the audience positioned weirdly close to the talent, Winkleman allowed conversations to drift and indulged in silly hyperbole – “That’s the best thing anybody’s ever said,”she yelped at Tom Allenafter a mildly funny gag about his fellow guests’ blathering. It may be that, following rigorous analysis of the first series, Winkleman would have found her rhythm in a second. But for a presenter stillriding high on the successof The Traitors, and who has shown herself to be sparky, spontaneous and funny fronting other shows, it wasn’t worth risking another critical drubbing.View image in fullscreenClaudia Winkleman hosts the first episode of her chatshow.Photograph: Matt Crossick/PAWinkleman is by no means the first presenter to come a cropper hosting a chatshow. In 2006, Davina McCall, then the boisterous host of Big Brother,was roundly pannedfor her self-titled chatshow which was dumped after one series. The same decade also brought the short-livedLily Allen and Friendsand Channel 4’s sub-parThe Charlotte Church Show, where both hosts’ lack of experience left them looking uncomfortable and out of their depth.The depressing common denominator in these cancelled shows is female hosts. Allen and Church were badly miscast but, like Winkleman, McCall seemed an ideal candidate. Yet, for reasons that are hard to fathom, the late-night chatshow has always been a male stronghold. Johnny Carson, Larry King, David Letterman, Jay Leno, Conan O’Brien, Jon Stewart and the Jimmys Fallon and Kimmel all ruled the chatshow roost in the US, while Michael Parkinson, Russell Harty, Terry Wogan, Jonathan Ross, Alan Carr and Graham Norton have done the same here. Comedians, it would appear, seem to land best with audiences. The only woman to have flourished in the format in the UK was under cover of a comic character: Mrs Merton, the creation of the late Caroline Aherne,brilliantly roasted her guestsin faux-naif style, asking Bernard Manning: “Who do you vote for now that Hitler’s dead?”View image in fullscreenCaroline Aherne with guest Patrick Duffy on The Mrs Merton Show.Photograph: ITV/REX/ShutterstockBut there is another more existential threat currently facing the chatshow that goes beyond a resistance to female hosts. Until recently, an A-lister with a new film/TV show/album to promote in the UK would make a beeline for either The Graham Norton Show or its ITV equivalent, The Jonathan Ross Show. But now there are scores of podcasts, most of them operating in video as well as audio, that offer not only easy exposure and a chance to plug their latest masterwork but longer and more in-depth conversations.Not all these podcasts are made equal – many are hosted by celebrities patently ill-suited to the job of listening and asking probing questions – but the public appetite for them remains undimmed. Once again, male hosts dominate, among them Joe Rogan, Steven Bartlett, Louis Theroux and Romesh Ranganathan, though women are getting more of a look-in. TakeBegin Again, the hit interview series about midlife reinvention launched in 2024 that has featured Fatboy Slim, Gisèle Pelicot, Richard E Grant and Gillian Anderson as guests. The host? That would be former chatshow kryptonite Davina McCall.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAll of which would suggest a Winkleman-fronted interview series is by no means dead in the water. Should she wish to have another crack at it, her best bet would be to dispense with the live audience and the giant sofa, and bypass traditional telly altogether.Fiona Sturges is an arts writer for the Guardian and other newspapersDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsClaudia WinklemanOpinioncommentShareReuse this content

title: Why The Polygamist is so much more than trash TV and a guilty pleasure streaming hit | Television | The Guardian
description: Deemed Africa’s first Netflix success, the show’s appeal is rooted in a universal story: what people tolerate for love and money
Author: Nesrine Malik
Headline: Deemed Africa’s first Netflix success, the show’s appeal is rooted in a universal story: what people tolerate for love and money
Content: A catfight … Jonasi creates bespoke worlds of attention for his women.Photograph: NetflixView image in fullscreenA catfight … Jonasi creates bespoke worlds of attention for his women.Photograph: NetflixThe Long WaveTelevisionWhy The Polygamist is so much more than trash TV and a guilty pleasure streaming hitDeemed Africa’s first Netflix success, the show’s appeal is rooted in a universal story: what people tolerate for love and moneyDon’t already get The Long Wave in your inbox? Sign up hereNesrine MalikWed 22 Jul 2026 08.56 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 09.41 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleOver the past few weeks, I have been gripped by The Polygamist, a new, record-breaking South African Netflix drama, which racked up more than 23 million views in its first month. I’ve been zealously talking about how special it is to anyone who will listen. And now it’s your turn, dear reader.View image in fullscreenTradition v modernity … an interesting thought experiment illustrating the African middle class’s trajectory.Photograph: NetflixThe Polygamist is by all accounts the first African smash hit of the streaming age. Released in mid June, the show has topped streaming charts in Africa, and broken into the global top five non-English series globally. It has also broken through all sorts of cultural and language barriers. African and western starshave ravedabout it. Its protagonists havebecome overnight celebrities. And its twists, turns and surrounding discourse so saturate social media that I have had to forgo Instagram and TikTok to avoid spoilers.On first encounter, The Polygamist looks like little more than a guilty pleasure, a high-gloss telenovela about infidelity. When I first pressed play on it, I had absolutely no intention of getting through its staggering 22 episodes. I just wanted to satiate my curiosity about the hype. Reader, I was wrong. So wrong. And now I’m cursed with the zeal of the converted. What we have here is not trash TV, but a profound rumination on tradition v modernity, and the broader universal trajectory of an African middle class.‘This isn’t polygamy, this is dirt’View image in fullscreenCynically weaponised … the relationship dynamics betray their so-called cultural underpinnings.Photograph: NetflixThe concept is simple. The titular polygamist, Jonasi Gomora played by S’dumo Mtshali, is a wealthy business tycoon with a big appetite for good food, expensive whisky, and beautiful women. He is married (of course) to glamorous social media influencer Joyce, portrayed by Gugu Gumede, and has three children. He claims and commands a number of other women in his orbit – none of whom dislodge his wife or main family unit. But tension starts to build when it becomes clear that Jonasi does not only want mistresses, spliced alongside his family life. He wants it all. Several homes, several wives, several sets of children.The predictable cat fighting over Jonasi is juicy and unhinged. Hands are thrown, wigs are snatched and things may or may not be set on fire. But this quickly unfurls into a number of far more sophisticated and unexpected storylines. One surrounds Jonasi’s powerful characterisation. In addition to his wealth, he is charming, playful, forceful, and persuasive. We marvel as he is able to inspire devotion no matter how badly he behaves. His character transcends that of the unfaithful scoundrel into that of the alpha who can create bespoke worlds of attention for each of his women. And so they yield again and again, ultimately (and reluctantly) accepting polygamy as the best arrangement. It is better to be one of Jonasi’s women – and to retain access to this intimate and enchanting world – than to be thrust out on their own.When Jonasi takes a second wife, the discourse machine gets going. His marriage quickly becomes a matter of public debate, as he and Joyce are public figures. She cleverly decides to own the second marriage, and gives candid interviews about it, promoting the act not as a humiliation for her, but as an expression of the capaciousness and generosity of polygamy. The way that polygamy is portrayed is skilfully subtle, teasing out all the complexities of these arrangements in a postcolonial context. On the one hand, Jonasi asserts his right to polygamy as cultural and traditional authenticity, something repressed by colonialism and the secularisation of African society. But Jonasi is also clearly a man who will say and do anything to get his way. And so, like the characters, I found myself torn between the legitimacy of African marriage rites and rituals(polygamy is legal in South Africa and several other African countries), and the sense that these evocative traditions can be cynically weaponised to excuse disloyalty and misogyny. As the actor Mtshalisaid in an interview for CNN: “This isn’t polygamy, this is dirt.”FetishisingbourgeoisvaluesView image in fullscreenHolding on to the rainmaker … Jonasi’s social mobility makes staying with him an attractive proposition.Photograph: NetflixThe tensions between tradition and modernity are also sharpened by the ravages of social mobility. The show transforms from soap into serious dramatic art when it flashes back to Jonasi’s childhood in Soweto, a township with high levels of poverty and crime. Young Jonasi is smart, with ambitions to leave, and uses his charm to persuade the women around him to support his escape from poverty. His backstory feels like a metaphor for all the ravages that rapid social mobility has wreaked on a generation of post-apartheid South Africans. But it’s also relevant to all societies in which profoundly unequal economies have driven people towards wealth acquisition as their only saviour. Despite his flaws, Jonasi continues to enthral those around him, because his journey of social mobility means that he is their saviour too. Values, principles, good character, are all jettisoned in the flight from economic hardship.Relatedly, the series lays bare the toxicity of fetishising bourgeois values: characters squabble over the latest iPhone, covet expensive alcohol and, in one instance, salivate over Wagyu beef. But more unexpectedly, it also leans heavily into the unglamorous side of the characters’ lives. There are sexually transmitted diseases, lost and emotionally devastated children, and instances of physical abuse. The Polygamist does not romanticise or idealise middle- and upper-class African society. Everyone in the show seems trapped in Jonasi’s orbit, suffering higher and higher tithes in the hope of holding on to the rainmaker.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThe Long WaveNesrine Malik, Micha Frazer-Carroll and the team guide you through the biggest Black stories from around the worldPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionBut there is love too. Amid the squalor and devastation, something real somehow always survives – the love that Jonasi’s wives and families still retain for him, and the kindness and dedication among them that still endures. This is the result of some blockbuster acting. Noluthando Shabalala in particular, who plays Jonasi’s daughter, Mpume, delivers a tear-jerking portrayal of a young adulthood mangled in the spokes of Jonasi’s cruelty. Beneath the wild outfits, technicolour makeup and terrible wigs, the actors are putting in a thespian shift for the ages.A quietly radical rollercoasterView image in fullscreenBlack and African universe … the directing team represent other parts of the continent, as well as South Africa.Photograph: NetflixThe Polygamist is a pan-African affair. It is adapted from a novel by Sue Nyathi, a Zimbabwean writer, and is directed by Akin Omotoso, a Nigerian, and Nthabi Tau and Rolie Nikiwe, who are both South African. It is striking how there are no white characters, no interest in even name-checking what calamity everyone is crawling out from underneath, and why the townships existed in the first place. This is an entirely Black and African universe, not concerned with translating itself to a foreign audience. And, paradoxically, that is the reason it has cut through the way it has. Through a particular lens, it tells a universal story, of how much human beings are willing to tolerate for love, and for money.Tap inHave you seen The Polygamist? Share your reaction to the show by replying to this, or emailing us atthelongwave@theguardian.comand we may include your response in our next issue.To receive the complete version of The Long Wave in your inbox every Wednesday,please subscribe here.Explore more on these topicsTelevisionThe Long WaveDramaSouth AfricaNetflixAfricanewslettersShareReuse this content

title: Venus, Vanishing by Rebecca Birrell review – art, desire and the rise of the Third Reich | Fiction | The Guardian
description: This portrait of a Jewish painter in bohemian Berlin is filled with insightful observations
Author: Gabrielle Schwarz
Headline: This portrait of a Jewish painter in bohemian Berlin is filled with insightful observations
Content: Rebecca Birrell is an art historian by training.Photograph: Sophie DavidsonView image in fullscreenRebecca Birrell is an art historian by training.Photograph: Sophie DavidsonFictionReviewVenus, Vanishing by Rebecca Birrell review – art, desire and the rise of the Third ReichThis portrait of a Jewish painter in bohemian Berlin is filled with insightful observationsGabrielle SchwarzWed 22 Jul 2026 04.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn the summer of 1937, two exhibitions sponsored by the Nazis opened in Munich. For the Degenerate Art exhibition, hundreds of confiscated works by banned modern artists – including Paul Klee, Wassily Kandinsky, Pablo Picasso – were gathered: a derisive demonstration of the supposedly anti-German spirit of contemporary culture. The show was also meant as a counterpoint to the firstGreat German Art Exhibition, essentially an art fair for state-sanctioned work, which ran annually until 1944. The official style of Aryan art was traditionalist neoclassicism; popular subjects included idealised female nudes, pastoral landscapes and heroic battle scenes. Hitler was among the main buyers. You probably haven’t heard of any of those artists.What if one of them were secretly a Jew, working under a pseudonym? Rebecca Birrell’s debut novel imagines this scenario, telling the story of a fictional Jewish artist who comes of age in Berlin in the last days of the Weimar republic. Hannah Sherman is working as a seamstress for the family business when she learns that she is about to be married off. She runs away, finding lodgings using the “desirable Christian” name of Schmidt, and begins to realise her artistic ambitions. A free-thinking New Woman, she also takes two lovers: Saul, a museum curator, and Charlotte, a dancer at a lesbian bar where Hannah likes to sketch.We all know where history is going. The worsening economic and political situation – which Hannah, our narrator, prefers to allude to only obliquely – engulfs what might otherwise have been a straightforward story of an artist’s progress. As the Great Depression sets in, Hannah turns to a former client for mending work. Elke Grese, a glamorous older woman, sees Hannah’s art and makes an offer of patronage so generous it would be mad to refuse. But as Hannah soon learns, there are strings attached. How far will she go to survive?double quotation markHannah is an arresting narrator when it comes to visual description – by contrast, her emotional landscape is not as vividly renderedBirrell is an art historian by training; in 2021 she publishedThis Dark Country, an idiosyncratic blend of group biography and cultural criticism about female artists working with still life in early 20th-century England. Her deep understanding of artists and their work is evident here. Both Hannah’s inner thoughts and her conversations with others are filled with imaginative and insightful observations, including but not limited to the old master paintings in Saul’s workplace (the Kaiser Friedrich, a real museum whose holdings were destroyed in a fire in 1945) and the postcard reproductions of famous works that she trades back and forth with family, friends and lovers.While painting her mother, for instance, Hannah is prompted to reflect on Rembrandt’s series of portraits of an elderly woman believed to be his own mother: “In my favourite, his mother’s ageing skin resembles wax, the wrinkles etched into her flesh echoed in the detail of her embroidered cloak, life weighing so heavily upon her she can hardly keep her eyes open.”Hannah sees the rest of the world through the eyes of an artist, too. On a car journey: “From the windows of the taxi, the clouds were zinc white, paint fresh from its tube.” This makes her an arresting narrator when it comes to visual description – although perhaps a touch too ready with similes and metaphors: are leaves really “like small hands in the breeze”?By contrast, her emotional landscape is not as vividly rendered. Without giving away too much of the plot – there are some good twisty turns – it often isn’t clear why Hannah gets involved with certain other characters, who seem more like sketches than people. When talking about feelings, she tends to resort to abstract nouns: “fear stirred in my chest”, “shock gave way to shame” and so on. You get the sense that she doesn’t understand her own motivations. “I didn’t interrogate why I wanted a role,” she admits at one point, when describing her choice to insert herself as a background figure in a painting of someone else. This is not unrealistic: she is, after all, a painter, not a novelist. But it can make for frustrating reading.Towards the end of the book, Birrell offers a glimpse of her own narratorial range with an abrupt shift in perspective, and for the first time enters the mind of another character. Elke – until now a rather distant and mysterious figure – is sitting in a car on a street in Munich, about to pay a visit to the Great German Art Exhibition of 1942. It’s a welcome moment of strangeness closer to the quirky style of Birrell’s nonfiction: a reminder that literature, like art, is at its best when embracing its fullest capacity for invention.Venus, Vanishing by Rebecca Birrell is published by Picador (£16.99). To support the Guardian, order your copy atguardianbookshop.comExplore more on these topicsFictionreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Ask E Jean review – Trump survivor turns out to be tougher than he thought as she fights on | Movies | The Guardian
description: Ivy Meeropol’s engrossing film follows former star columnist E Jean Carroll, now in her 80s, battles through an unfinished series of legal disputes
Author: Peter Bradshaw
Headline: Ivy Meeropol’s engrossing film follows former star columnist E Jean Carroll, now in her 80s, battles through an unfinished series of legal disputes
Content: Unbeaten … E Jean Carroll in Ask E Jean.Photograph: DogwoofView image in fullscreenUnbeaten … E Jean Carroll in Ask E Jean.Photograph: DogwoofMoviesReviewAsk E Jean review – Trump survivor turns out to be tougher than he thought as she fights onIvy Meeropol’s engrossing film follows former star columnist E Jean Carroll, now in her 80s, battles through an unfinished series of legal disputesPeter BradshawWed 22 Jul 2026 04.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 05.14 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDonald Trump has almost succeeded in an insidiously sneaky trick of auto-uncancelling on the #MeToo issue, implying that he is above and beyond and constitutionally exempt, despite being caught on tape boasting of sexual violence. Through a combination of bluster, bad faith, threats, abuse and the simple fact of being US president and flooding the zone on a grander international stage, and moreover with an aggressive army of online trolls at his disposal, he has got away with it. So far.But among his accusers, the one who has so far been successful in two civil suits is the journalist and former advice columnist E Jean Carroll, showing remarkable courage and indomitable good humour. She is the subject of this engrossing documentary from film-maker Ivy Meeropol, co-produced by former Vanity Fair editor Graydon Carter, who incidentally has perhaps a few hail marys of his own to offer forhaving at first ducked the Jeffrey Epstein story. Carroll had an uproarious career in New York media in the 80s and 90s, in which she was a wisecracking Rosalind Russell-slash-Joan Rivers figure, creating a tough broad persona; she was in fact sometimes less than sympathetic to Paula Jones and Anita Hill in the Bill Clinton and Clarence Thomas cases. Carroll had a TV advice show called Ask E Jean (given to her, as she points out without comment, by thenotorious exec Roger Ailes, himself an abuser brought low by #MeToo).In 2019, inspired by the Weinstein case, Carroll accused Trump of having raped her in 1996 in a changing room of New York’s Bergdorf Goodman department store: they had apparently bumped into each other in the store and had a bantering conversation befitting two New York celebrities. He had invited her to help him choose “lingerie” as a present and Carroll, on a deeply misjudged comic impulse, came along. The rape happened after that.Carroll did not therefore defend any libel action from Trump: on the contrary, she took out a civil suit against him for sexual assault and then a second for defamation after he made abusive responses, matched by his political base. In this second case, Trump himself appeared in court. The film doesn’t point it out, but this is a reasonably common strategy in such cases to get the woman to fold. But she didn’t fold. Both juries found in Carroll’s favour, one in the sum of $5m, the next for $83.3m, andthe courts have now forced him to pay the first of these.Elle magazine, which had employed Carroll as its wildly successful agony-aunt advice columnist, lost its nerve when the first lawsuit happened and cancelled her contract. Elle emerges from this film as its second biggest villain, though it’s not clear if any of its editors were approached for comment.The sheer creepiness of Trump is what is so striking: with nauseating contempt, he proclaimed that she “wasn’t his type” – as if that airy claim has anything to do with it. (Ironically, a photograph of Trump briefly meeting Carroll in the company of this then wife Ivana in 1987 shows that the two women did have a comparable style although perhaps everyone was influenced by 1980s New York blond brassiness.)The case has undoubtedly taken its toll on Carroll, who is now in her 80s: but she has been tougher than Trump and his lawyers thought. When Trump leaves the White House in 2028, it could be that this issue is not settled.Ask E Jean is in UK cinemas from 24 July.Explore more on these topicsFilmDocumentary filmsE Jean CarrollDonald TrumpreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Avatar Aang: The Last Airbender review – engaging TV-to-movie adventure | Animation in film | The Guardian
description: A feature-length continuation of the much-loved animated series provides enough for fans as well as the uninitiated
Author: Jesse Hassenger
Headline: A feature-length continuation of the much-loved animated series provides enough for fans as well as the uninitiated
Content: Eric Nam voices Aang in Avatar Aang: The Last Airbender.Photograph: Paramount+View image in fullscreenEric Nam voices Aang in Avatar Aang: The Last Airbender.Photograph: Paramount+Animation in filmReviewAvatar Aang: The Last Airbender review – engaging TV-to-movie adventureA feature-length continuation of the much-loved animated series provides enough for fans as well as the uninitiatedJesse HassengerWed 22 Jul 2026 07.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 14.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAvatar just can’t catch a break at the movies. Not the blue-cat-people franchise that makes at least a billion dollars each time, mind you – though that mega-successful series is part of what vexes big-screen versions of Avatar: The Last Airbender, a Nickelodeon animated series that predates the release of the similar-named, different-premised James Cameron film. Following the anime-inspired, fan-beloved show’s three-season run on cable, the material was adapted into a live-action feature, The Last Airbender, by no less than M Night Shyamalan, released six months after Cameron’s megahit. The Last Airbender was a decent-sized hit, but loathed by fans and critics alike – even today’s crop of Shyamalan boosters can’t find much positive to say about it – and failed to kickstart the hoped-for live-action film series. (Netflix has since made an unrelated live-action adaptation asa TV show.)The Dink review – Jake Johnson’s pickleball comedy serves up engagingly silly laughsRead moreMore recently, Paramount announced that an animated sequel film, Avatar Aang: The Last Airbender, following the characters years after the events of the series, would arrive in movie theaters – only to delay the release and eventually backtrack its theatrical status, turning it into a direct-to-streaming movie. The film was then leaked online, well ahead of its debut. Now it’s getting a token two-city theatrical release, simultaneously with its debut on Paramount+, to make sure it qualifies for this year’s Oscars. Given the franchise’s cinematic luck so far, the film-makers may not need to clear shelf space quite yet.But that’s nothing to do with the quality of the movie itself, which very much deserves a wider theatrical berth than it’s getting, and will probably look more impressive than at least a couple of this year’s eventual best animated feature nominees. Avatar: The Last Airbender was always a good-looking show, its Japanese-influenced animation bound only by TV budgets, but Avatar Aang takes its action-fantasy design further, with old-fashioned hand-drawn characters augmented by gorgeous computer-animated environments and backgrounds. Its colorful, kinetic style is so naturally appealing that animation fans may want to check it out even if they don’t know much about the source material.Should that audience dip into Avatar Aang, they shouldn’t be too lost. As with a lot of TV-to-movie adaptations, the movie seems more or less comprehensible to newbies, quickly explaining that the young adult Aang (Eric Nam) belongs to a near-extinct lineage of “airbenders” in a world where other super-powered tribes can control water, fire and earth. Aang, as established in the series and reiterated here, is also the legendary chosen “avatar” able to command all four elements. Since halting a century-long war at the end of the series, Aang has stayed with his childhood sweetheart Katara (Jessica Matten) as they attempt to rebuild the world in greater harmony. The movie reunites the now-grown Aang and Katara with their longtime pals Toph (Dionne Quan), an earthbender; Zuko (Steven Yeun), a firebender; and Katara’s goofy, non-bending brother Sokka (Román Zaragoza) to find an ancient artefact before the less peace-minded group known as The Denied can claim it as their own. Along the way, they unexpectedly encounter Tagah (Dave Bautista), another airbender who was frozen for years, as Aang was at the start of the series.None of this is as hard to follow as it might sound. Whether it will fully resonate with non-fans on an emotional level is another question – and maybe beside the point, given that this is a direct sequel to a three-season TV series (and, for that matter, the characters are all pretty immediately likable). The more pressing matter is whether the movie’s relative accessibility as a reintroduction will render it less satisfying to those who know and love these characters.Here, too, Avatar Aang deals with pretty typical TV-to-movie translation: all of the major beloved characters are present and accounted for (as are a handful of much smaller but equally delightful bit players), but don’t always feel as if they’re getting their due from a movie that has to focus on the relationship between Aang and his new possible ally Tagah. Worse, Tagah’s character arc will be painfully familiar not to just Avatar viewers, but anyone who has watched a movie where a mysterious new character is introduced and appears to serve as the lead’s mentor. Where could this possibly be leading?Well, mainly to plenty of well-staged action sequences, which may be a secondary concern for anyone who has followed these characters through 61 previous episodes. But there’s an inherent and refreshing novelty to simply revisiting this friend group having aged up another decade or so, and the theater-worthy animation provides any additional glow-up missing from the good-enough story. Though TV seems like a natural fit to continue following Aang and the rest, the prospect of a Star Trek-like film series, where more ambitious Avatar-related stories can be told on a larger canvas, remains a tantalizing one. If Avatar Aang is meant to serve as a proof of concept that this world can work on the big screen, consider that mission finally accomplished.Avatar Aang: The Last Airbender is out in select cinemas on 24 July and on Paramount+ on 25 JulyExplore more on these topicsAnimation in filmreviewsShareReuse this content

title: On the Sea review – tender fishermen’s boat-based tale of forbidden love far from prying eyes | Movies | The Guardian
description: Away from the disapproval of their Welsh fishing community, two men embark on a passionate affair in Helen Walsh’s poignant drama
Author: Peter Bradshaw
Headline: Away from the disapproval of their Welsh fishing community, two men embark on a passionate affair in Helen Walsh’s poignant drama
Content: Sincere … Barry Ward (Jack) and Lorne MacFadyen (Daniel) in Helen Walsh’s On the Sea.Photograph: Verve PicturesView image in fullscreenSincere … Barry Ward (Jack) and Lorne MacFadyen (Daniel) in Helen Walsh’s On the Sea.Photograph: Verve PicturesMoviesReviewOn the Sea review – tender fishermen’s boat-based tale of forbidden love far from prying eyesAway from the disapproval of their Welsh fishing community, two men embark on a passionate affair in Helen Walsh’s poignant dramaPeter BradshawWed 22 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 06.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleBarry Ward brings sincerity and force to this well-intended, well-actedBrokebackdrama of forbidden love in a remote north Wales fishing community. It comes from writer-director Helen Walsh, who is that rare and valuable figure in the arts world: a novelist who is of equal status as an original film-maker. (Her debut featureThe Violatorscame out 11 years ago.)Ward plays Jack, a middle-aged man beset with cares. He is getting too old for the hard physical labour of raking mussel beds; he co-owns this business jointly with his quarrelsome, boorish brother Dyfan (Celyn Jones), who resents the time Jack has spent away from work receiving treatment for cancer which is now in remission. Jack has been with his wife Maggie (Liz White) since they were 16; they are both concerned about their son Tom (Henry Lawfull) getting into bad company and failing to help with the mussel business – although they both like Tom’s smart, level-headed girlfriend Lois (a small role for Leisa Gwenllian, the star ofthe recent Welsh-language drama Effi o Blaenau).Tom’s hoodlum acquaintances have been menacing and harassing local fisherman Bernie (Danny Webb) with dire results; Bernie has been employing an itinerant deckhand called Daniel (Lorne MacFadyen) whose chance meeting with Jack is the spark that ignites the story. The truth is that Jack is secretly gay, or bi, and the film achieves its poignancy and gravitas in showing us this suppressed, denied identity that has run in parallel with his official existence for decades. He and Daniel clearly are attracted to each other, and both know about a cruising ground near a deserted tin mine. The two men take out Bernie’s little fishing boat and a passionate, glorious affair develops when they are, in the title’s significant words, on the sea, away from the disapproval and bigotry of their community.Interestingly, Jack’s gay identity is something that exists on a different plane to his outward persona: but it is no more or less real than the side of him that loves Maggie; though it is my regret about this film that the excellent Liz White is not given more scope to express her character’s inner life.On the Sea is in UK and Irish cinemas from 24 July.Explore more on these topicsFilmDrama filmsLGBTQ+ rightsWalesRomance filmsreviewsShareReuse this content

title: The Cherry Orchard review – RSC’s starry cast brings disappointing lack of sparkle | Royal Shakespeare Company | The Guardian
description:
Swan Theatre, Stratford-Upon-Avon
Tamara Harvey’s production of Chekhov’s final play can seem woolly in meaning and soft-edged tone mutes its force
Author: Arifa Akbar
Headline: Swan Theatre, Stratford-Upon-AvonTamara Harvey’s production of Chekhov’s final play can seem woolly in meaning and soft-edged tone mutes its force
Content: Helen Hunt plays Madame Ranyevskaya and Kenneth Branagh Lopakhin in The Cherry Orchard.Photograph: Johan PerssonView image in fullscreenHelen Hunt plays Madame Ranyevskaya and Kenneth Branagh Lopakhin in The Cherry Orchard.Photograph: Johan PerssonRoyal Shakespeare CompanyReviewThe Cherry Orchard review – RSC’s starry cast brings disappointing lack of sparkleSwan Theatre, Stratford-Upon-AvonTamara Harvey’s production of Chekhov’s final play can seem woolly in meaning and soft-edged tone mutes its forceArifa AkbarWed 22 Jul 2026 02.30 EDTFirst published on Tue 21 Jul 2026 19.19 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleLaura Wade’s reworking of Anton Chekhov’s final, plaintive play brings a dazzle of big names. Helen Hunt is the aristocratic matriarch Madame Ranyevskaya, Bill Pullman her brother, andKenneth Branagh(so soon after his lead role in the Royal Shakespeare’s production of The Tempest) their nemesis, Lopakhin, a shopkeeper’s son here who takes over the reins of the family estate and represents the rising forces against Russia’s old order of ossified elites.But for such a starry cast Tamara Harvey’s production has a disappointing lack of sparkle. You do not feel the story’s charge, its complications around love and its stinging losses. The tone is too soft-edged, almost endearing, and the cast such an ensemble that there is no magnetic centre to hold you.Wade’s new version, based on a translation by Helen Rappaport, seems less like a revisionist reimagining than an updating of language, comically benign and stranded in a pre-revolutionary Russia in which the two aristocratic siblings, Ranyevskaya and Gaev, speak in American accents, while Lopakhin and servant Yasha (Julian Moore-Cook) are Northern Irish. The point about the accents is unclear, and the production as a whole seems woolly in meaning.View image in fullscreenBill Pullman as Gaev.Photograph: Johan PerssonView image in fullscreenHelen Hunt, Guy Henry, Kenneth Branagh and Michael Elwyn.Photograph: Johan PerssonView image in fullscreenEsther Smith and Amber Gadd.Photograph: Johan PerssonThe work was regarded by Chekhov as a comedy with farcical elements and Harvey brings out the farce in places, while ancient manservant Firs (Michael Elwyn) is a highlight with his doddering comedy. This does not make up for the lack of emotional and dramatic impact, although the storyline around Ranyevskaya’s adopted daughter, Varya (Esther Smith), and her yearning for a marriage proposal from Lopakhin does develop some emotional dimensions.Like Branagh’s good-natured, sometimes joshing Prospero, his Lopakhin is amenable, lacking the sharp-pronged anger of Adeel Akhtar’s inBenedict Andrews’ astonishing version of the play at the Donmar Warehouse two years ago. When his class rage comes, after the auctioning of the estate, when he reveals that he is its owner, it is the most arresting moment of the night. But the drunken outburst melts away as abruptly after this scene, back to benign neutrality.‘Bored? You’re never good enough to get bored!’ Oscar-winner Helen Hunt on great roles, unruly audiences and her RSC debutRead moreHunt seems featureless and uncomplex as the entitled, passionate and grieving Ranyevskaya. She lacks personality in the early scenes and emanates generic concern in the later ones, when she feels the estate slipping away. Pullman, at least, brings charm and comic charisma, and you wish his role were not quite so minor.Anna Fleischle’s set design is full of faded grandeur, with chandelier and lace coverings across the furniture. There is a dash of expressionism in the bare branches twined around the room, which gesture at the cherry orchard beyond it. The sound design is subtle and effective, with birdsong and the grinding sound of trees being felled.Written in 1903, when the Russian aristocracy was on the cusp of crumbling, points about social discontent and a rising peasant class are made, often through the play’s eternal student and socialist, Trofimov (Alfred Enoch plays him as a likable prig rather than a straightforward firebrand). But they do not resonate in any significant way, either politically or dramatically.There is so much bustling on stage too and not enough languor. No one seems to feel any Chekhovian longueurs or ennui – not on that side of the stage anyway.Plays until 29 AugustExplore more on these topicsRoyal Shakespeare CompanyKenneth BranaghHelen HuntAnton ChekhovTheatreLaura WadereviewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘Humans are designed to connect outdoors’: community hiking in Slovakia’s High Tatras | Slovakia holidays | The Guardian
description: A walking holiday with a group of strangers to the highest range in the Carpathians promises adventure, stunning views and camaraderie
Author: Jessica Rawnsley
Headline: A walking holiday with a group of strangers to the highest range in the Carpathians promises adventure, stunning views and camaraderie
Content: ‘A startling expanse of rock and sky’: the group traverse a ridge in the High Tatras.Photograph: Jessica RawnsleyView image in fullscreen‘A startling expanse of rock and sky’: the group traverse a ridge in the High Tatras.Photograph: Jessica RawnsleySlovakia holidays‘Humans are designed to connect outdoors’: community hiking in Slovakia’s High TatrasA walking holiday with a group of strangers to the highest range in the Carpathians promises adventure, stunning views and camaraderieJessica RawnsleyWed 22 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 03.11 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWe are giddy as we reach the summit, breathless with exertionand a guileless, childlike joy. In every direction the jagged peaks of Slovakia’s High Tatras stretch out into the distance, nubs on a colossal spine, glittering lakes embedded like shards of glass far below the rocky, ice-streaked slopes. An eagle wheels above. Somewhere on the lunar landscape a marmot squeaks. Easy laughs ricochet around the group, photos are snapped, backs thumped, words of encouragement offered to the more vertigo-inclined.The summit of Slovakia’s Kôprovský štít had been playing a Kafkaesque game of mountain mirage with us for several hours, always just over the next ridge which, once conquered, revealed a string of further, seemingly endless ridges. Eight hours and about 23km after we had set off on our hike through the Vysoké Tatry mountain range, an eddy of smiles rippled from person to person (some, granted, of the more clenched-teeth variety). “Yes, team! We did it!” Roman, our high-spirited Slovakian mountain guide, stood atop the tip, clasping each of our hands in congratulation as we crested the peak.Just 24 hours earlier, we had been a ragtag group of 11 strangers making small talk in London’s Luton airport. But here, on a vertiginous mountain top 2,367 metres (7,765ft) above sea level, friendships were budding, veneers falling away. And this was it: the reason each of us had embarked on an expedition to the remote wilderness of the Tatras with a bunch of people we didn’t know. Connections forged during the shared toil of the climb; the sheer wonder at making it to this place where rock meets sky.View image in fullscreenThe group take a well-earned break on the rocky slopes of Slovakia’s High Tatras.Photograph: Jessica RawnsleyCommunity hiking is experiencing a surge in popularity, with countless groups springing up in the past few years, uniting tens of thousands of people for outdoor adventures. There are women-only groups, LGBTQ+ groups, Black, Indigenous and people of colour (Bipoc) collectives, those catering to slow walkers, “soft girls”, parents, dog owners. Our four-day trip to Slovakia was organised byhiked.upin partnership with Slovakian tour operatorTatra Escapes. Hiked.upbegan during Covid, with childhoodfriends Matt and Adam escaping lockdown stasis on walks around Essex, and has since evolved into a thousands-strong community offering expeditions across the UK and Europe.“The demographic is so vast,” Matt tells me as we pick our way across a boulder field. “Young people, older people, some super-fit, others new to hiking. Most come alone. People join for mental health reasons, or just to get out and have fun, meet new people. You connect with people you might not expect to talk to day-to-day at our events.”double quotation markHere, on a vertiginous mountain top, friendships were budding, veneers falling awayWe are a motley crew in ability, age and disposition, hailing from all corners of the UK. The roll call of professions includes a tennis coach, landscape architect, maths teacher, physiotherapist, art adviser and construction site manager. But the stories I’m told track similar contours: ennui at the digitisation of modern life and city confines; a yearning for nature and the chance to connect with like-minded individuals beyond booze-soaked social settings.Louis, a 24-year-old physiotherapist, believes community hiking taps into something fundamental. “Humans are designed to connect outdoors,” he says. “This is what we did thousands of years ago.” Emma, a 32-year-old teacher, sees hiking groups as a response to urban loneliness and atomisation. “People want to come together without it being centred on drinking or going out; connecting in a more conscious way.”I admit to a degree of trepidation on the first evening, sitting outside our wooden lodge inSlovak Paradise national park, the setting sun splashing gold and purple across the sky. A jangle of nerves and travel fatigue had created a somewhat jarring group dynamic; the quieter members practically tongue-tied while the extroverts bellowed across plates of creamybryndzovéhalušky(potato dumplings with creamy sheep’s cheese and crispy bacon). Was this collection of strangers going to find a rhythm, let alone a sense of camaraderie?View image in fullscreenThe High Tatras ‘compress an extraordinary diversity of terrain into a narrow alpine arc’.The following morning we tumbled out of the minibus on to the Štrba turistika trail. The sun beat down benignly as we followed a winding path along the mountainside, a valley of deep green pine fanning out below. Our other guide, Uri, a gentle giant with a calm demeanour and bottomless supply of small, sweet cherries, suggested we let silence settle among us, better to hear the birds, the wind, the whisper of the pines.The High Tatras – a string of snow-dusted peaks straddling the Slovak-Polish border – are the highest range in the Carpathians and among the most dramatic in Europe. Singular in their compact yet lofty nature – with peaks cresting 2,600 metres – they compress an extraordinary diversity of terrain into a narrow alpine arc.Long shrouded in mysticism and lore, for centuries few dared explore their rugged expanses, believed to be the domain of dragons and ogres. But over the past 250 years, mountaineering has become firmly embedded in Slovakian national identity, the Tatras etched on the national coat of arms and eulogised in the national anthem. The establishment ofTatranský národný parkin Slovakia in 1949 andTatrzański park narodowyin Poland in 1954 – one of the world’s first cross-border protected regions – has kept the land pristine and wild. Camping and swimming are not permitted, and while I longed to cool off in the crystalline alpine streams, I was thankful for the foresight.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Twice a weekSign up toThe TravellerGet travel inspiration, featured trips and local tips for your next break, as well as the latest deals from Guardian HolidaysEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionBefore long, we had broken free of pine and earth and were met with a startling expanse of rock and sky. Sun-dappled forest and wildflower-strewn meadows were replaced by clutches of dwarf pine and obstinate patches of ice amid a tumble of granite outcrops that jut from the earth like crooked shark’s teeth. Reality receded imperceptibly into the distance till there was nothing but the placement of each foot on stone, the kiss of cool mountain breeze, the deafening gallop of my heartbeat.double quotation markBefore long, we had broken free of pine and earth and were met with a startling expanse of rock and skyThere is something about the combination of physical exertion and shared adventure that peels back the layers in which we shroud ourselves. It was not long before group discussion had turned to bowel movements, disappointing orgasms and dating apps. We spent the second day weaving our way along a fairytale trail of wooden ladders, steep staircases and fallen logs through a valley enclosed by steep earthen banks and caves winking from limestone faces. Emerging into bright sunshine and a mountain refuge set on a sprawling viridescent meadow, we nursed refreshing pints of Zlatý Bažant, a comfortable silence settling among us like that between old friends.It didn’t all go smoothly. Tensions flared when an organisational miscommunication left the group without lunch on a nine-hour hike. But, ultimately, that unforeseen trial bound us closer together. When water was scarce, tummies grumbling and boots beginning to bite, it was kindness and generosity that ruled the day – last sips meted out equitably, faster walkers hanging back for blister-addled hobblers, emergency tampons and pee-buddies in abundance.Steeling ourselves for another ascent of about 2,000 metres, one member of the group erupted in a burst of jubilation: “I’ve got fig rolls!” A cheer rose up from our perch on the mountainside.The climb was gruelling, the views spectacular, but most of us had come in search of something a little harder to find. Listening to the chorus of gleeful voices echo across the valley, I thought we might just have found it.The four-day trip to Slovakia was provided byhiked.upandTatra Escapesand cost£650pp(£600 for a shared room). It includedKošiceairporttransfers, three nightsatRanč Podlesok Relaxon a B&B basis,andguides. Follow@hiked.upfor details of future expeditionsExplore more on these topicsSlovakia holidaysWalking holidaysEurope holidaysfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: How to turn beetroot leaves into a classic south Indian vegetable dish – recipe | Food | The Guardian
description: Simple spices, fresh coconut and a handful of lentils or nuts is enough to transform leaves normally destined for the compost
Author: Tom Hunt
Headline: Simple spices, fresh coconut and a handful of lentils or nuts is enough to transform leaves normally destined for the compost
Content: Tom Hunt’s beetroot leaf poriyal.Photograph: Tom Hunt/The GuardianView image in fullscreenTom Hunt’s beetroot leaf poriyal.Photograph: Tom Hunt/The GuardianWaste notFoodHow to turn beetroot leaves into a classic south Indian vegetable dish – recipeSimple spices, fresh coconut and a handful of lentils or nuts is enough to transform leaves normally destined for the compostTom HuntWed 22 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GooglePoriyalis south India’s version of sauteed spiced vegetables, and it can be made with just about any vegetable, though green beans or a leafy green such as spinach, moringa or amaranth are most commonly used. The vegetables are fried with onions and spices, a spoon of urad dal is added for texture (if you don’t have urad dal, a handful of cashews makes a delicious alternative), and it’s finished with a grating of fresh coconut. This fragrant, spicy and flavoursome side dish also works brilliantly with the earthy flavour of beetroot leaves, and I like to serve it with boiled beetroot, yoghurt, chapatis or/and rice.Beetroot leaf poriyal, or south Indian-style sauteed greensI’ve adapted this recipe from the incredibleMaunika Gowardhan, author ofCurry, one of the New York Times best cookbooks of 2025. She writes: “The key to cooking spinach poriyal is to let the greens cook without any water. As they wilt and release their moisture, they begin to take on the flavour of the spices.” Beetroot leaves, which feature in a lot of Indian recipes, cook just like spinach leaves, and I like to use the stalks, too, not least because they impart a lovely red colour to the dish. It’s best to add them with the onions to make sure they cook through. I love the addition of freshly grated coconut at the end, and bought one especially; it is optional, however, and can be replaced with rehydrated desiccated coconut.Serves2-4as a side dish1 bunch beetroot leaves(roughly 160g), or another leafy green (spinach, kale, cabbage)1½ tbspneutral oil, or ghee1 tsp black (or white/yellow) mustard seeds1 dried red chilli(optional)6-10 fresh curry leaves, or to taste2 tsp urid dal, or 1 small handful crushed cashews1 small onion, peeled and roughly chopped¼-½ tsp chilli powder35g freshly grated coconut, or 10g desiccated coconut, pre-soaked in a little warm water for 10 minutes1 handful fresh coriander, chopped from stem to leafTo serve (all optional)Boiled beetroot, cut into wedgesYoghurtChapatis or/and riceWash the bunch of beetroot leaves and strip the leaves off the stalks. Shred the leaves and cut the stalks into 1cm lengths.Put a wok or frying pan on a medium heat, add the oil or ghee and, once it’s hot, stir in the mustard seeds and optional dried chilli. When the seeds start to pop, add a few curry leaves and the urad dal or a handful of crushed cashews, then add the chopped onion, beetroot stems and chilli powder to taste. Saute, stirring, for five minutes, or until translucent, then add the leaves and saute until wilted. Season to taste, then stir in the freshly grated coconut with a few more curry leaves and a handful of chopped coriander, and serve.Explore more on these topicsFoodWaste notFood wasteVegetablesIndian food and drinkVegetarian food and drinkBeans, pulses and legumesHerbs and spicesfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Tropical, Mexican-style and Middle Eastern: Ravinder Bhogal’s fruit salad recipes | Food | The Guardian
description: Forget the underpowered, ready-made offerings of food chains: these vibrant mixes are bursting with fruit and notes of florals, citrus and spice
Author: Ravinder Bhogal
Headline: Forget the underpowered, ready-made offerings of food chains: these vibrant mixes are bursting with fruit and notes of florals, citrus and spice
Content: Ravinder Bhogal's shaved mango and cucumber salad with tajin and tequila dressing.Photograph: Phoebe Pearson/The Guardian. Food styling: Hanna Miller. Prop styling: Lucy Turnbull. Food styling assistant: Thea Hudson.View image in fullscreenRavinder Bhogal's shaved mango and cucumber salad with tajin and tequila dressing.Photograph: Phoebe Pearson/The Guardian. Food styling: Hanna Miller. Prop styling: Lucy Turnbull. Food styling assistant: Thea Hudson.FoodTropical, Mexican-style and Middle Eastern: Ravinder Bhogal’s fruit salad recipesForget the underpowered, ready-made offerings of food chains: these vibrant mixes are bursting with fruit and notes of florals, citrus and spiceRavinder BhogalWed 22 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleMost of our experiences with fruit salad are dull: straight out of a can in saccharine syrup or unripe, slippery, unidentifiable chunks hastily bought from a sandwich chain when you’re trying to be healthy. The best ones, however, are life-enhancing and made fresh at home. And, with summer offering up a bounty of ripeness, now is the best time to make them. Eat them sweet or savoury, heighten their flavour with floral waters, spices, aromats, herbs or citrus, and enjoy for breakfast, lunch or as a refreshing after-dinner treat.Shaved mango and cucumber salad with Tajín and tequila dressing (pictured top)Tajínis a mouth-puckering and addictive spice mix that’s often sprinkled over fruit; it makes an excellent rim for margaritas, too. If you can’t find it, mix a tablespoon of mild chilli powder, half a teaspoon of ground sea salt, a quarter-teaspoon of citric acid and the zest of one lime.Prep10 minCook10 minServes43 ripe mangoes,peeled1 large cucumber1-2tbsp Tajín, or to tasteFor the dressingJuice of2 limes2tbsp tequila1tbsp agave syrup30gfresh coriander, roughly chopped60ml avocado oilShave the mangoes and cucumber using a vegetable peeler, discarding the stones and seeds as you go, then arrange on a platter.To make the dressing, blitz the lime juice, tequila, agave syrup and coriander in a blender, then slowly drizzle in the oil until emulsified. Drizzle the dressing over the shaved mango and cucumber, and toss to coat. Scatter with the Tajín and serve at once.Tropical fruit salad with lemongrass, vanilla and ginger syrupView image in fullscreenRavinder Bhogal’s tropical fruit salad with lemongrass vanilla and ginger syrup.Make this the night before, to give the fruit time to soak up the flavours of the syrup. This is the type of fruit salad you can put into a large jar and take to a barbecue or picnic.Prep20 minCook15 minChill2 hr+Serves61 small pineapple, peeled, cored and cut into bite-sized chunks2 mangoes, peeled, pitted and cut into bite-sized pieces½ honeydew or cantaloupe melon, peeled, deseeded and cut into bite-sized chunks2 guavas,peeled and cut into bite-sized pieces20 lychees, stoned1 small papaya, cut into bite-sized piecesFor the syrup4tbsp golden caster sugar8 makrut lime leaves2lemongrass stalks, bashed with something heavy2cm pieceginger, peeled and sliced1 vanilla pod2 star anise500ml waterPut all the cut fruit in a large bowl. To make the syrup, put all the ingredients in a saucepan, bring to a boil, then simmer for 10 minutes. Take off the hob, leave to cool, then strain it over the fruit. Pop the bowl in the fridge for at least two hours and ideally overnight. This is wonderful served with ice-cream.Labneh with watermelon, berries, rose and pistachio dukkahView image in fullscreenRavinder Bhogal’s labneh with watermelon, berries, rose and pistachio dukkahAdd rosewater with caution and to taste, because some brands are stronger than others – you’re looking for lightly perfumed rather than granny’s bath water here!Prep15 minDrain24 hrInfuse30 minCook20 minServes6900g greek yoghurt1 big pinchflaky sea salt800g watermelon, peeled and cut into wedges, triangles or bite-sized pieces125g strawberries, hulled and halved125g raspberries2tsprosewaterFor the sweet dukkah80g pistachios40g pine nuts1tbsp poppy seeds2tbsp sesame seedsZest of1 large orange1 heapedtsp ground cinnamon¼tsp ground cardamom2tbsp runny honey1 fat pinchsea saltLine a deep bowl with muslin. Mix the yoghurt and salt, then scoop into the muslin, bring the edges of the cloth together to form a tight bundle and tie securely with string. Hang the bundle over the bowl and leave for 24 hours in the fridge. Much of the liquid will drain out and the remaining yoghurt will turn thick, dense and fudgy.The next day, remove the labneh from its cloth packaging and use the back of a spoon to spread it over a platter. In a bowl, sprinkle the watermelon and berries with rosewater, then leave at room temperature to infuse for 30 minutes.Meanwhile, make the dukkah. Heat the oven to 180C (160C fan)/350F/gas 4. In a bowl, mix all the dukkah ingredients, then spread over a lined baking sheet and roast for eight to 10 minutes, until nutty and toasty. Leave to cool a little, then bash roughly in a mortar.To serve, top the labneh with the watermelon and berries, drizzle over any juices from their bowl and sprinkle on the dukkah.Explore more on these topicsFoodFruitDessertSaladMexican food and drinkMiddle Eastern food and drinkHerbs and spicesrecipesShareReuse this content

title: Why hot weather and chocolate can go together | Food | The Guardian
description: Fridge-friendly nougat, malty ice-cream and a hybrid brownie-chocolate bar – your summer bites are sorted
Author: Annalisa Barbieri
Headline: Fridge-friendly nougat, malty ice-cream and a hybrid brownie-chocolate bar – your summer bites are sorted
Content: ‘Wow’: Heist’s milk honey nougat chocolatePhotograph: Heist ChocolateView image in fullscreen‘Wow’: Heist’s milk honey nougat chocolatePhotograph: Heist ChocolateThe chocolate diariesFoodWhy hot weather and chocolate can go togetherFridge-friendly nougat, malty ice-cream and a hybrid brownie-chocolate bar – your summer bites are sortedAnnalisa BarbieriWed 22 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 08.06 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe summer heat requires special treatment of chocolate. Few experts would agree withkeeping it in the fridge, but sometimes you have to gently shh them and do what feels right.Heist’s Milk and Honey Nougatis like a choc-coveredtorronemorbido: a big slab of milk chocolate covering honey-laced pistachio and almond nougat. It’s a disaster in hot weather. But, kept in the fridge … wow. The pre-marked pieces are so big and generous you can’t disobey them, slice your knife into it and then eat the piece within, say, three minutes before the whole lot turns molten in your fingers.View image in fullscreenPump Street has just launched aWalnut Brownie 60% Dark Milk Barwith Haitian beans. It features brownie crumb with English Sharpham Park caramelised, hand-chopped walnuts. It’s rich and deep in flavour.My father used to have an ice-cream shop, and he made the best chocolate ice-cream (which I think is difficult to get right) because he’d mix the chocolate with chestnuts to give the gelato a wonderful mouthfeel. I keep meaning to try to emulate it. Not the same but a very worthy chocolate ice-cream, since it’s the weather for it, is Ottolenghi’sSalted Malted Chocolate ice-creammade with 73% cocoa Ecuadorian chocolate.Soon I’ll be writing about making your own chocolate treats, but I am in love withDominique Ludwig’s salted caramel bars, which feature an oat and almond base, nut butter and dates whizzed up into a dreamy caramel and drizzled with (for me, very high cocoa content) chocolate. I customise her recipe with more nut butter than she suggests and sprinkle a few roasted peanuts into the caramel innards to make a bastard child of a Twix and a Snickers. Shop-bought can go hang.Explore more on these topicsFoodThe chocolate diariesChocolateIce-cream and sorbetrecipesShareReuse this content
